References.Title: EVALUACIÓN EX POST DEL PROYECTO INICIATIVA DE GESTIÓN DE INFORMACIÓN DE MOVILIDAD HUMANA EN EL TRIÁNGULO NORTE - NTMI
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The NTMI project is explicitly and centrally focused on improving migration data collection, analysis, dissemination, and information management systems across the Northern Triangle countries (Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras). The project title itself — 'Iniciativa de Gestión de Información de Movilidad Humana en el Triángulo Norte' — signals that data and information management is the core subject. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: harmonizing data collection methodologies across three countries (actions 1a, 1b), building national capacities for data collection and analysis (action 1c), developing migration observatories and regional information systems (action 1f), using administrative records for statistical purposes (action 1i), and producing migration profiles to inform policy (action 1j). The conclusions and recommendations sections appear particularly rich, addressing standardization of data origins, integration of registration systems, development of a unified form (Formato Único), and sustainability of information systems. The evaluation also discusses the DTM (Displacement Tracking Matrix) as a core tool, and the establishment of a regional migration observatory. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on conclusions at the results and component levels, as well as the recommendations on data standardization and regional database design, would appear most valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN EX POST DEL PROYECTO INICIATIVA DE GESTIÓN DE INFORMACIÓN DE MOVILIDAD HUMANA EN EL TRIÁNGULO NORTE - NTMI
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The NTMI project explicitly focused on tracking, monitoring, and supporting the reintegration of returned migrants — particularly returned NNA — across the three Northern Triangle countries. The project description discusses the challenges of receiving, assisting, and protecting returned migrants, and the conclusions note that information systems were developed to support reception, assistance, and reintegration processes. Key institutional partners include CAMR HN (Centros de Atención del Migrante Retornado), SIAMIR HN (Sistema de Información para el Migrante Retornado), and DAMI ES (Dirección de Atención al Migrante), all directly involved in return and reintegration. The conclusions mention sharing information between migration authorities to reduce re-victimization of returnees, and recommendations address the need for sustainable reintegration support systems. Actions 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes), 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through access to services and opportunities), and 21i (addressing needs of communities to which migrants return) appear most related. However, the report's contribution to Objective 21 is primarily through the information management lens rather than direct analysis of return and reintegration policy effectiveness. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the sections on institutional partnerships for returnee reception, the conclusions on information sharing to reduce re-victimization, and the recommendations on sustainable reintegration support, while noting that return and reintegration is one of several themes rather than the sole focus.

References.Title: EX– POST EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “PROVIDING TECHNICAL SUPPORT TO IMPROVE BORDER AND MIGRATION MANAGEMENT IN SUDAN”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 4, particularly around document security and identity verification systems. The project explicitly focused on training frontline officers in document examination and fraud detection, developing a Passport Examination Procedures' Manual (PEPM), and certifying master trainers at Khartoum International Airport's Forensic Lab. These activities relate closely to Action 4b (harmonizing travel documents and combating identity fraud) and Action 4d (facilitating access to personal documentation). The executive summary and conclusions sections appear most relevant, particularly the discussion of the training component's outputs and sustainability challenges (master trainers being rotated or leaving), as well as Recommendations 3 and 4 on strengthening document examination training and the Immigration Training Centre. A synthesis specialist examining document integrity systems and capacity-building for identity verification would likely find this report worth reviewing, though border management governance is the broader frame rather than migrant documentation rights per se.

References.Title: EX– POST EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “PROVIDING TECHNICAL SUPPORT TO IMPROVE BORDER AND MIGRATION MANAGEMENT IN SUDAN”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to be fundamentally relevant for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. Border and migration management is the explicit primary subject of the evaluated project, as reflected in the project title ('Providing Technical Support to Improve Border and Migration Management in Sudan'). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly related to this objective: capacity-building for frontline border officers (Action 11a), infrastructure and equipment at Khartoum International Airport (Action 11b), document fraud detection training (Action 11b), and the broader rationalization of Sudan's immigration and border management system. The evaluation covers all five OECD-DAC criteria applied to these border management interventions, with findings on effectiveness of training, sustainability of capacity gains, and institutional strengthening. Actions 11a (border management cooperation and capacity-building), 11b (integrated border management structures and procedures), and 11g (cross-border collaboration) appear most directly addressed. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on effectiveness (training outcomes, master trainer certification), sustainability (fragility of institutional gains post-political transition), and recommendations on the Immigration Training Centre would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The report also offers insights on the challenges of sustaining border management capacity in politically volatile contexts — a dimension with broader synthesis value.

References.Title: EX– POST EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “PROVIDING TECHNICAL SUPPORT TO IMPROVE BORDER AND MIGRATION MANAGEMENT IN SUDAN”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute some evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12, particularly around screening procedures and training of border officials. The project explicitly addressed training frontline staff on document examination and fraud detection — relating to Action 12b (specialized training for first responders and border officials) and Action 12c (referral mechanisms and screening measures). The visa system reform component also touches on Action 12a (transparency and accessibility of migration procedures, including technology to simplify application procedures). The effectiveness section discusses training outcomes and the master trainer certification programme, while the sustainability section raises concerns about the durability of these capacity gains. A synthesis specialist examining training systems for border officials and screening procedure standardization would likely find the training component analysis and the recommendations on the Immigration Training Centre (Recommendations 3 and 4) worth reviewing. However, the report's primary frame is border management governance broadly, with screening and referral as one component rather than the central focus.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION REPORT "EFFECTIVE AND SUSTAINABLE DIASPORA ENGAGEMENT FOR DEVELOPMENT IN THE CARIBBEAN"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The entire ESDEDC project is explicitly focused on enabling diaspora contributions to sustainable development in Caribbean countries — the project title, objectives, and all four outcomes directly address this theme. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: diaspora engagement mechanisms (relating to Action 19d on dedicated diaspora offices/focal points); diaspora investment and entrepreneurship support through 'Go See' visits (relating to Action 19e on targeted support programmes and financial products); skills and knowledge transfer from diaspora to home countries (relating to Action 19c and 19j); government policy and strategy development for diaspora engagement (relating to Action 19d); and the role of remittances in development (relating to Actions 19a and 19b). The evaluation covers multiple countries (Belize, Guyana, Jamaica, Suriname) and assesses what works and what doesn't in diaspora engagement programming, providing comparative evidence across different national contexts. For a synthesis specialist, the conclusions on sustainability of diaspora engagement mechanisms, the findings on 'Go See' visit effectiveness, the analysis of government capacity to engage diaspora, and the recommendations on SME development with diaspora would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The cross-cutting discussion of gender mainstreaming in diaspora engagement is also potentially relevant to Action 19's implementation. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION REPORT "EFFECTIVE AND SUSTAINABLE DIASPORA ENGAGEMENT FOR DEVELOPMENT IN THE CARIBBEAN"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 20. One of the four explicit project outcomes is that 'Caribbean countries are more equipped to channel remittances to contribute towards development,' and the evaluation includes a remittances workshop component with a survey of participants. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: government capacity to channel remittances toward development (relating to Action 20g on programmes promoting investments from remittance senders); awareness-raising around remittances' role in development (relating to Action 20f on financial literacy); and the broader policy environment for remittances in Caribbean countries. The conclusions note 'new knowledge and understanding gained around how to channel remittances to contribute towards development' as a sustainability factor. However, the excerpts do not reveal deep analysis of remittance transfer costs, market regulation, or financial inclusion mechanisms per se — the focus appears to be more on the development channeling dimension than on the transfer infrastructure. For a synthesis specialist, the remittances workshop findings and the conclusions on government capacity to channel remittances would be the most relevant sections to prioritize for review. The report appears to be one of several themes covered rather than exclusively focused on remittances.

References.Title: SECOND EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE RENTAL SUPPORT CASH GRANT APPLIED TO RETURN AND RELOCATION PROGRAMS IN HAITI - FINAL REPORT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly regarding sustainable reintegration and return to communities. The RSCG programs are explicitly described as 'return and relocation programs' — this language appears in the report title itself — and the evaluation's primary purpose is to assess whether displaced persons obtained and maintained safe and secure accommodation after relocation. This maps closely to GCM Objective 21's concern with creating conducive conditions for reintegration and ensuring that returning/relocating migrants are not displaced again. The evaluation appears to provide substantive evidence on: (1) the effectiveness of cash-based assistance in facilitating sustainable relocation (Action 21b on financial support in return/reintegration programmes); (2) socio-economic reintegration outcomes including livelihoods and housing stability (Action 21h on equal access to services and employment upon return); and (3) coordination mechanisms among multiple implementing agencies (relevant to Action 21a on cooperation frameworks). The report's findings on housing security maintenance after the grant period, economic outcomes, and the role of livelihoods programming would appear particularly valuable for synthesis specialists. The conclusions and lessons learned sections, as well as the overall effectiveness findings, would be priority sections for human reviewers. It should be noted that the population is internally displaced persons rather than international migrants, which may limit direct GCM applicability — human reviewers should assess this contextual fit carefully.

References.Title: EXPOST EVALUATION OF THE ZAMBIA MIGRATION PROFILE: SUPPORTING EVIDENCE-BASED MIGRATION-RELATED POLICY MAKING AND PLANNING IN ZAMBIA - PHASE II
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The entire project evaluated is the development of a Migration Profile for Zambia — a tool explicitly designed to collect, analyze, and disseminate disaggregated migration data to support evidence-based policymaking. The report title, evaluation objectives, and conclusions all center on this theme. The excerpts suggest substantive coverage of actions 1j (country-specific migration profiles with disaggregated data), 1g (integrating migration topics in national censuses — explicitly mentioned as a downstream result), 1h (labour force surveys with migration modules — also mentioned as a result), 1i (collaboration between migration data units and statistical offices — the TWG co-chaired by Immigration and ZamStats), and 1c (capacity building for data collection and analysis). The executive summary notes that the profile 'triggered a number of other processes such as the inclusion of more questions on migration in the upcoming national population and housing census; inclusion of migration questions in the labour force survey; the explicit mention of migration statistics as one of vital statistics that can be collected in the new Statistics Act.' The conclusions and recommendations sections appear particularly valuable for human reviewers, especially the discussion of data gaps, TWG continuity, and the profile update process. The evaluation also addresses the process value of profile development as a capacity-building exercise. This report would appear to be core evidence for any synthesis on GCM Objective 1.

References.Title: Final Evaluation Report  “Fostering Economic Security through Enhanced Reintegration Support  for Victims of Trafficking” (ERS)  May 2024
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The entire ERS project is explicitly designed to support victims of trafficking in persons (VoTs), making trafficking the central subject of the evaluation. The report appears to provide substantive evidence on Action 10e (measures addressing vulnerabilities of trafficking victims, facilitating access to justice and protection) and Action 10h (providing victims with physical, psychological and social recovery support). The excerpts suggest detailed analysis of: protection and assistance mechanisms for trafficking survivors (economic grants, psychosocial support, mentorship); outcomes of reintegration support in terms of physical, psychological and social recovery; gender dimensions of trafficking victimization (approximately 60% of trafficking victims are women; the project targets women specifically); and the role of national referral mechanisms in Ghana (Ghana Immigration, police, NADMO) and Nigeria (NAPTIP) in identifying and referring VoTs — relevant to Action 10c and 10i. The project background, theory of change, impact findings (Sections 4 and 5), and conclusions (Section 7) would appear particularly valuable for human reviewers conducting a synthesis on victim protection and assistance under Objective 10. The evaluation also touches on prevention of re-trafficking, which relates to Action 10j. It is possible the full report contains additional evidence on prosecution or awareness-raising beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Final Evaluation Report  “Fostering Economic Security through Enhanced Reintegration Support  for Victims of Trafficking” (ERS)  May 2024
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, and particularly for Action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through social protection, psychosocial assistance, vocational training, employment opportunities, and financial services) and Action 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes). The ERS project is explicitly a reintegration support programme for returned victims of trafficking, making sustainable reintegration the central subject of the evaluation. The excerpts suggest the report provides detailed evidence on: economic reintegration outcomes (business establishment, financial stability, self-sufficiency); psychosocial reintegration outcomes (mental health improvements, social cohesion, family relationships); gender-responsive programming (focus on women's empowerment, addressing gendered vulnerabilities); the role of partnerships with national actors (NAPTIP in Nigeria, NADMO and Ghana Immigration in Ghana) in the reintegration ecosystem; and sustainability of reintegration outcomes over time. The impact findings (Sections 4 and 5), contribution analysis and Maslow's hierarchy discussion (Section 7), and recommendations for future programming (Section 8) would appear particularly valuable for human reviewers. The evaluation also addresses climate resilience as a cross-cutting theme in the reintegration context. It is possible the full report contains additional evidence on return procedures and readmission cooperation beyond what the excerpts reveal, though the primary focus appears to be on post-return reintegration rather than the return process itself.

References.Title: ENHANCING IDENTITY AND BORDER MANAGEMENT IN CAMBODIA (EVIDENCE)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 4. The project's title explicitly references 'Identity and Border Management,' and the intervention logic centers on strengthening Cambodia's national identity and border management systems. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of travel document examination, identity fraud detection, passport inspection procedures, and the development of Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for border security and document verification. These themes align closely with Actions 4b (harmonizing travel documents, combating identity fraud) and 4a (improving civil registry systems). The project developed and translated the IOM Passport Examination Procedures Manual (PEPM2) into Khmer, trained officers on document examination, and worked on identity management frameworks. Conclusions C2, C5, C9, and C10 appear particularly relevant, as do the sections on Output 2.1 (SOPs for passport inspection) and Output 2.4 (PEPM2 training). The recommendations on publication quality (R3, R4) and training (R5) would also be of interest. Specialists should note that the excerpts suggest the identity management theme is central but intertwined with broader border management capacity-building, so the depth on specific identity documentation actions may vary across the full report.

References.Title: ENHANCING IDENTITY AND BORDER MANAGEMENT IN CAMBODIA (EVIDENCE)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. Border management is the explicit and central subject of the evaluated project — the title itself is 'Enhancing Identity and Border Management in Cambodia.' The excerpts suggest extensive analysis of border management capacity-building, including development of Standard Operating Procedures for border security and passport inspection, training of frontline and mid-level immigration officers, risk management planning, and institutional strengthening of the General Department of Immigration. These themes align closely with Actions 11a (border management cooperation, capacity-building, training), 11b (integrated border management structures, screening procedures, use of ICT), and 11c (review of border screening procedures). The evaluation covers the full project cycle with findings on effectiveness, sustainability, and impact of border management interventions. Particularly relevant sections for a synthesis specialist would include: the intervention logic (Results Matrix), conclusions C5, C9, C10, C11 on effectiveness and sustainability of border management outputs, recommendations R1 and R5 on operational-level work and training, and Lesson 2 on national staff capacity. The human rights dimension of border management (Q20, C12) also touches on Action 11c and 11g. The full report likely contains additional depth on specific border management mechanisms beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: ENHANCING IDENTITY AND BORDER MANAGEMENT IN CAMBODIA (EVIDENCE)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12. The project explicitly developed Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for border security, primary and secondary passport inspection, and visa management — directly relevant to Action 12c (establishing gender-responsive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures) and Action 12a (increasing transparency and accessibility of migration procedures). The training of immigration officers on these SOPs and on document examination also relates to Action 12b (specialized training for first responders and border officials). The evaluation's findings on the quality and uptake of SOPs (C5, C10), the cascade training challenges (C16), and sustainability of procedural improvements (C10, C11) would likely add value to a synthesis on screening and assessment procedures. Specialists would particularly want to review the sections on Output 2.1 (SOP development), Output 2.2 (training on SOPs), and the related conclusions and recommendations (R1, R5). However, the report's focus is somewhat broader than screening and referral specifically — it encompasses the full border management institutional framework — so Objective 12 is relevant but secondary to Objective 11 as the primary alignment.

References.Title: Final Evaluation Report; Cross-border Engagement between Côte d’Ivoire and Liberia to Strengthen Social Cohesion and Border Security
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential or near-essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. Border management in an integrated and coordinated manner appears to be one of the two central themes of the evaluated project, explicitly named in the project title ('Cross-border Engagement between Côte d'Ivoire and Liberia to Strengthen Social Cohesion and Border Security'). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: cross-border security cooperation between two countries (action 11a); joint patrols and civil-military coordination mechanisms (action 11a, 11g); capacity building for border management officials and security forces (action 11a); intelligence and information sharing between border forces (action 11b); and challenges related to official entry points, border infrastructure, and irregular crossings (action 11b, 11f). The conclusions on effectiveness and impact directly address border management outcomes, including 335 conflicts addressed, reductions in illegal fees and harassment, and improved trust between citizens and security forces. Recommendations on matching official entry points, building border infrastructure, and developing standard operating procedures for cross-border cooperation are directly relevant to action 11g. For a synthesis specialist, the findings sections on Outputs 1.1 and 1.2 (civilian-security collaboration and border management capacity), the impact conclusion, and the recommendations to national authorities would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review.

References.Title: Final Evaluation Report; Cross-border Engagement between Côte d’Ivoire and Liberia to Strengthen Social Cohesion and Border Security
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. Social cohesion is explicitly named as a primary objective of the evaluated project ('Cross-border Engagement between Côte d'Ivoire and Liberia to Strengthen Social Cohesion and Border Security'), and the excerpts suggest deep, sustained analysis of inclusion and cohesion mechanisms. The report appears to provide substantive evidence on: operationalization of local peace committees and cross-border dialogue platforms (action 16a, 16f); women's empowerment and leadership in peacebuilding (action 16e); economic integration through cross-border trade, savings schemes, and market access (action 16d); intercultural exchange and the spirit of 'vivre ensemble' (action 16a, 16h); and youth engagement in conflict prevention (action 16f). The conclusions on effectiveness, impact, and gender/equity directly address social cohesion outcomes, including 335 conflicts resolved, increased trust between communities and authorities, and women's enhanced roles in decision-making. The project's 32% gender-sensitive budget allocation for empowerment activities is highlighted as a best practice. For a synthesis specialist, the findings on Outputs 2.1 and 2.2 (conflict prevention platforms and cross-border social/cultural/economic initiatives), the impact and gender conclusions, and the recommendations on women's empowerment and community resources would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: ADDRESSING IRREGULAR MIGRATION IN SOUTHERN AFRICA - AN INTERNAL REVIEW OF PROJECT ACHIEVEMENTS (2010-2016)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The protection of vulnerable migrants — including unaccompanied migrant children (UMCs), victims of trafficking (VoT), asylum seekers, and smuggled persons — appears to be the central subject of the evaluated project. The project's overarching goal explicitly references 'upholding the rights of migrants,' and multiple project components directly address vulnerability identification, referral, and assistance. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis relevant to several associated actions: Action 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerability, including UMCs and trafficking victims), Action 7e (child protection systems and best interests of the child), and Action 7f (specialized procedures for unaccompanied and separated children). The capacity building component appears to have generated findings on improved skills in detecting trafficking victims and screening/referring vulnerable migrants. The direct assistance component documents AVR provision to over 1,522 vulnerable migrants. Recommendations address screening tools, referral mechanisms, and inter-sectoral coordination for vulnerable migrant case management. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on capacity building outcomes (particularly on trafficking detection and UMC referral), the direct assistance findings, and the recommendations on screening and referral mechanisms would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: ADDRESSING IRREGULAR MIGRATION IN SOUTHERN AFRICA - AN INTERNAL REVIEW OF PROJECT ACHIEVEMENTS (2010-2016)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Trafficking in persons appears as a significant and recurring theme throughout the project and evaluation. The capacity building component explicitly addresses detection of trafficking victims, and the excerpts note 'demonstrable increase in knowledge and skills were evident in all the six countries' including 'improved skills in knowledge in detecting victims of trafficking.' The project's beneficiaries explicitly include 'Victims of Trafficking (VoT),' and the direct assistance component provides services to trafficking victims. The excerpts also reference changes in counter-trafficking law as an unintended positive outcome of capacity building. This appears relevant to Actions 10c (monitoring irregular migration routes exploited by trafficking networks), 10e (measures addressing vulnerabilities of trafficking victims), 10g (strengthening legislation and procedures), and 10i (training programmes for public officials to identify trafficking). However, trafficking appears as one of several themes rather than the sole focus, and the excerpts suggest the analytical depth on trafficking-specific mechanisms may be somewhat limited compared to the broader vulnerability protection theme. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the capacity building findings sections and the recommendations on training and referral mechanisms, framing these as pointers for human reviewers rather than definitive characterizations.

References.Title: ADDRESSING IRREGULAR MIGRATION IN SOUTHERN AFRICA - AN INTERNAL REVIEW OF PROJECT ACHIEVEMENTS (2010-2016)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12. Screening, assessment, and referral mechanisms for vulnerable migrants appear as substantive themes in the evaluation. The capacity building component explicitly addresses improved skills in 'screening and referring vulnerable migrants such as Unaccompanied Migrant Children (UAMs),' and the direct assistance component references 'AVR screening tools and guidelines' developed through the project. The excerpts suggest analysis of referral mechanisms between government departments and NGOs, and the site observation at Musina border post appears to have assessed 'the process of irregular migration screening, the adequacy of tools used.' These elements relate to Actions 12b (specialized training for first responders on identification and referral), 12c (gender-responsive and child-sensitive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures), and 12d (identification and referral of unaccompanied migrant children). The recommendations section addresses ToT follow-up and CB package refinement in ways that connect to building sustainable screening and referral capacity. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the capacity building findings, the site observation case study on screening processes, and the recommendations on referral mechanisms, framing these as pointers for human reviewers.

References.Title: ADDRESSING IRREGULAR MIGRATION IN SOUTHERN AFRICA - AN INTERNAL REVIEW OF PROJECT ACHIEVEMENTS (2010-2016)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Assisted Voluntary Return (AVR) is one of the four core project components and appears as a substantive analytical theme throughout the evaluation. The direct assistance component documents over 1,522 vulnerable migrants returned through the project, and the evaluation includes interviews with AVR beneficiaries. The excerpts reveal important findings on return outcomes — notably that returnees 'regretted having volunteered to return home because they realized the conditions that led them to their migration in the first place remained the same or got worse,' with many contemplating repeat migration. This directly addresses the sustainability dimension of GCM Objective 21 and relates to Action 21b (gender-responsive return and reintegration programmes based on free, prior and informed consent) and Action 21h (sustainable reintegration). The evaluation also documents AVR screening tools and guidelines developed through the project, cross-border collaboration mechanisms for return, and recommendations for post-return monitoring and reintegration support. The recommendation to embed post-AVR data collection and consider reintegration support to prevent repeat migration cycles is particularly relevant to Action 21h. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the direct assistance findings, the AVR beneficiary interview results, and the recommendations on AVR consolidation and reintegration, framing these as pointers for human reviewers. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: DIGNITY AND RIGHTS IN CENTRAL ASIA FINAL PERFORMANCE EVALUATION
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to contribute meaningful evidence relevant to GCM Objective 3. The DAR program's Objective 1 — increasing citizen knowledge of human rights norms — is substantially implemented through information and awareness-raising campaigns, which align closely with GCM actions 3c and 3e. The excerpts describe extensive pre-departure orientation training (action 3e), multi-modal information campaigns (radio, TV, online, physical events, leaflets), hotline services providing information and referrals (action 3c), and training of journalists for ethical reporting. The executive summary section on Objective 1 findings appears particularly relevant, detailing reach of campaigns (13 million people), effectiveness of different media modes, and geographic coverage challenges in rural areas. The recommendations section also addresses information campaign design. Synthesis specialists examining evidence on pre-departure information provision and awareness-raising for migrants would likely find value in reviewing the Objective 1 findings and recommendations sections of this report.

References.Title: DIGNITY AND RIGHTS IN CENTRAL ASIA FINAL PERFORMANCE EVALUATION
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to contribute substantial evidence relevant to GCM Objective 7. The DAR program's core mandate involves identifying and assisting vulnerable migrants (VMs) and victims of trafficking (VOTs) — directly aligned with actions 7b, 7c, 7g, and 7k. The excerpts describe: Emergency Fund support for migrants in vulnerable situations (2,857 recipients); legal, medical, and psychosocial assistance; gender-responsive approaches (female EF targets, gender disaggregated data); referral mechanisms; shelter services; reintegration support; and legal assistance. The program explicitly targets women at risk, trafficking victims, labor migrants, and families of migrants. The executive summary sections on Objective 2 findings appear particularly relevant, detailing protection system outcomes, hotline services, and referral mechanisms. The recommendations on NRMs, reintegration, and EF expansion also directly address vulnerability response. Synthesis specialists examining evidence on protection systems for vulnerable migrants would likely find this report valuable, particularly the Objective 2 findings and the gaps analysis.

References.Title: DIGNITY AND RIGHTS IN CENTRAL ASIA FINAL PERFORMANCE EVALUATION
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Counter-trafficking in persons (CTIP) is the explicit and central focus of the DAR program and this evaluation. The report title, program objectives, and all evaluation questions directly address TIP prevention, protection, and prosecution — aligning with virtually all actions under GCM Objective 10. Specific alignments include: awareness-raising campaigns on TIP risks (action 10j); victim identification procedures and SOPs (action 10i, 10e); legal framework development aligned with the Palermo Protocol (action 10a, 10f); prosecution support and legal toolkit updates (action 10g); protection and assistance to VOTs including psychosocial support, income generation, and reintegration (action 10h); national referral mechanisms (action 10e); and training of law enforcement and service providers (action 10i). The evaluation covers five countries over five years with extensive qualitative and quantitative data. For a synthesis specialist, the executive summary findings on all three objectives, the gaps analysis (Q2), the TIP tier ranking discussion (Q4), and the recommendations sections would all appear highly relevant as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains substantially more evidence than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: DIGNITY AND RIGHTS IN CENTRAL ASIA FINAL PERFORMANCE EVALUATION
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to contain meaningful evidence relevant to GCM Objective 21, particularly on reintegration. The DAR program explicitly supports repatriation and reintegration of trafficking victims and vulnerable migrants — aligning with actions 21b and 21h. The excerpts describe: income generation grants for VOTs (455 recipients); Emergency Fund support enabling beneficiaries to 'reintegrate back into their homeland'; psychosocial, legal, and economic reintegration assistance; shelter services; and recommendations for sustainable reintegration plans linked to State Social Order funding. The gaps analysis also identifies reintegration as an area needing strengthening. The recommendations section on reintegration (under Objective 2) appears particularly relevant for synthesis specialists examining evidence on reintegration programming for trafficking victims and vulnerable migrants. The report's focus is specifically on reintegration of VOTs and VMs rather than general return migration, which should be noted as a scope consideration.

References.Title: Project End line Evaluation REDUCE TRANSNATIONAL SECURITY THREATS WHILE INCREASING BORDER SECURITY AND REGULAR MIGRATION IN UGANDA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. Border management in an integrated, secure, and coordinated manner is the explicit and central subject of the evaluation. The project title itself — 'Reduce Transnational Security Threats While Increasing Border Security and Regular Migration in Uganda' — directly reflects the GCM Objective 11 theme. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple associated actions: Action 11a (international and regional border management cooperation, joint cross-border trainings, capacity-building) is addressed through the maritime training program and inter-agency cooperation between DCIC, UPDF, and Uganda Police Force; Action 11b (integrated border management structures) is addressed through the updated DCIC operational guidelines and MIDAS systems; Action 11d (technical cooperation agreements for assets and equipment) is addressed through the provision of patrol vehicles and boats; Action 11g (cross-border collaboration on treatment of persons crossing borders) is addressed through the joint patrol operations on Lake Albert. The evaluation covers relevance, effectiveness, efficiency, sustainability, and impact of these border management interventions with dedicated sections on each. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on inter-agency coordination outcomes (DCIC-UPDF-UPF joint operations), maritime capacity building results, the updated operational guidelines, and sustainability mechanisms would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is likely that the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Project End line Evaluation REDUCE TRANSNATIONAL SECURITY THREATS WHILE INCREASING BORDER SECURITY AND REGULAR MIGRATION IN UGANDA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12. The project explicitly involved updating DCIC operational guidelines and manuals for immigration procedures, which relates directly to Action 12a (increasing transparency and accessibility of migration procedures) and Action 12c (establishing referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures). The May 2021 Operational Manual developed under the IOM project is highlighted as a key output, and the evaluation assesses its development process and alignment with international standards. The training of immigration officers in border screening and management also touches on Action 12b (specialized trainings for first responders and border officials). However, the excerpts suggest that procedural screening and individual assessment are secondary to the maritime security and capacity-building focus. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the sections on the DCIC operational guidelines development process and the training outcomes for border officials, while noting that this is one of several themes rather than the central focus.

References.Title: ÉVALUATION FINALE DU PROJET "FORAS — RENFORCEMENT DES OPPORTUNITÉS DE RÉINTÉGRATION"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 3, particularly regarding information provision to migrants at pre-departure and pre-return stages. The FORAS project explicitly includes 'Produit 1.1: Conseil et orientation des migrants' — providing detailed information on reintegration opportunities in countries of origin through awareness sessions and communication tools. The report discusses information campaigns, migrant orientation points (Points d'Orientation pour les Migrants) in Casablanca, Rabat and Oujda, and recommendations on mobile information missions and use of digital tools (MigApp, website, videos). This relates to Actions 3c (information points along migration routes) and 3e (pre-departure orientation). The conclusions and recommendations sections appear to contain substantive analysis of information effectiveness and gaps. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on Produit 1.1 outcomes, the recommendations on information and awareness (recommendations 1–8), and the discussion of information tools' effectiveness — though information provision appears as one of several themes rather than the sole focus.

References.Title: ÉVALUATION FINALE DU PROJET "FORAS — RENFORCEMENT DES OPPORTUNITÉS DE RÉINTÉGRATION"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The FORAS project is explicitly and entirely focused on voluntary return and sustainable reintegration — these are its primary subject matter. The report title itself ('FORAS — Renforcement des opportunités de réintégration') signals this centrality. The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of pre-departure preparation for reintegration (Produit 1.2), coordination between pre-return assistance and post-return reintegration support (Produit 1.3), and the sustainability of reintegration outcomes across eight West and Central African countries. The excerpts contain substantive analysis directly addressing Actions 21b (gender-responsive voluntary return and reintegration programmes with free, prior and informed consent), 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through social protection, vocational training, employment, psychosocial assistance), and 21i (addressing needs of communities to which migrants return). Key areas likely valuable for synthesis specialists include: the conclusions on the link between pre-departure orientation and post-return reintegration outcomes; the analysis of sustainability challenges; the discussion of coordination between OIM Morocco and country-of-origin missions; the 38 recommendations on improving reintegration programming; and the assessment of the AVRR programme's effectiveness. The report also appears to offer comparative evidence across multiple countries of origin, which would be particularly valuable. It is possible the full report contains additional depth beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Mid-term evaluation of the project: Addressing Mixed Migration Flows in the Western Balkans
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. One of the four project outcomes (Outcome 1) is explicitly dedicated to ensuring 'authorities and humanitarian responders have access to accurate, up-to-date data and enhanced knowledge on migrants arriving to, and present in the WB.' The excerpts indicate that the project includes a Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM) component and data management activities, and the evaluation assesses the effectiveness of these data-related interventions. The conclusions section notes that the project 'ensured a significant change in the accessibility of crucial information regarding migrant movements, influencing the awareness of institutional partners to make fact-based decisions.' This appears to relate to GCM actions 1a (improving migration data), 1c (building national capacities in data collection and analysis), and potentially 1f (regional observatories/research). The evaluation also includes recommendations on strengthening monitoring mechanisms and evidence-based decision making. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on Outcome 1 achievements, the coherence and effectiveness conclusions related to data management, and Recommendation 4 on strengthening monitoring mechanisms. However, the data theme is one of four outcomes and does not appear to be the dominant focus of the evaluation overall, which limits the score.

References.Title: Mid-term evaluation of the project: Addressing Mixed Migration Flows in the Western Balkans
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project's stated primary aim includes ensuring 'vulnerable migrants in the WB are better protected,' and Outcome 2 focuses on increasing reception and referral capacities for vulnerable migrants in migrant facilities. The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of protection and referral mechanisms, beneficiary satisfaction, and the identification and assistance of migrants in vulnerable situations. The excerpts reference mobile teams for outreach, tailored services in TRCs, and feedback mechanisms for beneficiaries — all relating to GCM actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerability), 7k (involving local authorities in identification and referral), and 7l (national policies to address needs of vulnerable migrants). The impact section notes significant changes in individual beneficiaries' lives and mentions risks such as trafficking within TRCs and measures taken to mitigate them (relating to 7b and 7e). The evaluation also covers unaccompanied and separated children implicitly through child-sensitive approaches. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the effectiveness and impact conclusions, the sections on Outcome 2 implementation, and the discussion of vulnerability mitigation measures. This is one of the report's dominant themes.

References.Title: Mid-term evaluation of the project: Addressing Mixed Migration Flows in the Western Balkans
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute some evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. Outcome 4 of the project explicitly addresses 'local authorities' strengthened capacities to address the needs of communities most affected by mixed migration movements in Republika Srpska,' and the project includes a dedicated Social Cohesion component (Outputs 4.1 and 4.2). The evaluation devotes specific attention to this component, including recommendations 4 and 5 which focus on strengthening monitoring of Social Cohesion outputs and clarifying how this component contributes to project impact. The conclusions note that 'for the first time, entry into migrant response-related activities within the territory of RS was made possible,' suggesting community-level integration work. This relates to GCM actions 16a (promoting mutual respect and social cohesion), 16c (national policy goals for inclusion), and 16f (community centres and programmes). However, the evaluation appears somewhat critical of this component's monitoring and clarity of contribution, and the excerpts suggest it is the weakest-analyzed outcome. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the Outcome 4 sections, the sustainability conclusions on social cohesion, and Recommendations 4 and 5, while noting the acknowledged limitations in monitoring data for this component.

References.Title: Mid-term evaluation of the project: Addressing Mixed Migration Flows in the Western Balkans
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Outcome 3 of the project is explicitly dedicated to 'migrants in transit in the WB have access to return and reintegration assistance,' and the Assisted Voluntary Return and Reintegration (AVRR) program is a core project component. The evaluation assesses the effectiveness, efficiency, impact, and sustainability of AVRR activities, including post-return reintegration support. The impact section notes that '113 individuals gained new life opportunities through the AVRR program.' The evaluation includes a dedicated FGD with AVRR beneficiaries in their countries of origin focusing on post-return experiences and reintegration effectiveness. Sustainability concerns about the continuity of return and reintegration services are also analyzed. This directly relates to GCM actions 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes based on free, prior and informed consent), 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through social protection, vocational training, employment), and 21i (addressing needs of communities to which migrants return). The evaluation also notes potential risks such as exploitation of non-repayable AVRR funds, which is relevant to action 21b's emphasis on genuine voluntariness. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the Outcome 3 effectiveness and impact sections, the FGD findings with AVRR beneficiaries, and the sustainability conclusions on return program continuity. It is possible the full report contains additional AVRR-specific analysis beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Socio-Economic Reintegration of Returnees and other Vulnerable Households in migration affected areas severely impacted by COVID-19 Pandemic
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. Social cohesion is an explicit project outcome (Outcome 3: Government of Lesotho improved ability to enhance social unity/cohesion through community development initiatives), and the evaluation assesses community development cooperatives specifically designed to promote social cohesion among returnees and host communities. Actions 16a (mutual respect and integration policies), 16d (labour market integration), and 16f (community centres/programmes for migrant participation) appear most related. The conclusions section discusses community commitment and collaboration as indicators of social cohesion progress. The community development projects section and the conclusions on social cohesion outcomes would appear particularly valuable for human reviewers to prioritize. The report likely offers evidence on what community-level reintegration and social cohesion programming looks like in a low-income, migration-affected context, though the short project duration limits depth of impact evidence.

References.Title: Socio-Economic Reintegration of Returnees and other Vulnerable Households in migration affected areas severely impacted by COVID-19 Pandemic
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute relevant evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 18. Vocational skills training is a central project activity, with 198 returnees trained across multiple trades (tailoring, hairdressing, catering, automotive, carpentry, electrical installation, etc.) identified through a local market assessment. The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of this training, including graduation rates (99%), business coaching participation (97%), and provision of startup kits and capital. Action 18e (global skills partnerships and training capacities) and 18g (bilateral programmes promoting skills development and entrepreneurship) appear most related. The trades skills training section, business coaching section, and conclusions on livelihood improvement would be most valuable for human reviewers. The report likely offers practical evidence on demand-driven vocational training design and implementation for returnee populations in a low-income context, though mutual recognition of qualifications across borders is not addressed.

References.Title: Socio-Economic Reintegration of Returnees and other Vulnerable Households in migration affected areas severely impacted by COVID-19 Pandemic
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Return and sustainable reintegration is the explicit and central subject of the evaluated project — the title itself is 'Socio-Economic Reintegration of Returnees and other Vulnerable Households in Migration Affected Areas severely impacted by COVID-19 Pandemic.' The evaluation directly assesses reintegration programming outcomes across multiple dimensions. Actions 21b (gender-responsive return and reintegration programmes with social and financial support), 21h (sustainable reintegration through vocational training, employment opportunities, financial services, and entrepreneurship), and 21i (addressing needs of communities to which migrants return) appear most directly addressed. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: vocational skills training for returnees, business coaching and startup capital provision, community development projects for social cohesion, government capacity building (DISCOSEC training), and lessons learned from phased reintegration programming. The conclusions, recommendations, project achievements sections (Outcomes 1, 2, and 3), and lessons learned section would all appear highly valuable for human reviewers conducting a synthesis on reintegration programming. The report likely offers evidence on what comprehensive reintegration support looks like in a low-income, COVID-affected context, including the role of government capacity building alongside individual livelihood support.

References.Title: Final Internal Project Evaluation Reduction of Economic and Human Loss and Displacement by Natural Disasters through Community-based Resilience building and the Capacity-building of the Government at All Levels in Papua New Guinea
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be highly valuable for a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its actions related to disaster risk reduction, climate resilience, and displacement prevention. The project evaluated is explicitly focused on reducing economic and human loss and displacement caused by natural disasters through community-based resilience-building — directly addressing the core theme of this objective. The report appears to provide substantive analysis across multiple associated actions: Action 2b (investing in resilience and disaster risk reduction), Action 2c (strengthening early warning systems and emergency preparedness mechanisms), Action 2i (developing adaptation and resilience strategies to natural disasters), and Action 2j (integrating displacement considerations into disaster preparedness strategies). The context analysis section documents PNG's extreme vulnerability to natural hazards and the scale of displacement, while the findings sections on Outcomes 1–4 assess the effectiveness of DRM capacity-building at national, provincial, and community levels. The Sendai Framework is explicitly referenced as an alignment framework. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on community-based DRM planning (Outcome 3), provincial DRM strategies (Outcome 2), and the sustainability discussion would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The report also addresses IDP reintegration (Outcome 4), touching on Action 2f (humanitarian-development nexus). It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: STRENGTHENING SOCIAL COHESION AND STABILITY IN SLUM POPULATIONS
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly around actions 2b and 2e. The SSCoS project explicitly targeted root causes of inter-communal conflict and migration pressures — youth unemployment, poverty, and lack of economic opportunity in urban slum settlements. The project context section describes Uganda's high youth unemployment (21% in Kampala), poverty trends, and structural barriers to economic growth, which are precisely the adverse drivers GCM Objective 2 seeks to address. The evaluation assesses interventions including vocational training, small business start-ups, cooperatives, and income-generating activities — all directly relevant to Action 2e (investing in human capital development, vocational training, youth unemployment reduction). The conclusions and recommendations discuss alignment with Uganda's NDP III, SDG 16, and youth empowerment programmes. The project's focus on preventing radicalization and violent extremism by addressing socioeconomic grievances also connects to Action 2b (investing in programmes to eliminate adverse drivers including employment creation and inclusive economic growth). Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the effectiveness section on vocational training and small business outcomes, the context section on structural drivers, and the recommendations on strategic linkages with national youth programmes. The report appears to offer concrete programmatic evidence on what works in addressing structural drivers in an urban migration context, though the primary framing is around social cohesion rather than migration drivers per se.

References.Title: STRENGTHENING SOCIAL COHESION AND STABILITY IN SLUM POPULATIONS
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. Social cohesion is explicitly named in the project title ('Strengthening Social Cohesion and Stability in Slum Populations'), and the evaluation's primary subject is a project designed to foster inclusion and cohesion between migrants, refugees, and host communities in Kampala's urban settlements. The project directly addresses Actions 16a (promoting mutual respect and social cohesion), 16d (labour market integration and decent work access), 16e (empowering migrant women), 16f (community centres and intercultural dialogue), and 16g (vocational and civic integration). The evaluation assesses outcomes across six result areas including increased community resilience, youth economic empowerment, and improved relations between migrants and host populations. Multiple conclusions and recommendations directly address social cohesion programming effectiveness, gender-transformative approaches, and the integration of refugees and host communities. The executive summary, effectiveness section, and conclusions on community cohesion outcomes would appear particularly valuable for human reviewers. The report also provides evidence on what programmatic approaches work in urban informal settlement contexts — a relatively underrepresented setting in GCM evidence. The full report likely contains additional depth on cohesion outcomes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Mid-Term Review of the project Understanding and Managing Internal Migration in Mongolia (UMIMM)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The UMIMM project's Outcome 1 is explicitly dedicated to improving the quality of national data on internal migration for evidence-based policies and programmes. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of data collection methodologies (specifically the Displacement Tracking Matrix/DTM), its limitations and potential, and efforts to integrate migration indicators into national censuses and surveys — directly relating to Actions 1a, 1c, 1g, and 1i. The effectiveness section discusses at length how DTM data complements traditional collection methods and fills gaps, while the recommendations section addresses the need for a comprehensive roadmap on improving migration data. Cooperation with the NSO and GASR is also noted. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on migration data (R1) under Effectiveness, the discussion of DTM methodology, and the recommendations on data governance would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is worth noting this report concerns internal rather than international migration, which may limit direct applicability to some GCM Objective 1 actions, but the data collection and evidence-based policy dimensions are strongly present.

References.Title: Mid-Term Review of the project Understanding and Managing Internal Migration in Mongolia (UMIMM)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to offer relevant evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 3. The UMIMM project includes a dedicated information campaign ('Should I stay or Should I go, research it') aimed at providing prospective migrants with information before migrating, and the delivery of information services to migrants through NGOs is explicitly discussed under Outcome 3. The excerpts describe information dissemination as one of the main contributions to reducing migrant vulnerability, touching on Actions 3c, 3d, and 3e. The effectiveness section discusses the information campaign's reach and the types of information services delivered (144 services including 'Information and support' category). Recommendations also address improving information delivery capacity of NGOs and local government. A synthesis specialist would want to review the targeted interventions (R3) section under Effectiveness and the recommendations on NGO capacity building, while noting that information provision is one of several themes rather than the sole focus.

References.Title: Mid-Term Review of the project Understanding and Managing Internal Migration in Mongolia (UMIMM)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to offer substantial relevant evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. Reducing migrant vulnerability is explicitly one of the three core outcomes of the UMIMM project (Outcome 3), and the excerpts discuss at length the vulnerability of internal migrants, the services delivered to address those vulnerabilities (psycho-social support, legal documentation, job support, education), and the COVID-19 response. The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of NGO-delivered services in reducing vulnerability for over 10,000 migrants, touching on Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for vulnerable migrants), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies), and 7k (involving local authorities in identification and assistance). The sections on targeted interventions under Effectiveness, the discussion of NGO service delivery, and recommendations 3-7 would be particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist to review. The gender-responsive approach and psycho-social support dimensions are also noted throughout.

References.Title: Mid-Term Review of the project Understanding and Managing Internal Migration in Mongolia (UMIMM)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to offer relevant evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. Improving access to services for internal migrants is a central theme of the UMIMM project, explicitly addressed under Outcome 3. The excerpts describe the delivery of 144 types of services to over 10,000 migrants through NGOs, covering health/psycho-social support, education, job support, documentation, and government services — directly relating to Actions 15c (holistic accessible service points at local level), 15e (health needs), and 15f (education access). The evaluation also discusses the role of khoroo personnel and Citizens' Integrated Public Service Centres in service delivery, and the sustainability challenges of NGO-based service provision. The sections on targeted interventions under Effectiveness, the discussion of NGO service delivery outcomes, and recommendations 3-7 would be particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist. The analysis of what types of services were most needed and how they were delivered through civil society-government partnerships appears substantive.

References.Title: Final evaluation of the project: Sustaining peace and social cohesion in Bosnia and Herzegovina through enhanced inter-municipal and inter-entity cooperation on local services (SPSC)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16, though with an important caveat: the social cohesion work is in a post-conflict inter-ethnic context rather than a migrant integration context. The project's core focus on fostering inclusive and cohesive societies, reducing prejudice between groups, empowering youth as active community members, and building institutional trust maps closely onto the themes of GCM Objective 16. Actions 16a (promoting mutual respect, integration policies, social cohesion), 16f (community centres facilitating intercultural dialogue), 16g (civic integration and peer-to-peer exchanges), and 16h (multicultural activities fostering mutual understanding) appear particularly relevant. The Sobičak educational hubs, youth-led media, inter-ethnic dialogue platforms, and community engagement activities described throughout the report closely parallel the kinds of inclusion and cohesion programming GCM Objective 16 envisions. The evaluation's analysis of what works in building social cohesion — safe spaces, repeated contact, linking dialogue to tangible outcomes, youth empowerment — would likely offer transferable lessons for migrant integration contexts. For a synthesis specialist, the effectiveness section on inter-community contact and trust-building, the lessons learned section on safe spaces and youth empowerment, and the conclusions on social cohesion outcomes would appear most valuable as starting points. The non-migration framing should be noted, but the evidence on cohesion-building mechanisms appears substantive and potentially transferable.

References.Title: Final evaluation of the project: Sustaining peace and social cohesion in Bosnia and Herzegovina through enhanced inter-municipal and inter-entity cooperation on local services (SPSC)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute some evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 17, particularly around countering divisive narratives, promoting evidence-based public discourse, and reducing prejudice between groups. The project explicitly addresses divisive narratives as a structural conflict driver, and youth-led media production aimed at shifting public discourse is a notable component. The perception survey data showing measurable reductions in prejudice between ethnic groups provides empirical evidence on attitude change — relevant to Action 17f (awareness-raising campaigns) and 17g (engaging community leaders to detect and prevent intolerance). The report's analysis of how structured dialogue and media engagement can shift public perceptions of 'the other' offers potentially transferable insights for migration contexts where xenophobia and discrimination are concerns. However, the context is inter-ethnic relations in a post-conflict society, not discrimination against migrants specifically, which limits direct applicability. The effectiveness section on youth-led media and public discourse, the PBF catalytic effects section, and the lessons learned on inclusive design would be the most relevant sections for a synthesis specialist to review, with the caveat that the non-migration framing requires careful interpretation.

References.Title: STRENGTHENING LABOUR MIGRATION MANAGEMENT IN MADAGASCAR (PROJECT NUMBER LM. 0283) - REPORT ON AN INTERNAL EX-POST PROJECT EVALUATION
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 5, particularly regarding bilateral labour mobility agreements and formal pathways for regular migration. The evaluation appears to explicitly examine efforts to negotiate Bilateral Labour Agreements (BLAs) with Saudi Arabia, Lebanon, and Mauritius as a core project outcome, and assesses the pace and progress of these negotiations. The project also produced guidelines for negotiating BLAs tailored to the Madagascar context, and the evaluation discusses the government's efforts to send workers to Canada through formal channels. These themes align closely with Actions 5a (bilateral labour mobility agreements) and 5b (facilitating regional labour mobility through cooperation arrangements). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of what enabled or constrained progress toward formal labour mobility pathways, including capacity gaps, inter-ministerial coordination challenges, and the role of the Blueprint as a strategic framework. Synthesis specialists working on GCM Objective 5 would likely want to review Matrix 3.1 (Outcome 2 and Output 2.1/2.2 sections) and the recommendations on BLA negotiation support, as these appear to contain the most directly relevant evidence. The full report may contain additional detail on BLA negotiation processes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION REPORT STRENGTHENING THE INSTITUTIONAL CAPACITY OF CRIMINAL JUSTICE ACTORS IN THE CARIBBEAN TO COUNTER TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS USING A VICTIM CENTERED APPROACH PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Trafficking in persons is the explicit and sole subject of the evaluated project, appearing in the project title, all evaluation questions, and throughout findings, conclusions, and recommendations. The report appears to directly address multiple associated actions: Action 10c (monitoring routes and strengthening cooperation on investigation and prosecution), Action 10e (measures addressing vulnerabilities of trafficking victims, access to justice), Action 10g (strengthening legislation and prosecution, avoiding criminalization of victims), Action 10h (protection and assistance to trafficking victims), and Action 10i (training programmes for law enforcement and criminal justice actors to identify trafficking). The evaluation covers four Caribbean countries over three years, assessing the effectiveness of capacity-building for criminal justice actors using a victim-centered approach — directly relevant to the GCM's emphasis on identification, protection, prosecution, and regional cooperation. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on effectiveness of training and capacity-building (law enforcement and criminal justice actors), the development and use of SOPs/guidelines, regional coordination outcomes, the victim-centered approach methodology, and the sustainability findings would appear particularly valuable. The conclusions on what worked (TOT strategy, needs assessment, regional task force meetings) and what gaps remain (conviction rates, baseline data) would likely contribute substantively to evidence synthesis on counter-TIP governance and victim protection.

References.Title: External Mid-Term Evaluation of the Western Hemisphere Program
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The WHP program explicitly focuses on migration management capacity building, with Pillar 1 outputs addressing national legislation, coordination mechanisms, and tools for migration management. The evaluation assesses cross-border cooperation initiatives, noting that 'activities recognized as most relevant were those along borders with high migrant passage, paired with strong collaboration among countries involved.' Country-level action points reference border management activities in Panama (Darién region), cross-border initiatives, and interinstitutional networking at border points. The program's whole-of-government approach to migration policy (output 1.2.b, 1.2.c) aligns with Actions 11a and 11b. The coherence section highlights positive coordination among countries for cross-border initiatives. These elements appear to relate to Actions 11a (border management cooperation, capacity building), 11b (integrated border management structures), and 11g (cross-border collaboration). Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the Pillar 1 findings sections, the coherence assessment on cross-border coordination, and the Panama and Honduras country action points as starting points for deeper review.

References.Title: Strengthening Coordination Mechanisms to Respond to Trafficking in Person and Ensure Justice and Protection for all Victims of Trafficking in Eswatini
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would almost certainly be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The entire project is explicitly dedicated to preventing trafficking in persons and protecting victims of trafficking in Eswatini — this is the primary and central subject of both the project and the evaluation. The project title, objectives, outcomes, outputs, and all evaluation questions directly address anti-trafficking themes. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across virtually all associated actions of GCM Objective 10: ratification and implementation of the Protocol to Prevent, Suppress and Punish Trafficking in Persons (10a — the project aligns with this instrument); implementation of the Global Plan of Action to Combat Trafficking in Persons (10b); strengthening cooperation on prevention, investigation, and prosecution (10c, 10d); measures addressing vulnerabilities of victims (10e); ensuring correct definitions in legislation (10f); strengthening legislation and prosecution (10g); providing protection and assistance to victims including shelter (10h); national training programmes for officials and law enforcement (10i); and awareness-raising campaigns (10j). For a synthesis specialist, the entire report appears highly relevant, with particular value in: the executive summary's assessment of coordination mechanisms and victim protection outcomes; the conclusions on effectiveness (C4, C5), impact (C10), and sustainability (C11, C13, C14); the recommendations on legislative reform (R3), partner engagement (R4), and border management (R6); and the lessons identified on IOM's role as seed funder for anti-trafficking work. The evaluation's assessment of what worked and what did not in strengthening a national anti-trafficking coordination framework in a Southern African context would likely provide valuable evidence for synthesis on this objective.

References.Title: Strengthening Coordination Mechanisms to Respond to Trafficking in Person and Ensure Justice and Protection for all Victims of Trafficking in Eswatini
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12, particularly regarding screening, assessment, and referral mechanisms for victims of trafficking. The project's Output 1.2 explicitly involves developing a Standard Operating Procedure (SoP) on identification, referral and protection of VoTs, and Output 1.3 involves training stakeholders on these guidelines — directly relevant to GCM actions 12b (specialized trainings for first responders on identification and referral of victims of trafficking) and 12c (gender-responsive and child-sensitive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures). The review and updating of the National Referral Mechanism (NRM) is a central project output. The evaluation appears to assess the quality and effectiveness of these referral procedures in some depth. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on Output 1.2 and 1.3 activities, the conclusions on effectiveness (C4, C5) regarding the NRM and SoP development, and the specific conclusion C19 on the limitations of the NRM review process would appear most relevant as starting points for deeper review. The report likely contains more detail on the referral mechanism development process than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN EX-POST DEL PROYECTO “APOYAR AL MINISTERIO DE TRABAJO Y LA ASAMBLEA NACIONAL EN LA FORMULACIÓN DE POLÍTICAS Y NORMAS RELATIVAS A LA MIGRACIÓN LABORAL Y LA MOVILIDAD HUMANA EN ECUADOR”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 6. The project appears to have substantively addressed labor migration standards and working conditions for migrants in Ecuador. Key outputs include: 'Sector público y privado tienen mayor conocimiento sobre estándares laborales, de reclutamiento e inclusión productiva de migrantes' (directly relevant to actions 6c, 6d, 6f on recruitment standards and labor rights) and the Manual para Inspectores de Trabajo, which appears to be a central product of the project and relates directly to action 6f (strengthening labor inspectors' abilities to monitor recruiters and employers). The project also addressed gender-responsive labor migration policies (action 6k) and engaged both public and private sector actors. The conclusions note that the Manual para Inspectores del Trabajo 'sigue vigente y es de uso permanente,' suggesting sustained impact.
