Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the sections on the Manual's development and use, the capacity-building activities for labor inspectors, and the findings on private sector engagement. The excerpts also indicate the project addressed trata de personas (trafficking) risks within the labor migration context, touching on action 6e. Note that the full report likely contains more detail on these outputs than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Evaluación final del Proyecto de fortalecimiento de la protección internacional, recepción e integración de refugiados en Argentina
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The COMRs (Centros de Orientación a Migrantes y Refugiados) appear to be a central subject of the evaluation, and they function as holistic, locally accessible service points for migrants and refugees — directly relevant to Action 15c (establishing holistic and easily accessible service points at local level). The report discusses access to health services, language training, legal orientation, psychosocial support, and economic inclusion programs provided through these centers. The evaluation's conclusions on COMR consolidation, the intercultural approach, and the extension of services to multiple nationalities would likely be valuable for a synthesis on migrant-inclusive service delivery. The sections on COMR results under the efficacy analysis, the recommendations on continuing COMR work (Recommendation IV), and the discussion of health support during COVID-19 appear particularly worth reviewing. The report also touches on Action 15e (health needs of migrants) and 15f (education and language training). The primary limitation is that service access is analyzed as part of a broader integration project rather than as a standalone service delivery evaluation.

References.Title: Evaluación final del Proyecto de fortalecimiento de la protección internacional, recepción e integración de refugiados en Argentina
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. The evaluated project is explicitly focused on the reception and integration of refugees and migrants in Argentina, making social inclusion and cohesion a core subject. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly relevant to this objective: intercultural dialogue and community sponsorship (Action 16a, 16f), language training and orientation programs (Action 16b), labor market integration initiatives including the Potenciar program and care economy courses (Action 16d), empowerment of migrant women (Action 16e), and the COMR model as a community-level integration facility (Action 16f). The evaluation's conclusions on efficacy, the Red de Patrocinio Comunitario, intercultural approaches, and the recommendations on labor insertion and gender mainstreaming all appear directly relevant. For a synthesis specialist, the efficacy analysis sections on COMR consolidation, the patrocinio comunitario network, language and intercultural programs, and the transversal criteria section on gender and human rights would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The report likely provides evidence on what works in community-based integration support, including under a government-led resettlement program — a relatively underexplored model in the literature.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE EMPLOYMENT THROUGH THE BEAUTIFUL KOSOVO PROGRAMME (EU-BK II)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly regarding Actions 2b (investing in programmes that address poverty, employment creation, and infrastructure) and 2e (investing in human capital development and employment creation to reduce irregular migration). The EU-BK II programme was explicitly designed to reduce poverty and unemployment — structural drivers of irregular migration — through infrastructure development and short-term employment creation. The report notes the 'important link between development, unemployment and irregular migration' as a core rationale for the evaluation. The executive summary and key findings sections discuss outcomes related to employment, poverty reduction, and community development in Kosovo. The recommendation to measure 'how the programme reduces risks of irregular migration by employment' further connects to this objective. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on programme outcomes, the employment component analysis, and the sustainability discussion, as these appear most directly relevant to understanding how development-oriented employment programmes address structural migration drivers. It should be noted that border management, smuggling, and climate-related migration — other aspects of Objective 2 — do not appear in the excerpts.

References.Title: MID-TERM EVALUATION OF OPEN CENTRE FOR MIGRANTS REGISTERED FOR ASSISTED VOLUNTARY RETURN AND REINTEGRATION (OCAVRR) PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The evaluation appears to examine a project explicitly designed to address migrant vulnerabilities, with vulnerability as a central organizing concept throughout. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: identification and assistance of vulnerable groups (pregnant women, single-parent families, unaccompanied minors, migrants with medical needs, elderly, homeless migrants); gender-responsive and child-sensitive service provision; psychological and medical support; and referral mechanisms. These relate closely to Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in situations of vulnerability), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies including health care and psychological services), 7e (account for migrant children in protection systems), and 7f (protect unaccompanied and separated children). The sections on 'Gender Mainstreaming and Assistance to Vulnerable People,' 'Reaching Beneficiaries and Target Groups,' and the discussion of SOPs for vulnerable migrant accommodation would appear particularly valuable for human reviewers to prioritize. The report also touches on 7k (involving local authorities and stakeholders in identification and referral). However, vulnerability is addressed primarily through the lens of shelter provision and AVRR rather than as a standalone analytical theme, which limits its depth relative to a FUNDAMENTAL score.

References.Title: MID-TERM EVALUATION OF OPEN CENTRE FOR MIGRANTS REGISTERED FOR ASSISTED VOLUNTARY RETURN AND REINTEGRATION (OCAVRR) PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The evaluation explicitly examines a project whose primary purpose is to facilitate assisted voluntary return and reintegration (AVRR) — this is the central subject of the entire evaluation. The excerpts suggest that multiple findings and conclusions directly address: safe and dignified return processes (Actions 21b, 21e); gender-responsive and child-sensitive return programmes with free, prior and informed consent (Action 21b); reintegration support for vulnerable returnees (Action 21h); cooperation frameworks between IOM, the Greek government, and countries of origin (Action 21a); individual assessments and SOPs for return procedures (Action 21e); and sustainability of return infrastructure (Actions 21f, 21i). The report appears to provide detailed operational evidence on how a voluntary return centre functions, including beneficiary profiles, service delivery, coordination mechanisms, and outcomes for 1,223 beneficiaries across multiple nationalities. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on 'Results and Outcomes Achievement,' 'Planned Changes and Main Outcomes,' 'Sustainability of the Project,' and the executive summary would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The evaluation also addresses reintegration (though implementation was incomplete at time of review), community conditions upon return, and the role of SOPs in standardizing return procedures. It is possible the full report contains additional evidence on reintegration outcomes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION REPORT - IRAQ COMMUNITY REVITALIZATION PROJECT - EVALUATION OF PHASES II, III, IV
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 18. Skills development is a central component of CRP, with multiple training modalities evaluated: vocational training, on-the-job training, small business development training, and farmer training. The evaluation provides substantive outcome data on training effectiveness — including goal achievement rates across phases (e.g., 62% of SBD participants achieved their goal, 51% of OJT participants), beneficiary assessments of training quality and importance, and analysis of what worked and what did not. This relates directly to actions 18e (building global skills partnerships and fostering skills development), 18g (bilateral programmes promoting skills development and mobility), 18h (remote/online skills development and on-the-job training), and 18j (innovative ways to recognize and assess skills including internship programmes). The evaluation also discusses the limitations of in-kind grants and toolkits as complements to training, which adds nuance to understanding what makes skills development effective. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on Individual Assistance outcomes (vocational training, OJT, SBD), the analysis of training quality and relevance, and the recommendations on redesigning training components would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The report does not address mutual recognition of qualifications across borders, which limits its relevance to some associated actions.

References.Title: MIDTERM EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “FOSTERING HEALTH AND PROTECTION TO VULNERABLE MIGRANTS TRANSITING THROUGH MOROCCO, TUNISIA, EGYPT, LIBYA AND YEMEN” (IOM)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be highly valuable for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project's explicit purpose is to foster health and protection for vulnerable migrants transiting through five countries, and the evaluation assesses how well this is achieved. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: identification and assistance to migrants in vulnerable situations (including VoTs, SGBV survivors, unaccompanied minors, and migrants in detention); gender-responsive and child-sensitive approaches (UASC support through AAMM, CMR training for GBV survivors); access to health care and psychosocial support for migrants regardless of status; and mobile response teams providing life-saving assistance in Yemen and Libya. Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerability), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies including health care and psychosocial services), 7e and 7f (child protection and unaccompanied children), and 7k (mobile response teams and local stakeholder involvement) appear particularly well-covered. The effectiveness, impact, and sustainability sections of the report would appear most relevant for human reviewers to prioritize, along with the recommendations on widening safety nets and engaging CSOs. The full report likely contains additional depth beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: MIDTERM EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “FOSTERING HEALTH AND PROTECTION TO VULNERABLE MIGRANTS TRANSITING THROUGH MOROCCO, TUNISIA, EGYPT, LIBYA AND YEMEN” (IOM)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The project's core purpose is to improve access to health and basic services for vulnerable migrants, and the evaluation directly assesses this. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: facilitating migrants' access to public health care services in Morocco, Tunisia, Egypt, Libya, and Yemen; operationalizing a ministerial decree granting access to public health institutions to vulnerable migrants in Tunisia (directly relevant to Action 15e); capacity building of health care providers on migrant-inclusive service delivery; direct provision of medical, humanitarian, and psychosocial assistance; and challenges of financial barriers, language barriers, and discrimination in accessing services. Actions 15c (holistic accessible service points), 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in national health policies and plans), and 15a (non-discriminatory service delivery) appear particularly well-covered. The effectiveness, impact, and sustainability sections — especially the Tunisia ministerial decree operationalization and Morocco government collaboration — would appear most valuable for human reviewers. The report likely offers rich evidence on both direct service delivery and health system strengthening approaches for migrant populations.

References.Title: Priority Implementation Actions of the AU-ILO-IOM-ECA Joint Programme on Labour Migration Governance for Development and Integration in Africa: JLMP-Priority Project
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The JLMP Priority project explicitly includes improving labour migration statistics as a core component — the project released the '2nd Report on Labour migration statistics in Africa' and collaborated with AU StatAfric and Statistics Sweden on migration statistics. The excerpts indicate that capacity-building workshops for statistical officers were held in 2019, and the project worked on collecting, utilizing and disseminating labour migration statistics including administrative data. These activities appear to relate directly to Actions 1a (comprehensive strategy for improving migration data), 1c (building national capacities in data collection), 1f (regional centres/observatories), and 1i (collaboration between migration data units and national statistical offices). The sections on Effectiveness and the project design description appear most relevant for human reviewers to prioritize. The report likely offers evidence on regional approaches to harmonizing migration statistics in Africa, which would be valuable for a synthesis on this objective. It is possible the full report contains more detailed analysis of statistical capacity-building outcomes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Priority Implementation Actions of the AU-ILO-IOM-ECA Joint Programme on Labour Migration Governance for Development and Integration in Africa: JLMP-Priority Project
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 23, though with important caveats. Unlike most IOM programme evaluations where international cooperation language is merely operational context, this report evaluates a multi-stakeholder continental governance initiative — the JLMP — whose primary object of inquiry is precisely the architecture of international cooperation for labour migration governance in Africa. The evaluation examines how the AUC, ILO, IOM, UNECA, RECs, and Member States collectively organize themselves to implement shared labour migration governance frameworks, which is substantively relevant to Actions 23a (supporting collective GCM implementation), 23d (capacity-building mechanisms), and 23e (bilateral/regional/multilateral partnerships). The Steering Committee governance analysis, the LMAC coordination mechanism, the coherence and complementarity sections, and the sustainability analysis of funding mechanisms all appear to offer evidence on how multilateral migration governance partnerships function in practice. The recommendations on business model development and exit strategy also address governance architecture questions. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the coherence/complementarity sections, the sustainability analysis, and the lessons learned on participatory design and self-regulating communities of interest — framing these as pointers for further review rather than definitive characterizations. The score is kept below 0.84 because the report's primary focus is on a specific labour migration programme rather than on global governance mechanisms per se, and the GCM is not explicitly the evaluative framework used.

References.Title: EVALUATION DU PROJET “PROMOUVOIR L’ENGAGEMENT DE LA DIASPORA TCHADIENNE AFIN DE SOUTENIR LE DEVELOPPEMENT DU TCHAD”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The project's very title — 'Promouvoir l'Engagement de la Diaspora Tchadienne afin de Soutenir le Développement du Tchad' — directly mirrors the language and intent of GCM Objective 19. The evaluation appears to assess a dedicated diaspora engagement initiative covering: (1) mobilization of diaspora competences in health and higher education (relating to Actions 19c and 19j on knowledge/skills transfer); (2) establishment of government structures and focal points for diaspora engagement, including an interministerial committee and steering committee (Action 19d on dedicated diaspora offices and focal points); (3) dialogue between the diaspora and the Chadian government (Action 19g on political participation and engagement); and (4) recommendations for making diaspora a cross-cutting theme in OIM programming (Action 19b on integrating migration into development planning). The conclusion explicitly addresses the effectiveness of diaspora-government dialogue, the creation of diaspora directories (mapping diaspora skills per Action 19j), and the potential for extending diaspora contributions to entrepreneurship and agriculture. Recommendations R1-R4 all directly address mechanisms for sustaining diaspora engagement for development. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on evaluation results (section 2.2), lessons learned (section 2.3), and prioritized recommendations (section 2.3.1) would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The full report likely contains additional evidence on diaspora engagement mechanisms beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Mid-term internal independent evaluation of the project “Counter-Trafficking in Persons Activity, C-TIP”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Counter-trafficking in persons (C-TIP) is explicitly the primary subject of the evaluated project, appearing in the project title, all three outcome areas, and throughout the evaluation questions, findings, and recommendations. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly aligned with GCM Objective 10's associated actions: legislative and legal framework development (anti-TiP law, NRM, SoPs — Actions 10a, 10f, 10g); investigation and prosecution capacity building for law enforcement and judiciary (Actions 10c, 10g); victim identification, protection, and assistance including shelter and reintegration services (Actions 10e, 10h); awareness-raising and hotline support (Action 10i, 10j); and institutional capacity of the national anti-trafficking agency APLTP (Actions 10b, 10d). The evaluation covers effectiveness, sustainability, and gender dimensions of these interventions in DRC — a country of origin, transit, and destination for trafficking. For a synthesis specialist, the conclusions on effectiveness (Outcome 1 on prosecution, Outcome 3 on victim protection), the sustainability analysis, the gender and human rights sections, and the lessons learned on NRM/SoPs development would appear particularly valuable starting points. The full report likely contains additional detailed findings beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: COUNTER TRAFFICKING PREVENTION PROJECTS REVIEW IDF-FUNDED PROJECTS 2008-2013
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The report explicitly evaluates IDF-funded counter-trafficking prevention projects, making trafficking in persons the central subject of inquiry. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis directly relevant to several associated actions: Action 10i (national training programmes to educate officials and law enforcement on identifying trafficking) is addressed through the Training result category, which is the most frequent result type (32% of results); Action 10j (awareness-raising campaigns on trafficking risks) is addressed through the Awareness Campaign result category and the 10 Awareness Raising projects reviewed; Action 10c (monitoring irregular migration routes exploited by trafficking networks) and Action 10e (addressing vulnerabilities of potential victims) are touched upon through the Policy Development project type. The report appears to provide evidence on what works in trafficking prevention programming — including sustainability of training, policy framework development, and awareness campaigns — which would be directly valuable for a synthesis on Objective 10. Particularly relevant sections for human reviewers would include: the result categories analysis (Training, Awareness Campaign, Development of Policy Framework), the sustainability findings (100% maintenance of Policy Framework results vs. 25% for Guidance Manuals), and the recommendations on stakeholder involvement and exit strategies. The report's focus on prevention (as distinct from protection/victim assistance) aligns well with the prevention-oriented actions under Objective 10.

References.Title: “STRENGTHENING THE IDENTIFICATION AND PROTECTIONOF VICTIMS OF TRAFFICKING AMONG REFUGEES IN RWANDA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project explicitly targets a highly vulnerable population — Congolese and Burundian refugees in Rwanda — who face particular risks of trafficking in persons. The excerpts suggest substantive engagement with vulnerability identification, referral mechanisms, and protection responses. The project's focus on identifying and protecting victims of trafficking among refugees directly relates to Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in situations of vulnerability, including victims of trafficking), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies addressing needs of migrant women and children), and 7k (involving local authorities in identification, referral and assistance of vulnerable migrants). The evaluation's discussion of Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs), referral mechanisms, and training of camp leadership and community members on TIP identification appears particularly relevant. The integration of gender equality in project design and implementation (Core Finding 2.3) also aligns with Action 7c. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the effectiveness sections on referral mechanisms and the conclusions on awareness-raising outcomes, as well as the sustainability discussion on legal and policy frameworks. The excerpts suggest this is one of several relevant themes, so the score reflects meaningful but not primary alignment.

References.Title: “STRENGTHENING THE IDENTIFICATION AND PROTECTIONOF VICTIMS OF TRAFFICKING AMONG REFUGEES IN RWANDA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The project title itself — 'Strengthening the Identification and Protection of Victims of Trafficking among Refugees in Rwanda' — directly mirrors the language and intent of this GCM objective. The report appears to provide core evidence on multiple associated actions: Action 10e (measures addressing vulnerabilities of those at risk of trafficking, facilitating access to justice and protection), Action 10g (strengthening legislation and procedures to enhance prosecution and avoid criminalization of victims), Action 10h (providing protection and assistance to victims), Action 10i (national information systems and training programmes to identify signs of trafficking), and Action 10j (awareness-raising campaigns on risks of trafficking). The evaluation covers Rwanda's legal and policy framework on TIP (Laws 51/2018, GBV law, child protection law), the multi-agency coordination structure (NSC, TC involving RNP, RIB, DGIE, NPPA, NCC), community sensitization in six refugee camps, training sessions on TIP referrals, and IEC materials development. The conclusions and recommendations sections directly address prosecution preconditions, referral mechanisms, and sustainability of counter-trafficking frameworks. For synthesis specialists, the effectiveness analysis (Core Findings 2.1-2.3), the sustainability discussion (Core Finding 6), and the recommendations on coordination structures and IEC materials distribution would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional detailed evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: ZIMBABWE MIGRATION DEVELOPMENT STRATEGIES PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The Zimbabwe Migration and Development Strategies (ZMDS) project's second specific objective was explicitly dedicated to 'supporting the formulation and application of a Zimbabwe Migration Profile, including research on selected indicators' — a direct parallel to GCM Objective 1's core theme. The excerpts suggest substantial analysis of data collection, dissemination, and use for evidence-based policymaking. Specific outputs described include: the Zimbabwe Migration Profile 2009, the Zimbabwe Health Workers' Survey, the Remittances Study, capacity building of ZIMSTAT for incorporating migration questions into the PICES survey, GIS training for census preparation, and a stakeholders' workshop on diaspora enumeration methodologies. These map closely to GCM Actions 1c (building national capacities in data collection), 1g (integrating migration topics in national censuses), 1h (household surveys), 1j (country-specific migration profiles), and 1d (data on effects and contributions of migrants). The excerpts also describe how migration statistics were incorporated into national planning instruments like the Medium Term Plan and monetary policies, illustrating the evidence-based policy link. The evaluation's findings on relevance, effectiveness, and sustainability sections all engage substantively with this theme. Human reviewers would particularly benefit from examining Chapter 3 (project outputs), Chapter 4 (discussion of findings on Objective 2), and the recommendations section on migration profile and research, as these appear most directly relevant to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1.

References.Title: ZIMBABWE MIGRATION DEVELOPMENT STRATEGIES PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The entire ZMDS project was explicitly designed to 'enhance the development impact of migration in Zimbabwe' and to harness diaspora contributions to national development — the core theme of GCM Objective 19. The excerpts suggest deep, multi-faceted engagement with this theme across all three project objectives. Specific elements mapping to GCM Objective 19's associated actions include: establishment of a Migration and Development Unit as a dedicated diaspora/migration focal point (Action 19d), diaspora engagement workshops in South Africa and Botswana (Action 19j), mainstreaming migration into national development frameworks like the Medium Term Plan (Action 19b), research on remittances and diaspora contributions to GDP (Action 19c), short-term return of health professionals as a diaspora contribution mechanism (Action 19i), and the development of a national migration and development strategic matrix. The evaluation's findings on relevance, effectiveness, sustainability, and impact all engage substantively with diaspora-development linkages. The excerpts also discuss the politicization of diaspora engagement as a threat, confidence-building between government and diaspora, and lessons on depoliticizing diaspora mobilization. Human reviewers would particularly benefit from examining Chapter 4 (discussion of findings across all objectives), Chapter 5 (SWOT analysis and lessons), Chapter 6 (conclusions and recommendations), and the executive summary sections on impact and sustainability, as these appear most directly relevant to a synthesis on GCM Objective 19.

References.Title: ZIMBABWE MIGRATION DEVELOPMENT STRATEGIES PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 20. The project explicitly supported research on remittances — 'The Flow, Impact and Regulatory Framework of Migrant Labor Remittances in Zimbabwe' — and the excerpts indicate this research had policy impact, with the 2009 monetary policy quoting the 7.2% contribution of Zimbabweans abroad to GDP for the first time. The excerpts also describe capacity building of ZIMSTAT to incorporate remittances questions in the PICES survey, and note that 'statistics on remittances and issues of diaspora engagement are key issues of national economic blueprints.' This maps to GCM Actions 20f (accessible information on remittance transfers) and the broader evidence-base for remittance policy. However, the excerpts do not appear to analyze remittance transfer costs, regulatory frameworks for remittance service providers, or financial inclusion mechanisms in depth — the more specific concerns of GCM Objective 20's associated actions. The remittances work appears to be primarily research/data-oriented rather than focused on transfer mechanisms or financial inclusion. Human reviewers would particularly want to examine the sections discussing the Remittances Study outputs and their policy implications, as well as the recommendations on further research, to assess whether the full report contains more substantive analysis relevant to GCM Objective 20.

References.Title: SAFEGUARDING THE WORKERS ON THE MOVE IN THE MENA REGION
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 6. Ethical recruitment is explicitly the central theme of the evaluated project — appearing in the project title, evaluation questions, and throughout all sections reviewed. The excerpts suggest that multiple findings directly address recruitment agency regulation, worker protection mechanisms, private sector engagement, and government capacity to promote ethical recruitment — all core themes under actions 6c, 6d, 6e, 6f, 6i, and 6l. The evaluation covers IOM tools such as IRIS (International Recruitment Integrity System) and CREST (Corporate Responsibility in Eliminating Slavery and Trafficking), both directly referenced in action 6l. The report appears to assess capacity building of labor inspectors, trade unions, private sector actors, and government officials on ethical recruitment standards across three MENA countries. For a synthesis specialist, the effectiveness section analyzing stakeholder engagement with ethical recruitment, the sustainability section on institutional embedding, and the recommendations on private sector and trade union roles would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The analysis of what worked and what did not in introducing ethical recruitment as a new topic to governments and private sector in Egypt, Jordan, and Morocco would likely contribute essential evidence on actions 6c, 6d, 6e, and 6l. It is possible that the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: SAFEGUARDING THE WORKERS ON THE MOVE IN THE MENA REGION
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project's stated objective is contributing to the protection of workers on the move, and the evaluation explicitly addresses exploitation of migrant workers in supply chains, hazardous working conditions, non-payment of wages, and passport confiscation — all forms of vulnerability addressed under actions 7b and 7d. The COVID-19 response component involved direct humanitarian assistance to migrant workers, touching on action 7j (support for migrants caught in crisis situations). The evaluation also discusses the particular vulnerability of migrant workers in the informal sector (action 7d) and the gender dimensions of vulnerability (action 7c), though the latter is noted as insufficiently addressed. However, vulnerability is addressed primarily as context for the ethical recruitment focus rather than as a standalone analytical theme. The sections on gender and human rights, impact, and the discussion of migrant workers in the informal sector would appear most relevant for human reviewers interested in GCM Objective 7, framed as pointers for further review rather than definitive characterizations.

References.Title: Synthesis Evaluation Extracting Learning from 2022 IOM Ex-Post Evaluations
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The synthesis evaluation appears to cover multiple Migration Profile projects (notably PR.0230 - Fiji and PO.0105 - China), which are explicitly designed to develop country-specific migration data products — directly relating to Action 1j (country-specific migration profiles with disaggregated data). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of data collection, disaggregation, and use for evidence-based policymaking. The recommendations section includes specific guidance on migration profile projects, including on gender-disaggregated data, accessibility of data products, and data sharing — all touching on Actions 1d, 1g, and 1j. The cross-cutting section on gender mainstreaming also discusses data disaggregation in results matrices. Additionally, the report notes weaknesses in monitoring and evaluation systems and indicators not being sufficiently SMART, which relates to Action 1a (harmonizing methodologies). For a synthesis specialist, the sections on migration profile projects (recommendations and effectiveness findings), the discussion of data disaggregation in gender mainstreaming, and the validity of project design section would appear particularly valuable as starting points. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal, particularly within the individual project evaluations reviewed.

References.Title: Synthesis Evaluation Extracting Learning from 2022 IOM Ex-Post Evaluations
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. Diaspora engagement appears to be a significant theme, with multiple projects explicitly focused on diaspora contributions to development (CD.0002 - Mauritius, CD.0006 - Djibouti, CD.0015 - Guinea). The report includes a dedicated recommendations subsection on diaspora-focused projects, addressing diaspora outreach approaches, mapping of diaspora professionals, feedback loops, and engagement platforms — relating to Actions 19d (government structures for diaspora engagement), 19j (mapping diasporas and skills transfer), and 19f (information and guidance for diaspora engagement). The coherence section also discusses diaspora-related projects in detail. The lessons and best practices section includes specific guidance on diaspora engagement approaches. However, diaspora contributions appear as one of several themes covered alongside border management, migration profiles, and healthcare. For a synthesis specialist, the diaspora-focused recommendations section, the coherence findings for CD.0006 and CD.0015, and the lessons on diaspora outreach would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review.

References.Title: Final Evaluation of the project "SUPPORTING THE IMPLEMENTATION OF AN E RESIDENCE PLATFORM IN CABO VERDE"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project explicitly addresses the creation of a digital residence permit system that generates reliable, disaggregated data on foreign residents — a core concern of this objective. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of data gaps (the discrepancy between census figures and DEF-registered residents), the limitations of the old paper-based system in producing disaggregated data, and how the new e-residence platform addresses these gaps. Action 1i (using administrative records such as residence permits for statistical purposes) appears particularly well-covered. Action 1d (collecting data on effects and benefits of migration for sustainable development) is also touched upon, notably in the recommendation to conduct a systematic study on the benefits of legal residence. The evaluation's discussion of the lack of reliable data on regularization rates and the recommendation for more rigorous estimation (Recommendation 5) further reinforces relevance. However, data collection and analysis is not the primary focus of the evaluation — it surfaces as a supporting theme rather than the central object of inquiry. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the Effectiveness section (Findings 5, 6, 9) and the recommendations sections for content on data gaps and the role of digital systems in improving migration data quality.

References.Title: Final Evaluation of the project "SUPPORTING THE IMPLEMENTATION OF AN E RESIDENCE PLATFORM IN CABO VERDE"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 4. The entire project is centered on developing and deploying an electronic residence permit system — directly addressing the core concern of this objective: ensuring migrants have proof of legal identity and adequate documentation. The project title, evaluation questions, and virtually all findings relate to this theme. Actions 4a (improving civil registry systems and investing in ICT solutions), 4b (harmonizing travel documents and investing in digitalization and biometric data), and 4d (facilitating access to personal documentation) appear to be directly and substantially addressed. The report provides evidence on the technical development of a biometric e-residence platform, the digitization of legacy archives, the decentralization of issuance across all islands, and the regularization of nearly 2,000 foreign residents. The Effectiveness section (Findings 5–9) and the Sustainability section appear particularly rich for synthesis purposes. The report also touches on the institutional framework (SNIAC mandate, DEF role) underpinning legal identity systems, which is relevant to Action 4a. Synthesis specialists should prioritize the Effectiveness and Sustainability sections, as well as the project description in the Introduction, as starting points for deeper review.

References.Title: RIPFUMELO FINAL EVALUATION REPORT - MID TERM EVALUATION OF RIPFUMELO PROJECT 2012-2016
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The Ripfumelo project explicitly targets migrant farm workers — a population in a situation of vulnerability — and addresses their health needs, HIV/AIDS risks, and access to services. The report discusses SGBV (Sexual and Gender Based Violence) in its acronym list, suggesting coverage of gender-based vulnerabilities. The evaluation examines how change agents and partnerships help reach vulnerable migrant populations, and findings discuss the conditions under which vulnerable migrants can access sensitive health services. Lesson 1 addresses the importance of non-threatening approaches for delivering sensitive information to vulnerable communities. The report also touches on OVCs (Orphan and Vulnerable Children) and LGBTI populations in its acronym list, suggesting broader vulnerability coverage. Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerability), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies addressing particular needs), and 7d (workplace vulnerabilities) appear most related. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the findings on change agent approaches and the sections on sustainability of services for vulnerable migrant populations, as well as any site-specific findings not fully captured in these excerpts.

References.Title: RIPFUMELO FINAL EVALUATION REPORT - MID TERM EVALUATION OF RIPFUMELO PROJECT 2012-2016
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The Ripfumelo project's core focus is on improving access to health services for migrant farm workers — a direct instantiation of Action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in health care policies and plans). The evaluation examines how IOM and implementing partners work to facilitate migrant access to HIV/AIDS services, HCT (HIV Counselling and Testing), ART, and primary health care in Mpumalanga and Limpopo provinces. Multiple findings and conclusions directly address service access barriers and enablers: Finding 3 discusses how farm owners now support health activities and release workers to seek health services; Finding 4 highlights the change agent approach as a mechanism for connecting migrants to services; Lessons 2 and 4 address government buy-in as critical for sustaining service access. The report also discusses the role of municipalities in providing transport and stipends to facilitate service access. Actions 15c (holistic accessible service points at local level) and 15e (health needs of migrants in health care policies) appear most directly addressed. For a synthesis specialist, the findings on change agent approaches, government mainstreaming of migration health, and sustainability of service delivery would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional site-specific evidence on service access beyond what these excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EQUI-HEALTH MID-TERM EVALUATION REPORT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly Action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in national and local health care policies, strengthening capacities, facilitating affordable and non-discriminatory access, reducing communication barriers, and training health care providers on culturally-sensitive service delivery). The Equi-Health project's explicit aim — improving access and quality of health care services, health promotion and prevention for migrants, Roma, and other vulnerable ethnic minority groups — maps directly onto this objective. The evaluation appears to assess a flagship EU-IOM initiative specifically designed to address migrant health access across multiple EU/EEA countries. Key themes visible in the excerpts include: training of health professionals and law enforcement on migrant health needs; Roma health mediator networks; MIPEX health strand comparing migrant health policies across 31 countries; national consultative committee processes facilitating inter-ministerial coordination on migrant health; and sustainability of health service adaptations for migrants. The executive summary, findings sections on all work packages, and the recommendations sections would all appear highly relevant for a synthesis specialist. Particularly valuable would be the discussion of what works in making health services accessible to irregular migrants and Roma, the analysis of training effectiveness for health professionals, and the policy recommendations on institutionalizing migrant-inclusive health service delivery. The report also engages with the question of how far project activities translate into actual improved health outcomes for migrants — a key evaluative question for any synthesis on this objective.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “SUPPORT FOR REGULARIZATION THROUGH THE IMPLEMENTATION OF THE SPECIAL TEMPORARY MIGRATION CATEGORY OF COSTA RICA.”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 5. The entire project evaluated is explicitly about creating and operationalizing a new regular migration pathway — the Special Temporary Category (CET) established by Executive Decree 43809 — for Cuban, Nicaraguan, and Venezuelan nationals whose refugee applications were pending or denied. This directly addresses Action 5d (flexible, rights-based migration schemes) and Action 5g (admission and stay based on humanitarian or other considerations for migrants compelled to leave their countries). The evaluation examines in depth: the design and implementation of this regularization pathway, barriers to access (economic, administrative, legal), institutional capacity to deliver the pathway, and outcomes for beneficiaries. Multiple evaluation criteria (relevance, coherence, effectiveness, impact, sustainability) are applied to assess how well the pathway functioned. The conclusions and recommendations sections contain substantive analysis on pathway design, risk management, and access barriers. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on relevance (barriers to access), effectiveness (institutional capacity, goal achievement), impact (beneficiary outcomes), and lessons learned would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The report likely contributes evidence on how temporary regularization pathways can be designed and implemented, what barriers emerge, and how to improve them — core questions for a synthesis on Objective 5.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “SUPPORT FOR REGULARIZATION THROUGH THE IMPLEMENTATION OF THE SPECIAL TEMPORARY MIGRATION CATEGORY OF COSTA RICA.”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project explicitly targets migrants in vulnerable situations — including women, unaccompanied minors, and persons with disabilities — and the evaluation assesses how well the project addressed their specific needs. Several elements are relevant: the partnership with Fundación Mujer to provide financial support to vulnerable migrants for documentation costs (relating to Action 7b on comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations); the evaluation's assessment of access barriers faced by the target population; the gender approach mainstreamed through IOM standards and donor requirements; and the discussion of how regularization status transitions reduce vulnerability (relating to Action 7h on facilitating status transitions). The evaluation questions explicitly ask about barriers faced by 'nationals of Cuba, Nicaragua and Venezuela, especially women, unaccompanied minors and people with disabilities.' The conclusions note that 'economic, social and emotional effects of people who migrate forcibly mean that interventions must consider a series of elements that allow adaptation to the conditions of migrants.' However, vulnerability is analyzed primarily through the lens of project design adequacy rather than as a standalone theme. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the relevance and coherence sections, the Fundación Mujer partnership discussion, and the recommendations on intercultural and human rights-based communication strategies.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN FINAL DE PROYECTO “ALINEANDO LA PROGRAMACIÓN DE LA OIM A LA IMPLEMENTACIÓN DEL PACTO MUNDIAL SOBRE MIGRACIÓN Y EL APOYO DE LA AGENDA 2030 EN AMÉRICA DEL SUR”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 23, particularly regarding IOM's institutional role in supporting GCM implementation and aligning migration governance with the 2030 Agenda. The project evaluated is explicitly titled around aligning IOM programming with GCM implementation and the 2030 Agenda across South American countries — making the GCM implementation support architecture a primary object of inquiry rather than mere operational context. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how IOM's regional office supported country offices in adapting to UN Development System reform and GCM implementation, which relates to Actions 23a (supporting states in implementing the GCM) and 23d (capacity-building mechanisms). The evaluation's conclusions on capacity-building effectiveness, sustainability of tools, and lessons learned about regional-global alignment of training agendas would likely be of interest to synthesis specialists examining how international organizations operationalize GCM implementation support. Particularly relevant sections appear to be the conclusions on efficacy (Conclusion 5-7), sustainability (Conclusions 12-13), and recommendations 1-4, which address the strategic approach to supporting country-level GCM implementation. However, it should be noted that the evidence is primarily about IOM's internal institutional processes rather than inter-state cooperation mechanisms per se, which somewhat limits its contribution to a synthesis on Objective 23's broader governance themes. Human reviewers should assess whether the full report contains more direct evidence on inter-institutional cooperation mechanisms beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: RAPPORT D’ÉVALUATION FINALE DE PROJET "MECANISME DE REPONSE ET RESSOURCES POUR LES MIGRANTS (PHASE II)" T05-EUTF-SAH-NE-01-01
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The MRRM II project appears centrally concerned with addressing vulnerabilities of migrants in transit through Niger — including direct humanitarian assistance on migration routes and in transit centers, specific provisions for women and unaccompanied minors (dedicated transit centers), and assistance to migrants in distress in the Sahara desert. The executive summary notes 'thousands of lives saved in the Nigerien desert' and highlights gender-responsive approaches. The project's focus on mixed migration flows — which inherently involve vulnerable populations — and the discussion of protection needs along dangerous routes aligns with Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies), and 7f (protection of unaccompanied children). The conclusions section and the gender criterion analysis would appear particularly relevant for human reviewers to prioritize. The full report likely contains more detailed analysis of vulnerability identification and response mechanisms than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: RAPPORT D’ÉVALUATION FINALE DE PROJET "MECANISME DE REPONSE ET RESSOURCES POUR LES MIGRANTS (PHASE II)" T05-EUTF-SAH-NE-01-01
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 8. Search and rescue operations in the Nigerien desert appear to be a core component of the MRRM II project — the executive summary explicitly references 'thousands of lives saved in the Nigerien desert' as a key impact. Recommendation R5 specifically addresses the need for sustainable, sovereign management of 'search, rescue and assistance to migrants in the Nigerien desert,' including through multilateral conventions and a potential sub-regional fund. This aligns closely with Actions 8a (procedures and agreements on search and rescue) and 8b (identifying dangerous transit routes and establishing prevention/response mechanisms). The Sahara desert route through Niger is one of the world's most deadly migration corridors, making this evaluation potentially valuable evidence for synthesis on life-saving operations. The conclusions section on impact and recommendation R5 would appear most relevant for human reviewers to prioritize. The full report likely contains more operational detail on search and rescue activities than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: RAPPORT D’ÉVALUATION FINALE DE PROJET "MECANISME DE REPONSE ET RESSOURCES POUR LES MIGRANTS (PHASE II)" T05-EUTF-SAH-NE-01-01
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Voluntary Assisted Return and Reintegration (AVRR) appears to be a central component of the MRRM II project. The evaluation explicitly examines: (1) facilitation of voluntary return for migrants transiting Niger; (2) socio-economic reintegration support in six West African countries of origin (Cameroon, Senegal, Côte d'Ivoire, Guinea Conakry, Mali, Sierra Leone); (3) cooperation between countries of origin, transit, and destination; and (4) the development of a new reintegration model based on lessons learned. The conclusions note both achievements and shortcomings in reintegration — 'disillusionment among returning migrants and their communities due to the limited success of actions directed at them' — providing nuanced evidence on reintegration effectiveness. This aligns closely with Actions 21b (gender-responsive return and reintegration programmes), 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through access to services and decent work), and 21i (addressing needs of communities to which migrants return). The sections on reintegration outcomes, the impact criterion analysis, and recommendation R5 on multilateral conventions would appear most valuable for human reviewers. The full report likely contains substantial additional detail on reintegration programming and outcomes.

References.Title: SYNTHESIS EVALUATION: EXTRACTING LEARNING FROM EVALUATIONS OF ASSISTED VOLUNTARY RETURN AND REINTEGRATION (AVR(R)/PARA) PROJECTS AND PROGRAMMES
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would almost certainly be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The entire report is explicitly and comprehensively focused on Assisted Voluntary Return and Reintegration (AVRR) — the core subject of GCM Objective 21. The report title, evaluation objectives, findings, conclusions, and recommendations all directly address safe and dignified return and sustainable reintegration, which are the central commitments of this objective. The excerpts suggest that the report provides substantive analysis across virtually all associated actions of Objective 21: Action 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes based on free, prior and informed consent) is addressed throughout, including the extended discussion of voluntariness (section 4.2.3) and the integrated approach to reintegration. Action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through access to social protection, psychosocial assistance, vocational training, employment, and financial services) is a central analytical theme, with multiple sections examining what constitutes sustainable reintegration and how to measure it. Action 21i (addressing needs of communities to which migrants return) is discussed in the context of community-level reintegration and partnerships. Action 21a (bilateral and multilateral cooperation frameworks) is touched upon through discussion of donor-government partnerships and the EU-IOM Joint Initiative. The report also provides a synthesis of 42 evaluation reports spanning 2008-2020, making it a particularly rich source of accumulated evidence. For a synthesis specialist, the sections most worth prioritizing would include: the executive summary's overview of the evolution of AVRR approaches; section 4.1 on cross-cutting findings not linked to evaluation criteria; section 4.2.3 on voluntariness; section 4.2.4 on M&E and communication; section 4.2.5 on monitoring tools for sustainable reintegration; and the overall conclusions (section 5). It is very likely that the full report contains substantially more evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal, given the breadth of the synthesis exercise described.

References.Title: Final Evaluation of the "Promoting Migration Governance in Zimbabwe (PMGZ) Programme
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to contain substantive content relevant to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The evaluation describes support to Zimbabwe National Statistics Agency (ZIMSTAT) to improve data management and the introduction of migration-related variables into intercensus data collection — directly relating to Action 1g (integrating migration topics into national censuses). The project also involved migration profiling and mapping surveys (diaspora profiling under Result 4), and the development of migration governance frameworks grounded in evidence. The excerpts suggest these are not merely peripheral mentions but are tied to specific result areas with dedicated analysis. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on Result 2 (ZIMSTAT support and data management) and Result 4 (diaspora profiling and mapping) would appear most worth reviewing, as they touch on Actions 1c, 1d, 1g, and 1j. The depth of analysis on data systems appears moderate rather than central, so this is scored as RELEVANT rather than FUNDAMENTAL. It is possible the full report contains more detailed treatment of data collection outcomes than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Final Evaluation of the "Promoting Migration Governance in Zimbabwe (PMGZ) Programme
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be highly valuable for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The project's Result 4 is explicitly dedicated to diaspora engagement and remittances mobilization — directly addressing Actions 19d (establishing diaspora offices and mechanisms), 19e (targeted support programmes for diaspora investment), 19f (accessible information and coordinated engagement), 19g (political participation of migrants), 19h (promoting diaspora-friendly policies), and 19j (partnerships with local authorities and diaspora organizations). The excerpts describe the development of a Diaspora Engagement and Remittances Mobilisation Strategy, diaspora profiling and mapping surveys, outreach engagements, a Diaspora Engagement Website, partnerships with six Rural District Councils for community-based projects, and the establishment of a Zimbabwean Diaspora Directorate. The evaluation also explicitly discusses the 'migration as development' concept and the challenges of diaspora engagement (mistrust, political history, heterogeneity of diaspora groups). The recommendations include a three-pronged approach (engaging, enabling, empowering) for diaspora engagement. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on Result 4 outcomes, the conclusions on diaspora engagement challenges, and the recommendations on diaspora policy would appear particularly valuable. The full report likely contains more detailed analysis of diaspora engagement mechanisms and outcomes than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: COUNTER TRAFFICKING PROTECTION PROJECTS REVIEW IDF-FUNDED PROJECTS 2008-2013
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Counter-trafficking protection is the explicit and primary subject of the evaluation — the report title, objectives, and all analytical content center on IDF-funded counter-trafficking protection projects. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis directly relevant to Actions 10e (measures addressing vulnerabilities of trafficking victims, access to justice, protection and assistance), 10g (legislation and procedures to avoid criminalization and ensure victim protection), 10h (physical, psychological and social recovery assistance for trafficking victims), and 10i (training programmes to identify signs of trafficking). Key areas of likely value for a synthesis include: the analysis of outcome sustainability across project types (Policy Development, Capacity Building for Services, Operational Tools/SOPs), the finding that Assistance Programs maintained 100% of outcomes and Training maintained 78%, the identification of NGOs and government as primary sustainability actors, and the recommendations on stakeholder involvement and policy development emphasis. The sections on 'Outcome Category,' 'Proportion of Maintained Outcomes by Outcome Category,' 'Organizations that Maintained Outcomes,' and 'Recommendations for Sustainability' appear particularly relevant as starting points for human reviewers. The report also touches on victim identification capacity building and training of officials — core themes under Objective 10. It is possible the full report contains additional evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: SOLOMON ISLANDS: DEVELOPING PLANNED RELOCATION GUIDELINES IN THE CONTEXT OF SLOW AND SUDDEN ONSET DISASTERS
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears highly relevant to GCM Objective 2, particularly its actions related to slow-onset and sudden-onset natural disasters, climate change adaptation, and planned relocation. The project's core subject — developing Planned Relocation Guidelines for communities in the Solomon Islands facing climate-related displacement — directly addresses Actions 2h, 2i, 2j, 2k, and 2l. The project title itself references 'slow and sudden onset disasters,' and the evaluation extensively discusses sea-level rise, temperature increase, coastal flooding, and disaster risk as drivers of displacement. The evaluation appears to provide substantive evidence on: (a) how a Pacific SIDS government developed policy frameworks for planned relocation as a climate adaptation strategy; (b) the process of developing evidence-based guidelines through community consultation; (c) the challenges of operationalizing relocation policy in a resource-constrained context; and (d) the nexus between climate change, disaster risk, and national security. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on the intervention logic, effectiveness conclusions (C5, C6, C9), sustainability (C10), and lessons identified would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The report likely contributes evidence on Action 2i (adaptation and resilience strategies including planned relocation) and 2l (coherent approaches to migration in the context of disasters). It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: SOLOMON ISLANDS: DEVELOPING PLANNED RELOCATION GUIDELINES IN THE CONTEXT OF SLOW AND SUDDEN ONSET DISASTERS
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears relevant to GCM Objective 5, specifically Action 5h, which calls for cooperation to 'identify, develop and strengthen solutions for migrants compelled to leave their countries of origin due to slow-onset natural disasters, the adverse effects of climate change, and environmental degradation, such as desertification, land degradation, drought and sea level rise, including by devising planned relocation and visa options.' The project's central output — Planned Relocation Guidelines — is precisely the type of instrument referenced in Action 5h. The evaluation provides evidence on how such guidelines were developed, adopted, and positioned for operationalization in a Pacific SIDS context. The conclusions on sustainability (C10), impact (C9), and the lessons identified about government buy-in and evidence-based approaches would likely be of interest to a synthesis specialist examining how states develop planned relocation frameworks. However, the report does not address visa options or broader regular migration pathways, so its relevance is concentrated on the planned relocation dimension of Action 5h. Specialists would want to review the sections on the intervention logic, effectiveness conclusions, and lessons identified, while noting that the report does not cover the full breadth of Objective 5.

References.Title: MIDTERM PERFORMANCE EVALUATION Scholarships for Educational Opportunities Project (SEO) EVALUATION REPORT February 2024
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be highly valuable for a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its associated action 2e (investing in human capital development through education, vocational training, and skills development to reduce irregular migration). The SEO project is explicitly designed to address structural drivers of irregular migration — poverty, lack of livelihood opportunities, and low educational attainment — through scholarships, technical education, and job placement. The evaluation's primary question directly asks about the program's contribution to mitigating irregular migration intentions, and multiple findings and conclusions address whether educational investment reduces the propensity to migrate. The report appears to provide substantive evidence on: (1) the relationship between economic frustration and migration intention (Conclusions 4 and 5); (2) the role of hope and livelihood prospects in shaping migration decisions (Findings 2, 3); and (3) the conditionality of staying on income-generating success. Action 2b (investing in SDG-aligned programs including education and employment creation) and 2d (investing in sustainable development and human capital) are also closely related. For a synthesis specialist, the findings on migration intention reduction, the conclusions on conditionality of staying, and the recommendations on resource provision and job placement would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: MIDTERM PERFORMANCE EVALUATION Scholarships for Educational Opportunities Project (SEO) EVALUATION REPORT February 2024
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7, particularly regarding the identification and support of migrants in vulnerable situations. The evaluation explicitly examines vulnerability profiles of participants — including single mothers, returnees, LGBTQI+ community members, violence survivors, and those with low income and education — and analyzes how these characteristics relate to migration intention. This connects to Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations) and 7c (gender-responsive migration policies addressing particular needs). The report appears to provide substantive analysis of: (1) which populations are most vulnerable and where they are located (Findings 4, 5, 6); (2) the relationship between psychological, economic, and migratory vulnerability and migration intention (Finding 5, Conclusion 5); and (3) psychosocial support services for vulnerable participants (Findings 8, 9, Conclusion 9). However, vulnerability is analyzed primarily in the context of program targeting and service delivery rather than as a standalone protection framework. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the findings on vulnerability profiles, the conclusions on psychosocial support, and Recommendation 6 on gender-responsive services, noting that this is one of several themes rather than the central focus.

References.Title: “FINAL EVALUATION OF PROJECT PO.0206: STRENGTHENING COORDINATED, WHOLE-OFSYSTEM SUPPORT TO GOVERNMENTS TO DEVELOP AND IMPLEMENT EFFECTIVE MIGRATION POLICIES AND PROGRAMMES IN LINE WITH THE 2030 AGENDA, THE GCM, AND OTHER RELEVANT FRAMEWORKS”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to contain meaningful content relevant to a synthesis on GCM Objective 17, particularly around Actions 17c (sensitizing media professionals on migration-related issues) and 17f (awareness-raising campaigns on positive contributions of migration). Outcome 4 of the project explicitly addresses combatting xenophobia, including the establishment of XenofobiaCero digital communities in Costa Rica and Dominican Republic, training of 56 media focal points on an advocacy protocol for migrant protection, and the development of communication products. The evaluation appears to assess the effectiveness and lessons learned from these anti-xenophobia initiatives, including the reduction in scope from three to two countries and lessons on digital community approaches. The conclusions note 'learned valuable lessons on the combat against Xenophobia using digital communities.' The recommendations and lessons learned sections also address communications and xenophobia-related activities. Synthesis specialists working on Objective 17 — particularly on media sensitization (Action 17c) and awareness-raising campaigns (Action 17f) — would likely find the sections on Outcome 4, the XenofobiaCero initiative, and the media focal point training worth reviewing as starting points. The excerpts suggest this is one of several outcomes rather than the sole focus, so the depth of analysis on xenophobia specifically may be moderate. It is possible the full report contains more detailed findings on these activities than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: “FINAL EVALUATION OF PROJECT PO.0206: STRENGTHENING COORDINATED, WHOLE-OFSYSTEM SUPPORT TO GOVERNMENTS TO DEVELOP AND IMPLEMENT EFFECTIVE MIGRATION POLICIES AND PROGRAMMES IN LINE WITH THE 2030 AGENDA, THE GCM, AND OTHER RELEVANT FRAMEWORKS”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to be among the most directly relevant for a synthesis on GCM Objective 23, and likely warrants careful review. The project's explicit purpose — strengthening coordinated, whole-of-system support to governments to implement the GCM in line with the 2030 Agenda — aligns closely with Objective 23's focus on international cooperation and global partnerships for GCM implementation. Critically, the evaluation examines this governance architecture as its primary object of inquiry, not merely as operational context. The report appears to assess: IOM's role as coordinator and secretariat of the UN Network on Migration (UNNM-LAC) and how this regional mechanism functions (Actions 23a, 23d, 23e); the whole-of-system, whole-of-government, and whole-of-society approach to GCM implementation (Action 23a); the integration of migration into UN Development System frameworks as a form of multilateral cooperation (Action 23b); the capacity-building mechanism for GCM implementation across member states (Action 23d); and the alignment of GCM implementation with the 2030 Agenda (Action 23e). The conclusions explicitly address the effectiveness of the UNNM-LAC as a regional cooperation mechanism, the role of civil society participation mechanisms, and the sustainability of international cooperation frameworks. The theory of change articulated in Conclusion 2 directly describes a multilateral governance model for GCM implementation. Synthesis specialists working on Objective 23 — particularly on Actions 23a (whole-of-government/society approaches), 23d (capacity-building mechanisms), and 23e (bilateral/regional partnerships for GCM implementation) — would likely find the sections on Outcomes 1 and 2, the conclusions on coherence and sustainability, and the lessons learned on UNNM coordination particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The evaluation's focus on projectization challenges and multi-year funding also offers institutional insights relevant to the sustainability of international cooperation frameworks.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF EXTERNAL PROJECT RESULTS “INCREASING NATIONAL AND LOCAL CAPACITY FOR PEACE IMPLEMENTATION IN COLOMBIA”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly regarding sustainable reintegration. The project explicitly addresses the reintegration of ex-FARC combatants (referred to as 'reincorporation') into communities, which — while not international return migration — involves many of the same programmatic elements as reintegration of returning migrants: psychosocial support, community reception, economic reintegration, restoration of social fabric, and coordination between national and local actors. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of reintegration programming through the ARN (Agency for Reincorporation and Standardization), including a 'pilot of integral return in productive environments' in Mesetas, Meta. The conclusions note that 'the entire Reincorporation strategy is at the head of the ARN and as expressed by the interviewees, they have been giving continuity to the actions.' Recommendations address ensuring permanence of professionals for knowledge transfer, incorporating territorial entities in local initiatives, and strengthening harmonization between national and local governments — all relevant to Action 21h (sustainable reintegration) and 21i (community needs). Synthesis specialists working on reintegration evidence (Actions 21b, 21h, 21i) would likely find value in reviewing the sections on reintegration programming, the pilot return initiative, and the sustainability conclusions, while noting that the subjects are ex-combatants rather than international migrants — a limitation that should be acknowledged.

References.Title: ENHANCING COOPERATION MEASURES TO EFFECTIVELY COMBAT TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS THROUGH CAPACITY BUILDING AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE IN AZERBAIJAN - PHASE III
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The entire project evaluated is explicitly dedicated to combating trafficking in persons in Azerbaijan, making this the dominant and primary theme throughout the report. The project title, evaluation questions, and all major components directly address TiP prevention, prosecution, and victim protection. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple GCM Objective 10 associated actions: (10c) monitoring of trafficking networks and cooperation on prosecution; (10e) measures addressing vulnerabilities of potential victims and facilitating access to justice; (10g) strengthening legislation and prosecution of traffickers while avoiding criminalization of victims; (10h) protection and assistance to victims including physical, psychological and social recovery; (10i) training programmes for public officials and law enforcement to identify trafficking signs; and (10j) awareness-raising campaigns for migrants on trafficking risks. The evaluation covers capacity building for government officials (Ministry of Interior CT Unit, APA), the National Referral Mechanism, victim identification and referral, direct assistance to VoTs, reintegration support, NGO monitoring and advocacy, private sector engagement, and prevention activities. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on training quality and effectiveness (APA programme), direct assistance to beneficiaries, NGO monitoring and advocacy, and the sustainability analysis of institutionalized CT training would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The full report likely contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: ASSESSMENT REPORT OF THE COMMUNITY STABILIZATION PROJECT IMPLEMENTED BY IOM IN HODH EL CHARAGUI
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly around actions related to investing in sustainable development, food security, resilience, and addressing structural drivers of displacement. The project explicitly targets communities affected by conflict-driven displacement (Malian refugees) and local Mauritanian communities facing extreme poverty, drought, and resource scarcity — conditions that compel movement. The project's integrated farms, water infrastructure, income-generating activities, and solar lamps are framed as interventions to reduce the need to migrate for livelihood (the objective-level target: 'majority of the local populations feel the decrease of needs to move for livelihood'). This aligns closely with GCM Action 2b (investing in food security, resilience, employment creation, gender equality) and Action 2d (investing in sustainable development to create conducive conditions). The excerpts also reference environmental degradation and drought as structural drivers, touching on Action 2i. The conclusions and recommendations sections appear particularly relevant, as they discuss sustainability, poverty reduction, and resilience-building. Human reviewers would likely want to examine the outcome-level analysis (Outcomes 1 and 2) and the conclusions section for evidence on how community stabilization interventions address adverse drivers of migration. Note that the report focuses on a specific project context (Mauritania/Mali border) and evidence is primarily qualitative.

References.Title: ASSESSMENT REPORT OF THE COMMUNITY STABILIZATION PROJECT IMPLEMENTED BY IOM IN HODH EL CHARAGUI
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 16, particularly around social cohesion between refugee and host communities. Social cohesion is a primary and explicit focus of the project — Outcome 3 directly targets 'increased communication and mutual understanding and acceptance between members of the local community and the refugee population, thus protecting and enhancing intra- and inter-community social cohesion.' The joint committee meetings between local Mauritanian communities and Malian refugees (Action 16a — promoting mutual respect and social cohesion), the integrated farms as shared economic spaces, and the overall community stabilization framing align closely with GCM Objective 16 themes. The project also addresses women's empowerment through income-generating activities (Action 16e), and community-level participation mechanisms (Action 16f). The evaluation assesses whether these interventions actually produced measurable changes in inter-community relations, providing empirical evidence on what works. For a synthesis specialist, the Outcome 3 analysis, the lessons learned section on participatory approaches, and the joint committee meeting findings would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The report likely offers rare evidence on conflict-prevention-oriented social cohesion programming in a refugee-host community context. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.
