References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE “REGIONAL RESPONSE TO COVID-19 PROGRAMME IN NORTH AFRICA (ALGERIA, EGYPT, MOROCCO AND TUNISIA)”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1, particularly regarding data collection systems, disaggregated data, and evidence-based programming. The evaluation appears to dedicate substantial attention to data collection deficiencies and M&E system weaknesses — themes that align closely with Actions 1a, 1c, and 1j. Conclusions 2 and 3 explicitly address the absence of systematic data collection, lack of disaggregated data by age, sex, and service type, inconsistent monitoring practices, and the absence of an integrated data management system. Recommendation 2 (M&E system) and Recommendation 3 (Data Collection) directly call for improved data infrastructure, standardized tools, and utilization-focused M&E systems. The report also references MiMOSA as a data management tool and discusses the need for migrant profiling during outreach — touching on Action 1j (country-specific migration profiles). However, the report's primary focus is on programme evaluation rather than migration data systems per se, and the data-related content appears as a cross-cutting concern rather than the central object of inquiry. Synthesis specialists working on evidence for GCM Objective 1 would likely find the sections on Conclusions 2–3 and Recommendations 2–3 most pertinent as starting points for deeper review.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE “REGIONAL RESPONSE TO COVID-19 PROGRAMME IN NORTH AFRICA (ALGERIA, EGYPT, MOROCCO AND TUNISIA)”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. Addressing vulnerabilities of migrants appears to be a core theme throughout the evaluation — the programme's overall objective was explicitly to 'reduce acute vulnerabilities and improve resilience' of migrants affected by COVID-19. The report engages substantively with multiple dimensions of vulnerability relevant to Actions 7a, 7b, 7c, 7e, 7f, and 7k. Programme beneficiaries include victims of trafficking and SGBV, unaccompanied children, migrants in government custody, single female-headed households, migrants with health issues, and elderly migrants — directly mapping to Action 7b. Conclusion 9 and Recommendation 9 explicitly address the need to refine vulnerability criteria with an intersectional perspective, covering gender, disability, LGBTQ+, and other identity dimensions. The report discusses protection assistance, MHPSS, health care access, and referral systems for vulnerable groups. Conclusion 6 addresses community feedback mechanisms linked to protection measures including child protection and PSEA. The sections on Conclusions 7–9 and Recommendations 7–9, as well as the programme description in Section 2.1, would appear most valuable for synthesis specialists to prioritize. The report likely offers concrete operational evidence on how vulnerability-focused programming was designed and implemented in a multi-country emergency context.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE “REGIONAL RESPONSE TO COVID-19 PROGRAMME IN NORTH AFRICA (ALGERIA, EGYPT, MOROCCO AND TUNISIA)”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute substantial evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly regarding migrant access to health services and other basic services. The programme's core activities directly address service access for migrants — including health assistance, protection services, food, shelter, NFIs, cash support, MHPSS, and livelihood support. Action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in national health policies) appears particularly well-covered: the report discusses IOM's advocacy for migrant inclusion in national COVID-19 health systems, vaccination plans, and health coverage frameworks across four countries. Conclusion 10 addresses IOM's advocacy for de facto inclusion of migrants in national health systems, and Recommendation 10 calls for continued advocacy for universal health care for migrant populations. Conclusion 8 notes 'persistent barriers in migrants' equitable access to healthcare services during the pandemic' and discusses unaddressed needs in health coverage. The multisectoral service delivery approach, fragmentation in service provision, and integration of migrants into national policies are analyzed with some depth. Synthesis specialists would likely find the sections on programme outputs (Section 2.1), country contexts (Section 1.1), Conclusions 8–10, and Recommendations 8–10 most relevant as starting points. The report appears to offer concrete operational evidence on barriers to and enablers of migrant access to basic services in a multi-country emergency context.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION REPORT - “PREVENTING UNSAFE MIGRATION FROM ALBANIA TOWARDS EUROPEAN UNION MEMBER STATES- FOLLOW UP CAMPAIGN”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 3. The entire project evaluated is an information and awareness-raising campaign on migration — making this objective's theme the explicit primary subject of the report. The project title itself ('Preventing unsafe migration from Albania towards European Union member states') and its core activities directly align with Action 3e (multilingual, gender-responsive, evidence-based information campaigns and awareness-raising events in countries of origin to promote safe, orderly and regular migration and highlight risks of irregular migration). The excerpts suggest multiple findings and conclusions directly address information dissemination effectiveness, campaign tools (workshops, social media, TV, leaflets), reach (3,687 survey respondents, 12 regions), and the quality of informative materials. The report also appears to address Action 3a (information on regular migration options) and Action 3d (information for migrants on rights and obligations). For a synthesis specialist, the executive summary's discussion of campaign outcomes, the conclusions on communication tools effectiveness (TV, workshops, social media), and the recommendations on future campaign design would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The evaluation of a Campaign Strategy Guide handed over to the Ministry of Interior also provides evidence on institutionalizing information provision. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Engaging Diaspora Communities to Support Micro-entrepreneurship in Morocco
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly around Actions 2b (investing in programmes to address adverse drivers including unemployment) and 2e (investing in human capital development, entrepreneurship, and reducing youth unemployment). The project's stated ultimate objective was to reduce youth unemployment — explicitly framed as a driver of irregular migration across the Mediterranean — by promoting crowdfunding as a financing mechanism for youth entrepreneurs. The executive summary and introduction sections appear to directly address the link between youth unemployment, lack of access to start-up financing, and irregular migration. The evaluation's conclusions and recommendations also reinforce this framing. However, the report's primary analytical focus is on crowdfunding as a financing mechanism and diaspora engagement, rather than on structural drivers of migration per se. The treatment of root causes appears more as framing/rationale than as the central object of analysis. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the project rationale section and the impact/sustainability sections, which appear to discuss the connection between economic opportunity creation and migration prevention most directly.

References.Title: Engaging Diaspora Communities to Support Micro-entrepreneurship in Morocco
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The project's central focus — engaging the Moroccan diaspora to support micro-entrepreneurship and local development through crowdfunding — directly aligns with the core theme of this objective. The report appears to provide substantive evidence on multiple associated actions: Action 19d (establishing government structures to engage diaspora, specifically through the MCMLA and MEF collaboration); Action 19e (developing targeted support programmes and financial products for diaspora investment and entrepreneurship, specifically the crowdfunding model); Action 19f (providing accessible information and mechanisms for diaspora financial engagement); and Action 19j (building partnerships between local authorities, diaspora, and migrant organizations for knowledge and skills transfer). The project title itself references diaspora engagement for development, and the evaluation systematically assesses the effectiveness, sustainability, and impact of diaspora-linked crowdfunding as a development financing mechanism. The executive summary, project description, and conclusions sections all appear to contain directly relevant evidence. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on the hybrid crowdfunding model, the policy/legal framework development (law 15-18), the diaspora-entrepreneur linkage mechanisms, and the recommendations on diaspora engagement platforms would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible that the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal, particularly in the findings sections.

References.Title: Implementación de planes de prevención de violencia basada en género y promoción de la autonomía de mujeres de las FARC en tránsito a la vida civil (Fase II)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly around sustainable reintegration (Action 21h). The evaluation appears to examine a comprehensive reintegration program for ex-FARC combatants — primarily women — transitioning to civilian life in Colombia. While the population is ex-combatants rather than returned migrants in the strict sense, the reintegration frameworks, challenges, and approaches documented are substantively relevant to Action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through social protection, vocational training, employment, psychosocial support, and community inclusion) and potentially Action 21i (addressing needs of communities receiving returnees). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: economic reintegration through IEE (cooperative initiatives, income generation); social reintegration through community care spaces (EICC) and GBV prevention plans; political reintegration through leadership and participation programs; sustainability challenges including discrimination, lack of political support, and insufficient time; and the importance of exit strategies for reintegration programs. The gender-responsive approach to reintegration documented here — including attention to practical and strategic gender gaps, community-based methodologies, and multi-stakeholder partnerships — would likely add value to a synthesis on sustainable reintegration, especially for evidence on gender-sensitive reintegration programming. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the sections on sustainability conclusions (4.1.3), the gender approach analysis, and the lessons learned on exit strategies and relevance-efficacy-sustainability linkages. It should be noted that the population is post-conflict ex-combatants, not returned international migrants, which may limit direct applicability — human reviewers should assess this contextual difference carefully.

References.Title: FINAL EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE “ENHANCEMENT OF NATIONAL CAPACITIES TO COMBAT HUMAN TRAFFICKING IN AZERBAIJAN (ENCT)” PROJECT AND “SUPPORTING THE ENHANCEMENT OF NATIONAL CAPACITIES TO COMBAT HUMAN TRAFFICKING IN AZERBAIJAN (SNCT) PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The entire project — 'Enhancement of National Capacities to Combat Human Trafficking in Azerbaijan' — is explicitly and comprehensively focused on preventing and combating trafficking in human beings. The report's title, evaluation questions, outcomes, outputs, and recommendations all directly address THB. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions of Objective 10: legislative capacity (Outcome 1 on judicial capacity for THB prosecution), victim identification and protection (Outcome 2 on protecting VoTs, including the IOM Manual for Direct Assistance), awareness-raising and training of public officials (Outcome 3 on diplomatic and government officials), and institutional capacity building across ten state agencies. Associated actions most directly covered appear to include 10e (measures addressing vulnerabilities of victims), 10g (strengthening legislation and prosecution), 10h (protection and assistance to victims), and 10i (training programmes for public officials). The executive summary, conclusions, lessons learned, and recommendations sections all appear particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist, as they contain assessments of what worked, what did not, and how capacity development in THB can be improved. The report also touches on gender-responsive approaches to THB and the specific needs of vulnerable groups. The full report likely contains considerably more detail on these themes than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Final Evaluation Report on COVID-19 Preparedness and Responses Project Implemented with the support of Sida in Six countries of Southern and the Horn of Africa
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project's core pillar — Information Management through the Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM) and flow monitoring — appears to be a primary subject of the evaluation. The excerpts suggest that multiple outputs across all six countries directly concern the collection, analysis, and dissemination of disaggregated migration data (demographic breakdowns by age, sex, nationality, origin/destination, vulnerability status). Actions 1a, 1c, and 1f appear particularly well-covered: the project built national capacities in data collection and analysis, produced dashboards shared with governments and stakeholders, and in several countries (e.g., Ethiopia, Mozambique, Malawi) DTM was implemented for the first time. The evaluation's effectiveness section dedicates substantial space to assessing whether DTM exercises informed COVID-19 responses, and the conclusions and recommendations explicitly call for strengthening DTM and flow monitoring activities. For a synthesis specialist, the country-level output sections (Ethiopia Output 1, Djibouti Output 1, Kenya Output 1, Malawi Output 1, Tanzania Output 1, Mozambique Output 1) and the lessons learned section would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Final Evaluation Report on COVID-19 Preparedness and Responses Project Implemented with the support of Sida in Six countries of Southern and the Horn of Africa
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. Border management capacity building is one of the two explicit pillars of the evaluated project, and the excerpts suggest it is analyzed in depth across all six countries. The project established new Border Controlling Points (Ethiopia), trained frontline border officials on IPC and border management (Kenya: 105 workers; Malawi: 295 workers; Mozambique: 141 workers), developed SOPs for PoE management (Kenya, Mozambique), conducted joint site visits and assessments with governments, and installed WASH infrastructure at border points (Tanzania). These activities directly relate to Actions 11a (border management cooperation and capacity building), 11b (integrated border management structures and procedures), and 11d (technical cooperation for border management assets). The evaluation's effectiveness section provides country-by-country assessment of border management outputs with quantified achievement rates. The coordination section also discusses multi-sectoral border management coordination. For a synthesis specialist, the Output 2 sections for each country (Ethiopia, Kenya, Malawi, Mozambique, Tanzania, Djibouti), the relevance section on PoE capacity gaps, and the recommendations on sustaining border management activities would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review.

References.Title: Evaluation of pre-departure orientation  training for Sweden-bound quota  refugees
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 3, particularly Action 3e (pre-departure orientation trainings) and Action 3d (providing newly arrived migrants with targeted information on rights and obligations). The evaluation explicitly assesses a pre-departure orientation (PDO) programme for quota refugees bound for Sweden — this is the primary subject of the report and directly corresponds to the actions under Objective 3. The excerpts suggest multiple findings directly address the effectiveness of information provision at the pre-departure stage, the content of orientation training (rights, responsibilities, practical life aspects), and the continuum of information pre- and post-arrival. The comparison with a control group who received no pre-departure information provides particularly valuable evidence on the impact of such programmes. Key sections for human reviewers to prioritize would include: Section 2.1.1 (PDO training content and participant satisfaction), Section 2.1.2 (control group findings demonstrating information gaps without PDO), Section 3.1 (analysis of whether project outputs led to expected outcomes), and the Conclusions and Recommendations (especially recommendations 1, 3, 5, and 6 on strengthening information continuity). The report also addresses post-arrival information gaps and the need for continuum between pre-departure and post-arrival services, touching on Action 3d. This appears to be core evidence for any synthesis on pre-departure orientation as an information provision mechanism.

References.Title: Evaluation of pre-departure orientation  training for Sweden-bound quota  refugees
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16, particularly Action 16b (pre-departure and post-arrival programmes including rights, language training, and orientation about social norms) and Action 16c (national policy goals on inclusion including labour market integration, family reunification, education). The evaluation explicitly assesses a pre-departure orientation programme designed to facilitate integration and social cohesion — the PDO covers Swedish cultural norms, language importance, rights and responsibilities, and practical aspects of life in Sweden. The findings on refugee preparedness for integration (individualistic society, reserved nature of Swedes, importance of learning Swedish) and the municipality perspectives on integration challenges are directly relevant to Action 16b and 16c. The continuum between pre-departure orientation and post-arrival integration support is a central theme, connecting to the broader inclusion agenda. Key sections for human reviewers would include: Section 2.1.1 (PDO training content on cultural norms and integration), Section 2.2 (municipality perspectives on integration readiness), Section 3.2 (lessons learnt on continuum of information), and Recommendations 4-6 (on strengthening the pre/post-arrival continuum). The report appears to be one of several themes covered — integration is the overarching goal but the evaluation focuses specifically on the PDO as a mechanism, so it is not the sole focus.

References.Title: FINAL INTERNAL INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF THE CAPACITY ENHANCEMENT FOR INSTITUTIONALIZED VICTIM CENTERED INVESTIGATIONS AND PROSECUTIONS OF TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS (TIP) CASES IN SOUTH AFRICA PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would almost certainly be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Trafficking in persons (TIP) is the explicit and exclusive subject of the evaluated project, and the evaluation comprehensively assesses a multi-year counter-trafficking capacity building initiative in South Africa. The project title itself — 'Capacity Enhancement for Institutionalized Victim Centered Investigations and Prosecution of Trafficking in Persons Cases in South Africa' — directly mirrors the language of GCM Objective 10. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple associated actions: Action 10c (monitoring routes and strengthening cooperation on prosecution and victim identification), Action 10e (addressing vulnerabilities of victims and facilitating access to justice), Action 10g (strengthening legislation and prosecution while avoiding criminalization of victims), Action 10h (providing protection and assistance to VOTs), and Action 10i (training programmes for public officials and law enforcement). The evaluation covers development of Standard Operating Procedures for victim identification and referral, sector-specific training manuals for multiple government departments (SAPS, NPA, DSD, DHA, DoH, DEL, DoJ), direct assistance to 29 VOTs including voluntary international repatriation, and strengthening of multi-stakeholder coordination through provincial task teams and NICTIP. For a synthesis specialist, the findings chapters on effectiveness (section 4.3), impact (4.5), and sustainability (4.6) would appear particularly valuable, as would the conclusions on institutional capacity building and the recommendations on scaling up. The evaluation's assessment of what worked in building a victim-centered TIP management system — and what remains to be done — would likely provide rich evidence for a synthesis on this objective.

References.Title: FINAL INTERNAL INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF THE CAPACITY ENHANCEMENT FOR INSTITUTIONALIZED VICTIM CENTERED INVESTIGATIONS AND PROSECUTIONS OF TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS (TIP) CASES IN SOUTH AFRICA PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12, particularly around Actions 12b and 12c. The project's core deliverable — development of harmonized Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for TIP victim identification, referral, and care — directly relates to Action 12c (establishing gender-responsive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures). The evaluation also assesses training of government officials across multiple departments on TIP identification, which relates to Action 12b (specialized training for first responders and government officials on identification and referral of trafficking victims). The excerpts suggest the SOPs and training manuals are analyzed in terms of their development process, stakeholder ownership, and prospects for implementation. The sections on effectiveness (section 4.3) and sustainability (4.6) would appear most relevant for human reviewers examining referral mechanism development and institutionalization. The report's focus on multi-stakeholder coordination for victim identification and referral is a substantive contribution, though the broader migration procedure certainty theme of Objective 12 is not fully addressed.

References.Title: IMPACT Study Report #2 COVID-19, RETURNEES AND IOM  IN THE HORN OF AFRICA: A Natural Experiment-Based Evaluation
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly its associated actions on sustainable reintegration (Actions 21b, 21h, and 21i). The evaluation explicitly examines the JI-HoA programme — a large-scale voluntary return and reintegration initiative in the Horn of Africa — assessing the effectiveness of reintegration assistance in building returnee resilience to shocks. The report title, evaluation questions, and all major analytical sections are directly oriented toward understanding what makes reintegration sustainable and what programme design features enhance returnee well-being post-return. Key areas of likely contribution to a synthesis include: (1) evidence on the relationship between timeliness of reintegration assistance delivery and outcomes (directly relevant to Action 21h on equal access to services and economic empowerment upon return); (2) comparative analysis of cash-based vs. in-kind assistance modalities and their effectiveness; (3) analysis of differential outcomes by gender, disability, and vulnerability status; (4) evidence on the role of community-based reintegration projects; (5) the finding that economic assistance contributed to mitigation but not recovery, with implications for programme design. The conclusions and recommendations sections appear particularly valuable for synthesis specialists, as do the findings on agriculture as a resilience strategy and the Emergency Cash Advance initiative in Ethiopia. The report also appears to offer methodological lessons on evaluating reintegration programmes under shock conditions. It is possible the full report contains additional evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal, but based on what is accessible, this appears to be core evidence for any synthesis on sustainable reintegration under GCM Objective 21.

References.Title: A PROCESS EVALUATION OF IOM’S INSTITUTIONAL RESPONSE TO SETTING UP AND IMPLEMENTING THE EUROPEAN UNION – IOM JOINT INITIATIVE FOR MIGRANT PROTECTION AND REINTEGRATION
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The EU-IOM Joint Initiative for Migrant Protection and Reintegration is explicitly and centrally focused on assisted voluntary return and sustainable reintegration — the core themes of GCM Objective 21. The report title, evaluation objectives, and all major sections directly address return and reintegration programming across 26 African countries. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: voluntary return operations and AVRR SOPs (relating to actions 21b and 21e); the new integrated approach to reintegration addressing economic, social, and psychosocial dimensions (action 21h); community-based reintegration (action 21i); partnerships with governments and civil society for reintegration (action 21b); and challenges in ensuring timely and sustainable reintegration support. The evaluation covers nearly 100,000 returning migrants across three African regions, representing a substantial evidence base. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on the new integrated approach to reintegration, the set-up and implementation of programmatic activities, and the conclusions and recommendations would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The report likely contains detailed operational evidence on what works and what challenges arise in implementing voluntary return and sustainable reintegration at scale — directly relevant to actions 21b, 21h, and 21i. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE MIGRATION GOVERNANCE INDICATORS PROGRAMME
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The MGI programme is explicitly designed as a data and evidence tool for migration governance assessment, with 94 indicators organized around the MiGOF dimensions. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how migration data is collected, analyzed, and used to inform policy — directly touching on actions 1a (comprehensive strategy for improving migration data), 1d (data on effects of migration for 2030 Agenda implementation), and 1j (country-specific migration profiles). The Migration Governance Profile and MGI Matrix appear to function as country-specific migration profiles (action 1j). The report's conclusions and recommendations on transparency and use of MGI data (Recommendation E) — including calls to publicly publish methodology, share data, and improve data visualization — are particularly relevant to actions 1a and 1e. The recommendation to strengthen the post-MGI action plan and use MGI results for evidence-based policymaking also touches on action 1d. However, the report's primary focus is on the governance and operational effectiveness of the MGI programme itself rather than on data collection methodology per se. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the Effectiveness section and Recommendation E on transparency and use of MGI, as well as the lessons identified section on methodology/data.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE MIGRATION GOVERNANCE INDICATORS PROGRAMME
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 23, and represents one of the stronger candidates for relevance in this report. The MGI programme is explicitly a multilateral migration governance initiative — it is an IOM-led instrument designed to help governments assess their alignment with the Migration Governance Framework (MiGOF) and implement the GCM and SDGs. The evaluation examines this as its primary object of inquiry, not merely as operational context. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: how international cooperation mechanisms (IOM-EIU partnership, whole-of-government approaches) function in practice (action 23a, 23e); how the MGI supports GCM implementation across 68 countries in nine regions (action 23a); the role of IOM as an international organization in supporting member states' migration governance (action 23d); and the alignment of the MGI with the 2030 Agenda and GCM (action 23a, 23b). The Coherence section (internal and external coherence with inter-state processes) and the Conclusions on the MGI's global footprint and crossroads appear particularly relevant. Recommendation A on the MGI operational model — including directions toward an index model updated annually across countries or full institutional integration — directly addresses how international cooperation mechanisms for migration governance can be structured and strengthened. The report's analysis of funding diversification (Recommendation F) and the role of the US Government as main donor also touches on the sustainability of international cooperation frameworks. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the Coherence findings, the Conclusions section, and Recommendations A, C, and F as starting points. Note that the score reflects that while Objective 23 is genuinely relevant here — because the MGI is itself a global governance mechanism being evaluated — the report's primary analytical focus is on programme effectiveness and operational design rather than on the broader architecture of international migration cooperation.

References.Title: MID TERM EVALUATION FOR DFID FUNDED PROGRAMME “SAFETY, SUPPORT AND SOLUTIONS IN THE CENTRAL MEDITERRANEAN ROUTE” IMPLEMENTED BY THE INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATION FOR MIGRATION (IOM)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to have substantial relevance for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The SSSII programme explicitly includes an Outcome 3 focused on data collection and analysis on migration trends, and the excerpts discuss DTM (Displacement Tracking Matrix) data products, their use by national authorities, and gaps in dissemination. The report's synthesis section and ToC review both engage with the problem statement that 'there is a lack of data and high-quality analysis on migration trends, flows, management and demand.' Specific content appears to address actions 1a (harmonizing data collection methodologies), 1c (building national capacities in data collection), and 1e (collaboration between global/regional databases). The excerpts note DTM data being used by national authorities in Spain and Italy, the Humanitarian Partner Tool Kit initiative, and challenges around data dissemination and language accessibility. The recommendations section (Recommendation 4 and 5) explicitly addresses how programme-generated data can be better used and how impact measurement tools can be developed. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the Outcome 3 findings sections and the ToC problem statement review on data gaps, as these appear to offer substantive evidence on data collection, analysis, and use for migration policy — though the full report may contain more depth than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: MID TERM EVALUATION FOR DFID FUNDED PROGRAMME “SAFETY, SUPPORT AND SOLUTIONS IN THE CENTRAL MEDITERRANEAN ROUTE” IMPLEMENTED BY THE INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATION FOR MIGRATION (IOM)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to be highly relevant — potentially fundamental — for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The SSSII programme's core mandate is explicitly about addressing vulnerabilities of migrants along the Central Mediterranean Route. The excerpts discuss: identification and assistance to vulnerable migrants including unaccompanied migrant children (UMCs) through SAR activities and transit centres (Actions 7b, 7e, 7f); SGBV-related support; psychosocial support (PSS); assistance to victims of trafficking; gender-responsive approaches; and referral mechanisms. The synthesis section notes IOM as 'the only organization providing direct assistance to victims of human trafficking' in Mali, and discusses support to UMCs in Niger and The Gambia. The evaluation questions explicitly ask whether 'the most vulnerable migrants along the CMR are reached as expected' and assess protection and health services. The ToC problem statements directly address migrant vulnerability. Synthesis specialists would likely want to prioritize the Outcome 1 findings sections (protection and direct assistance), the country-level findings on vulnerable populations, and the recommendations on capacity building for vulnerability response. Actions 7b, 7c, 7e, 7f, and 7k appear most directly addressed.

References.Title: MID TERM EVALUATION FOR DFID FUNDED PROGRAMME “SAFETY, SUPPORT AND SOLUTIONS IN THE CENTRAL MEDITERRANEAN ROUTE” IMPLEMENTED BY THE INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATION FOR MIGRATION (IOM)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to have relevant content for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The excerpts explicitly discuss IOM's role in combating trafficking: in Mali, IOM is described as 'the only organization providing direct assistance to victims of human trafficking' and is a member of the national committee addressing trafficking, including lobbying for a dedicated police brigade to identify and prosecute traffickers. The programme includes NAATIP (National Agency Against Trafficking in Persons) as an acronym, suggesting institutional engagement. The synthesis section discusses capacity building for identification and support of trafficking victims, and the evaluation questions ask about effectiveness of support to vulnerable migrants including VoT (Victims of Trafficking). Actions 10c (monitoring irregular routes exploited by trafficking networks), 10e (addressing vulnerabilities of trafficking victims), and 10i (training programmes to identify trafficking) appear most directly addressed. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the Mali country findings and the Outcome 1 sections on trafficking response, as these appear to offer substantive programmatic evidence.

References.Title: MID TERM EVALUATION FOR DFID FUNDED PROGRAMME “SAFETY, SUPPORT AND SOLUTIONS IN THE CENTRAL MEDITERRANEAN ROUTE” IMPLEMENTED BY THE INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATION FOR MIGRATION (IOM)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to have relevant content for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The SSSII programme explicitly focuses on improving migrant access to services along the CMR, and the evaluation questions directly ask 'Do migrants along the CMR have better access to services (directly or through referral mechanisms)?' The excerpts discuss Migrant Resource and Response Centres (MRRCs) as service delivery points, health services (including PSS, HIV, TB, STI-related services implied by acronyms), and the quality of services provided. The synthesis section discusses capacity building of CSOs to deliver services and the handover of MRRC management to local government in Senegal. Actions 15c (holistic and accessible service points at local level) and 15e (health needs of migrants) appear most directly addressed. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the Outcome 1 and 2 findings on service access and quality, and the MRRC-related content across country sections, as these appear to offer substantive evidence on migrant-inclusive service delivery.

References.Title: MID TERM EVALUATION FOR DFID FUNDED PROGRAMME “SAFETY, SUPPORT AND SOLUTIONS IN THE CENTRAL MEDITERRANEAN ROUTE” IMPLEMENTED BY THE INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATION FOR MIGRATION (IOM)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to be fundamental for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Return and reintegration is explicitly a core programme component (AVRR — Assisted Voluntary Return and Reintegration appears as a key acronym), and the evaluation questions directly ask 'How effective are return and reintegration initiatives supported under the programme?' The excerpts discuss: voluntary humanitarian return from Libya to CDI; reintegration support in Guinea, Senegal, Mali, and other countries; government ownership of reintegration infrastructure (MRRC handover in Senegal, shelter for UMCs in The Gambia); capacity building for reintegration actors; and the ORION (Operationalising an Integrated Approach to Reintegration) project. The synthesis section notes Algeria's improved return and reintegration results following greater government support. Actions 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes), 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration), and 21i (addressing needs of communities to which migrants return) appear most directly addressed. Synthesis specialists would likely want to prioritize the Outcome 2 findings sections across multiple country contexts, the sustainability findings on reintegration infrastructure, and the recommendations on government ownership of return programmes. The multi-country comparative evidence on reintegration effectiveness would likely be particularly valuable for synthesis purposes.

References.Title: STRENGTHENING THE REGIONAL EVIDENCE BASE TO SUPPORT POLICY DEVELOPMENT AND ENHANCE CAPACITIES TO MANAGE MIGRATION AND DISPLACEMENT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The evaluated project — 'Strengthening the Regional Evidence Base to Support Policy Development and Enhance Capacities to Manage Migration and Displacement' — appears to have data collection, analysis, harmonization, and dissemination as its explicit primary subject. The project title, all three stated outcomes, and the majority of evaluation questions directly address themes central to GCM Objective 1. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: harmonizing methodologies for data collection across the Central America/Caribbean region (aligning with Action 1a); improving international comparability of migration statistics (Action 1b); building national and regional capacities in data collection, analysis and dissemination (Action 1c); supporting regional data hubs and avoiding duplication across databases (Action 1e); and using administrative records for statistical purposes (Action 1i). The conclusions and recommendations sections appear particularly rich with evidence relevant to this objective — for example, recommendations on strengthening data utilization and digital literacy, developing data collection tools, improving M&E practices, and capacity building for data management. The project's work with DTM standards, KoBo tools, Data Dictionaries, and SDG indicator disaggregation by migration status further reinforces alignment with Actions 1c and 1d. For a synthesis specialist, the Effectiveness section (Section 5, referenced in conclusions), the Sustainability section, and the full Recommendations sections would appear to be the most valuable starting points. The excerpts strongly suggest this report would contribute core evidence to any synthesis on GCM Objective 1.

References.Title: EVALUATION FINALE DU PROJET "VERS UNE DIMENSION ENVIRONNEMENTALE DE L’AIDE À LA RÉINTÉGRATION POUR RÉDUIRE LA PRESSION DU CHANGEMENT CLIMATIQUE SUR LA MIGRATION EN AFRIQUE DE L’OUEST PROJET PILOTE DE L’OIM POUR L’AFRIQUE DE L’OUEST"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its actions related to climate change adaptation, environmental degradation, and migration drivers (actions 2h, 2i, and 2b). The project's very title — 'Vers une dimension environnementale de l'aide à la réintégration pour réduire la pression du changement climatique sur la migration en Afrique de l'Ouest' — directly addresses the nexus between environmental degradation and migration pressure. The excerpts suggest that the report explicitly examines desertification, soil degradation, and rainfall instability as drivers of rural-to-urban and cross-border migration in Senegal, particularly in the Kolda region. The pilot project tests community-based approaches combining migrant reintegration with climate change adaptation and disaster risk reduction — directly relevant to actions 2i (adaptation and resilience strategies) and 2b (investing in resilience and disaster risk reduction). Multiple conclusions and recommendations address the environmental-migration nexus, including the impact of awareness-raising on environmental perceptions (Conclusion 21), sustainability of green reintegration (Conclusions 22-24), and the broader goal of reducing forced migration from climate-affected regions. For a synthesis specialist, the Introduction section on environmental drivers of migration in Senegal, the pilot project description under Volet III, and Conclusions 17-21 on impact would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The report likely also contributes evidence on action 2d (investing in sustainable local development) and 2e (human capital development through green employment). It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION FINALE DU PROJET "VERS UNE DIMENSION ENVIRONNEMENTALE DE L’AIDE À LA RÉINTÉGRATION POUR RÉDUIRE LA PRESSION DU CHANGEMENT CLIMATIQUE SUR LA MIGRATION EN AFRIQUE DE L’OUEST PROJET PILOTE DE L’OIM POUR L’AFRIQUE DE L’OUEST"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly its actions related to sustainable reintegration (actions 21b, 21h, and 21i). The project is explicitly an AVRR (Assistance au Retour Volontaire et à la Réintégration) initiative, and the pilot project 'Médina Touat' directly tests an innovative community-based approach to sustainable reintegration combining economic empowerment with environmental adaptation. The evaluation systematically assesses reintegration outcomes across multiple dimensions: economic (Conclusions 18, 24), social (Conclusions 19, 25), environmental (Conclusion 21), and sustainability (Conclusions 22-25). Action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through equal access to social protection, vocational training, employment, and financial services) and action 21i (identifying and addressing needs of communities to which migrants return) appear directly addressed by the pilot project design and evaluation findings. The report also contributes evidence on action 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes — though Conclusion 26 notes this was insufficiently integrated). The development of IOM guidelines for integrating environmental dimensions into AVRR programmes (Conclusions 7-8) represents a potentially significant contribution to evidence on reintegration programming innovation. For a synthesis specialist, the executive summary conclusions on impact and sustainability, the Volet III project description, the lessons learned section, and recommendations 1-15 would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional detailed evidence on reintegration outcomes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: PROJECT EVALUATION REPORT CONSOLIDATION OF SRI LANKA BIOMETRICS PROJECT: BIOMETRICS PHASE 3
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 4. The entire project is explicitly centered on legal identity documentation — specifically biometric passport systems, fingerprint capture, ICAO-compliant digital photos, and the 'One Person-One Passport' policy of Sri Lanka. This aligns directly with Action 4b (harmonizing travel documents in line with ICAO specifications, investing in digitalization, strengthening biometric data mechanisms) and Action 4a (improving civil registry systems and investing in ICT solutions). The evaluation appears to assess the effectiveness, efficiency, sustainability, and impact of a multi-phase biometric identity management initiative, with findings on passport issuance rates, system modifications, institutional capacity, and passport ranking improvements. The executive summary, effectiveness section, and sustainability section would appear particularly valuable for human reviewers, as they contain substantive analysis of biometric system implementation outcomes, institutional capacity building for identity verification, and the downstream effects on passport integrity and international mobility. The connectedness section linking biometric passport work to e-passport aspirations is also likely relevant to Action 4b. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: PROJECT EVALUATION REPORT CONSOLIDATION OF SRI LANKA BIOMETRICS PROJECT: BIOMETRICS PHASE 3
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The project is explicitly framed as a border management initiative — the evaluation references IOM's Integrated Border Management Strategy as a key alignment framework, and the project involves fingerprint capture stations at Colombo International Airport, AFIS systems, and institutional capacity building for the Department of Immigration and Emigration. These elements relate to Action 11b (effective integrated border management through pre-screening, use of ICT, and efficient border crossing procedures) and Action 11a (capacity-building measures for border management). The connectedness section explicitly links the project to longer-term border management development priorities including e-passport. However, border management appears as one of several themes alongside identity documentation (Objective 4), and the report does not deeply analyze cross-border cooperation, human rights at borders, or the full range of Actions under Objective 11. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the context section (Section 4.1), the effectiveness findings on fingerprint capture station installation, and the connectedness section for content most relevant to integrated border management. The excerpts suggest this is a secondary but substantive theme.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation: CAPACITY BUILDING OF GEORGIAN AUTHORITIES IN MIGRATION DATA ANALYSIS
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project evaluated — 'Capacity building of Georgian authorities in migration data analysis' — is explicitly and centrally focused on building national capacity for migration data collection, analysis, and dissemination, which maps directly onto the core theme of this objective. The project title itself signals this alignment, and the excerpts confirm that the primary subject is the Unified Migration Data Analysis System (UMAS), a national infrastructure for migration data analysis. The evaluation appears to assess capacity-building efforts in data management, data exchange, data visualization (Tableau training), and the development of a comprehensive migration data management and analysis strategy — all of which correspond closely to Actions 1a (comprehensive strategy for improving migration data), 1c (build national capacities in data collection, analysis and dissemination), 1i (collaboration between state units responsible for migration data and national statistical offices), and 1j (country-specific migration profiles and evidence-based migration policies). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: the UMAS Needs Assessment and Gap Analysis report; training and consultancy activities for data analysts; data quality assessment; and the challenge of sustaining data governance capacity. The conclusions and recommendations sections appear particularly relevant, addressing sustainability of data systems, staff retention, and resource mobilization for continued UMAS development. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on effectiveness (what capacity-building approaches worked), sustainability (structural challenges to maintaining data governance), and the recommendations on project design and needs assessment would appear most valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Supporting local economic development in The Gambia, Guinea Conakry and Guinea Bissau (SLED)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 2. The SLED project's core purpose — addressing the structural drivers of irregular migration from The Gambia, Guinea Conakry, and Guinea-Bissau — aligns directly with this objective. The excerpts explicitly state that 'irregular migration flows from The Gambia, Guinea Conakry, and Guinea-Bissau are mostly economically motivated' and that 'the lack of opportunities for a decent, secure, and fulfilling future in their home countries often drives youth to embark on perilous migratory journeys.' The project addresses push factors including unemployment, economic pressures, and institutional fragility — all core themes under GCM Objective 2, particularly Actions 2b (investing in SDG-aligned programmes including employment creation and decent work) and 2e (investing in human capital development, vocational training, entrepreneurship, and youth unemployment reduction). The evaluation assesses outcomes related to youth employment services, income-generating activities, vocational training, and local economic development — all directly relevant to reducing adverse structural drivers. The effectiveness section discusses the extent to which these interventions actually discouraged irregular migration, and the contribution to impact section notes 'indications that the activities, particularly the awareness-raising campaigns, contributed to some young people deciding not to migrate irregularly.' For a synthesis specialist, the effectiveness findings on youth employment interventions (Outcome 2), the sustainability analysis of structural change, and the contribution to impact section would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional evidence on what works in addressing push factors in West African contexts.

References.Title: Supporting local economic development in The Gambia, Guinea Conakry and Guinea Bissau (SLED)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. Diaspora engagement for development is one of the three core outcomes of the SLED project, and the evaluation explicitly examines how diaspora members can contribute to sustainable development in their countries of origin. The excerpts indicate substantive analysis of diaspora mentorship programmes, skills transfer schemes, community-based projects organized with diaspora involvement, and the establishment of diaspora websites and data portals — all directly relevant to GCM Actions 19d (establishing diaspora offices and mechanisms), 19i (enabling diaspora professional engagement in home countries), and 19j (building partnerships for knowledge and skills transfer). The report also examines the integration of migration into national development policy (Outcome 1), relating to Action 19b (integrating migration into development planning). The sustainability section discusses diaspora website development, technical committees, and government capacity to engage diaspora — all relevant to Action 19d. The effectiveness section analyzes what worked and what did not in diaspora engagement, including the lesson that 'the involvement of the diaspora from the inception of the project would have allowed the completion of all activities under outcome 3.' For a synthesis specialist, the Outcome 3 effectiveness analysis, the sustainability findings on diaspora engagement mechanisms, and the recommendations on diaspora mentoring follow-up would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains richer evidence on diaspora engagement modalities and their effectiveness in West African contexts.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF IOM’S LEVEL 3 EMERGENCY RESPONSES
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1, particularly regarding IOM's Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM) as a data collection and dissemination tool. The DTM is described as 'possibly the most well-known and appreciated service provided by IOM' and is repeatedly highlighted across multiple sections as generating data used by the broader humanitarian community for flash appeals, Humanitarian Response Plans, and Multisectoral Needs Assessments. The excerpts suggest substantive discussion of how DTM data informs evidence-based responses (relating to Actions 1a, 1c, and 1d). The report also touches on monitoring and evaluation gaps, noting that only 15% of survey respondents think IOM effectively applies M&E during L3 responses, and that reporting focuses on outputs rather than outcomes. However, data collection is not the primary focus of the evaluation — it surfaces as an enabling factor within a broader operational assessment. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the Effectiveness and Impact sections, where DTM contributions are most substantively discussed, as well as the Knowledge Management subsection. The full report may contain additional relevant content beyond these excerpts.

References.Title: External Final Evaluation of the project ‘EVIDENCE-BASED MIGRATION POLICY PLANNING AND DISCOURSE IN NORTH MACEDONIA’
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project's very title — 'Evidence-Based Migration Policy Planning and Discourse in North Macedonia' — signals that data collection, analysis, and evidence-based policymaking are the central subjects of the evaluation. The excerpts suggest that multiple conclusions and recommendations directly address actions under Objective 1: the development and use of Country Migration Profiles (Action 1j), integration of migration modules into Labour Force Surveys (Action 1h), use of big data as supplementary data sources (Action 1c), Mirror Statistics for estimating migration stocks and flows (Action 1b), work on remittances data (Action 1d), and inter-institutional data exchange mechanisms (Action 1i). The evaluation appears to assess the effectiveness of innovative data tools, capacity-building for data collection and analysis, and the institutionalization of evidence-based practices across government agencies. Conclusions #1 and #2 in the excerpts are particularly directly relevant, as is the discussion of lessons learned around innovative tools. For a synthesis specialist, the conclusions section (especially Conclusions #1, #2, and #3), the lessons learned section (Lesson #2 and Best Practice #2), and the recommendations section (Recommendation #1.1) would appear to be the most valuable starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional quantitative or qualitative evidence on data capacity-building outcomes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: External Final Evaluation of the project ‘EVIDENCE-BASED MIGRATION POLICY PLANNING AND DISCOURSE IN NORTH MACEDONIA’
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 17, particularly around Actions 17c (promoting objective reporting and evidence-based information) and 17f (awareness-raising campaigns on positive contributions of migration). The project's third outcome explicitly targets improving public discourse and perceptions of migrants and refugees, and the evaluation assesses the effectiveness of Communication for Social Change approaches in shifting public attitudes (Conclusion #5). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of communication campaigns, public awareness events, and their short-term impact on perceptions — directly relevant to the 'evidence-based public discourse' dimension of Objective 17. However, this appears to be one of two or three major themes in the report (alongside data governance), and the analysis of discrimination or hate crime prevention (Actions 17a, 17b, 17d) does not appear prominent in the excerpts. For a synthesis specialist, Conclusion #5, the effectiveness section on communication activities, and Recommendation #1 would appear most relevant as starting points. The excerpts also note that lasting impact requires continued campaigns, which is a substantive observation for synthesis purposes. It is possible the full report contains more detailed analysis of specific campaign outcomes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL INTERNAL INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "INTEGRATION AND STABILIZATION SUPPORT THROUGH LIVELIHOODS FOR IDPS AND THE CONFLICT-AFFECTED POPULATION IN UKRAINE"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. The project's explicit goals include integration of IDPs into host communities and stabilization of host communities — themes that align closely with Objective 16's focus on inclusion and social cohesion. The project title itself references 'Integration and Stabilization Support,' and Conclusion 7 notes the project is on track to 'improve cooperation and connections between IDPs and host community members, and facilitate integration of IDPs.' The recommendations suggest expanding attention to social cohesion (explicitly mentioned). Action 16c (national policy goals on inclusion, labour market integration) and Action 16d (inclusive labour markets, access to decent work) appear particularly relevant, as does Action 16g (vocational and civic integration courses). The evaluation also covers vocational training, business development, and economic empowerment of IDPs alongside host community members — all relevant to Objective 16's theme. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the conclusions on outcome achievement (Conclusion 7), the cross-cutting issues section (Conclusion 14), and the recommendations on social cohesion expansion, as these appear most directly relevant. It should be noted that the population is IDPs (internal displacement) rather than international migrants, which may limit direct applicability to GCM Objective 16, but the integration and social cohesion evidence may still be transferable.

References.Title: FINAL INTERNAL INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "INTEGRATION AND STABILIZATION SUPPORT THROUGH LIVELIHOODS FOR IDPS AND THE CONFLICT-AFFECTED POPULATION IN UKRAINE"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute relevant evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The project explicitly aims to contribute to economic recovery of IDPs and support community development, with the theory of change linking individual economic empowerment to broader community benefits. The evaluation assesses how livelihoods support (grants for self-employment and micro-enterprise) enables displaced persons to contribute economically to their communities — resonating with Action 19e (targeted support programmes facilitating migrant and diaspora investments and entrepreneurship) and Action 19a (ensuring positive effects of migration for SDG realization). Conclusion 12 addresses contribution to long-term economic and social impacts, and Conclusion 7 notes the project promotes 'additional jobs and services for the community.' The recommendations on revolving fund mechanisms, grant scaling, and business fairs also relate to entrepreneurship support themes under Objective 19. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the conclusions on impact (Conclusion 12), outcome achievement (Conclusion 7), and the sustainability section (Conclusion 13), as well as the recommendations on grant and loan options. The IDP (internal displacement) context rather than international migration may limit direct applicability, but the evidence on economic empowerment and community contribution appears substantively relevant.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF IOM EQUI-HEALTH PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to contribute meaningful evidence relevant to GCM Objective 1, particularly around harmonized data collection on migrant health. The Equi-Health project explicitly produced a 'Report on migrant health data collection, including template for harmonized data collection on health assessment for migrants and approaches on referrals at the border and in detention' — directly touching on Actions 1a and 1b regarding harmonizing methodologies and improving international comparability. The MIPEX health strand reviews (31 country reports plus a comparative analysis) represent a substantial data collection and comparative analysis effort across EU Member States, relevant to Action 1j (country-specific profiles). The excerpts also reference the development of evidence-based policy tools and the need for a central repository or observatory for migration health data, touching on Actions 1e and 1f. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on the data collection report and MIPEX methodology, as well as the recommendations on consolidation of outputs, as these appear most directly relevant. The full report may contain additional detail on data harmonization approaches beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF IOM EQUI-HEALTH PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly Action 15e on incorporating health needs of migrants in national health care policies. The Equi-Health project's stated objective is explicitly to 'foster the improvement of access and appropriateness of health care services in EU/EEA Member States to meet the needs of migrants, the Roma and other vulnerable groups, including irregular migrants' — directly mirroring the language and intent of Objective 15. The report appears to generate core evidence across multiple dimensions: the MIPEX health strand reviews (31 country reports) systematically assess national laws and policies on migrant health access across EU Member States; the consensus guidelines on access to health services for irregular migrants directly address Action 15b (ensuring cooperation between service providers does not compromise access) and 15e; the thematic study on costs of non-provision of health care provides economic evidence for inclusive service delivery; and the training packages address culturally sensitive service delivery. The sections on MIPEX health reviews, the consensus recommendations on irregular migrants, the cost analysis, and the Roma health component (including health mediation) would appear particularly valuable for synthesis specialists to prioritize. The full report likely contains substantially more evidence on this objective than the excerpts reveal, given it is the central focus of the entire evaluated programme.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF IOM EQUI-HEALTH PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to contribute meaningful evidence relevant to GCM Objective 16, particularly through the Roma health component and the health mediation work. The European Network of Community Health Mediators initiative — involving intercultural mediation, community engagement, and institutionalization of health mediator roles — touches on Actions 16a (mutual respect and integration policies) and 16f (community centres and intercultural dialogue). The Roma health component explicitly addresses social inclusion of a marginalized minority group through health access. The MIPEX health reviews assess national policies on migrant inclusion in health systems, relevant to Action 16c (national policy goals on inclusion). However, social cohesion and inclusion are addressed primarily as means to health equity rather than as standalone analytical themes. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the Roma health sections, the health mediation network development, and the MIPEX country reports for content on inclusion policies, while noting that health access is the primary analytical lens throughout.

References.Title: A PROTECTION PROJECT- SUPPORTING GOVERNMENTAL AND NON-GOVERNMENTAL PARTNERS TO PROTECT MIGRANTS HUMAN RIGHTS ALONG THE EAST AFRICAN ROUTE
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The Protection Project is fundamentally oriented toward identifying and responding to the needs of vulnerable migrants — including victims of trafficking, unaccompanied minors, detained migrants, asylum seekers, and refugees — along the East African route. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of vulnerability identification, referral mechanisms, and direct assistance, relating closely to Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies addressing particular needs), 7e and 7f (protection of migrant children), and 7k (involving local authorities in identification and referral). Finding 10 addresses direct assistance to 620 migrants including 207 victims of trafficking; Finding 12 discusses fast-tracked resettlement for 376 trafficked persons; Finding 13 covers health assistance outcomes. Recommendations 13-15 address migrant rights, durable solutions, and access to services. The direct support section of the recommendations (Section 5.1) appears particularly relevant, as does the discussion of NRM and referral mechanisms. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the findings on direct assistance (Findings 10, 12, 13), the SCOT analysis, and the direct support recommendations section. The report appears to be one of several themes covered rather than exclusively focused on vulnerability, which prevents a FUNDAMENTAL score.

References.Title: A PROTECTION PROJECT- SUPPORTING GOVERNMENTAL AND NON-GOVERNMENTAL PARTNERS TO PROTECT MIGRANTS HUMAN RIGHTS ALONG THE EAST AFRICAN ROUTE
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Trafficking in persons (TIP) is a central and explicit focus of the Protection Project, appearing prominently in the project title, evaluation questions, and throughout the findings and recommendations. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions of anti-trafficking response that align closely with GCM Objective 10 actions. Key areas of likely relevance include: criminal justice capacity building for TIP investigation and prosecution (Actions 10c, 10d, 10g) — addressed in Finding 8 and Recommendations 3-4; victim identification, protection and assistance including fast-tracked resettlement for 376 Sinai trafficking survivors (Actions 10e, 10h) — addressed in Findings 10, 11, 12; awareness-raising and community-based prevention approaches including Community Conversations (Action 10j) — addressed in Findings 7, and Recommendations 10-12; and the distinction between TIP and smuggling (Action 10f) — referenced in Recommendation 9e. The report also appears to address national referral mechanisms (NRM), cooperation with UNHCR on victim assistance, and the challenges of defining and monitoring 'durable solutions' for trafficking victims. For synthesis specialists, the findings on direct assistance (Findings 10, 12), capacity building outcomes (Finding 8), Community Conversations effectiveness (Finding 7), and the full recommendations sections on prevention and direct support would appear to be the most valuable starting points for deeper review. The evaluation's SCOT analysis likely contains additional relevant evidence on the strengths and challenges of anti-trafficking programming in the East African context.

References.Title: A PROTECTION PROJECT- SUPPORTING GOVERNMENTAL AND NON-GOVERNMENTAL PARTNERS TO PROTECT MIGRANTS HUMAN RIGHTS ALONG THE EAST AFRICAN ROUTE
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. Access to basic services — particularly health services — for vulnerable migrants is a substantive focus of the Protection Project. Finding 13 explicitly addresses how 'project strategies for the provision of health assistance have contributed to improved health outcomes, in some cases life-saving, for migrants, while generating supplementary and ongoing benefits for non-migrants.' Finding 15 notes that strengthening health centres will generate ongoing benefits post-project. The direct support section discusses IOM's role in facilitating access to healthcare, psychosocial assistance, and refugee status determination for migrants in Egypt. Recommendation 15 addresses advocacy for access to migrants in detention facilities. These elements connect to Action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in national health care policies) and Action 15c (establishing accessible service points). The report also touches on barriers to service access for irregular migrants. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review Finding 13, Finding 15, and the direct support recommendations section, as well as the discussion of health centre strengthening as a sustainability outcome. The report appears to offer concrete evidence on health service delivery to vulnerable migrants in a mixed migration context.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “STRENGTHENING THE EVIDENCE BASE ON MIGRATION, ENVIRONMENT AND CLIMATE CHANGE TO ENHANCE IOM'S WORK IN SOUTH AMERICA”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project evaluated — 'Fortalecimiento de la base de evidencia sobre Migración, Ambiente y Cambio Climático en América del Sur' — is explicitly and centrally focused on strengthening the evidence base and data collection on migration, environment and climate change (MECC) in South America. The project title itself references data/evidence strengthening, and the evaluation questions directly address data gaps, data collection mechanisms, and evidence production. The excerpts suggest multiple conclusions and recommendations directly address: identifying gaps in MECC data across 10 countries (Actions 1a, 1c); harmonization challenges in data collection instruments (Action 1a, 1b); building national and institutional capacities for data collection and analysis (Action 1c); and producing disaggregated data including by gender and ethnicity (Action 1j). The executive summary explicitly notes 'la ausencia de armonización y alineamiento conceptual de las categorías utilizadas en los instrumentos de recolección de datos' as a key challenge — directly relevant to Actions 1a and 1b. Recommendations R3 and R5 specifically call for using regional study results to advance policy and conducting new regional studies on evidence production. For a synthesis specialist, the conclusions section (particularly C1, C4, C8, C9) and the recommendations (R3, R5) would appear most valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional relevant evidence on data gaps and capacity-building outcomes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “STRENGTHENING THE EVIDENCE BASE ON MIGRATION, ENVIRONMENT AND CLIMATE CHANGE TO ENHANCE IOM'S WORK IN SOUTH AMERICA”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its climate change and environmental degradation sub-theme (Actions 2h, 2i, 2k, 2l). The project is explicitly focused on migration, environment and climate change (MECC) in South America — a region described as 'highly vulnerable to the impacts of climate change, disasters and environmental degradation.' The excerpts suggest the project examined how environmental factors drive human mobility, which is directly relevant to Actions 2h (joint analysis to understand migration movements resulting from natural disasters and climate change) and 2i (adaptation and resilience strategies). The project's engagement with the Sendai Framework and the Task Force on Displacement (referenced in the context section) further connects to Actions 2j and 2l. However, the project's primary focus appears to be on data and evidence production rather than on directly addressing the drivers themselves or implementing adaptation strategies — making this RELEVANT rather than FUNDAMENTAL. The context and background sections (pages 8-9) and conclusion C1 would be most useful for a synthesis specialist to review, as they outline the environmental mobility dynamics in South America and the policy gaps the project sought to address.

References.Title: Bridging the Gap: Optimizing the Contribution of Labour Migration for Thailand’s Social and Economic Transformation
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful and substantive evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 5. The report appears to extensively examine the MoU (Memorandum of Understanding) process as the primary regular migration pathway for Myanmar workers into Thailand, analyzing its limitations, rigidities, and misalignment with employer needs — directly relevant to Actions 5c (review and revise existing options), 5d (flexible, rights-based labour mobility schemes), and 5e (effective skills matching). The excerpts suggest dedicated analysis of how the MoU process fails to accommodate MSME demand patterns (time-sensitivity, short-term contracts), and recommendations explicitly call for 'express' work permit processes, extended permit durations, cross-border and seasonal work permit mechanisms, and reduced restrictions on changing employers. The sections on 'Labour Migration Processes and Employer Perceptions' and the recommendations chapter appear particularly relevant for human reviewers. The report also touches on Action 5b through its discussion of bilateral MoU frameworks with Myanmar, Cambodia and Lao PDR. While the report is primarily a labour market study rather than a policy evaluation per se, the depth of analysis on regular migration pathway design and flexibility makes it a strong candidate for a synthesis on Objective 5.

References.Title: Bridging the Gap: Optimizing the Contribution of Labour Migration for Thailand’s Social and Economic Transformation
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute relevant evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 6. The report appears to examine recruitment practices for Myanmar migrant workers through both regular and irregular channels, analyzing the roles of licensed recruitment agencies versus unlicensed brokers, and the conditions under which each operates — relevant to Actions 6c (regulation of recruitment agencies) and 6g (allowing migrants to change employers). The Royal Ordinance discussion touches on Actions 6d (written contracts in mother tongue), 6h (prohibition on document confiscation), and 6i (equal labour rights). The report also discusses informality in recruitment and the shrinking market for licensed agents. The sections on 'Labour Migration Processes and Employer Perceptions' and the glossary entry on the Royal Ordinance appear most relevant. However, fair recruitment and decent work are not the central analytical focus — the report is primarily about economic contribution and governance efficiency — so this is a secondary rather than primary theme. Human reviewers should note that the report's perspective is predominantly employer-facing rather than worker-rights-focused.

References.Title: Bridging the Gap: Optimizing the Contribution of Labour Migration for Thailand’s Social and Economic Transformation
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 18. Skills development and recognition appear to be a primary analytical theme throughout the report, with dedicated sections and multiple recommendations directly addressing this objective.
