The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: the mismatch between current MoU processes (which restrict migrants to 'manual labour') and the need for skills qualification frameworks (Actions 18a, 18b); employer-led technical training practices and their limitations (Action 18e, 18f); recommendations to develop standardized curricula and training-of-trainers programmes (Action 18e); promotion of mutual recognition of skills with countries of origin (Action 18c); government subsidies for skills development targeting migrant workers (Action 18h); and the need for regular labour market assessments to identify skills demand (Action 18k). The recommendations chapter explicitly addresses skills matching integration into MoU processes, demand-driven technical skills investment, and bilateral skills recognition. The sections on 'Leveraging Labour Migration to Facilitate Sustainable Economic Growth' and the recommendations chapter appear most relevant for human reviewers. The report's focus on upskilling in the context of Thailand's economic transformation (Thailand 4.0, automation) provides additional depth on future skills demand relevant to Action 18e and 18h.

References.Title: Bridging the Gap: Optimizing the Contribution of Labour Migration for Thailand’s Social and Economic Transformation
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute relevant evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The report's central analytical focus on the socioeconomic contributions of migrant workers to Thailand's GDP (up to 6.6%), their role across economic sectors, and their importance for MSME growth directly addresses Action 19a (implementing the 2030 Agenda through migration's positive effects) and Action 19c (research on non-financial contributions of migrants). The report also examines how labour migration governance can be reformed to better enable migrants' development contributions — relevant to Action 19b (integrating migration into development planning). The executive summary and conclusions sections appear most relevant. However, the report focuses exclusively on destination country contributions and does not address diaspora engagement, remittances, or contributions to countries of origin, limiting its coverage of Objective 19's full scope. Human reviewers should note that the report's value for this objective lies primarily in its macro-level economic analysis of migrant contributions to Thailand's development trajectory.

References.Title: RAPPORT EVALUATION INTERMÉDIAIRE DU PROJET IDEE
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears highly relevant for a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly Action 2e (investing in human capital development, entrepreneurship, vocational training, and youth employment to reduce youth unemployment and avoid brain drain) and Action 2d (investing in sustainable development to create conducive conditions for communities). The IDEE project is explicitly designed to address structural drivers of irregular migration — poverty, youth unemployment, and lack of economic opportunity in Niger — by supporting entrepreneurship and job creation as alternatives to irregular migration. The project's second specific objective directly frames entrepreneurship support as 'an alternative to irregular migration.' The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how entrepreneurship support, skills training, microcredit, and business incubation contribute to economic inclusion of youth. The context section provides detailed analysis of Niger's socioeconomic conditions (poverty, youth unemployment, weak private sector) as structural drivers. The conclusions and recommendations sections appear particularly relevant for a synthesis specialist, as they assess the effectiveness of integrated entrepreneurship support in addressing these structural factors. The evaluation's findings on business revenue growth, employment creation, and capacity building would likely contribute meaningful evidence on Action 2e specifically. Note that the full report likely contains more depth on this theme than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN DEL PROGRAMA CONJUNTO DE PAZ PARA GENERACIÓN POST CONFLICTO PROYECTO NO.00100920
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be highly valuable for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. One of the program's three core results is explicitly dedicated to strengthening institutional mechanisms for the reception and reintegration of returned migrants (Resultado 2, managed by OIM). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: psychosocial support for returnees as a precondition for reintegration (lessons learned); entrepreneurship training and seed capital for returnees (conclusions and recommendations); strategic alliances with municipalities to reach returnees in their communities; and the challenges of sustainable reintegration beyond the 18-month program timeframe. These relate directly to Actions 21b (gender-responsive return and reintegration programs), 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through social protection, vocational training, employment, and financial services), and 21i (addressing needs of communities to which migrants return). The recommendations sections on microfinance access, entrepreneurship networks, labor intermediation, and anti-stigma campaigns appear particularly relevant for human reviewers to prioritize. The report also appears to offer evidence on what works and what doesn't in short-term reintegration programming — a valuable contribution to synthesis evidence. It is possible the full report contains additional depth on return cooperation mechanisms beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: THEMATIC EVALUATION OF IOM'S LABOUR MIGRATION AND MOBILITY STRATEGY AND INITIATIVES
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be highly valuable for a synthesis on GCM Objective 6. Ethical recruitment appears as one of the LMI division's explicit strategic focus areas ('Migration, Business & Human Rights: Promoting ethical recruitment and protection of migrant workers'), and the report identifies IOM as having 'strong impact' in ethical recruitment specifically. The report references IOM's IRIS (International Recruitment Integrity System) initiative directly, which aligns with GCM action 6l. The evaluation appears to assess IOM's role in shaping standards and partnerships with governments, private sector, and employers — relevant to actions 6c, 6d, 6e, and 6f. The conclusions section explicitly highlights ethical recruitment as an area of strong impact, and the Theory of Change includes outputs on 'supporting ethical recruitment and bridge between employers and the supply of workers.' For a synthesis specialist, the sections on LMI strategic focus areas, the conclusions table on impact achievements, and the Theory of Change outputs would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The full report likely contains more detailed programmatic evidence on ethical recruitment beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: THEMATIC EVALUATION OF IOM'S LABOUR MIGRATION AND MOBILITY STRATEGY AND INITIATIVES
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. Social inclusion and cohesion appear as explicit strategic focus areas of the LMI division ('Integration, Social Inclusion and Cohesion: Aiming to develop migrants, communities and governments capacities to achieve full inclusion and social cohesion across the migration continuum'). The report discusses IOM's efforts to empower migrants as development agents, amplify migrants' voices, and foster social cohesion — aligning with actions 16a, 16c, 16d, and 16f. The evaluation assesses IOM's effectiveness in migrant inclusion and integration, with the conclusions table identifying 'migrant inclusion/integration' as an area of strong impact. The Theory of Change includes outputs on 'protection-focused, social programming' and 'fiscal inclusion of diversity.' For a synthesis specialist, the sections on LMI strategic focus areas, the conclusions on effectiveness and impact in inclusion/integration, and the recommendations on cross-cutting themes and community engagement would appear particularly valuable. The full report likely contains more programmatic detail on social inclusion initiatives beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: THEMATIC EVALUATION OF IOM'S LABOUR MIGRATION AND MOBILITY STRATEGY AND INITIATIVES
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be highly valuable for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. Diaspora engagement appears as one of the LMI division's six explicit strategic focus areas, and the report identifies IOM as having played a 'leading role' in diaspora engagement — including organizing the first Global Diaspora Summit and Global Diaspora Exchanges. The report discusses IOM's work on 'transnational communities and diasporas,' migrant empowerment as development agents, and financial/economic empowerment — aligning with actions 19c, 19d, 19e, and 19j. The Diaspora Mapping Toolkit is highlighted as a good practice example relevant to action 19j. The conclusions table identifies 'diaspora engagement' as an area of strong impact, and the Theory of Change includes outputs on diaspora-related programming. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on LMI strategic focus areas (particularly diaspora engagement and financial/economic empowerment), the conclusions on impact, and the recommendations on community engagement and diaspora collaboration would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The full report likely contains more detailed programmatic evidence on diaspora initiatives beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Evaluability Assessment of the Cooperation on Migration and Partnership to Achieve Sustainable Solutions (COMPASS)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The evaluability assessment appears to substantively engage with data collection, disaggregation, and M&E system quality as core analytical themes. The excerpts suggest dedicated analysis of indicator design, data disaggregation by sex and age, metadata for indicators, and the adequacy of M&E tools and frameworks — all closely related to Actions 1a, 1c, and 1j. Key finding 2 and Conclusion 2 explicitly address measurement frameworks, indicator quality, and the need for disaggregated targets. Key finding 5 and Conclusion 5 assess the M&E system's capacity to produce reliable data. Recommendation 1 calls for developing a metadata database and prioritizing indicators, while Recommendation 3 specifically addresses sex-disaggregated data. However, the report's primary focus is on evaluability (readiness to be evaluated) rather than on migration data systems per se, and the data-related content is instrumental to the evaluation design rather than a standalone policy focus. Synthesis specialists working on GCM Objective 1 would likely find the sections on indicator assessment (Key Finding 2, Conclusion 2), M&E system design (Key Finding 5, Conclusion 5), and Recommendations 1 and 3 particularly worth reviewing as pointers for further analysis.

References.Title: Evaluability Assessment of the Cooperation on Migration and Partnership to Achieve Sustainable Solutions (COMPASS)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. Protection and assistance of vulnerable migrants appears to be a central pillar of COMPASS (Outcome 1: 'Migrants and their families are protected, assisted and empowered to pursue sustainable resolution of vulnerabilities and reintegration'). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of protection-related M&E challenges, gender-responsive approaches, and assistance to migrants in vulnerable situations — relating to Actions 7a, 7b, 7c, and 7l. Key Finding 4 and Conclusion 4 address gender-responsive approaches and the needs of women and girls. Key Finding 6 and Conclusion 6 discuss challenges in measuring user wellbeing for protection-related activities. Recommendation 2 calls for better follow-up on protection activities and user wellbeing indicators. However, the report's focus is on evaluability design rather than direct programme outcomes, which limits the depth of evidence on vulnerability responses per se. Synthesis specialists would likely find the sections on protection M&E challenges (Key Finding 6, Conclusion 6), gender-responsive approaches (Key Finding 4, Conclusion 4), and Recommendation 2 most relevant as starting points for deeper review.

References.Title: Evaluability Assessment of the Cooperation on Migration and Partnership to Achieve Sustainable Solutions (COMPASS)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Return and reintegration (R&R) appears to be a central and explicit focus of COMPASS, with Outcome 1 directly addressing protection, assistance, and reintegration of migrants, and Outcome 3 referencing 'promoting safe and dignified return and sustainable reintegration.' The AVRR (Assisted Voluntary Return and Reintegration) M&E framework is explicitly referenced as the primary framework to which COMPASS links its outcome-level indicators. The ecological approach — operating at individual, community, and structural levels — appears designed specifically to support sustainable reintegration outcomes. Key Finding 6 and Conclusion 6 discuss M&E challenges for AVRR-related indicators and wellbeing measurement. Key Finding 2 and Conclusion 2 address measurement of reintegration outcomes. Recommendation 1 includes specific indicators for reintegration sustainability (e.g., 'sustainability of economic initiatives led by returnees'). Recommendation 3 addresses gender-responsive R&R approaches. These themes closely align with Actions 21b (gender-responsive return and reintegration programmes), 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration), and 21i (addressing needs of return communities). Synthesis specialists working on GCM Objective 21 would likely find the sections on AVRR M&E framework linkages (Key Finding 6, Conclusion 6), reintegration outcome measurement (Key Finding 2, Recommendation 1), and the ecological approach to reintegration (Section 2.2, Conclusion 1) particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional evidence on return and reintegration programming beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE WORLD MIGRATION REPORT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The World Migration Report (WMR) is explicitly described as a vehicle for providing 'migration-related statistics' and 'evidence-based analysis,' and the evaluation directly assesses whether the WMR has succeeded in meeting 'a growing need for high quality, balanced research and analysis on migration.' The excerpts indicate that the WMR 2020 presents 'key information on migration and migrants (including migration-related statistics)' and that IOM's role in 'data collection and analysis' is a stated rationale for the report's evolution. The evaluation also examines the WMR's utility as a global reference point for migration research, touching on actions 1a (comprehensive strategy for improving migration data), 1b (international comparability of migration statistics), and 1e (collaboration between global databases). The conclusions and recommendations sections appear most relevant for human reviewers, particularly the discussion of the WMR as a 'primary reference point for migration research' and the analysis of its evidence base. However, the report is an evaluation of a publication process rather than a direct assessment of data collection systems, so its contribution to a synthesis on Objective 1 would be indirect — focused on how a flagship publication aggregates and disseminates migration data rather than on data collection mechanisms per se.

References.Title: INTERNAL INDEPENDENT FINAL EVALUATION OF PROGRAMME MIGRATION FOR DEVELOPMENT II - SUPPORTING THE ALBANIAN INSTITUTE OF STATISTICS (INSTAT) IN CONDUCTING A NATIONAL HOUSEHOLD MIGRATION SURVEY (HMS)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project evaluated is explicitly and centrally about improving migration data collection in Albania — the project title itself references conducting a National Household Migration Survey (HMS). The excerpts suggest that virtually all project activities, outputs, and outcomes directly address actions under Objective 1. Specifically: Action 1h (household surveys to collect information on migrants) appears to be the core activity of the project; Action 1g (integrating migration topics in national censuses) is touched upon through INSTAT's enhanced capacity benefiting the national census; Action 1i (collaboration between migration data units and national statistical offices) is a central feature of the IOM-INSTAT partnership; Action 1a (harmonizing methodologies) appears relevant given references to Eurostat alignment and standardization of definitions; Action 1c (building national capacities in data collection) is explicitly addressed through trainings, equipment procurement, and technical support to INSTAT; and Action 1j (country-specific migration profiles with disaggregated data) aligns with the HMS outputs. The evaluation questions, conclusions, and recommendations all revolve around data quality, capacity building, disaggregation by sex, and evidence-based policymaking. The executive summary, conclusions on relevance/effectiveness/sustainability, and lessons learned sections would all appear particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist. The innovative methodology for estimating emigrated households and the gender-disaggregated analysis approach are also noteworthy contributions.

References.Title: INTERNAL INDEPENDENT FINAL EVALUATION OF PROGRAMME MIGRATION FOR DEVELOPMENT II - SUPPORTING THE ALBANIAN INSTITUTE OF STATISTICS (INSTAT) IN CONDUCTING A NATIONAL HOUSEHOLD MIGRATION SURVEY (HMS)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly regarding sustainable reintegration of returnees (Action 21h and 21i). The project explicitly includes qualitative research (12 focus groups with returned migrants and KIIs in communities receiving high proportions of returnees) examining reintegration experiences. The recommendations section contains substantive content on reintegration challenges: registration of returnees at points of entry, strengthening local capacities for returnee services, employment and skills development for returnees, school reintegration for returnee children, housing and healthcare support, and identifying community needs. These align with Actions 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through social protection, employment, psycho-social assistance) and 21i (addressing needs of communities to which migrants return). However, the project is primarily a data collection exercise, and the reintegration content derives from survey findings rather than evaluation of a reintegration programme per se. The qualitative research sections (FGs and KIIs) and the recommendations on follow-up action (Section 5.2, Recommendation 1) would appear most relevant for a synthesis specialist to review, with the caveat that the analytical depth on reintegration programming is secondary to the data methodology focus.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION REPORT "CAPACITY-BUILDING IN INTEGRATED BORDER MANAGEMENT IN SOUTHERN MOZAMBIQUE" (TC.0861)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The project title itself — 'Capacity-Building in Integrated Border Management in Southern Mozambique' — directly mirrors the language of this objective, and the entire report is structured around evaluating an integrated border management (IBM) initiative. The excerpts suggest that multiple findings and conclusions directly address core themes of Objective 11: inter-agency coordination at borders (Actions 11a, 11b), development of standard operating procedures for border management (Action 11c), procurement of border management equipment (Action 11d), training of border officials (Action 11a, 11e), and the challenges of implementing a whole-of-government approach. The Theory of Change explicitly frames the project around 'adopting an integrated border management approach, reflected in SOPs and more effective coordination and cooperation among all the relevant border authorities.' The evaluation covers relevance, efficiency, effectiveness, impact, and sustainability of IBM capacity-building — providing rich evidence on what works and what doesn't in this context. For a synthesis specialist, the Key Findings section (Section 3), the Project Evaluation section (Section 4, particularly on effectiveness and sustainability), the results matrix, and the conclusions and lessons learned (Section 5) would all appear highly valuable. The report also offers evidence on the challenges of political engagement, multi-agency coordination, and SOP development in a fragile context — all directly relevant to Action 11a and 11b. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Meta-evaluation of IOM  Development Fund projects  (funded 2016-2021)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. Several of the 22 evaluated projects appear directly concerned with migration data collection, analysis, and dissemination — most notably PR.0234 (Georgia: Capacity Building of Georgian Authorities in Migration Data Analysis), PR.0206 (Tunisia: Support to the National Observatory for Migration), and PR.0222 (Senegal: Strengthening the Capacities of Migration Research Institutions). The meta-evaluation's discussion of project design quality — including issues with indicators, baselines, results matrices, and monitoring — also touches on data quality and measurement themes relevant to Actions 1c and 1j. The excerpts suggest that findings on these projects address institutional capacity for migration data, which aligns with Actions 1c (building national capacities) and 1f (regional centres/observatories). Additionally, Action 1j (country-specific migration profiles) appears directly relevant to PR.0163 (Panama: Migration Profile). For a synthesis specialist, the sections on effectiveness and sustainability for these specific projects, as well as the project design recommendations, would appear most worth reviewing. The meta-evaluation's cross-cutting nature means data-focused content is distributed rather than concentrated, but the coverage appears substantive enough to warrant review.

References.Title: Meta-evaluation of IOM  Development Fund projects  (funded 2016-2021)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 6. The project LM.0333 (Jamaica: Promoting Integrity in International Recruitment and Migrant Skill Development) appears directly relevant to Actions 6c, 6d, and 6l on fair and ethical recruitment. The Nepal project (LM.0321) on Migrant Resource Centres also likely addresses pre-departure information and worker protection relevant to Actions 6d and 6g. The meta-evaluation's findings on effectiveness, sustainability, and project design for these labour migration projects would appear to offer evidence on what works in fair recruitment programming. For a synthesis specialist, the effectiveness and sustainability sections covering LM.0333 and LM.0321 would be the most relevant starting points. The coverage is real but limited to a subset of the 22 projects, so this is a contributing rather than central source for Objective 6.

References.Title: Meta-evaluation of IOM  Development Fund projects  (funded 2016-2021)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. Multiple projects appear directly focused on integrated border management: IB.0030 (Bosnia and Herzegovina: Strengthening Government Capacities in Integrated Border Management), TC.1056 (Eastern and Southern Africa: Promoting a Regional Corridor Approach to Coordinated/Integrated Border Management), IB.0049 (Benin: Support to Migration Management), and MP.0346 (Burundi and DRC: Health and Humanitarian Border Management). These projects appear to address Actions 11a (international cooperation on border management), 11b (integrated border management structures), and 11d (technical cooperation). The meta-evaluation's findings on effectiveness, efficiency, and sustainability for these projects — including the notable example of USD 1.5 million follow-on funding for Benin — would likely offer substantive evidence on border management capacity-building outcomes. For a synthesis specialist, the effectiveness and sustainability sections covering these border management projects, as well as the project design recommendations, would appear most valuable as starting points for deeper review.

References.Title: Meta-evaluation of IOM  Development Fund projects  (funded 2016-2021)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. Multiple projects appear directly focused on diaspora engagement and migration-development linkages: CD.0003 (Lesotho: Enhancing Coordination and Strengthening Institutional Capacity to Engage with Basotho Diaspora), CD.0012 (Malawi: Mapping and Profiling Malawian Diaspora), CE.0392 (Afghanistan: Mapping Exercise to Support National Diaspora Policy), CE.0353 (Côte d'Ivoire: Developing an Online Tool to Map the Ivoirian Diaspora), CE.0409 (Armenia: Enhancing Development through Diaspora Engagement), and PO.0125 (Brazil: Enhancing Migration Governance). These projects appear to address Actions 19d (diaspora offices and mechanisms), 19j (mapping diasporas and skills transfer), and 19b (integrating migration into development planning). The meta-evaluation's findings on effectiveness, sustainability, and project design for these diaspora projects — including the Lesotho diaspora policy with allocated government budget — would likely offer substantive evidence on diaspora engagement programming. For a synthesis specialist, the sustainability section (particularly the Lesotho good practice example), the effectiveness findings for diaspora projects, and the project design recommendations would appear most valuable as starting points. The concentration of diaspora-focused projects makes this a potentially strong source for Objective 19 evidence synthesis.

References.Title: Finale Internal Evluation: ENHANCING THE CAPACITY TO MAINSTREAM ENVIRONMENT AND CLIMATE CHANGE WITHIN WIDER FRAMEWORK OF MIGRATION MANAGEMENT IN WEST AND CENTRAL AFRICA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its actions related to climate change, environmental degradation, and disaster risk reduction (actions 2h, 2i, 2k, 2l). The entire project evaluated — 'Enhancing the Capacity to Mainstream Environment and Climate Change within Wider Framework of Migration Management in West and Central Africa' — is explicitly focused on the migration-environment-climate change (MECC) nexus, which is the core subject of Objective 2's natural disaster and climate change provisions. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: IOM's capacity to address environmental drivers of migration in the WCA region (one of the world's most climate-vulnerable areas); support to national disaster risk reduction strategies (Senegal's National Risk Prevention Strategy via CADRI); climate resilience programming for vulnerable communities; and the linkage between slow-onset and sudden environmental events and migration patterns. The Relevance section (Finding 1, paragraphs 1.1-1.5) and the Effectiveness section (Findings 7-9, paragraphs 3.1-3.10) appear particularly valuable for human reviewers, as they contain substantive discussion of how environmental degradation drives migration and what institutional responses look like. The Sustainability section also touches on the need for a multi-year MECC roadmap, relevant to long-term structural responses. The report also references alignment with SDGs #1, #13, #14, #15 and the Paris Agreement, further anchoring it to Objective 2's framework. A synthesis specialist examining evidence on how institutions build capacity to address environmental drivers of migration would likely find this report highly relevant.

References.Title: FORTALECIENDO EL ANALISIS DE POLITICAS PUBLICAS SOBRE MIGRACION LABORAL BASADO EN EVIDENCIAS EN REPÚBLICA DOMINICANA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to have meaningful relevance for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project's core purpose was explicitly to strengthen evidence-based migration policy through knowledge generation — the project title itself references 'evidence-based public policy analysis on labor migration.' The excerpts suggest the INM's role in producing migration research and knowledge products was a central evaluation focus, relating to actions 1c (building national capacities in data collection and analysis) and 1d (collecting data on migration effects and contributions of migrants and diasporas to sustainable development). The executive summary notes the project supported INM in 'generating evidence-based knowledge for migration management,' and the theory of change section describes how research products were intended to inform policy. The report also references production of studies on diaspora contributions and labor migration — touching on action 1k (research on migration and sustainable development interrelationship). However, the report appears to focus more on institutional capacity and policy uptake than on data systems or statistical methodology per se, which limits its depth on some core Objective 1 actions (e.g., 1a, 1b, 1g). Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on INM capacity strengthening and the knowledge products generated, as well as the sustainability and impact sections, as pointers for further review. The full report may contain more detail on specific data products than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FORTALECIENDO EL ANALISIS DE POLITICAS PUBLICAS SOBRE MIGRACION LABORAL BASADO EN EVIDENCIAS EN REPÚBLICA DOMINICANA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The diaspora-development nexus is a central and explicitly analyzed theme throughout the report. The FIG-DOMEX (Fondo Internacional de Garantía para Dominicanos en el Exterior) study — a proposed financial guarantee mechanism to channel diaspora savings and credit toward investment in the Dominican Republic — directly addresses actions 19d (establishing government mechanisms for diaspora engagement) and 19e (developing targeted financial products for diaspora investment and entrepreneurship). The project also appears to have contributed to shifting government perceptions of the diaspora's role in development (impact section), and the INDEX institution is referenced as a relevant actor. The south-south exchange with Chilean mayors on local diaspora governance relates to action 19j (building partnerships between local authorities and diaspora organizations). The sustainability section notes ongoing government agenda items on diaspora integration in local development. The executive summary, impact section, and recommendations sections all appear particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist to review — especially the analysis of the FIG-DOMEX mechanism, the role of municipalities in diaspora engagement, and the recommendations on follow-up studies. The full report likely contains more detailed analysis of these mechanisms than the excerpts reveal, making it a high-priority document for synthesis on Objective 19.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "IMPROVING INFORMATION MANAGEMENT AND PLANNING CAPACITIES OF SERBIAN COMMISSARIAT FOR REFUGEES AND MIGRATION"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project's core purpose is explicitly the development and upgrading of an information management system (SCRM IMS/SMART software) for migration data collection, analysis and reporting. The excerpts suggest that multiple evaluation sections directly address data collection systems, disaggregated data entry (including gender-disaggregated data), data management capacity building, and the integration of databases — all closely aligned with Actions 1a (comprehensive data strategy), 1c (building national capacities in data collection), and 1i (using administrative records for statistical purposes). The project title itself references 'Information Management and Planning Capacities,' and the evaluation questions on effectiveness, sustainability and impact all center on the functionality and use of the data system. Sections on Effectiveness (4.2), Sustainability (4.5), and the Conclusions appear particularly valuable for human reviewers, as they assess whether the data management system achieved its goals, whether it is institutionally embedded, and what lessons emerge for future phases. The report also touches on data disaggregation by gender and vulnerability categories in the Gender and Human Rights sections. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF ONE ROOM SHELTER PROGRAMME FOR THE 2011 FLOODS RESPONSE IN SOUTH SINDH, PAKISTAN
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly around disaster risk reduction, resilience, and the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (Action 2a, 2b, 2i, 2j). The report evaluates a flood shelter programme explicitly designed with 'build back better' and DRR principles in response to a natural disaster that displaced millions. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how shelter construction with DRR features (raised plinths, lime stabilization, improved roofs) can reduce vulnerability to future floods — directly addressing structural factors that could compel displacement. The executive summary, conclusions (Section 5.4.5 on DRR impact), and recommendations (Sections 6.1.1, 6.3.1, 6.3.2) appear particularly relevant, discussing community resilience, replication of DRR practices, and integration into national disaster risk reduction policy. The strategic recommendation to include ORS within Pakistan's National DRR Policy (6.1.1) and the discussion of on-going programming between flood responses (6.1.3) would likely add value to a synthesis on how humanitarian shelter programmes can address structural drivers of displacement. Specialists would want to review the conclusions on DRR impact and the strategic recommendations, noting that the report focuses on internal displacement/shelter rather than cross-border migration per se.

References.Title: FINAL INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT: IMPROVING BORDER CAPACITY MANAGEMENT IN EMERGENCY RESPONSE TO IRREGULAR MIGRATION FLOWS IN DJIBOUTI
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The project title itself — 'Improving border capacity management in emergency response to irregular migration flows in Djibouti' — directly mirrors the language of this objective. The evaluation appears to comprehensively assess a border management capacity-building initiative, with the project's outcome explicitly framed as improving the national police's ability to 'effectively protect and manage the national borders in line with migrants' human rights.' This dual framing of security and human rights aligns closely with GCM Objective 11's core commitment. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple associated actions: Action 11a (international/regional border management cooperation, capacity-building, identification and referral of vulnerable migrants) appears addressed through the HBM training component and IOM's technical assistance role; Action 11b (integrated border management structures and procedures) is addressed through the operationalization of the Yoboki Patrolling Brigade and equipment provision; Action 11d (technical cooperation agreements for assets and equipment) is directly illustrated by the vehicle, night vision glasses, tents, and first aid kits provided; Action 11g (cross-border collaboration on treatment of persons crossing borders) is touched upon through the HBM framework. The evaluation's findings on the gap between border security priorities and human rights-based approaches (section 4.1.1), the effectiveness assessment of training and equipment provision (section 3.3), efficiency analysis (section 3.4), and sustainability concerns (section 3.5 and 4.1.5) would all appear particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist. The recommendations — especially Recommendations 1, 3, and 4 — offer practical insights on implementation challenges in border management capacity building. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT: IMPROVING BORDER CAPACITY MANAGEMENT IN EMERGENCY RESPONSE TO IRREGULAR MIGRATION FLOWS IN DJIBOUTI

The project includes Output 1.1 on training border officials on 'humanitarian Border Management and migrants vulnerability screening,' and activity 1.1.3 specifically concerns the 'Development of operational manual for the screening of vulnerable migrants passing through Yoboki' — directly relevant to Action 12c (gender-responsive and child-sensitive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures). The evaluation notes that a screening tool was developed and provided to border posts, though the full operational manual was not completed due to COVID-19. The HBM training component relates to Action 12b (specialized human rights trainings for border officials and first responders). However, screening and referral appear as one component within a broader border management project rather than the central focus. The sections on Output 1.1 effectiveness (section 3.3) and the analysis of HBM training content and reach (section 4.1.2) would be most relevant for a synthesis specialist to review, with the caveat that depth of analysis on screening procedures specifically appears limited in the excerpts accessed.

References.Title: ÉVALUATION EXTERNE FINALE DU PROJET IDEE– INITIATIVE POUR LE DÉVELOPPEMENT DE L’ENTREPRISE- DANS LES VILLES DE NIAMEY, DE TAHOUA ET DE ZINDER AU NIGER.

The project IDEE is explicitly framed as an intervention to reduce irregular migration by addressing its root causes — unemployment and lack of economic opportunity among youth in Niger. The excerpts state clearly that 'le manque d'emploi est un facteur qui conduit beaucoup de jeunes à la migration' and that the project aims to 'mitiger le risque de la migration irrégulière vers les pays industrialisés à travers la lutte contre la pauvreté et le chômage.' The evaluation assesses outcomes including approximately 426 jobs created, enterprise viability, and entrepreneurship capacity building. For a synthesis specialist, the conclusions section (Section 4) and the discussion of employment creation outcomes, as well as the recommendations table (Tableau 10), would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The report also touches on Action 2b through its focus on inclusive economic growth and youth empowerment. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence on the migration-development nexus beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: MID-TERM EVALUATION REPORT OF THE PROJECT “CONTRIBUTE TO THE ECONOMIC RECOVERY OF IRAQ THROUGH EMPLOYMENT CREATION AND REVITALIZATION OF LOCAL ECONOMIES”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its associated actions on employment creation, economic growth, and addressing structural factors that compel displacement. The project explicitly targets conflict-affected communities in Iraq with livelihood support, job creation, and economic revitalization — directly addressing the structural drivers of displacement and irregular migration. The context section describes how lack of income and employment is a key obstacle to return and durable solutions, which aligns closely with Action 2b (investing in programmes that address adverse drivers including employment creation and inclusive economic growth) and Action 2e (investing in human capital development through vocational training, skills development, and productive employment creation). The evaluation's findings on income increases (135% for CfW beneficiaries, 89% for ILA beneficiaries), business profit growth, and skills retention (85% of trainees using skills) appear to provide substantive evidence on the effectiveness of such interventions. The sustainability section also addresses how community engagement and economic empowerment contribute to durable solutions. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the effectiveness and impact sections, as well as the conclusions on sustainability, as these appear most directly relevant to Actions 2b, 2d, and 2e. The report's focus on returnees and IDPs in a post-conflict recovery context adds particular value for understanding how economic interventions can address structural drivers of displacement.

References.Title: MID-TERM EVALUATION REPORT OF THE PROJECT “CONTRIBUTE TO THE ECONOMIC RECOVERY OF IRAQ THROUGH EMPLOYMENT CREATION AND REVITALIZATION OF LOCAL ECONOMIES”

The project includes vocational training, farming training, on-the-job training, and job placement as components of Individual Livelihood Assistance (ILA), and the evaluation assesses the effectiveness of these interventions. The finding that 85% of training beneficiaries reported using their newly acquired skills, with potential for improved income generation beyond the project period, appears directly relevant to Action 18e (building global skills partnerships) and Action 18g (skills development and mobility programmes). The evaluation also discusses labour market assessments used to align training with local market needs, which relates to Action 18b (skills profiling tools) and Action 18h (demand-driven skills development). The business development training services (BDS) provided through EDF also touch on entrepreneurship skills development. However, the report does not appear to address mutual recognition of qualifications, cross-border skills portability, or formal credentialing systems — limiting its relevance to the skills development rather than recognition dimensions of Objective 18. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the effectiveness section on ILA outcomes and the sustainability section on skills retention, as these appear most directly relevant to Actions 18e, 18g, and 18h.

References.Title: MID-TERM EVALUATION REPORT OF THE PROJECT “CONTRIBUTE TO THE ECONOMIC RECOVERY OF IRAQ THROUGH EMPLOYMENT CREATION AND REVITALIZATION OF LOCAL ECONOMIES”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 19, particularly on the contributions of returnees and IDPs to local sustainable development through economic participation. The project's explicit aim — revitalizing local economies, creating employment, and supporting business development — aligns closely with Action 19e (targeted support programmes for migrant and diaspora investments and entrepreneurship) and Action 19b (integrating migration into development planning). The evaluation's impact data — including profit increases for EDF businesses (55%), income increases for ILA beneficiaries (89%), and employment rate improvements — appears to provide substantive evidence on how economic empowerment programmes can enable displaced persons to contribute to local development. The sustainability section discusses how community engagement and business formalization (registration with chambers of commerce) contribute to longer-term development outcomes. The project's design explicitly links individual economic empowerment to community-level economic revitalization, which is central to Objective 19's framing. The context section also frames the intervention within Iraq's post-conflict recovery and return dynamics, relevant to Action 19b. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the impact section, the sustainability/connectedness section, and the conclusions on economic revitalization outcomes as starting points for deeper review. It should be noted that the report focuses on returnees/IDPs rather than diaspora in the traditional sense, which may limit direct applicability to some Objective 19 actions.

References.Title: MID-TERM EVALUATION REPORT OF THE PROJECT “CONTRIBUTE TO THE ECONOMIC RECOVERY OF IRAQ THROUGH EMPLOYMENT CREATION AND REVITALIZATION OF LOCAL ECONOMIES”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute substantial evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly on sustainable reintegration of returnees. The project explicitly targets returnees and IDPs in post-conflict Iraq, with the stated goal of creating durable solutions that allow returnees to come back and stay. The evaluation's findings on income generation, employment, business development, and community stability appear directly relevant to Action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through vocational training, employment opportunities, decent work, and financial services) and Action 21i (identifying and addressing needs of communities to which migrants return). The sustainability section provides evidence on reintegration outcomes: beneficiaries planning to stay in their communities, confidence in covering family expenses, expanded social networks, and business formalization. The impact data comparing beneficiaries and non-beneficiaries in the same communities appears particularly valuable for understanding the effectiveness of reintegration support. The context section explicitly frames the intervention around enabling durable return solutions, noting that lack of income and employment is a key obstacle to sustainable return. The community engagement approach and local authority coordination also relate to Action 21i. Synthesis specialists would likely want to prioritize the impact section, sustainability/connectedness section, and the context section on return dynamics, as these appear most directly relevant to Actions 21h and 21i. The report's focus on economic reintegration rather than legal/procedural aspects of return limits its relevance to some Objective 21 actions (e.g., 21a-21g), but for the reintegration dimension it appears to be a strong source.

References.Title: “UPHOLDING THE RIGHTS OF MIGRANT WORKERS IN SPECIAL ECONOMIC ZONES (SEZS) BY ENSURING SUPPLY CHAINS ARE EXPLOITATION FREE: VIET NAM, CAMBODIA AND LAO PEOPLE’S DEMOCRATIC REPUBLIC”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 6. Ethical recruitment is the explicit and central subject of the evaluated project — appearing in the project title, stated outcomes, and throughout the executive summary and conclusions. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: recruitment agency regulation and the IRIS framework (Action 6l); promotion of ethical recruitment principles among private sector actors including MNCs and SMEs (Actions 6c, 6e); supply chain transparency and corporate social responsibility (Action 6e); enforcement of fair recruitment norms through capacity-building of public institutions (Action 6f); and the CREST framework as a tool for operationalizing ethical recruitment. The report appears to assess what works in promoting ethical recruitment in a Southeast Asian SEZ context, including multi-stakeholder approaches, the 'win-win' framing for private sector engagement, and training of recruitment agencies. For a synthesis specialist, the executive summary's discussion of CREST outcomes, the conclusions on private sector engagement strategies, and the recommendations on NGO engagement and awareness-raising would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The full report likely contains additional depth on these themes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EX-POST PROJECT EVALUATION REPORT "ENHANCING MIGRATION MANAGEMENT IN BELIZE THROUGH AN UPGRADE OF THE MIGRATION INFORMATION DATA ANALYSIS SYSTEM (MIDAS)"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The MIDAS upgrade is fundamentally a data collection and analysis system for migration, and the excerpts suggest substantive discussion of how the system generates data used for policy-making. Specifically, the report notes that 'new data is available to inform policy making' — with MIDAS data on permits, visas, CBICs and exits/entries used by multiple government agencies (DINS, Ministry of Labour, Financial Intelligence Unit, Belize Tourism Board) for 'statistical analyses, trends and profiling.' This touches on actions 1i (using administrative records including border records, visa and resident permits for statistical purposes) and 1j (developing evidence-based migration policies using disaggregated data). The report also discusses the migration history recording function of the system, which relates to data availability for policy. However, the report's primary focus appears to be on border management operations rather than data governance per se, and the data dimension is presented as a benefit rather than the central evaluation object. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the main findings section (particularly finding 2 on data availability) and the conclusions section for evidence on how border management information systems contribute to evidence-based migration policy — framing these as pointers for further review rather than definitive characterizations.

References.Title: EX-POST PROJECT EVALUATION REPORT "ENHANCING MIGRATION MANAGEMENT IN BELIZE THROUGH AN UPGRADE OF THE MIGRATION INFORMATION DATA ANALYSIS SYSTEM (MIDAS)"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. Border management is the explicit and central subject of the evaluation — the project title itself references 'Enhancing Migration Management in Belize through an upgrade of the Migration Information Data Analysis System (MIDAS).' The excerpts suggest the evaluation directly examines: integrated border management information systems (MIDAS) across multiple border crossing points; pre-screening mechanisms including APIS for the international airport (action 11b); use of ICT for border crossing procedures; INTERPOL watchlist verification (FIND database integration); capacity building for border officials; and cross-border identification systems (CBIC). These align closely with actions 11a (border management cooperation and capacity-building), 11b (effective integrated border management through pre-screening and ICT), and 11e (training border officials). The report appears to provide substantive analysis of border management effectiveness, efficiency gains, sustainability challenges, and recommendations for system improvement. For synthesis specialists, the main findings (particularly on improved security, efficiency of border crossing services, and INTERPOL integration), the conclusions section, and the recommendations on hardware/software maintenance and human resources would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional relevant evidence on border management outcomes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EX-POST PROJECT EVALUATION REPORT "ENHANCING MIGRATION MANAGEMENT IN BELIZE THROUGH AN UPGRADE OF THE MIGRATION INFORMATION DATA ANALYSIS SYSTEM (MIDAS)"

The MIDAS upgrade directly addresses migration procedure efficiency and screening capacity. The excerpts suggest substantive discussion of: streamlined border crossing procedures through digitalization; pre-screening via APIS (though noted as not yet fully operational); INTERPOL watchlist screening for all entrants; and training of senior border officers on 'Best Practices and Considerations for Senior Border Inspectors.' These relate to actions 12a (increasing transparency and accessibility of migration procedures through technology) and 12b (specialized training for border officials to facilitate identification and referral). The evaluation also assesses the effectiveness of training, which provides evidence on capacity-building for screening procedures. However, the report's focus is more on the technical system than on procedural safeguards or individual assessment processes per se. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on MIDAS functionality, the training effectiveness assessment, and the conclusions on what was and was not achieved — framing these as pointers for further review rather than definitive characterizations.

References.Title: EX-POST INTERNAL EVALUATION REPORT FOR IDF PROJECT: “STRENGTHENING THE CAPACITY OF THE GOVERNMENT OF KENYA TO MANAGE NATIONAL IDENTITY PROGRAMMES”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to be essentially a core evidence source for GCM Objective 4. The entire project is explicitly focused on strengthening Kenya's national identity management system — improving civil registration, issuance of national ID documents, and public awareness of documentation processes. This maps directly onto Objective 4's theme and several associated actions. Action 4a (improve civil registry systems, reach unregistered persons, strengthen capacities, invest in ICT) appears particularly well-covered: the excerpts describe capacity building of NRB officials, needs assessment of the civil registration system, digitization recommendations, and mobile registration outreach to remote/marginalized populations. Action 4d (facilitate access to personal documentation, non-discriminatory criteria, gender-responsive review) is also likely addressed through the gender mainstreaming lens and the focus on marginalized groups. The evaluation's findings on effectiveness (Outcome 1 on civil registration management and Outcome 2 on citizens' ability to acquire IDs), conclusions on institutional capacity, and recommendations on digital registration, SOPs for vetting, and support for marginalized groups would all be directly relevant. Human reviewers should prioritize the effectiveness section (Outcomes 1 and 2), the impact section on registration number increases, and the thematic recommendations on ID management and capacity building. The full report likely contains additional detail on civil registry system strengthening beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT ‘STRENGTHENING THE NATIONAL CAPACITIES IN COUNTER TRAFFICKING IN HUMAN BEINGS IN NORTH MACEDONIA’
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7, particularly regarding the identification, referral, and assistance of migrants in vulnerable situations and victims of trafficking. The project explicitly targets protection of 'trafficked persons, vulnerable migrants and groups vulnerable to migration related risks,' which aligns closely with Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerability), 7e and 7f (protection of migrant children, including unaccompanied minors), and 7k (involvement of local authorities and mobile response teams in identification and referral). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of capacity-building for social workers, law enforcement, and judiciary to identify and assist vulnerable persons, including victims of trafficking and unaccompanied minors. The conclusions on improved cooperation between government institutions and civil society in protection services, and the recommendations on case management methodology and mobile teams, appear directly relevant. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the findings sections on capacity-building outcomes and the conclusions on inter-institutional cooperation, as these appear to offer evidence on how multi-stakeholder approaches improve vulnerability response. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT ‘STRENGTHENING THE NATIONAL CAPACITIES IN COUNTER TRAFFICKING IN HUMAN BEINGS IN NORTH MACEDONIA’
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The project evaluated is explicitly titled 'Strengthening the National Capacities in Counter Trafficking in Human Beings in North Macedonia,' making trafficking in persons the central subject of the evaluation. The excerpts suggest extensive coverage of Actions 10c (monitoring routes exploited by trafficking networks), 10e (measures addressing vulnerabilities of victims and facilitating access to justice), 10g (strengthening legislation and procedures to enhance prosecution and avoid criminalization of victims), 10h (protection and assistance to victims), and 10i (training programmes for public officials and law enforcement to identify signs of trafficking). The evaluation covers capacity-building for law enforcement, social workers, judges and prosecutors — all directly relevant to identifying, referring, and assisting victims. Conclusions on improved inter-institutional cooperation, the development of training curricula now incorporated into national programmes, and the functioning of mobile teams for victim identification are particularly valuable. Recommendations on continuous in-service training, case management methodology, and local authority involvement also directly address Objective 10 themes. Synthesis specialists should prioritize the findings sections on capacity-building outcomes, the conclusions on inter-institutional cooperation and sustainability, and the recommendations — all of which appear to offer substantive evidence on national-level counter-trafficking capacity development.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT ‘STRENGTHENING THE NATIONAL CAPACITIES IN COUNTER TRAFFICKING IN HUMAN BEINGS IN NORTH MACEDONIA’

The project's core focus on capacity-building for law enforcement, social workers, and judiciary to identify, refer, and assist victims of trafficking aligns closely with these actions. The development of training curricula, Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs referenced via acronym), and mobile teams for identification and referral are directly relevant. Conclusions on improved cooperation and the groundwork for continuous capacity development mechanisms appear pertinent. However, the report's framing is primarily through a counter-trafficking lens rather than a broader migration procedure screening lens, which somewhat limits its direct applicability to the full scope of Objective 12. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the findings on training outcomes and the conclusions on referral mechanism development as starting points.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION REPORT- EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE "STRENGTHENING REGIONAL MULTI-NATIONAL COORDINATION FOR INCREASED PROTECTION OF VULNERABLE AND TRAFFICKED MIGRANT CHILDREN TRAVELLING THROUGH THE GULF OF ADEN MIGRATION ROUTE" PROJECT

The project's explicit focus on UAMs and separated children (SC) travelling through the Gulf of Aden migration route makes this a core theme throughout the evaluation. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: identification and referral mechanisms for vulnerable child migrants; family tracing and reunification (FTR) systems; best interest determination procedures; psychosocial and material assistance to UAMs; and the establishment of SOPs for registration and FTR endorsed by the Government of Ethiopia. The executive summary, findings on SO.4, ER1, ER4, ER5, ER6, and the impact section all appear directly relevant. The discussion of the collaborative IOM-UNICEF-MoWCA partnership for FTR, and the analysis of what services were provided to UAMs at different stages of the journey, would likely be particularly valuable for synthesis specialists examining how vulnerability responses for unaccompanied migrant children are operationalized in practice. The report also addresses gender-specific vulnerabilities of UAM girls. Human reviewers should prioritize the SO.4 findings, the impact section on government capacity development, and the ER6 discussion.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION REPORT- EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE "STRENGTHENING REGIONAL MULTI-NATIONAL COORDINATION FOR INCREASED PROTECTION OF VULNERABLE AND TRAFFICKED MIGRANT CHILDREN TRAVELLING THROUGH THE GULF OF ADEN MIGRATION ROUTE" PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The project explicitly targets trafficking in persons (TIP) in the context of child migration, and the evaluation assesses multiple TIP-related outcomes. Key areas of apparent relevance include: the passage of anti-TIP legislation in Ethiopia (Proclamation 909/2015) and Djibouti (Loi 133/AN/16) with child protection provisions (Actions 10a, 10g); awareness-raising campaigns on TIP risks through community conversations (Action 10j); the role of Anti-Trafficking Committees (ATCs) and Task Forces in implementing action plans (Action 10b); identification and assistance to trafficking victims among UAMs (Action 10e); and prosecution of traffickers/brokers at community level. The report also references the US State Department TIP report 2016 assessments for Ethiopia and Djibouti. The findings on SO.1, SO.2, ER2, ER5, and the impact section on legislation and community conversations appear most relevant. Human reviewers should note that while TIP is a central theme, the analysis is primarily from a child protection angle and may not cover all dimensions of GCM Objective 10 equally. The excerpts suggest this is a RELEVANT rather than FUNDAMENTAL report for this objective, as the TIP framework is the legal/policy context rather than the primary analytical lens.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION REPORT- EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE "STRENGTHENING REGIONAL MULTI-NATIONAL COORDINATION FOR INCREASED PROTECTION OF VULNERABLE AND TRAFFICKED MIGRANT CHILDREN TRAVELLING THROUGH THE GULF OF ADEN MIGRATION ROUTE" PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly regarding return and reintegration of UAMs and child migrants. The evaluation explicitly assesses the Assisted Voluntary Return (AVR) component of the project, including post-arrival assistance packages provided to UAMs returned from Saudi Arabia and stranded in Djibouti. Key areas of apparent relevance include: the family tracing and reunification (FTR) system as a component of return (Action 21g on child-sensitive return processes); the reinsertion grant provided to returning UAMs; the gap in sustainable reintegration assistance (livelihoods, vocational training, micro-grants) due to budget reprogramming (Action 21h); and the IOM-UNICEF-MoWCA collaborative partnership for FTR endorsed through SOPs. The report's frank assessment of the reintegration gap — noting that the reprogramming of funds prevented proper reintegration support — would likely be valuable for synthesis specialists examining the sustainability of return programmes for vulnerable migrants, particularly children. The SO.4 findings, the impact section on government capacity, and the recommendations on reintegration positioning appear most relevant. Human reviewers should note that the analysis focuses specifically on child migrants rather than adult returnees, and that the reintegration component was not fully implemented, limiting the evidence available.

References.Title: The Impact of Mobile Cinema Events on Potential Migrants in Guinea
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 3. The entire evaluation is explicitly centered on an information and awareness-raising campaign (CinemArena) targeting potential migrants in Guinea — directly addressing Action 3e (multilingual, gender-responsive, evidence-based information campaigns and awareness-raising events in countries of origin to promote safe migration and highlight risks of irregular migration). The report appears to provide rigorous causal evidence (difference-in-difference methodology) on whether such campaigns measurably change knowledge, risk perceptions, and migration intentions — precisely the outcomes relevant to Objective 3's commitment to provide accurate, timely, accessible information to migrants. Key contributions to a synthesis would likely include: (1) measured effects on knowledge of financial costs of irregular migration (+23%), awareness of dangers (+10%), and migration intentions (-10%); (2) baseline data revealing severe information gaps among potential migrants (only 10% felt well-informed); (3) evidence on which information formats and content work better (local language, local content, discussion formats); (4) limitations of one-off events and recommendations for sustained information provision. The executive summary, impact results section, and conclusions appear most valuable for human reviewers. Actions 3c and 3e are most directly addressed. The report also appears to test assumptions underlying information campaigns more broadly, making it valuable for evidence synthesis on the effectiveness of this approach.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF EXPANDING THE PROTECTION AND PROMOTION OF  MENTAL HEALTH AND PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT FOR  COMMUNITIES AFFECTED BY THE REFUGEE  EMERGENCY IN COX’S BAZAR, BANGLADESH
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7, particularly regarding the psychosocial and mental health dimensions of vulnerability among displaced populations. The program explicitly targets vulnerable groups — refugees, host community members, women, adolescent girls, the elderly, and people with disabilities — and the evaluation assesses how well services reached and addressed their needs. This aligns closely with Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in situations of vulnerability), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies including psychological counseling), and 7f (access to mental health services for unaccompanied and separated children). The conclusions section discusses barriers to participation for specific vulnerable groups, gender considerations, child protection, and the importance of culturally appropriate, participatory approaches. The cross-cutting themes on gender, children and adolescents, and 'do no harm' are particularly relevant. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on appropriateness, coverage, and the conclusions on gender and children/adolescents as starting points. It should be noted that the population is primarily a refugee population rather than migrants in the strict GCM sense, which may limit direct applicability, but the evidence on vulnerability-responsive service design is substantive.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF EXPANDING THE PROTECTION AND PROMOTION OF  MENTAL HEALTH AND PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT FOR  COMMUNITIES AFFECTED BY THE REFUGEE  EMERGENCY IN COX’S BAZAR, BANGLADESH
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly regarding health service access for displaced populations. The evaluation explicitly examines a multi-layered MHPSS program providing mental health and psychosocial services to both refugee and host community members — directly relevant to Action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in national health care policies, facilitating non-discriminatory access, and training health care providers on culturally-sensitive service delivery). The report addresses barriers to access for specific groups (host community, women, elderly, people with disabilities), cultural appropriateness of services, coverage gaps, and the integration of MHPSS with other basic services (WASH, shelter, livelihoods, GBV). The conclusions discuss equitable access, service integration, and the importance of migrant-inclusive service delivery. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on appropriateness, coverage, and the conclusions on do no harm and shared learning, which appear to contain substantive analysis relevant to Action 15e and 15c (holistic and accessible service points). The population is primarily refugees rather than migrants in the strict GCM sense, but the evidence on inclusive health service design is substantive and likely transferable.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “IMPROVING LABOUR MIGRATION MANAGEMENT IN BELIZE”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be highly valuable for a synthesis on GCM Objective 5. The entire project centers on developing a Temporary Work Permit Program (TWPP) framework for the agriculture sector in Belize — directly addressing actions 5d (flexible, rights-based labour mobility schemes including temporary and seasonal programmes) and 5e (skills matching through private sector involvement and labour market analysis). The evaluation explicitly examines whether a seasonal/temporary labour migration pathway was established for agricultural workers (citrus, banana, sugar), the barriers to its development, and the institutional steps taken. The LMNA conducted as part of the project directly informs action 5c (reviewing options for regular migration to address labour market needs). The executive summary, main findings, and recommendations sections all appear to contain substantive analysis relevant to this objective. Particularly valuable for synthesis specialists would be the sections on the TWPP framework development process, the barriers to government commitment, and the lessons learned about designing temporary labour migration programs in lower-capacity governance contexts. The evaluation's treatment of stakeholder engagement (private sector, government ministries) in designing labour mobility pathways also appears relevant to action 5e.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF IOM’S INSTITUTIONAL RESPONSE TO ADDRESS MIGRATION, ENVIRONMENT AND CLIMATE CHANGE NEXUS
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its sub-themes related to natural disasters, climate change, and environmental degradation as drivers of migration (Actions 2h, 2i, 2j, 2k, 2l). The entire report is centered on IOM's institutional response to the migration-environment-climate change nexus, which directly corresponds to the core concern of Objective 2 regarding adverse environmental drivers compelling people to leave. The report appears to explicitly examine how IOM addresses sudden-onset and slow-onset natural disasters, climate change adaptation, and environmental degradation as migration drivers — themes that map closely onto Actions 2h (joint analysis to understand migration movements from disasters and climate change), 2i (adaptation and resilience strategies), and 2l (coherent approaches to migration in the context of disasters). The Theory of Change section, the consolidated findings (particularly findings 1, 2, and 7), and the recommendations all appear to directly address how IOM supports governments in responding to environmentally-driven migration. The executive summary's discussion of IOM's three objectives — minimizing forced migration, ensuring protection for those displaced, and facilitating migration as adaptation — aligns closely with the spirit of Objective 2. For a synthesis specialist, the Theory of Change section, the consolidated findings table, and recommendations 1, 2, and 7 would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible that the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Informe final Evaluación ex post PROYECTO “ASISTENCIA Y PROMOCIÓN DE LA SALUD SEXUAL Y REPRODUCTIVA PARA MUJERES Y NIÑAS MIGRANTES Y REFUGIADAS DE VENEZUELA Y COMUNIDADES DE ACOGIDA EN COLOMBIA Y ECUADOR”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7, particularly regarding gender-responsive approaches to migrant vulnerability and access to health and GBV services. The project explicitly targets Venezuelan migrant and refugee women and girls — populations identified as facing compounded vulnerabilities — and evaluates interventions designed to address their specific needs in sexual and reproductive health (SRH) and gender-based violence (GBV). This aligns closely with Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations, including victims of sexual and gender-based violence), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies addressing health care, psychological services, and access to justice for GBV survivors), and 7k (involving local authorities and stakeholders in identification and assistance of vulnerable migrants). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of barriers to service access, the particular vulnerabilities of adolescent girls, irregular migrants, and GBV survivors, as well as the effectiveness of community-based approaches to reaching vulnerable populations. Conclusions on the humanitarian dimension of the project — including elimination of barriers to life-saving services for over 15,000 women and referral of ~4,000 GBV survivors — appear directly relevant. Recommendations on reaching the most vulnerable (Recommendation 19) and on GBV response capacity also touch on this objective. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the conclusions section (particularly conclusions 1, 2, 3, 7) and the recommendations on services for SSR and VBG, as well as the sections on institutional strengthening, as starting points for deeper review.

References.Title: Informe final Evaluación ex post PROYECTO “ASISTENCIA Y PROMOCIÓN DE LA SALUD SEXUAL Y REPRODUCTIVA PARA MUJERES Y NIÑAS MIGRANTES Y REFUGIADAS DE VENEZUELA Y COMUNIDADES DE ACOGIDA EN COLOMBIA Y ECUADOR”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly regarding health service access for migrants. The project's central purpose — ensuring sexual and reproductive health (SRH) services and GBV response for Venezuelan migrant and refugee women and girls — directly addresses Action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in national and local health care policies, facilitating affordable and non-discriminatory access, reducing communication barriers, and training health care providers on culturally-sensitive service delivery). The evaluation appears to provide substantial analysis of: barriers to health service access for irregular migrants (including legal/regulatory barriers in Colombia where irregular migrants cannot affiliate with the health system); models for eliminating those barriers (the 'Sin Barreras' model is highlighted as a good practice); institutional strengthening of health providers; and the humanitarian dimension of direct service delivery reaching over 15,000 women. The excerpts suggest multiple conclusions and recommendations directly address service access themes — including on diversifying service channels, outreach brigades, institutional capacity building, and sustainability of health service improvements. Conclusions 1, 2, and 7 appear particularly relevant, as do the recommendations on SRH and GBV services (Recommendations 14-16) and institutional strengthening (Recommendations 17-18). The analysis of regulatory barriers in Colombia (irregular migrants excluded from health system affiliation) and Ecuador (universal access in principle but persistent barriers in practice) would likely add comparative value to a synthesis on migrant health service access. Synthesis specialists should prioritize the conclusions section, the good practice description of the 'Sin Barreras' model, and the recommendations on service delivery and institutional strengthening.

References.Title: Informe final Evaluación ex post PROYECTO “ASISTENCIA Y PROMOCIÓN DE LA SALUD SEXUAL Y REPRODUCTIVA PARA MUJERES Y NIÑAS MIGRANTES Y REFUGIADAS DE VENEZUELA Y COMUNIDADES DE ACOGIDA EN COLOMBIA Y ECUADOR”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16, particularly regarding community empowerment and social cohesion between migrant and host communities. The project includes a substantial community mobilization component focused on empowering migrant and host community women as health promoters and community leaders — which relates to Action 16e (empowering migrant women) and Action 16f (community centers/programs facilitating migrant participation). The evaluation analyzes the effectiveness of a 'trainer of trainers' model for community leadership development, the strengthening of solidarity bonds between Venezuelan and host community women (noted as an unexpected positive result), and the integration of SRH promotion into community action agendas. Conclusions 3 and 4 appear most relevant, addressing community resilience, agency, and the empowerment process. The evaluation also touches on the humanitarian-development-peace nexus framing, which relates to broader inclusion goals. However, the empowerment focus is specifically within a health rights framework rather than broader social and economic inclusion, which somewhat limits its direct relevance to GCM Objective 16's full scope. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review conclusions 3-4 and the recommendations on community mobilization and leadership training (Recommendations 4-9) as starting points.
