References.Title: PREVENTION AND REINTEGRATION FOR ADOLESCENTS AND YOUTH IN CONFLICT WITH THE LAW IN THE NORTH CARIBBEAN COAST AUTONOMOUS REGION (RACCN) OF NICARAGUA PILOT PROJECT FINAL EVALUATION REPORT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly around sustainable reintegration (Action 21h). The pilot project's core focus is on the social and economic reintegration of adolescents and youth at risk and in conflict with the law — providing psychosocial support, vocational training, seed capital for micro-enterprises, and community-based accompaniment. While the population is not returning migrants per se, IOM explicitly drew on its experience in 'economic reintegration with migrants and victims of trafficking in persons' to design this intervention, suggesting the model is transferable. The reintegration methodology — life plans, psychological assessment, family engagement, vocational training, seed capital, follow-up — closely mirrors what Action 21h envisions for returning migrants (access to social protection, psycho-social assistance, vocational training, employment opportunities, financial services, entrepreneurship support). The conclusions and best practices sections contain substantive analysis of what worked and what did not in reintegration programming. The lessons learned section (Section IX) and the conclusions (Section VIII) on the reintegration model, seed capital strategy, and sustainability would appear most relevant for human reviewers to prioritize. A synthesis specialist should note that the population is youth in conflict with the law rather than returning migrants, but the reintegration model and evidence base may be directly applicable to migrant reintegration programming.

References.Title: Evaluation ex-post du projet « Soutenir la jeunesse avec des centres de conseil en emploi efficients et efficaces au Niger »
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly Action 2e (investing in human capital development, entrepreneurship, vocational training, employment creation to reduce youth unemployment). The project's stated overall objective is explicitly to 'contribute to providing alternatives to irregular migration by creating effective labour market integration mechanisms for youth in Niger.' The context section describes how poverty, rapid demographic growth, and lack of economic opportunity drive irregular migration to Algeria and Libya. The evaluation assesses whether employment counselling, capacity-building of ANPE, and labour market analysis contributed to reducing push factors. The conclusions section and strategic recommendations (promoting vocational training, entrepreneurship, AGR for migrants) appear directly relevant to Action 2e and 2d. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the context section (section 4.1), the impact and sustainability findings, and the strategic recommendations in section 7.1 as starting points — these appear to contain the most substantive treatment of how employment promotion can serve as an alternative to irregular migration. Note that the full report may contain additional depth beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Ex-Post Evaluation Report “Enhancing the Development Impact of Engagement with The Moldovan Diaspora – Homeland Partnerships”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The project's entire focus is on enhancing the development impact of diaspora engagement with Moldova — directly addressing the core theme of this objective. The report appears to provide substantive evidence on multiple associated actions: Action 19d (establishing government structures for diaspora engagement, specifically the Bureau for Relations with the Diaspora and the Diaspora Excellence Groups pilot); Action 19j (building partnerships between local authorities, diaspora organizations and migrant organizations for knowledge and skills transfer); Action 19c (non-financial contributions of diaspora including knowledge transfer and civic engagement); and Action 19b (integrating migration into development planning through the Diaspora-2025 strategy). The evaluation assesses the effectiveness, sustainability, and governance dimensions of diaspora-homeland partnerships — providing evidence on what works and what doesn't in operationalizing diaspora contributions to development. The executive summary, effectiveness and sustainability sections, and all nine recommendations appear particularly valuable for synthesis specialists. The evaluation's critical assessment of the DEG framework's limitations (lack of co-ownership, unclear roles, political instability) and the relative success of Outcome 2 (educational centers) would contribute nuanced evidence on implementation challenges. The full report likely contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN EX - POST DEL PROYECTO “LOS EFECTOS DEL CAMBIO CLIMÁTICO EN POBLACIONES VULNERABLES Y EN LA MOVILIDAD HUMANA EN EL SALVADOR, GUATEMALA, HONDURAS Y NICARAGUA, CENTROAMÉRICA”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The evaluated project was explicitly designed to collect empirical data on the nexus between climate change and human mobility in four Central American countries — a core data-generation initiative. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of the challenges encountered in data collection methodology, including the mismatch between the chosen data collection model and the type of evidence sought, cost overruns, and failure to validate research outputs. These issues relate directly to Actions 1a (comprehensive data strategies), 1c (building national capacities for data collection), and 1j (country-specific migration profiles). The report appears to document why the data collection effort fell short, offering lessons on what not to do in migration data initiatives — which could be valuable for a synthesis on evidence gaps and methodological challenges. The sections on 'Eficiencia' and 'Eficacia' in the executive summary, as well as the conclusions on coherence and the recommendations section, would be the most relevant starting points for human reviewers. The report's value is somewhat limited by the fact that the data collection ultimately failed, meaning it offers cautionary rather than positive evidence, but this itself is informative for synthesis purposes.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN EX - POST DEL PROYECTO “LOS EFECTOS DEL CAMBIO CLIMÁTICO EN POBLACIONES VULNERABLES Y EN LA MOVILIDAD HUMANA EN EL SALVADOR, GUATEMALA, HONDURAS Y NICARAGUA, CENTROAMÉRICA”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to be substantially relevant to GCM Objective 2, particularly its sub-theme on climate change, environmental degradation, and migration (Actions 2h, 2i, 2l). The project under evaluation was explicitly designed to investigate the link between climate change effects and human mobility in El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras, and Nicaragua — a direct focus on understanding environmental drivers of migration. The excerpts reference the 2016 'Hambre sin Fronteras' report on food insecurity and migration, the displacement of over 1 million people following Hurricanes Eta and Iota, and the project's alignment with the Platform on Disaster Displacement (PDD) and the Política Centroamericana de Gestión Integral de Riesgo de Desastres. These are precisely the frameworks referenced under Action 2l. The report also touches on the need to generate empirical evidence to inform adaptation and mitigation strategies (Action 2i). While the project did not fully achieve its objectives, the evaluation's analysis of why evidence-generation on climate-migration links failed in this regional context could offer useful lessons for synthesis on Objective 2. The context and background sections (section 1.1) and the coherence/relevance conclusions appear most pertinent for human reviewers to prioritize.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT ENHANCING SOCIAL PROTECTION FOR MIGRANTS IN VULNERABLE SITUATION IN SERBIA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project's explicit focus on 'migrants in vulnerable situations' aligns directly with this objective's core theme, and the excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple associated actions. The project appears to address Action 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerability, including women at risk, unaccompanied/separated children, victims of GBV) extensively — with 1,333 migrants receiving psychosocial support and counselling, including 1,246 UMC. Action 7c (gender-responsive migration policies addressing needs of migrant women and girls, including psychological counselling and GBV response) appears covered through dedicated gender-specific activities and case management for GBV victims. Action 7e (accounting for migrant children in national child protection systems) and Action 7f (protecting unaccompanied and separated children) appear to be central project components, with dedicated child protection training, Best Interests Assessments, and coverage of children's homes. Action 7k (involving local authorities and stakeholders in identification and referral) appears addressed through the migrant protection teams and CSO partnerships. The project title itself references 'migrants in vulnerable situations,' and the evaluation questions, findings, and conclusions all appear to directly engage with vulnerability reduction. For a synthesis specialist, the findings sections on psychosocial support outcomes (EQ5-7), the conclusions on good practices, and the recommendations on programmatic continuation would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The accredited PSS curriculum and its institutional embedding at RISP may also offer evidence on sustainable vulnerability response mechanisms.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT ENHANCING SOCIAL PROTECTION FOR MIGRANTS IN VULNERABLE SITUATION IN SERBIA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be highly valuable for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The project's stated aim — 'enhancing social protection of migrants through the increase in availability and quality of social services and community-based support programs' — maps directly onto this objective's core commitment. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of Action 15c (establishing holistic and accessible service points at local level that are migrant-inclusive) through the migrant protection teams operating across seven municipalities. Action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in health care policies, including mental health) appears addressed through the psychosocial support curriculum and outreach worker model. Action 15f (providing inclusive education to migrant children) appears touched upon through CSO activities including joint visits to cultural venues and vocational training. The evaluation's findings on whether services met expectations (EQ7), coverage rates (50% of migrants in reception centres), and sustainability of service delivery (EQ14-15) would likely provide evidence relevant to a synthesis on access to basic services. The sections on PSS curriculum accreditation, outreach worker deployment, and CSO community-based activities in the findings and conclusions appear particularly worth reviewing. The sustainability analysis regarding institutional embedding at RISP and potential continuation of outreach workers may also offer evidence on durable service access mechanisms.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT 'ADDRESSING HEALTH ISSUES OF SURVIVORS OF HUMAN TRAFFICKING IN JESSORE AND SATKHIRA IN BANGLADESH
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The project's explicit focus is on addressing health issues of survivors of human trafficking — making trafficking in persons the central subject of the evaluation. The report appears to provide substantive evidence on: identification and assistance to trafficking victims (Action 10e, 10h); awareness-raising campaigns and community-level information dissemination (Action 10i, 10j); capacity building of health and community workers to identify and respond to trafficking (Action 10i); referral mechanisms linking survivors to health services (Action 10e, 10h); and the particular vulnerabilities of women and children as trafficking victims (Action 10e). The evaluation covers both prevention (community awareness campaigns, CSG formation) and response (health service provision, referral cards, psychosocial support). The effectiveness chapter, lessons learned section, and recommendations all directly address trafficking survivor identification, protection, and assistance. The geographic focus on major trafficking corridors in Bangladesh (Benapole border, Satkhira) adds contextual specificity. Synthesis specialists should prioritize the effectiveness chapter (section 2.3), lessons learned, and recommendations sections, as well as the project description in section 1.2, as starting points. The full report likely contains additional relevant evidence on referral mechanisms and GO-NGO coordination for trafficking response beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT 'ADDRESSING HEALTH ISSUES OF SURVIVORS OF HUMAN TRAFFICKING IN JESSORE AND SATKHIRA IN BANGLADESH
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly Action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in national health care policies). The project's core objective was to strengthen integrated health services within the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare accessible to trafficking survivors — directly addressing migrant access to basic health services. The evaluation appears to provide substantive analysis of: health facility upgrading and equipment provision; capacity building of health providers for culturally-sensitive service delivery to trafficking survivors; referral mechanisms to facilitate health access; barriers to access (transport costs, stigma, lack of separate space); and GO-NGO coordination to improve service delivery. The effectiveness chapter (section 2.3) and recommendations sections appear particularly relevant, covering outcomes such as 245 health professionals trained, 4 facilities upgraded, 38 survivors receiving integrated health care, and 180 survivors reached with health information. The lessons learned on transport costs, referral cards, and community support groups as enablers of health access would likely add value to a synthesis on migrant health service access. Specialists should note that the 'migrants' in this context are trafficking survivors rather than migrants in the broader GCM sense, which may affect applicability depending on the synthesis scope.

References.Title: CONNECTING DIASPORA FOR DEVELOPMENT (CD4D) MIDTERM REPORT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The CD4D (Connecting Diaspora for Development) programme is explicitly designed to harness diaspora contributions to sustainable development in countries of origin — the core theme of this objective. The evaluation appears to directly examine diaspora knowledge and skills transfer (Actions 19c, 19i, 19j), diaspora motivation and engagement (Action 19d), and the conditions enabling or constraining diaspora contributions (Actions 19h, 19i). Multiple findings across the excerpts directly address these themes: participant motivation to contribute to countries of origin (pre- and post-assignment), types of knowledge transferred, interaction dynamics between diaspora participants and host institutions, and programme design features affecting diaspora engagement effectiveness. The report also touches on Action 19d (government structures facilitating diaspora engagement, here through IOM) and Action 19j (partnerships for knowledge and skills transfer between countries of origin and destination). For a synthesis specialist, the most valuable sections to prioritize would appear to be: Section 5.1 on host institution experiences (knowledge transfer dynamics), Section 5.2 on participant experiences (motivation, knowledge transfer types, personal development), and the recommendations on facilitating knowledge exchange among participants. The evaluation's multi-country scope (Afghanistan, Ethiopia, Ghana, Sierra Leone, Somalia) and its longitudinal design (baseline to mid-term) would likely add comparative value to a synthesis. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Expost Evaluation of the project "Enhancing Coordination and Strengthening Institutional Capacity to Effectively Engage with Basotho Diaspora"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The evaluation appears to explicitly examine a project designed to strengthen government capacity to engage with the Basotho diaspora as a contributor to national socio-economic development — which is the core theme of GCM Objective 19. The project title itself directly references diaspora engagement, and the evaluation's primary subject matter aligns closely with Actions 19d (establishing dedicated diaspora offices or focal points, diaspora policy advisory boards), 19j (building partnerships between local authorities, diaspora organizations and migrant organizations to promote knowledge and skills transfer), and 19c (investing in research on non-financial contributions of migrants and diasporas). The excerpts suggest multiple findings directly address: the establishment of a Diaspora Directorate within MoFA (Action 19d); the formulation of a National Diaspora Policy through widespread consultation (Action 19d); the formation of the Basotho Diaspora Association/BDA (Action 19j); and the mobilization of the diaspora to support the government's COVID-19 response through medical help, food and vaccine delivery (Action 19f). The report also appears to address Action 19b (integrating migration into development planning) through the alignment with the National Strategic Development Plan 2019-2023. The executive summary, findings on effectiveness and impact, and the sustainability section would appear particularly valuable starting points for human reviewers. The recommendations on sustaining the diaspora directorate and diaspora policy workplan are also directly relevant. It is possible the full report contains additional evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal, particularly in the detailed findings sections.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATION FOR MIGRATION (IOM) SOUTH SUDAN HEALTH AND WASH RESPONSE: 2014-2016
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The entire evaluation concerns IOM's provision of basic services — specifically health care and WASH (water, sanitation, hygiene) — to IDPs in PoC sites in South Sudan. This directly addresses actions 15c (holistic and accessible service points at local level, migrant-inclusive), 15e (health needs of migrants incorporated into health care policies and plans, strengthening capacities, facilitating access), and to a lesser extent 15a (non-discriminatory service delivery). The report title, evaluation questions, and all major sections (relevance, effectiveness, efficiency, coverage, sustainability) are organized around assessing the quality, reach, and impact of basic service delivery to a displaced/migrant population. Key contributions to a synthesis would likely include: evidence on coverage rates and gaps (119,463 individuals served), effectiveness of mobile vs. static clinic strategies, WASH infrastructure outcomes, challenges of service delivery in conflict/displacement contexts, and sustainability considerations. The executive summary, effectiveness, coverage, and recommendations sections appear most directly relevant for human reviewers. The report also discusses gender-responsive service delivery (reproductive health, MHPSS for women) and services for persons with disabilities, adding further depth. It is possible the full report contains additional quantitative evidence on service access beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “ PROMOTING AN INTEGRATED  GOVERNANCE RESPONSE TO COMBAT TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS AND  OTHER RELATED CRIMES AT BORDER REGIONS (BORDER-TIP)"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Trafficking in persons is the explicit and central subject of the entire evaluation — the project title, all three stated outcomes, and virtually all findings and recommendations directly address TIP prevention, prosecution, and victim protection. The report covers: establishment of Anti-Trafficking Task Forces and action plans (Actions 10b, 10c); capacity building for frontline officers on victim identification (Action 10i); legal counselling and court remediation for 35 victims (Action 10e, 10h); TIP profiling at border regions (Action 10c, 10d); referral mechanisms for victim protection (Action 10e, 10g); awareness-raising campaigns on TIP risks (Action 10j); and coordination between government and non-government actors (Action 10d). The evaluation also addresses prosecution challenges, law enforcement capacity, and the distinction between TIP and related crimes (Action 10f). For a synthesis specialist, the executive summary, conclusions on outcomes/impacts, and the full recommendations section (Prevention, Protection, Prosecution) would be particularly valuable starting points. The full report likely contains substantially more evidence on all associated actions than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “ PROMOTING AN INTEGRATED  GOVERNANCE RESPONSE TO COMBAT TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS AND  OTHER RELATED CRIMES AT BORDER REGIONS (BORDER-TIP)"
References.Abstract: The excerpts suggest meaningful relevance to GCM Objective 11. The project is explicitly set at Indonesia-Malaysia border regions and focuses on 'integrated governance' at border areas. The evaluation covers capacity building for frontline border officers including immigration offices, Indonesian Military Forces, Border Post and Management Offices, and the Indonesian National Police (Action 11a — border management cooperation and capacity building). The project also addresses identification and referral of trafficking victims at borders (Action 11a, 11c), and recommendations include enhancing police-to-police cooperation between border regions and involving border management agencies in TIP programs (Action 11g). The PLBN (Border Crossing Post) is listed as a key institutional partner. However, border management appears as the operational context for anti-trafficking work rather than the primary analytical focus. Synthesis specialists working on integrated border management would likely find the sections on frontline officer training, border-level referral mechanisms, and inter-agency coordination at borders most relevant for review.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “ PROMOTING AN INTEGRATED  GOVERNANCE RESPONSE TO COMBAT TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS AND  OTHER RELATED CRIMES AT BORDER REGIONS (BORDER-TIP)"
References.Abstract: The excerpts suggest relevant content for GCM Objective 12, particularly around screening, identification, and referral mechanisms. The project trained 196 frontline officers on victim identification and assistance (Action 12b — specialized training for first responders and government officials). The establishment of referral pathways for assisting victims of trafficking in each targeted area directly relates to Action 12c (gender-responsive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures). Recommendations explicitly call for evaluating and improving the referral mechanism and developing SOPs for victim assistance. The evaluation discusses challenges in identifying trafficking victims and coordinating referrals across institutions. These themes align with Actions 12b and 12c. Synthesis specialists might particularly review the sections on frontline officer training outcomes, referral mechanism implementation, and the recommendations on SOP development — framing these as pointers for further review rather than definitive characterizations.

References.Title: PARE 1+1 IMPROVING MOLDOVA'S REMITTANCE BASED INVESTMENT PROGRAM
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The PARE 1+1 program is explicitly designed to mobilize diaspora remittances for SME development and sustainable economic growth in Moldova — directly addressing Actions 19d (government structures for diaspora engagement), 19e (targeted support programmes and matching-grant mechanisms for migrant/diaspora investments and entrepreneurship), and 19j (partnerships to promote knowledge and skills transfer). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of a matching-grant mechanism (the 1+1 scheme), diaspora engagement strategies, and the institutional architecture supporting diaspora contributions. The evaluation of program components, identification of barriers to business sustainability, and recommendations for strengthening diaspora investment channels would likely add value to a synthesis on diaspora contributions to development. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the executive summary's discussion of program design and impact, Chapter 1's overview of the PARE program structure, and the recommendations sections on referral systems and access to finance — as these appear to contain the most directly relevant analytical content. The crowdfunding platform analysis also appears relevant to Action 19e and 19f (digital platforms for diaspora financial engagement). It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: PARE 1+1 IMPROVING MOLDOVA'S REMITTANCE BASED INVESTMENT PROGRAM
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 20. The PARE 1+1 program is fundamentally about leveraging remittance flows for productive investment — directly addressing Action 20g (programmes and instruments to promote investments from remittance senders in local development and entrepreneurship, including matching-grant mechanisms) and Action 20i (banking solutions and financial instruments for migrants). The report title itself references remittances, and the evaluation questions explicitly examine how effectively the program leverages remittances for MSME investment. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: the remittance landscape in Moldova (scale, trends, household impacts), the 1+1 matching-grant mechanism as a remittance investment instrument, barriers to financial inclusion for MSME beneficiaries (access to formal finance, collateral requirements, interest rates), and a crowdfunding platform as an innovative remittance channeling mechanism. The recommendations on bridging the demand-supply gap in Moldova's financial sector and the crowdfunding feasibility study appear particularly relevant to Actions 20e, 20g, and 20i. Synthesis specialists would want to prioritize the executive summary, the remittances section in Chapter 1, the access-to-finance recommendations in Chapter 2, and the crowdfunding platform analysis in Chapter 3. The report appears to offer both program-level evidence on matching-grant mechanisms and contextual evidence on remittance dynamics in a high-remittance, low-income country context.

References.Title: Trafficking in Persons Data in the Economic Community of West African States Region: Standardizing and Leveraging Administrative Data for Evidence
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The report's primary subject is explicitly the collection, standardization, and analysis of TIP administrative data in the ECOWAS region — this is the core purpose of the entire document. The excerpts suggest that the report directly addresses actions 1a (harmonizing methodologies for data collection), 1b (improving international comparability of migration statistics), 1c (building national capacities in data collection and analysis), 1f (regional mechanisms for data collection), and 1i (collaboration between state units and national statistical offices). The WATIP questionnaire development, the IC-TIP alignment, the data validation process, and the extensive discussion of challenges in data collection and comparability all appear to be substantive contributions to evidence on this objective. The executive summary's main takeaways are explicitly framed around data quality and disaggregation (age, sex, citizenship). The recommendations sections (6.3.1–6.3.4) directly address improving data systems, standardization, training for data collection, and national/regional coordination — all core to Objective 1. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on challenges (6.2.1–6.2.5), the recommendations tables (Tables 3–6), and the methodology section (2.1) would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional relevant evidence on data trends and patterns beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Trafficking in Persons Data in the Economic Community of West African States Region: Standardizing and Leveraging Administrative Data for Evidence
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Trafficking in persons is the explicit and sole subject of the entire report. The excerpts suggest substantive coverage of multiple associated actions: Action 10a (Palermo Protocol implementation is referenced throughout), 10c (monitoring TIP routes and patterns in the ECOWAS region), 10d (sharing information through regional mechanisms — the WATIP questionnaire), 10e (addressing vulnerabilities of women, men, girls and boys who are VOTs), 10f (ensuring definitions of TIP align with international law), 10g (strengthening legislation and prosecution), and 10i (national information systems and training). The report provides regional data on TIP patterns (types of exploitation, gendered patterns, child trafficking), discusses the criminal justice response, and formulates recommendations for strengthening TIP data systems and counter-trafficking responses. The executive summary's main takeaways on child trafficking, gendered patterns, diversification of TIP types, and COVID-19 impacts on case processing are directly relevant. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on regional TIP trends (Sections 4 and 5), the challenges section (6.2), and the recommendations (6.3) would appear particularly valuable. The full report likely contains additional evidence on national-level TIP patterns and legal frameworks beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation: Mapping Ethiopian Diaspora Residing in the United States of America
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project's core activity was a diaspora mapping exercise — collecting, analyzing, and disseminating data on Ethiopian diaspora in the United States — which aligns closely with several associated actions, particularly Action 1d (collecting data on contributions of migrants and diasporas to sustainable development) and Action 1k (research on the interrelationship between migration and sustainable development, contributions of diasporas). The excerpts indicate the project produced a mapping survey, a skills/knowledge gap report, a correlation analysis, a database of 400 diaspora organizations, and a roadmap — all evidence-based outputs. The evaluation explicitly assesses the quality, scope, and limitations of this data collection effort, including how political context reduced the sample size and representativeness. The sections on effectiveness and the recommendations on diaspora mapping methodology (e.g., careful budgeting of mapping scope, use of IOM mapping toolkit, re-interviewing samples) would appear particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist. The report also touches on Action 1j (country-specific migration profiles) and Action 1f (regional research on diaspora contributions). Specialists should note that the data collection is specific to one diaspora corridor (Ethiopia-US) and limited sectors (health and education), but the methodological lessons and evaluation of data quality appear substantive.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation: Mapping Ethiopian Diaspora Residing in the United States of America
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The project's entire purpose aligns with this objective: mapping the Ethiopian diaspora in the US to facilitate their contribution to Ethiopia's economic development, directly corresponding to Actions 19c (research on non-financial contributions of migrants and diasporas), 19d (establishing government structures for diaspora engagement, including the Diaspora Agency and Diaspora Trust Fund mentioned in the report), 19j (building partnerships to promote knowledge and skills transfer, including by mapping diasporas and their skills), and 19i (enabling migrants to carry out professional activities and engage in knowledge transfer in home countries). The project produced a diaspora database, a skills gap report, a correlation analysis, and a roadmap — all directly serving diaspora-development linkage goals. The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of diaspora mapping as a tool for development policy, the role of the GoE's Diaspora Policy, and the sustainability of diaspora engagement mechanisms. The excerpts suggest the conclusions, recommendations on diaspora mapping methodology, and the sustainability section would be particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist. The lessons learned on building trust with diaspora communities through 'ambassadors and influencers' and the discussion of political context effects on diaspora engagement appear especially rich. The full report likely contains additional evidence on diaspora engagement mechanisms beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EX - POST EVALUATION REPORT of “SUPPORTING CHINA’S ENGAGEMENT IN THE GLOBAL MIGRATION DEBATE” Project
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 23, particularly regarding how IOM supported a member state's engagement in global migration governance architecture. The project's explicit purpose — supporting China's engagement in the global migration debate, including GCM implementation — appears to go beyond standard operational coordination language and instead examines international cooperation mechanisms as a primary object of inquiry. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: China's integration into the UN migration network (with IOM nominated as coordinator), the multi-ministerial approach to aligning national policy with GCM principles, and the role of IOM Development Fund in facilitating a member state's participation in global migration governance. These themes relate to Actions 23a (supporting states in implementing the GCM), 23d (capacity-building mechanisms), and 23e (bilateral/multilateral partnerships for migration policy). The evaluation's findings on sustainability — particularly the follow-up GCM consultation in June 2021 and IOM's nomination as UN migration network coordinator — appear especially relevant for a synthesis on how international cooperation mechanisms sustain GCM engagement. Specialists would likely want to review the sustainability and impact sections, as well as the coherence section discussing IOM China's role in facilitating government participation in global frameworks. The excerpts also reveal limitations (short project duration, insufficient follow-up) that could inform understanding of what makes international cooperation for GCM implementation effective or ineffective. It should be noted that this report also contains significant operational language about coordination that is standard IOM context, so reviewers should focus on the governance architecture elements specifically.

References.Title: Final internal evaluation of the project “ENHANCING COUNTER-TRAFFICKING CAPACITIES IN GEORGIA BY SUSTAINING INTER-AGENCY COORDINATION”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The project's entire focus is on counter-trafficking capacity building in Georgia — the project title, objectives, outputs, and all major findings directly address trafficking in persons prevention, investigation, prosecution, and victim identification. This aligns closely with multiple associated actions: Action 10c (monitoring routes, strengthening cooperation on prevention, investigation, prosecution, and victim identification), Action 10e (addressing vulnerabilities of potential victims, facilitating access to justice), Action 10g (strengthening legislation and procedures to enhance prosecution), Action 10h (protection and assistance to VoTs), and Action 10i (training programmes for law enforcement and public officials to identify signs of trafficking, including forced/child labour). The excerpts reveal substantive analysis of: law enforcement training on financial investigation of TIP and child trafficking; inter-agency coordination between law enforcement, Labour Inspectorate, and Social Service Agency; the National Referral Mechanism; SOPs development; child trafficking investigations (which tripled 2017-2019); and labor exploitation identification. For a synthesis specialist, the conclusions on effectiveness of multi-agency training approaches, the impact section on child trafficking investigations, the sustainability findings on NRM and SOPs, and the recommendations on capacity building and inter-agency coordination would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review.

References.Title: FORMATION PROFESSIONNELLE ET OPPORTUNITES DE SUBSISTANCE POUR LES MIGRANTS REGULARISES AU MAROC
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. The project's explicit goal is the socioeconomic integration of regularized migrant women in Morocco, which aligns closely with this objective's core theme. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: labor market integration barriers and enablers (Actions 16c, 16d); intercultural dialogue and sensitization of public and private actors (Action 16a); empowerment of migrant women through vocational training and AGR development (Action 16e); and the role of civil society and community-level actors in facilitating integration (Action 16f). The evaluation appears to assess both the effectiveness of skills-building approaches and the structural conditions needed for genuine inclusion. The executive summary's discussion of training outcomes, private sector engagement gaps, and sustainability of integration mechanisms would appear particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist. The recommendations section on private sector engagement and intercultural dialogue also seems directly relevant. Reviewers should note that the report covers integration from the perspective of a pilot project evaluation, offering lessons on what works and what doesn't in practice — which is valuable for evidence synthesis on this objective.

References.Title: FORMATION PROFESSIONNELLE ET OPPORTUNITES DE SUBSISTANCE POUR LES MIGRANTS REGULARISES AU MAROC
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 18. The project's primary intervention is vocational training and skills development for regularized migrant women in Morocco, making skills development a central analytical subject. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: the effectiveness of practical skills training for entrepreneurship and AGR development (Actions 18e, 18g, 18j); the differential outcomes of two training models (one focused on savoir-faire/entrepreneurship, one on professional competencies for labor market access); barriers to translating skills training into employment outcomes; and the role of private sector and public training institutions in skills development partnerships (Actions 18e, 18f). The evaluation appears to assess both the quality of training and its downstream effects on employability and economic empowerment. For a synthesis specialist, the comparative analysis of the two training models (Heure Joyeuse vs. Fondation Orient Occident approaches), the findings on training satisfaction and outcomes, and the recommendations on systematic follow-up surveys would appear particularly valuable. The executive summary and the project description sections provide the clearest entry points. It is possible the full report contains additional depth on training methodology and outcomes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: MID-TERM EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT COMBATING TRAFFICKING IN HUMAN BEINGS IN BURKINA FASO THROUGH THE STRENGTHENING OF THE NATIONAL FRAMEWORK AND CAPACITIES IN IDENTIFYING VICTIMS AND IMPROVING DATA COLLECTION
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. Data collection on trafficking in persons is one of the two primary pillars of the evaluated project, appearing explicitly in the project title ('improving data collection') and constituting the entirety of Objective 2 in the project logical framework. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: the development of a national e-tool (Kobo-based) for TIP data collection (Activity 2.2); an assessment of existing data collection systems (Activity 2.1); advocacy for mainstreaming TIP questions into national surveys (Activity 2.3); training of governmental and non-state actors on data collection tools (Activities 2.4 and 2.5); and challenges related to data protection, technical capacity, and institutional leadership (MFSNF vs. INSD). These map closely to GCM Actions 1c (building national capacities in data collection), 1i (collaboration between migration data units and national statistical offices), and 1a (comprehensive strategy for improving migration data). The evaluation's findings and recommendations on data protection risks, cloud vs. server solutions, and institutional ownership are particularly likely to be of value to a synthesis specialist. Sections covering Activities 2.1–2.5 and the associated evaluation remarks appear most relevant for human reviewers to prioritize.

References.Title: MID-TERM EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT COMBATING TRAFFICKING IN HUMAN BEINGS IN BURKINA FASO THROUGH THE STRENGTHENING OF THE NATIONAL FRAMEWORK AND CAPACITIES IN IDENTIFYING VICTIMS AND IMPROVING DATA COLLECTION
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Combating trafficking in persons is the explicit and sole focus of the evaluated project, as stated in the project title and confirmed throughout all sections of the excerpts. The report appears to provide substantive analysis across multiple GCM Objective 10 actions: strengthening national anti-TIP frameworks and coordination mechanisms (Actions 10b, 10c); building prosecution and investigation capacities through training of judges, prosecutors, law enforcement officers, and social workers on victim-centered approaches (Actions 10e, 10g, 10i); developing national data collection systems for TIP cases (Action 10i); victim identification procedures and referral mechanisms (Actions 10e, 10g, 10h); and institutional coordination through a working group (Action 10d). The evaluation remarks on training curricula development (Activity 3.1), training of trainers (Activity 3.2), the national coordination working group (Activity 1.3), and the data collection e-tool (Activity 2.2) appear particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist. The findings on sustainability risks (data protection, technical capacity, budget constraints) and recommendations on institutional ownership (MFSNF leadership) would likely add nuanced evidence on implementation challenges. The full report likely contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FOURTH EVALUATION OF THE IOM DEVELOPMENT FUND
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 23, specifically regarding capacity-building mechanisms and international cooperation frameworks for migration governance. The IOM Development Fund is explicitly a multilateral capacity-building instrument — a global resource fund supporting developing Member States in strengthening migration management capacities. The evaluation examines the Fund's governance architecture, funding mechanisms, Member State ownership, and institutional effectiveness as a cooperative instrument. This aligns with Action 23d (capacity-building mechanisms mobilizing resources to assist States in fulfilling GCM commitments) and Action 23a (supporting States in implementing migration governance through technical and financial assistance). The report's analysis of the Fund's relevance, effectiveness, and impact as a multilateral cooperation tool — including discussions of Member State engagement, equitable regional allocation, and the Fund's role as 'seed funding' for innovative projects — appears substantively relevant to understanding how international cooperation mechanisms function in practice. Specialists working on a synthesis of evidence on multilateral capacity-building for migration governance would likely want to review the conclusions section (page 39) and the evaluation's findings on Fund management effectiveness, Member State ownership, and sustainability. However, reviewers should note that the report evaluates a specific fund rather than examining global governance architecture more broadly, and the excerpts suggest the primary focus is operational performance rather than governance theory.

References.Title: Final evaluation of the EU-IOM Joint Initiative for Migrant Protection and Reintegration-North Africa
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The evaluation appears to substantively engage with vulnerability as a central theme throughout the programme's design and implementation. The excerpts indicate that the JI-NA explicitly targeted migrants in vulnerable situations — including victims of trafficking, unaccompanied and separated children (UASCs), migrants with health needs, and stranded migrants in Libya — which directly relates to Actions 7b, 7c, 7e, and 7f. The conclusions note weaknesses in protection systems and the absence of adapted SOPs for individual migrants' needs including specific vulnerabilities. Recommendations 4 and 5 address migrant-centred approaches, psychological support, and vulnerability assessment. Recommendation 10 specifically addresses capacity for assisting people in vulnerable situations, psychosocial support, and best interest determination for children. The methodology section also discusses the rights-based approach and gender-sensitive analysis applied throughout. For a synthesis specialist, the conclusions on protection system gaps, the country-specific recommendations on UASCs (Morocco, Tunisia), and the discussion of Libya's specific humanitarian context would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence on vulnerability identification and referral systems beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Final evaluation of the EU-IOM Joint Initiative for Migrant Protection and Reintegration-North Africa
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The JI-NA programme is explicitly and comprehensively focused on voluntary return and sustainable reintegration — the core themes of GCM Objective 21. The programme title, all three strategic objectives, and the entire evaluation framework are organized around return and reintegration. The excerpts suggest extensive analysis directly addressing multiple associated actions: Action 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes based on free, prior and informed consent) is central to the AVRR and VHR programmes evaluated; Action 21h (sustainable reintegration with access to social protection, psychosocial assistance, vocational training, employment, and financial services) is addressed throughout the conclusions and recommendations; Action 21a (bilateral and multilateral cooperation frameworks) is addressed in recommendations on policy dialogue and coordination; and Action 21g (best interests of the child in return processes) appears in multiple country recommendations. The conclusions provide substantive assessment of programme effectiveness, efficiency, impact, and sustainability across five North African countries over five years, covering both voluntary return from North Africa and reintegration of North African nationals. For a synthesis specialist, the conclusions section (particularly on effectiveness of return operations, weaknesses in reintegration quality, and limited impact on protection systems), the country-specific recommendations, and the discussion of national authority engagement and ownership would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report almost certainly contains extensive additional evidence on return and reintegration processes, beneficiary experiences, and system-level outcomes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: "Strengthening Counter-Trafficking Response by Improving Identification, Investigation and Prosecution of Trafficking in Persons in Kazakhstan”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Trafficking in persons is the explicit and central subject of both the project evaluated and the evaluation itself — as reflected in the project title ('Strengthening Counter-Trafficking Response by Improving Identification, Investigation and Prosecution of Trafficking in Persons in Kazakhstan'). The report appears to directly address multiple associated actions under Objective 10: action 10a (Palermo Protocol implementation is referenced), action 10c (monitoring routes and strengthening cooperation on investigation and prosecution), action 10e (addressing vulnerabilities of victims, facilitating access to justice), action 10f (ensuring correct definitions in legislation and judicial prosecutions — Output 1.2), action 10g (strengthening legislation and prosecution — draft law, Output 1.1), action 10h (protection and assistance to victims — government-funded assistance discussed), and action 10i (training programmes for law enforcement to identify trafficking — Output 1.3 and mobile task forces). The evaluation covers legislative reform, law enforcement capacity-building, victim identification and referral mechanisms, and prosecution improvements — all core to Objective 10. For a synthesis specialist, the conclusions on relevance, effectiveness, and sustainability sections, as well as the findings on the mobile task forces (Output 2.3), the draft law (Output 1.1), and the guidance documents (Outputs 1.2 and 2.2) would appear particularly valuable starting points. The Kazakhstan context — including the TIP Report downgrade and labour trafficking gaps — also provides useful country-level evidence. It is likely that the full report contains substantially more relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL REPORT - EVALUATION OF THE IOM PROJECT: PROMOTING THE REDUCTION OF AND IMPROVING THE OPERATIONAL RESPONSE TO GENDER BASED VIOLENCE THROUGH THE DISPLACEMENT TRACKING MATRIX (DTM) AND CAMP MANAGEMENT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The evaluation appears to explicitly examine the IOM Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM) — a data collection, analysis and dissemination tool — and its enhancement with protection-enhanced indicators (GBV, child protection, trafficking in persons). Data collection methodology, standardization of indicators, centralized data warehousing, and the use of disaggregated data to inform humanitarian response are core subjects of the evaluation. The report title itself references the DTM, and multiple evaluation questions directly address data collection effectiveness, quality, analysis, and use. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how protection-enhanced DTM data are collected, standardized across nine operations, shared with actors, and used to guide action — closely relating to GCM Actions 1a (harmonizing methodologies), 1c (building national capacities in data collection), and 1j (developing country-specific profiles to inform evidence-based policies). The recommendations section (Table 10) contains detailed guidance on data collection methodology, indicator standardization, and reporting tools. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on DTM data collection methodology, the Central Data Warehouse standardization, and the recommendations on protection-enhanced data collection (items 6–15 in Table 10) would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The humanitarian/displacement context is somewhat distinct from the GCM's migration policy framing, but the data systems and methodological lessons are highly transferable.

References.Title: FINAL REPORT - EVALUATION OF THE IOM PROJECT: PROMOTING THE REDUCTION OF AND IMPROVING THE OPERATIONAL RESPONSE TO GENDER BASED VIOLENCE THROUGH THE DISPLACEMENT TRACKING MATRIX (DTM) AND CAMP MANAGEMENT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The evaluation's central focus is on identifying and responding to vulnerabilities — specifically GBV, child protection, and trafficking in persons risks — among internally displaced persons in camp and camp-like settings. This aligns closely with GCM Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in situations of vulnerability, including victims of sexual and gender-based violence), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies addressing needs of migrant women and girls, including access to counselling and justice), and 7e (child protection systems). The report examines survivor-centred approaches, referral pathways, psychological first aid, and coordination between CCCM and protection actors — all directly relevant to vulnerability response. The evaluation covers nine operations across multiple countries, providing comparative evidence. However, the report's focus is on IDPs in humanitarian settings rather than international migrants per se, which somewhat limits direct applicability to the GCM framing. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on survivor-centred approaches (Section 1.2 revised theory of change), the evaluation findings on referral pathway gaps, and the recommendations on GBV mainstreaming in CCCM (items 16–20 in Table 10) would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN TEMÁTICA DE LA ASISTENCIA HUMANITARIA DE EMERGENCIA DE LA OIM A PERSONAS REFUGIADAS Y MIGRANTES DE REPÚBLICA BOLIVARIANA DE VENEZUELA EN ECUADOR
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The evaluation appears to center on humanitarian assistance to a highly vulnerable population — Venezuelan refugees and migrants in Ecuador — with explicit attention to vulnerability identification and differential response. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: vulnerability-based prioritization criteria (SOP criteria covering women, children, elderly, persons with disabilities, LGBTIQ+, trafficking victims, GBV survivors); gender-responsive and intersectional approaches to assistance design (Conclusions C1, C2); specific assistance for persons with disabilities (a dedicated output); and referral pathways for cases of extreme vulnerability. Actions 7a (reviewing policies to avoid exacerbating vulnerabilities), 7b (comprehensive policies for vulnerable migrants), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies), 7e (child protection), and 7k (involving local authorities in identification and referral) appear particularly relevant. The conclusions and recommendations sections, along with the description of SOP prioritization criteria and the discussion of kit design for women, would be priority areas for human reviewers to examine. The full report likely contains additional depth on vulnerability assessment and response mechanisms beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN TEMÁTICA DE LA ASISTENCIA HUMANITARIA DE EMERGENCIA DE LA OIM A PERSONAS REFUGIADAS Y MIGRANTES DE REPÚBLICA BOLIVARIANA DE VENEZUELA EN ECUADOR
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The evaluation's primary subject — emergency humanitarian assistance including temporary shelter, hygiene kits, transport, and disability support — directly relates to migrant access to basic services. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: service delivery mechanisms and their adequacy (Conclusions C5-C8); quality assessment of services (C9-C10); barriers to service access including supply chain challenges; and the role of temporary shelters (CAT) as service delivery hubs. Actions 15c (establishing accessible service points at local level) and 15e (health needs of migrants) appear particularly relevant. The findings on timeliness and quality of assistance delivery, the recommendations on extending shelter duration (R6) and improving kit contents (R1, R2), and the discussion of service coordination through GTRM would be priority areas for human reviewers. However, the services analyzed are emergency humanitarian in nature rather than the broader basic services (education, health systems) envisioned in GCM Objective 15, which somewhat limits the score relative to Objective 7.

References.Title: Evaluation of Mental Health and Psychosocial Support in IOM
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The evaluation appears to substantively examine MHPSS programming for migrants in vulnerable situations across the migration cycle — including emergency-affected populations, returnees, displaced persons, trafficking victims, and other vulnerable groups. The excerpts suggest that IOM's MHPSS approach explicitly targets 'the most vulnerable' across all stages of migration, with attention to gender-responsive and child-sensitive approaches (actions 7b, 7c, 7f). The report discusses psychological and counselling services, access to care, and protection referrals — directly relevant to action 7c on gender-responsive migration policies including psychological counselling. The case studies (Ukraine/Poland, West and Central Africa, Colombia) appear to illustrate MHPSS responses to vulnerability in different migration contexts. The conclusions on relevance and effectiveness sections, and Recommendation 6 on building organizational capacity, would likely be most valuable for a synthesis specialist to review. The report's focus is primarily institutional/organizational rather than policy-level vulnerability reduction, which limits the score somewhat, but the programmatic content on serving vulnerable migrants appears substantive.

References.Title: Evaluation of Mental Health and Psychosocial Support in IOM
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly regarding psychosocial dimensions of sustainable reintegration (action 21h). The evaluation appears to substantively cover IOM's MHPSS programming in return and reintegration contexts — the AVRR (Assisted Voluntary Return and Reintegration) thematic area features prominently, with dedicated tools including the 'Reintegration Handbook,' 'Reintegration Counselling: A psychosocial approach,' and guidance notes for migrants with health needs including mental health conditions. The West and Central Africa case study focuses on migration management including return contexts, and the Colombia case study addresses transition and recovery. The report discusses psychosocial reintegration assistance at individual and community levels, counsellor support for returnees' emotional complexity, and MHPSS integration throughout the reintegration process. Action 21h on psycho-social assistance, social protection access, and vocational training for returning migrants appears directly addressed. For a synthesis specialist, the AVRR-related tools and guidance sections, the West and Central Africa case study, and the conclusions on effectiveness and sustainability would appear most relevant as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional evidence on reintegration programming beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Evaluation of IOM’s Institutional Approach to the Implementation of the Humanitarian Development Peace Nexus
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly around action 2f (strengthening collaboration between humanitarian and development actors through joint analysis, multi-donor approaches and multi-year funding). The entire evaluation centers on IOM's implementation of the Humanitarian-Development-Peace Nexus, which is fundamentally about addressing root causes and structural drivers of displacement and migration — the core concern of Objective 2. The report explicitly discusses addressing 'drivers of human mobility,' with a dedicated recommendation (C) on strengthening analysis of structural drivers of conflict and displacement, and recommendation (B) on programming that addresses 'multisectoral drivers of human mobility.' The evaluation's focus on resilience, recovery, and development programming in fragile contexts (Colombia, Iraq, Somalia) directly relates to action 2b (investing in programmes to eliminate adverse drivers) and 2f (humanitarian-development collaboration). The recommendations on multi-year funding (H), integrated programming (B), and joint assessments (C) are particularly relevant. Synthesis specialists would want to review the programming and assessment/analysis sections, as well as the conclusions, noting that the report examines these themes through an institutional/organizational lens rather than as direct programmatic outcomes for affected populations.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF IOM'S CONTRIBUTION TO THE MIGRATION DIALOGUE FOR SOUTHERN AFRICA (MIDSA)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 23. The evaluation's primary subject — MIDSA as an Inter-State Consultation Mechanism on Migration — is precisely the type of multilateral migration governance mechanism that Objective 23 concerns. The report appears to examine how IOM supports regional inter-state cooperation on migration governance across 16 SADC member states, which directly relates to Actions 23a (supporting collective GCM implementation), 23d (capacity-building mechanisms), and 23e (bilateral/regional/multilateral partnerships for migration policy). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: IOM's institutional roles in facilitating regional dialogue; the alignment of MIDSA with GCM principles (GCM integration is described as a 'constant feature of MIDSA deliberations'); sustainability of multilateral cooperation mechanisms; and the relationship between MIDSA and broader frameworks like the SADC and AU. The evaluation criteria sections (coherence, sustainability, effectiveness) all appear to engage with how international cooperation mechanisms function and can be strengthened. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on coherence (GCM alignment), sustainability (long-term viability of inter-state mechanisms), and recommendations on IOM's multiple roles would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The report likely offers rare evaluative evidence on how ISCMs function as vehicles for GCM implementation at the regional level — a relatively underexplored area in the evidence base.

References.Title: NAMIBIA MIGRATION PROFILE PROJECT, PROJECT EVALUATION REPORT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The evaluated project — the Namibia Migration Profile — is fundamentally about developing a country-specific migration profile with disaggregated data (Action 1j explicitly), building national capacity for data collection and analysis (Action 1c), and using that data to inform evidence-based policymaking (Actions 1d, 1g, 1i). The report title, project objectives, and all major evaluation criteria directly address these themes. The excerpts suggest multiple findings address: the development and dissemination of sex- and age-disaggregated migration data (Outcome 1 in Matrix 1), the inter-ministerial coordination mechanism for data sharing (Output 1.1, touching Action 1i), the data mapping/assessment exercise (Output 1.2), and the transition of data into policy (limited but growing, per Section 3). The recommendations on enhancing publicity, updating the profile, and strengthening institutional frameworks for data collection are directly relevant to Actions 1a, 1c, and 1j. For a synthesis specialist, the effectiveness matrix (Section 3.1), the sustainability and impact criteria (Section 3.2), and the recommendations section (Section 4) would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional detail on data gaps, disaggregation methodology, and capacity-building outcomes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: IOM'S PRIVATE SECTOR PARTNERSHIP STRATEGY 2016-2020: A THEMATIC EVALUATION
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 23, specifically regarding the role of non-state actors — particularly the private sector — in international partnerships for migration governance. The evaluation explicitly examines IOM's institutional strategy for building partnerships with private sector entities (companies, foundations, high-net-worth individuals), which relates to Action 23d on mobilizing technical, financial and human resources from the private sector and other sources to assist states in fulfilling GCM commitments, and Action 23e on developing targeted partnerships to address migration policy issues. The report appears to provide substantive analysis of: (1) the institutional architecture for managing such partnerships (DRD, USA for IOM, regional and country offices); (2) resource mobilization challenges and funding mechanisms; (3) the types of partnerships pursued (advocacy, innovation, resource mobilization, implementation); and (4) lessons learned about what makes private sector partnerships effective or ineffective. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on effectiveness (partnership types and outcomes), efficiency (funding mechanisms and investment returns), and the recommendations on resource mobilization and partnership management would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The report's comparative analysis of IOM against other UN agencies (UNHCR, WFP) on private sector engagement may also offer useful benchmarking evidence. It should be noted that this report examines private sector partnerships as an institutional strategy rather than inter-state cooperation mechanisms per se, which is the more traditional focus of Objective 23 — this limits the score somewhat. However, the GCM's Action 23d explicitly references the private sector as a partner in implementation, making this report's evidence genuinely relevant.

References.Title: RAPPORT FINAL D'EVALUATION DU PROGRAMME DE RENFORCEMENT DE CAPACITES DU GOUVERNEMENT GUINEEN SUR LA GESTION DES FRONTIERES (CBBM)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The CBBM project's core purpose appears to be building Guinea's capacity to collect, analyze, and centralize migration data at border posts — directly relating to Actions 1c (building national capacities in data collection and analysis) and 1i (enhancing collaboration between migration data units and using administrative records for statistical purposes). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of the MIDAS system installation and use, which is explicitly a migration data collection and analysis tool. The results matrix shows indicators tracking data collection performance, and the report discusses improvements in data quality and registration at borders. Key sections for human reviewers to prioritize would include the 'Principaux Résultats' section on MIDAS installation and use, the discussion of data centralization at DCPAF, and the recommendations on inter-connectivity between border posts and the central server. However, the report's focus is on operational capacity-building rather than on data policy frameworks or statistical standards per se, which limits its depth on some of Objective 1's more policy-oriented actions (e.g., 1a, 1b, 1g).

References.Title: RAPPORT FINAL D'EVALUATION DU PROGRAMME DE RENFORCEMENT DE CAPACITES DU GOUVERNEMENT GUINEEN SUR LA GESTION DES FRONTIERES (CBBM)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The entire project evaluated — the CBBM programme — is explicitly a border management capacity-building initiative for Guinea, making border management the central and primary subject of the evaluation. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly relevant to Objective 11: integrated border management structures (Actions 11a, 11b), use of information and communication technology for border crossing procedures (MIDAS system, biometric readers, servers — Action 11b), technical cooperation for equipment and capacity-building (Action 11d), and cross-border coordination (joint operations with Canada at COM, cooperation with France). The results matrix directly tracks border management outcomes including data collection, personnel training, infrastructure renovation, and patrol operations. The report appears to cover both air (Conakry airport), land (Boundou Fourdou, Kourémalé, Pamelap), and maritime (Centre des Opérations Maritimes) border management dimensions. For human reviewers, the 'Principaux Résultats' section, the results matrix (Matrice 1), and the 'Pérennisation' and 'Efficacité' sections would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The recommendations on inter-connectivity, energy supply, and extension to other border posts also offer practical insights for synthesis on integrated border management. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Final Evaluation of DS Netherlands Project: ‘Contribute to Durable Solutions Through Sustainable Returns and Reintegration in Iraq’
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. Access to basic services — particularly water, electricity, and livelihood support — is a central theme of the project and evaluation. Quick Impact Projects (QIPs) rehabilitated water distribution systems and electricity networks, directly addressing service access for displaced and returnee populations. The effectiveness section discusses outcomes for thousands of households gaining improved access to essential services. The coverage section examines whether vulnerable groups (PwDs, marginalized minorities) were able to access services equitably. Community Resource Centres provided reintegration services including psychosocial support. While the population is IDPs/returnees rather than international migrants per se, the evidence on inclusive service delivery, targeting of vulnerable groups, and infrastructure rehabilitation for underserved communities relates to Actions 15c (holistic accessible service points) and 15e (health needs). A synthesis specialist would likely want to review the effectiveness section on QIPs and CRCs, the coverage analysis, and recommendations on feedback mechanisms and intersecting vulnerabilities, while noting the IDP rather than international migrant context.

References.Title: Final Evaluation of DS Netherlands Project: ‘Contribute to Durable Solutions Through Sustainable Returns and Reintegration in Iraq’
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute relevant evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. Social cohesion is an explicit and recurring theme throughout the evaluation. Community-Led Initiatives (CLIs) are described as enhancing social cohesion by fostering collaboration between community members, local leaders, and government authorities. The impact section discusses how community-led activities 'fostered trust, reduced tensions, and contributed to localized stability.' Livelihood programs and vocational training relate to Action 16d (labour market integration). The project's multi-community approach — engaging returnees, IDPs, and host communities together — directly addresses the reciprocal inclusion dynamic central to Objective 16. Recommendations on psychosocial support and community-building initiatives further reinforce this theme. The population context (IDPs/returnees rather than international migrants) is a limitation for direct GCM applicability, but the evidence on social cohesion programming, community engagement approaches, and inclusion of marginalized groups would likely be valuable. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the effectiveness section on CLIs and community-driven initiatives, the impact section on social cohesion outcomes, and Recommendation 4 on psychosocial support.

References.Title: Final Evaluation of DS Netherlands Project: ‘Contribute to Durable Solutions Through Sustainable Returns and Reintegration in Iraq’
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Safe and dignified return and sustainable reintegration are the explicit and central subjects of the entire project and evaluation. The project title itself — 'Contribute to Durable Solutions Through Sustainable Returns and Reintegration in Iraq' — directly mirrors the language of Objective 21. The evaluation systematically assesses: facilitated voluntary movements (FVM) providing transportation and reinstallation grants (Action 21b on voluntary return programmes); reintegration services through Community Resource Centres (Action 21h on social protection, psychosocial assistance, vocational training); community-level needs identification and local authority engagement (Action 21i); infrastructure rehabilitation to create conducive conditions for return (Action 21h); and sustainability of reintegration outcomes. Multiple evaluation criteria — effectiveness, sustainability, impact — are analyzed specifically through the lens of return and reintegration quality. Recommendations directly address strengthening reintegration support, livelihood opportunities, psychosocial assistance, and community conditions. The 'Go-and-See' visits mentioned relate to informed consent dimensions of Action 21b. For a synthesis specialist, the effectiveness section on FVM activities, the sustainability analysis of reintegration outcomes, the impact section, and recommendations 1, 2, 4, and 7 would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The full report likely contains substantially more evidence on reintegration programming than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: IOM PARTNERSHIP ON HEALTH AND MOBILITY IN EAST AND SOUTHERN AFRICA (PHAMESA) - END OF THE PROGRAM EVALUATION
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in national and local health care policies and plans). The PHAMESA programme is explicitly and centrally focused on improving health service access for migrants and mobile populations in East and Southern Africa — this is the programme's core mandate. The evaluation examines service delivery and capacity building (SDCB) as a primary component, assessing how well the programme has strengthened health systems' capacity to serve migrant communities, reduced barriers to access, and built partner capacity. The executive summary, findings on effectiveness (Part 4), and the SDCB component recommendations (Section 4.1) appear particularly rich for a synthesis on migrant health service access. The report also addresses: the role of SBCC in promoting service uptake; capacity building of government and NGO service providers; sustainability of service delivery systems; and the challenge of avoiding parallel processes that cannot be absorbed by government providers — all directly relevant to action 15e. The human rights-based approach framing and gender-responsiveness dimensions add further value. Synthesis specialists should prioritize the SDCB findings, the sustainability section (Part 6/Section 5), and the effectiveness findings (Part 4) as starting points. It is likely that the full report contains substantially more evidence on health service access than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: A THEMATIC EVALUATION OF THE IOM'S MIGRATION CRISIS OPERATIONAL FRAMEWORK (MCOF)
