References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly regarding reintegration in post-crisis contexts. The MCOF explicitly covers the full crisis cycle including 'transition and recovery' phases, and '(Re)integration Assistance' and 'Community Stabilization and Transition' are among the 15 sectors of assistance. The South Sudan case study — involving IDPs returning to communities — likely contains substantive content on return and reintegration programming. The report discusses IOM's role in 'Progressive Resolution of Displacement Situations' (PRDS), which directly relates to durable solutions and sustainable reintegration (Actions 21b, 21h, 21i). The evaluation's analysis of effectiveness in transition and recovery phases, and the recommendations on strengthening post-crisis programming, would likely be relevant to a synthesis on reintegration. A synthesis specialist would particularly want to review the South Sudan case study sections and the findings on transition and recovery effectiveness, as well as recommendations on post-crisis programming. It should be noted that the reintegration discussed is primarily in the context of displacement rather than international return/readmission, which may limit direct applicability to some aspects of Objective 21.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT FOR “PROMOTING ETHICAL CONDUCT AND PROFESSIONALISING THE RECRUITMENT INDUSTRY IN SRI LANKA”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 6. The project title itself — 'Promoting Ethical Conduct and Professionalizing the Recruitment Industry in Sri Lanka' — directly mirrors the language of this objective. The excerpts suggest that virtually all project activities, outcomes, and recommendations are oriented around fair and ethical recruitment: development of a Code of Ethical Conduct (CoEC), Standard Operating Procedures for Licensed Foreign Employment Agencies (LFEAs), training curricula, monitoring mechanisms, and research on recruitment intermediaries. These align closely with Actions 6c (improving regulations on public and private recruitment agencies, mandatory monitoring mechanisms), 6f (strengthening enforcement of fair recruitment norms), 6d (ensuring migrant workers are informed of their rights and obligations), and 6l (taking into consideration ILO General Principles and IRIS). The executive summary, conclusions, and recommendations sections all appear to directly address recruitment industry regulation, capacity building, and ethical conduct enforcement. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on SOP development, the M&E framework for CoEC adherence, the training curriculum for LFEAs, the research on recruitment intermediaries, and the operational checklist for labour attachés would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT FOR “PROMOTING ETHICAL CONDUCT AND PROFESSIONALISING THE RECRUITMENT INDUSTRY IN SRI LANKA”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to contain meaningful content relevant to a synthesis on GCM Objective 14. A dedicated project output involves updating the Operational Manual for Labour Sections of Sri Lankan Diplomatic Missions in Destination Countries and developing an operational checklist for labour attachés in Sri Lankan embassies to validate foreign recruitment agents and protect labour migrants. Field research was conducted in Singapore, Jordan, and Kuwait to inform this work. This aligns with Action 14a (building consular capacities and training consular officers) and Action 14d (strengthening consular capacities to identify, protect and assist nationals abroad who are victims of labour rights violations). The recommendations specifically call for effective implementation of the checklist by labour attachés and annual monitoring of its utilization. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on the operational checklist development, the field research in destination countries, and the recommendations on labour attaché capacity would appear most relevant as starting points for deeper review. This is a secondary theme relative to the core focus on recruitment agency regulation, but appears substantive enough to warrant review for a synthesis on consular protection.

References.Title: Renforcement du dialogue binational entre Haïti et la République dominicaine pour promouvoir la cohésion sociale dans la zone frontalière
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. Social cohesion is explicitly the central objective of the evaluated project — the title itself references 'cohésion sociale' — and the evaluation systematically assesses how the project promoted inclusion, intercultural dialogue, and mutual respect between Haitian and Dominican communities. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly relevant to GCM Objective 16: intercultural dialogue mechanisms (action 16a, 16f), empowerment of migrant women and youth (action 16e), community-level integration programmes including the Laboratoires d'Innovation Sociale (action 16f, 16g), and efforts to shift public perceptions of migrants. The evaluation criteria of 'efficacité,' 'durabilité,' and 'sensibilité aux droits humains et au genre' all appear to generate findings directly relevant to social cohesion outcomes. The executive summary's conclusions on the transformation of social perceptions, the LIS methodology, and the recommendations on institutionalizing dialogue mechanisms would appear particularly valuable for synthesis specialists. The full report likely contains extensive evidence on community-level social cohesion programming in a fragile, cross-border context — a relatively underrepresented setting in the evidence base.

References.Title: Renforcement du dialogue binational entre Haïti et la République dominicaine pour promouvoir la cohésion sociale dans la zone frontalière
References.Abstract: The excerpts suggest this report would likely contribute substantial evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 17. Combating stigmatization of Haitian migrants and promoting evidence-based public discourse on migration are explicitly stated project objectives. The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of awareness-raising campaigns targeting border communities (action 17f), efforts to counter anti-Haitian discourse in the Dominican Republic (action 17g), and the project's contribution to changing perceptions of migrants. The theory of change explicitly references reducing stigmatization as a key mechanism. The evaluation's conclusions note that 'la transformation des perceptions sociales sur la migration demeure un enjeu' — indicating substantive analysis of this challenge. The sections on Result 1 (population awareness), the efficacy conclusions on sensitization campaigns, and the recommendations on coupling campaigns with economic actions would appear most relevant for synthesis specialists. The report likely offers evidence on what works (and what doesn't) in changing discriminatory attitudes in a polarized bilateral context — directly relevant to actions 17c, 17f, and 17g.

References.Title: INTERNAL FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “ASSISTANCE TO NIGERIEN RETURNEES IN AGADEZ AND ZINDER (ANRAZ)”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The ANRAZ project is explicitly and centrally focused on the return and reintegration of Nigerien migrants from Algeria, making this the dominant theme of the evaluation. The project title, objectives, and all three outcomes directly address return and reintegration: Outcome 1 covers direct assistance to returnees; Outcome 2 addresses institutional capacity for managing returns; Outcome 3 focuses on sustainable reintegration in communities of origin. The evaluation appears to provide substantive analysis across multiple dimensions of GCM Objective 21, including: the bilateral repatriation arrangement between Algeria and Niger (Action 21a); gender-responsive and community-inclusive reintegration programming (Action 21b); the sustainability of reintegration outcomes (Action 21h); and the identification of community needs upon return (Action 21i). The conclusions section provides detailed assessments of reintegration effectiveness, sustainability challenges, and gender considerations. The lessons learned section offers practical guidance on reintegration programme design. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on reintegration activities (Outcome 3), sustainability conclusions, gender analysis of reintegration, and the lessons learned on community involvement and power dynamics would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional evidence on reintegration modalities, beneficiary selection, and institutional capacity-building beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Final independent internal evaluation of the project “Recover from the COVID-19 Adverse Mobility Restrictions Induced Impacts in Madagascar (REfrAMe Madagascar)”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The evaluation explicitly examines an immigration and border management (IBM) project focused on strengthening integrated, coordinated border management at international points of entry in Madagascar. The project title, evaluation questions, and stated objectives all center on border management capacity — making this a core subject of the report rather than a peripheral theme. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly relevant to GCM Objective 11: (1) Action 11a — the project involved cross-agency coordination training (PAF, DGD, DVSSER, ACM, GN), joint simulation exercises, and capacity-building for border officials on health security measures, with findings on improved inter-agency coordination; (2) Action 11b — the project procured and installed ICT and medical equipment at three POEs, upgraded border crossing systems for disease surveillance, and implemented SOPs for health controls, with findings on IHR compliance improvements; (3) Action 11d — technical cooperation and equipment provision to strengthen border management capacities is a primary output of the project. The report appears to provide evidence on what works in building integrated border management systems in a developing country context (Madagascar), including challenges (time constraints, geographic coverage limited to 3 of 9 POEs, coordination difficulties), enabling factors (IOM dynamism, stakeholder commitment), and sustainability considerations. For a synthesis specialist, the Findings sections on Relevance (4.1), Effectiveness (4.3), and Sustainability (4.6) would appear particularly valuable as starting points, along with the Results Matrix (Table 1) which documents achieved indicators. The recommendations sections also contain actionable insights on border management system strengthening. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Final independent internal evaluation of the project “Recover from the COVID-19 Adverse Mobility Restrictions Induced Impacts in Madagascar (REfrAMe Madagascar)”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12. The project explicitly focused on developing and implementing Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for health controls at points of entry, training border officials on screening and case management of suspect cases, and conducting simulation exercises to test referral procedures — all of which relate directly to Action 12c (establishing gender-responsive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures) and Action 12b (specialized trainings for first responders and border officials). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of SOP development and implementation, with findings indicating that 158 frontline officials were trained on revised SOPs (exceeding the target of 120), and that participants demonstrated improved capacity to implement coordinated health controls. The Results Matrix documents indicators on the percentage of border officials trained who demonstrate increased capacities to coordinate and implement health controls. However, the screening and referral focus here is specifically on public health/disease surveillance rather than the broader migration status determination and protection referral context that GCM Objective 12 primarily addresses. This distinction means border management is the dominant theme (scoring higher on Objective 11) while Objective 12 captures a meaningful but secondary dimension. For a synthesis specialist, the Effectiveness section (4.3) and the Results Matrix would be the most relevant starting points, particularly the discussion of SOP development and training outcomes. The recommendations on mixing theoretical and practical training sessions may also offer transferable insights.

References.Title: RESPONDING TO THE NEEDS OF MOST VULNERABLE POPULATIONS IN LIBYA THROUGH COVID-19 PREVENTION, ASSISTANCE AND ACCESS TO VACCINATION
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project's explicit focus is on identifying and assisting migrants in situations of extreme vulnerability — particularly those in detention centres and at disembarkation points — which aligns closely with actions 7a, 7b, 7j, and 7k. The excerpts describe needs-based targeting of the most vulnerable migrants, provision of PPE, hygiene kits and medical supplies, health assessments in detention centres and urban areas, and engagement of local authorities in identification and assistance. The evaluation's findings on what worked in reaching vulnerable populations, the challenges posed by insecurity and limited access, and the recommendations for expanding services to address nutrition, WASH, and other infectious diseases would likely add value to a synthesis on vulnerability response. The sections on effectiveness (outputs delivered to migrants in DCs and DPs), the discussion of gender-responsive approaches, and the lessons learned on needs-based targeting appear particularly worth reviewing. The report also addresses the specific vulnerabilities of women and children in detention, touching on action 7c. Specialists should note that the analysis is primarily programmatic and evaluative rather than policy-analytical, but the operational evidence on vulnerability response in a complex humanitarian context appears substantive.

References.Title: RESPONDING TO THE NEEDS OF MOST VULNERABLE POPULATIONS IN LIBYA THROUGH COVID-19 PREVENTION, ASSISTANCE AND ACCESS TO VACCINATION
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in national health policies and plans, facilitating affordable and non-discriminatory access). The project's primary objective is explicitly to improve access to health services — including COVID-19 prevention, vaccination, and basic health care — for the most vulnerable migrants in Libya, regardless of their migration status. The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of this access in detail: PPE and hygiene kit distribution to over 7,000 migrants in detention centres and at disembarkation points, placement of medical staff at PoEs, awareness-raising campaigns, and vaccination campaign support. The findings on what worked in reaching migrants with health services, the challenges of access in a conflict-affected context, and the recommendations for expanding services to cover nutrition, WASH, and other infectious diseases all appear directly relevant to action 15e and potentially 15c (holistic, accessible service points). The effectiveness section (with detailed output data), the context section (describing health system gaps and migrant health vulnerabilities), and the recommendations section would appear particularly valuable for human reviewers conducting a synthesis on migrant access to basic services. The report also provides evidence on institutional capacity building for health service delivery, which relates to the 'strengthening capacities for service provision' dimension of action 15e.

References.Title: EXTERNAL INDEPENDENT END OF PROJECT SUMMATIVE EVALUATION OF ASSISTANCE TO DISCHARGED DEFENCE PERSONNEL WITHIN THE SCOPE OF DEFENCE SECTOR REFORM IN SERBIA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly regarding sustainable reintegration (Action 21h). The report evaluates a five-year programme explicitly designed to support the socio-economic reintegration of discharged military personnel into civilian life in Serbia, with components covering vocational training, salary support, and business creation grants. While the population is domestic discharged military rather than returned migrants in the strict GCM sense, the reintegration modalities, approaches, and lessons identified appear highly transferable to the sustainable reintegration of returning migrants. The report appears to offer substantive analysis of: what makes reintegration assistance effective (individualized counselling, humane approach, medium-term timeframe); the importance of business creation support with matching grant requirements for ownership and sustainability; the role of capacity building of national structures for handover; and the value of exit strategies and national ownership. The conclusions section explicitly compares this project favorably to other reintegration projects and identifies it as going 'beyond immediate needs.' The lessons identified sections (9.1-9.4) and the conclusions (Section 8) would appear particularly valuable for synthesis specialists examining reintegration programme design and effectiveness. The executive summary's discussion of business sustainability rates (~84% self-employed or generating income) and the grant modality design could contribute evidence on Action 21h. Specialists should note that the population is not returned migrants per se, which limits direct applicability, but the programmatic evidence on reintegration approaches appears substantively relevant.

References.Title: EVALUATION REPORT FOR FINAL INTERNAL EVALUATION OF PROJECT "COMBATING TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS IN GEORGIA AND ADDRESSING THE DEMAND SIDE OF TRAFFICKING"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Combating trafficking in persons is the explicit and sole primary subject of the evaluated project, as reflected in the project title itself ('Combating Trafficking in Persons in Georgia and Addressing the Demand Side of Trafficking'). The report appears to directly address multiple associated actions under Objective 10: Action 10c (monitoring irregular migration routes exploited by trafficking networks, strengthening bilateral cooperation on prosecution and victim identification); Action 10d (strengthening cooperation between relevant actors including financial intelligence — referenced in recommendations); Action 10e (measures addressing vulnerabilities of women, men, girls and boys at risk of trafficking, facilitating access to justice); Action 10g (strengthening legislation and procedures to enhance prosecution of traffickers, victim protection); Action 10h (protection and assistance to trafficking victims); Action 10i (training programmes for public officials and law enforcement to identify signs of trafficking); and Action 10j (awareness-raising campaigns for migrants on risks of trafficking). The evaluation covers the full project cycle including design, implementation, and outcomes across law enforcement capacity building, inter-agency coordination through the National Referral Mechanism, victim identification, and demand-side awareness campaigns at the Georgian-Turkish border. Key sections for human reviewers to prioritize include: the effectiveness analysis of the information campaign and its limitations; the capacity building findings on law enforcement, labor inspectors, and social workers; the sustainability conclusions on institutionalization of training; and the recommendations on prosecuting clients of trafficking victims and financial sector cooperation. The full report likely contains additional detailed findings beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT:  STRENGTHEN OPERATIONAL CAPACITIES FOR GAMBIAN  AUTHORITIES TO MANAGE BORDERS, RESPOND TO HEALTH CRISIS  AND SOCIO-ECONOMIC IMPACTS OF COVID-19
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. Border management in an integrated, secure and coordinated manner appears to be the central and dominant theme of the evaluated project. The project title explicitly references strengthening operational capacities to manage borders, and the evaluation assesses this as its primary outcome area. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple actions under Objective 11: action 11a (international and regional border management cooperation, capacity-building, whole-of-government approaches) is directly addressed through the Inter-Agency Border Coordinating Committees (IABCCs) and multisectoral stakeholder collaborations; action 11b (integrated border management structures, border crossing procedures, use of ICT) is addressed through MIDAS installation, training, and border infrastructure construction; action 11d (technical cooperation agreements for assets and equipment) is addressed through the speed boat and vehicle donations; and action 11g (cross-border collaboration on treatment of persons crossing borders) appears in the ECOWAS context and potential Senegal partnerships. The evaluation covers relevance, effectiveness, efficiency, impact, and sustainability of these border management interventions with dedicated sections and multiple findings. The sections on Interim Outcome 1 (strengthened maritime and land border management), the IABCC establishment, MIDAS implementation, and the recommendations on border management would appear particularly valuable for human reviewers to prioritize. The report likely offers evidence on what works in integrated border management capacity-building in a West African context, including lessons on sustainability of hard infrastructure and coordination mechanisms.

References.Title: FINAL REPORT - MID TERM EVALUATION MIDA FINNSOM HEALTH AND EDUCATION PROJECT SOMALIA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The MIDA FINNSOM Health and Education Project is explicitly designed around harnessing diaspora expertise for sustainable development in Somalia — this appears to be the central subject of the evaluation. The project directly operationalizes Actions 19c (research/evidence on non-financial contributions of diaspora), 19d (government structures to facilitate diaspora engagement), 19i (enabling diaspora to carry out professional activities in home countries), and 19j (knowledge and skills transfer between diaspora and country of origin). The excerpts suggest multiple findings directly address diaspora contributions to institutional capacity building in health and education, with quantified outcomes (patient intake increases, mortality rate reductions, curriculum development). The executive summary, project background, and conclusions sections all appear to contain substantive analysis of diaspora development contributions. The recommendations section also addresses diaspora recruitment, retention, and partnership strategies. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on diaspora expert outcomes in hospitals and universities, the sustainability analysis, and the recommendations on diaspora engagement strategies would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional relevant evidence on diaspora-development linkages beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Final Evaluation of the Community Revitalization Program (CRP)- Phase XII
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. Access to basic services is a central and recurring theme throughout the report, with substantive analysis across multiple sections. The program explicitly focuses on improving access to healthcare (primary health centres), shelter, legal services, and social services for IDPs, returnees, and host communities. The report discusses barriers to service access, health infrastructure investment, and the role of service access in supporting reintegration and preventing secondary displacement. This appears to touch on GCM Actions 15c (holistic and accessible service points), 15e (health needs of migrants in national health policies), and 15f (education access). The Relevance section discusses health activities and service access in depth; the Impact section reports on 'increased access to basic essential services' as a key impact theme; and Recommendation 2 addresses financial aid for medical expenses. The Sustainability section also identifies access to basic services as a key determinant of whether community members remain in their communities. Human reviewers would likely find the Relevance, Impact, and Sustainability sections, as well as Recommendations 2 and 3, particularly valuable as starting points. It should be noted that the report evaluates a specific program rather than national service delivery policy, which somewhat limits its generalizability for synthesis purposes.

References.Title: Final Evaluation of the Community Revitalization Program (CRP)- Phase XII
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. Social cohesion and reintegration appear as the dominant themes of the entire evaluation, explicitly named in program objectives, evaluation questions, and findings throughout. The report provides substantive analysis of social cohesion-strengthening activities (recreational events, community initiatives, sports, cultural activities), inclusion mechanisms, and the conditions enabling or undermining social reintegration of IDPs and returnees. Key impact themes reported include 'increased social cohesion,' 'empowerment,' and 'improved dignity.' The program's 'whole community approach' — supporting all community members regardless of status to avoid social division — is analyzed as a best practice directly relevant to GCM Actions 16a (mutual respect and social cohesion), 16f (community centres and intercultural dialogue), and 16h (multicultural activities). The report also discusses livelihood integration (Action 16d), gender empowerment (Action 16e), and school/youth inclusion. The Relevance, Impact, Sustainability, and Lessons Learned sections appear particularly rich for synthesis purposes, as does Recommendation 6 on social cohesion activities. The evaluation covers a 12-month program across 9 Iraqi governorates with mixed-methods data from 739 beneficiary surveys, 18 FGDs, and 26 KIIs, suggesting a substantial evidence base. Human reviewers would likely want to prioritize the Impact section's discussion of reintegration outcomes and the Lessons Learned section's best practices on inclusive programming.

References.Title: Final Evaluation of the Community Revitalization Program (CRP)- Phase XII
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Safe, dignified, and voluntary return and sustainable reintegration are the explicit and primary subjects of the entire evaluation. The program's stated objective is 'improving conditions for dignified and voluntary durable solutions to progressively resolve the displacement of IDPs through returns, local integration, and settlement in new locations.' The report provides substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly relevant to GCM Actions 21b (gender-responsive return and reintegration programmes), 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through social protection, psychosocial assistance, vocational training, employment), and 21i (identifying and addressing needs of communities to which migrants return). The evaluation covers facilitated voluntary movement (FVM) activities, housing rehabilitation, MHPSS for returnees, livelihood support, infrastructure investment, and capacity-building for reintegration actors — all core elements of sustainable reintegration. The report also discusses factors undermining sustainability of reintegration (policy changes, security instability, service gaps) and best practices for supporting dignified return. The mixed-methods evaluation across 9 governorates with substantial primary data provides a rich evidence base. Human reviewers would likely want to prioritize the Effectiveness, Impact, Sustainability, and Lessons Learned sections, as well as Recommendations 1, 2, 3, and 4, as starting points for deeper review. The J1 rehabilitation camp activities for returnees from Al-Hol camp (NES) also appear particularly relevant for synthesis on return and reintegration programming.

References.Title: Informe Inicial:  Evaluación Ex Post del  Proyecto de Fortalecimiento de  los procesos de retorno y  reintegración en el Paraguay
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to contribute meaningful evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project explicitly produced a study on migration dynamics with gender and inclusive data ('Dinámicas migratorias del Paraguay con enfoque de género y datos inclusivos'), which directly relates to actions 1b, 1d, and 1j. The excerpts indicate that generating disaggregated data on returned migrants — including socioeconomic, demographic, educational and labor profiles — was a core product of the project. The evaluation's conclusions on efficacy highlight that the project 'satisfied information needs on migration dynamics and, in particular, on Paraguayan nationals returned to Paraguay,' and Recommendation 4 explicitly calls for deepening evidence generation on returnee profiles using administrative records. The report also discusses the use of country-specific migration profiles (Table I on emigrant stocks by country of origin). However, data collection appears as one of two main project components rather than the sole focus, and the analysis is primarily about institutional capacity to generate data rather than data systems per se. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the efficacy conclusions, Recommendation 4, and the sections describing the migration dynamics study as starting points — particularly for actions 1d (effects and contributions of migrants) and 1j (country-specific migration profiles). The full report likely contains more detail on the data products generated.

References.Title: Informe Inicial:  Evaluación Ex Post del  Proyecto de Fortalecimiento de  los procesos de retorno y  reintegración en el Paraguay
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Return and reintegration of Paraguayan nationals is the explicit and central subject of the evaluated project, as stated in the project title ('Fortalecimiento de los procesos de retorno y reintegración en el Paraguay') and all evaluation questions. The excerpts suggest that multiple conclusions and findings directly address sustainable reintegration, institutional capacity for return management, and interinstitutional coordination — all core themes under actions 21b, 21h, and 21i. Specifically: the project developed an interinstitutional protocol for coordinating return and reintegration processes (action 21a/21b); it strengthened SEDERREC's capacity to provide reintegration services including vocational training, social assistance, and economic empowerment (action 21h); it generated evidence on returnee profiles to inform policy (action 21i); and it promoted a gender-responsive approach to return and reintegration (action 21b). The evaluation assesses all six OECD/DAC criteria against these themes, with conclusions rated 'excellent' for efficacy and impact. For a synthesis specialist, the conclusions on efficacy, impact, and sustainability, the recommendations to SEDERREC on interinstitutional coordination and evidence generation, and the good practices on collaborative management would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The full report likely contains substantially more evidence on reintegration programming than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: MID TERM EVALUATION REPORT OF THE PROJECT: " PREVENTION OF VIOLENT EXTREMISM THROUGH LOCAL VOICES AND INITIATIVES IN THE WESTERN BALKANS”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly around the reintegration of returning foreign terrorist fighters (FTFs) and their families. The excerpts suggest that the return and reintegration of FTFs became a central operational challenge during project implementation, significantly reshaping priorities in Albania, North Macedonia, and other Western Balkans countries. Specific activities under Outcome 6 appear directly relevant: conducting assessments of institutional capacity gaps to receive and reintegrate returning FTF families (Action 6.3.1), developing a Guidance Paper on strategies for addressing the needs of FTF family members (Action 6.3.2), and organizing validation workshops for inter-ministerial working groups (Action 6.3.3). These activities relate to GCM Objective 21's associated actions on sustainable reintegration (21h), identifying community needs upon return (21i), and gender-responsive reintegration programmes (21b). The excerpts also note that North Macedonia adopted a National Plan for Reintegration, Resocialization, and Rehabilitation of Returnees, supported in part by project activities. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on Outcome 6 activities in Albania and North Macedonia, the conclusions on FTF return challenges, and the recommendations on national reintegration mechanisms would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It should be noted that the reintegration focus here is specifically on FTFs rather than general migrant return, which may limit direct applicability depending on the synthesis scope. The full report may contain more detailed findings on reintegration outcomes than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Programme Evaluation Report Natural Disaster Consortium (NDC) 2015-2020
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its actions related to disaster risk reduction, resilience, and slow/sudden-onset natural disasters (Actions 2b, 2i, 2j, 2k). The programme explicitly addresses disaster preparedness, response and recovery in Pakistan — one of the world's most disaster-prone countries — and links these to reducing vulnerability and displacement drivers. The executive summary notes alignment with the Sendai Framework for DRR 2015-2030 (directly referenced in Action 2a), and the programme's theory of change centers on reducing vulnerability to future shocks. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of resilience-building, early warning, contingency planning, and multi-hazard response across multiple disaster types (floods, earthquakes, droughts, heatwaves). Recommendations on LRRD, climate change adaptation, and DRR (Recommendations 7, 9, 11) appear particularly relevant. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on programme effectiveness, lessons learned, and recommendations — especially content on slow-onset disaster response, community resilience, and the link between humanitarian response and longer-term development. The report does not focus on cross-border displacement or migration per se, which limits its score relative to the full scope of Objective 2.

References.Title: EXTERNAL PROCESS EVALUATION OF IOM'S RESPONSE TO THE LIBYA CRISIS
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The evaluation appears to explicitly examine a large-scale return and repatriation operation — the evacuation and repatriation of approximately 265,000 TCNs from Libya to more than 25 countries — which is the central operational subject of the report. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of the logistics, coordination, and institutional arrangements for return operations, touching on Actions 21a (bilateral/multilateral cooperation frameworks for return), 21b (return programmes), 21d (consular assistance for return), and 21e (safe and dignified return processes). Key content likely relevant for a synthesis includes: the discussion of IOM's Resettlement and Movement Management Division (RMM) and its role in repatriation; the Humanitarian Evacuation Cell (HEC) as a joint IOM-UNHCR coordination mechanism for return; the operational steps of the evacuation (registration, health assessment, ground transport, charter flights); and the findings on coordination challenges and inter-agency collaboration. The report also discusses the scale and speed challenges of responding to mass return movements. Recommendations on expanding staff pools, prepositioning equipment, and establishing emergency funds are directly relevant to improving return operation capacity. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review Chapter 4 (Efficiency and Effectiveness, especially sections 4.2-4.6), Chapter 5 (Information Management in the context of registration for return), and the findings and recommendations chapters for evidence on operational arrangements for large-scale return. The report does not appear to address reintegration sustainability in depth, which limits its relevance to the full scope of GCM Objective 21, but the return and readmission dimensions appear well covered.

References.Title: Innovative e-waste circular economy model is contributing to the resilience of communities in Uganda
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. The report appears to substantively address social cohesion between refugees and host communities, economic inclusion through cooperative employment, and empowerment through skills development — all core themes under this objective. The excerpts suggest that the cooperative model explicitly integrates both refugee and host community members, fostering collaboration and reducing social tensions (connecting to Action 16a on mutual respect and social cohesion, and Action 16f on community centres and intercultural dialogue). The report also discusses labour market integration through structured employment in the formal cooperative economy (Action 16d), gender empowerment through inclusive skills training for women and youth (Action 16e), and vocational training (Action 16g). Section 4.4 appears particularly relevant, with explicit discussion of social cohesion outcomes, gender equity, and community empowerment. The findings on income stability, skill development, and reduced dependency on humanitarian aid also speak to Action 16d. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review sections 4.3 (survey results on economic and social impact) and 4.4 (findings on social cohesion and empowerment), as well as the recommendations in section 5 on community engagement and economic resilience. It should be noted that social cohesion is one of several themes alongside environmental sustainability and circular economy, so it is not the sole focus of the report.

References.Title: Innovative e-waste circular economy model is contributing to the resilience of communities in Uganda
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The report appears to substantively address how displaced populations (refugees) can contribute to sustainable development through circular economy models, entrepreneurship, and community-led business development — themes closely aligned with Actions 19a (implementing the 2030 Agenda through migration's positive effects), 19b (integrating migration into development planning), and 19e (developing targeted support programmes for migrant entrepreneurship and investment). The cooperative model (BEMCOS) represents a concrete example of migrants contributing to local economic development through structured enterprise, connecting to Action 19d (government structures facilitating migrant contributions). The report also discusses innovative financing mechanisms, private sector partnerships, and market-based approaches that speak to Action 19e. Section 4.2 on the circular economy model and section 4.4 on findings regarding economic resilience and community empowerment appear particularly relevant. The recommendations in section 5 on economic resilience, private sector linkages, and scaling also provide policy-relevant insights. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review these sections, noting that the report focuses specifically on a displacement setting in an LDC context, which adds distinctive value for evidence on migrant contributions to development in resource-constrained environments. The report's focus is somewhat broader than just migrant contributions to development, also covering environmental sustainability.

References.Title: RE-HEALTH 2 EVALUATION REPORT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to have meaningful relevance for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The Re-Health2 project is fundamentally built around the collection, analysis, and use of health data on migrants and refugees through the e-PHR (electronic Personal Health Record) tool. The excerpts indicate that disaggregated data collection was a core activity — with specific counts of e-PHRs collected by country, site, and follow-up status — and that the project aimed to 'facilitate data collection to better understand and meet migrants' and refugees' health needs.' The report also references compliance with GDPR and data protection frameworks, touching on Action 1i (use of administrative records for statistical purposes while protecting personal data). The conclusion mentions the tool's potential for 'further technical improvements, including ensuring cross-system compatibility,' which relates to Action 1b on improving comparability of data systems. However, the data collection focus is specific to health assessments rather than broader migration statistics, which limits its direct applicability to the full scope of Objective 1. Synthesis specialists would likely find the sections on implementation site data volumes, the interfacing with IOM's MiMosa tool, and the feasibility/transferability discussion most relevant as pointers for deeper review.

References.Title: RE-HEALTH 2 EVALUATION REPORT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly Action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in national and local health care policies, strengthening capacities for service provision, facilitating affordable and non-discriminatory access, reducing communication barriers, and training health care providers on culturally-sensitive service delivery). The Re-Health2 project is fundamentally about improving migrants' and refugees' access to health services through a standardized electronic health record tool, and the report documents implementation across six EU countries with nearly 20,000 health assessments conducted. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: health service delivery mechanisms in reception settings; the role of health mediators in reducing communication barriers (language-specific mediators for Arabic, Farsi, Urdu); capacity-building for health professionals; coordination with national health authorities (Ministries of Health, KEELPNO); and challenges in service provision (lack of medical devices, pharmaceutical products, high staff turnover). The conclusion explicitly references the WHO Framework of Priorities and Guiding Principles to Promote the Health of Refugees and Migrants as a relevant framework. The project's stated objective — 'integration of newly-arrived migrants and refugees in the EU Member States' health systems' — directly maps to Action 15e. Synthesis specialists would likely find the country-level implementation sections (particularly Greece and Serbia given scale), the capacity-building sections, the challenges and solutions discussions, and the conclusion most valuable as starting points for deeper review. The report also touches on Action 15c (establishing accessible service points) through the reception center-based delivery model.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE HUMANITARIAN REPATRIATION FOR STRANDED MIGRANTS IN LIBYA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, vulnerability is a central and recurring theme throughout this report. The HRSM project explicitly targets migrants in situations of severe vulnerability — those detained in Libyan detention centres, victims of torture and abuse, trafficking victims, unaccompanied minors, and migrants stranded without means to return. The evaluation assesses whether the referral and screening system identifies the most vulnerable candidates, including trafficked persons and unaccompanied minors. The conclusions discuss the need for emergency cash support, shelters for trafficking victims, and psychological support. This appears to relate to GCM Objective 7 actions 7b (identification and assistance of vulnerable migrants), 7e and 7f (protection of migrant children and unaccompanied minors), and 7j (support for migrants caught in crisis situations). For a synthesis specialist, the sections on candidate screening, referral systems, and the conclusions on vulnerability-specific support would appear particularly worth reviewing. The report likely contributes meaningful evidence on how vulnerability identification and response operates in a complex humanitarian context, though the full depth of analysis would need to be confirmed in the complete report.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE HUMANITARIAN REPATRIATION FOR STRANDED MIGRANTS IN LIBYA
References.Abstract: Immigration detention in Libya is a central contextual and substantive element of this report. The HRSM project is explicitly designed as an alternative to indefinite detention — offering voluntary return to migrants detained in Libyan detention centres. The excerpts describe in considerable detail the conditions in detention centres (overcrowding, poor sanitation, torture, ill-treatment), the proliferation of detention centres beyond DCIM control, and the challenges of operating within this system. The recommendations include advocating against the detention of migrants in Libya and lobbying for improvement of conditions. This appears to relate to GCM Objective 13 actions on monitoring detention conditions (13a), reducing negative effects of detention (13f), and ensuring human rights compliance (13g). For a synthesis specialist, the contextual analysis of detention conditions and the discussion of voluntary return as an alternative to detention would appear particularly relevant. The report likely provides useful evidence on the humanitarian consequences of detention and the role of voluntary return programs in this context, though it does not directly evaluate detention policy reform.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE HUMANITARIAN REPATRIATION FOR STRANDED MIGRANTS IN LIBYA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The HRSM project is explicitly and entirely focused on facilitating safe and dignified voluntary return and sustainable reintegration — these are the core subjects of the evaluation. The report title, evaluation objectives, findings, conclusions, and recommendations all center on this theme. The evaluation assesses the full return cycle: pre-departure decision-making and screening, logistics, travel assistance, reception, reintegration planning, disbursement of reintegration grants, and monitoring of outcomes. This directly addresses GCM Objective 21 actions on voluntary return programs (21b), consular cooperation for return (21d), safe and dignified return processes (21e), and sustainable reintegration (21h). The evaluation appears to provide substantive evidence on: the effectiveness of voluntary return programs in humanitarian crisis contexts; challenges in coordinating between sending and receiving missions; the design and implementation of reintegration assistance (in-kind grants for business, education, housing, medical needs); monitoring of reintegration outcomes; and the specific vulnerabilities of returnees from Libya. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on reintegration planning and disbursement, the post-arrival assessment in Senegal and Nigeria, the conclusions on reintegration sustainability, and the recommendations on improving return and reintegration processes would appear particularly valuable starting points. It is likely that the full report contains considerably more detailed evidence on these themes than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: MIDA FINNSOM Health and Education Project Phase II
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The MIDA FINNSOM Phase II project is explicitly and centrally a diaspora engagement for development initiative — its entire design is premised on facilitating Somali diaspora experts' contributions to sustainable development in Somalia through temporary return and skills transfer. This aligns directly with the core theme of Objective 19 and several associated actions. Associated action 19i (enabling diaspora to carry out professional activities and engage in knowledge transfer in home countries without losing employment or residence status) appears to be the operational model being evaluated. Action 19j (building partnerships for knowledge and skills transfer between countries of origin and destination) is substantively addressed through the project's partnership architecture. Action 19d (establishing government structures for diaspora engagement, including advisory boards) is directly reflected in the advisory board co-chaired by Somali ministries. Action 19c (investing in research on non-financial contributions of diaspora) is addressed through the evaluation's impact assessment of knowledge transfer. The executive summary, effectiveness section, impact section, and sustainability section all contain substantive analysis directly relevant to Objective 19. The evaluation assesses the model's effectiveness, challenges (exit strategies, retention, sustainability), and recommendations for strengthening diaspora contributions — all core evidence needs for a synthesis on this objective. For human reviewers, the sections on effectiveness (skills transfer outcomes), sustainability (retention of trained staff, exit strategies), impact (policy development contributions), and recommendations (particularly nos. 8, 10, and 19i-related actions) would appear particularly valuable as starting points. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF MIGRAVENTURE PROJECT AND DEVELOPMENT OF GUIDELINES FOR E-MERGE PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The MigraVenture program appears to be explicitly designed around diaspora entrepreneurship and development contributions — core themes of Objective 19. The program title, stated objectives, and evaluation questions all center on enabling African diaspora migrants in Italy to invest in entrepreneurial ventures in their countries of origin, directly addressing Actions 19e (targeted support programmes and financial products for migrant and diaspora investments and entrepreneurship), 19d (government structures to account for diaspora potential), and 19j (partnerships to promote knowledge and skills transfer between countries of origin and destination). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: the effectiveness of financial mechanisms for diaspora investment (Chapter 3-4), barriers to diaspora entrepreneurship, the role of coaching and mentoring in supporting diaspora business development, and the broader co-development framework (Chapter 2.1 context section). The conclusions chapter (Chapter 5) appears to offer rich lessons learned on what works and what doesn't in diaspora entrepreneurship programs, including reflections on the complexity of return migration and entrepreneurship linkages, which would add nuanced evidence to a synthesis. The literature review on return migration and entrepreneurship (referenced in Chapter 1) may also provide useful framing. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review Chapters 3-4 (program analysis and evaluation criteria) and Chapter 5 (conclusions and recommendations), as well as the theory of change in Chapter 2.2. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Evaluation of the Y-med project
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 5. The Y-MED project appears to be explicitly designed around facilitating regular migration pathways — specifically, the use of Italy's legal framework for TCN internships (Article 27 of Legislative Decree 286/1998) as a regular migration channel. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how this legal pathway functions in practice, including challenges such as visa processing delays, the role of promoting agencies (enti promotori), and the conditions under which regular status can be extended for labour purposes. Actions 5d (flexible, rights-based labour mobility schemes including temporary and circular programmes) and 5b (facilitating cross-regional labour mobility through cooperation arrangements) appear most directly addressed. The sections on visa processing challenges, the legal framework for international internships, and the recommendations on engaging embassies to streamline visa issuance would appear particularly valuable for human reviewers conducting a synthesis on regular migration pathways. The report also touches on Action 5e (skills matching) and the conditions enabling or limiting circular mobility. The depth of analysis on the Italian legal framework for TCN internships and the practical barriers to its use makes this report likely more than peripherally relevant for Objective 5.

References.Title: Evaluation of the Y-med project
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 18. The Y-MED project appears to be explicitly and primarily focused on skills development, transfer, and circulation for young people from North African countries through internships at Italian companies — directly addressing the core theme of this objective. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple associated actions: Action 18e (building global skills partnerships that foster skills development in countries of origin and migrants in destination countries) appears to be the most central, as the project explicitly aims to build skills partnerships between Italy and Egypt, Libya, Morocco, and Tunisia. Action 18g (bilateral partnerships promoting skills development, mobility and circulation, including traineeships with options for employment after completion) appears directly addressed by the project design. Action 18j (internship programmes to recognize existing credentials and provide certificates for newly acquired skills) is also clearly relevant. The report analyzes in depth the effectiveness of skills development outcomes (soft vs. technical skills), the challenges of skills matching between interns' competences and company assignments, and the sustainability of skills acquired. The sections on effectiveness (skills development outcomes), impact (employability improvements), and the lessons learned on matching interns' profiles to company needs would appear particularly valuable for human reviewers. The recommendations on strengthening placement phases, widening university participation, and involving private sector actors in skills matching also directly address Objective 18 themes. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Evaluation of the Y-med project
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The Y-MED project is explicitly framed within IOM's Migration and Development strategy, aiming to empower young migrants as development actors in both origin and destination countries. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how internship-based skills circulation contributes to sustainable development — particularly through Action 19c (research on non-financial contributions of migrants, including knowledge and skills transfer) and Action 19j (building partnerships for knowledge and skills transfer between countries of origin and destination). The report also examines the project's alignment with IOM's Institutional Strategy on Migration and Sustainable Development and Private Sector Partnership Strategy. The sections on impact (interns' employability improvements, companies' awareness of skill circulation value) and the analysis of private sector engagement in countries of origin would appear particularly relevant for human reviewers. However, the development contribution angle is one of several themes — the report's primary analytical focus appears to be on skills development and labour mobility programming effectiveness rather than on development impact per se. The limited evidence of synergies between Italian and North African companies, and the modest impact on public stakeholder engagement, are also relevant observations for a synthesis on this objective.

References.Title: PROMOTING DEVELOPMENT-FRIENDLY MIGRATION POLICIES IN VIET NAM THROUGH ENHANCING THE EVIDENCE BASE FOR POLICY
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project evaluated — the Viet Nam Migration Profile (MPR) — is explicitly and centrally about producing a country-specific migration profile to serve as an evidence base for policy development, which maps directly onto Action 1j ('Develop and use country-specific migration profiles, which include disaggregated data on all migration-relevant aspects'). The project title itself references 'enhancing the evidence base for policy,' and the evaluation assesses the quality, use, and impact of the MPR as a data and evidence tool. The excerpts indicate that the evaluation examined: (a) whether the MPR was used as a reference for policy development; (b) capacity building for government officials in collecting, analysing and using migration data; (c) interagency coordination for data collection and sharing; and (d) gaps in the MPR's coverage (e.g., climate-migration nexus, indebtedness, children left behind). These themes align closely with Actions 1a, 1c, 1d, and 1j. The conclusions and recommendations sections appear particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist — especially the discussion of MPR quality, its use by government and development partners, and the recommendation to develop a generic Migration Profile Template and SOP. The evaluation also touches on monitoring and evaluation of capacity-building outcomes, relevant to Action 1c. A synthesis specialist should prioritize the executive summary, conclusions (Section 7), and recommendations for evidence on how migration profile projects function in practice and what conditions support their uptake in policy processes.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation: Enhancing Mechanisms for Prevention, Detection and Treatment of HIV/Aids and Tuberculosis Among Migrant and Mobile Populations in the South Caucasus Countries
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. A central component of the evaluated project was the Regional Migrant Health Survey, which aimed to collect comparable, disaggregated data on TB and HIV/AIDS incidence among migrant populations across three countries. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of data collection challenges — including methodological adaptation that undermined cross-country comparability, budget underestimation, and governance issues — which directly relates to Actions 1b (international comparability of migration statistics) and 1c (building national capacities in data collection). The report also discusses gender-disaggregated data provision across project activities (Action 1j). The evaluation's conclusions and recommendations on research-based projects (Section D) appear particularly relevant, as they address the limitations of the survey in producing 'actionable evidence' and the implications for policy planning. The sustainability section also touches on how the survey's limited evidentiary value constrained its policy influence. Synthesis specialists working on evidence-based migration data systems, particularly in health contexts, would likely find the findings on survey design, comparability challenges, and research governance valuable. The excerpts suggest this is a substantive but secondary theme — the primary focus is on health service delivery — so a RELEVANT rather than FUNDAMENTAL score is appropriate.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation: Enhancing Mechanisms for Prevention, Detection and Treatment of HIV/Aids and Tuberculosis Among Migrant and Mobile Populations in the South Caucasus Countries
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly Action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in national and local health care policies). The evaluated project is explicitly focused on enhancing mechanisms for prevention, detection, and treatment of HIV/AIDS and TB among migrant and mobile populations — making migrant access to health services the central subject of the evaluation. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly relevant to this objective: the development of migrant-sensitive health services, voluntary counselling and testing, referral and treatment continuity across borders, training of healthcare professionals, and alignment with national health policies in three countries. The evaluation's findings on effectiveness (health services to targeted migrant groups), sustainability (barriers to continued service provision), and impact (awareness raising, piloting migrant-sensitive services) all directly address Action 15e themes. The recommendations on research governance and sustainability planning also have implications for evidence-based health service design for migrants. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on effectiveness, impact, and sustainability — as well as the conclusions on cross-border health service cooperation — would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional relevant evidence on health service delivery models, training outcomes, and policy alignment beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION REPORT - EX-POST INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "IDENTIFYING GAPS AND ASSESSING NEEDS: A COMPARATIVE ASSESSMENT OF COUNTER TRAFFICKING EFFORTS IN COUNTRIES IN THE SOUTH CAUCASUS - AZERBAIJAN, GEORGIA, ARMENIA"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The entire project evaluated is explicitly focused on counter-trafficking (CT) efforts — developing a standardized CT needs assessment tool (CT-NAT) and producing evidence-based assessment reports on the CT landscape in Azerbaijan, Georgia, and Armenia. This appears directly in the project title, evaluation objectives, and throughout the conclusions and recommendations. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis relevant to multiple associated actions under Objective 10: Action 10c (monitoring and strengthening cooperation on prevention, investigation, prosecution, and victim identification) is addressed through the assessment of CT mechanisms and gaps; Action 10e (addressing vulnerabilities of trafficking victims, facilitating access to justice) is touched upon through discussions of victim identification and referral workshops; Action 10g (strengthening legislation and procedures, avoiding criminalization of victims) and 10i (training programmes to identify signs of trafficking) appear relevant given the capacity-building workshops on victim identification. The evaluation also assesses the effectiveness of CT stakeholder coordination, alignment with National Action Plans, and the quality of evidence-based recommendations — all core to GCM Objective 10. For a synthesis specialist, the conclusions on effectiveness (stakeholder use of reports, capacity contributions), the impact section (progress on recommendations in three countries), and the recommendations on tool refinement and NAP alignment would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional detail on specific CT gaps identified in each country beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN FINAL DEL PROYECTO "CENTRO DE INVERSIÓN Y FORMACIÓN PARA EL EMPRENDIMIENTO EN SENEGAL: FORMACIÓN DE FORMADORES Y ASISTENCIA PARA EL AUTOEMPLEO EN SENEGAL"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly around Action 2e (investing in human capital development, entrepreneurship, vocational training, and skills development to reduce youth unemployment and avoid brain drain). The project's explicit rationale is to create local economic opportunities so that young Senegalese people do not feel compelled to migrate elsewhere due to lack of opportunities. The executive summary directly states that 'the frustration generated by the lack of opportunities constitutes a frequent motive to try their luck in the capital, or in other countries,' and frames the project as addressing this driver. The report also aligns with Action 2d (investing in sustainable development at local and national levels) and Action 2b (employment creation, gender equality, inclusive economic growth). The conclusions section (C1, C16) and the executive summary appear most relevant for human reviewers to examine, as they discuss the project's role in reducing migration pressure through local development. The report also touches on returned migrants as beneficiaries, which connects to reintegration dimensions of Objective 2. Specialists conducting a synthesis on structural drivers of migration and local development interventions would likely find this report worth reviewing, though it is an evaluation of a specific project rather than a policy-level analysis.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN FINAL DEL PROYECTO "CENTRO DE INVERSIÓN Y FORMACIÓN PARA EL EMPRENDIMIENTO EN SENEGAL: FORMACIÓN DE FORMADORES Y ASISTENCIA PARA EL AUTOEMPLEO EN SENEGAL"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute some evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 19, particularly around Action 19e (developing targeted support programmes and financial products that facilitate migrant and diaspora investments and entrepreneurship) and Action 19a (ensuring full implementation of the 2030 Agenda through migration's positive effects). The project explicitly targets returned migrants as beneficiaries and aims to support their entrepreneurship and economic reintegration in Senegal, which connects to the broader theme of enabling migrants and returnees to contribute to sustainable development in their countries of origin. The executive summary notes that returned migrants are included as 'potential multiplying agents' and that the project supports local development through entrepreneurship. Conclusions C13-C16 discuss the development impact of the project, and the recommendations on strengthening entrepreneurship networks and financing (R13-R16) are relevant. The complementarity with AECID financing for business ideas (C4) also illustrates a model of linking training to investment support for returnees. Synthesis specialists examining how programmes support migrant contributions to development in countries of origin would likely find the conclusions and recommendations sections worth reviewing, though they should note that the report's primary focus is on youth employment broadly rather than diaspora/returnee contributions specifically.

References.Title: MIGRATION MANAGEMENT FOR LIBYAN AUTHORITIES TO IDENTIFY AND REGISTER MIGRANTS
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project's core purpose was to develop and operationalize an electronic registration system to collect, store, and share data on migrants rescued at sea and admitted to detention centres — making data collection and management the central subject of the evaluation. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of data collection infrastructure (hardware, software, connectivity), data management capacity building (training on migration data management and SOPs), and data protection considerations. Actions 1c (building national capacities in data collection and analysis), 1i (using administrative records for statistical purposes), and 1j (developing country-specific migration profiles) appear most directly related. The evaluation's findings on the challenges of operationalizing a centralized migration management database, the development of a Migration Data Management Guideline/SOP, and the training sessions on data management and registration procedures would likely be particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist. The sections on Relevance (5.1), Effectiveness (5.3), and the training overview table appear most worth prioritizing for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: INTERNAL EX-POST EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT CS.0837 "DEVELOPMENT OF A NATIONAL FRAMEWORK FOR DURABLE SOLUTIONS IN VANUATU" FUNDED BY THE IOM DEVELOPMENT FUND
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its actions related to disaster-induced displacement (actions 2h, 2i, 2j, 2k, 2l). The project's entire focus is on developing a national policy framework for durable solutions to climate change and disaster-induced displacement in Vanuatu — a Pacific Island nation highly vulnerable to natural hazards. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how governments can build capacity to manage displacement from sudden- and slow-onset disasters, directly addressing the need to 'integrate displacement considerations into disaster preparedness strategies' (action 2j) and 'develop coherent approaches to address challenges of migration movements in the context of sudden-onset and slow-onset natural disasters' (action 2l). The report also references alignment with the Sendai Framework and SDGs (action 2a/2b). Key sections for human reviewers to prioritize would include the Relevance findings (alignment with national needs, response to national gap), the Sustainability section (policy adoption and implementation), and the Conclusions and Recommendations — all of which appear to directly address how a government can institutionalize disaster displacement governance. The report's value for this objective is further enhanced by its documentation of a participatory policy development process, lessons learned on community consultation, and the challenges of operationalizing displacement frameworks in a small island developing state context.

References.Title: Priority Implementation Actions of the AU-ILO-IOM-ECA Joint Programme Migration Governance for Development and Integration in Africa (JLMP priority)-JOINT INDEPENDENT FINAL EVALUATION
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The JLMP Priority project appears to have dedicated substantial effort to labour migration statistics and data collection, with Output 1.3 explicitly focused on supporting 'the AUC and member states in the collection and analysis of labour migration data, including by producing a Labour Migration Statistics Report.' The excerpts describe the production of the 2nd and 3rd Edition of the Report on Labour Migration Statistics in Africa (covering 2008-2017), efforts to harmonize data collection methodology across Member States, the hiring of a statistician at the AUC, and coordination with AU STATAFRIC and national statistical institutes. The impact section notes that 'the expansion of MSs in data collection and harmonization proved successful.' These activities appear to relate directly to GCM Actions 1a (harmonizing methodologies), 1b (improving comparability), 1c (building national capacities), and 1f (regional centres for research). Recommendation 7 also explicitly calls for investing in labour market data collection and harmonization. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on project conclusions (pertaining to institutional capacity building outcomes and HCGE establishment), the context section (on remittance flows and diaspora engagement gaps), and the recommendations (on consolidating diaspora engagement mechanisms) would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible that the full report contains additional relevant evidence on diaspora investment facilitation and engagement mechanisms beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Evaluación Final Independiente: Acciones de Cohesión Social para la Reintegración Sostenible y la Prevención de la Violencia en El Salvador, Guatemala y Honduras

Social cohesion and migrant inclusion are explicit primary themes of the project — the title itself references 'Acciones de Cohesión Social' (Social Cohesion Actions). The evaluation appears to assess interventions directly related to Actions 16a (mutual respect, integration policies), 16f (community centres and intercultural dialogue), 16g (vocational and civic integration), and 16h (multicultural activities through arts and culture). The project's work with youth through artistic and cultural initiatives in Honduras (Product 1.2), community dialogue spaces in El Salvador and Guatemala (Product 1.1), and community participation structures (CODESECH in Honduras) all relate to social cohesion and inclusion mechanisms. Conclusions 8 and 9 on the CODESECH strengthening and the women's psychosocial program appear particularly relevant, as does the discussion of youth participation and community belonging (arraigo). Recommendations 4, 5, and 7 on strengthening youth networks and community structures would also be of interest. A synthesis specialist would likely want to review the sections on community-level interventions, the conclusions on impact and sustainability, and the lessons learned — particularly those addressing arts-based approaches to social cohesion and community participation structures for returned migrants.

The report appears to provide substantive analysis across multiple dimensions of reintegration: psychosocial (women's VBG support program in El Salvador), social (community cohesion, CODESECH strengthening in Honduras), and economic (livelihood gaps identified as a key barrier). The evaluation directly assesses the IOM's integrated reintegration framework across individual, community, and structural levels — a framework explicitly aligned with Action 21h's call for equal access to social protection, psychosocial assistance, vocational training, and employment. The report's conclusions on impact (Conclusions 8-10), sustainability (Conclusions 9-10), and the detailed lessons learned section appear particularly valuable for synthesis specialists. Key sections to prioritize would include: the description of IOM's integrated reintegration approach (section 1.1), the project results framework (section 1.1), conclusions on impact and sustainability, recommendations 1-7, and the lessons learned section — especially lessons 1, 6, 7, and 10 on reintegration programming design. The evaluation also addresses the multi-country (El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras) dimension of reintegration programming, offering comparative insights. It is possible the full report contains additional evidence on reintegration outcomes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how the project identifies and reaches vulnerable populations, with findings on effectiveness of community-based approaches (door-to-door, community dialogues) and referral systems. GCM Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies addressing health needs), and 7k (involving local authorities in identification and assistance) appear particularly relevant. The effectiveness section discusses how implementing partners reached beneficiaries at community levels and collaborated with local structures to enhance referral and uptake of services. The recommendations on mobile clinics, peer-to-peer education, and integration of change agents into government health worker systems would also be of interest. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the effectiveness and recommendations sections, noting that the vulnerability lens is health-specific rather than covering the full range of vulnerabilities addressed by GCM Objective 7.

References.Title: “IMPROVING SRHR-HIV OUTCOMES FOR MIGRANTS, ADOLESCENTS AND YOUNG PEOPLE AND SEX WORKERS IN MIGRATION-AFFECTED COMMUNITIES IN SOUTHERN AFRICA - 2016 – 2020”
