The project's core purpose is improving access to quality SRH and HIV services for migrants, AYPs, and sex workers in Southern Africa — directly addressing GCM Action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in national health care policies and plans, facilitating affordable and non-discriminatory access). The excerpts suggest substantial analysis of: service delivery mechanisms and their effectiveness; barriers to access in border communities; collaboration with government health departments across six countries; and strategies to reach underserved populations. The report also touches on Action 15c (establishing accessible service points) through its discussion of service delivery points, mobile clinics, and community-based approaches. The executive summary, effectiveness section, and recommendations all appear highly relevant. The finding that 260,437 beneficiaries were reached (83% of target) and the detailed discussion of what works (door-to-door, community dialogues, youth clubs) would likely contribute core evidence to a synthesis on migrant health service access. Specialists should prioritize the effectiveness section and recommendations on service delivery expansion, mobile clinics, and health worker training.

The evaluation appears to assess outcomes such as trust-building, citizenship strengthening, and cohesion sociale, which are directly relevant to this objective. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis in sections on the MRC's effects (evaluation objective ii), the community initiatives (evaluation objective iii), and the recommendations on implementation and differential approaches. Sections on 'Sobre el diseño técnico' and 'Sobre el enfoque diferencial y la participación comunitaria' would likely be particularly valuable for human reviewers. It should be noted that the population concerned is ex-combatants rather than international migrants, which limits direct applicability — but the evidence on community-level social cohesion interventions and their outcomes may still offer transferable insights for a synthesis on Objective 16, especially regarding Action 16f and 16a. Specialists should review with this contextual caveat in mind.

The evaluation assesses the Modelo de Reintegración Comunitaria across nine zones in Colombia, examining outcomes such as trust restoration, community cohesion, and sustainability of reintegration processes. While the population is ex-combatants rather than returning migrants per se, the reintegration frameworks, methodologies, and lessons learned appear highly transferable to migration reintegration contexts. The recommendations sections — particularly 'Sobre la implementación,' 'Sobre el diseño técnico,' and 'Sobre el enfoque diferencial' — would likely be valuable for synthesis specialists examining what works in sustainable reintegration programming. Action 21i (addressing needs of communities receiving returnees) also appears relevant given the community-centered approach. Specialists should note the ex-combatant rather than migrant returnee context, but the depth of reintegration analysis likely makes this report worth reviewing for a synthesis on Objective 21, especially given the relatively limited evidence base on community-level reintegration outcomes.

References.Title: Final Evaluation of the Enhancing Corporate Responsibility in Eliminating Slavery and Trafficking in Asia (CREST) Project
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 6. The CREST project's core mandate explicitly centers on ethical recruitment, elimination of modern slavery, and decent work for migrant workers — themes that appear prominently in the project title, evaluation questions, and throughout the findings and recommendations. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: private sector partnerships to implement ethical recruitment standards (Actions 6c, 6d, 6e); supply chain transparency and compliance mechanisms (Action 6e, 6f); the UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights as a framework (Action 6l); tools such as the Labour Migration Process Mapping Guide and Migrant Worker Guidelines for Employers (Actions 6d, 6l); remediation mechanisms for migrant worker grievances (Action 6j); and gender-specific vulnerabilities of women migrant workers in supply chains (Action 6k). The evaluation covers 17 private sector partnerships, training of 1,223 supply chain actors, and 149 remediation cases. For a synthesis specialist, the effectiveness section on private sector engagement, the conclusions on scaling up UNGP alignment, and the recommendations on deepening supply chain compliance would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The lessons learned on factors facilitating successful private sector partnerships and the best practices on setting foundations for strategic partnerships are also likely highly relevant. It is possible the full report contains additional evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Final Evaluation of the Enhancing Corporate Responsibility in Eliminating Slavery and Trafficking in Asia (CREST) Project
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The CREST project explicitly targets vulnerable migrant workers — including domestic workers, women-led households, financially disadvantaged workers, and workers with disabilities — and the evaluation assesses how well programming addressed their needs. The excerpts suggest substantive content on: identifying and assisting vulnerable migrant workers (Action 7b); workplace-related vulnerabilities and abuses (Action 7d); gender-responsive approaches to migrant women's vulnerabilities (Action 7c); access to remediation and grievance mechanisms (Action 7g); and COVID-19 direct assistance to vulnerable migrant workers. The evaluation's cross-cutting section on human rights and gender equality, the potential impact section (220,472 beneficiaries), and the recommendations on targeting vulnerable subgroups would appear most relevant for human reviewers. However, vulnerability is addressed primarily through a private sector/supply chain lens rather than as a standalone protection framework, which somewhat limits its direct applicability to all actions under Objective 7. Specialists should note this framing when reviewing.

The RDPP NA Protection Pillar is substantially oriented toward identifying and assisting migrants in vulnerable situations — including unaccompanied children, asylum seekers, detainees, victims of violence, and those in mixed migration flows across North Africa. The excerpts suggest substantive coverage of Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerability), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies including PSS and legal assistance), 7e and 7f (child protection systems and unaccompanied children), and 7g (legal assistance and representation). Specific activities documented include: case management for minors at risk, psychosocial support, legal aid, family reunification for unaccompanied children, and best interest assessments. The conclusions also discuss how contextual factors (e.g., lack of domestic asylum legislation) limit the ability to fully protect vulnerable migrants — which is analytically relevant for understanding barriers to implementing Objective 7 commitments. However, vulnerability is addressed as part of a broader protection programme rather than as a standalone analytical theme, and the evaluation's primary focus appears to be on institutional capacity building and flows management. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the direct assistance sections (Section 2.1) and the findings on Egypt and Libya (Section 4, not fully excerpted), as well as the conclusions on hindering factors, as starting points for deeper review.

The RDPP NA's direct support to mixed migration flows management component explicitly covers Refugee Status Determination (RSD), resettlement (RST), and AVRR — which are core migration procedure functions directly relevant to Actions 12c (referral mechanisms and individual assessments) and 12e (information on rights and procedures in mixed movement contexts). The report documents RSD decisions provided across multiple countries (e.g., 29,000+ in Egypt, 396 in Tunisia, 764 in Niger), and discusses how implementing partners substantially replaced governments in carrying out these functions due to lack of national legal frameworks. The conclusions and recommendations address the challenge of transitioning these functions to national authorities — which is analytically relevant for understanding the sustainability of screening and referral systems. The excerpts also reference identification and referral of potential asylum seekers in Morocco (6,010 individuals). Synthesis specialists would likely want to review Section 2.4 (direct support to flows management) and the findings sections on Tunisia and Egypt (Section 4), as well as the strategic recommendations on handing over RSD functions, as starting points for deeper review.

Assisted Voluntary Return and Reintegration (AVRR) is explicitly identified as one of the four main areas of intervention under the RDPP NA, and the report documents AVRR operations across multiple countries (673 in Algeria, 296 in Egypt, 248 in Tunisia, 51 in Mauritania). This directly relates to Actions 21b (gender-responsive voluntary return and reintegration programmes) and 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration). The report also discusses resettlement as a durable solution (1,206 refugees resettled from Libya), which relates to the broader durable solutions framework. Importantly, the evaluation analyzes the extent to which these interventions contributed to durable solutions — including hindering factors such as lack of national asylum legislation — which provides analytical depth relevant to understanding barriers to implementing Objective 21 commitments. The conclusions note that contribution to durable solutions was 'modest,' which is substantively relevant for a synthesis. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review Section 2.4 (direct support to flows management), the Tunisia and Libya case findings (Section 4, not fully excerpted), and the recommendations on conditionality and longer-term planning, as starting points for deeper review.

The entire project evaluated concerns the COMESA Free Movement Protocol — a regional free movement regime covering 19 member states — and the evaluation examines efforts to advance its signature and ratification. The excerpts discuss the status of FMP adoption, barriers to ratification (security concerns, preparedness gaps, legislative and administrative infrastructure), and the role of awareness-raising and capacity-building in advancing free movement. The evaluation also touches on overlapping regional initiatives (SADC facilitated movement protocol, AU one Africa passport) and how governments are navigating multiple free movement frameworks. Key sections for human reviewers to prioritize would include the findings on impediments to ratification, the discussion of government preparedness concerns, and the recommendations on targeted sensitization — all of which speak to the practical challenges of implementing regional free movement regimes. The report appears to be one of several themes covered (institutional capacity, awareness-raising), but free movement pathways is clearly the central substantive focus.

The excerpts suggest substantive engagement with data collection methodologies (Household Level Surveys, National Level Assessments), data harmonization challenges across six countries, and the development of an online information-sharing platform — all directly relevant to Actions 1a, 1b, 1c, and 1h. The project's comparative study across six countries and the development of country-specific maps also appear to relate to Actions 1f and 1j. Recommendations in the excerpts explicitly address improving data collection processes, harmonizing survey methodologies, and developing raw databases. The evaluation appears to assess the quality and rigor of these data collection activities, which would be valuable for a synthesis on evidence-based migration data systems. Specialists would particularly want to review the sections on Household Level Survey methodology, the National Level Assessment design, and the recommendations on data harmonization — though the full report may contain additional relevant analysis beyond what the excerpts reveal.

The MECLEP project is explicitly focused on the migration-environment-climate change nexus, testing migration as an adaptation strategy to environmental change — which is the core concern of these actions. The project title, evaluation questions, and stated objectives all center on understanding how environmental and climate drivers compel or enable migration, and how policy can respond. The excerpts discuss the Cancun Adaptation Framework, Paris Agreement, Sendai Framework, and UNFCCC mechanisms in relation to migration — all directly relevant to Action 2a and 2i. The project's work in six countries (Dominican Republic, Haiti, Kenya, Mauritius, Papua New Guinea, Viet Nam) on empirically testing migration as adaptation appears to directly address Action 2i's call for adaptation and resilience strategies. The policy dialogue sections and Technical Working Group activities appear relevant to Action 2l on coherent approaches. Specialists would particularly want to review the project background sections on environmental migration developments, the policy dialogue analysis, and the recommendations on anchoring findings in national adaptation agendas — these appear to be the most substantive contributions to this GCM objective.

The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of PMO training, theater outreach, SATMACs, and other information dissemination modalities, including pre-test/post-test knowledge gains. The Community Outreach Effectiveness section appears to contain substantive analysis of how well information reached beneficiaries, what worked and what did not (e.g., SMP-variant challenges in Chin State, theater group quality issues), and regional variation in outcomes. Recommendations also address improving PMO delivery. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the Community Outreach Effectiveness section and the recommendations on PMO and SATMAC services as starting points, noting that the report evaluates a specific country-level programme rather than a systemic information architecture.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF “INCREASING THE DEVELOPMENTAL IMPACT  OF LABOUR MIGRATION THROUGH STRENGTHENED GOVERNANCE  AND PARTNERSHIP” TWE LET PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The entire Twe Let project is explicitly framed around the developmental impact of labour migration — a core theme of GCM Objective 19. The project title itself references 'increasing the developmental impact of labour migration,' and the evaluation assesses how migration contributes to household income, poverty reduction, and sustainable development in Myanmar. Key areas of relevance include: the financial literacy training component focused on remittance management and household financial decision-making (action 19a, 19b); the assessment of whether migration decisions improve living conditions (action 19a); and the broader question of mainstreaming migration into development planning (action 19b). The evaluation's analysis of the nexus between migration and development outcomes, including the critical discussion of whether beneficiaries without migration connections should receive project services, directly engages with the theory of how migration contributes to development. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the Relevance section, Community Outreach Effectiveness section, and the project's theory of change discussion as starting points. The report's focus on Myanmar's rural migrant-sending communities provides country-level evidence on migration-development linkages.

Financial literacy training (FLT) is a central component of Twe Let, explicitly focused on helping migrant families make better use of remittances and manage household finances — directly relating to actions 20f (financial literacy and inclusion) and 20h (enabling migrant women to access financial literacy training). The evaluation assesses FLT Tier 1 and Tier 2 effectiveness, including knowledge gains on remittance management, savings practices, and income-generating activities. The report also discusses challenges in delivering complex accounting and savings content, and the strong beneficiary support for integrated PMO/FLT training. The project outcome framework explicitly includes 'improved financial and household management' and 'effective management of remittances and household finances' as outcomes. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the Community Outreach Effectiveness section on FLT components and the sustainability section's discussion of lasting savings behavior changes. The report provides country-level evidence on financial literacy programming for migrant-sending households, which is relevant to action 20f and 20h specifically.

The excerpts indicate that the remittances survey produced evidence that continued to be used by IOM and the government after project end, including for COVID-19 impact assessments. The evaluation also discusses the quality of the results matrix and indicator design, touching on evidence-based policymaking. However, data collection appears to be one component of a broader project rather than the central focus. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on the household survey methodology and results, as well as the sustainability discussion regarding continued use of the evidence base — framed as pointers for further review rather than definitive characterizations.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation: Strengthening the Management of Remittances for the Sustainable Development of Cameroon
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The project's explicit purpose — strengthening management of remittances for sustainable development of Cameroon — directly aligns with this objective's core theme. The excerpts suggest substantive coverage of multiple associated actions: action 19d (establishing government structures for diaspora engagement, including the Ministry of Foreign Affairs Direction of Cameroonians Abroad), action 19e (developing targeted support programmes for diaspora investments and entrepreneurship, including business creation and coaching), action 19j (building partnerships between local authorities, diaspora organizations and migrant organizations for knowledge transfer), and action 19c (investing in research on non-financial contributions of migrants and diasporas through the household survey). The evaluation appears to assess the effectiveness of diaspora engagement mechanisms, the sustainability of diaspora-linked entrepreneurship, and institutional capacity for diaspora policy — all core themes under Objective 19. The executive summary, effectiveness, impact, and sustainability sections would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The lessons identified section — noting that diaspora engagement increases when members are included from the beginning — also appears directly relevant. It is possible the full report contains additional evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation: Strengthening the Management of Remittances for the Sustainable Development of Cameroon
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 20. The project is explicitly titled 'Strengthening the Management of Remittances for the Sustainable Development of Cameroon' and the evaluation directly assesses a remittances-focused intervention. The excerpts suggest substantive coverage of multiple associated actions: action 20g (developing programmes to promote investments from remittance senders in local development and entrepreneurship, including matching-grant mechanisms) appears central to the project design; action 20f (providing accessible information on remittance transfer costs and promoting financial literacy) relates to the awareness-raising and training components; action 20h (enabling migrant women to access financial literacy training and formal remittance transfer systems) connects to the gender mainstreaming discussion; and action 20d (establishing conducive policy and regulatory frameworks for the remittance market) relates to the institutional capacity-building component. The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of training beneficiaries to use remittances productively, the sustainability of business creation from remittance-funded enterprises, and the policy environment for remittances in Cameroon. The household survey on remittance flows also directly supports evidence generation for this objective. The full report likely contains additional detail on remittance transfer mechanisms and policy recommendations beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: MID-TERM INTERNAL INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “STRENGTHENING NATIONAL EFFORTS TO PREVENT TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS AND REHABILITATE VICTIMS OF TRAFFICKING IN THE REPUBLIC OF MOLDOVA”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7, particularly regarding the identification, protection, and assistance of migrants in vulnerable situations. The evaluation discusses gender-responsive approaches (Action 7c), including specific attention to male VoT needs through the establishment of a dedicated Male VoT Centre, as well as services for women and children. The report also addresses access to legal assistance and justice (Action 7g), psychological and social recovery services, and referral mechanisms (Action 7k). Sections most worth reviewing include the effectiveness conclusions on Objective 1 (rehabilitation), the gender section, and the good practices discussion on the Male VoT Centre and CAP. The report's primary focus on a specific vulnerable population (VoT) and the depth of analysis on service provision make it a relevant, though not exclusively focused, source for this objective.

References.Title: MID-TERM INTERNAL INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “STRENGTHENING NATIONAL EFFORTS TO PREVENT TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS AND REHABILITATE VICTIMS OF TRAFFICKING IN THE REPUBLIC OF MOLDOVA”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The entire project evaluated is dedicated to preventing trafficking in persons and rehabilitating victims — making this the most direct alignment among all 23 objectives. The project title itself ('Strengthening national efforts to prevent trafficking in persons and rehabilitate victims of trafficking in the Republic of Moldova') maps directly onto the objective's core theme. The evaluation covers multiple associated actions in depth: Action 10e (measures addressing vulnerabilities of women, men, girls and boys who are or are at risk of becoming VoT, facilitating access to justice); Action 10g (strengthening legislation and procedures, avoiding criminalization of VoT); Action 10h (providing protection and assistance including physical, psychological and social recovery); Action 10i (training programmes and national information systems to identify trafficking); and Action 10j (awareness-raising campaigns on risks of trafficking). The evaluation's conclusions on effectiveness of rehabilitation services, the Male VoT Centre as an innovative good practice, hotline support, legal and regulatory framework development, and sustainability of state-run shelters all provide substantive evidence relevant to this objective. For human reviewers, the sections most worth prioritizing include: the effectiveness conclusions (Objective 1 on rehabilitation and Objective 2 on reintegration), the good practices section on the Male VoT Centre and CAP, the sustainability conclusions, and the recommendations on legal framework development. The full report likely contains additional detailed findings beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: MID-TERM INTERNAL INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “STRENGTHENING NATIONAL EFFORTS TO PREVENT TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS AND REHABILITATE VICTIMS OF TRAFFICKING IN THE REPUBLIC OF MOLDOVA”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly regarding sustainable reintegration of returning migrants (in this case, trafficking victims returning to Moldova). The project explicitly includes repatriation support (Output 2.1) and reintegration services (Outputs 2.2 and 2.3), with the evaluation assessing their effectiveness and sustainability. Action 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes with legal, social and financial support) and Action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through social protection, vocational training, employment opportunities and psychosocial assistance) appear most directly addressed. The evaluation discusses economic empowerment outcomes, the piloting of reintegration services, challenges in phasing out repatriation support to government, and the legal framework for reintegration — all substantive content for a synthesis on reintegration. The sustainability conclusions on reintegration services and the recommendations on legal framework development (Recommendations 7, 8, 9) would be particularly worth reviewing. The report's focus on a specific population (VoT) and the Moldova context should be noted, but the reintegration analysis appears substantive enough to contribute to a broader synthesis.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF RESULTS-BASED MANAGEMENT AT IOM
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1, particularly regarding the development and use of data systems for evidence-based management. The evaluation's central focus on Results-Based Management (RBM) at IOM involves substantial analysis of data collection, indicator development, monitoring systems, and the use of management information systems (PRIMA) — all of which relate to actions under Objective 1. Key findings in the excerpts address the lack of baseline data and targets (Key finding 10), the reliability of data collection tools such as the Institutional Questionnaire (Key finding 12), and the development of indicator metadata (Key finding 5). These appear to touch on actions 1a (comprehensive strategy for improving data), 1c (building national/institutional capacities in data collection), and 1j (developing evidence-based profiles/frameworks). Recommendations 2 and 3 explicitly call for gathering baseline information, finalizing indicator metadata guidance, and strengthening PRIMA as a data management tool. However, the data focus here is institutional/programmatic rather than migration statistics per se — it concerns IOM's internal performance measurement rather than migration data for policymaking. Synthesis specialists working on Objective 1 should review the findings on PRIMA, the IQ, and indicator development (Key findings 5, 9, 10, 12), while noting that the report's primary contribution is to organizational M&E systems rather than migration data collection in the GCM sense.

References.Title: EDUCATION, TRAINING AND KNOWLEDGE-SHARING ON MIGRATION IN TUNISIA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 17, particularly regarding the promotion of evidence-based public discourse on migration (Action 17c, 17f). The project's explicit aim was to shift perceptions and attitudes among policymakers and influencers toward more constructive, evidence-based understandings of migration — and the evaluation assesses the extent to which this was achieved. The executive summary notes that 'the project slowly informed a constructive public discourse on migration' and that it contributed to 'increased proliferation of networks of organizations advocating for migration-inclusive development agenda.' The evaluation also examines the role of media sensitization and the summer school in changing how migration is framed publicly. The shift from a security-based to a human rights and development-based approach to migration governance — assessed as an impact-level outcome — is directly relevant to Objective 17's concern with evidence-based, humane public discourse. Synthesis specialists would likely find the effectiveness and impact sections most relevant, particularly the analysis of how the summer school and roundtables influenced stakeholder perceptions and public discourse. The recommendations on media professionals and scientific committee transparency may also be of interest. Note that the excerpts do not reveal dedicated analysis of anti-discrimination legislation or hate crime responses, so relevance is concentrated on the public discourse and perception-shaping dimensions of Objective 17.

References.Title: COUNTER TRAFFICKING AND ADDRESSING IRREGULAR MIGRATION THROUGH STRENGTHENING BORDER AND MIGRATION MANAGEMENT, SENSITIZATION ON TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS AND BUILDING CAPACITY OF LAW ENFORCEMENT AND BORDER OFFICIALS IN LESOTHO
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Counter-trafficking in persons (TIP) is explicitly a primary subject of the evaluated project, appearing prominently in the project title, evaluation objectives, and throughout the findings and recommendations. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly relevant to GCM Objective 10 actions: training law enforcement (CGPU, border officials, prosecutors) to identify and investigate TIP cases (Action 10i); community sensitization campaigns on TIP risks reaching 26,000+ beneficiaries (Action 10j); establishment and capacity building of Community Based Protection Committees for victim identification and referral (Actions 10e, 10i); implementation of Standard Operating Procedures at ports of entry for TIP identification (Action 10c); cross-border cooperation between Lesotho and South Africa through the Cross-Border Crime Prevention Forum (Action 10d); and discussion of legal framework compliance including Lesotho's 2011 Anti-Trafficking Act and Tier 2 Watch List status (Action 10a, 10g). The evaluation also addresses child labour trafficking (herdboys), gender-disaggregated approaches, and victim protection mechanisms. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on effectiveness of TIP training for CGPU officers, the CBPC model for community-level identification and referral, the sensitization campaign design and reach, and the recommendations on establishing a dedicated TIP unit within LMPS would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: COUNTER TRAFFICKING AND ADDRESSING IRREGULAR MIGRATION THROUGH STRENGTHENING BORDER AND MIGRATION MANAGEMENT, SENSITIZATION ON TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS AND BUILDING CAPACITY OF LAW ENFORCEMENT AND BORDER OFFICIALS IN LESOTHO
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. Border management is a co-primary theme of the evaluated project alongside counter-trafficking, appearing in the project title and as a dedicated outcome (Outcome 3). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: training immigration officers and border officials on Standard Operating Procedures (Action 11b — integrated border crossing procedures); cross-border cooperation between Lesotho and South Africa through the Cross-Border Crime Prevention Forum and Heads of Border Agencies (Action 11g — cross-border collaboration); provision of equipment including CCTV surveillance at Moshoeshoe I International Airport and vehicles for immigration officers (Action 11d — technical cooperation and assets); capacity building of border officials on their mandates and inter-agency complementarity (Action 11a — whole-of-government approaches); and recommendations on monitoring compliance with SOPs and designating performance monitoring staff at POEs. The evaluation covers 14 official border control posts in Lesotho across multiple districts. Border management appears to be analyzed with comparable depth to counter-trafficking in the project design, though the excerpts suggest counter-trafficking may receive slightly more evaluative attention. For a synthesis specialist, the findings on SOP training effectiveness, the cross-border coordination mechanisms, and the recommendations on equipment provision and performance monitoring would appear particularly relevant. Sections on Outcome 3 achievements and the efficiency analysis of the training-of-trainers approach would be worth prioritizing for review.

References.Title: FINAL TERM EVALUATION OF STRENGTHENING DISASTER RISK REDUCTION CAPACITY AND PROGRAM COMMUNITY RESILIENCE IN WEST JAVA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly around disaster risk reduction, resilience building, and the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction — themes directly referenced in Action 2b (resilience and disaster risk reduction) and Actions 2i-2j (adaptation and resilience strategies, displacement considerations in disaster preparedness). The report explicitly evaluates a DRR capacity-building project aligned with the Hyogo Framework for Action (predecessor to Sendai), covering early warning systems, contingency planning, community resilience, and mitigation — all of which relate to reducing conditions that could compel displacement. The executive summary and conclusions discuss the paradigm shift from disaster response to risk reduction, the role of community-based disaster management groups, and the challenges of building institutional capacity for DRR. The recommendations section addresses mitigation, prevention, and poverty reduction as structural factors. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on community resilience outcomes, the discussion of early warning systems, and the analysis of institutional capacity gaps would appear most relevant as starting points. The report does not address international migration directly, but its evidence on DRR programming effectiveness is substantively relevant to Action 2b, 2i, and 2j. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant content beyond these excerpts.

References.Title: Final External Evaluation: Better Migration Management Programme Phase III
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. Protection of vulnerable migrants is explicitly one of BMM Phase III's three core components, and the excerpts suggest substantive analysis of protection services, victim identification, and assistance mechanisms. The executive summary notes that IOM 'met or exceeded most programme targets' in 'improving victim protection' and 'enhanced protection services for vulnerable migrants.' The evaluation appears to examine National Referral Mechanisms, community awareness campaigns, and direct assistance to vulnerable migrants — all relevant to Objective 7's associated actions, particularly 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies), and 7k (involving local authorities in identification and referral). Recommendation 6 specifically calls for further institutionalizing National Referral Mechanisms with sustainable funding and clear guidelines for cross-border referrals. The conclusions section also discusses protection services and cross-border cooperation for vulnerable migrants. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the effectiveness sections on protection outcomes and the recommendations on NRM institutionalization, though the excerpts suggest vulnerability is one of several themes rather than the sole focus.

References.Title: Final External Evaluation: Better Migration Management Programme Phase III
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 9. Combating smuggling of migrants is explicitly stated as a core objective of the BMM programme — the programme aims to 'address human trafficking and smuggling of migrants' and Component 2 focuses on 'supporting institutions to combat human trafficking and smuggling of migrants through integrated border governance.' The excerpts suggest the evaluation examines institutional capacity-building for law enforcement, cross-border cooperation mechanisms (including Inter-Agency Border Management Committees), and policy development related to smuggling — relevant to Objective 9's actions on capacity-building (9f), cross-border cooperation protocols (9c), and legislative measures (9d). The conclusions note progress in cross-border cooperation mechanisms and policy development. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the effectiveness sections on border governance outcomes and the findings on institutional capacity strengthening, as well as recommendations on cross-border cooperation. It should be noted that smuggling is treated alongside trafficking throughout, so evidence may not always be disaggregated between the two crimes.

References.Title: Final External Evaluation: Better Migration Management Programme Phase III
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Combating human trafficking is explicitly one of the two primary crime-related objectives of the BMM programme, appearing in the programme's overall objective statement and running through all three components. The evaluation examines IOM's contributions to anti-trafficking legislation, institutional capacity-building for identification and prosecution, victim protection services, and National Referral Mechanisms — directly relevant to Objective 10's associated actions on prosecution (10g), victim protection and assistance (10h), training and awareness (10i, 10j), and monitoring irregular routes exploited by trafficking networks (10c). The executive summary notes achievements in 'improving victim protection' and the conclusions discuss progress in protection services and cross-border cooperation. Recommendation 6 specifically addresses institutionalizing National Referral Mechanisms for trafficking victims. The evaluation covers multiple countries in the East and Horn of Africa, providing comparative evidence across different national contexts. Synthesis specialists should prioritize the effectiveness sections on protection outcomes, the findings on institutional capacity for victim identification and referral, and the sustainability analysis regarding NRM continuity — these appear to offer the most direct evidence on Objective 10 actions based on the excerpts reviewed.

References.Title: Final External Evaluation: Better Migration Management Programme Phase III
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute substantial evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. Integrated border governance is explicitly Component 2 of the BMM programme, and the excerpts suggest the evaluation examines border management capacity-building, cross-border cooperation mechanisms, and training of border officials across multiple countries. The executive summary notes that IOM 'met or exceeded most programme targets' in 'strengthening institutional capacities' and 'enhanced migration governance,' with specific mention of Inter-Agency Border Management Committees (IBMCs) in Somalia as examples of improved operational coordination. The evaluation covers border management in Djibouti, Ethiopia, Kenya, Somalia, South Sudan, Sudan, and Uganda — providing multi-country evidence relevant to Objective 11's actions on international cooperation (11a), integrated border management structures (11b), and cross-border collaboration (11g). Training of border officials is specifically mentioned as surpassing targets. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the effectiveness sections on border governance outcomes, the findings on IBMC functionality, and the recommendations on cross-border cooperation mechanisms. The conflict in Sudan and its effects on border management in South Sudan also provides evidence on adaptive border management in fragile contexts.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN FINAL DEL PROYECTO “APOYO A LA INTEGRACIÓN SOCIOECONÓMICA Y FORTALECIMIENTO DE LOS SISTEMAS DE SALUD Y DE LAS ORGANIZACIONES DE LA SOCIEDAD CIVIL EN EL CONTEXTO DE COVID-19 Y SU IMPACTO EN LA POBLACIÓN VENEZOLANA Y RECEPTORA” EN ECUADOR
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The project explicitly addresses access to basic services for Venezuelan migrants, including health system strengthening (referenced in the project title and scope) and access to childcare through Centros de Desarrollo Infantil (CDI). The evaluation assesses beneficiaries' perceptions of access to public services and labor markets, and the project includes strengthening civil society organizations and health systems. The conclusions note positive perceptions of service access among beneficiaries. The CDI component directly relates to Action 15f (inclusive education and early childhood development) and Action 15e (health needs of migrants). However, the excerpts suggest that the socioeconomic inclusion component (proyecto SEMILLA) receives more analytical depth than the health/CDI components in the sections reviewed. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the sections on Eficacia (findings 5-9) and the CDI coordination findings (findings 28-31), as well as the conclusions on service access perceptions, as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains more substantive analysis of health system strengthening than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN FINAL DEL PROYECTO “APOYO A LA INTEGRACIÓN SOCIOECONÓMICA Y FORTALECIMIENTO DE LOS SISTEMAS DE SALUD Y DE LAS ORGANIZACIONES DE LA SOCIEDAD CIVIL EN EL CONTEXTO DE COVID-19 Y SU IMPACTO EN LA POBLACIÓN VENEZOLANA Y RECEPTORA” EN ECUADOR
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. Socioeconomic integration and social cohesion between Venezuelan migrants and the Ecuadorian host community appear to be the central themes of the evaluated project, explicitly named in the project title ('Apoyo a la integración socioeconómica'). The evaluation assesses multiple dimensions directly relevant to GCM Objective 16: labor market integration through seed capital and entrepreneurship training (Action 16d), empowerment of migrant women through economic independence (Action 16e), mutual recognition and intercultural dialogue between migrants and host communities (Action 16f, 16a), and skills development through vocational training (Action 16g). The executive summary and conclusions contain substantive analysis of integration outcomes, including beneficiary perceptions of labor market access, community integration, and emotional empowerment. Conclusion C4 explicitly addresses 'reducción de vulnerabilidades...a través del reconocimiento mutuo entre la población migrante y comunidad de acogida,' and C7 discusses the emotional and social impact of integration processes. The recommendations on workshop design to include host communities (R3), continuity of seed capital programs (R4), and post-project follow-up (R8) all directly address integration programming. For a synthesis specialist, the Eficacia and Impacto sections, along with the conclusions C3, C4, and C7, would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The report likely offers rich evidence on what works in migrant-host community integration programming in a South American context.

References.Title: EMERGENCY ASSISTANCE FOR DISPLACED AND VULNERABLE COMMUNITIES IN BOLIVAR, VENEZUELA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project explicitly targets vulnerable populations — including children under five, pregnant women, elderly persons, persons with disabilities, and indigenous communities — in a context of displacement and crisis. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how the project identified and responded to migrant and displaced persons' vulnerabilities, including through mobile health teams, gender-responsive programming, and assistance at PASI quarantine points for returning Venezuelans. Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerability), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies addressing health needs), and 7j (support for migrants caught in crisis situations) appear most related. The conclusions and recommendations sections — particularly those on coverage of children and pregnant women, gender-inclusive service delivery, and coordination with state health authorities — would appear most relevant for human reviewers to prioritize. The report's context of a complex humanitarian emergency with mixed migration flows adds analytical value. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EMERGENCY ASSISTANCE FOR DISPLACED AND VULNERABLE COMMUNITIES IN BOLIVAR, VENEZUELA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The project's core purpose is explicitly to improve access to basic health services for vulnerable and displaced populations in remote areas of Venezuela — directly aligning with the objective's central theme and particularly Action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in health care policies and plans, facilitating affordable and non-discriminatory access). The report appears to provide substantive evidence on: mobile health team delivery models for hard-to-reach populations; provision of medicines and medical supplies to vulnerable groups; health service delivery to indigenous communities; gender-responsive health programming (gynecological care, maternal health); and community health worker training as a mechanism for extending service reach. The project title itself references emergency assistance for displaced and vulnerable communities, and the evaluation criteria of relevance, efficacy, coverage, efficiency and coordination all appear to be assessed through the lens of health service access. For a synthesis specialist, the findings sections on coverage (including gaps in pediatric and obstetric care), the efficacy analysis (beneficiary satisfaction, service quality), and the recommendations on strengthening pediatric and obstetric care (Recommendation VI) and scaling health access (Recommendation I) would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: CONSOLIDATE PROTECTION ASSISTANCE SERVICES TO VULNERABLE MIGRANTS ALONG MIGRATORY ROUTES IN EGYPT (CASE)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The CASE project is explicitly and centrally focused on identifying and assisting vulnerable migrants — including victims of trafficking, SGBV survivors, unaccompanied minors, and stranded migrants — which maps directly onto the core theme and several associated actions of Objective 7. The project title itself references 'protection assistance to vulnerable migrants,' and the MPA unit's work on localized direct assistance, vulnerability and eligibility assessments, and referral mechanisms aligns closely with Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies addressing needs of migrant women and children), 7e and 7f (child protection and unaccompanied minors), 7g (legal assistance and access to justice), and 7k (involvement of local authorities and mobile response teams in identification and referral). The executive summary, conclusions, and recommendations sections all contain substantive content on vulnerability identification, tailored assistance, and protection service delivery. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on localized direct assistance (LDA), vulnerability/eligibility assessment SOPs, the roving team model, and the Migrant Community Council would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The report also touches on gender-responsive programming (prioritization of SGBV survivors, single parents, women at risk), which is directly relevant to Action 7c. It is possible the full report contains additional depth on these themes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: CONSOLIDATE PROTECTION ASSISTANCE SERVICES TO VULNERABLE MIGRANTS ALONG MIGRATORY ROUTES IN EGYPT (CASE)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Victims of trafficking (VoT) are explicitly identified as a primary target beneficiary group throughout the report, and the project's vulnerability and eligibility assessment SOPs give high prioritization to trafficking victims. The project operates within a broader institutional framework that includes the National Coordinating Committee on Combating and Preventing Trafficking in Persons (NCCTIP), suggesting engagement with anti-trafficking governance structures. This aligns with Actions 10e (measures addressing vulnerabilities of trafficking victims, facilitating access to justice) and 10h (protection and assistance to trafficking victims, including physical, psychological and social recovery). However, the excerpts do not reveal deep analytical treatment of trafficking-specific interventions, prosecution, or prevention campaigns as distinct themes — trafficking appears as one vulnerability category within a broader protection assistance framework. The sections on LDA eligibility criteria, vulnerability assessments, and the SOPs would be the most relevant starting points for a synthesis specialist reviewing this report. The full report may contain more dedicated content on trafficking responses than the excerpts suggest.

References.Title: CONSOLIDATE PROTECTION ASSISTANCE SERVICES TO VULNERABLE MIGRANTS ALONG MIGRATORY ROUTES IN EGYPT (CASE)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The Localized Direct Assistance (LDA) component of the CASE project explicitly provides vulnerable migrants with access to medical services, legal assistance, education, and socio-economic support — directly relevant to Actions 15c (holistic and accessible service points at local level) and 15e (health needs of migrants). The report discusses the challenges migrants face in accessing IOM services (hotline capacity, in-person access), which relates to the accessibility dimension of service provision. The project's outreach activities and the Migrant Community Council also function as mechanisms to connect migrants to available services. However, the analysis in the excerpts is primarily operational rather than policy-oriented, and the report does not appear to engage with the systemic or legislative dimensions of service access (e.g., Actions 15a, 15b, 15d). For a synthesis specialist, the sections on LDA service delivery, the hotline and intake capacity challenges, and the recommendations on staffing and demand management would be the most relevant starting points.

References.Title: CONSOLIDATE PROTECTION ASSISTANCE SERVICES TO VULNERABLE MIGRANTS ALONG MIGRATORY ROUTES IN EGYPT (CASE)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The Assisted Voluntary Return and Reintegration (AVRR) component is one of the three core pillars of the CASE project, and the report devotes substantial attention to both the return process and reintegration support for migrants returning to Sudan. The project explicitly attempted to shift from individual to community reintegration approaches, directly relevant to Action 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes based on free, prior and informed consent) and Action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through access to social protection, vocational training, employment, and financial services). The report also discusses institutional cooperation between IOM Egypt, the Government of Egypt, and the Government of Sudan on processing and reintegration of Sudanese returnees, touching on Action 21a (bilateral/multilateral cooperation frameworks). The challenges encountered — political unrest in Sudan, inability to implement community reintegration activities, coherence issues with the EU-IOM Joint Initiative — provide substantive evaluative evidence on the conditions for sustainable reintegration. The sections on AVRR implementation, reintegration monitoring, the community reintegration approach, and the lessons learned on Sudan context would appear particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist. The report also contains IOM monitoring survey data on returnee outcomes (business creation, self-sufficiency), which, while limited, represents direct evidence on reintegration sustainability. It is likely the full report contains additional depth on AVRR modalities and reintegration outcomes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: STRENGTHENING LABOUR MIGRATION MANAGEMENT IN BOTSWANA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project's primary intervention included a skills assessment/labour market data collection exercise in Selebi-Phikwe, explicitly aimed at generating 'strategic information' to guide evidence-based economic and migration planning. The evaluation appears to assess how this data was collected, disseminated, and utilized — directly touching on actions 1c (building national capacities in data collection and analysis), 1d (collecting data on migration effects for sustainable development), and 1j (developing country-specific migration/labour profiles). The executive summary notes that 'the knowledge imparted... and the data generated have potential to contribute to a future economic revitalization,' and the findings section discusses the limited utilization of the skills assessment report by stakeholders. The evaluation also references IOM's work with Statistics Botswana on migration data collection and a Migration Profile. For a synthesis specialist, the findings on data utilization gaps, the skills assessment methodology, and the recommendations on follow-up mechanisms would appear particularly relevant as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence on data collection processes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: STRENGTHENING LABOUR MIGRATION MANAGEMENT IN BOTSWANA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute some meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 18. The project's skills assessment component — auditing available skills in the Selebi-Phikwe region, identifying skills gaps, and profiling skills lost due to mine closure — appears directly relevant to actions 18e (building global skills partnerships and fostering skills development) and 18k (screening credentials and providing information on skills assessment). The evaluation discusses the skills assessment methodology, its findings on the regional skills landscape, and critically, the limited utilization of the skills assessment report by stakeholders. The recommendation to pilot reskilling of former miners through TVET institutions also touches on action 18e. The Mauritius study tour, which explored how Mauritius manages human resource development and skills, adds further relevance. For a synthesis specialist, the findings on skills assessment design and utilization gaps, and the recommendations on follow-up reskilling interventions, would appear particularly worth reviewing. However, the report's primary analytical focus is on institutional capacity building and project management effectiveness rather than skills recognition systems per se, which limits its depth on this objective.

References.Title: IMPACT EVALUATION OF IOM'S ASSISTED VOLUNTARY RETURN AND REINTEGRATION (AVRR) PROGRAMME - FINAL REPORT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The AVRR programme is explicitly the subject of this evaluation, and it directly addresses the core commitments of Objective 21 — voluntary return, dignified return processes, and sustainable reintegration. The report appears to provide detailed evidence on multiple associated actions: Action 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes with free, prior and informed consent), Action 21h (sustainable reintegration through social protection, vocational training, employment opportunities, financial services, and entrepreneurship support), and Action 21i (identifying needs of communities to which migrants return). The excerpts suggest the evaluation covers all three stages of the AVRR programme (pre-departure, travel, post-arrival reintegration), assesses the sustainability of reintegration outcomes, examines whether assistance deters re-migration, and provides detailed analysis of business start-up outcomes, cash grants, medical assistance, education/training, and job placement. The executive summary, conclusions and recommendations sections all appear highly relevant, with particular value in the analysis of reintegration sustainability, business closure rates, the gap between promised and delivered services, and the conditions under which returnees consider re-migration. The treatment of unaccompanied minors also relates to Action 21g. An evaluator conducting a synthesis on GCM Objective 21 should treat this report as a primary source and review it in full.

References.Title: Final assessment report of the project "Addressing converging crises and unprecedented increase in the needs of internally displaced persons, returnees and their families in northern Central America"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to contain substantive content relevant to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project explicitly supported information management systems for internally displaced persons and migrants in transit, including the launch of an Institutional Open Data Platform in Guatemala, strengthening of a geospatial information platform on shelters and IDPs in El Salvador (with equipment and software donations), and improvements to information systems including forms, database structures, dashboards and automated reports. The excerpts also indicate that 294 government officials were trained in camp coordination and management including site planning tools (AutoCAD, QGIS). Output 1.1 is explicitly framed around identifying and recording protection and humanitarian assistance needs of IDPs and migrants in transit. The report appears to address Actions 1c (building national capacities in data collection and analysis) and 1j (country-specific migration profiles and evidence-based policy) most directly. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on Result 1 and Output 1.1, as well as the conclusions discussing DTM-based data sharing and the recommendations on institutional strengthening approaches, would likely be the most valuable starting points. The report's focus is primarily operational/humanitarian rather than statistical methodology, so it would contribute evidence on practical data system strengthening rather than on statistical standards or international comparability.

References.Title: Final assessment report of the project "Addressing converging crises and unprecedented increase in the needs of internally displaced persons, returnees and their families in northern Central America"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its actions related to disaster risk reduction, resilience building, and addressing climate-related displacement. The project is explicitly framed around responding to the compounding effects of climate disasters (hurricanes Eta, Iota, Julia, Amanda, Cristobal), COVID-19, and structural vulnerability in Northern Central America — the very drivers addressed by Objective 2. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of disaster preparedness and response mechanisms (Actions 2c, 2i, 2j), including improvement of shelter infrastructure, training in disaster response, revision of emergency management manuals, and strengthening of early warning and contingency systems. The report also engages with the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (Action 2a) and addresses resilience-building at community and institutional levels (Action 2b). The context section provides detailed analysis of climate-migration linkages in Central America. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on project outputs (particularly Results 2 and 4), the conclusions on resilience strengthening, and the recommendations on contingency planning and sustainability would appear most relevant. The report's primary focus is on humanitarian response rather than structural development investment, which somewhat limits its depth on the broader economic and governance dimensions of Objective 2, but its treatment of disaster-displacement linkages appears substantive.

References.Title: Final assessment report of the project "Addressing converging crises and unprecedented increase in the needs of internally displaced persons, returnees and their families in northern Central America"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project is explicitly designed to address vulnerabilities of IDPs and migrants in transit, with a stated focus on women, children, adolescents, LGBTIQ+ persons, and other vulnerable groups. The excerpts indicate substantive content on: protection services for vulnerable migrants (Action 7b), gender-responsive approaches including GBV prevention (Action 7c), access to information on health, hygiene and protection (Output 3.1), and provision of humanitarian assistance kits to meet essential needs. The project also addresses Action 7j (migrants in crisis situations accessing humanitarian assistance) and Action 7k (involving local authorities in identification and referral). The conclusions explicitly note that 'protection needs of internally displaced persons and migrants in transit have been addressed through the provision of objective information to the vulnerable population on health, hygiene and protection, including gender-based violence.' For a synthesis specialist, the sections on Results 3 and 4, the conclusions on protection services, and the recommendations on GBV prevention and disability inclusion would appear most relevant. The report's treatment of vulnerability is primarily operational (what assistance was provided) rather than analytical (what factors create vulnerability), which somewhat limits its depth for a synthesis on the structural dimensions of Objective 7.

References.Title: Final assessment report of the project "Addressing converging crises and unprecedented increase in the needs of internally displaced persons, returnees and their families in northern Central America"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute some evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The project explicitly addresses access to basic services for migrants in transit and IDPs, including health, hygiene, sanitation, and shelter services. The distribution of 25,591 kits (including hygiene kits, kitchen kits, shelter kits, PPE) and the improvement of 12 collective centers to meet Sphere Handbook standards directly relates to Action 15c (establishing accessible service points) and Action 15e (health needs of migrants). The project also addresses information provision on health and protection services. The context section explicitly notes that people in shelters face 'limited access to water, supplies, sanitation facilities, housing and access to health services.' However, the report's framing is primarily around emergency humanitarian assistance in shelter settings rather than systematic integration of migrants into national service delivery systems, which is the broader focus of Objective 15. For a synthesis specialists, the sections on Results 3 and 4, the kit distribution data, and the shelter infrastructure improvements would appear most relevant, with the caveat that the evidence pertains specifically to emergency/transit contexts rather than longer-term service integration.

References.Title: PROJECT EVALUATION REPORT LM.0290: ENHANCED SKILLS  DEVELOPMENT AND QUALIFICATION  RECOGNITION OF LABOUR MIGRANTS FROM  BANGLADESH
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would almost certainly be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 18. The project title itself — 'Enhanced Skills Development and Qualification Recognition of Labour Migrants from Bangladesh' — directly mirrors the language of Objective 18. The excerpts suggest the entire project is organized around skills development and qualification recognition for migrant workers, making this a core subject rather than a peripheral theme. Multiple associated actions appear directly addressed: Action 18a (developing standards for mutual recognition of qualifications) is reflected in the project's work on the National Skills Development Policy and NTVQF; Action 18b (promoting transparency of certifications and National Qualifications Frameworks) appears central to the BMET Action Plan and BTEB engagement; Action 18e (building global skills partnerships) is reflected in partnerships with City & Guilds, SQA, and ILO; Action 18j (innovative ways to recognize formally and informally acquired skills) is addressed through the Recognition of Prior Learning (RPL) component; and Action 18k (screening mechanisms and information on skills assessment prior to departure) appears relevant to the counselling and awareness components. The Technical Assessment Study 'Maximizing the Potential of Labour Migration through Skills Development and Certification,' the Tracer Study, the Labour Market Survey, and the BMET Action Plan all appear directly relevant to Objective 18 evidence needs. For a synthesis specialist, the effectiveness findings on outcomes 1-3, the sustainability analysis of the BMET Action Plan, the gender gap analysis regarding female-dominated sectors, and the recommendations on IOM niche in skills-migration linkages would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains substantially more evidence on this objective than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Colombia: Implementación de un piloto del ‘Modelo de Reincorporación temprana en entornos productivos’ con ex miembros de FARC
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly its reintegration dimension. The evaluation explicitly examines a pilot program designed to support the sustainable reintegration of ex-combatants (FARC-EP) into civilian life through vocational training and psychosocial support — themes that closely parallel GCM Objective 21's commitment to 'create conducive conditions for personal safety, economic empowerment, inclusion and social cohesion in communities, in order to ensure that reintegration of migrants upon return to their countries of origin is sustainable.' While the population is ex-combatants rather than returning migrants per se, the programmatic logic, challenges, and lessons are highly analogous to Action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through vocational training, employment opportunities, psycho-social assistance, and social protection) and Action 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive reintegration programmes). The report appears to provide substantive analysis of: (1) the design and implementation of a reintegration model (MRT) combining productive and psychosocial components; (2) barriers to participation including gender-specific obstacles (lack of childcare); (3) lessons on monitoring assumptions and ensuring program coherence in contexts with multiple competing service providers; and (4) the importance of gender-sensitive monitoring. For a synthesis specialist, the executive summary's discussion of the MRT model, Section 4.1 on dropout and gender barriers, Section 4.2 on enrollment challenges and coherence with other interventions, and Section 5 conclusions would appear most relevant as starting points. It should be noted that the population is post-conflict ex-combatants rather than international migrants, which may limit direct applicability, but the reintegration programming lessons appear transferable. The full report likely contains additional detail on reintegration outcomes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION: IOM TIMOR-LESTE COUNTER TRAFFICKING PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to have meaningful relevance for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project's third outcome explicitly focuses on 'collection and analysis of national migration data for future policy development in respect of irregular migration, trafficking in persons and smuggling in Timor-Leste,' and Output 3.1 concerns development of a national data management and protection system. The Migration Research and Planning Centre (MRPC) is a named project partner responsible for data collection and analysis. The lessons learned section notes that 'due consideration for evidence-based programming should be considered from project design stages,' and Recommendation 4 explicitly calls for 'prioritization of identification of trafficked persons and improvement of data collection' including 'development of data management systems to collect, synthesize, analyse, and disseminate data.' These elements touch on GCM Actions 1c (building national capacities in data collection) and 1j (country-specific migration profiles). Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on Outcome 3 and Output 3.1, as well as the lessons learned and recommendations sections, for evidence on how national migration data systems are built in low-capacity contexts. The excerpts suggest this is a secondary rather than primary focus of the evaluation, but substantive enough to warrant review.

References.Title: EVALUATION: IOM TIMOR-LESTE COUNTER TRAFFICKING PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Counter-trafficking is the explicit and central subject of the entire evaluation — the project title is 'Strengthening government and service providers responses to human trafficking in Timor-Leste: a capacity building initiative,' and the evaluation covers five consecutive IOM counter-trafficking projects spanning 2007 to the evaluation period. The report appears to directly address multiple GCM Actions under Objective 10: Action 10e (measures addressing vulnerabilities of trafficking victims, access to justice, protection and assistance), Action 10g (strengthening legislation and procedures to enhance prosecution and avoid criminalization of victims), Action 10h (providing victims with protection and assistance including physical, psychological and social recovery), and Action 10i (national information systems and training programmes to identify trafficking). The project's three outcomes map closely onto prosecution capacity (Outcome 1 on standard operating procedures), victim protection (Outcome 2 on direct assistance), and data/information systems (Outcome 3). The lessons learned on national ownership, evidence-based programming, and management structures, as well as all five recommendations, appear directly relevant. Synthesis specialists should prioritize the project description, conclusions, lessons learned, and recommendations sections, as well as the logic model table, as likely starting points for deeper review. The evaluation of five sequential projects also offers longitudinal perspective on what works in counter-trafficking programming — a relatively rare evidence type.

References.Title: EVALUATION: IOM TIMOR-LESTE COUNTER TRAFFICKING PROJECT
References.Abstract: The excerpts suggest this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12, particularly regarding Action 12b (specialized training for first responders and government officials on identification and referral of trafficking victims) and Action 12c (gender-responsive referral mechanisms and standardized operating procedures). The project's first outcome explicitly concerns 'implementation of national operating procedures to refer, protect and provide assistance to victims of trafficking,' and Output 1.1 focuses on increased MSS leadership in implementing standard operating procedures and referral guidelines. Training curricula development and seven 5-day trainings are listed as indicators. The lessons learned on management structures and the recommendations on institutionalizing standard operating procedures are also relevant. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the project description, the logic model table (particularly Outcome 1 and Output 1.1), and the recommendations section. This is a secondary theme relative to GCM Objective 10, but the referral mechanism and SOP content appears substantive enough to warrant targeted review for a synthesis on Objective 12.

References.Title: FINAL PROJECT EVALUATION BUILDING SUSTAINABLE PEACE AND PROMOTING HUMAN SECURITY OF CROSS-BORDER COMMUNITIES AND MOBILE POPULATIONS THROUGH INTEGRATED BORDER SECURITY AND MANAGEMENT AND COMMUNITY-BASED PEACEBUILDING ACTIVITIES

This directly relates to GCM Objective 10 actions on training and awareness (10i), awareness-raising campaigns (10j), monitoring irregular routes exploited by trafficking networks (10c), and capacity building for identification and protection of victims (10e). The project covers both official and unofficial border crossings as trafficking routes, involves multiple government agencies, and includes community-level sensitization. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on training outcomes for border officials, community sensitization effectiveness, inter-agency coordination on counter-trafficking, and recommendations on decentralizing sensitization campaigns would appear particularly valuable. The evaluation covers Lesotho — a country of origin, transit, and destination — providing a specific geographic and institutional context. It is possible the full report contains additional depth on victim identification procedures and referral mechanisms beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL PROJECT EVALUATION BUILDING SUSTAINABLE PEACE AND PROMOTING HUMAN SECURITY OF CROSS-BORDER COMMUNITIES AND MOBILE POPULATIONS THROUGH INTEGRATED BORDER SECURITY AND MANAGEMENT AND COMMUNITY-BASED PEACEBUILDING ACTIVITIES

This directly relates to GCM Objective 11 actions on international border management cooperation (11a), integrated border management structures (11b), technical cooperation agreements for assets and equipment (11d), and cross-border collaboration (11g). The LBMMA (Local Border Management and Migration Assessment) appears to be a key analytical tool examined in the evaluation. For a synthesis specialist, sections on inter-agency coordination outcomes, equipment effectiveness, the LBMMA methodology and results, and recommendations on CCTV management and border agency leadership would be particularly valuable starting points. The bilateral Lesotho-South Africa dimension also offers evidence on cross-border cooperation mechanisms in a Southern African context.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT: STRENGTHEN BORDER MANAGEMENT AND SECURITY IN MALI AND NIGER THROUGH CAPACITY BUILDING OF BORDER AUTHORITIES AND ENHANCED DIALOGUE WITH BORDER COMMUNITIES

The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: integrated border management (IBM) frameworks (Actions 11a, 11b), cross-border cooperation between Mali and Niger (Action 11g), capacity building of border authorities including training of trainers (Action 11a, 11d), community-security force dialogue mechanisms (CPCs) as part of whole-of-government approaches (Action 11a), and infrastructure rehabilitation for border management agencies (Action 11d). The evaluation covers all six OECD-DAC criteria applied to this border management intervention, providing evidence on what works and what doesn't in conflict-affected border zones. For a synthesis specialist, the effectiveness section on border management capacity outcomes, the sustainability analysis of institutional mechanisms (IBSM framework), the lessons learned on cross-border activities, and the recommendations on training plans and coordination mechanisms would appear particularly valuable. The report also offers evidence on the challenges of implementing integrated border management in insecure environments, which is underrepresented in the literature. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT: STRENGTHEN BORDER MANAGEMENT AND SECURITY IN MALI AND NIGER THROUGH CAPACITY BUILDING OF BORDER AUTHORITIES AND ENHANCED DIALOGUE WITH BORDER COMMUNITIES
