The project explicitly addresses community engagement, dialogue between communities and security forces, and social cohesion in border areas — themes that align with Actions 16a (mutual respect, social cohesion) and 16f (community centres/programmes for intercultural dialogue). The creation and reinforcement of Community Peace Committees (CPCs), communal and departmental committees for coordination, and community-based events all relate to building inclusive and cohesive societies. The empowerment of women groups (Action 16e) and youth engagement through RGAs also connect to this objective. The evaluation's effectiveness and sustainability sections appear to contain substantive analysis of what community engagement mechanisms worked and why, which could contribute to a synthesis on social cohesion approaches. However, the primary frame is border security rather than migrant inclusion per se — the 'communities' are border communities rather than migrant populations specifically. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the effectiveness section on CPCs and community engagement activities, and the lessons learned on associating security forces with community activities, while noting this caveat about the population focus.

References.Title: EVALUATION INTERNE DU PROJET (DIASPORA CONGOLESE)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The entire project — 'Renforcement des capacités pour une gestion stratégique de la diaspora congolaise' — is explicitly designed to mobilize diaspora contributions to sustainable development in Congo, directly addressing the core theme of this objective. The excerpts suggest substantive engagement with multiple associated actions: Action 19d (establishing dedicated diaspora offices and focal points — the DCE and Cellule de mobilisation are central project partners); Action 19i (enabling diaspora professionals to carry out activities in their home country without losing employment status — the temporary mission model); Action 19j (building partnerships for knowledge and skills transfer between diaspora and country of origin); and Action 19c (research on non-financial contributions of migrants and diasporas). The evaluation appears to assess the effectiveness of government capacity building for diaspora management, the operationalization of diaspora mobilization structures, and the outcomes of temporary expert missions in health and education — all core to Objective 19. The Matrix 1 and Matrix 2 sections, the conclusions on capacity building outcomes, and the recommendations for a MIDA Phase 2 would appear particularly valuable for human reviewers. The report also discusses lessons learned about diaspora engagement models (MIDA approach), which could contribute comparative evidence to a synthesis. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF MIDA SOMALIA  PHASE I
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to have meaningful relevance for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1, particularly through Component 2 of the MIDA Somalia project. The excerpts indicate that Component 2 was specifically designed to establish a civil register in Puntland 'through an accurate population information system by collecting personal data of Puntland residents' — directly touching on Action 1a (improving migration data systems) and Action 1i (using administrative records including population registers for statistical purposes). The executive summary and scope sections describe this as an essential step for planning democratic actions and governance structures. The evaluation appears to assess the effectiveness of this civil registration pilot, which would provide evidence on national data collection capacity-building efforts (Action 1c). However, the excerpts suggest that the evaluation team's review of Component 2 was limited to desk review and discussions with the IP, which may limit the depth of evidence available. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on Component 2 findings and the Garowe-based interviews with URS/SGI staff, as these appear most directly relevant to data collection and civil registry themes. The full report may contain more substantive analysis of the civil registration pilot than what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF MIDA SOMALIA  PHASE I

The project description notes this was 'an essential step to planning democratic actions to set up coherent governance structures.' The evaluation assesses this civil registration pilot, which would provide evidence on civil registry capacity-building efforts. However, the excerpts note that the evaluation team's review of Component 2 was limited to desk review and IP discussions, which may constrain the depth of evidence. Synthesis specialists would want to review the Component 2 sections and any findings related to the Puntland civil registration pilot, particularly the Garowe-based interviews. The full report likely contains more detail on this component than the excerpts reveal.

This directly addresses multiple associated actions: Action 19c (research on non-financial contributions of migrants and diasporas, including knowledge and skills transfer), Action 19d (establishing mechanisms for diaspora contributions, including diaspora focal points), Action 19i (enabling migrants to carry out professional activities and engage in knowledge transfer in home countries), and Action 19j (building partnerships for knowledge and skills transfer between countries of origin and destination). Particularly valuable for synthesis specialists would be the sections on policy and legislation achievements by diaspora experts, the sustainability assessment, and the recommendations on diaspora database maintenance and institutional support — these appear to offer concrete evidence on what works and what challenges arise in diaspora-led development contributions. Several associated actions appear closely aligned: Action 16a (promoting mutual respect for cultures and integration policies), Action 16f (community centres/programmes for intercultural dialogue), and Action 16h (multicultural activities such as festivals — exemplified by the Dabugit Egae festival). The evaluation's findings on DoMA's capacity to design and implement integration projects, the effectiveness of community outreach campaigns, and the cultural orientation strategy for refugees all appear directly relevant. References.Title: EVALUACIÓN FINAL DEL PROYECTO “STRENGTHENING MECHANISMS FOR HUMANITARIAN ASSISTANCE TO VENEZUELAN MIGRANTS, COLOMBIAN RETURNEES AND HOST COMMUNITIES IN COLOMBIA”

The DTM is explicitly one of the five project components and appears to receive substantive analytical attention throughout the evaluation. The excerpts suggest analysis of data collection methodology, quality of information gathered, disaggregation by population characteristics (age, gender, vulnerability status), and the use of data for policy and decision-making purposes. Relevant associated actions include 1a (improving migration data collection and dissemination), 1c (building national capacities in data collection), and 1j (developing country-specific migration profiles). The evaluation appears to examine how DTM survey data was used to characterize migrant households with children and adolescents (NNAJ), and discusses limitations in data quality due to time pressures — content that would be valuable for a synthesis on evidence-based migration data systems. The conclusions and recommendations sections specifically address the need to share DTM results with enumerators and local authorities, and to use data as input for policy design. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on the DTM component findings, the discussion of data quality challenges, and the recommendations on information use — though border management and humanitarian assistance appear to be equally prominent themes in the report.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN FINAL DEL PROYECTO “STRENGTHENING MECHANISMS FOR HUMANITARIAN ASSISTANCE TO VENEZUELAN MIGRANTS, COLOMBIAN RETURNEES AND HOST COMMUNITIES IN COLOMBIA”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 4. Registration and documentation is explicitly one of the five project components, and the excerpts suggest substantive analysis of documentation processes for Venezuelan migrants and Colombian returnees. The evaluation reports a 111% achievement rate on registration and documentation targets, and the conclusions discuss challenges in providing documentation more efficiently. Associated actions most relevant include 4a (improving civil registry systems), 4c (ensuring adequate consular documentation), and 4d (facilitating access to personal documentation). The report appears to examine how documentation assistance was adapted during COVID-19 and what challenges remained. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on the registration and documentation component findings, the discussion of efficiency challenges, and the recommendations on streamlining documentation processes — though this is one of several components covered rather than the sole focus of the evaluation.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN FINAL DEL PROYECTO “STRENGTHENING MECHANISMS FOR HUMANITARIAN ASSISTANCE TO VENEZUELAN MIGRANTS, COLOMBIAN RETURNEES AND HOST COMMUNITIES IN COLOMBIA”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. Addressing vulnerabilities in migration appears to be a central theme throughout the evaluation, with explicit attention to identifying and assisting the most vulnerable populations — particularly children and adolescents (NNA/NNAJ), unaccompanied minors, women, and families. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how the project identified and prioritized vulnerable groups, what needs remained unmet (e.g., child care, sexual and reproductive health for adolescents, education), and how vulnerability-focused assistance was adapted during COVID-19. Relevant associated actions include 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies addressing needs of women and children), 7e (accounting for migrant children in child protection systems), and 7f (protecting unaccompanied and separated children). The evaluation's conclusions and recommendations explicitly address gaps in vulnerability coverage and advocate for stronger articulation with child protection authorities (ICBF). Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on coverage of vulnerable populations, the discussion of unmet needs for NNA, and the recommendations on child-focused programming — these appear to be among the most analytically developed themes in the excerpts reviewed.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN FINAL DEL PROYECTO “STRENGTHENING MECHANISMS FOR HUMANITARIAN ASSISTANCE TO VENEZUELAN MIGRANTS, COLOMBIAN RETURNEES AND HOST COMMUNITIES IN COLOMBIA”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. Access to basic services for migrants appears to be the primary stated objective of the project itself — the first expected result explicitly states that migrants, returnees and vulnerable community members have 'improved access to basic services to meet their urgent humanitarian needs.' The evaluation examines multiple service delivery components: shelter/accommodation (CATM), humanitarian transport, registration and documentation, and infrastructure improvements to service facilities. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of service access outcomes, barriers, gaps (health, education, psychosocial support, sexual and reproductive health), and the role of institutional coordination in enabling service delivery. Relevant associated actions include 15a (non-discriminatory service delivery), 15c (holistic and accessible service points at local level), 15e (health needs of migrants), and 15f (inclusive education for migrant children). The evaluation's conclusions and recommendations extensively address unmet service needs and advocate for stronger articulation with local and national service providers. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the sections on service access outcomes, the discussion of coverage gaps for NNA and women, and the recommendations on institutional coordination — these appear to be among the most analytically developed themes in the report. The full report likely contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION: MONITORING PROGRESS IN ACHIEVING THE MIGRATION TARGETS OF THE 2030 AGENDA FOR SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT IN ARMENIA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project's core purpose — building Armenia's capacity to monitor migration and development (M&D) indicators within the 2030 Agenda — is directly aligned with this objective's theme of improving migration data collection, analysis, and use for evidence-based policymaking. The project title itself references 'monitoring progress' on migration targets, and the evaluation explicitly assesses outcomes related to improved data collection and usage, development of M&D indicators and methodology, and strengthening of ArmStat's (the national statistical office) capacities. Actions 1a (comprehensive strategy for improving migration data), 1c (building national capacities in data collection and analysis), 1d (collecting data on migration's effects on sustainable development), and 1i (enhancing collaboration between migration data units and national statistical offices) appear particularly well-covered. The excerpts suggest multiple findings and conclusions directly address data collection improvements, the Migration Snapshot as new data produced, and the Voluntary National Review (VNR) as a monitoring mechanism. The sections on effectiveness, impact, and sustainability appear most relevant for human reviewers to prioritize, as they contain substantive analysis of data capacity outcomes and the monitoring process established.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION: MONITORING PROGRESS IN ACHIEVING THE MIGRATION TARGETS OF THE 2030 AGENDA FOR SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT IN ARMENIA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The project explicitly focuses on integrating migration into Armenia's 2030 Agenda implementation — a core theme of this objective. Actions 19a (ensuring full implementation of the 2030 Agenda by fostering positive effects of migration) and 19b (integrating migration into development planning and sectoral policies) appear particularly well-covered. The project's work on nationalizing SDG targets related to migration and development, producing the Migration Snapshot, and supporting the VNR process all relate to embedding migration in national development frameworks. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how migration data and monitoring can support development planning. However, the report's primary focus is on the data and monitoring infrastructure rather than on migrants' or diasporas' direct contributions to development, which is the core of Objective 19. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the sections on effectiveness and impact, as well as the lessons identified, which discuss the M&D framework and its integration into national SDG reporting. The full report may contain additional relevant content on this theme beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: ACOMPAÑAMIENTO A POBLACIÓN VÍCTIMA DEL DESPLAZAMIENTO FORZADO A TRAVÉS DE UNIDADES MÓVILES EVALUACIÓN FINAL DE PROYECTO
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7, particularly regarding the protection of children (NNA) in situations of forced displacement. The project evaluated is explicitly designed to address the vulnerabilities of internally displaced children and their families — a population whose situation closely parallels that of migrants in vulnerable situations under the GCM framework. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: (a) interdisciplinary and differential care models for vulnerable populations (relating to Actions 7b and 7c); (b) child protection systems and best interests of the child (Action 7e); (c) psychosocial support and access to services for displaced children (Actions 7b, 7c, 7k); and (d) gender-responsive and ethnically differentiated approaches (Action 7a). The report's conclusions and recommendations address the adequacy of protection frameworks and the integration of human rights and gender approaches. The sections on pertinencia (relevance), eficacia (effectiveness), and cuestiones transversales (cross-cutting issues on human rights and gender) appear most relevant for human reviewers to prioritize. Note that the population is internally displaced rather than international migrants, which may limit direct applicability depending on the synthesis scope, but the evidence on child-sensitive, interdisciplinary vulnerability response is likely transferable.

References.Title: ACOMPAÑAMIENTO A POBLACIÓN VÍCTIMA DEL DESPLAZAMIENTO FORZADO A TRAVÉS DE UNIDADES MÓVILES EVALUACIÓN FINAL DE PROYECTO

The project evaluated centers on mobile units (Unidades Móviles) that deliver interdisciplinary services — including psychosocial support, rights-based assistance, and emergency humanitarian care — to internally displaced children and families across all 32 Colombian departments. This appears directly relevant to Actions 15c (holistic, accessible service points at local level, migrant-inclusive) and 15e (health needs of migrants in local health care policies). The report's analysis of service delivery models, coverage (over 1.1 million persons served), and differential approaches for ethnic minorities and other vulnerable groups would likely add value to a synthesis on service access for displaced/migrant populations. The sections on eficacia (effectiveness of service delivery) and pertinencia (relevance of the service model to beneficiary needs) appear most worth reviewing. The primary limitation is that the population is internally displaced rather than international migrants, which may affect direct applicability depending on synthesis scope.

References.Title: STRENGTHENING SOCIAL COHESION AND STABILITY IN SLUM POPULATIONS PROJECT (SSCOS)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. Social cohesion is the explicit and central theme of the SSCoS project — it appears in the project title itself ('Strengthening Social Cohesion and Stability in Slum Populations'). The evaluation appears to directly assess interventions designed to foster inclusion and cohesion in diverse urban communities, including refugees and migrants alongside host community members. Multiple actions under Objective 16 appear strongly aligned: Action 16a (promoting mutual respect and social cohesion), Action 16d (inclusive labour markets and decent work for migrants), Action 16f (community centres and intercultural dialogue), and Action 16g (vocational and civic integration). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of community engagement activities (town halls, community dialogues, Music Dance and Drama sessions), youth economic empowerment (vocational training, small business start-ups, cooperatives), and capacity building for community resilience. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on community engagement mechanisms, youth employment outcomes, and the multi-sectoral approach to building social cohesion would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The project's focus on both host and migrant/refugee populations in an urban slum context offers potentially distinctive evidence on inclusion dynamics.

References.Title: KENYA: ENGAGING MIGRANTS AND DIASPORA COMMUNITIES FOR AN INCLUSIVE AND CLIMATE RESILIENT BLUE ECONOMY
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its associated actions related to climate change adaptation, environmental degradation, and building resilience (actions 2b, 2i, and 2l). The project explicitly addresses climate change resilience for migrant and pastoralist communities in Kenya's Tana River region — a drought-prone, climate-vulnerable area — and frames its interventions around reducing the structural drivers that compel displacement. The project title itself references 'climate resilient blue economy,' and the evaluation assesses how well the project strengthened community resilience to climate change through diaspora and migrant engagement. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of livelihood support for migrant communities (fishing, agribusiness, pastoralism) as in-situ alternatives to forced migration, which directly relates to action 2i ('develop adaptation and resilience strategies to slow-onset natural disasters and adverse effects of climate change'). The executive summary, conclusions (C9, C16), and recommendations (R2) appear particularly relevant for human reviewers, as they assess the effectiveness of livelihood interventions in building climate resilience. The report also engages with Kenya's National Climate Change Action Plan and SDG 13. It is worth noting that border management, not climate resilience, is the dominant theme in many GCM Objective 2 syntheses, so this report's focus on the migration-environment-climate change (MECC) nexus at the community level would likely add distinctive value.

References.Title: KENYA: ENGAGING MIGRANTS AND DIASPORA COMMUNITIES FOR AN INCLUSIVE AND CLIMATE RESILIENT BLUE ECONOMY
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The project's core purpose — engaging migrants and diaspora communities for climate-resilient blue economy development in Kenya — directly addresses the central theme of this objective. The project explicitly works to harness diaspora resources and knowledge for sustainable development (actions 19c, 19d, 19j), establishes government-diaspora linkages through the Technical Working Group and Ministry of Foreign Affairs Diaspora Directorate (action 19d), and evaluates the effectiveness of diaspora engagement mechanisms. The evaluation assesses how well diaspora knowledge transfer contributed to climate resilience and blue economy outcomes (C9), the role of the TICAD 7 Symposium in catalyzing diaspora engagement, and the sustainability of diaspora-government connections. The project also addresses action 19b (integrating migration into development planning) through the MECC policy agenda work, and action 19j (building partnerships between local authorities and diaspora organizations). Key sections for human reviewers to prioritize include: the executive summary's discussion of diaspora-government connections (C9), the conclusions on sustainability of diaspora engagement (C10, C11), recommendations on scaling livelihood initiatives (R2), and the lessons identified on community-based development approaches. The full report likely contains additional evidence on diaspora engagement mechanisms beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION: CONTRIBUTING TO THE ACHIEVEMENT OF SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT GOALS RELATED TO MIGRATION MANAGEMENT IN THE DOMINICAN REPUBLIC
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project's core purpose was to support the Dominican Republic in measuring SDG progress related to migration management, which directly involves migration data collection, analysis, and indicator development. The excerpts suggest substantive engagement with actions closely aligned with Objective 1: the use of the National Immigrant Surveys (ENIs) to create baselines (actions 1g, 1h), the development of migration-related indicators through collaboration with the National Statistics Office (ONE) (action 1i), and the application of the Migration Governance Index (MGI) as an innovative measurement tool (action 1a, 1c). The project explicitly worked to identify migration-related indicators contributing to SDG targets, which aligns with action 1d on data for sustainable development. The effectiveness section highlights the use of a digital platform for data collection and the establishment of baselines for comparison — both directly relevant to evidence synthesis on data systems. Human reviewers would likely find the effectiveness and impact sections particularly valuable, as they appear to contain substantive analysis of how migration data systems were strengthened and institutionalized. The collaboration with ONE and the secondment of consultants to statistical institutions also appears relevant to action 1i on enhancing collaboration between migration data units and national statistical offices.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION: CONTRIBUTING TO THE ACHIEVEMENT OF SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT GOALS RELATED TO MIGRATION MANAGEMENT IN THE DOMINICAN REPUBLIC
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 19, particularly regarding the integration of migration into development planning and SDG implementation (actions 19a and 19b). The project's explicit purpose was to support the Dominican Republic in measuring SDG progress related to migration management, which directly relates to action 19a on implementing the 2030 Agenda and action 19b on integrating migration into development planning. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how government entities were mobilized to embed migration governance into national development frameworks, including the National Development Strategy (END) and Agenda 2030. The use of the Voluntary National Review (VNR) as a milestone and the engagement of multiple ministries (MEPyD, MIREX, MIP) in SDG-related migration planning appear particularly relevant. The sustainability section, which discusses how processes were institutionalized beyond project closure, would likely be valuable for synthesis specialists examining how migration-development policy coherence is achieved and sustained. Human reviewers would likely want to examine the effectiveness and sustainability sections for evidence on how migration was mainstreamed into national development planning processes. The report appears to be one of several themes covered, so it scores in the RELEVANT rather than FUNDAMENTAL range for this objective.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN EX-POST DEL PROYECTO: DESARROLLO DE UN MAPA INTERACTIVO DE MIGRACIÓN Y SALUD PARA LA REGIÓN MESOAMERICANA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project evaluated — development of an interactive migration and health map for the Mesoamerican region — is fundamentally about collecting, consolidating, and disseminating disaggregated migration and health data to support evidence-based policymaking. The project title itself reflects this core focus. The excerpts suggest that multiple evaluation dimensions directly address data collection, disaggregation (including by sex/gender), data gaps, comparability across countries, and the use of data for policy development. The evaluation appears to assess the quality and usability of the interactive map as a data platform, the degree to which data were disaggregated by sex and other variables, limitations in official data collection systems, and the extent to which the tool informed policy. These themes align closely with GCM Actions 1a (comprehensive strategy for improving migration data), 1b (international comparability), 1d (data on effects of migration for sustainable development), 1e (support for global/regional databases), and 1j (country-specific migration profiles). The sustainability section explicitly flags data currency and geolocation as key challenges, and the gender section highlights limitations in disaggregated data. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on the interactive map's data architecture, the gender/disaggregation analysis, the coherence findings on data duplication with other platforms, and the sustainability recommendations on data institutionalization would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN EX-POST DEL PROYECTO: DESARROLLO DE UN MAPA INTERACTIVO DE MIGRACIÓN Y SALUD PARA LA REGIÓN MESOAMERICANA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly Action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in national and local health care policies and plans). The project's explicit purpose is to strengthen the evidence base and technical capacity for migrant-sensitive public health policy in Mesoamerica — directly addressing the health service dimension of GCM Objective 15. The evaluation appears to assess whether the interactive map and executive course contributed to health authorities' capacity to design migrant-inclusive health policies and programs. The project's stated priorities — protecting the right to health of migrant populations and strengthening capacities for migrant-sensitive public health action — align closely with Action 15e's emphasis on strengthening capacities for service provision and training health care providers. The impact section notes short-term positive changes in visibility of migration-health linkages, and the sustainability section raises concerns about whether the tools will continue to inform health policy. The gender section's discussion of differentiated health impacts on migrant women and children also touches on Action 15e's non-discriminatory access dimension. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on the project's contribution to migrant health policy capacity (Outcome 1 and 2 analysis), the impact assessment on policy influence, and the sustainability recommendations on institutionalizing the map within health data platforms would appear most relevant as starting points. The report's focus is on the data/capacity infrastructure enabling health service access rather than direct service delivery, which should be noted as a limitation for synthesis purposes.

References.Title: EVALUATION MI-PARCOURS DU PROGRAMME D'APPUI À LA STRATÉGIE NATIONALE DE LA MIGRATION (PASNGM)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The PASNGM explicitly includes a component dedicated to improving migration data and knowledge (Composante 1: Appui à la coordination et pour une meilleure connaissance de la migration), and the excerpts describe substantive activities related to integrating a migration module into the national census (RGPH), conducting migration-related studies, and supporting a university research laboratory on migration. These activities relate directly to GCM Actions 1g (integrating migration topics in national censuses), 1f (establishing regional research centres/observatories), and 1h (conducting surveys on migrants). The evaluation appears to assess the effectiveness of these data-collection and knowledge-generation efforts, including the FNUAP-supported census module and planned studies on labour permits and trafficking. Section 5.1 (Composante 1 results) and the conclusions section appear particularly relevant for human reviewers, as they discuss what was achieved in terms of data production and what remains to be done. The report also touches on dissemination and use of migration data for policymaking, relevant to Action 1d. While data is not the sole focus of the report, it appears to be a substantive and dedicated theme with analytical depth in the excerpts reviewed.

References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The PASNGM includes a dedicated component (Composante 3) on protection of migrants and refugees, covering unaccompanied minors, asylum seekers, child trafficking victims, and vulnerable migrants more broadly. The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of protection measures, including capacity building for asylum processing, support for talibé children, and actions for refugees at M'Bera camp. These relate to GCM Actions 7b (assistance to vulnerable migrants), 7e and 7f (protection of migrant children and unaccompanied minors), and 7g (access to legal assistance). Section 5.3 (Composante 3) appears particularly relevant for human reviewers, as it discusses both what has been achieved and what remains to be done in terms of protection of vulnerable migrants. The report also touches on access to legal aid and the situation of refugees, adding breadth to its relevance for this objective. While protection is one of four main components, it receives dedicated analytical attention in the excerpts.

These activities relate directly to GCM Actions 11a (international border management cooperation), 11b (integrated border management structures), and 11d (technical cooperation agreements). The evaluation also involves the OIM and Guardia Civil as implementing partners, adding an international cooperation dimension. Section 5.4 (Composante 4) and the general conclusions appear particularly valuable for human reviewers, as they provide detailed assessment of what worked and what did not in border management capacity building in a West African context. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The project title itself — 'Promoting a regional corridor approach to coordinated/integrated border management in Eastern and Southern Africa' — directly mirrors the language of this objective. The evaluation appears to comprehensively assess a multi-country initiative explicitly designed to strengthen integrated, coordinated border management at One Stop Border Posts (OSBPs) and other Border Crossing Points (BCPs) in Malawi, Zambia, and Zimbabwe. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple associated actions: action 11a (international/regional border management cooperation, capacity-building, cross-border training — the project involved training, SOPs, and stakeholder coordination across three governments); action 11b (integrated border management structures, efficient crossing procedures, use of ICT — the MIDAS installation at Mchinji BCP directly addresses this); action 11g (cross-border collaboration on treatment of persons crossing borders). The evaluation also addresses gender mainstreaming in border management (critiquing absence of gender considerations in SOPs), touching on the gender-responsive dimension of Objective 11. The project's regional corridor approach, COMESA alignment, and multi-government coordination are all directly relevant. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on effectiveness (achievement of border management outcomes), impact (MIDAS installation, SOP development, reduced clearance times), sustainability (capacity building, follow-up projects), and the recommendations on MIDAS implementation and SOP updating would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Expost evaluation of the project "Promoting a Regional Corridor Approach to Coordinated/Integrated Border Management in Eastern and Southern Africa"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12, particularly around action 12a (transparency and accessibility of migration procedures, use of technology to simplify procedures) and action 12c (gender-responsive referral mechanisms, standardized operating procedures developed in coordination with authorities). The project developed Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for border management at targeted BCPs, which directly relates to the standardized procedures dimension of Objective 12. The installation of MIDAS at Mchinji BCP — described as reducing clearance time and improving reporting — relates to action 12a's emphasis on technology to improve procedural efficiency. The evaluation's critique that SOPs lacked gender considerations is also relevant to action 12c's gender-responsive dimension. However, the excerpts suggest the project's primary framing is around integrated border management and trade facilitation rather than screening and referral per se, and there is no evident focus on individual assessment of migrants or protection referrals. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the sections on SOP development, MIDAS implementation outcomes, and the recommendations on updating SOPs, while noting that the screening/referral dimension of Objective 12 may be less central than the procedural efficiency dimension.

References.Title: Development and Implementation of a Central American Joint Initiative on The Health of Migrants
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly Action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in national and local health care policies and plans). The project's central purpose — creating a regional technical coordination platform to advocate for migrant-inclusive, gender-sensitive health policies and increase investment in migrant health access to services — directly addresses the health service access dimension of GCM Objective 15. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: (1) multi-stakeholder coordination to improve health service access for migrants across Central America and Mexico; (2) advocacy for migrant-inclusive health policies through the INCOSAMI platform; (3) engagement with PAHO/WHO, COMISCA, and civil society to strengthen health governance for migrants; and (4) challenges in sustaining policy-level change. The evaluation's findings on effectiveness (noting that policy change was 'less evident but showed potential') and sustainability concerns are directly relevant to understanding what works in implementing Action 15e at a regional level. The recommendations on platform sustainability and funding mechanisms also offer practical lessons. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on effectiveness (policy advocacy outcomes), impact (short-term changes in stakeholder awareness and cross-sectoral working), and sustainability would appear most valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence on health service access beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: ÉVALUATION DU PROJET « RENFORCEMENT DE LA GESTION DES FLUX MIGRATOIRES EN MAURITANIE À TRAVERS UN MEILLEUR CONTRÔLE DES FRONTIÈRES ET L'ASSISTANCE AUX MIGRANTS EN SITUATION IRRÉGULIÈRE »
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly relevant to this objective: (1) construction and equipping of border posts (Actions 11b, 11d); (2) training of police and gendarmerie on border procedures and human rights (Actions 11a, 11c, 11g); (3) development of standard operating procedures (SOPs) for border control (Action 11b); (4) creation of an inter-institutional coordination committee between Police, Customs, and Gendarmerie (Actions 11a, 11b); (5) the tension between security-oriented border management and rights-based approaches (Actions 11c, 11f, 11g); and (6) bilateral cooperation with Spain (joint police unit, gendarmerie cooperation) (Action 11a). The excerpts discuss: the design of the AVRR programme including collective AGR (income-generating activities) for reintegration (Action 21b, 21h); concerns about group cohesion and community tensions upon return (Action 21h, 21i); the importance of free and informed consent in voluntary return (Action 21b); and lessons from Côte d'Ivoire experience with community AGR (Action 21h). References.Title: EXPOST EVALUATION of the Migration, Environment and Climate Change: Evidence for Policy in Southern Africa and the Indian Ocean Project
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The MECC project was explicitly designed to generate an evidence base on migration, environment and climate change — conducting country-level assessments in four countries (Madagascar, Mauritius, Mozambique, Namibia), developing blueprint documents translating research into policy recommendations, and holding national and regional dialogues. The evaluation assesses the quality, methodology, and dissemination of these research products. Actions 1c (building national capacities in data collection and analysis), 1d (collecting data on effects of migration for sustainable development), and 1k (research on interrelationship between migration and sustainable development) appear most directly related. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how evidence was generated, its quality, and critically, its limited dissemination and utilization — which is directly relevant to the 'use' dimension of Objective 1. The sections on 'Overall Findings and Conclusions' (particularly points a, d, e) and the 'Analysis of Project Effectiveness based on the Results Framework' (Output 1.1) would appear most valuable for human reviewers. However, the report's focus is on evaluating a specific project rather than on data systems or statistical infrastructure per se, which limits its score relative to a more data-system-focused evaluation.

References.Title: EXPOST EVALUATION of the Migration, Environment and Climate Change: Evidence for Policy in Southern Africa and the Indian Ocean Project
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its sub-theme on natural disasters, climate change, and environmental degradation (Actions 2h, 2i, 2j, 2k, 2l). The project's entire raison d'être is the nexus between migration, environment and climate change — examining how climate-related hazards (droughts, floods, cyclones, desertification, sea-level rise) drive migration in Southern Africa and the Indian Ocean. The evaluation assesses a project explicitly designed to build evidence and policy capacity on this nexus. Actions 2h (joint analysis to map migration movements resulting from natural disasters and climate change), 2i (adaptation and resilience strategies taking into account migration implications), and 2k (harmonizing approaches at subregional/regional levels for persons affected by natural disasters) appear most directly addressed. The project background section provides detailed country-level documentation of climate-related drivers of migration in Madagascar, Mauritius, Mozambique and Namibia. The findings on capacity building, policy blueprint development, and regional forum outcomes are directly relevant to understanding how states are building capacity to address climate-environment-migration linkages. The sections on project background, overall findings (points a, b, f), and the DAC criteria analysis (relevance, sustainability) would be particularly valuable for human reviewers. The regional dimension — engaging SADC and IOC member states — also speaks to Action 2k on harmonizing subregional approaches.

References.Title: REPORT ON IOM DEVELOPMENT FUND 2017 EX-POST EVALUATIONS

The excerpts suggest substantive discussion of data collection challenges, the importance of stakeholder consultation in data processes, and the need to promote migration data products to wider audiences for policy use. The recommendations section on Migration Profiles specifically addresses how to maintain data collection momentum and link profiles to policy development. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on Migration Profile sustainability, the recommendations on post-project engagement to promote data products, and the discussion of evidence-based policymaking linkages would appear most relevant as starting points. The report also touches on monitoring and evaluation data systems, though this is more operational than directly tied to Objective 1 actions. It is possible the full report contains more granular evidence on data collection methodologies than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: REPORT ON IOM DEVELOPMENT FUND 2017 EX-POST EVALUATIONS

Specialists would particularly want to review the sustainability findings on TiP projects and the dedicated counter-trafficking recommendations section.

References.Title: External Evaluation of the Project ‘Institutionalize Health Care Improvement through Temporary Returns of Somali Diaspora Health Professionals to Somaliland through Migration for Development in Africa (MIDA),” a project funded by the Government of Finland

The MIDA FINNSOM IV project is explicitly designed around diaspora contributions to sustainable development — specifically, temporary return of Somali diaspora health professionals to transfer skills and strengthen health institutions in Somaliland. This appears to be the central subject of the evaluation. The project title, evaluation questions, and all major findings directly address diaspora engagement for development, relating closely to actions 19c (research on non-financial contributions of diaspora including skills transfer), 19d (government structures facilitating diaspora contributions), 19h (policies optimizing diaspora benefits through flexible modalities), 19i (enabling diaspora to carry out professional activities in home countries), and 19j (partnerships for knowledge and skills transfer). The excerpts suggest multiple findings address the effectiveness of the diaspora-national model, unintended positive consequences of diaspora engagement (new partnerships, permanent returns, resource mobilization), sustainability of diaspora contributions, and recommendations for diaspora networks and trust funds. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on the diaspora-national peer model effectiveness, unintended positive consequences of diaspora engagement, sustainability challenges, and recommendations on diaspora networks and trust funds would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The evaluation also appears to provide evidence on what motivates diaspora participation and how to sustain diaspora contributions — directly relevant to action 19i and 19j. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: External Evaluation of the Project ‘Institutionalize Health Care Improvement through Temporary Returns of Somali Diaspora Health Professionals to Somaliland through Migration for Development in Africa (MIDA),” a project funded by the Government of Finland
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly regarding sustainable reintegration and voluntary return. The MIDA FINNSOM IV project involves temporary and in some cases permanent return of Somali diaspora professionals to Somaliland, with the evaluation examining sustainability of these returns and reintegration into professional life. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of what enables diaspora to return (action 21b on voluntary return programmes), conditions for sustainable reintegration (action 21h on reintegration into community life), and the role of professional networks in sustaining returnees. The report discusses unintended positive consequences including some diaspora permanently relocating, the motivations for return, and recommendations for alumni networks and incentive structures. However, this is voluntary diaspora return for development purposes rather than return of irregular migrants — a different context from the core focus of Objective 21. A synthesis specialist working on diaspora return and reintegration dimensions of Objective 21 would likely find the sections on diaspora motivation, sustainability of return, and recommendations on network building and incentive structures worth reviewing, while noting the development-oriented rather than forced/irregular migration return context. The full report may contain more detailed analysis of return sustainability than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION FINALE DU PROJET ENHANCING BORDER MANAGEMENT TO COUNTER TERRORISM IN BURKINA FASO

The project explicitly aims to 'reinforce la cohésion sociale dans une zone frontalière' (strengthen social cohesion in a border area), and Outcome 2 is titled 'Strengthened collaboration and cooperation among cross border populations and local authorities.' The evaluation appears to assess the effectiveness of community engagement activities, dialogue between border communities and local authorities, and the shift from fear to trust and cooperation — themes that align with Action 16a (promoting mutual respect and social cohesion) and Action 16f (community centres/programmes facilitating migrant participation). A synthesis specialist would want to review the sections on Outcome 2 and Output 3, particularly the community consultation and cooperation plan activities, while noting that the border security framing somewhat limits direct applicability to GCM Objective 16's integration-focused commitments.

The excerpts suggest that multiple conclusions directly address: community-level prevention and identification of trafficking (Actions 10c, 10i, 10j); awareness-raising campaigns targeting migrants and prospective migrants on trafficking risks (Action 10j); capacity-building of civil society organizations to identify trafficking signs and refer cases (Action 10i); psychosocial assistance to victims and vulnerable persons (Action 10e, 10h); and the legal/policy framework including Nicaragua's Ley No. 896 and alignment with the Palermo Protocol (Action 10a, 10b). The sections on Producto 1.3, the Norma Técnica, and conclusions 1 and 3 would be most relevant for synthesis specialists. The depth of analysis on trafficking specifically — versus the broader LOMH framework — is uncertain from the excerpts alone.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION: INITIATIVE FOR ETHICAL RECRUITMENT IN MOROCCO (IREM)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 6. Ethical recruitment is the explicit and central subject of the evaluated project — 'Initiative for Ethical Recruitment in Morocco (IREM)' — appearing in the project title, evaluation questions, and throughout all findings and conclusions. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis directly relevant to multiple associated actions: Actions 6c and 6f (regulation of recruitment practices and strengthening labour inspectors' enforcement capacities — the train-the-trainers workshop for labour inspectors is a key output); Action 6d (partnerships with stakeholders including employers, trade unions, and government bodies to promote awareness of ethical recruitment standards); Action 6i (treatment and rights of migrant workers in the labour market); and Action 6l (development of national policies on labour mobility drawing on ILO principles). The evaluation assesses what worked and what did not in raising awareness on ethical recruitment among key Moroccan institutions (MTIP, ANAPEC, MDCMREAM, CGEM, UMT), the challenges of introducing this concept in a new context, and the sustainability of changes in recruitment practices. For a synthesis specialist, the findings sections on effectiveness (Output 1.2 on training of labour inspectors, sensitization sessions) and sustainability (follow-up gaps after train-the-trainers), as well as the conclusions on programmatic approach and lessons identified, would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION: INITIATIVE FOR ETHICAL RECRUITMENT IN MOROCCO (IREM)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. The integration of migrant workers into Morocco's labor market is a central theme throughout the evaluation — explicitly described as a core focus alongside ethical recruitment. This aligns with Action 16d (working towards inclusive labor markets and full participation of migrant workers in the formal economy) and Action 16c (developing policy goals for labor market integration). The evaluation examines institutional efforts by MTIP and ANAPEC to adapt practices for migrant worker integration, the challenges of reaching employers and trade unions, and the gradual adaptation of practices. The excerpts also note that the project addressed anti-discrimination and pro-diversity practices (Output 1.1 training modules), connecting to Action 16a. However, social cohesion, community integration, and cultural inclusion dimensions appear less developed than the labor market integration focus. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the effectiveness findings on institutional practice changes and the sustainability section on what integration gains persisted, framing these as pointers for further review rather than definitive characterizations.

References.Title: Final evaluation of the Associazioni Migranti per il Co Sviluppo (A.MI.CO.) Programme
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The A.MI.CO. Programme is explicitly designed to empower migrants and diaspora associations to contribute to sustainable development — this is the programme's stated core purpose. The report appears to directly address multiple associated actions under Objective 19, most notably: Action 19d (establishing mechanisms to facilitate diaspora contributions, including diaspora focal points and support structures — here embodied by IOM Italy's structured support system); Action 19e (targeted support programmes for migrant and diaspora investments and entrepreneurship, including the Award and Grant funding schemes); Action 19j (building partnerships between local authorities, diaspora organizations and migrant associations to promote knowledge and skills transfer between countries of origin and destination). The excerpts suggest that the evaluation systematically assesses the Programme's effectiveness, efficiency, impact and sustainability in enabling diaspora associations to design and implement co-development projects both in Italy and in countries of origin. The analysis of causal mechanisms and domains of change (individual, institutional, systemic) appears particularly valuable for a synthesis on how diaspora engagement in development can be structured and supported. Key sections for human reviewers to prioritize would include the Effectiveness section (on Award and Grant components), the Impact section (especially the contribution analysis and theory of change discussion), and the Sustainability section (on durable results and scalability). The Recommendations section also appears highly relevant, particularly on maintaining the Training-Award-Grant pathway and creating EU funding linkages. It is possible the full report contains additional evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: THE TEMPORARY RETURN OF QUALIFIED NATIONALS PROJECT (TRQN) III
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The TRQN III project is explicitly and centrally focused on enabling diaspora communities to contribute to sustainable development in their countries of origin — which is the core theme of GCM Objective 19. The project title, objectives, evaluation questions, findings, conclusions, and recommendations all directly address diaspora engagement for development. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: diaspora motivation and commitment to contribute to countries of origin (action 19c on non-financial contributions); matching diaspora skills with institutional needs in nine target countries (action 19j on knowledge and skills transfer partnerships); the role of diaspora organizations (action 19d on government structures for diaspora engagement); intergenerational differences in diaspora participation; and the conditions under which diaspora contributions are most effective. The conclusion section explicitly states that 'individuals can make a difference through diaspora return' and analyzes what enables or constrains diaspora contributions. The recommendations address IOM, MoFA, host institutions, and diaspora communities — all relevant to action 19d and 19i on enabling diaspora professional engagement. For a synthesis specialist, the findings sections on capacity change and skills transfer, the case studies of Ghana, Georgia and Afghanistan, the conclusions on fragile vs. stable country contexts, and the recommendations on diaspora self-funding and project creation (action 19j) would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal, particularly in the 26 formal findings.

References.Title: FINAL EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "GHANA INTEGRATED MIGRATION MANAGEMENT APPROACH" (GIMMA)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to have substantive relevance for GCM Objective 1. Component 3 of the GIMMA project is explicitly dedicated to strengthening Ghana's migration data management system, including developing a national migration database, establishing a data management framework, and making migration data available to policymakers. The excerpts indicate that this component received dedicated analysis, including criticism about the labour-intensive nature of the database and doubts about the value of the information it would generate. The executive summary and project objectives sections directly address actions related to GCM 1j (country-specific migration profiles/National Migration Profile is mentioned), 1c (building national capacities in data collection), and 1i (collaboration between migration data units and statistical offices). The conclusions also note that the data management component was dependent on GIS capacity to finalize data entry. Synthesis specialists working on evidence about national migration data system development in sub-Saharan Africa would likely find the sections on Component 3 (SO3 and its Expected Results) and the analysis/conclusions sections particularly worth reviewing, noting that the report appears to offer lessons on implementation challenges rather than success stories in this domain.

References.Title: FINAL EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "GHANA INTEGRATED MIGRATION MANAGEMENT APPROACH" (GIMMA)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to have meaningful relevance for GCM Objective 3. Component 2 of GIMMA explicitly addresses making information resources on safe and legal migration accessible to potential migrants, returnees and migration-affected communities. The Migration Information Centres (MIC) and Migration Information Bureaus (MIB) are central project elements, and the excerpts note that the MIC reached over 62,670 people from 73 communities through media outreach (radio and TV). This directly relates to GCM 3e (multilingual, gender-responsive information campaigns and awareness-raising) and 3c (accessible information points along migration routes). The Legal Handbook development also relates to information provision for GIS officers. The executive summary and conclusions sections highlight these as among the most valued interventions. Synthesis specialists on information provision to migrants and communities would likely find the sections on Component 2 outcomes, the MIC/MIB operational model, and the outreach methodology particularly worth reviewing as a case study of community-level information dissemination in West Africa.

References.Title: FINAL EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "GHANA INTEGRATED MIGRATION MANAGEMENT APPROACH" (GIMMA)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to have strong relevance for GCM Objective 11. Border management is a central theme of the GIMMA project — Component 1 explicitly addresses strengthening GIS institutional capacity for migration management, including the construction and equipping of five border posts (prefabricated border posts with equipment and materials), which is highlighted as one of the most valued interventions. The Legal Handbook on domestic, regional and international legal instruments governing migration also relates to GCM 11 (ensuring border officials operate in accordance with international law). The training of GIS officers and the Immigration Service Academy and Training School (ISATS) capacity building relate to GCM 11a (capacity-building measures for border management) and 11b (effective integrated border management structures). The conclusions discuss the whole-of-government approach needed for integrated migration management. Synthesis specialists on border management capacity development in West Africa would likely find the sections on Component 1 outcomes, the border post construction and equipping, the Legal Handbook development process (including the Training of Trainers model), and the lessons on institutional capacity development particularly worth reviewing. The analysis section on capacity development (inputs, human resources, institutional framework) appears especially relevant.

References.Title: MIDTERM EVALUATION OF THE CORPORATE RESPONSIBILITY IN ELIMINATING SLAVERY AND TRAFFICKING IN ASIA (CREST) PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 6. Ethical recruitment is one of the most explicit and central themes of the CREST initiative — it appears in the project title, stated outcomes, and throughout the evaluation's findings and conclusions. The excerpts suggest that multiple findings and conclusions directly address recruitment fee elimination, supply chain transparency, human rights due diligence, and decent work conditions — all core themes under Actions 6c, 6d, 6e, 6f, 6l, and potentially 6i. The report appears to provide substantive evidence on: (1) how private sector companies can be engaged to implement ethical recruitment standards (Actions 6c, 6e); (2) the role of the IOM International Recruitment Integrity System (IRIS) in promoting ethical recruitment (Action 6l); (3) supply chain transparency and due diligence mechanisms (Action 6e); (4) remediation processes for labour rights violations (Action 6j); and (5) the challenges of enforcing standards through non-disclosure agreements. The evaluation's conclusions and recommendations sections appear particularly rich for synthesis purposes — especially Conclusions 1, 2, and 4, and Recommendations 1-3, which address scope of engagement with migrant workers, strategic partnerships, and the empowerment of rights holders. Sections on effectiveness (Outcome 2 on ethical recruitment implementation) and lessons learned (particularly lessons 1, 3, and 4 on partnership dynamics and NDA constraints) would likely be high-priority starting points for human reviewers. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: MIDTERM EVALUATION OF THE CORPORATE RESPONSIBILITY IN ELIMINATING SLAVERY AND TRAFFICKING IN ASIA (CREST) PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7, particularly regarding workplace-related vulnerabilities of migrant workers. The CREST initiative explicitly targets migrant worker exploitation in supply chains, and the excerpts suggest substantive analysis of vulnerability factors including debt bondage, restricted movement, document retention, and lack of grievance mechanisms — touching on Actions 7b and 7d. The report also discusses gender dimensions of vulnerability (Action 7c) through references to CREST's Gender Strategy and the needs of women migrant workers. Conclusion 4 on direct engagement with migrant workers and Recommendation 3 on empowerment of migrant workers appear particularly relevant, as they address the HRBA requirement to empower rights holders — a core principle of this objective. However, vulnerability is addressed primarily through the lens of private sector accountability rather than as a standalone protection theme, and the report does not appear to engage with the full range of vulnerability situations covered by Objective 7 (e.g., unaccompanied children, migrants in detention, crisis situations). Human reviewers would likely want to focus on the effectiveness sections (Outcomes 1 and 2), Conclusion 4, and Recommendation 3 as starting points, while noting that vulnerability is one of several themes rather than the primary analytical focus.

References.Title: EX-POST INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "PILOTING TEMPORARY LABOUR MIGRATION OF GEORGIAN WORKERS TO POLAND AND ESTONIA"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 5. The project's core purpose is explicitly to 'develop operational frameworks for facilitating worker mobility from Georgia to Poland and Estonia' — directly addressing the creation of regular migration pathways. The excerpts suggest substantive coverage of bilateral labour mobility arrangements (Actions 5a, 5b), temporary and circular migration schemes (Action 5d), and skills matching (Action 5e). The project tested a temporary labour migration pilot scheme, developed due diligence protocols for employers, and engaged with the EU-Georgia Mobility Partnership and Visa Liberalization Action Plan. Conclusions address the operational model tested, lessons learned, and the framework created for worker mobility. The sections on Outcome 1 (regulatory frameworks), Outcome 3 (operational model testing), and the recommendations on exit strategy and government ownership would appear particularly valuable for human reviewers. The report also touches on the Estonia pilot not being tested due to external factors, which offers evidence on implementation challenges for regular pathway development.

References.Title: EX-POST INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "PILOTING TEMPORARY LABOUR MIGRATION OF GEORGIAN WORKERS TO POLAND AND ESTONIA"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 6. Ethical recruitment is explicitly embedded in the project's core design: Outcome 2 states that labour migration management structures should be capacitated 'in adherence to ethical recruitment standards and practices,' and Outcome 3 describes testing 'a labour migration operational model on the basis of ethical recruitment standards.' The IOM International Recruitment Integrity System (IRIS) is listed among project acronyms, suggesting direct engagement with Action 6l. Output 2.1 explicitly addresses 'regulatory mechanisms and management of temporary labour migration in compliance with the ethical recruitment principles.' The project involved private employment agencies (APrEA), developed due diligence protocols for employers, and addressed conflict of interest with private recruiters. Recommendations address ensuring agencies are aware of roles to avoid conflict of interest, and future programming recommendations include ensuring appropriate employer information before departure to avoid TIP cases. Actions 6c (regulation of recruitment agencies), 6d (written contracts and worker awareness), 6f (enforcement of fair recruitment norms), and 6l (IRIS) appear most directly addressed. The conclusions on effectiveness, the recommendations sections, and the outputs related to Outcome 2 would be priority sections for human reviewers conducting a synthesis on this objective.

References.Title: INTERNAL INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “STRENGTHENING MONGOLIA’S EFFORTS TO PREVENT AND RESPOND TO HUMAN TRAFFICKING” FINAL EVALUATION
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The entire project — and therefore the evaluation — is explicitly dedicated to preventing and responding to human trafficking in Mongolia, making this the most directly aligned GCM objective. The report title itself ('Strengthening Mongolia's Efforts to Prevent and Respond to Human Trafficking') signals this alignment. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple associated actions: awareness-raising campaigns targeting migrants and communities (Action 10j), capacity-building for law enforcement and government officials to identify trafficking signs (Action 10i), victim identification and protection mechanisms (Action 10e, 10g, 10h), legislative strengthening (Action 10a, 10g), coordination between government and civil society (Action 10c), and baseline research on trafficking dynamics (Action 10d). The evaluation covers all three project outcomes related to coordination, victim identification/protection, and reintegration. Key sections for human reviewers to prioritize would include: the effectiveness analysis of Outcomes 1-3 (covering identification, protection, and reintegration), the conclusions on referral mechanisms and standardized processes, the cross-cutting gender and human rights sections, and all eight recommendations — particularly those on legislation, training integration, and NGO engagement. The full report likely contains substantially more evidence on specific anti-trafficking interventions than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: INTERNAL INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “STRENGTHENING MONGOLIA’S EFFORTS TO PREVENT AND RESPOND TO HUMAN TRAFFICKING” FINAL EVALUATION
References.Abstract: The excerpts suggest meaningful relevance for GCM Objective 12, particularly regarding referral mechanisms and screening for trafficking victims. The report extensively discusses challenges in victim identification and referral — noting that 'Mongolia has not developed a formal mechanism for assistance and protection for trafficked persons to date,' that 'victim identification and referrals were not sufficiently systematic,' and that 'challenges were encountered in translating knowledge and skills acquired during training into consistent victim identification and protection by LEA due to the absence of clear standardized processes.' These themes align closely with Actions 12b (specialized training for first responders on victim identification and referral) and 12c (gender-responsive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures). The recommendation to 'support the government of Mongolia to build or improve current legislation and processes for clear guidelines and well-defined responsibilities' also directly addresses Action 12c. A synthesis specialist would likely find the effectiveness analysis of Outcome 1 and 2, the conclusions on referral mechanism gaps, and the recommendations on standardized processes particularly valuable for a synthesis on screening and referral systems.

References.Title: INTERNAL INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “STRENGTHENING MONGOLIA’S EFFORTS TO PREVENT AND RESPOND TO HUMAN TRAFFICKING” FINAL EVALUATION
References.Abstract: The excerpts suggest meaningful relevance for GCM Objective 21, particularly regarding reintegration of trafficking victims. Outcome 3 of the project explicitly aimed for 'VoTs who return home are economically self-sufficient, psychosocially well, and socially integrated into their communities,' directly aligning with Action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through social protection, psycho-social assistance, vocational training, and employment). The report discusses challenges in reintegration — noting that 'targets under Outcome 3 were hardly reached' due to COVID-19 and 'lack of clear referral processes with defined stakeholder responsibilities for providing reintegration assistance.' The project also provided 'return assistance' and worked on 'improved collaboration with service providers.' The recommendation to complement awareness-raising with livelihood interventions also relates to Action 21h. A synthesis specialist would likely find the analysis of reintegration challenges (Outcome 3 effectiveness section), the discussion of referral process gaps, and the conclusions on coordination between NGOs and government for reintegration assistance particularly relevant for a synthesis on sustainable reintegration programming.

References.Title: INTERNAL FINAL EVALUATION REPORT OF THE PROJECT “STRENGTHENING PROTECTION FOR VULNERABLE POPULATIONS AND IMPROVING ACCESS TO PSYCHOSOCIAL SUPPORT TO DISPLACED CHILDREN IN EMERGENCY SETTINGS IN MOZAMBIQUE”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7, particularly regarding the protection of unaccompanied and separated children (Action 7f), child-sensitive psychosocial support (Action 7c), and referral mechanisms for vulnerable populations (Actions 7b and 7k). The project explicitly targets displaced children, victims of GBV, human trafficking, and unaccompanied migrant children — all groups central to Objective 7. The evaluation appears to assess the effectiveness of referral pathways, community-based protection structures (CCPs, CLGRCs, DRGs), and psychosocial support delivery — all directly relevant to Actions 7b, 7c, 7e, 7f, and 7k. The executive summary and conclusions sections discuss behavioral change in children and parents, capacity building of protection actors, and the functioning of referral pathways. The recommendations on including parents/caregivers in PSS activities and strengthening community-based approaches are particularly relevant to Action 7c and 7k. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the Findings sections (3.1 Effectiveness, 3.3 Impact, 3.4 Sustainability) and the Recommendations section for evidence on how community-based child protection and referral systems function in emergency settings. Note that the full report may contain additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Evaluación del proyecto UNJP/GUA/035/PBF Construir la cohesión social de las comunidades que reciben jóvenes retornados como un puente hacia una reintegración pacífica y efectiva
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. The project's explicit title — 'Construir la cohesión social de las comunidades que reciben jóvenes retornados como un puente hacia una reintegración pacífica y efectiva' — directly mirrors the language of this objective. Social cohesion and inclusion of returned migrants are the central subjects of the evaluation. The excerpts suggest that multiple findings and conclusions directly address: youth empowerment and agency (actions 16a, 16f, 16g), intercultural coexistence and community integration (action 16a, 16h), participation in community decision-making (action 16f), and gender inclusion in community activities (action 16e). The Centros de Convivencia Juvenil component — involving arts, psychosocial support, and community coexistence — appears particularly aligned with actions 16f and 16h. The evaluation's findings on youth empowerment as the primary catalytic result, the community-based monitoring strategy, and the analysis of what enabled or hindered social cohesion would likely be valuable for a synthesis specialist. Sections most worth prioritizing for human review appear to include: the findings on Resultado 3 (Centros de Convivencia Juvenil), the conclusions on catalytic effects and innovation (Conclusión 7), and the findings on youth empowerment (Hallazgo 12, 21, 22). The full report likely contains additional relevant evidence on integration processes and community reception of returned migrants beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Evaluación del proyecto UNJP/GUA/035/PBF Construir la cohesión social de las comunidades que reciben jóvenes retornados como un puente hacia una reintegración pacífica y efectiva
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be highly valuable for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly its associated actions on sustainable reintegration. The project is explicitly designed to support the reintegration of returned youth migrants into their communities of origin in Guatemala, making reintegration the central operational focus. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: reintegration outcomes and challenges (actions 21b, 21h), community conditions for sustainable reintegration (action 21i), psychosocial assistance for returning migrants (action 21h), livelihood and entrepreneurship support upon return (action 21h), and the role of local institutions in supporting reintegration (action 21i). The evaluation's findings on what worked and what did not in reintegration — including the gap between expectations and outcomes for entrepreneurship, the importance of psychosocial support, and the sustainability challenges — would likely provide valuable evidence for a synthesis on reintegration effectiveness. Particularly relevant sections for human reviewers appear to include: the findings on Resultado 2 (economic and social spaces for reintegration), the conclusions on sustainability (Conclusión 5), the findings on youth empowerment as the primary reintegration outcome (Hallazgo 12), and the recommendations on community-based sustainability strategies (Recomendación 6). The evaluation also provides evidence on the theory of change for reintegration programming — specifically the finding that returned migrants were not a source of conflict, which has implications for how reintegration programs are designed. It is likely that the full report contains additional evidence on reintegration processes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION REPORT PROJECT "PREVENTING UNSAFE MIGRATION FROM ALBANIA TOWARDS EUROPEAN UNION MEMBER STATES" CAMPAIGN "CHOOSE OPPORTUNITIES: NOT IRREGULAR MIGRATION!"
