References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 3. The entire evaluated project is an information and awareness-raising campaign targeting potential migrants, unaccompanied minors, families, and communities across all 12 regions of Albania. The campaign's core purpose aligns directly with GCM Objective 3 Action 3e — promoting multilingual, gender-responsive, evidence-based information campaigns and awareness-raising events in countries of origin to promote safe, orderly and regular migration and highlight risks of irregular migration. The report appears to provide substantive evidence on: the design and effectiveness of information campaigns (executive summary, strengths/weaknesses section), the use of interactive information sessions and leaflets as communication tools, the acquisition of knowledge by target groups on asylum rules and visa liberalization, and the multiplier effects of information dissemination through institutional channels. The evaluation methodology (questionnaires, focus groups, in-depth interviews) generates evidence on what information resonates and what gaps remain. The strengths and weaknesses section (Section 7) and conclusions (Section 8) would be particularly valuable for synthesis specialists, as they contain direct assessments of campaign design quality, communication tool effectiveness, and recommendations for future campaigns. The project title itself centers on preventing unsafe migration through information — making this a core rather than peripheral contribution to Objective 3 evidence.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION REPORT PROJECT "PREVENTING UNSAFE MIGRATION FROM ALBANIA TOWARDS EUROPEAN UNION MEMBER STATES" CAMPAIGN "CHOOSE OPPORTUNITIES: NOT IRREGULAR MIGRATION!"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 5, particularly regarding visa liberalization and regular migration pathways. The campaign explicitly included a pillar on 'information on regular migration programmes especially for youth' and 'visa liberalization rules in EU countries.' The evaluation assesses how well these messages were absorbed by target populations. The conclusions section recommends creating new programmes of regular migration beneficial for both migrants and states, including dedicated programmes for young people studying abroad, and notes that administrative procedures for regular migration to EU countries are laborious. The reference to German legislation facilitating regular migration for Albanian citizens (footnote 10) and recommendations for bilateral agreements and administrative facilitations touch on GCM Objective 5 actions 5b (visa liberalization) and 5d (flexible labour mobility schemes). The sections on 'Promote regular ways of migration' in the conclusions and the background discussion of visa liberalization regime would be most relevant for synthesis specialists to review, though regular migration pathways are one of several campaign themes rather than the sole focus.

References.Title: Evaluación Intermedia del proyecto “Asistencia en la reintegración económica a jóvenes migrantes potenciales y retornados en Guatemala”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly around actions 2b and 2e. The project evaluated explicitly aims to reduce irregular migration by addressing structural drivers — poverty, lack of education, unemployment — through vocational training, scholarships, and economic reintegration support for youth in high-emigration municipalities. The executive summary describes the project's goal as improving 'human security of young potential migrants and returned migrants... to reduce irregular migration and prevent re-emigration,' directly engaging with the logic of addressing root causes. The contextual analysis of the three municipalities (Joyabaj, Malacatancito, Santa María Visitación) discusses poverty, low educational attainment, and limited economic opportunity as migration drivers, which aligns with action 2e on human capital development and youth unemployment reduction. The report also touches on climate vulnerability and natural disasters as migration drivers (action 2i). However, the report's primary focus is on project implementation quality (coherence, efficiency, sustainability) rather than a deep causal analysis of structural drivers per se. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the contextual sections on migration drivers and the efficacy criterion findings on whether training/scholarship interventions reduce migration intent, as well as the sustainability conclusions. It is possible the full report contains more analytical depth on structural drivers than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Evaluación Intermedia del proyecto “Asistencia en la reintegración económica a jóvenes migrantes potenciales y retornados en Guatemala”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly around actions 21b (gender-responsive return and reintegration programmes) and 21h (sustainable reintegration through vocational training, employment, and social services). The evaluated project is explicitly and centrally focused on economic reintegration of returned migrants in Guatemala, making this a core subject of the evaluation. The project title itself — 'Asistencia en la reintegración económica a jóvenes migrantes potenciales y retornados en Guatemala' — directly signals this alignment. The executive summary describes support to reception centers (Centros de Recepción de Migrantes Retornados) in Guatemala City and Tecún Umán, provision of KITs to returnees, and vocational training and scholarships for returned youth. The coherence criterion findings assess how the project strengthens institutional capacity for dignified return (action 21h). The pertinence criterion discusses whether returnees are adequately covered and how to improve their inclusion in training programs. The efficacy criterion addresses whether returnees are being reached as intended beneficiaries. The sustainability criterion examines whether reintegration benefits will persist. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on coherence with the Instituto Guatemalteco de Migración, the pertinence findings on returnee coverage gaps, the efficacy recommendations on expanding returnee enrollment, and the sustainability conclusions on institutional capacity would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The report also provides contextual data on returnee volumes (94,216 in 2022) and municipal-level return statistics. It is possible the full report contains additional evidence on reintegration program design and outcomes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Access to Digital identity for People on the Move in Europe
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would almost certainly be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 4. The entire report is explicitly organized around ensuring migrants have proof of legal identity and adequate documentation — this is the primary subject of the evaluation. The report title itself ('Access to Digital Identity for People on the Move in Europe') directly maps onto Objective 4's core commitment. The excerpts suggest extensive analysis of: civil registry systems and birth registration for migrant children (Actions 4a, 4e — addressed in Recommendations 6 and the birth registration section); consular documentation and countries of origin's role in issuing travel documents (Action 4c — addressed in Recommendations 3 and 5); access to personal documentation and barriers faced by migrants (Action 4d — addressed throughout); statelessness prevention (Action 4e — explicitly discussed in the birth registration section); and ensuring migrants without proof of identity can access services (Action 4f — addressed in Recommendations 7 and 11). The analysis covers the full spectrum from foundational legal identity to digital credentials (eID), with dedicated sections on registration challenges, credential issuance, and access to services. Recommendations 1–13 all relate directly to this objective. For a synthesis specialist, the Analysis section (Strengths and Limitations), the Recommendations on Registration of Migrants, Issuance of Credentials, and the EU Framework sections would be particularly valuable starting points. The full report likely contains substantially more evidence than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Access to Digital identity for People on the Move in Europe
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 14. The report substantively analyzes the role of consular services in providing civil registration and identity documentation to migrants abroad — a core function under Actions 14c, 14e, and 14f. Recommendation 3 explicitly addresses reinforcing consular capacities in host countries, including increasing the footprint of consular services, training consular staff, streamlining registration procedures, and supporting migrants with information about their rights — directly mapping onto Actions 14a, 14c, and 14f. The analysis of barriers migrants face in accessing consular services (limited geographic presence, capacity constraints, political sensitivities for asylum-seekers) provides substantive evidence on consular protection gaps. The discussion of birth registration regularization through consular services (birth registration section) also relates to Action 14f. However, consular protection is analyzed primarily through the lens of identity documentation rather than as a comprehensive consular assistance framework. The sections on cooperation with countries of origin and Recommendation 3 would be particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist to review, with the caveat that consular protection is one of several themes rather than the central focus.

References.Title: Access to Digital identity for People on the Move in Europe
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The report dedicates substantial analysis to migrants' access to services — both online and in-person — and the role of identity documentation as an enabler or barrier to service access. The 'Access to Services' section in the limitations analysis directly addresses how identification-related barriers, legal status conditions, and other obstacles prevent migrants from accessing services (relating to Actions 15a, 15b, and 15c). Recommendation 10 explicitly addresses delinking basic service provision from residence status for irregular migrants (Action 15b), developing digital literacy as part of integration (Action 15f), and creating eServices centers (Action 15c). The analysis of irregular migrants' unique barriers — fear of authorities, discriminatory practices, stringent administrative requirements — provides substantive evidence on service access challenges (Actions 15a–15d). The discussion of the Reception Directive and rights of asylum-seekers to access services also contributes evidence on the legal framework for service provision. However, the report's primary lens is digital identity and eGovernment rather than comprehensive service delivery systems. The 'Access to Services' limitations section and Recommendations 9–10 would be particularly valuable starting points for a synthesis specialist, who should note that health, education, and social protection services are discussed primarily as context rather than as subjects of deep analysis.

References.Title: FINAL REPORT for Third Party Monitoring for Post Intervention  Monitoring EU ICsP Jubbalandv& Hirshabelle
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16, particularly regarding social cohesion in conflict-affected and displacement contexts. Social cohesion is a central outcome measured throughout the report — 55% of households reported increased social cohesion, and the report explicitly states that 'social cohesion, which, in the opinion of the TPM, a more effective indicator to measure for this stabilization programme.' The report analyzes how community infrastructure (streetlights, schools, police posts) contributes to social cohesion, inter-clan relations, and community inclusion — themes closely aligned with actions 16a (mutual respect and social cohesion), 16f (community centres and intercultural dialogue), and 16i (school environments promoting inclusion). The Community Action Plan (CAP) process, described as fostering inclusive community participation and trust-building, also relates to action 16a. The conclusions and recommendations sections appear particularly relevant, as they discuss social cohesion measurement, community engagement processes, and the role of infrastructure in fostering inclusion. Specialists should note that the population includes IDPs and conflict-affected communities rather than international migrants in a traditional destination-country sense, which may limit direct applicability but remains relevant for displacement-context social cohesion evidence.

References.Title: POSITIVE ALTERNATIVES FOR EGYPTIAN YOUTH AT-RISK OF IRREGULAR MIGRATION (PLAYA)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Trafficking in persons (TIP) prevention, protection, and prosecution is the explicit and central subject of the PLAYA project evaluation. The project title, all three components (prevention, protection, prosecution), and the majority of outputs directly address TIP. Actions 10e (measures addressing vulnerabilities of TIP victims, access to justice), 10g (strengthening legislation and prosecution of traffickers), 10h (protection and assistance to TIP victims), 10i (training programmes to identify TIP signs), and 10j (awareness-raising campaigns on TIP risks) all appear substantially covered. Specifically: Output 1.6 addresses awareness-raising on TIP risks (Action 10j); Output 2.2-2.4 address NRM operationalization and protection services for VoTs (Actions 10e, 10h); Output 3.1 addresses prosecution capacity building for judges and prosecutors (Action 10g); the legislative framework review on domestic workers (Output 2.1) relates to Action 10e. The evaluation's effectiveness section (6.3), impact section (6.4), and sustainability section (6.5) would likely contain the most directly relevant evidence for a synthesis specialist. The recommendations on NGO capacity building, multi-stakeholder approaches, and NRM strengthening also appear highly pertinent. This report appears to be a core source for any evidence synthesis on GCM Objective 10.

References.Title: SUPPORTING THE GOVERNMENT OF GHANA IN MAINSTREAMING MIGRATION IN THE NATIONAL CENSUS
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project's explicit purpose — supporting the Government of Ghana in mainstreaming migration in the national census — directly corresponds to Action 1g ('Improve national data collection by integrating migration-related topics in national censuses'). The project title itself signals this alignment. The excerpts suggest that multiple conclusions and recommendations directly address data collection, disaggregation, analysis, and dissemination of migration statistics — all core themes under Objective 1. Specifically, the report appears to cover: (1) integration of migration-specific questions in the census questionnaire (Action 1g); (2) production of a Migration Thematic Report with sex-disaggregated data (Actions 1a, 1d); (3) strengthening inter-institutional coordination for data sharing via the Migration Desk Officers Platform and MoU (Actions 1a, 1i); (4) capacity-building for data collection and analysis (Action 1c); (5) use of migration profiles for evidence-based policymaking (Action 1j); and (6) collaboration with academia (MoU with Centre of Migration Studies, Action 1k). The executive summary, conclusions (C1–C9), and recommendations (R1–R4) all appear to substantively engage with data collection, management, dissemination, and use. The sections on sustainability and impact also directly address whether data collected is being used for policy purposes. For a synthesis specialist, the conclusions on the Migration Thematic Report, the Migration Desk Officers Platform, and the recommendations on data access and institutionalization would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review.

References.Title: PROJECT: EXTERNAL REVIEW OF «MAINSTREAMING THE CONCEPT OF MIGRATION AND DEVELOPMENT INTO STRATEGIES, POLICIES AND ACTIONS IN BIH: DIASPORA FOR DEVELOPMENT (D4D) PHASE 2 »
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The entire D4D project — and thus the entire evaluation — is explicitly and centrally focused on enabling diaspora contributions to sustainable development in BiH, which is the core theme of GCM Objective 19. The project title, evaluation questions, and all three components directly address diaspora engagement for development. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple associated actions: Action 19b (integrating migration into development planning — the project's mainstreaming goal), Action 19c (research on non-financial contributions of diaspora — ToK, knowledge transfer), Action 19d (government structures for diaspora engagement — MHRR, diaspora strategies, focal points), Action 19e (targeted support programmes and financial products for diaspora investment — the investment scheme, D4startups), Action 19h (policies optimizing diaspora benefits — strategy development at state/entity/LG levels), Action 19i (enabling diaspora to carry out professional activities in home countries — ToK, IMP), and Action 19j (partnerships between local authorities and diaspora organizations — LG component). The evaluation covers effectiveness, sustainability, impact, and recommendations for scaling up diaspora engagement mechanisms. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on the investment scheme, ToK mechanisms, LG diaspora strategies, and sustainability findings would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Final Evaluation Report External Evaluation of the WBAWARE Information Campaign
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 3. The entire evaluation appears to center on an information campaign ('Migrants talk to migrants') explicitly designed to provide accurate, timely, and accessible information to migrants at various stages of migration — directly aligning with the objective's core theme and several associated actions. The campaign targeted transit migrants in the Western Balkans and potential migrants in countries of origin, using multilingual video content, social media platforms, and in-person information sessions — closely corresponding to Actions 3c (information points along migration routes), 3d (targeted information for newly arrived migrants), and 3e (multilingual, evidence-based information campaigns in countries of origin). The executive summary and recommendations sections appear particularly rich with evidence on: campaign design and content strategy (multilingual, peer-to-peer messaging); channel effectiveness (YouTube, Facebook, in-person sessions); audience targeting and segmentation; and the balance between risk messaging and information on legal alternatives including AVRR. The evaluation's discussion of what worked and what did not in reaching migrants with relevant information — including the critique of messaging on AVRR and the recommendation to tailor content to subgroups — would likely add substantial value to a synthesis on how information campaigns can effectively reach migrants. Human reviewers should prioritize the Key Findings section, the campaign description, and Recommendations 1, 3, 4, 5, 6, and 8, which appear most directly relevant to Actions 3c, 3d, and 3e.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE “GOVERNANCE OF LABOUR MIGRATION SOUTH AND SOUTH-EAST ASIA” (GOALS)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The GOALS programme explicitly included activities to improve gender-disaggregated data on migration, with Outcome 3 dedicated to strengthening the evidence base on labour migration. The excerpts indicate substantive activities including assessments of gender data on migration in Bangladesh and Nepal, training for national statistics offices, technical meetings on international labour migration statistics, and the establishment of a regional Knowledge Hub. The evaluation appears to assess progress on these data-related activities and notes that 'data gaps are still notable' despite improvements. This relates particularly to Actions 1c (building national capacities in data collection), 1h (household and labour force surveys), and 1j (country-specific migration profiles). The sections on Outcome 3 effectiveness and the conclusions noting evidence base improvements would appear most relevant for human reviewers to prioritize. The report's focus on gender-disaggregated data specifically adds value for synthesis specialists interested in the gender dimension of Action 1a. However, data governance is one of three outcome areas, not the dominant theme, which limits the score.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE “GOVERNANCE OF LABOUR MIGRATION SOUTH AND SOUTH-EAST ASIA” (GOALS)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 6. Fair and ethical recruitment is explicitly one of the three thematic dimensions of Outcome 2, with dedicated activities including policy dialogues on regularisation of sub-agents' roles in Pakistan, Nepal and Bangladesh; adaptation of UN Women's toolkit for gender-responsive employment and recruitment; and development of a framework for prevention of gender-based violence and harassment against women migrant workers. The evaluation assesses progress on these activities and notes 'increased capacity of CPMS regarding fair and ethical recruitment.' This relates to Actions 6c (regulation of recruitment agencies), 6d (written contracts and worker awareness), 6k (specific needs of women migrant workers), and 6l (national policies on labour mobility). The sections on Outcome 2 effectiveness (fair and ethical recruitment dimension), the gender mainstreaming case study, and the recommendations on societal perceptions of women migrants would appear most relevant for human reviewers. The report's tri-agency approach (IOM, ILO, UN Women) likely brings a holistic perspective on recruitment governance that would add value to a synthesis. However, recruitment is one of three outcome areas, and the excerpts suggest analysis may be less deep than for reintegration.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE “GOVERNANCE OF LABOUR MIGRATION SOUTH AND SOUTH-EAST ASIA” (GOALS)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 18. Skills development and qualifications recognition is explicitly one of the three thematic dimensions of Outcome 2, with the South Asia Qualifications Referencing Framework (SAQRF) as a major programme output. A dedicated case study examines 'strategies employed to garner support from diverse South Asian governments for the Qualifications Referencing Framework.' The evaluation assesses the SAQRF's development and national implementation prospects, noting that 'the entire region can now benefit from the South Asia Qualifications Referencing Framework developed.' This relates directly to Actions 18a (standards for mutual recognition), 18b (compatibility of National Qualifications Frameworks), and 18c (bilateral/regional mutual recognition agreements). The SAQRF case study (Annex), the Outcome 2 effectiveness sections on skills recognition, and the sustainability findings regarding the framework would appear most valuable for human reviewers. The programme also mentions a skills passport in Pakistan, touching on Action 18l (documentation tools for credentials). The tri-agency approach and regional focus provide a distinctive perspective on multilateral qualifications recognition processes in South Asia that would likely add value to a synthesis.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE “GOVERNANCE OF LABOUR MIGRATION SOUTH AND SOUTH-EAST ASIA” (GOALS)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly on sustainable reintegration. Reintegration is described as 'the most pressing issue' for CP countries and receives the most prominent treatment among the programme's thematic areas. The programme developed and piloted gender-responsive reintegration models in India and Sri Lanka, mapped existing reintegration services across selected South Asian Member States, and developed regional reintegration frameworks and guidelines. A dedicated case study examines 'gender-responsive reintegration models in Sri Lanka and India.' The evaluation assesses ownership, sustainability, and effectiveness of these reintegration efforts, noting that 'reintegration pilots were successful, and ownership was expressed to maintain them.' This relates directly to Actions 21b (gender-responsive return and reintegration programmes), 21h (sustainable reintegration through social protection, vocational training, and decent work), and 21i (addressing needs of communities to which migrants return). The case study on reintegration models, the Outcome 2 effectiveness sections on reintegration, the sustainability findings, and the conclusions on ownership would appear most valuable for human reviewers. The programme's explicit focus on women returnees' vulnerabilities and the tri-agency approach (bringing gender, labour, and migration expertise) would likely provide distinctive evidence for a synthesis on gender-responsive reintegration in South Asia. It is possible the full report contains additional depth on reintegration beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN INTERNA EX-POST DEL PROYECTO: FORTALECER LAS POLÍTICAS PÚBLICAS PARA PROTEGER Y EMPODERAR A LAS MUJERES MIGRANTES EN MESOAMÉRICA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to have meaningful relevance for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project CT.1082 explicitly produced a 'diagnóstico legislativo y de políticas públicas sobre mujeres migrantes en la región' (legislative and public policy diagnostic on migrant women in the region), which the excerpts describe as generating evidence on the state of public policies and migration instruments directed at migrant women. The excerpts indicate this diagnostic was used as a reference for resource mobilization and identified a scarcity of public policies or migration instruments directed at migrant women — directly relevant to Actions 1d (data on effects and contributions of migrants) and 1j (country-specific migration profiles). The report also discusses how evidence generation was one of three key pillars of the project ('generación de evidencias, desarrollo de políticas y espacios de abogacía'). The sustainability section notes the diagnostic is being updated, suggesting ongoing relevance. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on the diagnostic product, its methodology, and its use as evidence for policy development would appear most pertinent. The report likely contributes evidence on how sex-disaggregated, gender-focused data collection can inform migration policy at the regional level, particularly Action 1j. However, the excerpts suggest data collection is instrumental to the broader policy development goal rather than the primary focus, which limits the score somewhat.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN INTERNA EX-POST DEL PROYECTO: FORTALECER LAS POLÍTICAS PÚBLICAS PARA PROTEGER Y EMPODERAR A LAS MUJERES MIGRANTES EN MESOAMÉRICA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project CT.1082 is explicitly focused on protecting and empowering migrant women — a group specifically identified under Action 7b and 7c. The excerpts indicate the project produced 'Lineamientos' (guidelines) covering each stage of the migration cycle with recommendations on 'atención y protección' (care and protection), directly relevant to Actions 7b and 7c on gender-responsive migration policies addressing particular needs and vulnerabilities of migrant women. The project also involved multi-sectoral dialogue including security, migration, and gender officials, suggesting engagement with referral mechanisms (Action 7k). The diagnostic on legislation and public policies for migrant women in the region appears to assess the policy landscape for addressing vulnerabilities. The report's conclusions note the project's innovation in creating evidence and guidance where none previously existed for migrant women's protection. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on the Lineamientos content and methodology, the diagnostic results on policy gaps, and the conclusions on impact regarding sensitization of officials would appear most relevant. The report likely contributes evidence on how regional policy frameworks can be developed to address gender-specific vulnerabilities in migration, particularly Actions 7a, 7b, and 7c. The full report may contain more detail on specific vulnerability categories and protection mechanisms than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EX-POST INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "INCREASING CAPACITIES FOR ADDRESSING IRREGULAR MIGRATION IN SERBIA"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The project is explicitly framed as an immigration and border management (IBM) initiative, with the Border Police Directorate (BPD) as a primary beneficiary. The Humanitarian Border Management (HBM) training curriculum and HBM Handbook are central outputs, designed to build capacities of border police to handle mixed migration flows in a rights-compliant manner. The project supported development of protection indicators and SOPs for border officials, a study visit for border police, and contributed to a national strategy on countering irregular migration. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of border management capacity building, including training effectiveness, coordination between national and local border authorities, and the challenge of managing large-scale irregular migration flows. Actions 11a (international border management cooperation, identification and referral of vulnerable migrants), 11b (integrated border management structures), and 11g (cross-border collaboration on treatment of persons crossing borders) appear most directly related. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the effectiveness section on Outcome 1 (particularly outputs 1.2, 1.3, 1.5), the impact section on institutional capacity building, the sustainability section on embedding HBM training in institutional structures, and the lessons learned on coordination between national and local levels. The report appears to offer practical evidence on what works in building border management capacity in a crisis context.

References.Title: EX-POST INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "INCREASING CAPACITIES FOR ADDRESSING IRREGULAR MIGRATION IN SERBIA"
References.Abstract: The excerpts suggest relevant content for GCM Objective 12, particularly around screening, assessment, and referral mechanisms for irregular migrants. The project developed protection indicators and standard operating procedures (Output 1.2) — directly relevant to Action 12c (gender-responsive and child-sensitive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures). The HBM training for border police and centre staff addresses Action 12b (specialized human rights training for first responders and government officials). The assessment of the Centre at Padinska Skela examined readiness to handle increased numbers of migrants and specific needs of vulnerable groups, touching on individual assessment processes. The conclusions note that the project helped build 'knowledge and skills to provide needs- and vulnerability-sensitive services to irregular migrants.' Actions 12b and 12c appear most directly related. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the effectiveness section on Outputs 1.2 and 1.3, the impact section on institutional capacity building, and the sustainability section on embedding SOPs and training in institutional structures. However, the depth of analysis on screening and referral procedures specifically is not entirely clear from the excerpts, and this theme is somewhat intertwined with the broader border management focus.

References.Title: EX-POST INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "INCREASING CAPACITIES FOR ADDRESSING IRREGULAR MIGRATION IN SERBIA"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to be highly relevant for a synthesis on GCM Objective 13. The Reception Centre for Foreigners at Padinska Skela is explicitly described as a 'closed migrant reception centre (immigration detention)' and is a central focus of the project. The evaluation examines the first independent assessment of the Centre's readiness and capacity, using a 'rights-centred approach' aimed at 'minimizing the negative impact of immigration detention on migrants' physical and mental wellbeing.' The human rights section explicitly addresses immigration detention conditions, and a lesson learned specifically addresses 'mainstreaming and fulfilment of migrants' human rights in an immigration detention setting.' The evaluation discusses physical conditions, accommodation, sanitation, and the needs of vulnerable groups (women, men, children) within the detention setting. Actions 13a (independent monitoring of migrant detention), 13c (reviewing legislation and practices related to immigration detention), 13f (reducing negative effects of detention by guaranteeing due process and access to services), and 13g (training authorities on human rights-consistent detention administration) appear most directly related. Synthesis specialists would likely want to prioritize the human rights section, the effectiveness section on Output 1.1 (assessment of the Centre), the gender section on findings from the Centre assessment, and the lesson learned on immigration detention. The recommendations on strategizing to improve immigration detention conditions are also likely valuable. The excerpts suggest this report could offer practical evidence on rights-based assessment of immigration detention facilities and the challenges of improving detention conditions in a crisis context.

References.Title: INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF IOM PROJECT : ENHANCING NATIONAL CAPACITIES AND COOPERATION FOR THE PREVENTION OF TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS (TIP), PROTECTION OF VICTIMS AND PROSECUTION OF TRAFFICKERS IN ETHIOPIA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The entire evaluation is explicitly focused on an IOM anti-trafficking project in Ethiopia, with trafficking in persons appearing in the project title, all evaluation questions, and all findings and recommendations. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis directly addressing multiple associated actions: legislative framework strengthening (Actions 10a, 10f, 10g — new TIP Proclamation); criminal justice capacity building for police and judiciary (Action 10i); victim identification and referral mechanisms (Actions 10e, 10h); awareness raising and community-level prevention (Action 10j); prosecution of traffickers (Action 10g); and coordination mechanisms (Action 10d). The report appears to cover the full 3P framework (prevention, protection, prosecution) with dedicated sections on each. For a synthesis specialist, the findings sections on effectiveness (3.2), impact (3.4), and sustainability (3.5), as well as all four recommendation sections (4.1–4.4), would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The Community Conversations model as an alternative to traditional awareness raising (Finding 9) and the National Referral Mechanism development (Finding 8) would likely be especially relevant for Actions 10e and 10i respectively. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF IOM PROJECT : ENHANCING NATIONAL CAPACITIES AND COOPERATION FOR THE PREVENTION OF TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS (TIP), PROTECTION OF VICTIMS AND PROSECUTION OF TRAFFICKERS IN ETHIOPIA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly regarding sustainable reintegration of returned migrants and trafficking victims. The project included direct assistance to 65 victims of trafficking, and the evaluation explicitly identifies reintegration as a major gap area with dedicated recommendations (Section 4.4, Recommendations 12–14). Finding 11 directly addresses the lack of resources for sustainable reintegration monitoring. The mass deportation of 165,000 Ethiopians from Saudi Arabia and the government's response also provides context relevant to return and readmission (Action 21a, 21b). Recommendation 12 on developing a reintegration strategy with livelihood options and monitoring, and Recommendation 13 on a reintegration manual, appear directly relevant to Action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through social protection, vocational training, employment). However, reintegration appears as one of several themes rather than the central focus, and the analysis is primarily framed around trafficking victims rather than broader return migration. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review Section 3.5 (sustainability), Finding 11, and Section 4.4 (reintegration recommendations) as starting points, while noting that the full report may contain more detailed analysis of reintegration outcomes than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "STRENGTHENING SURVEILLANCE AND BILATERAL COORDINATION CAPACITY ALONG THE COMMON BORDER BETWEEN BELARUS AND UKRAINE (SURCAP PHASE II)"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The project title itself — 'Strengthening Surveillance and Bilateral Coordination Capacity along the Common Border between Belarus and Ukraine' — directly reflects the core theme of integrated, secure and coordinated border management. The excerpts suggest the evaluation explicitly examines: bilateral border management cooperation between two states (Actions 11a, 11g); integrated border management (IBM) principles and frameworks (Action 11b); procurement and deployment of surveillance equipment and technical cooperation (Action 11d); cross-border coordination mechanisms including the Common Contact Point (CCP) (Actions 11a, 11b); border guard training and capacity-building (Action 11a); and standard operating procedures at border crossing points. Multiple evaluation criteria (relevance, effectiveness, efficiency, impact, sustainability) are applied to these border management themes. The good practices section and recommendations sections appear particularly rich with operational insights on bilateral coordination, CCP design, and equipment deployment. For a synthesis specialist, the findings on the CCP establishment and functioning, the bilateral coordination mechanisms, and the recommendations on IBM strategy implementation would appear most valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Independent Evaluation of IOM Project: Integrated Support Programme for Populations  Affected by Displacement in Sudan (DTM Component)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The evaluated project — IOM's Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM) in Sudan — is fundamentally a data collection, analysis, and dissemination system for displaced populations. The report's primary subject is precisely the design, implementation, and impact of a mechanism to collect, process, and share disaggregated data on IDPs, returnees, and refugees. This aligns directly with the core theme of Objective 1 and several associated actions. Actions 1a (comprehensive strategy for improving migration data), 1c (building national capacities in data collection and dissemination), 1e (collaboration between databases), and 1j (country-specific migration profiles) appear particularly well-covered. The excerpts suggest multiple formal findings address data quality, timeliness, dissemination, and use for humanitarian planning — including Finding 1 (DTM as primary data source), Finding 8 (data sharing weaknesses), and Finding 12 (gaps in data management). Recommendations 2, 3, and 4 directly address data standardization, SOPs, and monitoring mechanisms. The report also discusses biometric registration (relevant to 1c), disaggregated data by displacement status and location, and the challenge of data sharing with partners. For a synthesis specialist, the findings sections on data collection methodology, data sharing processes, and the recommendations on DTM strategy and SOPs would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The full report likely contains additional depth on data management practices beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: MID-TERM EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT  CHINA: STRENGTHENING BILATERAL  COUNTER-TRAFFICKING COOPERATION  WITH MONGOLIA AND HONG KONG SAR
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Counter-trafficking is the explicit and sole focus of the evaluated project, appearing prominently in the report title, evaluation questions, and all five project objectives. The excerpts suggest that multiple findings and conclusions directly address actions under GCM Objective 10, including: Action 10c (monitoring irregular migration routes exploited by trafficking networks and strengthening bilateral/regional cooperation on prevention, investigation, prosecution, and victim identification — directly addressed through the China-Mongolia police cooperation framework, joint training, and border area activities); Action 10d (sharing information and intelligence through transnational mechanisms — addressed through the establishment of an informal police exchange platform and high-level bilateral meetings); Action 10e (measures addressing vulnerabilities of trafficking victims, facilitating access to justice — addressed through victim identification guidelines, referral mechanisms, and direct assistance to VOTs); Action 10g (strengthening legislation and procedures to enhance prosecution and protect victims — addressed through MoU operationalization and legal counseling procedures); Action 10h (protection and assistance to trafficking victims, including physical, psychological and social recovery — addressed through MGEC assistance, shelter, vocational training, and reintegration support); and Action 10i (training programmes for public officials and law enforcement — addressed through multiple joint training workshops). For a synthesis specialist, the findings sections on effectiveness (Section 5.4), the discussion of bilateral cooperation mechanisms, victim identification and referral training outcomes, and the recommendations on project design revision would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: MID-TERM EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT  CHINA: STRENGTHENING BILATERAL  COUNTER-TRAFFICKING COOPERATION  WITH MONGOLIA AND HONG KONG SAR
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 11, particularly regarding cross-border cooperation on counter-trafficking. The project explicitly focuses on border area cooperation between China and Mongolia, police-to-police cooperation, and joint training for border officials — themes that align with Actions 11a (international border management cooperation, joint cross-border trainings, capacity-building) and 11g (cross-border collaboration among neighboring states). The joint training for PSB and CAB officials in Inner Mongolia (border region), the high-level bilateral meetings, and the discussion of challenges in cross-border cooperation (cultural differences, language barriers, incompatible legal frameworks) appear substantively relevant. However, border management is addressed primarily through the lens of counter-trafficking rather than as a standalone governance theme, and the report does not examine broader border management systems, screening procedures, or technology. The findings on bilateral cooperation effectiveness (Section 5.4, Objectives 1 and 2) and the conclusions on transnational cooperation challenges (Section 6.1) would likely be the most relevant sections for a synthesis specialist to review, with the caveat that border management is instrumental to the counter-trafficking focus rather than a primary analytical subject.

References.Title: MID-TERM EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT  CHINA: STRENGTHENING BILATERAL  COUNTER-TRAFFICKING COOPERATION  WITH MONGOLIA AND HONG KONG SAR
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute some evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12, particularly regarding victim identification and referral mechanisms. The project includes development and use of 'Guidelines on Victim Identification, Protection and Provision of Assistance to Victims of Trafficking' and multiple training workshops for law enforcement and service providers on victim identification and referral — directly relevant to Action 12b (specialized trainings for first responders and government officials on identification and referral of trafficking victims) and Action 12c (gender-responsive and child-sensitive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures). The pre/post training evaluations showing knowledge gains among PSB and CAB officials, and the training-of-trainers sessions in Mongolia, appear to offer concrete evidence on capacity-building for identification and referral. However, the report's focus is specifically on trafficking victim identification rather than broader migration screening and assessment procedures. The findings on training effectiveness under Objectives 1 and 2 (Section 5.4) and the discussion of referral mechanism development would be the most relevant sections for a synthesis specialist to consult.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN FINAL DEL PROYECTO FORTALECIMIENTO INSTITUCIONAL CON PERSPECTIVA DE GÉNERO PARA GESTIONAR LA MIGRACIÓN IRREGULAR ENTRE COLOMBIA Y VENEZUELA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12, particularly around Actions 12b and 12c (training for government officials on identification and referral, and establishing gender-responsive referral mechanisms). One of the project's two main results is explicitly the development of protocols for identification, attention, orientation, and referral of migrants and returnees with a gender perspective — directly aligned with this objective. The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of training 78 local government officials on these protocols, examines whether the protocols meet international standards, and evaluates gender-responsiveness of referral mechanisms. The recommendations section (Table 8) provides substantive analysis of what worked and what needs improvement in these referral systems. The conclusions note that protocols were developed with gender focus for specific service areas. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on R1 (Protocolos para la identificación, atención y referenciación migrantes) in both the executive summary and Chapter 6 recommendations would appear most relevant, along with the efficacy findings on protocol implementation. The report appears to offer practical evidence on local-level referral mechanism development in a border context, though the excerpts suggest the evaluation was conducted 18 months post-project which may affect the depth of implementation evidence.

References.Title: REGIONAL PROGRAMME AND DIALOGUE ON FACILITATING SAFE AND LEGAL MIGRATION FROM SOUTH ASIA TO THE EUROPEAN UNION - FINAL REPORT - EXTERNAL EVALUATION
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 5. Facilitating legal migration pathways from South Asia to the EU is explicitly the central purpose of the programme, as reflected in the programme title itself. The excerpts discuss bilateral labour mobility arrangements, the challenge of harmonizing EU immigration policies, and the need for frameworks that facilitate regular labour migration — directly relating to actions 5a, 5b, 5c, and 5d. The discussion of EU labour market needs (Denmark's labour shortage, Italy's reliance on South Asian workers) and the barriers to formalizing migration pathways appears substantive. The conclusions and recommendations sections explicitly address strengthening frameworks for facilitated labour migration and labour matching. Synthesis specialists would likely find the sections on inter-regional dialogue outcomes, bilateral cooperation frameworks, and the analysis of EU-South Asia migration pathway challenges particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN FINAL DEL PROYECTO “PROMOCIÓN DE CONTRIBUCIONES POSITIVAS DE LA MIGRACIÓN EN AMÉRICA DEL SUR A TRAVÉS DE LOS MEDIOS DE COMUNICACIÓN Y SU INTERACCIÓN CON OIM”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to be highly relevant — likely essential — for a synthesis on GCM Objective 17, particularly its associated action 17c (promoting independent, objective and quality reporting by media professionals, sensitizing media on migration-related issues and terminology, and investing in ethical reporting standards) and 17f (promoting awareness-raising campaigns on positive contributions of migration based on evidence and facts). The project's explicit purpose is to promote evidence-based, positive narratives about migration through journalism training, awards, and digital campaigns in South America — directly operationalizing these GCM commitments. The evaluation appears to assess the effectiveness of journalism capacity-building (training, mentorship, awards/competitions) in shifting how migration is covered in media, which would provide concrete implementation evidence for Objective 17. Key areas of likely value for a synthesis include: (i) the analysis of journalism training effectiveness in changing reporters' framing of migration (conclusions on efficacy and impact sections); (ii) the assessment of the Premio Suramericano de Periodismo and Concurso de Fotomigración as incentive mechanisms for quality migration reporting; (iii) the formation of the Red Suramericana de MigraPeriodistas as a sustainability mechanism; (iv) the digital awareness campaign's reach and effectiveness; and (v) the internal IOM staff media training as a complementary strategy. The conclusions on impact note that journalists reported changed perceptions and incorporation of rights/diversity framing — directly relevant to 17c. Recommendations on continuing journalism engagement, monitoring media coverage, and expanding campaigns also align with 17f. Human reviewers should prioritize the efficacy and impact analysis sections, as well as the sustainability conclusions, as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional quantitative data on media coverage changes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION A POSTERIORI DU PROJET « VERS L’INTEGRATION DU NEXUS MIGRATION, ENVIRONNEMENT ET CHANGEMENT CLIMATIQUE DANS LES POLITIQUES PUBLIQUES AU NIGER »
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The Nexus MECC project was explicitly designed to address the absence of qualitative and quantitative data on the migration-environment-climate change nexus in Niger, making data collection and evidence generation a core project rationale. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of data gaps, the production of a national study on migration, environment and climate change (disaggregated by gender), and the use of this evidence base to inform public policy. The evaluation appears to assess the quality of data produced, the limitations of the monitoring and results framework (weak indicators, insufficient documentation of effects), and the extent to which evidence was used in policymaking. These themes relate closely to GCM Objective 1 actions, particularly 1a (comprehensive data strategy), 1d (data on migration effects for sustainable development), and 1j (country-specific migration profiles). The executive summary and conclusions sections — particularly the discussion of indicator quality, the national study, and the policy note — appear most relevant for human reviewers. The evaluation's critique of weak results indicators and insufficient data documentation would likely add value to a synthesis examining evidence-based policymaking under Objective 1. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant content on data systems beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION A POSTERIORI DU PROJET « VERS L’INTEGRATION DU NEXUS MIGRATION, ENVIRONNEMENT ET CHANGEMENT CLIMATIQUE DANS LES POLITIQUES PUBLIQUES AU NIGER »
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute some evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its actions related to climate change, environmental degradation, and migration (actions 2h, 2i, 2l). The Nexus MECC project is explicitly centered on the interrelationship between migration, environment, and climate change in Niger — a country facing desertification, land degradation, and drought. The excerpts indicate the project produced a national study on these linkages, supported Niger's Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC/CDN) at COP26, and contributed to raising awareness among key actors about the need to integrate climate change into planning documents. These contributions appear directly relevant to understanding how environmental drivers of migration are being addressed. However, the project's focus appears to be primarily on governance, data, and policy integration rather than on direct interventions to reduce adverse drivers or build resilience at community level. The sections on impact and sustainability, as well as the discussion of the national study's findings, would appear most relevant for human reviewers interested in this objective. The evaluation's treatment of climate-migration linkages and policy integration is likely more valuable than its coverage of direct resilience-building actions.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation: Bosnia and  Herzegovina: Strengthening  Government Capacities in  Integrated Border Management
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The project title itself — 'Strengthening Government Capacities in Integrated Border Management' — directly mirrors the language of this objective. The excerpts suggest the evaluation comprehensively examines the development of a BiH IBM Strategy and Action Plan (2019-2023), new draft legislation on Border Control aligned with EU/Schengen acquis, and cross-governmental coordination mechanisms — all core themes under Actions 11a, 11b, and 11g. Multiple findings and conclusions directly address integrated border management governance, inter-agency coordination, legislative harmonization, and capacity building. The project's explicit alignment with EU IBM standards and the Schengen Code, the participatory development of border management instruments, and the analysis of sustainability and political constraints on implementation would likely provide valuable evidence for synthesis specialists. Sections most worth prioritizing for human review include: the Effectiveness section (outputs achieved, working group processes), the Impact section (short-medium term changes including coordination improvements), the Sustainability section (political constraints on law adoption), and the Conclusions and Recommendations. The excerpts also suggest relevant content on cross-governmental approaches to IBM that could inform Action 11a on whole-of-government approaches.

References.Title: Promoting a human security approach to disaster displacement and environmental migration policies integrating the COVID-19 pandemic response in the Eastern Caribbean
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its sub-themes on natural disasters, climate change, and environmental degradation (Actions 2h, 2i, 2j, 2k, 2l). The programme title itself — 'Promoting a human security approach to disaster displacement and environmental migration policies integrating the COVID-19 pandemic response in the Eastern Caribbean' — directly addresses the intersection of environmental drivers and migration. The executive summary explicitly references 'minimizing the adverse drivers and structural factors that compel people to leave their country of origin — paying specific attention to natural disasters, the adverse effects of climate change, and environmental degradation.' Output 1.2 on 'stronger integration of mobility in climate strategies' and Output 2.2 on 'cross border evacuations' directly map to Actions 2i and 2j. The programme's engagement with the Platform on Disaster Displacement (PDD) and the Cancun Adaptation Framework further reinforces alignment with Action 2l. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on programme outcomes (particularly Outcomes 1 and 2), the conclusions on policy coherence, and the recommendations on CDEMA partnership would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains substantive analysis of disaster displacement policy frameworks across nine Eastern Caribbean states.

References.Title: IOM/UNDP JOINT CONFLICT REDUCTION PROGRAMME SUPPORTED BY THE EUROPEAN UNION FINAL EVALUATION REPORT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16, particularly around social cohesion promotion in conflict-affected contexts. The JCRP's core programmatic focus on 'social cohesion, peace consolidation and pluralism' aligns closely with the objective's theme of fostering inclusive societies. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: inter-tribal and inter-community dialogue processes (relating to action 16a on mutual respect and social cohesion); peace festivals and cultural events promoting coexistence (action 16h on multicultural activities); and community-level reconciliation initiatives. The programme's theory of change explicitly links peace dividends to sustaining social cohesion, and field interviews reportedly confirmed 'peace dividends have greatly contributed to cultural change.' While the report's primary framing is conflict reduction rather than migrant integration per se, the social cohesion evidence base appears substantive and transferable. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on peace dividend approaches, community-level dialogue outcomes, and the lessons learned on hardware-software balance would appear most relevant as starting points for deeper review. It should be noted that the population of concern includes conflict-affected communities with cross-border mobility rather than migrants in a conventional destination-country integration context — this distinction matters for how the evidence would be applied in a synthesis.

References.Title: CROSS BORDER MOBILITY, IRREGULAR MIGRATION AND HIV AND AIDS: THE SAFE JOURNEY INFORMATION CAMPAIGN 2005-2010
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 3. The Safe Journey Information Campaign is fundamentally an information dissemination initiative, and the evaluation explicitly assesses the impact and coverage of information provision to migrants and potential migrants at all stages. The project title itself references information dissemination, and the evaluation questions center on whether information reached intended beneficiaries and changed knowledge and behavior. This aligns closely with Action 3e (promoting multilingual, gender-responsive, evidence-based information campaigns and awareness-raising events in countries of origin to promote safe, orderly and regular migration and highlight risks of irregular migration). The excerpts reveal substantive analysis of: information sources used by migrants (TV, health centers, peer educators, radio); awareness levels of the campaign; knowledge gaps on migration rules and visa procedures; effectiveness of different communication channels (edutainment, road shows, IEC materials, Safe Zones, radio); and recommendations on improving information reach through billboards, posters, and mobile outreach. The conclusions and recommendations sections directly address information dissemination strategy. For a synthesis specialist, the findings on campaign coverage, channel effectiveness, and knowledge outcomes — particularly in the findings chapter (Chapter 3) and the conclusions/recommendations sections — would appear most valuable as starting points for deeper review. The report likely contains additional relevant evidence in the full findings chapter beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: CROSS BORDER MOBILITY, IRREGULAR MIGRATION AND HIV AND AIDS: THE SAFE JOURNEY INFORMATION CAMPAIGN 2005-2010
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The campaign explicitly targeted vulnerable populations — youth aged 15-24, women and girls, and irregular migrants — and the evaluation assesses how well it addressed their vulnerabilities. The excerpts reveal substantive discussion of: sexual abuse risks faced by migrants abroad (mentioned in all 9 Safe Zone FGDs); gender-based violence as a campaign theme; the particular vulnerabilities of young women and girls; and the need for more targeted support for vulnerable groups including youth with disabilities. This aligns with Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies addressing needs of migrant women and girls), and 7a (reviewing policies to ensure they don't exacerbate vulnerabilities). The report also discusses the Reception and Support Centre for returned migrants, touching on assistance mechanisms. However, vulnerability is one of several themes rather than the singular focus, and the analytical depth on vulnerability response mechanisms is moderate. For a synthesis specialist, the FGD findings on risks faced by migrants (particularly sexual abuse and gender-based violence) and the recommendations on targeting vulnerable groups would appear most relevant for review.

References.Title: FORTALECIMIENTO DEL PROCESO DE INTEGRACIÓN DE MIGRANTES QUE RETORNAN A EL SALVADOR (PIRE)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The PIRE project is explicitly and centrally focused on the reintegration of returning migrants to El Salvador — this is the primary subject of the evaluation. The project title itself ('Fortalecimiento del proceso de integración de Migrantes que Retornan a El Salvador') directly maps onto GCM Objective 21's core concern with sustainable reintegration. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly relevant to GCM Objective 21's associated actions: inter-institutional coordination mechanisms for managing returnee services (Action 21h), capacity-building for civil servants attending to returnees (Action 21h), the VRES referral centres as a reintegration support infrastructure (Action 21b, 21h), lessons learned from Mexico and trilateral meetings on best practices in return and reintegration (Action 21a), gender-responsive approaches in the communications campaign (Action 21b), sustainability of reintegration programmes through government handover (Action 21a, 21b), and the regional diffusion of the model to Honduras and the Northern Triangle (Action 21a). The evaluation covers relevance, effectiveness, efficiency, impact, and sustainability of a reintegration programme — all directly informative for evidence synthesis on GCM Objective 21. For human reviewers, the sections most worth prioritizing appear to be: the findings on capacity-building outcomes (impact section), the conclusions on sustainability and government handover, the lessons learned on inter-institutional coordination, and the recommendations on programme design for reintegration. The regional dimension (trilateral meeting, Mexico visit, Honduras replication) would likely be particularly valuable for a synthesis examining cross-country learning on reintegration models.

References.Title: REAL TIME EVALUATION OF IOM'S HUMANITARIAN RESPONSE TO THE SOUTH SUDAN CRISIS - FINAL REPORT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The IOM Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM) appears prominently throughout the report as a core operational tool used to collect disaggregated data on displacement trends, locations, vulnerabilities and needs of affected populations. The excerpts indicate that DTM data was used widely by humanitarian organizations to inform the response, and that IOM launched biometric registration in May 2014 to improve data quality and reliability. The executive summary explicitly lists DTM capability as a best practice contributing to IOM's success. This relates particularly to Actions 1a (comprehensive data strategy), 1c (building national capacities in data collection and analysis), and 1j (country-specific profiles with disaggregated data). The sections on IOM's operational capacity and the background/context sections appear most relevant for human reviewers to examine. However, the data collection focus is instrumental to humanitarian response rather than being the primary subject of the evaluation, which centers on L3 procedures and cluster coordination — so this is relevant but not fundamental.

References.Title: CAMP COORDINATION AND CAMP MANAGEMENT AND DISASTER RISK MANAGEMENT CAPACITY BUILDING PROGRAMME IN SOUTHERN AFRICA: PHASE III
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly around disaster risk reduction and preparedness as structural factors affecting displacement. The programme explicitly addresses disaster risk management (DRM) capacity building in Southern Africa — a region prone to floods, cyclones, earthquakes, and droughts — which directly relates to Actions 2i (adaptation and resilience strategies to natural disasters), 2j (integrating displacement considerations into disaster preparedness strategies), and 2k (addressing vulnerabilities of persons affected by natural disasters). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how national and sub-national DRM systems can better prepare for and respond to displacement caused by natural hazards. The programme's focus on contingency planning, minimum humanitarian standards, and coordination mechanisms for internally displaced persons (IDPs) appears directly relevant to Actions 2g and 2j. Sections IV.1 (Relevance/Adequacy), IV.3 (Impact), and the Executive Summary would appear most valuable for human reviewers to prioritize. The report likely contributes evidence on how capacity building in DRM can reduce vulnerability to displacement-inducing disasters, though it does not address the broader structural economic or governance drivers of migration.

References.Title: THEMATIC EVALUATION OF REINTEGRATION ACTIVITIES IN SAHEL AND LAKE CHAD UNDER THE PROJECT “EU-IOM JOINT INITIATIVE FOR MIGRANT PROTECTION AND REINTEGRATION ”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The evaluation is explicitly and comprehensively focused on reintegration programming — the EU-IOM Joint Initiative for Migrant Protection and Reintegration — which is the central subject of GCM Objective 21. The report title, evaluation questions, and all major findings and recommendations directly address sustainable reintegration of returnees in the Sahel and Lake Chad region. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: economic reintegration assistance (microbusiness, vocational training, cash-for-work) relating to Action 21h; psychosocial assistance and social reintegration relating to Actions 21b and 21h; community-level reintegration and social cohesion relating to Action 21i; national government and civil society capacity-building for reintegration sustainability; and the integrated approach combining economic, social, and psychosocial dimensions. The 84% beneficiary satisfaction rate, evidence on positive outcomes for returnees' self-respect and livelihoods, and the detailed recommendations on improving reintegration programming all directly advance understanding of what constitutes sustainable reintegration under Action 21h. For a synthesis specialist, the executive summary, detailed recommendations sections, and findings on individual vs. collective reintegration modalities would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The full report likely contains substantially more evidence on reintegration outcomes than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Final internal evaluation of the” The UN COVID-19 Response and Recovery Multi-Partner Trust Fund” (UN COVID-19 MPTF)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The project's core focus is on strengthening border management capacities at Guinea-Bissau's 15 official Points of Entry (PoEs) in the context of COVID-19. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: border health surveillance systems (Outputs 1.1-1.4); capacity building for PoE staff on detection, notification, isolation and case management; provision of SOPs for border procedures; rehabilitation of isolation facilities at border crossings; and coordination between border authorities and health systems. These themes relate closely to Actions 11a (international border management cooperation, identification and referral of vulnerable migrants), 11b (integrated border management structures and screening procedures), and 11g (cross-border collaboration on treatment of persons crossing borders). The findings sections on Outputs 1.1-1.4 and the conclusions section appear most relevant for human reviewers to prioritize. The report also touches on the challenge of irregular crossings through alternative routes. While the border management focus is specifically health-oriented (COVID-19), the capacity-building and procedural strengthening elements appear substantive enough to contribute to a synthesis on integrated border management. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Final internal evaluation of the” The UN COVID-19 Response and Recovery Multi-Partner Trust Fund” (UN COVID-19 MPTF)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute some evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12, particularly regarding screening and referral mechanisms at borders. The project explicitly developed and implemented SOPs for detection, isolation, notification, and case management at PoEs — directly relevant to Action 12c (gender-responsive and child-sensitive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures). The capacity building provided to PoE staff on screening, surveillance, and referral (Outputs 1.1 and 1.3) also relates to Action 12b (specialized trainings for first responders and border officials). The focus group discussions with PoE staff confirming improved knowledge on screening and referral procedures appear particularly relevant. However, the screening and referral focus is specifically health/COVID-19 oriented rather than addressing broader migration status determination procedures. Synthesis specialists on GCM Objective 12 would likely want to review the findings on Outputs 1.1 and 1.3, and the conclusions section, while noting the health-specific rather than migration-procedure focus.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF IOM’S MIGRATION DATA STRATEGY
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The evaluation's primary subject — IOM's Migration Data Strategy 2020–2025 — is directly and comprehensively concerned with the global evidence base on migration, data collection, analysis, dissemination, and use for evidence-based policymaking. These are the core themes of GCM Objective 1. The MDS's three objectives (strengthen the global evidence base, build state capacity for data management, ensure evidence-based programming) map almost directly onto Actions 1a, 1c, 1d, and 1e. The excerpts suggest extensive analysis of: IOM's efforts to harmonize and standardize migration data (Action 1a, 1b); capacity-building for states and partners (Action 1c); the Migration Data Portal and other global databases (Action 1e); use of big data and innovation (Action 1c); and the role of data in informing SDG implementation (Action 1d). The evaluation's conclusions and recommendations directly address data governance, data strategy design, institutional capacity for data, and sustainable funding for data systems — all highly relevant to a synthesis on this objective. For human reviewers, the executive summary, conclusions sections 6.1–6.6, and recommendations 1–8 would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The full report likely contains additional depth beyond what the excerpts reveal, particularly in the findings sections referenced throughout.

References.Title: FINAL INTERNAL EVALUATION REPORT BETTER MIGRATION MANAGEMENT EUTF PROGRAM IOM IMPLEMENTATION OF PHASE I
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The evaluation appears to substantively examine Component 3 of BMM, which focuses on 'identification, assistance and protection of migrants in need' — directly relevant to actions 7b, 7c, 7k, and 7l. The excerpts indicate that the project achieved targets for Component 3 (Output 3.1 and 3.3) related to 'improved identification, assistance and protection for Victims of Trafficking (VoT) and vulnerable migrants, especially women and children.' The evaluation discusses Migrant Resource Centres (MRCs), mobile patrol systems, medical referral systems, and assistance to vulnerable migrants across multiple countries (Djibouti, Somalia, Ethiopia, Kenya). The conclusions section notes specific outcomes such as the MRS in Djibouti improving healthcare access and changing community perceptions toward migrants. Recommendations address reintegration processes and follow-up procedures for vulnerable migrants. Gender-responsive approaches are also discussed. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the Component 3 findings sections and the conclusions on MRC operations and referral systems, as these appear to offer substantive evidence on identification and assistance mechanisms for vulnerable migrants. The full report likely contains more detailed country-level evidence than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL INTERNAL EVALUATION REPORT BETTER MIGRATION MANAGEMENT EUTF PROGRAM IOM IMPLEMENTATION OF PHASE I
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute some evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The evaluation explicitly addresses trafficking in persons (TiP) as a core concern of the BMM project, with Component 3 focused on 'identification, assistance and protection for Victims of Trafficking (VoT) and vulnerable migrants.' The excerpts indicate that the project achieved targets related to VoT identification and assistance (Output 3.1 and 3.3), and the Khartoum Process framework explicitly targets trafficking. The National Referral Mechanism (NRM) is mentioned as a government-led initiative. The evaluation also discusses awareness-raising activities and the need for better reintegration processes for potential VoTs. Recommendations address sustainability of Component 3 and suggest 'involving survivors of trafficking and smuggling testimonies.' However, the excerpts do not provide deep analysis of prosecution mechanisms, legislative frameworks, or financial flows associated with trafficking networks. Synthesis specialists would want to review the Component 3 findings and conclusions sections, particularly on VoT identification, referral mechanisms, and assistance — framing these as pointers for further review rather than definitive characterizations. Actions 10c, 10e, 10g, and 10h appear most related.

References.Title: FINAL INTERNAL EVALUATION REPORT BETTER MIGRATION MANAGEMENT EUTF PROGRAM IOM IMPLEMENTATION OF PHASE I
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. Component 2 of BMM explicitly focuses on 'capacity building in integrated border management (IBM),' and the evaluation devotes substantial attention to assessing IBM capacity building across multiple countries (Djibouti, Ethiopia, Kenya, Uganda, Somalia, Sudan, South Sudan).
