The excerpts discuss IBM activities including MIDAS deployment, One-Stop Border Posts (OSBPs), mobile patrols, and equipment provision. The evaluation notes that 'governments have improved capacities in integrated border management in place' as a key outcome indicator, and discusses challenges in measuring this. The conclusions note that 'capacity of the governments is significantly developed' with examples from Djibouti (Evisa system) and Sudan (MIDAS in Arabic). Recommendations address whether to scale up MIDAS or strengthen IBM/OSBP broadly. Actions 11a, 11b, and 11d appear most directly addressed. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the Component 2 findings sections and the country-level conclusions on IBM capacity building outcomes, noting that the full report likely contains more detailed evidence than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Ex-Post Evaluation of the Project “ICT Strategy for Integrated Border Management in Rwanda”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 4. The project explicitly concerns the digitalization and integration of Rwanda's immigration documentation systems — including e-passports, laissez-passer, visas, work permits, and electronic archiving — which aligns closely with Actions 4a (improving civil registry and identity systems through ICT) and 4b (harmonizing travel documents and investing in digitalization). The evaluation notes that the DIP portal integrates multiple document types into one system, improving efficiency and integrity of issued documents. The evaluator observed real-time processing of e-passports and laissez-passer documents. The project's alignment with ICAO standards is referenced in the acronyms section. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on DIP portal outcomes (effectiveness findings), the discussion of biometric passport readiness, and the sustainability analysis would appear particularly worth reviewing. The report's focus is primarily on the government system side rather than migrant access to documentation, which somewhat limits its direct relevance to some actions under Objective 4, but the ICT infrastructure for documentation issuance is a core theme.

References.Title: Ex-Post Evaluation of the Project “ICT Strategy for Integrated Border Management in Rwanda”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The project title itself — 'ICT Strategy for Integrated Border Management in Rwanda' — directly mirrors the language of this objective. The evaluation explicitly examines the development and implementation of an integrated border management ICT strategy, including the creation of the DIP portal, interoperability of DGIE systems, and interface with government online services. This aligns closely with Action 11b (establishing integrated border management structures using ICT, pre-reporting by carriers, and information technology) and Action 11a (capacity-building measures for border management). The Advanced Passenger Information (API) system mentioned in the report directly relates to pre-reporting by carriers under Action 11b. The evaluation covers effectiveness, sustainability, and impact of these systems in depth. For a synthesis specialist, the effectiveness findings on DIP portal outcomes, the discussion of API implementation, the sustainability analysis on government ownership, and the recommendations for further ICT investment would appear particularly valuable. The report also touches on One Stop Border Posts (OSBPs), decentralization of services to 30 districts, and real-time information exchange — all core themes under Objective 11. This appears to be a core evidence source for any synthesis on integrated border management ICT systems.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF IOM CASH-BASED INTERVENTIONS (CBI)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 20, particularly regarding financial inclusion of migrants (Action 20e, 20i). The evaluation explicitly discusses digital financial inclusion, mobile money, electronic vouchers, bank transfers, debit cards, and the use of financial service providers (FSPs) as delivery mechanisms for CBIs. The report discusses long-term agreements with FSPs, the introduction of mobile money and voucher systems, and the challenges of financial infrastructure in different contexts. The evaluation also references the COVID-19 experience prompting emphasis on portability of assistance and exploration of emerging technologies for more flexible, mobile and safe delivery. The discussion of digital CBIs in Indonesia (ATM cards, bank accounts) and the broader analysis of innovative delivery mechanisms appears substantively relevant to Actions 20e (innovative technological solutions for transfer) and 20i (banking solutions and financial instruments for migrants). A synthesis specialist would likely want to review the sections on delivery mechanisms, FSP partnerships, and the findings on digital inclusion and financial access, noting that financial inclusion is treated as an operational dimension of CBI delivery rather than as a standalone policy objective — but the evidence on what works in practice for financial access could still add value to a synthesis on this objective.

References.Title: Evaluación Final del Proyecto Retorno y ReintegraciReintegración en el Tri Triángulo Norte de CentroamCentroamérica OIM
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to contain substantive content relevant to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project's fourth objective explicitly addresses generating and using data for policies, programs and actions — described in quarterly reports as 'improving the availability, quality and use of information to strengthen the reception and reintegration process.' The executive summary and recommendations sections discuss information systems development, delays in implementation, and the importance of disaggregated data for evidence-based policymaking on return and reintegration. The recommendations section includes a dedicated sub-section on 'Producción y uso de la información' (Production and use of information), calling for interinstitutional integration of information systems, use of a 'ficha única' (single record), and data linkage from consular services through community reintegration. These elements appear to relate to GCM Actions 1a (comprehensive data strategy), 1i (administrative records for statistical purposes), and 1j (country-specific migration profiles). However, data systems appear to be one of several components rather than the central focus. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on information systems development, the fourth project objective, and the recommendations on data integration — framing these as pointers for further review rather than definitive characterizations of the full report.

References.Title: Evaluación Final del Proyecto Retorno y ReintegraciReintegración en el Tri Triángulo Norte de CentroamCentroamérica OIM
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project explicitly targets vulnerable populations — unaccompanied and separated children (NNA), families, and other returnees with acute needs — and the evaluation assesses how well their vulnerabilities were identified and addressed throughout the reception and reintegration process. The executive summary discusses psychosocial support, medical and psychological first aid, triage and referral mechanisms, and the need for continuous protection beyond reception centers — all closely related to Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies addressing particular needs), 7e (child protection systems), and 7f (protection of unaccompanied and separated children). The recommendations section calls for standardized medical and psychological first aid, detection and referral of cases, and strengthening interinstitutional protection networks. The context section also highlights the increasing vulnerability profile of returnees (more families, women, NNA, exposure to violence). Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the reception component findings, the recommendations on protection mechanisms, and the discussion of vulnerability profiles — noting that vulnerability is addressed primarily in the return/reception context rather than across the full migration cycle.

References.Title: Evaluación Final del Proyecto Retorno y ReintegraciReintegración en el Tri Triángulo Norte de CentroamCentroamérica OIM
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Return and reintegration is the explicit and central subject of the entire evaluation — the project title is 'Retorno y Reintegración en el Triángulo Norte de Centroamérica' and the evaluation covers all phases from reception upon arrival through sustainable reintegration in communities. The report appears to provide substantial analysis across multiple dimensions directly corresponding to GCM Objective 21's associated actions: reception and post-arrival assistance (Action 21b — gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes), psychosocial, social and economic reintegration at individual, community and structural levels (Action 21h — equal access to social protection, psychosocial assistance, vocational training, employment), community needs assessment (Action 21i — identifying needs of return communities), and child-sensitive return procedures (Action 21g — best interests of the child in return processes). The evaluation also addresses sustainability of reintegration, institutional capacity, and the humanitarian-development nexus — all central to Action 21's framing of 'sustainable reintegration.' The executive summary, conclusions, and recommendations sections all appear densely relevant. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on reintegration outcomes across three dimensions (psychosocial, social, economic), the conclusions on sustainability challenges, the recommendations on institutional strengthening and model interventions, and the discussion of the humanitarian-development nexus would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is likely that the full report contains substantially more evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: RAPPORT D'ÉVALUATION DU PROJET SIRIRI
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16, particularly around social cohesion, community inclusion, and intercultural dialogue. The SIRIRI project's core objectives explicitly address social cohesion between communities at risk of conflict, promotion of 'living together' (vivre ensemble), inclusive dialogue between communities and local authorities, and cultural/sporting events to foster mutual understanding — all closely aligned with actions 16a (mutual respect and social cohesion), 16f (community centres and intercultural dialogue), and 16h (multicultural activities through sports and cultural events). The evaluation appears to contain substantive analysis of social cohesion outcomes, including perception surveys measuring indicators such as solidarity, belonging, communication across communities, and cohabitation. The executive summary notes a 101% average improvement in perception of 'living together,' and the impact section describes concrete examples of inter-community interaction being restored (e.g., crossing the bridge in Kaga-Bandoro, lifting of 'the red line' in Boda). While the context is conflict-affected communities in CAR rather than classic destination-country integration, the mechanisms and outcomes examined are directly relevant to social cohesion evidence. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the impact section, the perception survey methodology and results (Section 2.2), and the conclusions and recommendations. Note that the population is internally displaced and conflict-affected rather than international migrants per se — this contextual difference should be acknowledged in any synthesis use.

References.Title: MID-TERM INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO THE GOVERNMENT OF KYRGYZSTAN TO STRENGTHEN THE LEGAL AND OPERATIONAL FRAMEWORK FOR COMBATING TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The entire project evaluated is explicitly dedicated to combating trafficking in persons — this is the primary and dominant subject of the report. The project title, all four objectives, and the full scope of evaluation findings directly address anti-trafficking efforts. The excerpts suggest substantive coverage of multiple associated actions: Action 10a (legislative alignment with international standards, including UNTOC protocols); Action 10e (victim-centered approaches, identification and protection of victims); Action 10f (ensuring correct legal definitions of TIP in legislation); Action 10g (strengthening prosecution of traffickers, avoiding criminalization of victims); Action 10h (protection and assistance to victims through NRM and SOPs); and Action 10i (training programmes for law enforcement, prosecutors and judges on identifying TIP). The report appears to offer rich evidence on legislative reform processes, NRM development, capacity building for criminal justice actors, and awareness-raising campaigns — all core themes under Objective 10. For a synthesis specialist, the conclusions on effectiveness of legal reform (Objective 1 of the project), the NRM and SOP development process (Objective 2), prosecutorial and judicial training outcomes (Objective 3), and sustainability of anti-trafficking mechanisms would appear particularly valuable. The full findings sections (referenced but not fully excerpted) likely contain additional granular evidence. This report appears to be a strong candidate for in-depth review for any synthesis on GCM Objective 10.

References.Title: MID-TERM INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO THE GOVERNMENT OF KYRGYZSTAN TO STRENGTHEN THE LEGAL AND OPERATIONAL FRAMEWORK FOR COMBATING TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12, particularly Action 12b and 12c. The project developed national identification criteria and Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for identifying and referring victims of trafficking — directly relevant to Action 12c (establishing gender-responsive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures). The training of judges, prosecutors, and law enforcement on victim identification and referral relates to Action 12b (specialized human rights and trauma-informed trainings for first responders and government officials). The NRM development process, including validation of SOPs and engagement of multiple ministries, appears to be substantively analyzed in the report. However, the screening and referral focus is specifically on TIP victims rather than migrants broadly, which somewhat limits its scope for a general synthesis on Objective 12. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the conclusions on NRM and SOP development (section 5, Effectiveness and Sustainability subsections) and the recommendations on SOP implementation and training (recommendations 10, 12, 15) as starting points, noting that the referral mechanisms discussed are trafficking-specific.

References.Title: FINAL REPORT - EVALUATING THE EFFECTIVENESS OF REGIONAL MIGRATION PROGRAM MODELS ON PROVIDING ASSISTANCE TO VULNERABLE MIGRANTS
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. Addressing vulnerabilities in migration appears to be one of the two primary subjects of the evaluation — explicitly named in the evaluation title ('providing assistance to vulnerable migrants') and in both evaluation questions. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of direct assistance to vulnerable migrants across multiple categories including unaccompanied migrant children (UAMC), SGBV survivors, trafficking victims, and migrants in crisis situations. Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerability), 7c (gender-responsive policies), 7e and 7f (child protection and unaccompanied children), and 7j (migrants in crisis situations) appear particularly well-covered. The evaluation assesses IOM's effectiveness in identifying and assisting vulnerable migrants, critiques gaps in psychosocial assistance and SGBV response capacity, and recommends linking direct assistance with capacity building. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on direct assistance effectiveness (Q2), the discussion of AVR services, the analysis of MRCs and their limitations, and the recommendations on direct assistance (Section 5.2.2) would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence on vulnerability identification and referral mechanisms beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL REPORT - EVALUATING THE EFFECTIVENESS OF REGIONAL MIGRATION PROGRAM MODELS ON PROVIDING ASSISTANCE TO VULNERABLE MIGRANTS
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The evaluation appears to substantively examine government capacity building for border and migration management — a core theme under Actions 11a (border management cooperation and capacity building) and 11e (child protection at borders). The excerpts suggest analysis of training for border officials, screening procedures, and referral mechanisms for vulnerable migrants at borders. The evaluation explicitly notes that IOM's capacity building often did not reach 'frontline personnel who engage with migrants, including members of the security and border services,' and recommendations specifically call for greater focus on border and migration agency officials. The sections on capacity building effectiveness (Q1, Section 3) and the pillar-specific recommendations (Section 5.2.1) would appear particularly relevant for a synthesis specialist to review. However, border management is one of several themes covered, and the report's primary lens is on capacity building effectiveness rather than border management systems per se.

References.Title: FINAL REPORT - EVALUATING THE EFFECTIVENESS OF REGIONAL MIGRATION PROGRAM MODELS ON PROVIDING ASSISTANCE TO VULNERABLE MIGRANTS
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Assisted Voluntary Return (AVR) is explicitly identified as a major program component and is described as one of the most praised forms of IOM direct assistance. The evaluation covers AVR services provided to nearly 10,000 migrants in the HoA in FY 2015, and IOM staff rated AVR as highly effective. The report also discusses the large-scale return of migrants from Yemen to the Horn of Africa as a case of effective emergency response. These elements relate directly to Actions 21b (gender-responsive return and reintegration programmes), 21e (safe and dignified return), and 21h (sustainable reintegration). The evaluation also discusses transit centers processing returnee children in Ethiopia, touching on Action 21g (child-sensitive return). For a synthesis specialist, the sections on direct assistance effectiveness (Q2, Section 4) — particularly the AVR discussion and the Yemen return response — and the recommendations on linking direct assistance with capacity building (Section 5.2.2) would appear particularly valuable. However, reintegration sustainability and readmission cooperation are not analyzed in depth in the excerpts reviewed, and the full report may contain more on these dimensions.

References.Title: 2014 - 2018 FINAL EVALUATION IOM X ASIA CAMPAIGN
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 3. The IOM X programme is fundamentally an information and awareness campaign targeting migrants and communities at various stages of migration, using both online and offline channels to disseminate accurate information on safe migration, trafficking risks, and available protections. This aligns closely with GCM Action 3e (multilingual, gender-responsive, evidence-based information campaigns and pre-departure orientation) and Action 3c (information points along migration routes). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of campaign effectiveness in reaching target audiences, the role of language (dubbing vs. subtitles), platform selection, and the combination of online/offline engagement. The executive summary, effectiveness section, and lessons learned appear particularly relevant for human reviewers. The report's focus on Communications for Development (C4D) as a methodology for information dissemination to migrants and communities is directly pertinent. Specialists should note that the report covers information provision as a prevention tool rather than as a rights-based service, which may limit its applicability to some associated actions.

References.Title: 2014 - 2018 FINAL EVALUATION IOM X ASIA CAMPAIGN
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The IOM X programme is explicitly and centrally a counter-trafficking initiative — its stated objective is 'to inspire social resilience to human exploitation and Trafficking in Persons (TIP) in the Asia Pacific Region.' The evaluation assesses a four-year, multi-country campaign specifically designed to prevent human trafficking through awareness-raising, behavior change communications, and stakeholder engagement. This aligns directly with GCM Actions 10i (national information systems and training to identify trafficking) and 10j (awareness-raising campaigns for migrants on trafficking risks). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of campaign effectiveness in increasing knowledge about trafficking, shifting attitudes, and promoting protective behaviors — all core to GCM Objective 10. Particularly relevant sections for human reviewers appear to include: the effectiveness section (knowledge and attitude change outcomes), the sustainability section (government uptake of materials), the lessons learned (celebrity use, video effectiveness, platform selection), and the recommendations (stakeholder engagement, behavior change measurement). The report also addresses the specific vulnerabilities of women and men in different sectors (domestic work, fishing), relating to Action 10e. The scale of reach (682 million people, 239 videos) and the C4D methodology offer potentially valuable evidence for synthesis on awareness-raising approaches to TIP prevention.

References.Title: Final External Evaluation of “Protecting vulnerable migrants and stabilizing communities in Libya – Phase II" (EUTF) Program
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project's primary focus is explicitly on protecting vulnerable migrants — including unaccompanied and separated children, women-headed households, victims of trafficking, people with disabilities, and the elderly — which directly aligns with the core theme of this objective and actions 7b, 7c, 7e, 7f, and 7k. The evaluation appears to substantively assess protection case management, specialized assistance to vulnerable groups, MHPSS services, referral mechanisms, and access to services for migrants in irregular situations. Multiple findings and recommendations directly address vulnerability identification, protection responses, and service access. The executive summary, conclusions, and recommendations sections all engage extensively with vulnerability-related themes. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on protection case management effectiveness, MHPSS service delivery, assistance to specific vulnerable groups (UASCs, trafficking victims, women), and the referral system analysis would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Final External Evaluation of “Protecting vulnerable migrants and stabilizing communities in Libya – Phase II" (EUTF) Program
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. Improving access to basic services for migrants is a central and explicit focus of the project and evaluation. The evaluation substantively assesses access to health services (including MHPSS, mobile clinics, referrals), food and NFI distribution, and protection services for migrants, IDPs, and host communities. Multiple findings and recommendations directly address barriers to service access (language, medication shortages, insufficient MHPSS staff), service quality, and health system capacity-building. These elements align closely with actions 15c, 15e, and 15a. The executive summary, conclusions, and recommendations all engage extensively with service access themes. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on health service delivery effectiveness, MHPSS accessibility, NFI distribution, and recommendations on integrating MHPSS into public hospitals would appear particularly valuable. The evaluation also addresses non-discrimination in service access (gender, disability), relevant to action 15a. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Final External Evaluation of “Protecting vulnerable migrants and stabilizing communities in Libya – Phase II" (EUTF) Program
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute substantial evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. Social cohesion between migrants and host communities is an explicit and recurring theme throughout the evaluation, addressed under Specific Objective 2. The evaluation assesses community engagement activities, vocational training for both migrants and Libyan youth, World Migrant Day events, and community improvement projects — all directly relevant to actions 16a, 16f, 16g, and 16h. Multiple findings and recommendations address the challenges of improving social cohesion, particularly regarding Black African migrants, and the limited interaction between migrant and host communities. The evaluation also covers vocational training and employability (action 16d) and women's empowerment through self-confidence training (action 16e). For a synthesis specialist, the sections on social cohesion activities, YESS vocational training outcomes, community engagement effectiveness, and recommendations on creating meaningful interaction spaces would appear particularly valuable. The honest assessment of limited social cohesion impact adds analytical value for synthesis purposes.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION: MIGRATION PROFILE FOR THE REPUBLIC OF MALDIVES
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The evaluated project — 'Migration Profile for the Republic of Maldives' — is explicitly and centrally about developing a country-specific migration profile (directly corresponding to Action 1j), building national capacity for migration data collection and analysis (Action 1c), and using disaggregated migration data to support evidence-based policymaking. The project title, evaluation questions, and virtually all findings and conclusions directly address these themes. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how migration data was gathered, consolidated, and used (or not used) for policy planning, including discussion of data gaps, secondary vs. primary data challenges, and the limitations of the final product due to political changes. The executive summary, effectiveness section, impact section, and sustainability section all appear to engage directly with data quality, dissemination, and utilization themes. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on effectiveness (capacity-building in data collection), impact (MP as a reference document for policy), sustainability (keeping the MP as a 'living document'), and lessons learned (on data collection methodology and local organization involvement) would appear particularly valuable as starting points. Actions 1c, 1d, and 1j appear most directly addressed. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: PACIFIC ADAPTATION THROUGH LABOUR MOBILITY IN THE LOWLYING ATOLL STATES OF KIRIBATI, MARSHALL ISLANDS AND TUVALU
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The PALM project's primary outputs were explicitly centered on data collection and labour market information systems (LMIS) — Output 1.1 ('Data available relating to labour mobility and labour markets in target countries') and Output 1.2 ('LMIS established and populated with relevant data in Kiribati and Tuvalu'). The evaluation appears to assess the effectiveness of these data systems, their sustainability, and their use to inform evidence-based policy — directly aligning with Actions 1c (building national capacities in data collection), 1d (analysing data on migration effects for sustainable development), and 1j (developing country-specific migration profiles). The executive summary and context sections indicate substantive analysis of data gaps, LMIS design and implementation, and the use of labour market data for policy. Recommendations 1, 4, 7, and 9 specifically address data system strengthening, documentation, baseline assessments, and SMART indicators. For a synthesis specialist, the findings sections on LMIS establishment and use in Kiribati and Tuvalu, as well as the recommendations on data management and evidence-based policy, would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional relevant evidence on data system capacity-building beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: PACIFIC ADAPTATION THROUGH LABOUR MOBILITY IN THE LOWLYING ATOLL STATES OF KIRIBATI, MARSHALL ISLANDS AND TUVALU
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly regarding climate change adaptation and migration (Actions 2i and 2h). The PALM project is explicitly framed around climate change adaptation through labour mobility in low-lying atoll states facing sea level rise — Kiribati, Tuvalu, and RMI are described as facing 'serious threat of permanent inundation from sea level rise' (IPCC). The project's long-term goal directly addresses migration as a sustainable development and climate change adaptation strategy. This aligns with Actions 2i (developing adaptation and resilience strategies to slow-onset natural disasters including sea level rise, taking into account migration implications) and 2b (investing in programmes addressing climate change adaptation). However, the report's primary analytical focus is on labour market data systems and policy rather than on the drivers of migration per se — the climate change framing is more contextual than analytically central. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the context section (section 4.1) and the Theory of Change discussion, as well as any findings on how labour mobility functions as a climate adaptation strategy, framing these as pointers for further review rather than definitive characterizations.

References.Title: PACIFIC ADAPTATION THROUGH LABOUR MOBILITY IN THE LOWLYING ATOLL STATES OF KIRIBATI, MARSHALL ISLANDS AND TUVALU
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 5, particularly regarding labour mobility pathways and climate-related migration solutions. The project explicitly aims to support governments in maximizing labour mobility as a climate change adaptation strategy, which aligns with Actions 5d (flexible, rights-based labour mobility schemes), 5h (cooperating to identify solutions for migrants compelled to leave due to slow-onset natural disasters and sea level rise), and 5b (facilitating regional labour mobility through cooperation arrangements). The context section discusses Pacific labour mobility schemes (Australia, New Zealand seasonal worker programmes) and the RMI Compact of Free Association as existing pathways. The project's National Labour Migration Policy (NLMP) support work in Kiribati and Tuvalu relates to Action 5c (reviewing pathways for regular migration). However, the report's primary focus is on data systems rather than pathway design per se. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the context section on existing labour mobility schemes and any findings on how the project products informed pathway development, framing these as pointers for further review.

References.Title: MIGRATION FOR DEVELOPMENT IN THE WESTERN BALKANS (MIDWEB) FINAL EVALUATION
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 3. The MIDWEB project's core component — the Migrant Service Centres (MSCs) — appears to be fundamentally about providing accurate and timely information to potential migrants at all stages of migration, directly aligning with actions 3a (centralized publicly accessible information on regular migration options), 3c (information points along migration routes), 3d (information for newly arrived migrants), and 3e (pre-departure orientation and awareness-raising). The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of information dissemination to potential migrants, students, and diaspora communities, and examines whether MSCs successfully reached their target groups. The executive summary and key findings sections appear to contain substantive analysis of information provision outcomes, including what worked and what did not (e.g., returnees not being effectively reached). The recommendations on updating information guides, expanding destination country guides, and establishing information management mechanisms would also be relevant. Specialists conducting a synthesis on Objective 3 would particularly want to review the sections on MSC performance, target group reach, and sustainability of information services — noting that the report covers a specific regional context (Western Balkans, 2011-2012) which may limit generalizability but offers detailed programmatic evidence.

References.Title: MIGRATION FOR DEVELOPMENT IN THE WESTERN BALKANS (MIDWEB) FINAL EVALUATION
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 14. The MIDWEB project included a dedicated component on strengthening consular services, with training provided to 94 consular staff on topics including labour migration, migrant rights, the role of a labour attaché, and how to assist irregular migrants — directly relevant to actions 14a (building consular capacities and training consular officers) and 14d (strengthening consular capacities to identify and assist nationals in vulnerable situations). The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of these trainings, with focus group participants unanimously agreeing that trainings increased their ability to do their jobs. The recommendation to develop 'Consular Academies' for continued training of new consular staff is also directly relevant to action 14a. The sections on consular staff training outcomes and the recommendation on institutionalizing consular training would appear particularly valuable for human reviewers conducting a synthesis on this objective. The report covers a specific regional context but offers evaluative evidence on consular capacity-building effectiveness that would likely be useful.

References.Title: MIGRATION FOR DEVELOPMENT IN THE WESTERN BALKANS (MIDWEB) FINAL EVALUATION
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The MIDWEB project is explicitly framed around migration and development (M&D), with its overall objective being 'to contribute to the positive impact of labour migration on socio-economic development in the Western Balkans.' The Temporary and Virtual Return (TVR) component — a central element of the evaluation — directly addresses actions 19i (enabling migrants and diasporas to carry out professional activities and engage in knowledge transfer in home countries) and 19j (building partnerships for knowledge and skills transfer between countries of origin and destination). The evaluation assesses 71 TVR assignments, examining whether skills were effectively transferred from diaspora members to home country institutions. The diaspora engagement component relates to actions 19d (establishing mechanisms to facilitate diaspora contributions) and 19j (mapping diasporas and their skills). The recommendation to engage in diaspora-mapping exercises directly mirrors action 19j. The evaluation also examines the sustainability of diaspora-development linkages and the conditions under which TVR can contribute to development. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on TVR outcomes, diaspora engagement effectiveness, sustainability of skills transfer, and the recommendations on extending TVR and diaspora mapping would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The report covers a specific regional context (Western Balkans) but provides detailed evaluative evidence on diaspora-development programming that would likely be core evidence for a synthesis on this objective.

References.Title: RAPPORT D'ÉVALUATION FINALE PROJET "AIDE AUX ENFANTS ET FEMMES VULNÉRABLES DANS LES ZONES FRONTALIÈRES ENTRE HAÏTI ET LA RÉPUBLIQUE DOMINICAINE"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project's explicit focus on 'aide aux enfants et femmes vulnérables' (assistance to vulnerable children and women) in border zones makes vulnerability reduction the central theme of both the intervention and the evaluation. The excerpts suggest extensive coverage of: identification and assistance to vulnerable migrants (Actions 7b, 7k); gender-responsive approaches to migrant women and girls (Action 7c); child protection systems including unaccompanied children and family reunification (Actions 7e, 7f); referral mechanisms for vulnerable migrants (Action 7b, 7k); psychosocial support and legal assistance (Action 7c); and AGR and school reintegration as vulnerability reduction measures. The evaluation assesses multiple vulnerability categories (women at risk, unaccompanied children, deportees, trafficking victims) and the effectiveness of protection mechanisms across four border points. The conclusions on efficacy, gender equality, and sustainability all directly address vulnerability reduction outcomes. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on Output 2.2 service delivery, the gender equality conclusions, the appropriation and sustainability findings, and the recommendations on strengthening integral assistance would appear particularly valuable. The evaluation of CRF (Centres de Ressources Frontaliers) as protection mechanisms and the assessment of referral system effectiveness seem especially relevant to Actions 7b and 7k.

References.Title: RAPPORT D'ÉVALUATION FINALE PROJET "AIDE AUX ENFANTS ET FEMMES VULNÉRABLES DANS LES ZONES FRONTALIÈRES ENTRE HAÏTI ET LA RÉPUBLIQUE DOMINICAINE"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Anti-trafficking is an explicit and central objective of the project — the full title references 'aide aux enfants et femmes vulnérables' and the project aims to 'améliorer les capacités des acteurs locaux dans leur lutte contre la migration irrégulière et la traite des personnes.' The excerpts suggest coverage of: capacity building for identification of trafficking victims (Action 10i); awareness-raising campaigns on trafficking risks (Action 10j); the CNLTP (Comité National de Lutte contre la Traite des Personnes) as a key coordination partner; standardized operating procedures for victim identification and referral; and a study on human trafficking for sexual and labour exploitation in Ouanaminthe. The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of training, referral mechanisms, and coordination structures specifically targeting trafficking. The extension phase (Result 3) explicitly addresses 'protection et de prévention de la violence faite aux femmes' including trafficking victims. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on training outcomes, SOP development, coordination mechanisms (CDC and CTT), and the conclusions on harmonization and capacity building would appear most relevant. The evaluation of the CNLTP's role and the hotline 840 for victim assistance also relate to Actions 10e and 10h. Border monitoring for trafficking routes (Action 10c) is also addressed.

References.Title: RAPPORT D'ÉVALUATION FINALE PROJET "AIDE AUX ENFANTS ET FEMMES VULNÉRABLES DANS LES ZONES FRONTALIÈRES ENTRE HAÏTI ET LA RÉPUBLIQUE DOMINICAINE"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. Border management in an integrated and coordinated manner appears to be a core theme of the evaluated project. The excerpts suggest substantive coverage of: integrated border management at four official crossing points (Actions 11a, 11b); capacity building for border officials and protection actors (Action 11a); referral mechanisms for vulnerable migrants at borders (Action 11a); child protection at borders including unaccompanied children (Action 11e); cross-border cooperation between Haiti and the Dominican Republic (Action 11g); and the establishment of CRF (Centres de Ressources Frontaliers) as border management infrastructure. The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of coordination mechanisms (CDC, CTT, local coordination groups) and the operationalization of border protection systems. The conclusions on alignment, harmonization, and sustainability directly address the functioning of integrated border management. The recommendations on strengthening IBESR, ONM, and local authorities' roles at border points are particularly relevant to Action 11a. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on CRF operationalization, coordination mechanism effectiveness, and the lessons learned on border monitoring would appear most valuable as starting points for deeper review.

References.Title: RAPPORT D'ÉVALUATION FINALE PROJET "AIDE AUX ENFANTS ET FEMMES VULNÉRABLES DANS LES ZONES FRONTALIÈRES ENTRE HAÏTI ET LA RÉPUBLIQUE DOMINICAINE"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12. The project explicitly developed standardized operating procedures (SOP/POS) for screening and referral of vulnerable migrants, which is central to GCM Objective 12. The excerpts suggest coverage of: development and implementation of standardized referral mechanisms (Action 12c); training of first responders and border officials on identification and referral procedures (Action 12b); child-sensitive procedures for unaccompanied children (Action 12d); and a common data management system for registering vulnerable migrants. The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of these screening and referral systems across four border points, noting both successes (SOP manual developed participatively, training conducted) and limitations (incomplete standardization of registration processes, database management challenges). The conclusions on efficacy and the recommendations on strengthening CRF procedures and standardizing internal processes are directly relevant to Actions 12b and 12c. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on SOP development, training outcomes, and the assessment of referral mechanism effectiveness would appear particularly valuable. Note that Objectives 7, 10, 11, and 12 all have strong claims on this report — specialists should be aware that the report covers multiple interconnected themes.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation: Support to the Government of Senegal to Establish a National Migration Policy
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 23, specifically regarding the development and endorsement of national migration policies as a foundation for international cooperation frameworks. The project's core subject — supporting a government to establish a National Migration Policy — is directly relevant to Action 23e (bilateral, regional or multilateral partnerships that develop targeted solutions to migration policy issues) and Action 23a (supporting states in implementing migration governance frameworks). The evaluation appears to examine how IOM, as an international organization, supported Senegal in building national migration governance capacity, which is a concrete example of international technical cooperation for migration governance. The recommendations to align the NMP with the Global Compact for Migration and SDGs, and the discussion of IOM's role in supporting political endorsement processes, appear particularly relevant. The lessons identified about policy development processes — including the importance of political buy-in, the use of the Policy Repository Platform, and the development of guidelines for policy-oriented projects — would likely add value to a synthesis on how international cooperation supports national migration governance. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the conclusions and recommendations sections, as well as the lessons identified, which appear to address IOM's institutional role in supporting national migration policy development. It should be noted that the report also contains substantial operational language about coordination that is standard IOM context rather than evidence on global governance mechanisms per se — human reviewers should distinguish between these elements.

References.Title: MIGRANTS RESCUE AND ASSISTANCE IN AGADEZ REGION (MIRAA) – PHASE III
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The evaluation dedicates a substantive section (Section 8 - Data Collection and Monitoring) to critiquing data collection practices, and the recommendations section (E) contains no fewer than nine specific recommendations (13-21) addressing data quality, disaggregation, inter-operability of databases, and analytical capacity. The excerpts suggest the evaluation explicitly addresses the inadequacy of current data disaggregation by gender, age, disability and other vulnerability categories — directly relevant to Actions 1a and 1c. The recommendation to work with a social scientist to improve data collection tools and develop comparable indicators (recommendations 18-20) also touches on Action 1b. The profiling database discussion and the critique of MARS as a data collection tool appear particularly relevant for human reviewers to examine. However, data collection is not the primary focus of the evaluation — it is one of several themes — and the analysis appears to be at the programme/operational level rather than at the national or global statistical system level envisioned by Objective 1. Sections 8 and recommendations E would be the priority areas for a synthesis specialist to review.

References.Title: MIGRANTS RESCUE AND ASSISTANCE IN AGADEZ REGION (MIRAA) – PHASE III
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to contain substantive content relevant to a synthesis on GCM Objective 3, particularly Action 3c (information points along migration routes) and Action 3d (providing newly arrived migrants with information on rights and obligations). The evaluation dedicates Section 1 (Communication/Information as Aid) to the challenge of ensuring beneficiaries receive accurate, timely and accessible information about services, rights, and return processes. The recommendations (Section A) propose concrete audiovisual tools, multilingual content, and reinforced training for security staff as information intermediaries. The excerpts suggest this is analyzed with some depth — including the consequences of poor communication (frustration, violence toward staff) and structural barriers (language diversity, reliance on national delegates). A synthesis specialist examining evidence on information provision to migrants in transit would likely find the communication findings and recommendations sections worth reviewing, though information provision is one of several themes rather than the central focus of the evaluation.

References.Title: MIGRANTS RESCUE AND ASSISTANCE IN AGADEZ REGION (MIRAA) – PHASE III
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The evaluation's core subject — providing protection and assistance to migrants in vulnerable situations along the Niger migration corridor — maps closely onto Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerability), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies including psychosocial support), 7e and 7f (protection of migrant children and unaccompanied minors), and 7j (support for migrants caught in crisis situations). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: vulnerability identification and referral systems in transit centres; psychosocial support provision; protection of unaccompanied minors; gender-responsive approaches; and the particular vulnerabilities of migrants expelled from Algeria. The gender and inclusiveness section (Section 6) and staff support section (Section 7) both contain analysis directly relevant to vulnerability response quality. Recommendations on gender (Section C), staff capacity for child protection (recommendations 8-9), and partner cooperation for psychological support (recommendation 22) are particularly pertinent. A synthesis specialist on Objective 7 should prioritize the protection/vulnerability findings sections and the gender and inclusiveness conclusions. References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly Action 15e on incorporating health needs of migrants in national and local health care policies. The PHAMESA II programme is fundamentally about ensuring migrants and migration-affected communities have access to migrant-sensitive health services — this is explicitly Outcome 3 of the programme ('Migrants and migration-affected communities have access to and use migration-sensitive health services in targeted spaces of vulnerability'). The report appears to provide substantial analysis of: (1) the Service Delivery and Capacity Building (SDCB) Framework for generating demand, enabling supply, and creating an enabling environment for migrant-sensitive health services; (2) barriers to health service access for migrants including discriminatory perceptions, lack of policy recognition, and struggling public healthcare systems; (3) capacity building of health providers; (4) the development of tools to assess migrant-sensitivity of health services (recommendation xiv); and (5) policy and legislative gains for migrant health rights. The Effectiveness section on Outcome 3, the Relevance section on beneficiaries' needs, and recommendations xiv and related findings would be particularly valuable starting points for human reviewers. The report also addresses sexual and reproductive health, communicable diseases, and non-communicable diseases as specific health service areas. This appears to be core evidence for a synthesis on migrant access to health services.

References.Title: EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE MIGRATION HEALTH PROGRAMME
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The evaluation explicitly examines border management capacity-building at Points of Entry (PoE) between Cameroon, Chad, and the CAR — a core theme of this objective. The projects evaluated focus on strengthening border health screening, IPC measures, cross-border coordination, and training of border personnel, which appear directly related to Actions 11a (international border management cooperation, joint cross-border trainings, capacity-building), 11b (integrated border management structures and procedures), and 11g (cross-border collaboration on treatment of persons crossing borders). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: coordination mechanisms through the working group on PoE (Canada II project); training of border health personnel on preparedness and referral protocols; development of joint Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) between Cameroon and CAR; and challenges in cross-border harmonization of COVID-19 testing and IPC standards. The findings and recommendations sections appear particularly relevant — specifically the discussion of coordination gaps, SOP development, and the working group on PoE. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the project descriptions (Section 1.2), the findings on coherence and effectiveness, and the recommendations for the IOM Cameroon Management Team. The report's focus on a specific regional border management context (Central Africa) with documented implementation challenges adds contextual value. Note that the border management lens here is primarily a public health one, which may limit direct applicability to some GCM 11 actions focused on security and irregular migration prevention.

References.Title: EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE MIGRATION HEALTH PROGRAMME
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute some evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12. The evaluation examines health screening and referral procedures at Points of Entry, which appears directly related to Action 12c (gender-responsive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures at borders) and Action 12b (specialized trainings for border officials on identification and referral). The projects include training border health personnel on screening and referral protocols, developing joint SOPs on testing and referrals between Cameroon and CAR, and conducting simulation exercises on proper screening and referral of cases. The Norway project's activity on 'supporting the development of joint Standard Operating Procedures on testing and referrals between Cameroon and the CAR' appears particularly relevant. However, the screening and referral focus here is specifically on infectious disease/public health rather than migration status determination or protection referrals — which may limit direct applicability to some GCM 12 actions. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the project descriptions for Canada I and Norway projects, and the findings on effectiveness and impact related to training outcomes. The excerpts I accessed suggest this is a relevant but not primary source for GCM Objective 12.

References.Title: AN EVALUATION OF IOM’S ENGAGEMENT IN AND CONTRIBUTION TO INTER-STATE CONSULTATION MECHANISMS ON MIGRATION (ISCMS)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 23. The evaluation explicitly examines inter-state consultation mechanisms on migration (ISCMs) as its primary object of inquiry — these are precisely the kinds of multilateral migration governance mechanisms and international cooperation architectures that Objective 23 concerns. The report's stated purpose is to assess IOM's engagement in and contributions to ISCMs, which are described as 'State-led, informal, non-binding dialogues' that promote inter-state cooperation, policy dialogue, and joint approaches to migration governance at regional, inter-regional, and global levels. This directly maps onto Objective 23's focus on international cooperation frameworks and global partnerships for migration governance. The excerpts suggest multiple dimensions of relevance: the report appears to analyze how ISCMs function as cooperation mechanisms (Actions 23b, 23e), IOM's institutional role in supporting multilateral migration governance (Action 23a, 23d), the relationship between ISCMs and broader frameworks like the Global Compact and 2030 Agenda (Action 23b), and the effectiveness of inter-state dialogue in producing convergent migration policies. The executive summary notes that ISCMs 'continue to shape approaches to migration governance among Member States' and that 'many of the recommendations made by the different ISCMs were incorporated into the final text of the Global Compact' — directly relevant to Objective 23's concern with aligning migration governance with international frameworks. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on relevance (IOM's mandate alignment with ISCMs), effectiveness (how ISCMs produce cooperative outcomes among states), outcome and impact (how ISCMs reinforce multilateral migration governance), and the recommendations on IOM's strategic engagement would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The report also appears to cover a wide range of ISCMs across regions (RCM, SACM, MIDWA, Bali Process, PAFoM, GFMD), providing comparative evidence on how international cooperation mechanisms function in practice. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal, particularly in the detailed findings sections.

References.Title: Migrants as Messengers Phase 2- External Final Evaluation
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 3. The 'Migrants as Messengers' project is fundamentally an information and awareness-raising campaign, making it directly relevant to GCM Objective 3's commitment to provide accurate, timely, and accessible information on migration. The project explicitly evaluates how effectively information about irregular migration risks and safe alternatives was disseminated to potential migrants — directly aligning with Action 3e (promoting multilingual, gender-responsive, evidence-based information campaigns and awareness-raising events). The excerpts suggest multiple findings and conclusions directly address: the effectiveness of peer-to-peer communication channels (community engagement, media, social media) for reaching target audiences; the quality and balance of key messages about irregular migration risks versus legal alternatives; the use of local languages and culturally appropriate formats (testimonials, drama); and the role of media training in enabling ethical reporting on migration stories. The recommendation to 'ensure a more consistent and systematic approach to disseminating all the key messages' and to develop 'targeted messages for different audiences' directly addresses information provision quality. The geographic scope (seven West African countries) and the focus on pre-departure information for potential migrants aligns closely with Action 3e. For a synthesis specialist, the findings sections on communication channels, digital content review, and the conclusions on messaging balance would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review.

References.Title: Migrants as Messengers Phase 2- External Final Evaluation
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 17. The MaM project explicitly aims to shape public discourse and perceptions about migration through awareness-raising campaigns — directly aligning with Action 17f (promoting awareness-raising campaigns to inform public perceptions about positive contributions of safe migration) and Action 17c (promoting independent, objective reporting by media outlets, including sensitizing media professionals on migration-related issues). The evaluation appears to substantively assess: the effectiveness of media training in enabling ethical and professional reporting on migration stories; the quality and balance of messaging in digital content produced; the role of peer-to-peer communication in shaping community attitudes toward returnees; and the engagement of community and religious leaders in changing perceptions. The conclusion noting 'improved coexistence and respect within members of the community regarding returnees' and the recommendation to 'engage media through non-transactional partnerships that emphasize the importance of the topic of irregular migration as an issue of public interest' directly address public discourse shaping. The media training component and its evaluation would appear particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist, as would the findings on messaging balance and the use of testimonials. The excerpts suggest this is a substantive secondary theme, though the primary focus is on campaign efficiency and volunteer capacity rather than discrimination elimination per se.

References.Title: Migrants as Messengers Phase 2- External Final Evaluation
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly regarding sustainable reintegration. The MaM project works specifically with returnee migrants as volunteer 'messengers,' making their reintegration experience a central element of the project design. The evaluation appears to substantively assess: the psychosocial support provided to returnee volunteers and its impact on their wellbeing and reintegration; the formation of returnees' associations (16 associations noted, 4 led by female volunteers) as institutional reintegration support; the improvement of community attitudes toward returnees ('improved coexistence and respect'); and the skills and employment prospects gained through project participation. These elements connect to Action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through social protection, psychosocial assistance, vocational training, and employment opportunities) and Action 21b (gender-responsive reintegration programmes). The Most Significant Change findings on individual-level changes for returnees and the sustainability conclusions on returnee associations would appear particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist. However, the report evaluates a communication campaign that uses returnees as volunteers rather than a dedicated return and reintegration programme, so reintegration is a secondary rather than primary focus. Specialists should review the sections on volunteer wellbeing, association formation, and community reception of returnees.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN INTERNA DEL PROYECTO IDF “FORTALECIMIENTO DEL ENFOQUE DE DERECHOS HUMANOS EN LAS POLÍTICAS MIGRATORIAS DE LOS ESTADOS PARTE DEL MERCOSUR Y ESTADOS ASOCIADOS”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project's central theme — strengthening human rights approaches in migration policies for MERCOSUR states — directly relates to protecting migrants in vulnerable situations. The excerpts suggest the studies examined access to social rights for regional migrants and Haitian migrants (a particularly vulnerable population), and the Manual was designed to inform about migrant rights protections. The evaluation discusses how the project addressed (or failed to adequately address) the needs of vulnerable migrant populations, including through the Manual's limitations for direct use by migrants in precarious conditions. Actions 7a (reviewing policies to avoid exacerbating vulnerabilities), 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations), and 7g (access to legal assistance) appear most related. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the sections on project efficacy, the discussion of the Manual's limitations for vulnerable populations, and the sustainability section's assessment of the current policy environment for rights-based migration governance. The full report may contain additional relevant evidence on vulnerability frameworks beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN INTERNA DEL PROYECTO IDF “FORTALECIMIENTO DEL ENFOQUE DE DERECHOS HUMANOS EN LAS POLÍTICAS MIGRATORIAS DE LOS ESTADOS PARTE DEL MERCOSUR Y ESTADOS ASOCIADOS”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. One of the project's core products was a study on 'social rights and public policies' for regional migrants in São Paulo, which directly examines migrants' access to basic services (economic, social and cultural rights) at the local level. The evaluation discusses how this study documented governance of migration in São Paulo including service access across multiple nationalities, and how it contributed to legitimizing a local migration governance model. Actions 15a (non-discriminatory service delivery), 15c (accessible service points at local level), and 15f (inclusive education access) appear most related. The sections on project efficacy — particularly the discussion of the São Paulo study's findings on local governance and service access — and the sustainability section's discussion of the current policy environment would be most valuable for synthesis specialists to review. The excerpts suggest this study may contain substantive empirical evidence on migrant access to social services in a major urban context, though the full report would need to be reviewed to confirm the depth of this analysis.

References.Title: FINAL INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "SUPPORT TO BORDER POLICE IN ADDRESSING INCREASED MIGRATION FLOWS ALONG THE WESTERN BALKAN ROUTE"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The evaluation appears to substantively address the identification and assistance of vulnerable migrants throughout, with IOM mobile teams explicitly tasked to respond to needs of vulnerable migrants — including women with children, unaccompanied minors, elderly and disabled persons. The project's humanitarian border management training component addresses protection standards and specific vulnerabilities (Output 1), and the mobile teams provide direct assistance including health care, transportation, and interpretation (Output 2). The evaluation also notes limitations in rights-based and gender analysis, which is itself relevant evidence for a synthesis. Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerability), 7f (unaccompanied and separated children), and 7k (mobile response teams for identification and referral) appear most directly addressed. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on Output 2 (mobile teams), the cross-cutting issues section on rights and gender, and the conclusions on impact and sustainability of mobile team operations. The excerpts suggest some analytical depth on vulnerability response, though the evaluation acknowledges gaps in systematic vulnerability analysis.

References.Title: FINAL INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "SUPPORT TO BORDER POLICE IN ADDRESSING INCREASED MIGRATION FLOWS ALONG THE WESTERN BALKAN ROUTE"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. Border management is the explicit primary subject of the project and evaluation — the project title itself references 'Border Police' and 'Migration Flows,' and the entire evaluation assesses a border management capacity-building initiative. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly relevant to GCM Objective 11: humanitarian border management training (Actions 11a, 11c, 11g), equipment procurement for border management including surveillance and registration (Action 11d), referral mechanisms for migrants in vulnerable situations at borders (Action 11a), and coordination between IOM and the Border Police Directorate. The evaluation appears to assess effectiveness of border management training, the integration of humanitarian principles into border operations, and the practical application of border management procedures in a mixed migration context. Actions 11a (identification, referral and protection at borders), 11c (screening and individual assessment procedures), and 11d (technical cooperation for equipment) appear most directly addressed. For synthesis specialists, the sections on Output 1 (humanitarian border management trainings), Output 3 (equipment), the conclusions on training effectiveness and institutionalization, and the recommendations on future training needs would appear particularly valuable starting points. The evaluation also touches on the challenge of balancing security and humanitarian considerations at borders, which is central to Objective 11.

References.Title: FINAL INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "SUPPORT TO BORDER POLICE IN ADDRESSING INCREASED MIGRATION FLOWS ALONG THE WESTERN BALKAN ROUTE"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12. The project explicitly focuses on 'processing, registration and referral of migrants and refugees,' which maps directly to the core theme of this objective. The humanitarian border management training component (Output 1) appears to address standardized identification and referral procedures, with trainees reportedly gaining 'clarity on which actors to work with and the respective mandates.' The evaluation discusses the training content covering 'migrant rights, protection standards and specific vulnerabilities' and 'practical ways to apply the learning to concrete situations' — relevant to Actions 12b (specialized training for first responders) and 12c (referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures). The mobile teams also contribute to referral functions. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the Output 1 sections on training content and outcomes, the conclusions on institutionalization of training, and the recommendations on updating practical exercises. The excerpts suggest substantive but not exhaustive coverage of this theme — border management is the dominant frame, with screening/referral as a key component rather than the sole focus.

References.Title: MID-TERM EVALUATION OF THE AU-ILO-IOM JOINT LABOUR MIGRATION PROGRAMME (JLMP)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The evaluation appears to dedicate substantive attention to labour migration data and statistics as a distinct outcome area (Outcome 3), covering capacity building on data collection and analysis, the development of Labour Market Information Systems (LMIS), and collaboration with Statistics Sweden and the Pan African Institute of Statistics. The excerpts suggest analysis of progress toward establishing data collection frameworks, producing disaggregated labour migration statistics, and building national capacities — relating to Actions 1c, 1f, and 1h. The executive summary notes that 'technical capacity of MSs has been strengthened on data collection and analysis' and discusses LMIS development in Cote d'Ivoire. However, data governance appears to be one of four outcome areas rather than the central focus, and the depth of analysis on data-specific findings seems moderate. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the Outcome 3 effectiveness section and related conclusions, which appear to contain the most substantive treatment of this theme. The full report may contain more detail on data capacity building than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: MID-TERM EVALUATION OF THE AU-ILO-IOM JOINT LABOUR MIGRATION PROGRAMME (JLMP)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 18. The evaluation appears to dedicate substantive attention to mutual recognition of skills and qualifications as part of Outcome 2, covering EAC's finalized regulation on Mutual Recognition of Skills (MRS), development of regulations on mutual recognition of professional qualifications, and the envisioned MoU between Nigeria, Togo, and Ghana on harmonization of occupational standards. These directly relate to Actions 18a, 18c, and 18b. The excerpts suggest analysis of both progress achieved and remaining gaps across five RECs and five MSs. The effectiveness section on Outcome 2 and the conclusions section appear to contain the most substantive treatment of this theme. However, skills recognition appears alongside social protection as part of a combined outcome, so the depth of analysis specifically on skills recognition may be somewhat diluted. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the Outcome 2 effectiveness sections and related conclusions and recommendations, which appear to offer evidence on the challenges of implementing mutual recognition frameworks at the regional level in Africa.

References.Title: MID-TERM EVALUATION OF THE AU-ILO-IOM JOINT LABOUR MIGRATION PROGRAMME (JLMP)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 22. Social protection portability and social security for migrant workers appears to be one of the most prominent themes in the evaluation, explicitly addressed under Outcome 2. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: EAC's finalized Regulation on Social Security Benefits Coordination, roadmaps for extending social protection to migrant workers in ECCAS and COMESA, development of social security frameworks and guidelines, and the Migrant Welfare Programme (MWP) compendium. These directly relate to Actions 22a, 22b, and 22c. The evaluation appears to assess both progress and significant remaining gaps across multiple RECs and MSs, offering evidence on the practical challenges of implementing portability frameworks in an African regional context. The effectiveness section on Outcome 2, the conclusions on effectiveness and sustainability, and the recommendations sections appear particularly valuable for synthesis specialists. The Africa-GCC collaboration dimension also adds cross-regional evidence on social protection coordination. It is possible the full report contains additional depth on specific country-level experiences beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Mission d’évaluation finale du projet « Renforcement de la résilience face aux risques liés aux catastrophes naturelles au Burundi »
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1, particularly regarding risk mapping, data collection, and the use of geospatial data platforms for evidence-based policymaking. The project's first component explicitly focused on producing multi-hazard risk maps and making them available through the GeoNode online platform — a data infrastructure initiative directly relevant to Actions 1a, 1c, and 1e. The excerpts indicate that a consortium conducted a comprehensive multi-risk assessment and cartography exercise, and that data was integrated into contingency plans at provincial and communal levels. Recommendations specifically address the sustainability of the GeoNode platform and data renewal mechanisms, suggesting substantive analysis of data governance challenges. The report also appears to discuss capacity-building for national institutions (ISTEEBU, BCG, CNH, IGEBU) in data collection and analysis, touching on Action 1c. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on cartography outcomes, GeoNode platform sustainability, and recommendations on data governance would appear most relevant as starting points. The full report likely contains more detail on the technical aspects of the mapping exercise than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Mission d’évaluation finale du projet « Renforcement de la résilience face aux risques liés aux catastrophes naturelles au Burundi »
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its sub-themes on disaster risk reduction, climate change adaptation, and resilience-building as means to address drivers of displacement. The project's explicit focus on 'Renforcement de la résilience face aux risques liés aux catastrophes naturelles' directly addresses Actions 2b (resilience and disaster risk reduction), 2i (adaptation and resilience strategies to natural disasters), and 2j (integrating displacement considerations into disaster preparedness). The excerpts note that 85% of internal displacements in Burundi were due to natural disaster-related catastrophes, and the project aimed to reduce this through contingency planning, community-level mitigation, and institutional capacity-building aligned with the Sendai Framework — explicitly referenced in the report. The executive summary highlights community resilience outcomes (83% of respondents reporting improved sense of security), mitigation works at scale, and the integration of contingency plans into local planning documents (PTBA). The conclusions and recommendations sections appear to contain substantive analysis of sustainability of DRR gains. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on community resilience outcomes, mitigation activities, contingency planning integration, and the lessons learned section would be particularly valuable starting points. The report's alignment with the Sendai Framework (Action 2a) and its treatment of displacement drivers (Action 2i/2j) make it a strong candidate for in-depth review.

References.Title: EVALUATION FINAL DU PROJET « COMPRENDRE ET TRAITER LES FACTEURS DE CONFLITS LE LONG DES ROUTES MIGRATOIRES D’AGADEZ »
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. Social cohesion between migrants in transit and host communities is the explicit primary subject of the project and evaluation.
