The project title itself — 'Comprendre et Traiter les facteurs de conflits le long des routes migratoires d'Agadez' — directly addresses inter-community relations. The evaluation assesses outcomes on: (1) improved relations between transit migrants and host communities (Résultat 1); (2) economic inclusion through subventions for youth and women (Résultat 2); (3) changes in community perceptions of migrants (46.7% of individual beneficiaries report positive perceptions vs. 27.6% in the broader community); (4) intercultural dialogue and sensitization activities; and (5) gender-responsive programming (women as 38.29% of beneficiaries). These directly relate to Actions 16a (mutual respect and social cohesion), 16c (national policy goals for inclusion), 16d (labour market integration), 16e (empowerment of migrant women), and 16f (community centres and intercultural dialogue). The conclusion and impact sections appear particularly valuable for human reviewers, as they contain direct assessments of social cohesion outcomes and the effectiveness of economic empowerment as a tool for improving host-migrant relations. The recommendations section also addresses sustainability of social cohesion gains. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal, particularly in the findings sections on each evaluation criterion.

References.Title: EVALUATION FINAL DU PROJET « COMPRENDRE ET TRAITER LES FACTEURS DE CONFLITS LE LONG DES ROUTES MIGRATOIRES D’AGADEZ »
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 17. The project explicitly addresses perceptions of migrants in host communities and includes sensitization and awareness-raising activities aimed at reducing tensions and changing attitudes toward migrants in transit. The evaluation measures shifts in community perceptions (46.7% of beneficiaries vs. 27.6% in the broader community report positive perceptions of migrants), which relates directly to Action 17f (awareness-raising campaigns to promote positive perceptions of migration) and Action 17g (engaging community leaders to prevent intolerance). The media and civil society organizations are mentioned as project beneficiaries, touching on Action 17c (promoting objective reporting). However, the report does not appear to focus on discrimination as a legal or rights-based issue, hate crimes, or formal anti-discrimination mechanisms — the framing is more about conflict reduction and social cohesion than discrimination per se. The impact section on perception changes and the sensitization activities would be the most relevant sections for a synthesis specialist to review, though they should note that discrimination is not the central analytical frame of the evaluation.

References.Title: MIGRANTS AS MESSENGERS': THE IMPACT OF PEER TO PEER COMMUNICATION ON POTENTIAL MIGRANTS IN SENEGAL
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 3. The entire evaluation appears to be centered on the effectiveness of a migration information campaign (Migrants as Messengers) designed to provide accurate information to potential migrants about the risks of irregular migration — directly corresponding to Action 3e ('promote multi-lingual, gender-responsive and evidence-based information campaigns and organize awareness-raising events'). The report appears to provide rigorous causal evidence (via RCT) on whether information campaigns change knowledge, risk perception, and migration intentions — core questions for any synthesis on Objective 3. Key contributions likely include: (1) evidence that peer-to-peer information delivery is more effective than top-down approaches; (2) quantified effects on self-reported information levels (+19%), risk awareness (+25%), and irregular migration intention (-20%); (3) analysis of information gaps among potential migrants (one in three not well-informed); (4) recommendations on follow-up communication channels and targeting. The executive summary, sections 1.2–1.3 (misinformation and evidence gaps), Chapter 4 (impact results), and Chapter 5 (lessons learned) appear most relevant for human reviewers. Action 3e is most directly addressed, with some relevance to 3c (information points along routes) and 3d (targeted information for migrants). The pioneering use of RCT methodology in this field adds particular value for evidence synthesis.

References.Title: Proyecto "Apoyo a la integración socioeconómica y fortalecimiento de los sistemas de salud y de las organizaciones de la sociedad civil en el contexto de COVID -19 y su impacto en la población venezolana y receptora"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The entire project evaluated is explicitly designed to improve access to basic services — specifically health (vaccination, sexual and reproductive health) and education — for Venezuelan migrants and host communities in Colombia. This theme appears in the project title, the evaluation questions, and throughout the findings and conclusions. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: barriers to service access for migrant populations (Actions 15e and 15f); health system strengthening for migrant-inclusive service delivery including vaccination and SRR services (Action 15e); inclusive education provision for migrant children and adolescents including infrastructure, school kits, transport, and feeding programmes (Action 15f); and the role of community networks and local health facilities in facilitating access. The conclusions section appears particularly rich, discussing quality improvements in health services, humanization of care for migrants, and educational integration outcomes. The recommendations section also directly addresses sustainability of service access gains. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on Resultado 1 (education component) and Resultado 2 (health component), as well as the conclusions and recommendations, would appear to be the most valuable starting points for deeper review. The report likely offers concrete evidence on what works in migrant-inclusive service delivery in a Latin American context, including under COVID-19 constraints — a relatively underrepresented context in the evidence base.

References.Title: Proyecto "Apoyo a la integración socioeconómica y fortalecimiento de los sistemas de salud y de las organizaciones de la sociedad civil en el contexto de COVID -19 y su impacto en la población venezolana y receptora"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. Social cohesion and integration between Venezuelan migrants and host communities appears to be a central theme of the project, particularly in the education component. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: intercultural integration activities in schools (Action 16h — multicultural activities; Action 16i — welcoming school environments); anti-xenophobia and anti-discrimination programming (Action 16i); psychosocial support and cultural sensitivity training for teachers and students; and outcomes related to migrant children's participation and belonging in educational settings. The conclusions section discusses 'cambio de conciencia cultural' (cultural consciousness change), increased female participation in school governance, and reduced xenophobia — all directly relevant to Actions 16a, 16f, 16h, and 16i. The report appears to offer evidence on both the design and outcomes of integration-focused programming in a context of large-scale Venezuelan migration. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the conclusions on integration outcomes and the recommendations on sustaining cultural transformation programmes, though they should note that social cohesion is one of two major themes alongside health service access, and the depth of analysis on integration may be somewhat limited by the evaluation's dual focus.

References.Title: Proyecto "Apoyo a la integración socioeconómica y fortalecimiento de los sistemas de salud y de las organizaciones de la sociedad civil en el contexto de COVID -19 y su impacto en la población venezolana y receptora"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute some evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 17. The project explicitly addresses xenophobia and discrimination against Venezuelan migrants in Colombian schools, and the evaluation appears to assess outcomes in this area. The conclusions discuss evidence of reduced xenophobia, increased empathy, and cultural transformation in educational institutions — relevant to Actions 17f (awareness-raising campaigns) and 17g (engaging educators and community leaders to prevent intolerance). The report also discusses gender discrimination reduction and increased participation of female students. However, anti-discrimination work appears to be a component of the broader education integration programme rather than a standalone focus, and the analysis of discrimination outcomes seems qualitative and relatively brief in the excerpts. The sections on conclusions regarding xenophobia and gender impacts, and the recommendations on sustaining cultural transformation, would be the most relevant for a synthesis specialist to review, with the caveat that this is not the primary analytical focus of the evaluation.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation: Enhancing the Reintegration Programme for Overseas Filipino Workers in line with the Migration and Development, and Crisis Management Frameworks in the Philippines
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly its reintegration dimension. The evaluation explicitly examines a project designed to enhance the reintegration programme for Overseas Filipino Workers — reintegration is the central and primary subject of the entire report. The project title, evaluation objectives, Results Matrix, findings, conclusions, and recommendations all directly address sustainable reintegration of returning migrants. This aligns closely with GCM Objective 21 Action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through social protection, vocational training, employment, and financial services) and Action 21b (gender-responsive return and reintegration programmes). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: (1) policy-level reintegration frameworks (National Masterplan, Department Orders, Reintegration Summit Declaration); (2) service delivery improvements for returning OFWs (duty bearer training, handbooks, services menu); (3) institutional capacity building (ISO certification, OWWA-NRCO); and (4) sustainability of reintegration outcomes. The evaluation's OECD-DAC criteria assessment (relevance, effectiveness, efficiency, impact, sustainability) applied specifically to a reintegration programme would likely provide rich evidence for a synthesis on this objective. For human reviewers, the sections on effectiveness (Outcome 1 and 2 achievements), impact, sustainability, and the lessons identified would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The full report likely contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal, particularly in the findings sections (pages not fully excerpted).

References.Title: Informe de Evaluación Final USAID Tropical Storm IDA Reconstruction Project JMPL Schools
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly around actions 2b (investing in education and resilience) and 2i (developing resilience strategies to disasters). The project explicitly frames improved access to quality education as a means to reduce irregular migration drivers, noting that 'access to educational opportunities is essential to keep at-risk youth away from violence and irregular migration.' The report also integrates disaster risk reduction as a structural resilience-building measure. The executive summary and findings sections appear to contain substantive analysis linking educational infrastructure investment to reduced push factors for migration. The conclusions and recommendations sections — particularly those addressing the INJMPL's role in keeping youth away from violence and irregular migration — would be most relevant for a synthesis specialist to review. The report appears to treat education access and disaster resilience as intertwined structural factors, which aligns well with Actions 2b and 2i. Worth noting that this is one of several themes and the primary focus is infrastructure reconstruction rather than migration drivers per se.

References.Title: MDG ACHIEVEMENT FUND : SUPPORTING GENDER EQUALITY AND WOMEN'S EMPOWERMENT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Human trafficking is an explicit and substantive focus of the joint programme, with the JP described as having driven the consultation and drafting process for a proposed Law against Human Trafficking in Timor-Leste. The report discusses Timor-Leste as a destination and source country for sex trafficking, the development of standard operating procedures (SOPs) for police on human trafficking, capacity building for government officials and NGOs, referral mechanisms for trafficking victims, and IEC campaigns. These elements align closely with actions 10a (ratification/implementation of the Palermo Protocol — explicitly mentioned), 10c (monitoring irregular migration routes exploited by trafficking networks), 10e (measures addressing vulnerabilities of trafficking victims), 10g (strengthening legislation and prosecution), 10h (protection and assistance to trafficking victims), and 10i (training programmes to identify trafficking). The sections on Outcome 1 (legal frameworks) and Outcome 2 (referral mechanisms and support services) appear most directly relevant. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review these sections, along with the conclusions on sustainability of anti-trafficking measures and the lessons learned on capacity building. The report's focus on a fragile, post-conflict context adds contextual value. Note that the excerpts suggest the anti-trafficking law was still pending approval at time of evaluation, which may limit some conclusions.

References.Title: Final Evaluation of Technical Support to Government of Nepal to implement Disaster Risk Reduction and Management (DRRM) Act
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its actions related to disaster risk reduction, resilience, and the Sendai Framework. The project explicitly supports Nepal's implementation of the DRRM Act 2017, which aligns directly with Action 2a (promoting implementation of the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction 2015-2030) and Action 2b (investing in resilience and disaster risk reduction). The report notes that project activities align with the Sendai Framework's priorities, and the project contributed to formulation of disaster risk reduction policies, regulations, and institutional arrangements at federal, provincial, and local levels. Actions 2c (strengthening early warning systems and emergency procedures), 2i (developing adaptation and resilience strategies), and 2j (integrating displacement considerations into disaster preparedness strategies) also appear touched upon through the project's work on early warning, preparedness plans, and emergency operation centers. However, the report does not explicitly frame these activities in terms of reducing drivers of migration — the connection is implicit rather than direct. The sections on project impacts (federal, provincial, and local level policy changes) and the operational recommendations on early warning, risk assessment, and disaster preparedness would be most relevant for human reviewers to examine.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION “Enhancing Protection and Improving Knowledge on the Risks of Irregular Migration in Sudan - Phase V”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The entire project is explicitly designed to address the vulnerabilities of migrants in Sudan — including victims of trafficking, unaccompanied children, stranded migrants, and those in administrative detention. The project title, objectives, and evaluation questions are all centered on protecting vulnerable migrants and reducing their vulnerabilities. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions relevant to GCM Objective 7: identification and assistance of vulnerable migrants (Actions 7b, 7k); gender-responsive approaches (Action 7c); psychosocial support and medical assistance (Action 7b); support to unaccompanied migrant children (Action 7f); access to legal assistance (Action 7g); and community-based protection activities (Action 7k). The evaluation's conclusions on effectiveness, impact, and sustainability all directly engage with how well the project reduced migrant vulnerability. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on MRRC service delivery, the conclusions on effectiveness (including the discussion of medical assistance, AVRR, and community safe houses), and the recommendations on multi-stakeholder referral mechanisms and self-reliance among migrant communities would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional evidence on vulnerability identification and response beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION “Enhancing Protection and Improving Knowledge on the Risks of Irregular Migration in Sudan - Phase V”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Trafficking in persons (TIP) is explicitly referenced as a key concern throughout the project — the MRRC serves victims of trafficking (VoTs), and the project works with the Counter Trafficking Unit (CTU) and the National Committee to Combat Trafficking (NCCT). The evaluation appears to assess the project's effectiveness in identifying and assisting VoTs, which relates to Actions 10e (measures for victims of trafficking) and 10h (protection and assistance to victims). The project's community outreach and awareness-raising components also touch on Action 10j (awareness-raising campaigns on risks of trafficking). However, the report's primary focus is on broader migrant protection and assistance rather than anti-trafficking specifically, and the excerpts do not reveal deep analysis of prosecution, law enforcement cooperation, or trafficking network disruption. A synthesis specialist would likely want to review the sections on MRRC service delivery to VoTs, the discussion of referral mechanisms, and the conclusions on effectiveness — framing these as pointers for further review rather than definitive characterizations of the full report's anti-trafficking content.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION “Enhancing Protection and Improving Knowledge on the Risks of Irregular Migration in Sudan - Phase V”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The MRRC functions explicitly as a 'one stop shop' for providing vulnerable migrants with basic services — medical assistance, psychosocial support (PSS), food, non-food items (NFIs), and legal orientation. The evaluation assesses the effectiveness and efficiency of this service delivery model in considerable depth. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: health service access for migrants (Action 15e), including challenges such as soaring medical costs, suspension of medical testing, and referral to private medical centres; holistic service points at local level (Action 15c); and the challenges of maintaining service access during political and economic crises. The conclusions on effectiveness discuss service delivery outcomes, backlogs, and the impact of external factors on service provision. The recommendations on creating a different model of service delivery and promoting self-reliance among migrant communities are also relevant. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on MRRC service delivery effectiveness, the discussion of medical assistance challenges, and the conclusions on sustainability of service provision would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION “Enhancing Protection and Improving Knowledge on the Risks of Irregular Migration in Sudan - Phase V”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Assisted Voluntary Return and Reintegration (AVRR) is explicitly identified as one of the two key achievements of the project with potential for long-lasting impact. The evaluation assesses the AVRR component in considerable depth across multiple criteria: effectiveness (including delays in the AVRR process, monitoring challenges, and the 6-month post-return assessment gap); impact (sustainability of returned migrants' small businesses and reintegration into communities); and sustainability (the absence of systematic impact evaluation of AVRR). The excerpts suggest analysis relevant to Actions 21b (gender-responsive return and reintegration programmes based on free, prior and informed consent), 21h (facilitating access to social protection, vocational training, employment and financial services for returning migrants), and 21i (addressing needs of communities to which migrants return). The recommendation for a tailored and comprehensive AVRR approach — noting that the standard package is not necessarily responsive to every migrant's needs — would appear particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist. Sections on impact conclusions, effectiveness conclusions regarding AVRR, and the recommendations on AVRR would be priority areas for deeper review. The full report likely contains more detailed AVRR data and analysis beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EXTERNAL INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF COMMIT  PROJECT: FACILITATING THE INTEGRATION OF RESETTLED  REFUGEES IN CROATIA, ITALY, SPAIN AND PORTUGAL
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 3, particularly Action 3e (pre-departure orientation trainings) and Action 3d (providing newly arrived migrants with information on rights and obligations). The COMMIT project's core focus on pre-departure orientation (PDO) tools — including videos, guidelines, and virtual meetings between refugees and host communities — directly relates to providing accurate, timely information to migrants before and after arrival. The evaluation assesses whether these tools generated 'realistic expectations' among refugees about destination countries, which aligns closely with Action 3e's emphasis on promoting safe migration through pre-departure orientation. The executive summary and conclusions sections appear particularly relevant, especially the discussion of EFF1 (PDO tools perceived as useful in building appropriate expectations) and EFF2 (guidelines on vulnerability management raising awareness among refugees about their rights). The recommendations on scaling up PDO tools (R2) and piloting them (R9) would also be of interest to synthesis specialists. This is one of the report's dominant themes, though the focus is specifically on resettled refugees rather than migrants broadly, which slightly limits generalizability.

References.Title: FINAL EXTERNAL INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF COMMIT  PROJECT: FACILITATING THE INTEGRATION OF RESETTLED  REFUGEES IN CROATIA, ITALY, SPAIN AND PORTUGAL
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7, particularly Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in situations of vulnerability), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies addressing particular needs), and 7f (protecting unaccompanied and separated children). The COMMIT project explicitly focuses on vulnerable groups — women, youth, and people with mental health and psychosocial support needs — as a cross-cutting theme throughout PDO and post-arrival phases. The evaluation assesses whether guidelines on gender, youth, and MHPSS improved capacity to address vulnerability situations (EFF2), and whether these tools raised awareness among operators and refugees about rights. The conclusions note that guidelines were 'recognised to be effective by the majority of project's stakeholders in addressing situations of vulnerability.' The recommendations on including LGBTQI-focused PDO sessions (R3) and improving mentor training for vulnerable groups (R6) are also relevant. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the EFF2 findings sections and the conclusions on vulnerability management, as well as the recommendations table. The report appears to offer practical evidence on how vulnerability-sensitive tools can be integrated into resettlement programming, which is relatively specific and actionable for a synthesis.

References.Title: FINAL EXTERNAL INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF COMMIT  PROJECT: FACILITATING THE INTEGRATION OF RESETTLED  REFUGEES IN CROATIA, ITALY, SPAIN AND PORTUGAL
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 16, particularly Actions 16b (pre-departure and post-arrival programmes), 16f (community centres and mentorship programmes), and 16a (promoting mutual respect and social cohesion). The COMMIT project's core objectives — enhancing PDO, systematizing community mentorship schemes, and fostering welcoming communities — map directly onto these actions. The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of mentorship programs in facilitating refugee integration into host communities (EFF4), the impact of sensitization sessions on reducing prejudices in host societies (EFF3), and the linkage between pre-departure and post-arrival phases (EFF1). The conclusions explicitly address whether community mentors facilitated refugee interaction with social contexts, and whether sensitization sessions improved host community reception. Recommendations R4 (systematizing mentorship), R5 (structured sensitization sessions), R6 (including former refugees as mentors), and R8 (NGO cooperation) are directly relevant to Action 16f. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the EFF3 and EFF4 findings sections, the conclusions on mentorship and sensitization, and the recommendations table. The report appears to offer one of the more detailed evaluations of community-based mentorship and sensitization approaches for refugee integration available in the IOM evaluation portfolio.

References.Title: ÉVALUATION FINALE DU PROJET "Direct life-saving and protection assistance for conflict-affected populations in eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project includes a dedicated DTM (Displacement Tracking Matrix) component — Résultat 1 explicitly focuses on collecting and sharing accurate, timely displacement data with humanitarian partners to support coordination and response planning. The evaluation appears to assess the effectiveness of Mobility Tracking Assessments and Emergency Tracking Tool activities (activities 1.1.1 and 1.1.2), and an August 2022 survey measuring the perceived utility of DTM products among partner organizations is mentioned. These elements relate directly to Actions 1a (improving migration data collection and dissemination), 1c (building national capacities in data collection), and 1e (supporting global/regional databases). The evaluation also used disaggregated data collection methods and quantitative surveys. However, data collection is one of several themes covered alongside CCCM, shelter, and WASH, and the excerpts suggest the DTM component may not receive the deepest analytical treatment relative to other components. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the sections on Résultat 1 and the partner survey on DTM utility, as these appear most directly relevant to GCM Objective 1 actions. The full report likely contains more detail on DTM outcomes than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: ÉVALUATION FINALE DU PROJET "Direct life-saving and protection assistance for conflict-affected populations in eastern Democratic Republic of the Congo"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project explicitly targets conflict-affected displaced populations — among the most vulnerable groups — and the evaluation assesses how well the project identified and assisted vulnerable households (including through vulnerability assessments, activity 3.1.4), protected women and girls (gender-differentiated latrines, female-only FGDs), and addressed protection needs. The cross-cutting themes section covers human rights, gender responsiveness, and accountability to affected populations. Recommendations address documentation of vulnerability selection criteria, complaint mechanisms for sensitive cases, and ensuring dignity in service delivery. These relate to Actions 7a (reviewing policies to avoid exacerbating vulnerabilities), 7b (providing support to migrants in vulnerable situations), 7c (gender-responsive policies for women and girls), and 7k (involving local authorities in identification and assistance). The evaluation's focus on IDPs in conflict settings, gender-disaggregated analysis, and protection-oriented recommendations makes it potentially valuable for synthesis on vulnerability. Specialists would want to review the cross-cutting themes sections (gender, human rights, accountability) and the recommendations on vulnerability selection and complaint mechanisms.

References.Title: "Enhancing the protection of Vulnerable migrants in Tunisia through Emergency Assistance and Support to Health Surveillance and Service Providers" and "Enhancing access of stranded migrants in Tunisia to protection and AVRR"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The evaluated projects are explicitly designed to address vulnerabilities of irregular migrants stranded in Tunisia, with the stated objective of enhancing protection of vulnerable migrants through emergency assistance, health services, legal assistance, and support to victims of trafficking. The excerpts suggest multiple findings directly address vulnerability identification, assistance provision, and protection gaps — aligning closely with Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies including health and psychosocial services), 7g (access to legal assistance), 7j (support for migrants in crisis situations), and 7k (involvement of local authorities in identification and referral). The executive summary and conclusions discuss the critical gaps in government protection systems, IOM's role in filling these gaps, and the growing vulnerability of migrants exacerbated by COVID-19. The findings on emergency shelter, health assistance, legal services, and the challenges of serving highly vulnerable populations (including trafficking victims) appear particularly relevant. Sections on Finding 1, Finding 2, and the conclusions on longer-term planning would likely be priority areas for human reviewers. The report also touches on mental health needs and the importance of psychosocial support — a dimension of vulnerability often underexplored in evidence syntheses.

References.Title: "Enhancing the protection of Vulnerable migrants in Tunisia through Emergency Assistance and Support to Health Surveillance and Service Providers" and "Enhancing access of stranded migrants in Tunisia to protection and AVRR"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The evaluated projects explicitly aim to improve access to emergency shelter, food, health services, and legal assistance for irregular migrants — directly aligning with Actions 15c (holistic and accessible service points), 15e (health needs of migrants in national health policies), and 15b (ensuring cooperation between service providers does not exacerbate vulnerabilities). The executive summary and findings discuss IOM's role in filling critical gaps in service provision, the challenges of accessing services for irregular migrants, and the government's limited engagement. Finding 1 on unmet needs for health and protection assistance, Finding 6 on capacity building through health equipment, and the discussion of mental health service gaps appear particularly relevant. The 'Health for all' days initiative and the cultural mediator in Sfax are highlighted as good practices for improving migrant access to services. However, the report evaluates a specific IOM project rather than a national service delivery system, which somewhat limits its generalizability. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the findings on health service provision and the recommendations on longer-term service planning.

References.Title: "Enhancing the protection of Vulnerable migrants in Tunisia through Emergency Assistance and Support to Health Surveillance and Service Providers" and "Enhancing access of stranded migrants in Tunisia to protection and AVRR"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Assisted Voluntary Return and Reintegration (AVRR) is one of the two central pillars of both evaluated projects, alongside emergency assistance. The excerpts indicate that 753 migrants received return assistance and 162 received reintegration support, including 7 victims of trafficking. Finding 9 directly addresses AVRR outcomes and raises questions about sustainability of reintegration — directly relevant to Action 21b (gender-responsive return and reintegration programmes) and Action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration). Recommendation 3 calls for measuring reintegration quality and sustainability, and Recommendation 5 explicitly addresses linking emergency assistance with more sustainable systems and longer-term planning. The evaluation also discusses the government's policy of encouraging AVRR as its primary migration response, and the challenges of ensuring voluntary and informed consent. The sections on Finding 9, Recommendation 3, Recommendation 5, and the conclusions on longer-term planning would appear particularly valuable for human reviewers working on a synthesis of evidence on AVRR effectiveness and reintegration sustainability. The report also raises important methodological questions about how to measure reintegration outcomes — relevant to evidence synthesis methodology. It is possible the full report contains additional AVRR-specific analysis beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: MIDTERM INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE “REGIONAL PROGRAMME AGAINST HUMAN TRAFFICKING (UKRAINE, MOLDOVA, GEORGIA, BELARUS) PHASE II”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The entire programme evaluated — the Regional Programme Against Human Trafficking (RPaHT 2) — is explicitly and comprehensively focused on preventing, combating, and responding to trafficking in persons across four countries (Ukraine, Moldova, Georgia, Belarus). The report appears to directly address virtually all associated actions under GCM Objective 10: prevention campaigns and awareness-raising (Action 10j), strengthening prosecution and legislation (Action 10g), identification and protection of victims (Actions 10e, 10h), national and local training programmes (Action 10i), regional cooperation and information sharing (Action 10d), and policy reform (Action 10b). The evaluation covers all four 'Ps' of anti-trafficking response (prevention, protection, prosecution, partnerships), assesses the effectiveness of NRMs, referral mechanisms, civil society advocacy, and regional knowledge exchange. The executive summary, conclusions, good practices, and recommendations sections all appear highly relevant. Specific sections that human reviewers should prioritize include: the good practices section (youth summer schools, e-learning platforms, private sector collaboration, information campaigns, NRM revision), the conclusions on effectiveness and sustainability, and the recommendations on programme continuation. The report likely contains substantial evidence on what works in regional anti-trafficking programming that would be directly valuable for a GCM Objective 10 synthesis.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF MIDA FINNSOM HEALTH PROJECT, PHASES I-III
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The MIDA FINNSOM Health Project is explicitly and centrally about enabling diaspora health professionals to contribute to sustainable development in their countries of origin through temporary returns — which is the core theme of Objective 19. The project title, evaluation questions, and all major findings directly address diaspora contributions to health system strengthening in Somaliland and Puntland. Multiple actions under Objective 19 appear directly relevant: Action 19c (research on non-financial contributions of migrants and diasporas, including knowledge and skills transfer) is perhaps the most central, as the entire evaluation assesses the impact of diaspora knowledge transfer. Action 19d (facilitating diaspora contributions through government structures and mechanisms, including MIDA coordinators at Ministries of Health) is also directly addressed. Action 19i (enabling diaspora to carry out professional activities and knowledge transfer in home countries) is the operational model of the entire project. Action 19j (building partnerships for knowledge and skills transfer between countries of origin and destination) is also substantively covered. The executive summary, project overview, sections on skills transfer (5.3.1), capacity building (5.3.2), assessment of the MIDA concept (5.4), sustainability (Section 7), and good practices (Section 6) would all appear particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist to review. The report provides rare empirical evidence — including quantitative survey data from both diaspora experts and local staff — on the effectiveness and sustainability of diaspora-led development contributions in a fragile state context. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation: Strengthening Government Capacity in the Development and Implementation of the National Strategic Action Plan on Migration Health in Nepal
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly Action 15e on incorporating health needs of migrants in national and local health care policies and plans. The project's primary purpose — supporting the Government of Nepal in developing a National Migration Health Policy — directly addresses the core commitment of this objective. The excerpts suggest that multiple findings and conclusions directly address health service access for migrants: the National Migration Health Policy was endorsed with domestic budget funding, the PDHA guidelines were developed, the health component of PDOT curriculum was revised, and a government focal point was established within the Ministry of Health and Population (MoHP). The project appears to have mainstreamed migrant health within the national health policy and the 15th development plan. For a synthesis specialist, the impact section (rating: Excellent – 5) and sustainability section would appear particularly valuable as starting points, as they describe the institutionalization of migrant health within national health systems — directly relevant to Action 15e. The findings on government capacity building and the inclusive policy development process would also likely contribute evidence on how to effectively integrate migrant health into national health frameworks. It is possible that the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal, particularly in the detailed findings sections.

References.Title: Impact evaluation of the UN Secretary General’s Peacebuilding Fund-supported  East Darfur Assalaya-Sheiria-Yassin  Triangle of Peace and Coexistence project
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2. The entire evaluation appears centered on a peacebuilding intervention explicitly designed to address structural drivers of displacement and conflict in Darfur — including land disputes, weak governance, lack of basic services, and inter-communal tensions. These align closely with Action 2b (investing in programmes addressing poverty, food security, education, rule of law, good governance, and peaceful societies) and Action 2f (strengthening collaboration between humanitarian and development actors). The project's theory of change, described in Section 2, directly targets conditions that compel people to leave — land conflicts, lack of services, insecurity — and the evaluation measures whether these were reduced. The qualitative results in Section 6.7 explicitly show residents attributing stability and population retention to improved services and conflict resolution. The discussion in Section 7 also engages with the limits of local peacebuilding when national-level drivers remain unaddressed. For a synthesis specialist, Sections 2 (theory of change), 6.1 (conflict results), 6.7 (qualitative results on services and stability), and 7 (discussion of local vs. national drivers) would appear particularly valuable as starting points. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Impact evaluation of the UN Secretary General’s Peacebuilding Fund-supported  East Darfur Assalaya-Sheiria-Yassin  Triangle of Peace and Coexistence project
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. Access to basic services for displaced and returned populations is one of the two primary outcome domains of the evaluated project. Section 6.2 presents quantitative results on satisfaction with sanitation, administrative, water, and health services; Section 6.3 presents results on school enrollment and attendance; and Section 6.7.2 provides qualitative evidence on how improved services contributed to stability and reduced reasons to leave. The project explicitly aimed to make 'quality basic services accessible to all' (Outcome 2 in Section 2), and the evaluation measures whether this was achieved. This aligns with Action 15f (inclusive and equitable quality education to migrant children) and Action 15c (establishing accessible service points). The finding that school enrollment increased by 11 percentage points, with larger effects for girls, and that sanitation and administrative service satisfaction improved significantly, would likely add value to a synthesis on service access for displaced/migrant populations. For a synthesis specialist, Sections 6.2, 6.3, and 6.7.2 would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The report's focus on returnees and IDPs rather than international migrants in the traditional sense is worth noting, but the evidence on service access for displaced populations remains substantively relevant.

References.Title: Impact evaluation of the UN Secretary General’s Peacebuilding Fund-supported  East Darfur Assalaya-Sheiria-Yassin  Triangle of Peace and Coexistence project
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The evaluated project explicitly targeted returnees and IDPs, with 'durable solutions for the return of IDPs and refugees' as Outcome 1 of the project's theory of change (Section 2). The evaluation measures whether conditions for sustainable reintegration were created — including land conflict resolution, service access, and intentions to remain in the village (H4). Section 6.6 disaggregates results by displacement status, and Section 6.5 reports on returnees' intentions to leave. The qualitative results in Section 6.7 describe how improved services and conflict resolution contributed to population retention and stability. This aligns with Action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through access to services, justice, and employment) and Action 21i (addressing needs of communities to which migrants return). The finding that the project reduced land conflicts — a key barrier to durable return — and improved services is directly relevant to reintegration sustainability. For a synthesis specialist, Sections 2 (theory of change on durable solutions), 6.1 (land conflict results), 6.5 (returnee intentions), 6.6 (disaggregation by displacement status), and 6.7 (qualitative evidence on stability) would appear particularly valuable as starting points.

References.Title: FINAL EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE “ENHANCING COOPERATION MEASURES TO EFFECTIVELY COMBAT TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS THROUGH CAPACITY BUILDING AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE IN AZERBAIJAN – PHASE VI (ECMCT)” PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Combating trafficking in persons is the explicit and central subject of the evaluated project — the project title, all evaluation questions, and the entire results framework are organized around anti-trafficking capacity building in Azerbaijan. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly relevant to GCM Objective 10 actions: capacity building for identification and referral of VoTs (Actions 10e, 10i), training of law enforcement, judiciary, health professionals and consular staff (Action 10i), awareness-raising campaigns for migrants and the general public (Action 10j), direct assistance and protection for trafficking victims including shelter, psychosocial support, legal aid and reintegration (Action 10h), development of Standard Operating Procedures for MIA (Action 10e), and strengthening of the National Referral Mechanism (Actions 10c, 10e). The report also addresses institutional capacity development challenges, sustainability of victim support services, and coordination among 40+ actors in the NRM — all highly relevant to a synthesis on this objective. For human reviewers, the executive summary, conclusions, and recommendations sections appear particularly rich, as do the sections on shelter NGO support, the NRM coordination challenges, and the MIA SOP development. The full report likely contains additional evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal, particularly in the findings sections.

References.Title: FINAL EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE “ENHANCING COOPERATION MEASURES TO EFFECTIVELY COMBAT TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS THROUGH CAPACITY BUILDING AND TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE IN AZERBAIJAN – PHASE VI (ECMCT)” PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12, particularly regarding Action 12b (specialized training for first responders and government officials on identification and referral of trafficking victims) and Action 12c (gender-responsive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures). The project explicitly focuses on training judges, prosecutors, health professionals, psychologists, consular staff, and police on identification and referral of VoTs — core themes under this objective. The development of SOPs with the MIA is highlighted as a significant achievement. The report also discusses the NRM as a referral mechanism and the gap between potential and officially identified VoTs, which relates to screening and assessment procedures. However, the report's framing is primarily through the anti-trafficking lens rather than migration procedure management broadly. Human reviewers would likely find the sections on SOP development, training outcomes for first responders, and NRM coordination most relevant for a synthesis on this objective.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "CONTRIBUTING TO THE ACHIEVEMENT OF SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT GOALS RELATED TO MIGRATION MANAGEMENT IN THE DOMINICAN REPUBLIC"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to be fundamentally relevant to GCM Objective 1. The project's core purpose is to help the Dominican Republic identify, define, and measure migration-related indicators within the SDG framework — directly addressing the collection, analysis, and use of migration data for evidence-based policymaking. The excerpts suggest substantive engagement with actions 1a (elaborating strategies for improving migration data), 1c (building national capacities in data collection and analysis), 1d (collecting and analyzing data on migration's effects for the 2030 Agenda), and 1i (enhancing collaboration between migration data units and national statistical offices). The work with ONE (National Office of Statistics) to identify feasible migration indicators, the feasibility study mapping 244 SDG indicators, and the development of an online data collection platform for the MGI all appear directly relevant. The executive summary and background sections (Section 1.2) describing the indicator identification process, the MGI methodology, and the secondment to ONE would likely be the most valuable starting points for a synthesis specialist. The recommendation on documentation handover for long-term data collection (Recommendation 5) also touches on data system sustainability. The report appears to offer concrete operational evidence on how national migration data capacity-building can be structured, which would be valuable for a synthesis on this objective.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "CONTRIBUTING TO THE ACHIEVEMENT OF SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT GOALS RELATED TO MIGRATION MANAGEMENT IN THE DOMINICAN REPUBLIC"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears highly relevant to GCM Objective 19, particularly actions 19a (ensuring full implementation of the 2030 Agenda and fostering positive effects of migration for SDG realization) and 19b (integrating migration into development planning and sectoral policies). The project's explicit purpose is to integrate migration into the Dominican Republic's SDG measurement and reporting framework, and to align migration governance with the National Development Strategy (END). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how migration can be mainstreamed into national development planning processes, how migration-related SDG indicators can be identified and measured, and how government institutions can be supported to track migration's contribution to sustainable development. The executive summary, background section (1.2), and conclusions (Section 3) would likely be most valuable for a synthesis specialist examining how migration is integrated into national SDG implementation. The MGI methodology as a tool for tracking SDG target 10.7 ('well-managed migration policies') is also directly relevant. The report appears to offer practical operational evidence on mainstreaming migration into development planning — a relatively underexplored area in evaluation evidence.

References.Title: INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF IOM PROJECT: ENHANCING PROTECTION AND IMPROVING KNOWLEDGE ON THE RISKS OF IRREGULAR MIGRATION IN SUDAN PHASE II
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The MRRC is explicitly designed to address the needs of vulnerable migrants — including refugees, asylum-seekers, unaccompanied minors, trafficking victims, stranded migrants, and others with multiple vulnerabilities — in a transit context. The project title itself references 'protection' and 'risks of irregular migration,' and the evaluation's primary focus is on how effectively the MRRC identifies and responds to migrant vulnerabilities. Multiple findings directly address vulnerability response: Finding 1 (integrated, appropriate solution to unmet needs of vulnerable migrants), Finding 4 (assistance to migrants and AVRR), Finding 5 (expanding service reach to different communities), and Finding 7 (sustainability of protection activities). The Concluding Comments discuss psycho-social support gaps, intake procedure deficiencies that limit identification of migrants needing mental health services, and the importance of referral networks — all directly relevant to actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerability), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies including counselling), 7k (involving local authorities and stakeholders in identification and referral), and 7l (national policies for migrants in vulnerability). Recommendations 1, 2, 7, and 10 also address protection systems and community engagement. Synthesis specialists should prioritize the Findings sections (particularly Findings 1, 4, 5, 7) and the Concluding Comments for evidence on vulnerability identification, referral mechanisms, and multi-stakeholder protection approaches in a transit migration context.

References.Title: INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF IOM PROJECT: ENHANCING PROTECTION AND IMPROVING KNOWLEDGE ON THE RISKS OF IRREGULAR MIGRATION IN SUDAN PHASE II
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12, particularly regarding referral mechanisms and screening procedures. The evaluation's Concluding Comments extensively discuss the absence of a standardized intake procedure at the MRRC, the lack of uniform intake data, and the limited capacity to identify migrants needing psychological or mental health services — directly relevant to action 12c (gender-responsive and child-sensitive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures). Recommendations 1 and 2 explicitly call for development of SOPs for client management and referrals, and the appointment of a case manager to standardize service provision is highlighted as a key development. Finding 4 notes underutilization of counselling services, which the Concluding Comments link to inadequate screening and self-referral assumptions. These themes align with actions 12b (specialized training for first responders on identification and referral) and 12c (standardized operating procedures for referral). The report appears to offer practical operational evidence on the challenges of establishing effective screening and referral systems in a resource-constrained transit migration context. Synthesis specialists should review the Concluding Comments and Recommendations 1-2 as starting points, noting that this is one of several themes rather than the sole focus.

References.Title: INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF IOM PROJECT: ENHANCING PROTECTION AND IMPROVING KNOWLEDGE ON THE RISKS OF IRREGULAR MIGRATION IN SUDAN PHASE II
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The MRRC is explicitly described as a 'one-stop shop' for basic service provision to migrants who otherwise lack access — directly relevant to action 15c (establishing holistic and accessible service points at local level). The evaluation assesses the breadth and quality of services provided, including medical assistance, counselling, psycho-social support, language classes, and AVRR — touching on actions 15a (non-discriminatory service delivery) and 15e (health needs of migrants). Finding 1 establishes the MRRC as an integrated solution to previously unmet service needs; Findings 4 and 5 assess service uptake and reach; Finding 7 addresses sustainability of service provision. The Concluding Comments discuss gaps in psycho-social and mental health service access, the importance of referral networks with external service providers, and the challenge of ensuring migrants can access the full range of available services. Recommendations 1, 2, and 7 address strengthening service delivery systems and community-based service provision. Synthesis specialists should review Findings 1, 4, 5, and 7, as well as the Concluding Comments on service gaps and the recommendations on SOPs and community engagement, as these appear most directly relevant to evidence on migrant-inclusive service delivery in transit contexts.

References.Title: INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF IOM PROJECT: ENHANCING PROTECTION AND IMPROVING KNOWLEDGE ON THE RISKS OF IRREGULAR MIGRATION IN SUDAN PHASE II
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly regarding Assisted Voluntary Return and Reintegration (AVRR). AVRR is explicitly identified as a key project output, with Finding 4 noting that the project achieved its AVRR targets. The Concluding Comments discuss in detail the challenges encountered in a case involving 41 Nigerian AVRR beneficiaries, including issues of recipient understanding of the scheme, trust, business plan development, and the coordination gap between sending and receiving IOM offices — directly relevant to action 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes based on free, prior and informed consent) and 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration). The report also discusses the premature nature of considering a government exit strategy for MRRC, touching on action 21a (bilateral cooperation frameworks for return). Recommendations related to AVRR and government engagement (Recommendations 4 and 10) provide additional relevant content. Synthesis specialists should review Finding 4, the AVRR-related discussion in the Concluding Comments, and Recommendation 10 as starting points for evidence on AVRR programme implementation challenges and reintegration support in a transit migration context. It should be noted that AVRR is one of several themes rather than the sole focus of the evaluation.

References.Title: MALAWI MIGRATION PROFILE PROJECT - PROJECT EVALUATION REPORT 2017
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The entire project evaluated — the Malawi Migration Profile — is explicitly centered on collecting, consolidating, and disseminating disaggregated migration data to support evidence-based policymaking. The project title, objectives, outputs, and evaluation questions all directly address this theme. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: country-specific migration profile development (Action 1j); capacity building for national data collection and management (Action 1c); sex and age-disaggregated data availability (Actions 1b, 1g); inter-ministerial coordination for data sharing (Action 1i); and the utility of migration data for policymaking (Action 1d). Multiple findings and recommendations in the excerpts directly address data gaps, data quality, dissemination challenges, and institutional capacity for ongoing data collection. For a synthesis specialist, the effectiveness matrix (Section 3.1), the sustainability discussion (Section 3.2), and the recommendations on re-engagement and publicity would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The report also offers evidence on what happens when a migration profile is produced but not adequately promoted — a practical lesson for Action 1j implementation. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Building Facility (WBIBM) & Western Balkans Readmission Capacity Building Facility (WBCAP)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The WBIBM project is explicitly and centrally focused on integrated border management capacity-building across the Western Balkans — this appears in the project title, evaluation questions, and throughout the findings and recommendations. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly relevant to GCM Objective 11's associated actions: Action 11a (international/regional border management cooperation, capacity-building, joint trainings — evident in study visits to EU member states, Frontex partnerships, risk-based training); Action 11b (integrated border management structures, pre-reporting by carriers via API/PNR, ICT use, biometric systems); Action 11c (review of border screening procedures, due process, human rights compliance); Action 11e (child protection at borders, training border officials); and Action 11g (cross-border collaboration on treatment of persons crossing borders). The evaluation covers six Western Balkans countries and assesses both operational outcomes and policy/legal reforms. For a synthesis specialist, the Impact section on WBIBM, the Effectiveness section on target achievement, the Sustainability section on institutionalization of border management reforms, and Recommendations 6, 7, and 8 would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The protection-sensitive and gender-responsive border management dimensions (Humanitarian Border Management curriculum, vulnerability assessment trainings) also directly address the GCM's emphasis on human rights-compliant border management.

References.Title: Building Facility (WBIBM) & Western Balkans Readmission Capacity Building Facility (WBCAP)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12. The WBIBM and WBCAP projects appear to have substantively engaged with screening, assessment and referral mechanisms at borders and in readmission contexts. Relevant elements include: development of SOPs for vulnerable migrants and returnees (Action 12c — gender-responsive and child-sensitive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures); vulnerability assessment trainings for border officials (Action 12b — specialized human rights and trauma-informed trainings for first responders); and the Humanitarian Border Management curriculum development. The eRCMS digital case management system also relates to Action 12a (transparency and accessibility of migration procedures through technology). However, screening and referral appear as components within broader border management and readmission themes rather than as the primary analytical focus. The cross-cutting themes discussion in the Relevance section and the capacity-building findings in the Impact section would be most pertinent for a synthesis specialist to review, with the understanding that this is one of several themes covered rather than the central focus.

References.Title: Building Facility (WBIBM) & Western Balkans Readmission Capacity Building Facility (WBCAP)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The WBCAP project is explicitly and centrally focused on return, readmission and reintegration (RRR) cooperation — appearing in the project title, evaluation questions, and throughout the findings and recommendations. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly relevant to GCM Objective 21's associated actions: Action 21a (bilateral, regional and multilateral cooperation frameworks and readmission agreements — evident in bilateral meetings with CoOs, RGRS platform, readmission negotiations with Bangladesh, Pakistan, Nepal, Iraq, Morocco); Action 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes — SOPs for vulnerable migrants, protection-sensitive return protocols); Action 21c (identification of nationals and biometric identifiers for readmission — eRCMS digital case management, biometric systems); Action 21e (safe and dignified return following individual assessment — rights-based return protocols); and Action 21h (sustainable reintegration — Recommendation 9 on reintegration support). The evaluation covers six Western Balkans countries and assesses both operational outcomes and policy/legal reforms in readmission. For a synthesis specialist, the Impact section on WBCAP, the Effectiveness section on readmission targets, the Sustainability section on institutionalization of readmission frameworks, and Recommendations 9 and 10 would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The eRCMS digital case management system analysis and the RGRS regional coordination platform assessment would likely offer particularly useful evidence on multilateral readmission cooperation mechanisms.

References.Title: INDEPENDENT FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "SUPPORT TO STRENGTHENING MIGRATION AND ASYLUM MANAGEMENT IN SERBIA"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project explicitly targets identification of vulnerabilities and referral of vulnerable migrants to protection services as a core intervention area. Outcome 3 directly addresses 'rights of migrants upheld as a result of improved reception conditions, identification of vulnerabilities and referrals to specialised services,' with Output 3.1 focused on field workers' skills for working with migrants. The capacity building needs assessment identified gaps in 'identification of vulnerable migrants, such as victims of gender-based violence, torture, forced marriage, trafficking and others.' The Training of Trainers (TOT) format for field workers, the field work manual and curriculum on protection of women and children, and the referral mechanisms discussed all relate to Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerability), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies), 7f (unaccompanied and separated children), and 7k (involving local authorities in identification and referral). Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the sections on Outcome 3 findings, the TOT design and outcomes, and recommendations 7 and 8 regarding further capacitating field workers. The excerpts suggest this is one of the report's primary substantive themes, though border management and asylum quality are equally prominent.

References.Title: INDEPENDENT FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "SUPPORT TO STRENGTHENING MIGRATION AND ASYLUM MANAGEMENT IN SERBIA"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12. The project's core focus on strengthening asylum decision-making quality (Outcome 1), improving screening and referral of vulnerable migrants (Outcome 3), and building capacity of government officials in asylum procedures aligns closely with this objective. Specific elements include: training on writing legal asylum decisions (Output 1.1), e-curriculum on asylum jurisprudence (Output 1.2), development of Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs mentioned in acronyms), field work manual and curriculum on identification and referral, and TOT for field workers. These relate directly to Actions 12b (specialized human rights trainings for government officials and first responders), 12c (gender-responsive and child-sensitive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures), and 12e (ensuring relevant information on rights and procedures is communicated in mixed movement contexts). The study visit to Greek reception centers (Output 3.2) also relates to cross-regional learning on screening and referral practices. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the sections on Outcome 1 and Outcome 3 findings, the curriculum and handbook development process, and recommendations 4, 9, and 10 regarding institutionalization and updates related to new asylum laws.

References.Title: Final Internal Evaluation for Enhancing Knowledge on Remittances and Diaspora Engagement in South Sudan
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project's core activity was commissioning a remittances study to address a significant data gap — the Bank of South Sudan had not issued disaggregated data on overseas development assistance since 2013, and there was no tracking mechanism for remittance flows. The evaluation appears to substantively assess the quality, reach, and impact of this data collection effort, including disaggregation by gender and demographic characteristics. The excerpts suggest direct relevance to Action 1d (collecting data on effects and benefits of migration and diaspora contributions to sustainable development) and Action 1j (developing country-specific migration profiles including remittance transfer costs). The unexpected outcome of the Bank of South Sudan implementing a transactional system to capture remittance inflows/outflows — reportedly inspired by the study — is also directly relevant to Action 1i (enhancing collaboration between state units and statistical offices). The evaluation's findings on barriers to data collection (distrust among diaspora, low survey participation), lessons learned on feasibility assessment, and recommendations on communication plans would likely add value to a synthesis on evidence-based migration policymaking. Reviewers should prioritize the Effectiveness section (output 1.1 indicators), the Impact section on unintended effects, and the Lessons Learned section. Note that data collection is a means to an end in this project rather than the primary programmatic focus, which slightly limits depth on the mechanics of data systems.

References.Title: Final Internal Evaluation for Enhancing Knowledge on Remittances and Diaspora Engagement in South Sudan
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The project is explicitly and centrally focused on diaspora engagement and the contribution of the South Sudanese diaspora to development in their country of origin — the core theme of Objective 19. The evaluation appears to assess a project that directly operationalizes multiple associated actions: Action 19d (establishing government structures for diaspora engagement, including a dedicated diaspora desk under the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and a technical advisory working group); Action 19j (mapping diasporas and building partnerships between local authorities, diaspora organizations, and government to promote knowledge transfer); and Action 19c (investing in research on non-financial contributions of migrants and diasporas). The remittances study and policy draft are directly relevant to Action 19b (integrating migration into development planning). The evaluation's substantive analysis of barriers to diaspora engagement (distrust, government turnover, lack of political will), the documented attitude change toward the diaspora, and the unexpected outcome of the Bank of South Sudan implementing a remittance tracking system all provide rich evidence for a synthesis on this objective. Reviewers should prioritize the Relevance section (diaspora engagement rationale), the Impact section (attitude changes, capacity strengthening, unintended effects), the Sustainability section (ownership challenges), and the Recommendations. The Effectiveness section's output indicators on the technical advisory working group and policy proposals are also highly relevant. It is possible the full report contains additional evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Final Internal Evaluation for Enhancing Knowledge on Remittances and Diaspora Engagement in South Sudan
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 20. Remittances are the explicit primary subject of the project evaluated — the project title itself is 'Enhancing Knowledge on Remittances and Diaspora Engagement in South Sudan.' The evaluation substantively assesses a project that directly addresses multiple associated actions: Action 20d (establishing conducive policy and regulatory frameworks for the remittance market — the policy draft is a core output); Action 20f (providing accessible information on remittance transfer costs — the remittances study serves this purpose); and Action 20i (developing banking solutions for migrants — the Bank of South Sudan's new transactional system for tracking remittance inflows/outflows is an unexpected outcome directly linked to this project). The evaluation also addresses gender dimensions of remittances (Action 20h — enabling migrant women to access formal remittance systems, noting that high transfer costs disproportionately affect women). The analysis of barriers to remittance data collection, the policy development process, and the challenges of government endorsement of remittance recommendations all provide substantive evidence for a synthesis on this objective. Reviewers should prioritize the Effectiveness section (output 1.1 and 1.2 indicators), the Impact section (unintended effects on Bank of South Sudan systems), the Sustainability section, and all Recommendations. The Relevance section's discussion of South Sudan as both a sending and receiving country of remittances provides useful contextual framing. It is possible the full report contains additional evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: ÉVALUATION FINALE EXTERNE DU PROJET : RENFORCEMENT DE CAPACITÉS POUR UNE MEILLEURE COORDINATION, PROTECTION ET POURSUITE DE LA TRAITE DES PERSONNES À MADAGASCAR (PROJET CT. 1007)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7, particularly regarding vulnerable migrants who are victims of trafficking. The project explicitly targeted victims/survivors of trafficking — predominantly women and children — and the evaluation assesses the quality and effectiveness of protection and assistance provided to them. The executive summary, effectiveness section, and conclusions discuss identification of vulnerable migrants, referral mechanisms, direct assistance, and the structural weaknesses of the protection system. Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations, including victims of trafficking), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies addressing needs of migrant women and girls), 7e (child protection systems), and 7k (involving local authorities in identification and referral) appear most related. The sections on Result 2 (protection of victims/survivors), the discussion of the national referral mechanism (SOP), and the recommendations on protection approaches would be particularly worth reviewing. However, the report's focus is specifically on trafficking victims rather than vulnerability in migration more broadly, which somewhat limits its scope for a general synthesis on Objective 7.

References.Title: ÉVALUATION FINALE EXTERNE DU PROJET : RENFORCEMENT DE CAPACITÉS POUR UNE MEILLEURE COORDINATION, PROTECTION ET POURSUITE DE LA TRAITE DES PERSONNES À MADAGASCAR (PROJET CT. 1007)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The entire project — and therefore the evaluation — is explicitly and comprehensively focused on combating trafficking in persons in Madagascar. The project title directly references trafficking in persons, and the evaluation covers all major dimensions of GCM Objective 10: prosecution of traffickers (actions 10c, 10d, 10g), protection and assistance to victims (actions 10e, 10h), awareness-raising and prevention (action 10i, 10j), capacity building of law enforcement and judicial actors (action 10i), national coordination mechanisms (BNLTEH), and data collection (action 10d). The evaluation assesses effectiveness of training programs for police, gendarmerie, and justice personnel; the functioning of national referral mechanisms; direct assistance to trafficking victims; and the development of a national database. The executive summary, effectiveness section, impact section, conclusions, and recommendations all directly address GCM Objective 10 themes. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on prosecution capacity building (Result 1), victim protection mechanisms (Result 2), the BNLTEH coordination role (Result 3), and the recommendations on the four pillars (prevention, protection, prosecution, partnership) would be particularly valuable starting points. The evaluation also provides evidence on gender dimensions of trafficking and structural challenges in national anti-trafficking systems in a low-income country context.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN INTERNA (FINAL) Modelo de reincorporación en entornos productivos con exmiembros de FARC-EP
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 18, particularly around skills development programming (Actions 18e, 18g, 18j). The project's core activity is a vocational training programme using a 'learning by doing' methodology, resulting in formal technical certification through SENA (Colombia's national vocational training institution). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of training design, methodology effectiveness, participant satisfaction, and certification outcomes — all directly relevant to skills development evidence. The evaluation assesses whether training prepared participants technically for productive activities, identifies gaps in the training model, and makes recommendations for improving skills development programmes. While the population (ex-combatants) differs from the typical migrant worker context of GCM Objective 18, the programme design, certification mechanisms, and lessons learned about vocational training effectiveness would likely be of interest to synthesis specialists examining skills development models. The sections on efficacy findings (participant satisfaction, technical preparation, methodology assessment) and recommendations on training design (defining courses in advance, including transversal topics, expanding study opportunities) appear most relevant for human reviewers to prioritize.
