The excerpts I accessed may not capture the full depth of skills-related analysis in the complete report.

References.Title: EVALUATION FINAL DU PROJET « APPUI AU RENFORCEMENT DE LA GESTION DE LA MIGRATION AU NIGER—PHASE II »
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project's second output (Extrant 2) is explicitly dedicated to strengthening migration data management capacities — including the establishment of an integrated migration data platform at the INS (Institut National de la Statistique), cartography of data producers and users, and training of migration data producers. The excerpts suggest that multiple findings and conclusions directly address data collection, harmonization, disaggregation, and use for evidence-based policymaking — all core themes under Actions 1a, 1c, 1i, and 1j. The evaluation's impact section notes improvements in data quality, digitalization of migration data, and the availability of disaggregated data for decision-making. Recommendations explicitly address data center infrastructure, synchronization of regional data, and capacity building for data collection actors. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on Extrant 2 outcomes, the impact criterion discussion on data quality improvements, and the recommendations to the donor and state regarding data infrastructure would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible that the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION FINAL DU PROJET « APPUI AU RENFORCEMENT DE LA GESTION DE LA MIGRATION AU NIGER—PHASE II »
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 4. The project's third output explicitly includes support to the DST (Direction de la Surveillance du Territoire) for digitalization of travel documents (laissez-passer/sauf-conduits), and the evaluation notes an increase in laissez-passer issued between 2020-2022. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of consular documentation processes, digitalization of travel documents, and support to consular services for issuing travel documents to returning migrants — relating to Actions 4b, 4c, and 4d. Recommendations to the donor include supporting e-visa implementation and a data center for the DGECM-R. However, documentation appears as one of several themes rather than the sole focus. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the sections on Extrant 3 outcomes, the impact discussion on laissez-passer issuance, and the recommendations on document digitalization, framing these as pointers for further review rather than definitive characterizations.

References.Title: EVALUATION FINAL DU PROJET « APPUI AU RENFORCEMENT DE LA GESTION DE LA MIGRATION AU NIGER—PHASE II »
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The project's third output is explicitly dedicated to strengthening coordination capacities for voluntary assisted returns (Retour Volontaire Assisté — RVA), including support to national and regional actors, consular services of countries of origin, and institutions handling unaccompanied minors in return contexts. The evaluation notes the digitalization of laissez-passer issuance, increased document production for returning migrants, periodic coordination meetings among return actors, and technical support to consular services — all directly relevant to Actions 21b, 21c, 21d, and 21h. The conclusions section dedicates substantial discussion to return coordination outcomes. Recommendations address consular documentation, identification of migrants, and support for return-related services. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on Extrant 3 outcomes, the impact discussion on laissez-passer issuance, the conclusions on return coordination, and the recommendations on consular and institutional support for returns would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible that the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: MIGRATION CRISIS SUPPORT TO ADDRESS MIGRANT EXPULSIONS IN WESTERN TANZANIA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears highly relevant for a synthesis on GCM Objective 4. The central activity of the evaluated project was the biometric and biographic e-registration of irregular migrants, resulting in the issuance of PVC registration cards that provided protection from deportation and enabled access to rights and services. This directly relates to action 4g (issuance of registration cards to persons living in a municipality, including migrants) and action 4a (improving civil registry systems and investing in ICT solutions). The evaluation discusses in depth the design, implementation challenges, and outcomes of the e-registration system — including coverage gaps, errors of inclusion, and the transformative impact of the registration card on migrants' freedom of movement and access to services. The sections on Outcome 3, Outcome 4, and the Value Added discussion appear particularly relevant for human reviewers. The evaluation also addresses the development of SOPs for registration and the training of ISD officials, which may contribute evidence on institutional capacity for documentation. This report would likely be valuable for a synthesis specialist examining migrant documentation systems and their protection impacts in sub-Saharan Africa.

References.Title: MIGRATION CRISIS SUPPORT TO ADDRESS MIGRANT EXPULSIONS IN WESTERN TANZANIA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears relevant for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project was explicitly designed as a humanitarian response to protect vulnerable irregular migrants facing forced expulsion, and the evaluation assesses outcomes related to migrant protection, human rights, and reduction of abuse. The evaluation discusses: the shift from forced expulsion to protection-sensitive approaches (Outcome 1); reduced abuse of irregular migrants by border officials following HBM training (Outcome 2); the protection afforded by the PVC registration card (Outcome 4); and specific attention to child protection best practices (Section 5.3). These elements relate to actions 7a (reviewing policies to avoid exacerbating vulnerabilities), 7b (providing support to migrants in vulnerable situations), 7e (accounting for migrant children in protection systems), and 7j (support for migrants caught in crisis situations). The sections on project effectiveness, value added, and best practices appear most relevant for human reviewers. The evaluation's treatment of how registration cards transformed migrants' access to rights and protection from harassment is particularly noteworthy for a synthesis on vulnerability reduction.

References.Title: MIGRATION CRISIS SUPPORT TO ADDRESS MIGRANT EXPULSIONS IN WESTERN TANZANIA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to be core evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. Humanitarian Border Management (HBM) is one of the two central pillars of the evaluated project, alongside migrant registration. The evaluation assesses: HBM capacity assessments conducted in multiple countries (Outcome 2); training of border officials in HBM principles; development of Standard Operating Procedures for border management; resuscitation of Border Management Committees; and behavior change among immigration officials in their treatment of irregular migrants. These elements directly relate to actions 11a (international border management cooperation and capacity-building), 11c (reviewing border screening procedures to ensure due process), and 11g (cross-border collaboration on treatment of persons crossing borders). The evaluation also addresses the tension between state sovereignty and humanitarian principles in border management — a core theme of Objective 11. Sections on Outcome 2, the project's effectiveness assessment, and the lessons learned chapter appear particularly valuable for human reviewers. The report's evidence on how HBM training translated into behavioral change among border officials would likely be especially useful for a synthesis on this objective.

References.Title: MIGRATION CRISIS SUPPORT TO ADDRESS MIGRANT EXPULSIONS IN WESTERN TANZANIA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears relevant for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The project included an Assisted Voluntary Return (AVR) component for former Burundian refugees and irregular migrants, and the evaluation assesses progress toward safe and dignified return management. Key themes include: the shift from forced expulsion (Operation Kimbunga) to voluntary, protection-sensitive return approaches (directly relevant to action 21e on ensuring return follows individual assessment and due process); the development of SOPs for returns; and the recommendation to link the project to post-return/integration programming in recipient countries (Recommendation 2 in programmatic recommendations). The evaluation also discusses the prohibition of collective expulsion as a principle underpinning the project. However, the AVR component appears to have been less fully implemented than the registration component, and reintegration programming is noted as absent rather than analyzed in depth. The sections on Outcome 1, the conclusions on effectiveness, and the programmatic recommendations appear most relevant for human reviewers. The report's evidence on transitioning from forced to voluntary return in a humanitarian crisis context would likely contribute to a synthesis on this objective.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION: TECHNICAL SUPPORT AND CAPACITY BUILDING TO IMPROVE CROSS-BORDER TB CONTROL AND CARE OF TAJIK MIGRANT WORKERS
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly Action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in national health care policies and plans). The project's core focus is on improving TB health services and care for Tajik migrant workers — a population explicitly identified as facing inequalities in health service provision. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: capacity building for health service delivery (Clinic Expert Commission), pre-departure health assessments, cross-border health care coordination, and policy-level contributions to migrant health. The evaluation assesses effectiveness, impact, and sustainability of these health access improvements. The lessons identified note the holistic approach from policy level to practical technical support as a success factor. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on effectiveness (health service capacity building outcomes), impact (cooperation between state structures, pre-departure health assessment improvements), and sustainability (integration into state institutions) would appear most relevant as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional evidence on migrant health service access beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATION FOR MIGRATION'S ONGOING ACTIVITIES ON SUPPORT TO THE FLASH APPEAL FOR THE HAITI EARTHQUAKE AND CHOLERA OUTBREAK
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The evaluation covers IOM's delivery of basic services — particularly WASH (Water, Sanitation and Hygiene), shelter, and psychosocial support — to internally displaced persons in post-earthquake Haiti. The report appears to substantively analyze service delivery effectiveness, sustainability challenges, and the risks of service gaps as funding diminishes. The executive summary explicitly flags the need to preserve 'life-saving services, especially in the case of WASH and shelter,' and Section 4.5 on sustainability discusses the challenge of maintaining essential services as a 'provider of last resort.' This resonates with GCM actions 15c (holistic accessible service points) and 15e (health needs of migrants in health care policies). The report's discussion of community mobilization teams as frontline service delivery mechanisms and the analysis of service continuity risks during camp closure transitions would likely be of interest to synthesis specialists examining how basic services are maintained for displaced/migrant populations in crisis contexts. Reviewers should particularly examine Sections 4.2 (Effectiveness), 4.3 (Efficiency), and 4.5 (Sustainability) for relevant content. Note that the population served is IDPs rather than international migrants per se, which may limit direct applicability depending on the synthesis scope.

References.Title: PARTNERSHIP ON HEALTH AND MOBILITY IN THE MINING SECTOR PROJECT (2013-16) IN SOUTH AFRICA, LESOTHO, SWAZILAND AND MOZAMBIQUE END OF PROJECT EVALUATION REPORT FINAL REPORT PART I
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly regarding health service access for migrants (Action 15e). The evaluation appears to explicitly examine a health service delivery program for migrant mineworkers, with 'migrant sensitive health service delivery' named as a core project objective. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of barriers to health service access (poverty, transport costs, stigma, treatment adherence challenges), the role of Change Agents in generating demand for health services, and the introduction of new TB services in Lesotho and Swaziland. The conclusion section notes that 'a demand for health services was increased' but that 'considerable barriers to access... associated with poverty' remain. The project's alignment with the WHA Resolution on 'Right to Health for Migrants' and the IOM Service Delivery and Capacity Building Framework directly maps to Action 15e's call to 'incorporate the health needs of migrants in national and local health care policies and plans.' Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the service delivery findings sections (Outcome 3 discussion), the local-level conclusions on Change Agents, and the recommendations on health system strengthening. The full report likely contains more detailed country-level evidence on migrant health service access than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Midterm Evaluation of IOM East and Horn of Africa Regional Strategy 2020 – 2024
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 23, though with important caveats. The evaluation's primary subject — the IOM EHoA Regional Strategy 2020-2024 — is itself a regional governance and cooperation instrument, and the evaluation explicitly examines how IOM positions itself as a strategic actor within multilateral migration governance frameworks. This goes beyond ambient operational language: the evaluation assesses IOM's institutional role in shaping regional migration governance, its partnerships with RECs (IGAD, EAC, African Union), its alignment with global frameworks (GCM, SDGs, Kampala Declaration), and its capacity to influence migration policy at regional and national levels. These themes relate substantively to actions 23a (supporting collective GCM implementation), 23b (regional cooperation to address structural migration drivers), and 23e (bilateral/regional partnerships on migration policy). The excerpts suggest that the evaluation examines the Regional Strategy as a mechanism for operationalizing international cooperation — including MoUs with IGAD and EAC, joint regional programmes, and IOM's role within UNCTs and with Member States. The conclusions and recommendations sections appear particularly relevant, discussing IOM's leadership role, partnership consolidation, and alignment with international frameworks. A synthesis specialist would want to review the conclusions on IOM's regional governance role and the recommendations on partnership consolidation and framework coherence, while noting that the report's primary focus is on IOM's internal strategic planning effectiveness rather than on inter-state cooperation architecture per se. The full report likely contains more on this topic than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: BETWEEN TWO SOCIETIES : REVIEW OF THE INFORMATION, RETURN AND REINTEGRATION OF IRAQI NATIONALS TO IRAQ (IRRINI) PROGRAMME
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 3. Information provision is a dedicated component of the IRRINI programme and appears prominently in the evaluation's scope, conclusions, and recommendations. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how information about return is conveyed to potential returnees — including the quality, timing, format, and accessibility of information — relating closely to Actions 3c (information points along migration routes) and 3e (pre-departure orientation). Key observations include that returnees are not mentally prepared to absorb information until they have decided to return, that acronym-heavy and fragmented presentations reduce comprehension, and that information about cash vs. in-kind components is insufficiently clear. Recommendations on improving reception centre presentations, using digital tools (blogs, Facebook), and audio recordings of presentations appear directly relevant. Sections 12.1 and 13.1 ('Information about return' and 'Improve how information on IRRINI is provided') would be particularly valuable starting points for human reviewers. The report's focus is on return-stage information rather than information across the full migration cycle, which somewhat limits its breadth for this objective.

References.Title: BETWEEN TWO SOCIETIES : REVIEW OF THE INFORMATION, RETURN AND REINTEGRATION OF IRAQI NATIONALS TO IRAQ (IRRINI) PROGRAMME
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The IRRINI programme — voluntary return and reintegration of Iraqi nationals to Iraq — is the explicit and sole subject of this evaluation. The report's title, evaluation questions, findings, conclusions, and recommendations all center on the themes of safe and dignified return and sustainable reintegration, which are the core commitments of Objective 21. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple associated actions: Action 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes with free, prior and informed consent) is directly addressed through analysis of voluntariness perceptions, cash grants, and reintegration support; Action 21h (sustainable reintegration through social protection, vocational training, employment, and financial services) is extensively analyzed through sections on the cash component, reintegration support, business establishment, and follow-up; Action 21i (addressing needs of return communities) is touched upon in recommendations. The report also engages with the concept of 'safe and dignified return' explicitly, citing UNHCR and ICRC frameworks. Key sections for human reviewers include the executive summary, sections 12.1–12.5 (conclusions on all programme components), and section 13 (recommendations), particularly 13.1 and 13.2. The analysis of what makes reintegration sustainable or unsustainable — including the role of social networks, employment conditions, programme design, and transnational feedback loops — would likely add substantial value to an evidence synthesis on this objective.

References.Title: MID TERM REVIEW OF EU-­IOM JOINT INITIATIVE FOR MIGRANT PROTECTION AND REINTEGRATION IN THE HORN OF AFRICA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The entire programme evaluated — the EU-IOM Joint Initiative for Migrant Protection and Reintegration in the Horn of Africa — is explicitly and primarily focused on safe, dignified voluntary return and sustainable reintegration. This is the central subject of the evaluation, appearing in the programme title, all evaluation objectives, and throughout the findings and conclusions. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis directly addressing Actions 21b (gender-responsive return and reintegration programmes with free, prior and informed consent), 21h (sustainable reintegration through social protection, psychosocial assistance, vocational training, employment, and financial services), and 21i (addressing needs of communities to which migrants return). Key areas of likely value for a synthesis include: the analysis of AVRR programme effectiveness and challenges (waiting times, bottlenecks); the assessment of economic reintegration approaches (over-reliance on business start-ups, need for diversification); the discussion of community-based reintegration as a complement to individual assistance; the evaluation of MRC service delivery infrastructure; the analysis of government partnership and national ownership challenges; and the recommendations on sustainable reintegration beyond the programme period. The conclusions section and recommendations sections appear particularly rich for synthesis purposes. Human reviewers should prioritize the findings on reintegration effectiveness, community-based approaches, and sustainability — as well as the country-specific analysis for Djibouti, Ethiopia, Somalia, and Sudan.

References.Title: EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF AN IDF REGIONAL PROJECT: STRENGTHENING CAPACITY FOR THE PROMOTION AND PROTECTION OF HUMAN RIGHTS OF MIGRANTS IN A VULNERABLE SITUATION IN SOUTH AMERICA (PRODESHA)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project's explicit focus on 'migrants in a vulnerable situation' appears throughout the report title, project objectives, and evaluation findings. The excerpts suggest substantive engagement with the protection of vulnerable migrants, including migrant children (Outcome Three involves research on the legal framework for migrant children), and the project's three lines of action directly address capacity building for authorities who identify and assist vulnerable migrants. This relates closely to Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerability), 7e (account for migrant children in national child protection systems), and 7f (protect unaccompanied and separated children). The conclusions section notes the project 'responds to an identified need' for improved application of regional mechanisms to address human rights of migrants, particularly vulnerable ones. For a synthesis specialist, the findings on Outcomes One and Three (sections on effectiveness and sustainability) and the conclusions on the project's approach to vulnerable migrant protection would appear most relevant as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains more detailed analysis of vulnerability identification and response mechanisms beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Mid-term evaluation for People to People Support for Building Community Resilience through Recovery and Reconstruction in Nepal Project
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its disaster risk reduction and resilience-related actions. The project explicitly addresses disaster resilience, reconstruction, and the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction 2015-2030 — directly aligning with Action 2a (promoting implementation of the Sendai Framework) and Action 2i (developing adaptation and resilience strategies to natural disasters). The project's construction of multi-purpose evacuation centres, open space mapping, and training of local government officials on disaster risk reduction and management all appear substantively relevant to Actions 2i and 2j (integrating displacement considerations into disaster preparedness strategies). The executive summary and conclusions sections explicitly reference alignment with the Sendai Framework Priority 1 and Priority 4, as well as SDG 13.1 on strengthening resilience and adaptive capacity. The context section describing Nepal's multi-hazard profile and the 2015 Gorkha earthquakes provides relevant background on disaster-driven displacement. For a synthesis specialist, the conclusions and recommendations sections — particularly on risk factors, roles and responsibilities, and awareness raising — would appear most valuable as starting points, alongside the project description of evacuation centre construction and open space identification. The report's focus is on in-country resilience building rather than cross-border migration per se, which is worth noting, but the disaster-displacement nexus is central to this objective.

References.Title: PSYCHOLOGICAL SUPPORT TO CRISIS AFFECTED, DISPLACED AND MIGRANT YOUTH AND THEIR FAMILIES IN SYRIA AND NEIGHBORING COUNTRIES
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16, particularly regarding social cohesion and community-level integration approaches. The nonviolent communication and community-based mediation training appears directly relevant to Action 16a (promoting mutual respect, social cohesion and inclusion) and Action 16f (community centres and intercultural dialogue). The art-based interventions training, which used theatre, puppetry, and creative expression to facilitate community dialogue and social cohesion among conflict-affected populations, also connects to Action 16h (multicultural activities through arts). The Executive Masters program explicitly combined psychosocial support with dialogue and peace-building, with graduates reporting work on prejudice acknowledgment and coexistence between Arab and Kurdish communities — directly relevant to social cohesion objectives. The conclusions and recommendations explicitly call for fostering dialogue, social cohesion, and community-based mediation as integrated responses. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on nonviolent communication training (qualitative results), the Masters program evaluation, and the conclusions/recommendations section, which appear to offer the most substantive content on community cohesion and dialogue approaches in a displacement context.

References.Title: Ex-post internal evaluation of the project “Improved Migration Management through Policy Development in Kyrgyzstan”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 23, particularly regarding the role of international organizations in supporting national migration governance capacity and aligning national policy frameworks with global migration governance instruments. The project explicitly supported a national government in developing a Migration Policy Concept aligned with the GCM, MiGOF, and SDGs — making it a concrete case study of how international cooperation and IOM's institutional support function to advance GCM implementation at the national level. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: IOM's role as a facilitating partner for government capacity building (actions 23a and 23d); alignment of national policy with global frameworks (GCM, MiGOF, SDGs) as a primary project objective; and the 'seed funding' model of IDF as a mechanism for enabling timely national policy responses. The good practices section explicitly frames the project as demonstrating the value of IDF's 'seed funding' in enabling policy-window responses — which speaks directly to international cooperation mechanisms for GCM implementation. The conclusions on sustainability and institutional capacity also touch on the ongoing international partnership dimension. For a synthesis specialist, the executive summary's framing of the project's alignment with global frameworks, the good practices section on IDF's role, and the recommendations on multi-stakeholder programming would appear most relevant as starting points. It should be noted that much of the cooperation language in the report is operational context, but the project's explicit focus on aligning national policy with GCM and MiGOF elevates it above the typical ambient cooperation language threshold for Objective 23.

References.Title: Increasing the Knowledge Base on Community Cohesion and Mobility Dynamics in the Context of Climate Change and Environmental Degradation through Select Country-based Case Studies within the MENA Region
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project's core purpose was to generate an evidence base on climate change, environmental degradation, community cohesion, and mobility dynamics in Libya and Sudan — directly engaging with data collection, analysis, and dissemination for policy purposes. The excerpts indicate that the project involved primary data collection (both quantitative and qualitative) in multiple locations across both countries, literature reviews, and the production of research reports and policy papers. The evaluation assesses the quality and utilization of this evidence generation effort. Actions 1d (collecting and analyzing data on effects of migration for sustainable development) and 1k (research on interrelationship between migration and sustainable development dimensions) appear most closely related. The sections on effectiveness (output-level results on research documents) and the discussion of evidence utilization by UN agencies and governments would appear most relevant for human reviewers. However, the report's focus is on evaluating a specific research project rather than on data systems, statistical harmonization, or national data infrastructure — so it does not cover the full breadth of Objective 1's associated actions. It is possible the full report contains more detail on data methodologies than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Increasing the Knowledge Base on Community Cohesion and Mobility Dynamics in the Context of Climate Change and Environmental Degradation through Select Country-based Case Studies within the MENA Region
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its actions related to climate change, environmental degradation, and migration (actions 2h, 2i, and 2l). The project's explicit purpose was to enhance the evidence base on how climate change and environmental degradation impact community cohesion and mobility decisions in Libya and Sudan — countries in the MENA region described as severely affected by climate change and fragility. The project title, evaluation questions, and stated objectives all center on understanding the nexus between environmental drivers and migration, which is the core concern of Objective 2's climate-related actions. The excerpts suggest the evaluation assessed whether evidence generated on these drivers was utilized by key actors for policy and programming. The context section explicitly discusses slow-onset and sudden-onset events, natural resource depletion, and climate as a threat multiplier in conflict-affected settings — all directly relevant to actions 2h and 2i. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on project relevance to national priorities, effectiveness of evidence generation, and the conclusions on policy utilization would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The evaluation's discussion of the climate-mobility nexus in MENA, and the challenges of sustaining evidence use in conflict-affected contexts (Sudan), would likely add distinctive value to a synthesis on this objective.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE VOLUNTARY RETURN ASSISTANCE IN LIBYA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. Vulnerability identification and response appears to be a significant cross-cutting theme throughout the evaluation. The excerpts suggest substantive coverage of: identification of vulnerable migrants (trafficking victims, unaccompanied minors, victims of abuse and sexual violence) through screening forms and medical assessments; the 20% reintegration grant targeting the most vulnerable; procedures for unaccompanied minors (UMC) including best-interest determinations, legal guardian requirements, and family tracing; and the psychosocial and economic vulnerabilities of returnees. Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations), 7e (child protection systems), and 7f (unaccompanied and separated children) appear most directly addressed. The sections on UMC procedures (Section 3.2.1 on forms), candidate screening, and the conclusion's discussion of psychological and economic vulnerabilities would be particularly worth reviewing. The report appears to offer operational-level evidence on how vulnerability identification and response works in a complex humanitarian context, though the analytical depth may be uneven across vulnerability categories.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE VOLUNTARY RETURN ASSISTANCE IN LIBYA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The entire evaluation is explicitly focused on a Voluntary Humanitarian Return programme — examining the design, implementation, and outcomes of return and reintegration assistance for stranded migrants in Libya. The excerpts suggest that multiple evaluation dimensions directly address core themes of Objective 21: the voluntariness and dignity of return (counselling, informed consent, Declaration Form for Voluntary Return); sustainable reintegration support (socio-economic reintegration packages including micro-business, education, medical and housing assistance for 20% of returnees); coordination between sending and receiving missions; monitoring of reintegration outcomes; and the specific challenges of reintegrating vulnerable returnees including UMCs. Actions 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes with free, prior and informed consent), 21g (best interests of the child in return processes), and 21h (sustainable reintegration through social protection, vocational training, employment and financial services) appear most directly addressed. The sections on project design relevance (Section 3.1), implementation and organization (Section 3.2), post-departure forms and reintegration plans, UMC procedures, monitoring of reintegration, and the conclusion's discussion of reintegration challenges and recommendations would all appear particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist. The report also offers evidence on operational challenges in delivering return and reintegration in a complex humanitarian context (Libya), which is relatively rare in the evidence base. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal, particularly in sections not included in the excerpts reviewed.

References.Title: INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF THE UNITED NATIONS PILOT PROJECT FOR STRENGTHENING MIGRANT INTEGRATION AND SOCIAL COHESION IN SOUTH AFRICA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. Social cohesion and migrant inclusion are the explicit, central subjects of the entire Joint Programme — as reflected in the programme title itself ('Strengthening Migrant Integration and Social Cohesion'). The evaluation directly assesses Outcome 2 ('Social Inclusion and Peaceful Co-existence Reinforced between Migrants and Host Communities'), which the report explicitly links to the GCM principle of 'whole-of-society' and 'whole-of-government' approaches. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly relevant to Objective 16's associated actions: community peace initiatives and multi-stakeholder forums (Action 16f), socio-economic empowerment activities (Action 16d), gender-sensitive approaches to migrant women's participation (Action 16e), intercultural dialogue and exchange activities (Actions 16a, 16h), and early warning systems for conflict prevention. Quantitative outcome data are reported (70% reduction in perceived xenophobia; 70% of respondents reporting increased social inclusion), alongside qualitative assessments of community-level change. The conclusions and recommendations sections appear particularly rich for synthesis purposes, addressing sustainability of social cohesion mechanisms, the 'whole-of-society' implementation modality, and lessons on what works in community-level integration programming. For a synthesis specialist, the effectiveness findings, the sustainability analysis of multi-stakeholder forums, and the recommendations on scaling up social cohesion programming would appear to be the most valuable starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF THE UNITED NATIONS PILOT PROJECT FOR STRENGTHENING MIGRANT INTEGRATION AND SOCIAL COHESION IN SOUTH AFRICA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be highly valuable for a synthesis on GCM Objective 17. Countering anti-migrant narratives and addressing xenophobia are explicit primary objectives of the Joint Programme — appearing in the programme title ('Countering Anti-Migrant Narratives') and throughout the evaluation's analytical framework. The excerpts suggest substantive engagement with Actions 17a (legislation on hate crimes — the NAP and Hate Crimes Bill are analyzed), 17d (mechanisms to prevent and detect xenophobia and racial discrimination, including early warning systems), 17f (awareness-raising campaigns to promote positive perceptions of migration), and 17g (engagement of community leaders, civil society, and government to detect and prevent intolerance). The report analyzes the South African legislative context (NAP, NDP, MTSF) in relation to combating racism and xenophobia, assesses the effectiveness of community-level counter-narrative interventions, and evaluates government institutional capacity to recognize and respond to xenophobia. The context section provides rich background on anti-migrant sentiment trends in South Africa. For a synthesis specialist, the findings on counter-narrative effectiveness, the analysis of government institutional engagement with the NAP, and the recommendations on sustaining anti-xenophobia mechanisms would appear particularly valuable. The report likely contains additional relevant evidence in the full findings sections not captured in these excerpts.

References.Title: MID-TERM EVALUATION 2016-2018 TAJIK-AFGHAN INTEGRATION, RESILIENCE AND REFORM BUILDING PROGRAMME - TAIRR
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. Border management cooperation between Tajikistan and Afghanistan appears to be a central, primary focus of the TAIRR project and this evaluation. The excerpts suggest substantial analysis of: cross-border coordination mechanisms (Border Management Coordination Centers/BMCCs), capacity building for Tajik and Afghan border agencies (Output 1), joint training and cooperation frameworks, and infrastructure investments supporting border management. These directly relate to Actions 11a (international border management cooperation, joint cross-border trainings, capacity-building), 11b (integrated border management structures), and 11d (technical cooperation agreements for assets and equipment). The executive summary dedicates significant space to border agency cooperation outcomes, noting that border forces now have required facilities, equipment, and established relationships. The establishment of BMCCs as coordination platforms appears particularly relevant to Action 11b. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on BMCC establishment, border agency capacity building outcomes, and cross-border cooperation findings would appear most valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional detailed findings on border management beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: BUREAU RÉGIONAL OIM DAKAR EVALUATION DU PROJET : RENFORCER LA GESTION CONJOINTE DES FRONTIÈRES ENTRE LE MALI ET LA MAURITANIE
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The evaluation explicitly examines a joint border management project between Mali and Mauritania, with the project title directly referencing 'gestion conjointe des frontières.' The report appears to provide substantive analysis of: (1) cross-border cooperation mechanisms and their limitations (section e on joint management contributions), directly relevant to actions 11a and 11g; (2) integrated border management infrastructure including the MIDAS system deployment at Gogui border post, relevant to action 11b; (3) capacity-building through training of border officials, relevant to action 11a; (4) challenges of coordinating border management across two national systems with different institutional structures and political sensitivities, highly relevant to actions 11a and 11g; and (5) the gap between formal joint management objectives and actual implementation outcomes. The evaluation's analysis of why joint border management is difficult to achieve in practice — including institutional disparities, political sensitivities, and coordination failures — would likely provide valuable evidence for synthesis specialists examining the effectiveness of integrated border management approaches. Sections e (joint management contributions), f (coordination difficulties), and the recommendations sections (particularly p on performance improvement) appear most relevant for human reviewers to prioritize. The report also touches on community involvement in border security (section g), relevant to action 11a's whole-of-government approach.

References.Title: Regional Migrant Response Plan for the Horn of Africa and Yemen
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The MRP's core mandate is to address humanitarian and protection needs of migrants in vulnerable situations along the Eastern Route — one of the world's most dangerous migration corridors. The excerpts describe the MRP's target beneficiary groups (departing, transit, stranded, returnee migrants) and the range of protection risks they face, including GBV, trafficking, forced labor, detention, and exploitation. The evaluation assesses the MRP's effectiveness in addressing these vulnerabilities through a coordinated, multi-partner response. The Migrant Protection Working Group is a dedicated sub-committee, and protection outcomes are central to the MRP's Theory of Change. The evaluation's discussion of funding gaps limiting protection responses, the gender and disability cross-cutting analysis, and the assessment of partner engagement in protection activities are all potentially relevant to GCM Objective 7 actions — particularly 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies), 7j (support for migrants in crisis situations), and 7k (involving local authorities in identification and referral). Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the effectiveness section on SO1 and SO2 outcomes, the conclusions on protection gaps, and the cross-cutting gender/disability findings. The excerpts suggest this is a relevant but not fundamental contribution — protection is a core theme but the evaluation's primary lens is coordination framework functioning rather than protection outcomes per se.

References.Title: Regional Migrant Response Plan for the Horn of Africa and Yemen
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Return and reintegration are explicitly central themes of the MRP — the framework covers returnee migrants as a primary target group, and the evaluation assesses the MRP's effectiveness in supporting voluntary and involuntary returnees. The excerpts note that approximately 120,000 Ethiopian and 50,000 Yemeni nationals returned involuntarily in 2019, and that returnees face significant vulnerabilities including lack of access to basic services and strong motivation for re-migration. The evaluation discusses the MRP's development dimension (SO3) focused on reintegration, noting it was severely underfunded and underperforming — a substantive finding relevant to GCM Objective 21 actions on sustainable reintegration (particularly 21b, 21h, and 21i). The conclusions explicitly address the gap in reintegration programming and host community support. Recommendations 1 and 6 address the need to strengthen the development/reintegration dimension. The evaluation also covers the coordination framework for return responses across multiple countries (Ethiopia, Somalia, Djibouti, Yemen), relevant to Action 21a on bilateral/multilateral cooperation frameworks. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the effectiveness section on SO3 outcomes, the conclusions on reintegration gaps, and the recommendations on strengthening the development dimension. The excerpts suggest this is a relevant contribution — return and reintegration are core MRP themes, though the evaluation's primary lens is coordination framework functioning rather than reintegration outcomes per se.

References.Title: FINAL REPORT VOICES FROM THE UNDERGROUND: END OF PROJECT EVALUATION 2014-2016
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The evaluation centers on the situation of Mozambican migrant mine workers, ex-miners, and their families — a population facing significant vulnerabilities related to labor rights violations, lack of access to compensation and social benefits, and limited legal recourse. The project explicitly aimed to provide legal services and counseling to this vulnerable population, touching on Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations), 7d (addressing workplace-related vulnerabilities), and 7g (access to independent legal assistance). The sections on legal services (Work package 2) and the impact assessment of AMIMO's capacity to assist clients would appear particularly relevant for human reviewers. The evaluation's discussion of barriers to accessing justice, the lack of feedback to clients on legal cases, and the structural challenges facing ex-miners and their families provides substantive — if sobering — evidence on vulnerability response mechanisms. Synthesis specialists should note that the report evaluates a civil society capacity-building initiative rather than a state-led protection system, which may limit generalizability but adds value on the role of non-governmental actors in addressing migrant vulnerabilities.

References.Title: FINAL REPORT VOICES FROM THE UNDERGROUND: END OF PROJECT EVALUATION 2014-2016
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 22. The portability of social security entitlements and earned benefits for Mozambican migrant mine workers is a central theme throughout the evaluation. The project explicitly addressed access to provident funds, compensation for occupational diseases, and social benefits for miners and ex-miners — directly aligned with Actions 22b (bilateral/multilateral social security agreements on portability) and 22c (integration of portability provisions into national frameworks). The evaluation discusses: the critique of the 1964 bilateral agreement governing labor migration between South Africa and Mozambique; advocacy for portability of social benefits; the challenge of unclaimed provident funds held by South African funds; research on access to workers' compensation for non-national mineworkers; and the macro-litigation against TEBA under South Africa's PAIA to access personal information of mine workers. The sections on Work package 3 (advocacy for policy change) and Work package 2 (legal services including compensation cases) would appear particularly valuable for human reviewers conducting a synthesis on GCM Objective 22. The evaluation also provides evidence on the practical barriers to portability — including bilateral governance gaps, lack of information, and limited civil society capacity — that would enrich a synthesis on this objective. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION: TECHNICAL SUPPORT TO THE BUREAU OF MANPOWER, EMPLOYMENT AND TRAINING (BMET) ON OVERSEAS MARKET INFORMATION AND TO BANGLADESHI MIGRANT WORKERS ON RECOGNITION OF PRIOR LEARNING
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears highly relevant for a synthesis on GCM Objective 6. Ethical recruitment is explicitly one of the two primary themes of the evaluated project, with dedicated sections on ethical recruitment outcomes, obstacles, and recommendations. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: the adoption of the International Recruitment Integrity System (IRIS) among Bangladeshi recruitment agencies (relating to Action 6l on IRIS); the development of an online jobs portal to improve employment information (relating to Action 6d on worker awareness); and the broader challenges of moving Bangladesh toward ethical recruitment practices (relating to Actions 6c and 6f on regulation and monitoring of the recruitment industry). The evaluation's conclusions and recommendations section B ('Ethical recruitment') and the discussion of IRIS adoption would likely be particularly valuable starting points for human reviewers. The report also notes the involvement of BAIRA (Bangladesh Association of International Recruitment Agencies) as a stakeholder, suggesting analysis of recruitment agency engagement. It is possible the full report contains more detailed findings on recruitment regulation than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION: TECHNICAL SUPPORT TO THE BUREAU OF MANPOWER, EMPLOYMENT AND TRAINING (BMET) ON OVERSEAS MARKET INFORMATION AND TO BANGLADESHI MIGRANT WORKERS ON RECOGNITION OF PRIOR LEARNING
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 18. Recognition of Prior Learning (RPL) for Bangladeshi migrant workers is explicitly the central subject of the evaluated project, appearing in the project title itself. The excerpts suggest the evaluation provides substantive analysis directly relevant to multiple associated actions: Actions 18a and 18b (developing standards and frameworks for mutual recognition of qualifications — the project examined qualification alignment between Bangladesh's NTVQF and destination country systems); Action 18j (innovative ways to recognize formally and informally acquired skills — RPL certification is the core mechanism evaluated); Action 18k (screening mechanisms and information on skills assessment prior to departure); and Action 18i (documentation recognizing skills acquired on the job). The evaluation appears to document in detail the obstacles to skills recognition and qualification alignment, the complexity of RPL implementation, and the limited employment benefits observed for certified workers — all of which would constitute valuable evidence for a synthesis on what works and what doesn't in skills recognition for migrant workers. The longitudinal and mapping studies discussed in the excerpts, the 20 recommendations referenced in Annex 1, and the conclusions section A ('Skills recognition') would likely be particularly valuable for human reviewers. The full report likely contains considerably more detail on these themes than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO MINISTRY OF PEACE AND RECONSTRUCTION IN THE IMPLEMENTATION OF PSYCHOLOGICAL COUNSELLING AND SUPPORT SERVICES TO CONFLICTED AFFECTED PERSONS
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7, particularly regarding psychosocial and mental health support for vulnerable populations affected by conflict. The project explicitly targets conflict-affected persons (CAPs) including women, children, SGBV survivors, torture victims, and marginalized communities — groups that align closely with the vulnerable populations described under Actions 7b and 7c. The evaluation discusses gender-responsive approaches, community-based service delivery, and the inclusion of groups not formally categorized as CAPs (such as SGBV and torture survivors). Action 7c specifically calls for 'psychological and other counselling services' for migrant women and girls, which is the core service this project delivers. The recommendations section on rights-based approaches and gender sensitivity, as well as the findings on community-based outreach and inclusion of marginalized groups, would appear particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist. The report's focus is not on migrants in the strict GCM sense but on conflict-affected persons, which limits direct applicability; however, the psychosocial service delivery model and vulnerability-response framework appear substantively relevant. Specialists should review the 'Results of the evaluation' section and the recommendations on gender sensitivity and rights-based approaches.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN FINAL DEL PROYECTO “APOYO A LA INTEGRACIÓN SOCIOECONÓMICA Y FORTALECIMIENTO DE LOS SISTEMAS DE SALUD Y DE LAS ORGANIZACIONES DE LA SOCIEDAD CIVIL EN EL CONTEXTO DE COVID-19 Y SU IMPACTO EN LA POBLACIÓN VENEZOLANA Y RECEPTORA” EN PERU
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. Access to basic services — specifically education and health — appears to be the explicit primary subject of the evaluated project, with these themes prominently featured in the project title, objectives, and evaluation questions. The excerpts suggest that multiple conclusions directly address outcomes related to migrant access to education (NNA enrollment, kit distribution) and health services (direct payment mechanisms for hospital care), corresponding closely to Actions 15e (health needs of migrants) and 15f (inclusive and equitable quality education for migrant children). The executive summary reports over 90% beneficiary satisfaction across education, health, and economic integration axes, and the conclusions assess the effectiveness of interventions in improving access to these services. The context section documents structural barriers to service access (only 1 in 10 Venezuelans had SIS health coverage; ~100,000 NNA lacked access to formal education), providing a baseline against which the project's contributions can be assessed. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on the education axis (kit distribution, enrollment outcomes) and health axis (Pago Directo mechanism, hospital partnerships) would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review, especially for Actions 15e and 15f. The full report likely contains additional findings on service access barriers and intervention effectiveness beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN FINAL DEL PROYECTO “APOYO A LA INTEGRACIÓN SOCIOECONÓMICA Y FORTALECIMIENTO DE LOS SISTEMAS DE SALUD Y DE LAS ORGANIZACIONES DE LA SOCIEDAD CIVIL EN EL CONTEXTO DE COVID-19 Y SU IMPACTO EN LA POBLACIÓN VENEZOLANA Y RECEPTORA” EN PERU
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. The project's stated goal of 'socioeconomic integration' and its four axes — education, health, economic integration, and social integration — align closely with Objective 16's themes of inclusion, labour market participation, and social cohesion. The economic integration axis (skills training and certification via SENATI to facilitate formal labour market access) relates to Action 16d (inclusive labour markets and formal economy participation). The social integration axis, focused on anti-discrimination and anti-xenophobia communication campaigns, directly addresses Action 16i (welcoming school environments, combating xenophobia) and Action 16f (community activities promoting mutual respect). The conclusions note that communication campaigns achieved acceptance among host communities regarding messages against discrimination. However, social cohesion and inclusion appear as project objectives rather than deeply analyzed phenomena — the excerpts do not reveal dedicated analytical sections on integration outcomes or cohesion dynamics. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on the economic integration axis outcomes and the social integration/communication campaign results, particularly for Actions 16d and 16f, while noting that the analytical depth may be limited compared to the education and health axes.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN FINAL DEL PROYECTO “APOYO A LA INTEGRACIÓN SOCIOECONÓMICA Y FORTALECIMIENTO DE LOS SISTEMAS DE SALUD Y DE LAS ORGANIZACIONES DE LA SOCIEDAD CIVIL EN EL CONTEXTO DE COVID-19 Y SU IMPACTO EN LA POBLACIÓN VENEZOLANA Y RECEPTORA” EN PERU
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute relevant evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 17. The project explicitly includes an 'Eje Integración Social' (Social Integration Axis) focused on communication campaigns to prevent discrimination and xenophobia against Venezuelan R&M — directly corresponding to Actions 17f (awareness-raising campaigns on positive contributions of migration) and 17c (promoting objective reporting and countering intolerance). The executive summary states that 'communication campaigns achieved a level of acceptance in the host population regarding messages disseminated in the framework of the fight against discrimination and xenophobia.' The conclusions confirm this as one of four key outcomes. However, the excerpts suggest this axis is one of four project components and may not receive the deepest analytical treatment — the quantitative survey components focused on education and health rather than the social integration axis. The sections on the social integration axis outcomes and the communication campaign design and reach would appear most relevant for a synthesis specialist focused on Objective 17, particularly for Actions 17f and 17g, though the depth of evidence may be more limited than for the education and health components.

References.Title: “ACTION TO SUPPORT THE NATIONAL COORDINATING COMMITTEE ON COMBATING AND PREVENTING ILLEGAL MIGRATION AND TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS (NCCPIM&TIP) TO CREATE A SAFE AND SECURE ENVIRONMENT IN EGYPT”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The evaluation explicitly examines a project whose entire purpose was to combat and prevent trafficking in persons in Egypt, making it directly aligned with this objective's core theme. The report appears to provide substantive analysis across multiple associated actions: Action 10a (Egypt's ratification of the Palermo Protocol and Law 64/2010 are discussed); Action 10c (monitoring irregular migration routes exploited by trafficking networks); Action 10e (measures addressing vulnerabilities of women, children, and migrants at risk of trafficking); Action 10g (strengthening legislation and prosecution of traffickers); Action 10h (protection and assistance to VoTs, including the shelter protocol); Action 10i (training programmes for law enforcement, media, and CSOs to identify trafficking); and Action 10j (awareness-raising campaigns on trafficking risks). The report's conclusions and recommendations directly address the effectiveness of Egypt's National Strategy and National Action Plan against trafficking, the functioning of the national coordinating committee (NCCPIM&TIP), challenges in victim identification and referral (NRM gaps), training outcomes for law enforcement and civil society, and sustainability of anti-trafficking frameworks. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on effectiveness (training outcomes, NS/NAP development), impact (knowledge improvements among law enforcement and CSOs, shelter protocol), sustainability (NRM gaps, campaign sustainability), and recommendations (follow-on support, governance issues) would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The governance analysis — particularly how institutional dynamics affected anti-trafficking coordination — may offer distinctive insights for evidence synthesis on national anti-trafficking systems.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION OF THE IOM PROJECT “SUPPORTING STRATEGIC INTERVENTIONS TOWARDS EFFECTIVE AND SUSTAINABLE LABOUR MIGRATION MANAGEMENT AND INFORMATION SHARING IN TANZANIA”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears highly relevant for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. One of the four core project outcomes explicitly focused on developing a Labour Market Information System (LMIS) capable of generating labour migration statistics, and another outcome concerned developing an electronic work permit system to generate and use up-to-date labour migration data. The evaluation assesses the extent to which these data infrastructure goals were achieved — and concludes they largely were not at the strategic level, offering substantive analysis of why. This directly relates to Actions 1a (comprehensive strategy for improving migration data), 1c (building national capacities in data collection), 1i (collaboration between migration data units and statistical offices), and 1j (country-specific migration profiles). The executive summary and conclusions sections appear particularly relevant, as they discuss the LMIS roadmap, the feasibility study for the electronic work permit, and the failure to establish a functioning labour statistics generation system. Recommendations also address the need for better partnerships with the National Bureau of Statistics and ILO — directly relevant to Action 1i. A synthesis specialist would likely want to review the findings on Outcome 2 (LMIS) and Outcome 3 (electronic work permit) in depth, as these appear to offer substantive evidence on the challenges of building national migration data systems in a low-capacity government context.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION FOR THE PROJECT “IMPROVING NATIONAL EBOLA VIRUS DISEASE PREPAREDNESS AND SURVEILLANCE MEASURES IN RWANDA”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project explicitly includes a Population Mobility Mapping (PMM) component — a data collection initiative designed to make population-mobility related information available to national and local authorities (Output 1.1). The excerpts indicate that PMM tools were developed and revised to capture data relevant to both EVD and COVID-19 preparedness, and a key recommendation is that IOM finalize and publish the PMM Report. This relates to actions 1a (improving migration data collection and dissemination), 1i (using administrative/border records for statistical purposes), and 1j (developing country-specific migration profiles). However, the data collection dimension appears to be one of two main outputs rather than the sole focus, and the excerpts suggest limited depth of analysis on data quality, disaggregation, or comparability standards. The sections on Output 1.1 and the recommendation on finalizing the PMM Report would be the most relevant for human reviewers to prioritize, though the full report may contain more detail on the PMM methodology and results than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION FOR THE PROJECT “IMPROVING NATIONAL EBOLA VIRUS DISEASE PREPAREDNESS AND SURVEILLANCE MEASURES IN RWANDA”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The project is explicitly framed around the IOM Health Border and Mobility Management (HBMM) framework and focuses on strengthening capacities at Points of Entry (PoEs) — a core concern of Objective 11. The excerpts indicate that 35 PoEs were capacitated for EVD and COVID-19 screening, health care workers at borders were trained, and screening tools were developed and implemented. This relates closely to actions 11a (border management cooperation and capacity-building), 11b (effective integrated border crossing procedures including pre-screening), and 11g (cross-border collaboration on treatment of persons crossing borders). The evaluation's effectiveness findings on PoE capacitation, the coherence findings on alignment with international frameworks, and the recommendations on continuing PoE support would be particularly valuable for human reviewers to examine. The report appears to offer concrete evidence on health-integrated border management, which is a relatively underrepresented dimension in border management synthesis literature.

References.Title: INFORME FINAL EVALUACIÓN INDICADORES PROGRAMA REINTEGRACIÓN Y PREVENCIÓN DEL RECLUTAMIENTO, RPR
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly its reintegration dimension (Action 21h). The RPR program explicitly focuses on reintegration of ex-combatants into communities, measuring outcomes such as community acceptance, attitude change toward legality and non-violence, and institutional coordination for reintegration support. While the population is FARC ex-combatants rather than returned migrants, the reintegration programming model — including psychosocial support, community-based reintegration (MRC), vocational training (MEFR), and institutional coordination — is directly analogous to the sustainable reintegration frameworks envisioned under GCM Objective 21. The report appears to provide quantitative evidence on reintegration outcomes (acceptance rates, attitude change) and qualitative insights on what works in community-based reintegration. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on PPR indicators (community acceptance, attitude change), the MRC initiative results (12.9 percentage point improvement in acceptance), and the conclusions on factors enabling successful reintegration would appear most relevant as starting points. The institutional coordination index may also offer methodological insights for Action 21's implementation. It should be noted that the population is not international migrants returning to their country of origin, which is a meaningful distinction — human reviewers should weigh this carefully.

References.Title: ENGAGEMENT DE LA DIASPORA POUR LA MIGRATION ET LE DEVELOPPEMENT AU BURUNDI
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The entire project is explicitly designed to engage the Burundian diaspora in contributing to national development — which is the core theme of this objective. The report title itself ('Engagement de la Diaspora pour la Migration et le Développement au Burundi') signals direct alignment. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple associated actions: Action 19d (establishing dedicated diaspora offices/focal points — the Direction de la Diaspora is a central actor throughout); Action 19j (building partnerships for knowledge and skills transfer between diaspora and country of origin, including diaspora mapping); Action 19c (non-financial contributions of diaspora through skills transfer); Action 19b (integrating migration into development planning through the Politique Nationale de la Diaspora and Politique Nationale de Migration); and Action 19i (enabling diaspora professionals to carry out activities in their home country without losing status). The evaluation appears to assess effectiveness, sustainability, and impact of diaspora engagement mechanisms across multiple sectors (health, governance, private sector). Key sections for human reviewers to prioritize would include: Section 3 (principales conclusions on diaspora week, skills transfer outcomes, and policy development); Section 4.1 (pertinence analysis confirming alignment with national priorities); Section 4.2 (efficacité analysis across all four components); and Section 4.4 (durabilité/impacts, including recommendations for sustaining diaspora engagement). The political crisis context and its effect on diaspora willingness to engage also provides valuable real-world evidence on barriers to diaspora contribution. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation Report on Climate Change and Disaster related Migration in Mongolia - DTM
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project's core purpose was to improve the Government of Mongolia's capacity to collect, analyse, and use climate change and natural disaster-related internal migration data for policymaking — directly aligning with the objective's theme and several associated actions. The project title itself references migration data (DTM), and the evaluation questions explicitly assess data collection capacity, data availability, and data use in policymaking. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: capacity building for migration data collection (Actions 1c, 1d), development of a DTM-based evidence base on migration flows (Actions 1b, 1j), and the gap between data availability and actual policy use (Action 1d). Output 1.2 ('Evidence and data on current migration flows and needs in selected soums is developed and made accessible') and Output 1.1 (improved skills to track migration) are directly relevant to Actions 1c and 1d. The conclusions and recommendations sections appear particularly valuable for synthesis specialists, especially the discussion of sustainability of data collection capacity, the limitations of using DTM tool proficiency as a proxy for knowledge change, and the finding that DTM data was referenced for COVID-19 response — illustrating real-world data use. Sections on effectiveness, sustainability, and recommendations 2, 3, and 7 would be priority reading for a synthesis specialist. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation Report on Climate Change and Disaster related Migration in Mongolia - DTM
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its sub-theme on natural disasters, climate change, and environmental degradation (Actions 2h, 2i, 2j). The project explicitly addresses climate change and disaster-related internal migration in Mongolia, including the role of dzuds (severe winter storms) and droughts in driving rural-to-urban migration. The excerpts suggest substantive discussion of the linkage between climate change, natural disasters, and forced migration, as well as the government's capacity to coordinate disaster response to minimize forced displacement (Output 1.3 on the action plan for coordination). However, the project's primary focus appears to be on data collection and government capacity rather than on directly addressing the structural drivers themselves — the report acknowledges it is 'too premature' to assess impacts on migrants' livelihoods and preparedness. Actions 2h (joint analysis to map migration movements from disasters) and 2j (integrating displacement into disaster preparedness) appear most directly covered. The context and introduction sections, as well as the impact and sustainability conclusions, would be most relevant for a synthesis specialist to review, particularly for evidence on the gap between data availability and policy action on climate-induced migration drivers.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF IOM-UNDP PROJECT: “BEYOND BENTIU PROTECTION OF CIVILIAN SITE (POC) YOUTH REINTEGRATION STRATEGY”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. Social cohesion and inclusion appear to be the central analytical themes of the evaluation, with the project explicitly titled around 'Creating Conditions for Peaceful Coexistence' between IDPs, returnees, and host community members. The excerpts suggest multiple findings directly address social cohesion outcomes, inter-ethnic dialogue, and community integration — core themes under Actions 16a (promoting mutual respect and social cohesion), 16c (developing policy goals for inclusion), and 16f (establishing community centres for migrant participation in receiving society). The evaluation reports that '58% of the youths interviewed reported reduction of violence among the youth and communities' and that the project 'surpassed the target number of male and female participating in social cohesion platforms by 650 (108%).' The formation of inter-ethnic peace committees, youth dialogue platforms, and community infrastructure rehabilitation all directly relate to fostering inclusion and social cohesion. The executive summary, conclusions, and recommendations sections all appear particularly rich with evidence on social cohesion mechanisms and outcomes. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on peace committee formation, youth dialogue platforms, and the lessons learned on indirect approaches to social cohesion would appear most valuable as starting points for deeper review. The context is internal displacement rather than international migration, but the social cohesion and inclusion mechanisms are highly analogous and likely transferable.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF IOM-UNDP PROJECT: “BEYOND BENTIU PROTECTION OF CIVILIAN SITE (POC) YOUTH REINTEGRATION STRATEGY”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be highly valuable for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly on sustainable reintegration. The project is explicitly titled a 'Youth Reintegration Strategy' and the evaluation covers reintegration of IDPs and returnees into host communities — directly addressing Action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through social protection, vocational training, employment, and community inclusion) and Action 21i (identifying needs of communities to which migrants return). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of reintegration mechanisms, including livelihoods support, infrastructure rehabilitation, social cohesion platforms, and community-based approaches. The evaluation assessed whether 'strengthened economic and social interdependencies for the IDPs, returnees and host community through livelihoods and rehabilitation of critical community infrastructure' were achieved. The conclusions and recommendations sections appear particularly rich with evidence on what works for sustainable reintegration in fragile contexts. The context is internal displacement rather than international return migration, but the reintegration mechanisms, challenges, and lessons learned are highly analogous and likely transferable to international return contexts. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on reintegration outcomes, livelihoods programming, and the lessons learned on integrated approaches would appear most valuable as starting points for deeper review.

References.Title: ÉVALUATION FINALE DU PROJET “Initiatives de Stabilisation Communautaire dans le Nord du Niger (COSINN) ”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. Social cohesion is the explicit and central theme of the COSINN project — the project title itself references 'community stabilization,' and the evaluation systematically assesses social cohesion outcomes. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: intercommunal dialogue mechanisms, citizen engagement with local authorities, trust-building between communities and governance structures, and inclusion of women and youth in community life. The evaluation measures social cohesion perceptions quantitatively (84% of beneficiaries vs. 56% of non-beneficiaries perceiving improvement) and qualitatively. Actions 16a (mutual respect and social cohesion), 16f (community centres and intercultural dialogue), 16g (civic integration), and 16h (cultural and social activities) appear most related. The context involves a migration-affected region where community tensions partly involve migrant populations (Sudanese arrivals mentioned). However, the primary focus is on host community cohesion rather than migrant integration per se, which is the framing of GCM Objective 16. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the impact and efficacy sections, the gender section, and the conclusions on social cohesion measurement — framing these as pointers for further review rather than definitive characterizations. The full report likely contains additional relevant evidence on community dialogue mechanisms and participatory governance approaches.

References.Title: Supporting Peace, Security and Stability in Northern Regions through Strengthening Border Management Capacities and Inclusive Community Engagement Project
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. Border management is one of the two explicit primary subjects of the evaluated project, appearing prominently in the project title, evaluation objectives, and throughout the findings and recommendations. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: integrated border management committee (IBMC) formation and operationalization; capacity building of border management officials; cross-border coordination between South Sudan and Sudan (relating to Actions 11a and 11g); border infrastructure investments; training of border officials on crime prevention and migrant protection (Action 11a); and the development of SOPs and guidelines for border management.
