The evaluation appears to assess both institutional capacity gaps and coordination mechanisms at Points of Entry, which aligns closely with Actions 11a (international/regional border management cooperation), 11b (integrated border management structures), and 11d (technical cooperation for border management). For a synthesis specialist, the sections on IBMC formation and operationalization, cross-border coordination challenges with Sudan, capacity building outcomes, and recommendations on equipment and training would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The report also provides contextual evidence on border management in a fragile, conflict-affected state — a relatively underrepresented context in border management evidence bases. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: MIDTERM EVALUATION OF IOM GENDER EQUALITY POLICY 2015-2019
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 23, specifically regarding IOM's institutional role in implementing global gender equality frameworks and accountability mechanisms. The evaluation explicitly examines IOM's implementation of the UN System-wide Action Plan on Gender Equality and the Empowerment of Women (UN-SWAP) — a multilateral accountability framework — and assesses IOM's progress against its 15 performance indicators. The report also discusses IOM's alignment with the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development and the Migration Governance Framework (MiGOF), which are central to GCM Objective 23's concern with aligning migration governance with broader international frameworks (Action 23a, 23b, 23e). The evaluation appears to assess how IOM, as a 'Related Organization' within the UN system, is fulfilling its commitments under a global governance framework — which is substantively different from mere operational coordination language. The excerpts suggest the report may offer evidence on: how international accountability frameworks (UN-SWAP) drive institutional change within IOM; the gap between IOM's performance and that of other UN Funds and Programmes (IOM meets 6 of 15 indicators vs. an average of 11); and the role of senior management commitment and resource allocation in implementing global policy commitments. For a synthesis specialist, the Effectiveness section on UN-SWAP indicator performance and the Sustainability section on long-term institutionalization would appear particularly relevant as starting points. It should be noted that this relevance is specific to the sub-theme of multilateral accountability frameworks and IOM's institutional role — not to the broader international cooperation architecture of the GCM itself. The full report may contain additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Fortalecimiento del rol de la OIM como Coordinadora de la Red de Naciones Unidas sobre Migración en América del Sur
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 23. The evaluation explicitly examines IOM's role as Coordinator of the UN Network on Migration in South America — a multilateral governance mechanism established specifically to support GCM implementation across member states. This is precisely the type of international cooperation architecture that Objective 23 concerns. The project title itself ('Strengthening IOM's Role as Coordinator of the UN Network on Migration in South America') signals direct alignment. The excerpts suggest that multiple conclusions and recommendations directly address: (i) the functioning and strengthening of the Regional UN Network on Migration and National Networks (relating to Action 23a and 23d on supporting collective GCM implementation); (ii) IOM's coordination with the UN Network Secretariat in Geneva and regional offices (relating to Action 23e on bilateral and multilateral partnerships); (iii) civil society and government engagement in GCM review processes including the IMRF (relating to Action 23a on whole-of-society approaches); and (iv) capacity-building of country offices to fulfill coordination roles (relating to Action 23d on capacity-building mechanisms). Unlike most IOM reports where cooperation language is merely operational context, here the governance architecture itself — the UN Migration Network, its regional and national nodes, IOM's secretariat function — is the primary object of evaluation. The conclusions on sustainability, regional articulation, and the Civil Society Dialogue Mechanism appear particularly valuable for synthesis specialists to review, as they offer evidence on what makes multilateral GCM coordination mechanisms effective or ineffective. The lessons learned section on coordination rhythms and the practices highlighted around the Civil Society Mechanism would also appear relevant. It is possible the full report contains additional evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal, but based on what is available, this appears to be a core source for Objective 23 synthesis work.

References.Title: EU4 Security, Accountability and Fight against Crime in Georgia (SAFE) – Support to Integrated Border Management (IBM) in Georgia
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. Integrated Border Management is the explicit and central subject of the SAFE IBM project — the project title, objectives, activities, and evaluation framework all revolve around IBM. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly aligned with GCM Objective 11 actions: Action 11a (international border management cooperation, capacity-building, whole-of-government approaches) is addressed through the project's multi-agency engagement with MIA, Border Police, Coast Guard, Customs, and OTA, as well as training programs and interagency coordination mechanisms. Action 11b (integrated border management structures, border crossing procedures, ICT use) appears extensively covered through equipment procurement (surveillance cameras, passport scanners, radio communication), training modernization, and the IBM Strategy 2023-2027 development. Action 11c (review of border screening and individual assessment procedures) is touched upon through training on profiling, interview skills, and human rights. Action 11d (technical cooperation agreements for equipment and assets) is directly addressed through the project's equipment procurement and technical assistance activities. Action 11g (cross-border collaboration on treatment of persons crossing borders) appears in the study visits and interagency cooperation sections. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on project effectiveness, good practices (particularly on capacity building, equipment procurement, and interagency cooperation), and recommendations would appear most valuable as starting points. The evaluation's application of OECD-DAC criteria to an IBM intervention provides structured evidence on what works in border management capacity-building. It is likely that the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: “FORTALECIMIENTO INSTITUCIONAL PARA LA PROMOCIÓN DE LOS DERECHOS DE LOS MIGRANTES IRREGULARES EN TRÁNSITO POR NICARAGUA”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project's explicit purpose is to strengthen institutional capacity to protect and assist migrants in vulnerable situations transiting through Nicaragua — a core theme of Objective 7. The excerpts suggest substantive coverage of: identification and differentiated assistance for vulnerable migrants (women, pregnant women, trafficking victims, smuggled migrants, persons with health issues) aligning with Actions 7b and 7c; gender-responsive infrastructure improvements at the Centro de Atención a Migrantes (separate spaces for men and women, family accommodation gaps) relating to Actions 7a and 7c; training of border officials on differentiated, rights-based treatment of vulnerable migrants relating to Action 7k; and the challenge of providing appropriate support regardless of migration status (Action 7b). The project title, evaluation objectives, context section, conclusions, and recommendations all prominently address vulnerability in migration. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on infrastructure improvements at the reception center, training outcomes for border officials, and recommendations on expanding capacity for families and medical care would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional evidence on vulnerability identification and response mechanisms beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: “FORTALECIMIENTO INSTITUCIONAL PARA LA PROMOCIÓN DE LOS DERECHOS DE LOS MIGRANTES IRREGULARES EN TRÁNSITO POR NICARAGUA”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The project explicitly focuses on strengthening border management capacity at Nicaraguan border posts (Peñas Blancas, Guasaule, Las Manos, El Espino), with training of first-line border officials on rights-based, differentiated treatment of migrants — directly relating to Actions 11a and 11c. The evaluation assesses outcomes of capacity-building for border officials, gender-responsive procedures at border crossings, and referral mechanisms for vulnerable migrants. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on training outcomes for border officials, the sustainability mechanism through the Escuela Migratoria, and the conclusions on coordination between DGME and OIM would appear particularly relevant. Border management is a central — though not the sole — theme of this report, alongside the vulnerability/reception center focus.

References.Title: ENHANCING HUMAN SECURITY AND BUILDING RESILIENT SOCIETIES IN DISADVANTAGED COMMUNITIES OF ARMENIA

The project's explicit goal is to address root causes of human insecurity in disadvantaged communities of Armenia, directly targeting structural factors that drive emigration — including poverty, unemployment, food insecurity, and lack of social services. The excerpts indicate that the project addresses Actions 2a (SDG implementation, Sendai Framework), 2b (poverty eradication, food security, education, economic growth, gender equality, resilience and DRR), 2d (sustainable development investment), and 2e (human capital development, vocational training, employment creation). The report notes that 'new employment opportunities in communities with frequent labour migration have reduced the number of out-migrating persons,' directly linking project interventions to migration drivers. The project targets regions with the highest emigration rates in Armenia (Shirak at 18.5%, Lori at 16.2%). The background section, effectiveness findings, and sustainability conclusions would appear particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist. The DRR components (Output 1.2) also relate to Action 2c (early warning systems). The report's discussion of COVID-19's potential to worsen poverty and migration indicators adds nuance relevant to understanding structural drivers. Human reviewers should prioritize the sections on project objectives, effectiveness analysis, and the discussion of labour migration outcomes.

References.Title: Final Evaluation of the Project "Draft the Future: Towards a Diaspora Forum in Italy
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The entire project evaluated — 'Draft the Future! Towards a Diaspora Forum in Italy' — appears to be directly and comprehensively focused on empowering diaspora organizations (DOs) to contribute to sustainable development through Italy's development cooperation system. The excerpts suggest strong alignment with multiple associated actions: Action 19d (establishing government structures and mechanisms for diaspora engagement, including diaspora policy advisory bodies) is directly addressed through the creation of CIDCI as a national interlocutor; Action 19j (building partnerships between local authorities, diaspora organizations, and migrant organizations for knowledge and skills transfer) appears throughout the findings on regional networks and local authority engagement; Action 19c (research on non-financial contributions of migrants and diasporas) is touched upon through the project's evidence base; and Action 19b (integrating migration into development planning) is addressed through the project's alignment with Law 125/2014 and the 2030 Agenda. The evaluation's theory of change, effectiveness findings, impact assessment, sustainability analysis, lessons learned, and recommendations all appear to directly address how diaspora organizations can be empowered as development actors. Particularly valuable for human reviewers would be: the theory of change section (outcomes 1-8 on diaspora forum creation and DO empowerment), the effectiveness and impact findings on CIDCI's creation and DO capacity strengthening, the lessons learned on regional networks and multi-stakeholder partnerships, and the best practices on grants and training as empowerment tools. The report also appears to offer evidence on what works and what doesn't in formalizing diaspora engagement in national development cooperation systems — a relatively underexplored area in the evidence base.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION REPORT MULTI-SECTORIAL LIVELIHOOD SUPPORT TO VULNERABLE COMMUNITIES IN ZIMBABWE
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project explicitly uses IOM's Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM) as a core component — described as one of three distinct project components — to track IDPs, migrant returnees, and vulnerable host communities. The excerpts indicate that baseline assessments, multi-sectoral village assessments, return intention surveys, and progress monitoring reports were conducted and used to generate disaggregated data on displaced populations. The evaluation itself draws on multiple DTM datasets (over 1,500–1,700 respondents each) collected at different points. This appears to relate to GCM actions 1c (building national capacities in data collection), 1d (collecting data on effects of migration), and 1j (developing country-specific migration profiles). However, data collection is one of three project components rather than the sole focus, and the depth of analysis on data systems per se — as opposed to using data for program management — is unclear from the excerpts. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review Section 2.2.1 (Information Management and Assessments) and Section 3.4.3 (IOM DTM Secondary Data) as starting points, as well as the conclusions section referencing DTM report production and distribution. It is possible the full report contains more substantive analysis of the DTM methodology and its contribution to evidence-based policymaking than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION REPORT MULTI-SECTORIAL LIVELIHOOD SUPPORT TO VULNERABLE COMMUNITIES IN ZIMBABWE
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project explicitly targets vulnerable populations — IDPs, migrant returnees, and host communities — and addresses multiple vulnerability dimensions including psychosocial wellbeing, protection from GBV, child protection, trafficking awareness, and economic vulnerability. The ICRS component (Section 2.2.2) appears to directly address actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerability), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies addressing needs of women and girls), and 7k (involving local authorities in identification and referral). The training of Community Health Workers and counsellors on child protection, trafficking, and GBV disclosure, as well as peer support group mechanisms for referral to specialized services, appear relevant to action 7b and 7k. The evaluation also references negative coping mechanisms (child labour, early marriage, transactional sex) as vulnerability indicators. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review Section 2.2.2 (Protection), the conclusions on ICRS effectiveness, and any findings on MHPSS outcomes. The excerpts suggest vulnerability is a central rather than peripheral theme, though the depth of analysis on specific vulnerability typologies is unclear from what was reviewed.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION REPORT MULTI-SECTORIAL LIVELIHOOD SUPPORT TO VULNERABLE COMMUNITIES IN ZIMBABWE
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Safe and dignified return and sustainable reintegration of migrant returnees and IDPs appears to be the central subject of the evaluated project. The project title itself — 'Multi-Sectoral Livelihood Support to Vulnerable Communities in Zimbabwe' — and its stated objectives explicitly address reintegration of migrant returnees and IDPs. The excerpts indicate the project directly addresses GCM actions 21b (gender-responsive return and reintegration programmes with legal, social and financial support), 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through social protection, psychosocial assistance, vocational training, employment opportunities), and 21i (identifying and addressing needs of communities to which migrants return). The evaluation covers over 472,409 returnees in the context of COVID-19, assessing outcomes across economic self-sufficiency, social stability, and psychosocial wellbeing — all dimensions of sustainable reintegration under action 21h. The ICRS component addresses psychosocial reintegration support, while the IGA component addresses economic reintegration. The evaluation framework explicitly assesses impact on 'resilience capacities' and 'economic self-sufficiency' of returnees. For synthesis specialists, the conclusions section (5.1), the effectiveness and impact sections (referenced in the evaluation framework), and the recommendations (5.2) would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The full report likely contains detailed findings on reintegration outcomes across multiple dimensions that would be highly relevant to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE IOM PROJECT "IMPROVING THE PROTECTION OF VULNERABLE MIGRANTS TRAVELLING THROUGH THE HORN OF AFRICA, 2013-2015, AND PREVIOUS PHASES" EVALUATION REPORT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 3, particularly regarding Actions 3c and 3e. Awareness-raising and information provision to migrants and sending communities is a core component of the project across multiple countries. The report discusses information campaigns in communities of origin (Ethiopia), information about rights and options for safe voluntary returns (Djibouti, Puntland, Somaliland), and the establishment of Migration Response Centers (MRCs) as information and assistance points along migration routes. Finding 7 directly addresses the effectiveness of migration-related information distribution and attitude change in sending communities, though noting limited hard evidence. Recommendation 8 on longer-term funding for behavior change interventions is also relevant. The sections on awareness-raising effectiveness (Finding 7) and the project's activities in Ethiopia and transit countries would be the most useful starting points for human reviewers. However, information provision appears as one of several project components rather than the sole focus, so depth is moderate.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE IOM PROJECT "IMPROVING THE PROTECTION OF VULNERABLE MIGRANTS TRAVELLING THROUGH THE HORN OF AFRICA, 2013-2015, AND PREVIOUS PHASES" EVALUATION REPORT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project's central purpose is explicitly the protection of vulnerable migrants — the title itself ('Improving the Protection of Vulnerable Migrants Travelling through the Horn of Africa') signals this as the primary subject. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions of vulnerability: migrants facing physical and sexual abuse from smugglers and traffickers, harsh climatic conditions, lack of legal documentation, absence of legal protection, and dangerous security conditions. The project addresses Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations), 7j (support for migrants caught in crisis situations), and 7k (involving local authorities in identification and assistance). Finding 9 directly addresses emergency services and protection mechanisms for migrants. Finding 8 covers government capacity to provide relevant information and assistance. The project's work in Djibouti, Puntland, and Somaliland on direct emergency assistance and the Ethiopia component on awareness-raising both relate to vulnerability reduction. Recommendations 2 and 4 on capacity building and coordination also bear on sustainable vulnerability response. Human reviewers should prioritize the findings sections on emergency services (Finding 9), government capacity (Finding 8), and the sustainability section (5.4), as well as the context section describing the vulnerability profile of migrants in the region. The full report likely contains additional evidence on vulnerability identification and response beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE IOM PROJECT "IMPROVING THE PROTECTION OF VULNERABLE MIGRANTS TRAVELLING THROUGH THE HORN OF AFRICA, 2013-2015, AND PREVIOUS PHASES" EVALUATION REPORT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly regarding voluntary return and reintegration. Assisted Voluntary Returns (AVR) is explicitly listed as a core project activity, and raising migrants' awareness of 'options for safe voluntary returns' is described as a primary focus in Djibouti, Puntland, and Somaliland. This relates directly to Actions 21b (gender-responsive voluntary return programmes based on free and informed consent) and 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration). The project's multi-country approach to return also touches on Action 21a (bilateral/regional cooperation frameworks). However, the excerpts do not reveal deep analytical findings specifically on return outcomes, reintegration sustainability, or readmission procedures — AVR appears as a project activity rather than as a subject of dedicated evaluative analysis. Human reviewers should examine the full findings sections on emergency services and government capacity (Findings 8 and 9) and the sustainability section for any deeper treatment of return programming outcomes. It is possible the full report contains more substantive evidence on AVR than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF IOM PROJECT: ENHANCING PROTECTION AND IMPROVING KNOWLEDGE ON THE RISKS OF IRREGULAR MIGRATION IN SUDAN
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 3. The project explicitly includes an awareness raising component aimed at informing migrants about risks of irregular migration, and the evaluation devotes substantial attention to assessing the effectiveness of this component. Finding 5 addresses IOM's reconceptualization of awareness raising, and Recommendations 7 and 8 provide detailed guidance on outreach and information strategies. The MRRC itself functions partly as an information point for migrants (relating to Action 3c — accessible information points along migration routes). The evaluation's critique of one-way information campaigns and endorsement of more interactive, needs-based approaches (Community Conversations model) would likely add value to a synthesis on what works in migrant information provision. The sections on awareness raising reconceptualization (Finding 5, Section 4.1 concluding comments, Recommendations 7-8) appear most relevant for human reviewers. The report also touches on Action 3e (awareness-raising campaigns and pre-departure orientation). Worth noting that information provision is one of several themes, not the sole focus.

References.Title: INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF IOM PROJECT: ENHANCING PROTECTION AND IMPROVING KNOWLEDGE ON THE RISKS OF IRREGULAR MIGRATION IN SUDAN
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project's core purpose is to enhance protection for migrants in vulnerable situations — including refugees, asylum seekers, trafficking victims, and irregular migrants — which directly aligns with this objective. The MRRC is explicitly designed to provide identification, assistance, and referral services to migrants in vulnerable situations (relating to Actions 7b, 7c, 7k). Finding 6 addresses progress in supporting migrants across protection and health concerns; Finding 10 documents positive impact on migrant service users; and Finding 2 assesses the MRRC as an appropriate strategy for addressing multi-faceted protection needs. The evaluation covers mixed migration flows including Eritreans, Ethiopians, and other nationalities, with attention to different vulnerability profiles. Recommendations 1-4 on MRRC management directly concern strengthening systems for identifying and assisting vulnerable migrants. The concluding comments discuss the MRRC as a 'one-stop shop' for protection services including referrals — closely aligned with Action 7k (involving local authorities and stakeholders in identification and referral). For human reviewers, Findings 2, 6, and 10, along with the MRRC management recommendations and concluding comments, appear most relevant as starting points. The full report likely contains additional evidence on vulnerability response mechanisms beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF IOM PROJECT: ENHANCING PROTECTION AND IMPROVING KNOWLEDGE ON THE RISKS OF IRREGULAR MIGRATION IN SUDAN
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The MRRC is explicitly designed as a service access point for migrants, providing basic medical assistance, psychosocial support, information, referrals, and assisted voluntary return — directly relating to Action 15c (holistic and accessible service points at local level that are migrant-inclusive). Finding 6 documents progress in supporting migrants across protection and health concerns, and Finding 10 provides evidence of positive impact on migrant service users. The evaluation assesses the MRRC model's effectiveness as a one-stop shop for service delivery to migrants regardless of status. Recommendations 1-4 address strengthening service provision systems, quality standards, and client management — all relevant to improving migrant access to basic services. The concluding comments discuss the MRRC's role in providing a 'full range of services' to migrants. For human reviewers, Findings 2, 6, 8, and 10, along with the MRRC management recommendations, appear most relevant. The report's focus on service delivery to mixed migration populations including irregular migrants is particularly pertinent to Action 15b (ensuring cooperation does not compromise irregular migrants' access to services). Worth noting that service access is one of two main themes alongside awareness raising.

References.Title: PROGRAMME EVALUATION REPORT, NATIONAL DISASTER CONSORTIUM ( 2015-2020), MULTI-YEAR HUMANITARIAN PROGRAMME (MYHP)-PILLAR 1
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly around disaster risk reduction, resilience-building, and climate-related displacement prevention. The programme explicitly addresses Sendai Framework for DRR 2015-2030 (Actions 2a and 2i are directly relevant), and the report discusses interventions targeting slow-onset disasters (drought, heatwave), sudden-onset disasters (floods, earthquakes), and community resilience-building — all of which relate to Actions 2h, 2i, 2j, and 2k. The executive summary notes the programme's alignment with national policy frameworks and Sendai Framework priorities. The discussion of LRRD (Linking Relief, Rehabilitation and Development) approaches, disaster preparedness training across 112 districts, and the Response Trigger Mechanism also touches on Action 2c (early warning and emergency procedures). The sections on lessons learned (particularly items 6 and 7 on response triggers and slow-onset disasters) and recommendations (items 8, 9, 11) appear particularly relevant for human reviewers to examine. It is worth noting that this report concerns internal displacement and disaster-affected populations in Pakistan rather than cross-border migration per se, which somewhat limits its direct applicability to GCM Objective 2's framing around international migration drivers.

References.Title: Final evaluation of the project: “Bosnia and Herzegovina: Enhancing social cohesion in communities hosting people on the move”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly regarding health services and education access for migrants. The project explicitly addresses health service provision in Temporary Reception Centers (TRCs), including COVID-19 prevention, health screenings, vaccination campaigns, and the development of public health guidelines (policies) for ongoing health care delivery. The report also discusses enrollment of migrant youth in vocational secondary schools and the issuance of certificates valid in EU countries. These themes connect directly to Actions 15e (health needs of migrants in national health policies) and 15f (inclusive education for migrant children and youth). The executive summary, conclusions, and sustainability sections appear particularly relevant — they discuss the development of health policies for TRCs, capacity strengthening of public health institutions, and educational inclusion. The report also touches on psychosocial support and infrastructure improvements that facilitate service access. A synthesis specialist would likely want to review the sections on public health coordination, the health policy outputs, and the educational inclusion findings, while noting that service access is analyzed primarily through a social cohesion lens rather than as a standalone rights-based framework.

References.Title: Final evaluation of the project: “Bosnia and Herzegovina: Enhancing social cohesion in communities hosting people on the move”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. Social cohesion between migrants/refugees/asylum-seekers and host communities is the explicit and central subject of the entire project and evaluation. The project title itself — 'Bosnia and Herzegovina: Enhancing social cohesion in communities hosting people on the move' — directly mirrors the language of this objective. The evaluation assesses outcomes across individual, relationship, community, and institutional domains of social cohesion change, providing multi-level evidence on what works. Actions 16a (mutual respect, integration policies), 16f (community centres and intercultural dialogue), 16g (peer-to-peer exchanges, vocational and civic integration), and 16h (multicultural activities through sports, arts, cultural events) are all directly addressed through the project's social cohesion activities — workshops, theater, sports events, cultural exchanges, the No Nation Fashion initiative, and community dialogues. The PULSE survey social cohesion index provides quantitative evidence on cohesion trends in project vs. non-project communities. The report's conclusions, impact section, and recommendations sections appear particularly rich for synthesis purposes, especially the analysis of what types of interactions (meaningful engagement vs. mere proximity) drive cohesion improvements, the role of infrastructure works as 'buyout' for social cohesion, and the differential effects on women and youth. The institutional domain findings on strengthened cooperation between local communities, municipalities, and TRCs also relate to Action 16f. This report appears to be a strong candidate for in-depth review by any synthesis specialist working on Objective 16.

References.Title: Final evaluation of the project: “Bosnia and Herzegovina: Enhancing social cohesion in communities hosting people on the move”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute substantial evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 17, particularly regarding media reporting and public discourse on migration. One of the project's three explicit outcomes is 'Foster a change of narrative over refugee and migrant issues in the media and public discourse,' which directly aligns with Action 17c (promoting independent, quality reporting and sensitizing media professionals). The project trained journalists and public institution spokespersons on sensitive reporting, developed a publicly available curriculum (syllabus), and engaged BIRN — a prominent investigative journalism outlet — on migration themes. The report also addresses changing public perceptions and attitudes toward migrants (Outcome 2), awareness-raising campaigns, and the No Nation Fashion initiative as a tool for countering negative stereotypes. Actions 17c (media sensitization), 17f (awareness-raising campaigns on positive contributions of migration), and 17g (engaging community leaders to prevent intolerance) all appear covered. The evaluation's conclusions note limitations in measuring the actual effects of journalist training on reporting quality, which itself is a relevant evidence gap for synthesis purposes. The sections on media activities (BIRN engagement, journalist training curriculum, social media campaigns) and the impact section on changing perceptions would be particularly valuable for human reviewers to examine. This is scored slightly below the FUNDAMENTAL threshold because the media/discourse theme, while prominent, shares space with the social cohesion and health themes, and the evaluation acknowledges gaps in evidence on media outcome achievement.

References.Title: External ex-post combined Evaluation: of IOM project support for the implementation of the Migration Information and Data Analysis System (MIDAS)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The MIDAS system is fundamentally a migration data collection and management tool — its core purpose is to register, process, and analyze data on persons entering and exiting Paraguay. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how the system enabled the DGM to 'better monitor data about migration flows,' and the project objectives explicitly reference generating 'reports on entry and exit of persons through selected border posts.' This appears directly relevant to actions 1a (improving migration data collection), 1c (building national capacities in data collection and analysis), 1i (using administrative records including border records for statistical purposes), and 1j (developing country-specific migration profiles). The evaluation also touches on data quality and reliability challenges, noting that 'several challenges prevented the system from operating efficiently and produce continued reliable results.' The conclusions and recommendations section on sustainability and project design would appear particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist, as they address the conditions under which border data systems can be maintained and produce reliable outputs. The cross-cutting issues section also briefly mentions that MIDAS 'provides an opportunity to gain insights into migrant profiles and behavior,' which connects to disaggregated data use. It is possible the full report contains more detailed analysis of data outputs and their policy use than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: External ex-post combined Evaluation: of IOM project support for the implementation of the Migration Information and Data Analysis System (MIDAS)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The entire evaluation concerns the implementation of MIDAS — a border information management system — across ten border posts in Paraguay, making border management the explicit and central subject of the report. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis directly relevant to multiple associated actions: action 11b (integrated border management through ICT, pre-screening, and efficient border crossing procedures) appears to be the core focus; action 11a (international cooperation on border management capacity-building) is addressed through IOM-GoP collaboration; action 11d (technical cooperation agreements for border management assets and equipment) is covered through the equipment provision and technical assistance described across all five projects; and action 11g (cross-border collaboration) is touched upon through the Interpol connection. The evaluation appears to provide rich evidence on what makes border management technology implementations succeed or fail, including infrastructure requirements, government ownership, training, sustainability planning, and institutional continuity. For a synthesis specialist, the findings on effectiveness and sustainability, the conclusions on project design and handover, and the recommendations on infrastructure assessment and government capacity would appear particularly valuable. The lessons learned section also offers transferable insights for similar border management initiatives. The full report likely contains additional operational detail beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Evaluation of the Forstering Health and Protection to vulnerable migrants transiting though Morocco, Tunisia, Egpyte, Libya, Yemen and Sudan
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project explicitly targets 'vulnerable migrants' throughout, and the excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how health and protection services are delivered to migrants in situations of vulnerability — including victims of trafficking (VoTs), victims of GBV, unaccompanied and separated children (UASCs), and migrants in conflict-affected settings. This aligns closely with Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerability), 7c (gender-responsive services including health care and psychosocial support), and 7k (involvement of local actors in identification and assistance). The report appears to cover multiple dimensions of vulnerability response: direct medical and psychosocial assistance, referral mechanisms, mobile clinics, and partnerships with CSOs. The sections on Outcome 2 (Direct Humanitarian Support) and the cross-cutting issues section on gender and human rights would appear most relevant for human reviewers to prioritize. The report's multi-country scope across MENA also adds comparative value. It is one of several themes covered, so the score reflects relevance rather than centrality.

References.Title: Evaluation of the Forstering Health and Protection to vulnerable migrants transiting though Morocco, Tunisia, Egpyte, Libya, Yemen and Sudan
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly Action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in national and local health care policies and plans). The project's entire mandate centers on improving health access for vulnerable migrants and advancing Universal Health Coverage — themes that map directly onto this objective. The excerpts suggest extensive analysis across multiple dimensions: direct health service delivery (medical, humanitarian, psychosocial), policy advocacy for migrant-inclusive national health strategies, capacity building of health providers, and referral to public health infrastructure. Key achievements highlighted — such as inclusion of migrants in national COVID-19 vaccination campaigns, the National Strategic Plan on Migration & Health in Morocco, and inclusion of migrants in Egypt's National AIDS Program — represent concrete evidence on how migrant health access is being operationalized at national level. Actions 15a (non-discrimination in service delivery), 15b (safe access without immigration enforcement risk), 15c (accessible service points), and 15e are all substantively addressed. For human reviewers, the Effectiveness section (Outcomes 1, 2, and 3), the Impact section, the Sustainability section, and the country-specific recommendations would appear most valuable as starting points. The multi-country scope across six MENA countries adds significant comparative value for a synthesis. It is possible the full report contains even richer evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: IOM/UNDP JOINT CONFLICT REDUCTION PROGRAMME MID-TERM REVIEW
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16, particularly around social cohesion and community inclusion in conflict-affected contexts. Social cohesion is an explicit and central theme of the JCRP — the programme's overall objective is to 'strengthen Government and civil society initiatives that promote social cohesion, peace consolidation and pluralism.' Multiple programme outputs directly address social cohesion: Output 2 focuses on inclusive peace processes and inter-tribal dialogues; Output 5 supports community stabilization and resilience through activities that 'promote diversity and social cohesion.' The report's theory of change, findings on effectiveness, and recommendations all engage substantively with social cohesion outcomes. This connects most directly to GCM Actions 16a (promoting mutual respect and social cohesion), 16f (community centres and intercultural dialogue), and 16h (multicultural activities). It is worth noting that the 'migrants' in this context are primarily internally displaced persons and cross-border nomadic communities rather than international migrants in the conventional GCM sense — synthesis specialists should be aware of this framing difference. The sections on programme outputs (particularly Outputs 2 and 5), the theory of change, lessons learned, and recommendations on scaling up the peace dividend approach would appear most relevant for human reviewers to prioritize. The full report likely contains additional evidence on social cohesion programming beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION REPORT MID-TERM INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO THE GOVERNMENT OF KYRGYZSTAN TO STRENGTHEN THE LEGAL AND OPERATIONAL FRAMEWORK FOR COMBATING TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The entire project evaluated is explicitly dedicated to combating trafficking in persons — this is the central subject of both the project and the evaluation. The report title itself references 'combating trafficking in persons,' and all four project objectives directly address TIP prevention, prosecution, and victim protection. The excerpts suggest substantive coverage of multiple associated actions: Action 10a (legislative alignment with international standards, including the Palermo Protocol framework); Action 10e (victim-centered approach, identification and protection of VoTs); Action 10f (legal definitions and distinctions in TIP legislation); Action 10g (strengthening prosecution of traffickers, avoiding criminalization of victims); Action 10h (protection and assistance to VoT, NRM development); Action 10i (training programmes for law enforcement, prosecutors, judges on identifying trafficking); and Action 10j (awareness-raising campaigns on TIP risks). The executive summary, conclusions on effectiveness, and sustainability sections all appear particularly rich with evidence on legislative reform, NRM establishment, prosecutorial capacity building, and awareness raising. The findings on legal amendments adopted, SOPs developed, training outcomes, and prosecution statistics would likely be highly valuable for a synthesis on this objective. Human reviewers should prioritize the effectiveness and sustainability sections, as well as the recommendations, for the most substantive evidence.

References.Title: EVALUATION REPORT MID-TERM INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO THE GOVERNMENT OF KYRGYZSTAN TO STRENGTHEN THE LEGAL AND OPERATIONAL FRAMEWORK FOR COMBATING TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12, particularly regarding Action 12b (specialized training for first responders and government officials on identification and referral of victims of trafficking) and Action 12c (gender-responsive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures). The project's development of a National Referral Mechanism (NRM), Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for victim identification and referral, national identification criteria, and training for prosecutors, judges, and law enforcement on victim-centered approaches are all directly relevant. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of these mechanisms in the effectiveness and sustainability sections. The conclusions on NRM development, SOP validation, and training outcomes appear particularly relevant. A synthesis specialist should review the sections on Objective 2 (NRM development) and Objective 3 (prosecutorial and judicial training) for the most pertinent content on screening, identification, and referral procedures.

References.Title: RE-HEALTH INTERNAL EVALUATION SUMMARY REPORT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears highly relevant for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The RE-HEALTH project's core deliverables center on developing an electronic Personal Health Record (e-PHR) system and associated database for collecting, storing, and analyzing health data on migrants and refugees. The excerpts suggest substantive engagement with data collection methodology, data standardization, and data management — directly relating to Actions 1a (harmonizing methodologies for data collection), 1c (building national capacities in data collection), and 1i (enhancing collaboration to produce migration-related statistics using administrative records). The report discusses the development of a platform for data storage accessible to transit and destination countries, the feasibility study assessing the e-PHR's acceptability and impact, and the challenges of data collection in field settings. The sections on D-8 (Electronic PHR), D-9 (Online platform and data sets), and D-10 (Training on utilization of PHR and data collection requirements) would appear particularly valuable for human reviewers conducting a synthesis on evidence-based data collection for migration policy. The report also touches on data protection considerations and interoperability with national systems. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: RE-HEALTH INTERNAL EVALUATION SUMMARY REPORT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly Action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in national health care policies and plans). The RE-HEALTH project's core purpose is to expand access to health services for migrants and refugees arriving in Europe through standardized health assessment tools (e-PHR), training of health professionals and mediators, and establishing linkages between reception areas and national health systems. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: health service delivery capacity (training of 150+ health professionals and mediators), culturally-sensitive service delivery (health mediation model, multilingual support), reducing communication barriers (health mediators for represented languages), and linkages between migrant reception and national health systems. The feasibility study (D-6) assessing the e-PHR's acceptability and impact, the training deliverables (D-7), and the e-PHR implementation (D-8) all appear directly relevant to Action 15e. The report also touches on continuity of care, cost-effectiveness of health provision, and the challenge of ensuring migrants receive health assessments beyond emergency care — all core concerns under Objective 15. Human reviewers conducting a synthesis on migrant access to health services should prioritize the sections on D-7, D-8, and D-6, as well as the conclusions section. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: ÉVALUATION EX-POST DU PROGRAMME PILOTE DE  VOLONTARIAT DES JEUNES ISSUS DE LA DIASPORA  MALGACHE (PROGRAMME LOHARANO)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The LOHARANO programme is explicitly and centrally designed to mobilize diaspora youth for sustainable development contributions in Madagascar — this is the programme's raison d'être. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly relevant to GCM Objective 19: (1) establishing government structures for diaspora engagement (Action 19d) — the MAE's dedicated diaspora direction and its institutional capacity-building are central evaluation themes; (2) diaspora voluntary and philanthropic engagement (Action 19f) — the volunteering mechanism itself; (3) knowledge and skills transfer by diaspora (Action 19c and 19j) — the report analyzes non-financial contributions including cultural exchange, skills transfer to entrepreneurs and students; (4) partnerships between local authorities, diaspora organizations, and communities (Action 19j) — the three-commune community approach and partnerships with civil society organizations are extensively analyzed. The report also appears to address Action 19h (flexible modalities for diaspora travel and engagement) and Action 19i (professional engagement without losing status). Key sections for human reviewers to prioritize would include: the analysis of the 'dispositif d'enracinement' as a model for diaspora community integration, the findings on MAE institutional capacity, the impact assessment on local entrepreneurs and communities, and the lessons learned and recommendations sections which offer transferable insights for diaspora engagement programme design. The report's pilot nature and its explicit focus on replicability make it particularly valuable for evidence synthesis on diaspora development contributions.

References.Title: Ex-post internal evaluation of the project “Building the Capacity of the Mauritian Government to Strengthen Linkages with the Mauritian Diaspora”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1, particularly around diaspora mapping and profiling as a form of migration data collection. The project explicitly produced a mapping and profiling report covering the Mauritian diaspora in three countries (Australia, Canada, UK), and a core project outcome was ensuring 'the Government of Mauritius' diaspora engagement is informed by accurate and up to date data and evidence.' The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of data collection methodology, advisory group involvement, and the use of findings to inform policy — directly relating to Actions 1d (data on contributions of migrants and diasporas), 1j (country-specific migration profiles), and 1k (research on migration and sustainable development). The evaluation also discusses a 'diaspora database' established through the mapping exercise and data protection considerations. The conclusions and sustainability sections discuss whether the mapping report is being used to inform policies and programmes. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the effectiveness and sustainability sections, particularly the results matrix breakdown and the discussion of the mapping report's utilization, as well as the recommendations. Note that the report's primary focus is on government capacity-building and diaspora engagement mechanisms, so data collection is one of two main themes rather than the sole focus.

References.Title: Ex-post internal evaluation of the project “Building the Capacity of the Mauritian Government to Strengthen Linkages with the Mauritian Diaspora”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The project's stated overall objective — 'contribute to increased diaspora contribution to the social and economic development of Mauritius' — directly mirrors the language and intent of Objective 19. The excerpts suggest the evaluation comprehensively examines mechanisms for diaspora engagement with development, including: establishment of a Technical Working Group and Diaspora Cell (Action 19d — dedicated diaspora offices/focal points); diaspora mapping and profiling to understand skills and contributions (Action 19j — mapping diasporas and skills); a National Action Plan for diaspora engagement (Action 19b — integrating migration into development planning); diaspora advisory groups and dialogue series (Action 19d — diaspora policy advisory boards); and the Ghana study tour examining best practices in diaspora engagement. Multiple findings and conclusions directly address whether these mechanisms are functional, sustainable, and contributing to development outcomes. The evaluation also discusses indirect impacts such as attitude shifts toward diaspora engagement and the creation of a diaspora database. For a synthesis specialist, the results matrix breakdown (particularly Outcome 1 and 2 indicators), the sustainability section on product/mechanism utilization, the impact section on attitude shifts, and the recommendations on National Action Plan validation and diaspora dialogue continuation would appear particularly valuable as starting points. Actions 19c, 19d, 19g, 19h, and 19j appear most directly addressed. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “IMPROVING KNOWLEDGE, ENFORCEMENT AND COORDINATION IN COUNTER-TRAFFICKING”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The entire project — 'Improving Knowledge, Enforcement, and Coordination in Counter-Trafficking' — is explicitly and comprehensively focused on preventing, combating, and eradicating trafficking in persons, directly mirroring the language and substance of GCM Objective 10. The project title, all three outcomes, and all nine outputs directly address counter-trafficking themes. Multiple associated actions appear well-covered: Action 10a (ratification and implementation of the Palermo Protocol — Rwanda's legal framework ratification is discussed); Action 10c (monitoring trafficking routes and strengthening cooperation on prosecution and victim identification); Action 10e (measures addressing vulnerabilities of trafficking victims, access to justice); Action 10g (strengthening legislation and prosecution, avoiding criminalization of victims — the Ministerial Order and NAP development); Action 10h (protection and assistance to trafficking victims — Isange One Stop Center, National Referral Mechanism); Action 10i (training programmes for law enforcement, prosecutors, judges — 157 officials trained); Action 10j (awareness-raising campaigns). The evaluation's findings on effectiveness (training outcomes, coordination mechanisms, legal framework development), sustainability, and lessons learned would likely provide substantive evidence across multiple associated actions. Synthesis specialists should prioritize the effectiveness section (Outcomes 1-3 analysis), the conclusions on sustainability factors, and the recommendations — particularly those on finalizing the NAP and Ministerial Order, and on building upon law enforcement training results. The gender and human rights section also appears relevant given trafficking's disproportionate impact on women and girls.

References.Title: Final Evaluation of the EU-IOM Joint Initiative for migrant protection and reintegration in the horn of Africa
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The JI-HoA explicitly included a 'Migration Data' pillar as one of its five activity pillars, and the evaluation appears to have assessed this pillar substantively. The excerpts indicate that the Regional Data Hub (RDH) was a core component of the programme, with activities including establishing the East and Horn of Africa Regional Data Hub, expanding DTM flow monitoring methods, conducting regional research and analysis, engaging governments in data collection, and providing information management and technical training. The evaluation's Specific Outcome 1 directly addresses whether 'partner countries and relevant stakeholders developed or strengthened evidence-based return and reintegration procedures,' with data generation as a key mechanism. The conclusions section explicitly notes that 'the JI-HoA has made important contributions to the availability of data and research on migration trends in the region' and that stakeholders 'explicitly appreciated the work of the Regional Data Hub in terms of data production and capacity building.' Recommendation 4 specifically calls for continued support to the Regional Data Hub. These elements relate closely to GCM actions 1a (harmonizing methodologies), 1c (building national capacities in data collection), and 1f (regional centres for research and training). The sections most worth reviewing for a synthesis specialist would be the Specific Outcome 1 findings, the conclusions on data contributions, and Recommendation 4. However, the data theme is one of several covered, and the report's primary focus is on return and reintegration rather than data systems per se, which prevents a FUNDAMENTAL score.

References.Title: Final Evaluation of the EU-IOM Joint Initiative for migrant protection and reintegration in the horn of Africa
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The JI-HoA explicitly operated with a 'protection' pillar and a rights-based approach, and the evaluation assesses how well the programme identified and responded to migrant vulnerabilities. The excerpts indicate that the programme provided dedicated individual support for the most vulnerable cases, conducted vulnerability assessments, and offered specialized assistance including medical escorts, psychosocial support, and complementary reintegration assistance based on vulnerability assessments. The relevance section notes 'clear examples of enhanced attention to the needs of women and persons with disabilities' and the programme's 'strong focus on protection, identified as part of a rights-based approach.' The conclusions describe migrants facing 'dire situations (including abuse, violence and exploitation)' and the programme's role in addressing these. These elements relate to GCM actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerability), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies), and 7j (support for migrants in crisis situations). The sections most worth reviewing for a synthesis specialist would be the relevance findings on cross-cutting concerns, the protection pillar activities in the intervention logic, and the conclusions on individual-level impact. The treatment of vulnerability is substantive but embedded within a broader return-and-reintegration focus rather than being the primary analytical lens, which prevents a FUNDAMENTAL score.

References.Title: Final Evaluation of the EU-IOM Joint Initiative for migrant protection and reintegration in the horn of Africa
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Safe and dignified voluntary return and sustainable reintegration are the explicit, central subjects of the entire JI-HoA programme and its evaluation. The programme title itself — 'EU-IOM Joint Initiative for Migrant Protection and Reintegration' — directly mirrors the language of GCM Objective 21. The evaluation's three specific objectives map almost perfectly onto GCM Objective 21's associated actions: SO2 (safe, humane, dignified voluntary return) corresponds to actions 21b and 21e; SO3 (sustainable reintegration) corresponds to actions 21h and 21i; and SO1 (evidence-based return and reintegration procedures) corresponds to action 21a. The evaluation provides substantive analysis across all dimensions of return and reintegration: pre-return assistance, voluntary return processes, post-arrival reception, individual reintegration support, community-based reintegration, and structural/governance interventions. The conclusions directly address sustainability of reintegration, the role of community-based projects, and the challenges of achieving durable outcomes. All seven recommendations relate to improving return and reintegration programming. The evaluation covers a five-year programme across four countries (Djibouti, Ethiopia, Somalia, Sudan) with over 10,000 beneficiaries, providing substantial empirical evidence. For a synthesis specialist, the findings on Specific Outcomes 2 and 3, the sustainability analysis, the community-based reintegration findings, and all recommendations would be particularly valuable starting points. The integrated approach to reintegration (individual, community, structural levels) provides a rich framework for evidence synthesis on action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration) and 21i (addressing needs of communities of return). This report appears to be core evidence for any synthesis on GCM Objective 21.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION AGADEZ MIGRATION PROJECT (AGAMI) PROJECT 19/10- 10/11 2016
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to contain substantive content relevant to GCM Objective 1. The AGAMI project includes a Flow Monitoring Point (FMP) system that collects quantitative and qualitative data on migrant flows, and the report discusses the Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM) as a tool. The recommendations section dedicates considerable space to improving data collection and management systems — including consolidating multiple databases into one, establishing timetables for data submission, involving a statistician and IT expert, training government staff in FMP procedures, and expanding FMP coverage. These recommendations touch on Actions 1a (improving migration data strategies), 1c (building national capacities in data collection), and 1f (regional research and data sharing). The excerpts suggest the information/data unit section of the recommendations and the discussion of monitoring tools would be most valuable for a synthesis specialist to review. The report also mentions studies on 'Migration impact on the Agadez region economy' and 'Employment opportunities in countries of migration origin,' which relate to Action 1d and 1k. However, data collection is not the primary focus of the evaluation — it appears as a supporting theme alongside the core AVR and reintegration activities.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION AGADEZ MIGRATION PROJECT (AGAMI) PROJECT 19/10- 10/11 2016
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to contain meaningful content relevant to GCM Objective 7. The AGAMI project explicitly targets vulnerable migrants in transit centers, providing medical care, psychosocial support, shelter, food, and NFIs — directly relating to Actions 7b and 7c. The report discusses the MRRM approach as a 'multifaceted approach, combining voluntary return, direct assistance, and information campaigns' for vulnerable migrants. The project expanded its budget specifically to cover increased vulnerable cases (medical and psychosocial support). The report also mentions collaboration with HCR on asylum referral mechanisms (Annex 7 lists 'Procedure of Asylum referral mechanism IOM-HCR'), touching on Action 7k. The context section describes migrants stranded in Niger due to lack of means, overwhelmed local communities, and mixed migration flows — framing vulnerability as a central concern. The recommendations on reintegration support timing and monitoring also relate to sustained assistance for vulnerable returnees (Action 7b). A synthesis specialist would likely find the sections on direct assistance in transit centers, the MRRM approach description, and the asylum referral mechanism particularly relevant for Actions 7b, 7c, and 7j.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION AGADEZ MIGRATION PROJECT (AGAMI) PROJECT 19/10- 10/11 2016
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Assisted Voluntary Return (AVR) and sustainable reintegration are the explicit primary subjects of the AGAMI project and this evaluation. The report title itself references 'response to mixed migration flows' and the MRRM approach centers on voluntary return and reintegration. The evaluation assesses 'regional IOM reintegration procedures' as a stated objective. Multiple sections directly address return and reintegration: the AVR recommendations section discusses improving procedures, databases, and monitoring; the reintegration section provides detailed analysis of implementation challenges, timing issues, partner networks, and monitoring approaches across multiple countries of origin (Senegal, Mali, Gambia, Nigeria). The report covers Actions 21b (gender-responsive return and reintegration programmes with free, prior and informed consent), 21h (facilitating access to social protection, vocational training, employment and financial services for returnees), and 21i (addressing needs of communities to which migrants return). The IOM Reintegration guideline (AGAMI) is listed as an annex, suggesting detailed procedural documentation. For a synthesis specialist, the AVR recommendations section, the reintegration section, the executive summary's discussion of the MRRM approach, and the findings on reintegration effectiveness across multiple countries of origin would be particularly valuable starting points. The report likely offers rare evidence on multi-country reintegration program implementation in a West African context.

References.Title: IRAQ’S ECONOMIC RECOVERY THROUGH EMPLOYMENT CREATION AND REVITALIZATION OF LOCAL ECONOMIES (PHASE III)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its associated actions on investing in sustainable development, employment creation, and addressing structural drivers of displacement. The program's explicit objective — contributing to Iraq's economic recovery through employment creation and revitalization of local economies — directly addresses the structural factors (lack of employment, livelihood insecurity, inadequate infrastructure, climate vulnerability) that compel displacement. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions: vocational training and skills development (Action 2e), infrastructure rehabilitation to support climate resilience (Actions 2i, 2d), support for returnees and conflict-affected communities (Action 2b), and the program's coherence with Iraq's National Adaptation Plan and poverty reduction strategies. The executive summary and conclusions sections appear particularly rich in evidence on how employment creation, SME support, and community infrastructure address push factors for displacement. The cross-cutting section on environmental considerations and the discussion of climate-affected communities further strengthen alignment with Actions 2h and 2i. Human reviewers would likely find the effectiveness, impact, and sustainability sections most valuable for synthesis purposes, as they contain outcome-level evidence on whether these interventions actually reduce displacement drivers.

References.Title: IRAQ’S ECONOMIC RECOVERY THROUGH EMPLOYMENT CREATION AND REVITALIZATION OF LOCAL ECONOMIES (PHASE III)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. The program explicitly addresses social cohesion, inclusion of displaced persons alongside host community members, and economic empowerment — themes central to Objective 16. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: inclusive programming regardless of residential status (host community, IDP, returnee) supporting social cohesion (Action 16a, 16d); women's economic empowerment through livelihood initiatives and SME support (Action 16e); vocational training and skills development for labour market integration (Action 16g); and community-level impacts on social inclusion. The impact section explicitly identifies 'strengthened social cohesion' and 'enhanced empowerment' as top program outcomes, with the majority of beneficiaries reporting greater social cohesion. The gender inclusion section and the discussion of inclusive programming approaches appear particularly relevant. Human reviewers would likely find the impact section (social cohesion outcomes), the gender inclusivity section, and the effectiveness section on livelihood and employment access most valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF IOM'S APPROACH FOR PROVIDING ASSISTANCE AND PROTECTION TO MIGRANT CHILDREN, INCLUDING UNACCOMPANIED AND SEPARATED ONES
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to contain substantive content relevant to GCM Objective 1, particularly regarding data collection on migrant children. The executive summary explicitly discusses gaps in standardized data collection tools for migrant children, notes that data is 'not always' disaggregated by age and sex for this group, and references IOM's Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM) being updated to integrate child protection indicators. The conclusions section reinforces this, recommending that 'ROs and COs should strengthen the evidence base analysis and knowledge by designing projects aiming to improve data collection systems at national level.' Recommendation 2 also calls for mapping of children-oriented programmes with disaggregated beneficiary information. These elements appear to touch on Actions 1a (harmonizing data collection methodologies), 1c (building national capacities in data collection), and 1j (country-specific migration profiles with disaggregated data). A synthesis specialist working on GCM Objective 1 would likely find value in reviewing the sections on data gaps, DTM updates, and the recommendations on evidence base strengthening — though data is not the primary focus of the evaluation, which centers more on child protection programming effectiveness.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF IOM'S APPROACH FOR PROVIDING ASSISTANCE AND PROTECTION TO MIGRANT CHILDREN, INCLUDING UNACCOMPANIED AND SEPARATED ONES
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The evaluation is explicitly and primarily focused on IOM's approach to assisting and protecting migrant children in vulnerable situations — the core theme of GCM Objective 7. The report title itself signals this focus, and the evaluation questions, conclusions, and recommendations all center on vulnerability identification, protection, and assistance. Multiple associated actions appear directly addressed: Action 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerability, especially unaccompanied/separated children), Action 7c (gender-responsive migration policies addressing needs of migrant women, girls and boys), Action 7e (accounting for migrant children in national child protection systems), and Action 7f (protecting unaccompanied and separated children through specialized procedures, family reunification, legal guardianship, and access to services). The executive summary discusses IOM's six pillars of child-oriented assistance, the effectiveness of reintegration support, psychosocial assistance, and the gaps in child protection policy. The conclusions section directly addresses best interest assessments, family reunification, and alternative care. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on IOM's operational strategies across the six pillars, the conclusions on effectiveness of assistance modalities, and the recommendations on child protection policy institutionalization would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is likely that the full report contains substantially more evidence on vulnerability response mechanisms than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF IOM'S APPROACH FOR PROVIDING ASSISTANCE AND PROTECTION TO MIGRANT CHILDREN, INCLUDING UNACCOMPANIED AND SEPARATED ONES
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Trafficking in persons — particularly child trafficking — appears to be a significant secondary focus of the evaluation alongside broader child protection. The executive summary explicitly references IOM assisting over 95,000 trafficked persons since 2010, approximately one third of whom were migrant children, and discusses IOM's counter-trafficking activities as part of its child-oriented programming. The report discusses awareness-raising campaigns on trafficking (school campaigns, textbooks), identification and assistance to child victims of trafficking, monitoring challenges for VoTs, and prevention activities. These appear to touch on Actions 10e (measures addressing vulnerabilities of those at risk of trafficking), 10h (protection and assistance to victims), 10i (training programmes to identify trafficking signs), and 10j (awareness-raising campaigns). The sections on prevention activities, monitoring of VoTs post-assistance, and the discussion of IOM's counter-trafficking pillar would appear particularly relevant for a synthesis specialist. However, trafficking is addressed as part of a broader child protection evaluation rather than as a standalone focus, which limits the depth of analysis compared to a dedicated counter-trafficking evaluation.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF IOM'S APPROACH FOR PROVIDING ASSISTANCE AND PROTECTION TO MIGRANT CHILDREN, INCLUDING UNACCOMPANIED AND SEPARATED ONES
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Assisted Voluntary Return and Reintegration (AVRR) for migrant children appears to be a significant component of IOM's child-oriented programming evaluated in this report. The executive summary explicitly discusses AVRR as part of Pillar III (Assistance and Protection), and notes that 'income generating activities and post-reunification and reintegration assistance to migrant children and their families proved to be effective.' The report also discusses monitoring challenges for returned children (ideally 2.5 years post-reintegration but limited to 6-9 months due to financial constraints), family tracing and reunification, and the effectiveness of reintegration support including vocational training and grant schemes. These appear to touch on Actions 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes), 21g (return processes involving children requiring best interest determination and appropriate reception arrangements), and 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through social protection, psychosocial assistance, vocational training). The sections on AVRR effectiveness, monitoring challenges, and reintegration outcomes would appear particularly relevant for a synthesis specialist. The full report likely contains more detailed evidence on child-specific return and reintegration programming than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Climate Security in the Pacific Project
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its actions related to climate change, environmental degradation, and displacement (actions 2h, 2i, 2j, 2k, 2l). The Climate Security in the Pacific Project (CSP) is explicitly focused on assessing and addressing climate security risks in Pacific atoll nations — Kiribati, Tuvalu, and RMI — which are among the most vulnerable to sea-level rise, flooding, and climate-induced displacement. The project background section extensively discusses how climate change threatens to displace populations, disrupt livelihoods, and undermine peace and security in these small island states. The project's outcomes directly address building capacity to understand and respond to climate-related security risks, including displacement and forced migration pathways. The evaluation's sections on effectiveness, sustainability, and impact discuss how the project sensitized governments to embed climate security into national policies and adaptation plans — directly relevant to action 2i (adaptation and resilience strategies). The cross-cutting discussion of gender, youth, and vulnerable groups in the context of climate impacts also touches on action 2b. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the project background (Section 2.1), the effectiveness section, and the impact section, which appear to contain the most substantive analysis of climate-security linkages. The report's focus is not on migration per se but on the upstream drivers — climate security risks — that could compel movement, making it relevant but not central to a migration-focused synthesis.

References.Title: Evaluation of IOM’s Strategic and Operational Response to the COVID-19 Pandemic
