References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The evaluation appears to give substantive attention to IOM's use of data and evidence-based approaches in its COVID-19 response, with the Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM) explicitly highlighted as a key tool for evidence-based programming and decision-making. Recommendation F specifically addresses 'Data integration and M&E for future global crises,' calling on IOM's Global Data Institute to consider data needs and systems for future crises — touching on actions 1a, 1c, and 1d. The executive summary notes that 'an evidence-based approach and the use of data, particularly through the DTM was integral to IOM's response and made a significant contribution to wider UN efforts.' The evaluation also discusses multidisciplinary data collection across health, socio-economic, and points of entry (PoE) initiatives, and notes that core indicators were created but not widely used. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on data use in the COVID-19 response (particularly the Impact and Sustainability sections) and Recommendation F would appear most relevant as starting points. The report's focus is on pandemic response rather than migration data systems per se, so this is a secondary rather than primary theme, but the depth of treatment appears sufficient to warrant review.

References.Title: Evaluation of IOM’s Strategic and Operational Response to the COVID-19 Pandemic
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The evaluation gives recurring attention to vulnerable migrants throughout, including migrants in camps, irregular migrants, stranded migrants, migrant women and children, and those in need of MHPSS. The Impact section explicitly discusses 'assistance to migrants (including vulnerable migrants)' as a key impact area, noting IOM's role in including migrants in national vaccination plans and reaching hard-to-reach populations. Recommendation I addresses gender considerations and heightened risks for migrant women during the pandemic. Recommendation J on holistic health services for migrants also touches on vulnerability. The evaluation discusses IOM's work in camp settings (Bangladesh, Greece), support to stranded migrants, and AVRR programmes for vulnerable returnees — relating to actions 7b, 7c, 7j, and 7k. However, vulnerability is addressed as one of several themes rather than the primary analytical focus. For a synthesis specialist, the Impact section (particularly the subsection on assistance to vulnerable migrants) and Recommendations I and J would appear most relevant as starting points for review.

References.Title: Evaluation of IOM’s Strategic and Operational Response to the COVID-19 Pandemic
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. Access to health services for migrants is a central theme throughout the evaluation, with IOM's health programming explicitly identified as the primary driver of its COVID-19 response. The evaluation discusses IOM's work to ensure continuity of essential health services for vulnerable communities, inclusion of migrants in national vaccination plans, WASH services, MHPSS, and the repurposing of Migration Health Assessment Centers. Recommendation J specifically calls for a more holistic health service for migrants, addressing action 15e directly. Recommendation G advocates for universal health coverage for migrants and their inclusion in national responses — also directly relevant to 15e. The evaluation also discusses socio-economic services including cash-based interventions (CBI) and MHPSS. The Impact section contains substantive discussion of health service delivery outcomes. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on health programming effectiveness (Impact section, Recommendation J), the discussion of migrant inclusion in national health plans, and Recommendation G on universal health coverage would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional relevant evidence on health service access beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Informe de la evaluación final para el Proyecto de “Fortalecimiento de capacidades para prevenir y combatir el tráfico ilícito de migrantes por mar en México”.
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project's Resultado 2 component appears explicitly focused on improving the collection, processing, and exchange of information on migrant smuggling by sea in Mexico — a direct instantiation of the data collection and evidence-based policymaking theme at the heart of Objective 1. The excerpts suggest that multiple outputs directly address data gaps: the 'Mar Adentro' diagnostic study identifying maritime smuggling routes, the 'Diagnóstico sobre procesos de recolección, procesamiento e intercambio de información,' and the COVID-19 rapid assessment. The evaluation questions explicitly ask about changes in 'availability of relevant information and its use' and how the project 'strengthened capacities of Mexican authorities in collection, processing and exchange of information.' Conclusions and recommendations directly address the need for more and better data, statistical quality assessments, and integration of indicators into Mexico's national statistical system (SNIEG). Actions 1a (comprehensive data strategy), 1c (building national capacities in data collection), 1i (collaboration between migration units and national statistical offices), and 1j (country-specific migration profiles) appear most directly addressed. For human reviewers, the conclusions section (3.1) and recommendations section (3.2) — particularly those on MigranTIC, INEGI collaboration, and the Centro de Excelencia — would appear most valuable as starting points. The full report likely contains additional depth in the findings sections not captured in these excerpts.

References.Title: Informe de la evaluación final para el Proyecto de “Fortalecimiento de capacidades para prevenir y combatir el tráfico ilícito de migrantes por mar en México”.
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 9. The project's explicit and primary focus is on preventing and combating the smuggling of migrants (tráfico ilícito de migrantes) by sea in Mexico — precisely the subject of Objective 9. The project title, evaluation questions, all three knowledge products, and the regional policy work all center on this theme. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: smuggling routes (Action 9b — sharing information on smuggling routes and modus operandi), inter-institutional coordination for detection and response (Action 9c — cooperation protocols along migration routes), regional policy frameworks through the CRM (Action 9f — prevention partnerships), and capacity building of Mexican authorities. The 'Mar Adentro' study identifying four maritime smuggling routes, the diagnostic on information systems, and the regional work plan 2019-2025 all appear directly relevant to multiple associated actions. The evaluation's conclusions on partial achievement of objectives, lessons learned about inter-agency implementation (OIM-UNODC), and recommendations on strengthening data platforms and regional cooperation would likely provide valuable evidence for a synthesis. Human reviewers should prioritize the executive summary, section 1.1 (project context), section 3.1 (conclusions), and section 3.2 (recommendations) as starting points, noting that the full report likely contains richer findings on smuggling response mechanisms.

References.Title: TOWARDS AN EFFECTIVE MIGRANTS' RIGHT TO HEALTH IN CENTRAL ASIA: ASSISTING GOVERNMENTS IN ENHANCING THE PROVISION OF HEALTH SERVICES FOR MIGRANTS - KYRGYZSTAN
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly Action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in national and local health care policies and plans). The project's explicit purpose — 'strengthening migrants' right to health by increasing the availability and accessibility of health services for migrants' — directly aligns with the core theme of this GCM objective. The project title itself references 'Provision of Health Services for Migrants,' and the evaluation assesses the development of 'comprehensive and evidence-based migration health policies and legislation.' Multiple conclusions directly address health service access: the assessment of existing legislation and policies (Output 1.1), recommendations for improving legal frameworks (Output 1.2), capacity building of Ministries of Health, and the establishment of dialogue on migrants' right to health. The evaluation also notes that migrants were recognized as a separate category requiring dedicated health policy attention — a key step toward migrant-inclusive service delivery. For a synthesis specialist, the conclusions on effectiveness (particularly Outcomes 1 and 2 on legal frameworks and political commitment), the sustainability section on government ownership, and the recommendations on follow-up capacity building and insurance system alignment would appear particularly valuable. The regional scope covering Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, and Turkmenistan provides comparative evidence on health service provision challenges in Central Asia. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: MALDIVES: STRENGTHENING GOVERNMENT CAPACITY IN MIGRATION HEALTH POLICY DEVELOPMENT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly Action 15e on incorporating health needs of migrants in national health care policies and plans. The project's primary objective is explicitly to promote migrants' right to health through developing a National Migration Health Policy and Coordination Framework — directly addressing the core theme of this GCM objective. The evaluation examines in depth the process of developing a policy framework to ensure non-discriminatory access to health services for all migrants regardless of status, including undocumented workers. Multiple evaluation criteria (relevance, effectiveness, sustainability, impact) are assessed through the lens of health service access for migrants. The report appears to contain substantive analysis of: the barriers migrants face in accessing health services (insurance gaps, documentation status, affordability); the policy design process for migrant-inclusive health service delivery; the coordination mechanism for migrant health oversight; and the sustainability challenges for institutionalizing migrant health access. The executive summary, conclusions (particularly C1, C5, C7, C8), and recommendations (R1, R2, R3) would appear particularly valuable starting points for human reviewers conducting a synthesis on this objective. The Maldives context — where 40% of the workforce is foreign and a large proportion are undocumented — makes this a particularly instructive case study for evidence on health access policies for migrants in vulnerable situations.

References.Title: LIFE-SAVING ASSISTANCE TO BURUNDIAN REFUGEES IN TANZANIA PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7, particularly regarding the identification and assistance of vulnerable migrants (Actions 7b, 7e, 7f, 7j, 7k). The evaluation appears to dedicate substantive attention to People with Special Needs (PSNs) — including unaccompanied and separated children (UMC), pregnant women, the elderly, and persons with disabilities — across Outcome 4. The excerpts describe fit-to-travel checks as a systematic identification mechanism, medical escort provision, pre-arrival communication to camp command about PSN profiles, and cumulative tracking of 14,306 PSNs including 2,974 UMC. Recommendation 4 specifically calls for strengthening the PSN information base. The report also addresses dignified transportation standards and the particular challenges faced by vulnerable groups during transit. For a synthesis specialist, the Outcome 4 section and associated recommendations would appear most relevant as starting points, along with the discussion of fit-to-travel checks as a vulnerability identification mechanism at border entry points. The report's focus on emergency humanitarian response means vulnerability is addressed in a specific operational context rather than as a standalone policy theme, which limits its depth somewhat.

References.Title: LIFE-SAVING ASSISTANCE TO BURUNDIAN REFUGEES IN TANZANIA PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 8. The project's explicit objective — 'To prevent loss of lives and suffering among Burundian refugees through dignified emergency evacuation, transportation and relocation' — directly aligns with the life-saving core of GCM Objective 8. The report appears to provide substantial evidence on coordinated international efforts to save lives in a mass displacement emergency context. Key areas of alignment include: emergency evacuation and transportation operations from border entry points to camps (relating to Action 8a on protecting migrants' right to life); fit-to-travel checks as a systematic screening mechanism; inter-agency coordination through the Transport Sector Working Group; and the role of communication systems in emergency preparedness. The evaluation's impact assessment concludes that 'loss of lives and suffering among Burundian refugees was prevented through dignified emergency evacuation and relocation.' The report also touches on the value of IOM's contribution relative to other partners in life-saving operations. For a synthesis specialist, the executive summary's impact assessment, Outcome 3 (transportation to safety), Outcome 4 (PSN access to safety), and the conclusions on IOM's critical contribution would appear most valuable as starting points. The report provides a concrete operational case study of coordinated life-saving response at scale (200,000+ people), which would be rare and valuable evidence for a synthesis on this objective.

References.Title: JOINT FINAL EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF PROJECTS CT.0783 AND CT.0810
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7, particularly regarding identification, referral, and assistance to vulnerable migrants — specifically victims of trafficking. The evaluation covers Output 3.1 (VoT referral mechanism), Output 3.2 (training on identification and assistance), and Output 3.3 (direct assistance services including medical, psychosocial, legal, and reintegration support). These activities relate closely to GCM Actions 7b (assistance and protection for migrants in vulnerable situations, including trafficking victims), 7c (gender-responsive policies for migrant women), 7e (child protection systems), and 7k (local authority involvement in identification and referral). The evaluation appears to assess both the design and effectiveness of vulnerability response mechanisms in a low-capacity context. Sections 5.3 (Outcome 3), the conclusions on protection (Section 7), and recommendations on VoT protection (Section 8C) would appear most relevant for human reviewers. The report likely offers evidence on the challenges of establishing referral systems in fragile contexts, which would add value to a synthesis on vulnerability responses.

References.Title: JOINT FINAL EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF PROJECTS CT.0783 AND CT.0810
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Counter-trafficking is the explicit and central subject of both projects evaluated. The report appears to cover all major dimensions of GCM Objective 10: prevention through awareness campaigns (Actions 10i, 10j), protection and assistance to VoT including physical, psychological and social recovery (Action 10h), prosecution support through legal frameworks and training (Action 10g), and partnership/coordination mechanisms (Actions 10c, 10d). The evaluation covers Madagascar's ratification of the Palermo Protocol (Action 10a), the development of a National Action Plan, an inter-ministerial coordination body (BNLTEH), a VoT referral mechanism, training for service providers, direct assistance to 29 VoT, and a large-scale awareness campaign. The report also addresses both internal and international trafficking, with attention to women and children. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on Outcome 3 (protection mechanisms, Section 5.3), Outcome 1 (coordination and PNA, Section 5.1), the conclusions (Section 7), and recommendations (Section 8) would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The evaluation's treatment of implementation challenges in a fragile, low-capacity context adds distinctive value. It is likely that the full report contains additional analytical depth beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: JOINT FINAL EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF PROJECTS CT.0783 AND CT.0810
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute some evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12, particularly regarding screening, assessment, and referral mechanisms for trafficking victims. Output 3.1 describes the development of a VoT identification and referral manual with standardized screening, needs assessment, risk assessment, and M&E forms. Output 3.2 covers training for government and civil society service providers on using these tools. These activities relate closely to GCM Action 12b (specialized training for first responders on identification and referral of trafficking victims) and Action 12c (gender-responsive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures). The evaluation appears to assess both the design process and early implementation of these mechanisms in a context with limited existing capacity. Sections 5.3 (Outputs 3.1 and 3.2) and the recommendations on VoT protection (Section 8C) would appear most relevant for human reviewers. The report's value for this objective is somewhat secondary to its core focus on counter-trafficking broadly, but the referral mechanism content appears substantive enough to warrant review.

References.Title: EVALUATION FINALE DE L’INITIATIVE CONJOINTE UE-OIM POUR LE RENFORCEMENT DE LA GESTION DES FRONTIÈRES, LA PROTECTION ET LA RÉINTÉGRATION DES MIGRANTS EN MAURITANIE
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, border management appears to be one of the two or three primary themes of this evaluation, making it likely essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The programme's first specific objective was explicitly to 'strengthen border management and the involvement of cross-border communities,' and the evaluation dedicates substantial analysis to this component. The excerpts suggest the report covers: capacity-building for police, gendarmerie, and customs officers (relating to Actions 11a and 11b); creation and support of 18 village committees along borders (Action 11a, 11g); bilateral crisis simulation exercises at Gogui and Gouraye (Action 11a); construction and equipment of border posts (Action 11d); and cross-border community engagement in border governance. The evaluation assesses relevance, effectiveness, efficiency, and sustainability of these border management activities with dedicated sections and specific recommendations. For a synthesis specialist, the findings and conclusions sections on border management capacity-building, the recommendations on village committees and border post training, and the discussion of sustainability of border management gains would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION FINALE DE L’INITIATIVE CONJOINTE UE-OIM POUR LE RENFORCEMENT DE LA GESTION DES FRONTIÈRES, LA PROTECTION ET LA RÉINTÉGRATION DES MIGRANTS EN MAURITANIE
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, social cohesion and community inclusion appear to be a meaningful analytical theme in this evaluation. The programme included awareness-raising activities on social cohesion and migrant inclusion, described as having a 'durable impact in communities through group discussions and a significant artistic activity.' The use of art as a vector for social cohesion is highlighted as an innovative practice and good practice worth replicating. The evaluation also discusses the creation of village committees along borders as a mechanism for community engagement and conflict management — including intercommunal conflicts. These elements relate to GCM Objective 16's associated actions, particularly 16a (promoting mutual respect and social cohesion), 16f (community centres and intercultural dialogue), and 16h (multicultural activities through arts). The evaluation's conclusions and recommendations sections, as well as the effectiveness section on social cohesion messaging, would appear most relevant for a synthesis specialist. The score reflects that social cohesion is a genuine but secondary theme alongside border management and return/reintegration.

References.Title: EVALUATION FINALE DE L’INITIATIVE CONJOINTE UE-OIM POUR LE RENFORCEMENT DE LA GESTION DES FRONTIÈRES, LA PROTECTION ET LA RÉINTÉGRATION DES MIGRANTS EN MAURITANIE
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, diaspora engagement appears to be one of the primary and distinctive themes of this evaluation, likely contributing meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The programme's third specific objective was explicitly to 'support circular and temporary migration of the Mauritanian diaspora,' and the evaluation dedicates substantial analysis to this component. The excerpts suggest the report covers: creation of an online recruitment platform (diaspora.mr) for diaspora experts (relating to Action 19d on diaspora mechanisms and 19j on knowledge transfer); facilitation of temporary return of Mauritanian diaspora experts to provide technical assistance to government ministries (Actions 19i and 19j); adaptation of the diaspora programme during COVID-19 to recruit medical profiles; assessment of results, challenges, and sustainability of diaspora missions. The evaluation highlights this as an innovative practice and recommends its continuation and expansion. For a synthesis specialist, the effectiveness section on diaspora mission results, the conclusions on circular migration sustainability, and the recommendations on diaspora network mapping would appear particularly valuable. The score reflects that diaspora engagement is a genuine primary theme, though the report also covers border management and return/reintegration with comparable prominence.

References.Title: EVALUATION FINALE DE L’INITIATIVE CONJOINTE UE-OIM POUR LE RENFORCEMENT DE LA GESTION DES FRONTIÈRES, LA PROTECTION ET LA RÉINTÉGRATION DES MIGRANTS EN MAURITANIE
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, voluntary return and sustainable reintegration appear to be one of the central themes of this evaluation, likely making it essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The programme's second specific objective explicitly addressed 'strengthening migration management and governance and ensuring protection, return and sustainable reintegration of migrants,' and the evaluation dedicates substantial analysis to this component. The excerpts suggest the report covers: facilitation of voluntary returns including document acquisition in collaboration with consular missions (Actions 21c and 21d); reintegration assistance including social, medical, and vocational support (Action 21h); assessment of timeliness and efficiency of voluntary return processes; evaluation of reintegration training quality and recommendations for improvement; and sustainability of reintegration outcomes. The evaluation identifies weaknesses in reintegration training (too narrow, no follow-up) and makes specific recommendations on improving training diversity and post-return monitoring. For a synthesis specialist, the effectiveness section on voluntary returns and reintegration, the efficiency section on return timelines, the conclusions on reintegration quality, and the recommendations on training reform would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: BUILDING THE RESILIENCE OF COMMUNITIES AFFECTED BY CLIMATE CHANGE AND ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION IN GUINEA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to be highly relevant — likely fundamental — for a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its actions related to climate change adaptation, resilience, and the migration-environment nexus (Actions 2h, 2i, 2b). The project's explicit purpose is to build resilience of communities affected by climate change and environmental degradation in Guinea, directly addressing the structural drivers of migration. The excerpts suggest the evaluation examines: (1) how climate change and environmental degradation drive internal migration and displacement in Guinea; (2) institutional capacity-building on the MECC (Migration, Environment and Climate Change) nexus; (3) community-level livelihood interventions to reduce climate-induced migration pressures; and (4) the integration of MECC considerations into national policymaking. The project title, objectives, and evaluation questions all center on climate-environment-migration linkages. The executive summary, conclusions (C1, C2, C11), and recommendations (R1, R4) appear particularly relevant for a synthesis specialist. The discussion of sustainable livelihoods, women's agricultural empowerment, and community resilience as responses to climate-driven migration pressures aligns closely with Actions 2b and 2i. The evaluation of what worked and what did not in building community resilience would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on minimizing adverse drivers of migration.

References.Title: Evaluación externa final para el regional proyecto: “Fomento de inclusión social de las mujeres migrantes en las políticas públicas en CentroAmérica y República Dominicana: prevención de la violencia basada en género"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The entire project is explicitly designed to address the vulnerabilities of migrant women to gender-based violence throughout the migration cycle — in origin, transit, destination, and return contexts. The project title, objectives, all three results, and the evaluation questions center on identifying and responding to the specific vulnerabilities of migrant women (Actions 7b, 7c most directly). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: vulnerability identification and protection mechanisms; gender-responsive approaches to migrant women at risk; psychosocial support and economic empowerment as vulnerability reduction tools; the intersection of irregular migration and exposure to GBV; and the role of COVID-19 in exacerbating vulnerabilities. The conclusions section explicitly discusses how the project changed the paradigm around migrant women from passive recipients to active agents facing specific risks. Recommendations address intersectionality, inclusion of girls and LGBTIQ+ populations, and data on vulnerability factors (Actions 7a, 7c, 7l). For a synthesis specialist, the context/background section, the efficacy and impact criteria sections (4.1.1, 4.1.2), and the lessons learned chapter (section 5) would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The full report likely contains additional relevant evidence on vulnerability response mechanisms beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Evaluación externa final para el regional proyecto: “Fomento de inclusión social de las mujeres migrantes en las políticas públicas en CentroAmérica y República Dominicana: prevención de la violencia basada en género"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. The project's core objective — promoting the social inclusion of migrant women in public policies — directly aligns with this GCM objective. Result 3 explicitly addresses socioeconomic reintegration and empowerment of migrant women, and the evaluation analyzes the effectiveness of employability and entrepreneurship programs as inclusion mechanisms (Action 16e on empowering migrant women, Action 16d on labour market integration). The conclusions discuss how the project advanced the inclusion agenda at regional institutional level, and lessons learned provide detailed analysis of what worked and what did not in economic empowerment programming. The paradigm shift described — from viewing migrant women as passive to recognizing their agency — is directly relevant to inclusion and social cohesion narratives. For a synthesis specialist, the efficacy section (4.1.1), the impact section (4.1.2), and the lessons learned on the empowerment component (sections 5.1.2 and 5.2.2) would appear particularly valuable. Border management does not appear to be the central focus, and the inclusion theme competes with GBV prevention as the dominant analytical lens.

References.Title: Evaluación externa final para el regional proyecto: “Fomento de inclusión social de las mujeres migrantes en las políticas públicas en CentroAmérica y República Dominicana: prevención de la violencia basada en género"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly on the reintegration dimension. Result 3 of the project explicitly addresses 'acompañamiento integral durante la reintegración socioeconómica de las mujeres migrantes y sus familias,' and the evaluation analyzes the effectiveness of reintegration programming including employability, entrepreneurship, and psychosocial support components. The context section notes that returning and deported migrants face discrimination and difficulties achieving sustainable reintegration. Recommendations provide detailed guidance on improving reintegration program design (Actions 21b and 21h most directly). The lessons learned section contains specific evidence on what worked and what did not in reintegration support for migrant women. However, the return and readmission procedural dimensions (safe and dignified return, readmission agreements) are not substantively analyzed — the focus is on post-return reintegration support rather than the return process itself. For a synthesis specialist, the efficacy section on Result 3, the recommendations section 4.2.1 (point 2e), and the lessons learned on the empowerment component (sections 5.1.2 and 5.2.2) would appear particularly valuable as starting points.

References.Title: PROYECTO IDF "FORTALECIENDO LAS CAPACIDADES DE LOS PAÍSES DEL MERCADO COMÚN DEL SUR (MERCOSUR) SOBRE: TRATA DE PERSONAS Y MUJER MIGRANTE" CT.0975 INFORME FINAL DE EVALUACIÓN
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Trafficking in persons (TdP) is the explicit and dominant subject of the evaluated project, appearing in the project title, all five results, and throughout the evaluation questions, findings, conclusions, and recommendations. The project directly addresses multiple actions under Objective 10: capacity building for identification and referral of trafficking victims (Actions 10c, 10i), development of regional cooperation mechanisms (Action 10d), training of health professionals on victim assistance (Action 10h, 10i), and regional tools such as the MERCOSUR Guide for Early Detection of Trafficking Situations at border crossings (Action 10c). The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of these interventions across MERCOSUR member states, providing evidence on what works and what does not in regional anti-trafficking cooperation. Particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist would be: (1) the analysis of the FEM Guide's application and limitations (R1 sections), (2) the RMAAM articulation mechanism and its operational challenges (R2 sections), (3) the health sector guide for trafficking victim assistance (R4 sections), and (4) the cross-cutting findings on gender mainstreaming in anti-trafficking tools. The report also provides evidence on South-South cooperation modalities for anti-trafficking (R5). The evaluation's honest assessment of implementation gaps — including the mechanism existing on paper but not functioning in practice — would be particularly valuable for synthesis on what conditions enable or hinder effective anti-trafficking regional cooperation.

References.Title: PROYECTO IDF "FORTALECIENDO LAS CAPACIDADES DE LOS PAÍSES DEL MERCADO COMÚN DEL SUR (MERCOSUR) SOBRE: TRATA DE PERSONAS Y MUJER MIGRANTE" CT.0975 INFORME FINAL DE EVALUACIÓN
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute relevant evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 12, particularly regarding screening and referral mechanisms for trafficking victims. The MERCOSUR Guide for Early Detection of Trafficking Situations at Border Crossings (R1) is directly related to Action 12b (specialized training for first responders and border officials on identification and referral of trafficking victims) and Action 12c (gender-responsive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures). The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of training on this guide, the degree to which participants can apply gender considerations in screening, and the challenges of institutionalizing referral procedures. The RMAAM mechanism (R2) also involves referral protocols (Protocolo de funcionamiento de la red MERCOSUR) and associated forms for case management. The evaluation's findings on the gap between tool creation and actual operational use, and on the need for validation with frontline users, would be particularly relevant for a synthesis on what enables or hinders effective screening and referral systems. Synthesis specialists should particularly review the R1 and R2 sections, as well as the recommendations on tool design and validation with operational staff.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION FOR THE PROJECT: “PSYCHOSOCIAL AND MEDICAL ASSISTANCE TO URBAN REFUGEE SURVIVORS AND THOSE AT RISK OF SEXUAL AND GENDER BASED VIOLENCE (SGBV) IN KAMPALA, UGANDA”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project explicitly targets migrants in situations of vulnerability — specifically urban refugee survivors of SGBV and those at heightened risk, including women, children, persons with disabilities, older persons, and youth. The project title, objectives, and all major interventions are organized around identifying, assisting, and protecting vulnerable migrants. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple associated actions: Action 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerability, including victims of SGBV) appears directly addressed through individual counseling, medical assistance, and case management; Action 7c (gender-responsive migration policies addressing needs of migrant women and girls, including psychological counseling and access to justice for SGBV cases) is a core theme; Action 7e and 7f (child protection systems, Best Interest Assessments for unaccompanied/separated children) are explicitly covered through BIA activities; and Action 7k (involving local authorities and stakeholders in identification and referral) is addressed through the referral pathway analysis. The conclusions, recommendations, and output tables all directly engage with vulnerability response mechanisms. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on effectiveness of counseling and medical assistance, the BIA findings, the referral pathway analysis, and the recommendations on holistic programming and partnerships would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION FOR THE PROJECT: “PSYCHOSOCIAL AND MEDICAL ASSISTANCE TO URBAN REFUGEE SURVIVORS AND THOSE AT RISK OF SEXUAL AND GENDER BASED VIOLENCE (SGBV) IN KAMPALA, UGANDA”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The project is fundamentally about ensuring urban refugees — regardless of their status — can access psychosocial, medical, and protection services. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: Action 15c (holistic and accessible service points at local level, migrant-inclusive) through the project's community-based counseling and medical referral model; Action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in health care policies, facilitating affordable and non-discriminatory access, reducing communication barriers) through the medical assistance component at Nakasero Hospital and engagement with government health facilities; and the broader theme of migrant-inclusive service delivery. The conclusions note that government and partner services are insufficient to fill the gap, and recommendations address strengthening referral systems and government capacity — directly relevant to Action 15e. The report also touches on language barriers and culturally sensitive service delivery. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on medical assistance effectiveness, referral pathway analysis, and recommendations on government health system engagement would appear most relevant. The report's focus on a specific vulnerable sub-population (SGBV survivors) within the broader migrant population is a useful lens for understanding service access barriers.

References.Title: EVALUATION REPORT OF THE PROJECT STRENGTHENING SECURITY IN DJIBOUTI AND SAFETY OF MIGRANTS THROUGH COUNTER-TRAFFICKING, MARINE SAFETY AND EMERGENCY ASSISTANCE
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project explicitly targets vulnerable migrants — including victims of trafficking, migrants at sea, and those in emergency situations — and the evaluation assesses the effectiveness of protection and assistance services at the Obock MRC. The evaluation discusses psycho-social assistance quality (noting that 'bad psycho-social assistance can be more harmful than no assistance at all'), gender-specific needs of migrant women and children, and the reinforcement of hosting structures. These themes relate to Actions 7b (assistance to migrants in vulnerable situations), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies including psycho-social services), and 7j (support for migrants in crisis situations). The lessons learned and recommendations sections — particularly on MRC management stability, staff reinforcement, and humanitarian assistance along migration routes — appear most relevant for human reviewers to prioritize. The evaluation's critique of the absence of migrant satisfaction data also touches on monitoring vulnerability responses. This is one of the report's substantive themes, though it is analyzed somewhat indirectly through operational assessment rather than as a standalone vulnerability framework.

References.Title: EVALUATION REPORT OF THE PROJECT STRENGTHENING SECURITY IN DJIBOUTI AND SAFETY OF MIGRANTS THROUGH COUNTER-TRAFFICKING, MARINE SAFETY AND EMERGENCY ASSISTANCE
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 8. Marine safety and rescue at sea operations are explicitly one of the four project outcomes ('Rescue at sea operations are strengthened'), and the project title itself references 'marine safety.' The evaluation assesses the strengthening of rescue at sea operations, provision of equipment for rescue at sea, and the development of SOPs for the Coast Guard. These themes directly relate to Actions 8a (procedures and agreements on search and rescue) and 8b (identifying dangerous transit routes and preventing migrants from going missing). The recommendations section specifically addresses Coast Guard SOPs, technical training with the Italian Military Base, and stages in the Mediterranean — all highly relevant to Action 8a. The efficiency and effectiveness sections discuss equipment provision delays and capacity building for marine rescue. For a synthesis specialist, the findings on rescue at sea capacity building, the SOPs development process, and the recommendations on technical training would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains more detailed operational findings on marine rescue beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION REPORT OF THE PROJECT STRENGTHENING SECURITY IN DJIBOUTI AND SAFETY OF MIGRANTS THROUGH COUNTER-TRAFFICKING, MARINE SAFETY AND EMERGENCY ASSISTANCE
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 9. The project explicitly addresses smuggling of migrants as a core component — Outcome 1 concerns information on 'risks of trafficking and smuggling,' and Outcome 2 concerns investigation of 'trafficking and smuggling of migrants reported at the IOM's MRC.' The evaluation discusses capacity building for law enforcement ('forces de l'ordre') on migration issues including smuggling, and the awareness-raising campaigns targeting smuggling risks. These themes relate to Actions 9c (cooperation protocols along migration routes) and 9f (prevention measures in partnership with states and stakeholders). The recommendation to better distinguish between smuggling and trafficking responses (specific recommendation in conclusions) is directly relevant to Action 9e. The lessons learned on getting law enforcement entities together and the capacity building findings appear most relevant for human reviewers. However, the evaluation's treatment of smuggling appears somewhat intertwined with trafficking rather than as a fully distinct analytical focus, which limits its depth on smuggling-specific evidence.

References.Title: EVALUATION REPORT OF THE PROJECT STRENGTHENING SECURITY IN DJIBOUTI AND SAFETY OF MIGRANTS THROUGH COUNTER-TRAFFICKING, MARINE SAFETY AND EMERGENCY ASSISTANCE
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Counter-trafficking is explicitly the primary focus of the project — it appears in the project title ('counter-trafficking'), and the project outcomes include investigation of trafficking cases at the MRC and increased information availability on trafficking risks. The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of capacity building for law enforcement on trafficking identification and response, awareness-raising campaigns on trafficking risks, and the functioning of the MRC as a response mechanism. These themes directly relate to Actions 10c (monitoring irregular migration routes exploited by trafficking networks), 10e (measures addressing vulnerabilities of trafficking victims), 10i (training programmes for law enforcement to identify trafficking), and 10j (awareness-raising campaigns on trafficking risks). The evaluation's findings on capacity building effectiveness (including the critique of psycho-social assistance training quality), the lessons learned on law enforcement coordination, and the recommendations on distinguishing trafficking from smuggling responses appear particularly valuable for human reviewers. The discussion of the MRC's role in identifying and assisting victims of trafficking would also be relevant to Action 10e and 10h. It is possible the full report contains more detailed operational findings on anti-trafficking responses beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: External ex-post Evaluation: UNDERTAKING A MAPPING EXERCISE AND QUALITATIVE NEEDS ASSESSMENT TO SUPPORT THE NATIONAL DIASPORA POLICY OF AFGHANISTAN
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1, particularly regarding diaspora mapping as a data collection exercise. The project's primary output was a diaspora mapping report covering Afghan diaspora in four countries (Australia, Canada, Netherlands, USA), involving an online survey of 210 respondents and 23 interviews. The evaluation explicitly assesses the quality, accessibility, and use of this data product — including the fact that the mapping report was never publicly released, limiting its utility as evidence for policymaking. This connects directly to Actions 1d (data on contributions of migrants and diasporas to sustainable development), 1j (country-specific migration profiles), and 1k (research on interrelationship between migration and sustainable development). The evaluation's findings on the mapping methodology, the ten-month validation delay, the failure to publish the report, and the recommendation to make it publicly available all appear substantively relevant to questions about how diaspora data is collected, managed, and disseminated for evidence-based policymaking. Sections most worth reviewing for a synthesis specialist would include the discussion of Output 1.1 (mapping completion and accessibility), the efficiency findings on managing the mapping activity, and the recommendations on sharing the diaspora mapping report. The report's treatment of data quality and dissemination challenges appears to offer practical lessons for Action 1d and 1j specifically.

References.Title: External ex-post Evaluation: UNDERTAKING A MAPPING EXERCISE AND QUALITATIVE NEEDS ASSESSMENT TO SUPPORT THE NATIONAL DIASPORA POLICY OF AFGHANISTAN
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to be essentially core evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The evaluated project was explicitly designed to support Afghan diaspora engagement in Afghanistan's development — directly aligning with the objective's central theme. The project title, evaluation questions, and all major outputs relate to diaspora contributions to sustainable development. Several associated actions appear directly addressed: Action 19d (establishing government structures for diaspora engagement, including dedicated diaspora focal points and policy advisory mechanisms) is a primary focus, with findings on the challenges of appointing stable GoIRA focal points and the failure to formally endorse the National Diaspora Policy. Action 19j (mapping diasporas and their skills to maintain links with country of origin) is the project's central output. Action 19c (research on non-financial contributions of migrants and diasporas) is addressed through the qualitative needs assessment. Action 19h (promoting migration policies that optimize diaspora benefits) is addressed through the National Diaspora Policy development process. The evaluation's findings on what enabled and constrained diaspora policy development — including government capacity, political stability, coordination challenges, and the value of diaspora mapping as an evidence base — would likely provide substantive lessons for a synthesis on this objective. Sections most worth prioritizing for human reviewers include: the effectiveness findings on GoIRA capacity building and focal point challenges, the impact assessment on policy contributions, the sustainability analysis, the coherence section on complementary diaspora programmes (RQA, CD4D, Danish Refugee Council mapping), and the recommendations on future diaspora engagement. The report also appears to offer cautionary evidence on the risks of diaspora policy investments in fragile/conflict-affected contexts — a dimension relevant to Action 19d and 19j implementation.

References.Title: Evaluation of the Corporate Responsibility in Eliminating Slavery and Trafficking in Fashion (CREST) Programme and Regional Roadmap on Ethical Recruitment and Responsible Business to Eliminate Exploitation and Trafficking in Bali Process Member States
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 6. Fair and ethical recruitment is the explicit and central focus of the CREST Fashion Programme — it appears in the programme title ('Corporate Responsibility in Eliminating Slavery and Trafficking in Fashion'), the evaluation questions, and throughout findings, conclusions, and recommendations. The programme directly addresses multiple associated actions: Action 6c (regulation of recruitment agencies, prohibition of recruitment fees), 6d (partnerships with employers and worker organizations, written contracts), 6e (private sector cooperation, supply chain transparency), 6f (enforcement of fair recruitment norms), 6i (labour rights for migrant workers), 6k (needs of women migrant workers), and 6l (alignment with ILO General Principles on Fair Recruitment and UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: private sector engagement in ethical recruitment (Findings 4, 5); multi-stakeholder dialogue on recruitment standards; the WorkersJoint app eliminating middleman recruiters; supply chain mapping via the eMin app; and policy advocacy for legislative change. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on private sector engagement (Effectiveness), the Bali Process Project outcomes, Conclusions 1 and 5, and Recommendations 1 and 5 would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The full report likely contains additional depth on recruitment mechanisms beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Evaluation of the Corporate Responsibility in Eliminating Slavery and Trafficking in Fashion (CREST) Programme and Regional Roadmap on Ethical Recruitment and Responsible Business to Eliminate Exploitation and Trafficking in Bali Process Member States
References.Abstract: The excerpts reviewed suggest meaningful coverage of migrant worker vulnerability, though as part of a broader programme rather than as a standalone analytical focus. The programme explicitly targets protection of migrant workers against exploitation, forced labour, and trafficking — themes central to GCM Objective 7. Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations, including workers facing exploitation), 7c (gender-responsive policies for migrant women), and 7d (addressing workplace vulnerabilities) appear particularly relevant. The evaluation discusses intersecting vulnerabilities of migrant workers (women, ethnic minorities, LGBTIQ+, persons with disabilities) and critiques the programme for treating migrants as a homogeneous group. Conclusion 4 and the gender/human rights section address vulnerability dimensions. However, vulnerability is addressed primarily through the lens of labour exploitation and recruitment rather than as a comprehensive vulnerability management framework. A synthesis specialist would likely find the sections on gender and human rights mainstreaming, the discussion of intersecting vulnerabilities, and Recommendation 4 worth reviewing for evidence on vulnerability-responsive programming approaches.

References.Title: Evaluation of the Corporate Responsibility in Eliminating Slavery and Trafficking in Fashion (CREST) Programme and Regional Roadmap on Ethical Recruitment and Responsible Business to Eliminate Exploitation and Trafficking in Bali Process Member States
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report likely contains relevant evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Trafficking in persons is explicitly named in the programme title ('Eliminating Slavery and Trafficking in Fashion') and the programme's overall goal references protection against 'exploitation, slavery (forced labour), and human trafficking.' Actions 10e (addressing vulnerabilities of those at risk of trafficking, access to justice), 10i (training programmes to identify signs of trafficking including forced labour), and 10j (awareness-raising campaigns on trafficking risks) appear particularly relevant. The programme's work on pre-departure orientation, safe migration training, and supply chain transparency all connect to anti-trafficking objectives. However, the excerpts suggest the programme addresses trafficking primarily through a labour rights and supply chain lens rather than through criminal justice or victim protection frameworks that are more central to GCM Objective 10. A synthesis specialist would want to review the sections on migrant worker training, supply chain mapping, and the discussion of forced labour indicators, while noting that the anti-trafficking dimension may be more implicit than explicitly analyzed in the full report.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "ADDRESSING THE NEEDS OF STRANDED AND VULNERABLE MIGRANTS IN TARGETED SENDING, TRANSIT AND RECEIVING COUNTRIES"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project's explicit focus on 'stranded and vulnerable migrants' places vulnerability identification and response at the center of the evaluation. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: vulnerability criteria used to identify migrants (relating to Action 7b on identification and assistance); specific support measures for vulnerable groups including women, unaccompanied minors, and victims of trafficking (Actions 7b, 7c, 7e, 7f); psychosocial assistance gaps for returnees; and the role of Case Management Committees in referral and assistance. The executive summary notes that 'a significant portion was identified as vulnerable according to precise project criteria,' and the AVRR component analysis discusses how vulnerability shaped assistance design. The cross-cutting section on gender sensitivity also touches on Action 7c. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the AVRR component sections and the cross-cutting analysis on gender and do-no-harm, as these appear most directly relevant to vulnerability response mechanisms. It is possible the full report contains more detailed vulnerability assessment findings beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT "ADDRESSING THE NEEDS OF STRANDED AND VULNERABLE MIGRANTS IN TARGETED SENDING, TRANSIT AND RECEIVING COUNTRIES"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The AVRR (Assisted Voluntary Return and Reintegration) component is a primary subject of the evaluation, and the project title itself references 'stranded and vulnerable migrants' in sending, transit, and receiving countries — directly mapping onto the return and reintegration framework of GCM Objective 21. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: voluntary return operations across multiple challenging contexts including Yemen, Morocco, and Tanzania (Actions 21a, 21b, 21e); reintegration assistance design including financial support, in-kind aid, and income-generating activities (Action 21h); sustainability of reintegration and design flaws in current approaches (Action 21b, 21h); community-based reintegration approaches and their potential (Action 21i); monitoring and follow-up gaps for returnees (Action 21f); cooperation between IOM, embassies, and governments in facilitating returns (Action 21a, 21d); and the Ethiopian advanced reintegration model as a source of good practices. The executive summary, AVRR lessons learned section (5.3.2), and the conclusion all appear to contain directly relevant analysis. The report also examines regional coordination mechanisms supporting AVRR (Action 21a). For a synthesis specialist, the AVRR component analysis, lessons learned on reintegration design, and the comparative country experiences would appear particularly valuable as starting points. It is likely the full report contains considerably more detailed AVRR findings beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation: Strengthening the Knowledge Base to Better Protect and Assist Migrants in a Situation of Vulnerability in Mali
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to have meaningful relevance for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project title itself — 'Strengthening the Knowledge Base to Better Protect and Assist Migrants in a Situation of Vulnerability in Mali' — signals a data and knowledge focus. The excerpts indicate the project had two explicit components: 'Data and knowledge' and 'vulnerability identification support.' The project produced a study on drivers of migration, a vulnerability identification questionnaire (still in use in Mali and other West African countries), and worked on data collection and analysis related to migration patterns and trends. These activities appear to relate to GCM actions 1b (documenting migration patterns and trends), 1c (building national capacities in data collection and analysis), and 1j (developing country-specific migration profiles). The sustainability section notes the vulnerability identification questionnaire continues to be used, suggesting some depth of analysis on data tools. However, the excerpts suggest data collection was one of two components rather than the sole focus, and the analysis of data systems appears secondary to the protection/capacity-building narrative. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on the 'Data and knowledge' component, the study on drivers of migration, and the vulnerability identification questionnaire — framed as pointers for further review rather than definitive characterizations of the full report.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation: Strengthening the Knowledge Base to Better Protect and Assist Migrants in a Situation of Vulnerability in Mali
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project title explicitly references protecting and assisting 'migrants in a situation of vulnerability,' and the excerpts suggest this is the central analytical focus throughout. Key elements directly relevant to Objective 7 include: the development and continued use of a vulnerability identification questionnaire (relating to action 7b on identification and assistance); the creation of a National Referral Mechanism (NRM) for vulnerable migrants validated by the government in 2021 (relating to actions 7b and 7k on referral mechanisms and involving local authorities); the One-Stop centre for victims of gender-based violence (relating to action 7c on gender-responsive services); capacity-building of government and civil society actors to identify and assist vulnerable migrants (actions 7b, 7k); and protection of victims of trafficking (action 7b). The report appears to provide substantive analysis across multiple associated actions, with findings on effectiveness, sustainability, and impact of vulnerability identification and referral systems. For synthesis specialists, the sections on the NRM creation process, the vulnerability identification questionnaire, the One-Stop centre outcomes, and the capacity-building findings would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The long-term impact assessment (NRM validated 3+ years after project end) may offer especially useful evidence on sustainability of vulnerability response systems.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation: Strengthening the Knowledge Base to Better Protect and Assist Migrants in a Situation of Vulnerability in Mali
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to have substantial relevance for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The excerpts indicate that counter-trafficking was a significant component of the project's work, with explicit references to: capacity-building of government and civil society actors in relation to counter-trafficking; protection of victims of trafficking (VOTs) through the One-Stop centre; the NRM for vulnerable migrants which appears to encompass trafficking victims; and training activities noted as highly relevant by interviewees in relation to human trafficking. The project's alignment with IOM's regional plans 'to strengthen capacities of the government and civil society, in particular concerning human trafficking' is explicitly noted. These elements appear to relate to GCM actions 10e (addressing vulnerabilities of trafficking victims), 10g (strengthening legislation and procedures), 10h (providing protection and assistance to victims), and 10i (training programmes to identify trafficking). However, the excerpts suggest trafficking is one of several vulnerability themes rather than the exclusive focus, and the analytical depth on trafficking-specific mechanisms versus broader vulnerable migrant protection is not fully clear from the excerpts alone. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the capacity-building findings, the NRM development sections, and the One-Stop centre outcomes as starting points — framing these as pointers for further review.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation: Strengthening the Knowledge Base to Better Protect and Assist Migrants in a Situation of Vulnerability in Mali
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to have meaningful relevance for a synthesis on GCM Objective 12, particularly regarding referral mechanisms and screening/identification procedures. The development of a National Referral Mechanism (NRM) for vulnerable migrants is a central outcome of the project, directly relating to action 12c (establishing gender-responsive and child-sensitive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures). The vulnerability identification questionnaire developed by the project relates to screening and individual assessment functions (action 12c). The capacity-building trainings for government officials on identifying vulnerable migrants and trafficking victims also relate to action 12b (specialized trainings for first responders and government officials). The excerpts suggest these are substantive analytical themes with dedicated findings on effectiveness and sustainability. However, the procedural/legal certainty aspects of Objective 12 (actions 12a, 12e) appear less prominent. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the NRM development narrative, the vulnerability identification questionnaire sections, and the training effectiveness findings as starting points for deeper review.

References.Title: CAPACITY-BUILDING FOR DIASPORA ENGAGEMENT IN MOZAMBIQUE (CE.0298) EX-POST EVALUATION REPORT, 2018
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The entire project is explicitly designed to facilitate diaspora engagement in Mozambique's national development agenda — directly aligning with the core commitment of Objective 19. The excerpts suggest substantive coverage of several associated actions: Action 19d (establishing government structures for diaspora engagement, including INACE as a dedicated diaspora office and the TWG as an inter-ministerial coordination mechanism) appears to be a central focus; Action 19j (building partnerships between local authorities, diaspora organizations, and migrant organizations to promote knowledge and skills transfer, including diaspora mapping) is reflected in the diaspora network creation, the national forum, and the recommendation for diaspora mapping; Action 19f (providing accessible information through digital platforms for diaspora engagement) is addressed through the website, Facebook page, and WhatsApp group; Action 19g (enabling political participation of migrants in countries of origin) is touched upon through the national forum and government-diaspora dialogue mechanisms; Action 19h (promoting migration policies that optimize diaspora benefits) is reflected in the project's overall design and the study tour to Ethiopia. The evaluation assesses effectiveness, sustainability, and impact of these mechanisms with dedicated sections and recommendations. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on the TWG establishment and sustainability, the national diaspora forum, the communication tools assessment, and the recommendations on diaspora mapping and incentives would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The gender mainstreaming section also offers relevant observations on how diaspora engagement programmes can better account for gender dynamics.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF CANADA'S MEMBERSHIP IN THE INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATION FOR MIGRATION
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 23, specifically regarding multilateral migration governance and inter-state cooperation mechanisms. The evaluation explicitly examines Canada's membership in the IOM as its primary object of inquiry — assessing how a member state participates in, influences, and derives value from a global intergovernmental migration organization. This is substantively different from the ambient cooperation language found in most IOM programme evaluations. The excerpts suggest analysis of: Canada's role in IOM governing bodies (Council, SCPF), Canada's influence on IOM policy direction and strategic decisions (Finding 8), interdepartmental coordination challenges in implementing a whole-of-government approach to multilateral engagement (Findings 5-6), and the value-for-money of multilateral membership (Findings 9-10). These themes relate to Actions 23a (supporting collective implementation through whole-of-government approaches), 23d (capacity-building mechanisms and mobilizing resources), and 23e (bilateral and multilateral partnerships on migration policy). The recommendations on high-level bilateral meetings and monitoring of IOM budget reform also speak to how states can strengthen their engagement with multilateral migration governance architecture. For a synthesis specialist, the findings on Canada's influence within IOM governance structures, the analysis of interdepartmental coordination challenges, and the conclusions on value-for-money of multilateral membership would appear particularly relevant as starting points for deeper review. The report appears to treat international cooperation governance as its central subject rather than merely as operational context — which is the key distinguishing criterion for Objective 23 relevance. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EXTERNAL INDIPENDENT EVALUATION OF ADMIN4ALL:  SUPPORTING SOCIAL INCLUSION OF VULNERABLE MIGRANTS  IN EUROPE
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The project's core purpose — enhancing municipal capacity to provide inclusive and accessible social services for migrants — aligns closely with Actions 15c (establishing holistic, accessible service points at local level that are migrant-inclusive) and 15a (ensuring non-discriminatory service delivery). The evaluation appears to assess how municipalities across 7 EU countries improved their service delivery systems for migrants, including through one-stop shops (explicitly mentioned for Milan), coordination mechanisms, and capacity-building of frontline social workers. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: municipal service provision models, barriers to access, and coordination between service providers — all directly relevant to Objective 15. The conclusions section discusses sustainability of service improvements, and the good practices section highlights demand-driven, tailored approaches to service delivery. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the effectiveness section (section 4, referenced but not fully excerpted), the conclusions on impact and sustainability, and the good practices section. The anti-discrimination toolkit and mainstreaming migration across municipal departments are also relevant to Action 15a. It is possible the full report contains additional service-delivery evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EXTERNAL INDIPENDENT EVALUATION OF ADMIN4ALL:  SUPPORTING SOCIAL INCLUSION OF VULNERABLE MIGRANTS  IN EUROPE
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to be fundamentally relevant to GCM Objective 16. Social inclusion and integration of migrants in EU municipalities is the explicit primary subject of the ADMIN4ALL project and its evaluation. The project's three components — capacity-building for municipal staff, support to local coordination, and sharing of good practices — directly address Actions 16a (promoting mutual respect and best practices on integration), 16c (developing policy goals for migrant inclusion in societies), and 16f (establishing community-level programmes facilitating migrant participation). The evaluation assesses outcomes across 35 municipalities in 7 EU countries, examining how local authorities developed more inclusive policies and services. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: intercultural competence training, anti-discrimination measures, local coordination structures, peer exchange mechanisms, and community-level integration initiatives. The good practices section explicitly discusses intercultural dialogue, community intervention, and the 'Table of Coexistence' in Malaga. The conclusions address political engagement, leadership on integration, and transnational networks of municipalities — all highly relevant to Objective 16. Synthesis specialists working on evidence for Objective 16 would likely find the effectiveness section, impact assessment, good practices section (5.2), and recommendations particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The report's multi-country comparative dimension adds further value for synthesis purposes.

Finding 4 addresses the project's activities related to 'mitigating and addressing the protection concerns of migratory populations,' which includes outreach and information campaigns. Recommendation 2 specifically calls for monitoring outreach activities to assess their effects — suggesting substantive (if critical) engagement with information dissemination as a project component. The evaluation notes IOM has used 'a rich and varied set of methods' for outreach to migrants and potential migrants for several years, and critiques the lack of evidence on effectiveness. This self-critical analysis of information campaign effectiveness could be valuable for a synthesis on Action 3e (awareness-raising campaigns and pre-departure orientation). The evaluation dedicates Finding 5 to assessing progress on this outcome, and Recommendation 3 explicitly calls for reinvigorating the labour migration component and engaging destination countries systematically. The discussion of bilateral engagement with Gulf destination countries and the role of ILO collaboration would likely be of interest to synthesis specialists examining Actions 5a and 5b. Sections covering Finding 5, Recommendation 3, and the budget allocation for Outcome 3 would be most relevant for human reviewers, though the full report likely contains more detail on specific activities than the excerpts suggest.

References.Title: INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF IOM'S PROJECT "HORN/GULF OF ADEN/YEMEN: IMPROVING PROTECTION OF MIGRANTS - PHASE III"

The project explicitly targets highly vulnerable populations: irregular migrants, refugees, asylum seekers, trafficking victims, unaccompanied minors, and stateless persons in contexts of extreme hardship (physical/sexual abuse, harsh conditions, loss of life at sea). Finding 4 — the most positively assessed outcome — directly evaluates protection services for vulnerable migrants, including emergency assistance, Assisted Voluntary Return and Reintegration (AVRRR), and direct services. The evaluation covers vulnerability responses across multiple countries (Ethiopia, Djibouti, Somaliland, Puntland, Yemen), offering comparative evidence. Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerability), 7j (support in crisis situations), and 7k (local authority involvement in identification and referral) appear particularly well-covered. For human reviewers, Finding 4, the sections on direct emergency assistance and AVRRR services, and Recommendation 4 on stakeholder ownership would appear most relevant as starting points. The full report likely contains considerably more detail on specific protection activities and their outcomes than the excerpts reveal.

References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Assisted Voluntary Return and Reintegration (AVRRR) is explicitly identified as a core project activity in Djibouti, Somaliland, and Puntland, and is part of the project's protection mandate. The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of these return and reintegration services as part of Finding 4. The project operates in a complex mixed migration context where return involves highly vulnerable populations, offering evidence on the challenges of dignified return in difficult operational environments. Actions 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes) and 21h (sustainable reintegration support) appear most directly addressed. The focus group discussions with returning Ethiopian migrants in Addis Ababa and Djibouti also provide some beneficiary-level perspective on return experiences. For human reviewers, Finding 4 and the sections describing AVRRR activities across country offices would appear most relevant as starting points, though the full report likely contains more operational detail on return programming than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation: Strengthening Internal Migration Policies in Bolivia
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The evaluated project — 'Strengthening Internal Migration Policies in Bolivia' — appears to have been fundamentally about producing disaggregated data on internal migration trends to fill a recognized evidence gap and support evidence-based policymaking. The excerpts indicate that the project's core outputs were (1) a comparative study analyzing trends, characteristics and determinants of internal migration across six strategic sectors, and (2) strategic guidelines for public policy formulation. The evaluation explicitly discusses data collection methodology, the absence of updated migration statistics (last census data from 2001 and 2012), and the role of national statistical institutions (INE, UDAPE). This aligns closely with GCM Actions 1a (comprehensive strategy for improving migration data), 1g (integrating migration topics in national censuses), 1j (country-specific migration profiles), and 1d (data on effects of migration for sustainable development). The executive summary, results matrix, and conclusions sections appear most relevant for human reviewers. The evaluation's discussion of data gaps, the value of the comparative study as a reference, and the limitations in translating data into policy would likely contribute substantive evidence to a synthesis on this objective. Note that this concerns internal migration rather than international migration, which may limit direct applicability to some GCM actions, but the data governance and evidence-based policymaking dimensions remain highly pertinent.

References.Title: BASELINE REPORT FOR THE BOLSTERING ETHICAL RECRUITMENT POLICIES AND PRACTICE TO ENHANCE SAFE AND ORDERLY LABOUR MIGRATION PATHWAYS, PREVENT EXPLOITATION AND BETTER PROTECT MIGRANT WORKERS FROM UGANDA
