The report assesses Private Recruitment Agencies (PRAs) against the IOM International Recruitment Integrity System (IRIS) principles, which directly maps to Action 6l. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: recruitment fee prohibition (Action 6c), written contracts and transparency of terms (Action 6d), regulation and monitoring of recruitment agencies (Actions 6c, 6f), freedom of movement and document confiscation (Action 6h), and gender-specific vulnerabilities of women migrant workers including domestic workers (Action 6k). The IRIS principle performance scores (e.g., 34.7% on freedom of movement, 48% on transparency of terms) provide concrete benchmarking data. Recommendations address strengthening enforcement, bilateral agreements, and IRIS certification uptake. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on IRIS principle adherence rates, PRA regulatory compliance, pre-departure training quality, and the conclusions on enabling conditions for ethical recruitment would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: BASELINE REPORT FOR THE BOLSTERING ETHICAL RECRUITMENT POLICIES AND PRACTICE TO ENHANCE SAFE AND ORDERLY LABOUR MIGRATION PATHWAYS, PREVENT EXPLOITATION AND BETTER PROTECT MIGRANT WORKERS FROM UGANDA
References.Abstract: The excerpts suggest meaningful relevance to GCM Objective 7. The report documents vulnerabilities of Ugandan migrant workers — particularly young women — including exploitation, physical and sexual abuse, and fatalities in destination countries (Middle East). It discusses the lack of a national referral mechanism for trafficking victims (Action 7b), challenges in accessing remedy (Action 7g), and the particular vulnerabilities of domestic workers (Actions 7b, 7d). The report also references 650 trafficking victims documented in 2018. While vulnerability is not analyzed as a standalone theme with dedicated sections, it permeates the ethical recruitment framework throughout. The conclusions section on referral mechanisms and the discussion of PRAs' challenges in protecting workers in destination countries would likely be worth reviewing for a synthesis on migrant vulnerability. Specialists should note that vulnerability is addressed primarily through the lens of recruitment regulation rather than as an independent analytical focus.

References.Title: MID-TERM EVALUATION OF “AUSTRALIA PROPOSING ORGANISATION FOR COMMUNITY SUPPORT PROGRAMMES
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. The evaluation's primary focus is the integration and social inclusion of resettled humanitarian migrants in Australia — directly aligned with this objective's core theme. The programme objective explicitly targets 'sustainable integration of migrants resulting in positive contribution to the community,' and the primary evaluation question asks 'to what extent have resettled migrants achieved sustainable integration.' The excerpts suggest multiple findings and conclusions directly address integration outcomes, barriers, and enabling factors — all central to GCM Objective 16. Specific connections to associated actions include: pre-departure and post-arrival orientation programmes (Action 16b); labour market integration and employment challenges (Action 16d); English language training as an integration enabler (Actions 16b, 16g); community group connections and social cohesion (Action 16f); and gender-sensitive approaches to integration (Action 16e). The five key external factors identified as barriers to integration (formal service navigation, community connections, English language, health/wellbeing, employment/qualifications) provide substantive evidence on what drives or hinders inclusion. Lessons Learned 3 and 4 on tailoring support to individual circumstances and timing of integration activities appear particularly valuable for synthesis purposes. For human reviewers, the effectiveness conclusions (Section 7.2), all eight recommendations, and the lessons learned section would be priority areas for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional evidence on integration outcomes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: MID-TERM EVALUATION OF “AUSTRALIA PROPOSING ORGANISATION FOR COMMUNITY SUPPORT PROGRAMMES
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 18. The difficulty of securing recognition of overseas qualifications and its impact on careers is identified as one of the five key external factors affecting integration, and is described as having 'the largest impact on overall integration.' Lesson 3 is dedicated entirely to this theme, noting that qualification recognition challenges particularly affect secondary applicants and that career impacts are often not realized until well after arrival. Recommendation 5 specifically addresses the need to encourage applicants to plan for altered career prospects, and Recommendation 1 calls for the Orientation Guide to address skill recognition. These connect substantively to Actions 18a (standards for mutual recognition), 18k (screening mechanisms and information on qualification assessment prior to or shortly after arrival), and 18j (innovative ways to recognize formally and informally acquired skills). The report's value for a synthesis on Objective 18 lies primarily in its evidence on the lived experience of qualification non-recognition as a barrier to integration, and on what information and support interventions can help migrants navigate this challenge. For human reviewers, the effectiveness conclusions on employment/qualification challenges (Section 7.2), Lesson 3, and Recommendations 1 and 5 would be the most relevant sections to prioritize. The analysis appears substantive enough to contribute meaningfully, though the report does not examine recognition systems or policy frameworks directly.

References.Title: RAPPORT D'ÉVALUATION EXTERNE PROJET DE RÉINTÉGRATION ET D'APPUI PSYCHOSOCIAL DES MIGRANTS RETOURNÉS DE L'ALGÉRIE ET RENFORCEMENT DE LEURS COMMUNAUTÉS D'ACCUEILS A ZINDER

The project explicitly targets the structural drivers of irregular migration — poverty, lack of economic opportunities, and demographic pressures in the Zinder/Kantché region — through income-generating activities (AGR) and community stabilization. The evaluation assesses whether these interventions effectively reduce the vulnerability that compels people to migrate irregularly. The executive summary and introduction provide substantive contextual analysis of the economic, demographic, and geographic factors driving migration from Niger to Algeria, and the project's theory of change is explicitly built around addressing these structural factors. The evaluation's conclusions and lessons learned discuss the effectiveness of AGR-based interventions in stabilizing communities and reducing irregular migration incentives. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on project conception/pertinence, the lessons learned on AGR effectiveness, and the discussion of community stabilization outcomes would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The report likely contributes evidence on what works in addressing structural migration drivers in Sahelian contexts, though border management appears to be one of several themes covered.

References.Title: RAPPORT D'ÉVALUATION EXTERNE PROJET DE RÉINTÉGRATION ET D'APPUI PSYCHOSOCIAL DES MIGRANTS RETOURNÉS DE L'ALGÉRIE ET RENFORCEMENT DE LEURS COMMUNAUTÉS D'ACCUEILS A ZINDER
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7, particularly regarding actions 7b, 7c, and 7l. The project explicitly targets vulnerable returned migrants — including women and children who experienced stigmatization, trauma, and economic precarity following forced return from Algeria — and provides psychosocial support and economic assistance. The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of vulnerability reduction interventions, including psychosocial support activities (focus groups, group discussions, community sensitization, participatory theater) and economic empowerment through AGR. The evaluation discusses the identification of vulnerable populations using MARP methodology, the referral of specific cases, and the integration of psychosocial and economic support. The sections on the psychosocial support component (section 2.2.2) and the evaluation's assessment of pertinence and effectiveness appear particularly relevant for a synthesis specialist. References.Title: RAPPORT D'ÉVALUATION EXTERNE PROJET DE RÉINTÉGRATION ET D'APPUI PSYCHOSOCIAL DES MIGRANTS RETOURNÉS DE L'ALGÉRIE ET RENFORCEMENT DE LEURS COMMUNAUTÉS D'ACCUEILS A ZINDER

The project is explicitly and primarily focused on the reintegration of returned migrants from Algeria into their communities of origin in Niger, making this the core subject of the evaluation. The project title itself references 'réintégration et appui psychosocial des migrants retournés,' and the evaluation systematically assesses the effectiveness of reintegration interventions including AGR support, psychosocial assistance, and community strengthening. The evaluation addresses: (1) the design and implementation of voluntary return and reintegration support (action 21b); (2) access to psychosocial assistance, vocational training, employment opportunities and social cohesion for returning migrants (action 21h); and (3) identification of community needs and integration into local development strategies (action 21i). The evaluation provides substantive evidence on what works in sustainable reintegration, including the role of community groupements, psychosocial support, economic empowerment, and local authority engagement. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on project conception and pertinence, the AGR component assessment, the psychosocial support evaluation, the lessons learned, and the recommendations matrix would appear particularly valuable. The evaluation also provides evidence on the challenges of achieving sustainable reintegration within short project timeframes (9 months), with recommendations for minimum 18-month implementation periods — directly relevant to evidence on reintegration program design. It is possible that the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EXTERNAL EVALUATION - ENHANCING RESILIENCE AND PROTECTION OF MARGINALIZED COMMUNITIES AFFECTED BY PROTRACTED CONFLICTS AND EFFECTS OF ADVERSE CLIMATIC CONDITIONS IN KENYA

The project explicitly targets communities affected by 'protracted conflicts and adverse climatic conditions' — themes directly aligned with actions 2b (investing in resilience and disaster risk reduction), 2i (adaptation and resilience strategies to climate and environmental degradation), and 2f (strengthening humanitarian-development collaboration). The executive summary and introduction describe how drought, flooding, resource competition, and conflict drive vulnerability and displacement in Northern Kenya. The project's three-pronged approach — peacebuilding, livelihoods, and social protection — maps onto structural drivers addressed under Objective 2. The conclusions and recommendations on expanding livelihood programming and multi-year community stabilisation are particularly relevant to actions 2b and 2e. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on peacebuilding outcomes, livelihood programming effectiveness, and the contextual analysis of Northern Kenya's vulnerability drivers. Note that the report focuses on community-level programming rather than national policy, which somewhat limits its contribution to higher-level structural analysis under Objective 2.

References.Title: EXTERNAL EVALUATION - ENHANCING RESILIENCE AND PROTECTION OF MARGINALIZED COMMUNITIES AFFECTED BY PROTRACTED CONFLICTS AND EFFECTS OF ADVERSE CLIMATIC CONDITIONS IN KENYA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7, particularly around actions addressing SGBV, trafficking, and protection of vulnerable groups. The project's social protection component explicitly targets sexual and gender-based violence (SGBV) and trafficking — themes central to actions 7b and 7c. The executive summary notes that 'issues of sexual and gender-based violence and trafficking remain poorly understood, marginally addressed and rampant throughout target areas,' and recommendations call for expanding support to victims, establishing safe houses, and developing designated spaces in police facilities and hospitals. Reviewers should note that the social protection analysis appears to be one of three programmatic pillars, so depth may be moderate rather than comprehensive.

References.Title: INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF IOM PROJECT: ENSURING A COMPREHENSIVE APPROACH TO ADDRESS SMUGGLING AND TRAFFICKING IN EGYPT INCLUDING EGYPT'S SINAI PENINSULA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The ECAST project explicitly targeted vulnerable migrants — including trafficked persons, smuggled migrants, unaccompanied migrant children, and migrants in detention — and the evaluation assesses the effectiveness of protection and assistance services provided to them. This aligns closely with Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations), 7e (accounting for migrant children in child protection systems), and 7f (protecting unaccompanied and separated children). The report notes that direct assistance reached over 1,300 migrants (Finding 5), discusses the absence of a government shelter for trafficked persons as a critical gap (Recommendation 8), and addresses referral mechanisms through the NCCM hotline. The evaluation's treatment of unaccompanied migrant children as a priority concern — including study tours and working group development — is particularly relevant to Actions 7e and 7f. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review Finding 5 on direct assistance impact, Finding 9 on UMC groundwork, and Recommendation 8 on shelter re-establishment, as well as the conclusions section discussing gaps in victim identification and protection. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of vulnerability response mechanisms, though border management and identification procedures may be covered more fully in sections not included in the excerpts.

References.Title: INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF IOM PROJECT: ENSURING A COMPREHENSIVE APPROACH TO ADDRESS SMUGGLING AND TRAFFICKING IN EGYPT INCLUDING EGYPT'S SINAI PENINSULA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 9. Smuggling of migrants is one of the two explicit primary subjects of the ECAST project, appearing in the project title and throughout the evaluation. The report evaluates efforts to: develop Egypt's first anti-smuggling legislation (Action 9d — establishing smuggling as a criminal offence), build government capacity to investigate and prosecute smuggling (Action 9f — prevention and capacity building), distinguish between smuggling and trafficking (Action 9e), and protect smuggled migrants rather than criminalizing them (core GCM Objective 9 commitment). Finding 9 identifies the contribution to Egypt's draft Illegal Migration and Anti-Smuggling Law as a major achievement, and the conclusions discuss the normative significance of defining irregular migrants as victims rather than criminals. The evaluation also covers awareness-raising targeting source communities (Action 9f) and government capacity building. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review Finding 9 on legislative contributions, the conclusions section on the anti-smuggling law's normative framework, and Recommendations 7-8 on programmatic follow-up. The project's '4Ps' framework (Prevention, Prosecution, Protection, Partnerships) maps directly onto GCM Objective 9's associated actions. It is likely that the full report contains additional relevant evidence on smuggling response mechanisms beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: INDEPENDENT EVALUATION OF IOM PROJECT: ENSURING A COMPREHENSIVE APPROACH TO ADDRESS SMUGGLING AND TRAFFICKING IN EGYPT INCLUDING EGYPT'S SINAI PENINSULA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Trafficking in persons is the other explicit primary subject of the ECAST project, appearing in the project title and throughout the evaluation. The report evaluates a comprehensive anti-trafficking programme covering: prosecution capacity building and legislative support (Actions 10g — strengthening prosecution, avoiding criminalization of victims); victim identification and protection services including shelter (Action 10h — protection and assistance to victims); awareness-raising campaigns (Action 10j — awareness campaigns for migrants and prospective migrants); referral mechanisms through the NCCM hotline (Action 10e — facilitating access to justice); and government capacity building (Action 10i — training programmes for officials). The evaluation assesses Egypt's Tier 2 Watch List status and the government's limited compliance with minimum anti-trafficking standards, providing important context on national response gaps. Finding 5 on direct assistance impact, Finding 9 on major achievements, and Recommendation 8 on shelter re-establishment are particularly relevant. The conclusions section discusses the absence of an operational government shelter as a critical gap, the failure to convict traffickers, and the importance of victim-centered approaches. Synthesis specialists would want to prioritize the findings sections on prosecution, protection, and capacity building, as well as the conclusions and recommendations. The full report likely contains substantially more evidence on anti-trafficking programming effectiveness than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Final External Project Evaluation "Enhancing Successful Reintegration of Veterans from the Conflict in Eastern Ukraine and their Family Members through Socio-Economic Support" (FC.0198)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to have meaningful relevance for a synthesis on GCM Objective 16, particularly around social cohesion and community-based inclusion approaches. The project explicitly targets social and economic reintegration of veterans into communities, with community support groups (CSGs) serving as vehicles for social cohesion building. The report discusses community-based activities, intercultural and inter-group engagement, inclusion of vulnerable groups (women, people with disabilities, IDPs), and the building of community connections — all themes closely aligned with GCM Objective 16's actions on social cohesion (16a, 16c, 16f). The good practices section highlights community-based approaches and networking as effective inclusion mechanisms. The conclusions discuss how community bonds built during the project persisted into wartime, suggesting durable social cohesion outcomes. While the beneficiaries are veterans rather than international migrants per se, the programmatic model — community-based inclusion, grassroots CSG support, multi-stakeholder engagement for social integration — appears transferable and potentially valuable for synthesis on GCM Objective 16. Synthesis specialists may want to review the 'Good Practices' section (5.1) and the conclusions on CSG capacity and community cohesion outcomes as starting points, while noting the domestic reintegration context differs from typical international migration settings.

References.Title: Final External Project Evaluation "Enhancing Successful Reintegration of Veterans from the Conflict in Eastern Ukraine and their Family Members through Socio-Economic Support" (FC.0198)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to be highly relevant — potentially essential — for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly its associated actions on sustainable reintegration (21b, 21h, 21i). The project's explicit purpose is enhancing successful reintegration of veterans into civilian life, which maps closely onto GCM Objective 21's commitment to 'create conducive conditions for personal safety, economic empowerment, inclusion and social cohesion in communities, in order to ensure that reintegration of migrants upon return to their countries of origin is sustainable.' The report evaluates a multi-component reintegration programme covering psychosocial support (MHPSS), livelihood/economic reintegration, community cohesion, and institutional capacity building — all dimensions addressed under Action 21h (equal access to social protection, psycho-social assistance, vocational training, employment opportunities) and 21i (addressing needs of communities to which migrants return). The evaluation assesses effectiveness, sustainability, and good practices of reintegration programming, providing evidence on what works and what doesn't. While the beneficiaries are conflict veterans rather than international migrants returning from abroad, the programmatic model and evaluation evidence appear directly applicable to reintegration programming more broadly. Synthesis specialists would likely want to prioritize the conclusions section (Section 5), the good practices section (5.1), the lessons learned section (5.2), and the recommendations — all of which contain substantive analysis of reintegration outcomes and programme design. The full report likely contains additional relevant evidence in the findings sections not captured in these excerpts.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “CONSOLIDATE DIRECT ASSISTANCE AND ASSISTED VOLUNTARY RETURN AND REINTEGRATION SERVICES IN EGYPT (CARE)”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The CARE project appears centrally focused on identifying and assisting vulnerable migrants in Egypt, with the Migrant Protection and Assistance unit providing tailored, case-by-case support. The excerpts suggest substantive coverage of vulnerability identification and response, including: eligibility and vulnerability assessments outlined in SOPs that prioritize victims of trafficking, LGBTI individuals, SGBV survivors, single parents, unaccompanied migrant children, and women at risk (aligning with Actions 7b, 7c, 7e, 7f); provision of medical, legal, housing, socioeconomic and educational assistance to over 2,700 vulnerable migrants (Action 7b); referral networks and partnerships with ERC, UNHCR, NGOs and government actors for identification and assistance (Action 7k); and mobile outreach teams extending reach to vulnerable migrants outside Cairo (Action 7k). The gender-responsive approach is explicitly discussed in conclusions and lessons learned. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on direct assistance SOPs, vulnerability assessment criteria, referral network development, and the effectiveness and sustainability sections would appear particularly valuable as starting points. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence on specific vulnerability categories beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “CONSOLIDATE DIRECT ASSISTANCE AND ASSISTED VOLUNTARY RETURN AND REINTEGRATION SERVICES IN EGYPT (CARE)”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute some evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The CARE project appears to have provided substantial direct assistance to over 2,700 vulnerable migrants, including medical, legal, housing, educational and socioeconomic assistance — directly relating to Action 15e (health needs) and Action 15f (education access). The partnership with the Egyptian Red Crescent, including donation of medical equipment for ERC clinics serving migrant-dense communities, appears to relate to Action 15c and 15e (strengthening accessible service points and health service capacity). The excerpts suggest analysis of service access challenges for irregular migrants and the role of referral networks in facilitating access. However, access to basic services appears as one component of a broader protection programme rather than the central analytical focus. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on direct assistance provision, the ERC partnership, and sustainability considerations around service continuity would appear most relevant as starting points for deeper review.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “CONSOLIDATE DIRECT ASSISTANCE AND ASSISTED VOLUNTARY RETURN AND REINTEGRATION SERVICES IN EGYPT (CARE)”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Assisted Voluntary Return and Reintegration (AVRR) is one of the two primary components of the CARE project, and the excerpts suggest extensive analysis of return and reintegration programming across multiple dimensions. The report appears to directly address: facilitation of AVRR for 463 vulnerable or stranded migrants including those in government custody (Actions 21b, 21e); gender-responsive return programming with high numbers of women assisted (Action 21b); community reintegration approaches being piloted in Comoros including a labour market needs assessment (Actions 21h, 21i); entrepreneurial training for migrants ahead of return to support sustainable reintegration (Action 21h); partnership development with IOM Comoros including funding of a physical office and staff (Action 21a); sustainability considerations for reintegration programming including exit strategies and sustainability indicators (Action 21h); and challenges in facilitating returns to Sudan and Ethiopia due to political instability (Action 21a). The evaluation covers all six OECD-DAC criteria including effectiveness, impact and sustainability of AVRR and reintegration, providing multi-dimensional evidence. For a synthesis specialist, the effectiveness section on AVRR outcomes, the impact section on reintegration results, the sustainability section on reintegration programming, and the recommendations on entrepreneurial training monitoring and sustainability planning would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The full report likely contains substantially more evidence on AVRR design, implementation and outcomes than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF IOM'S DISASTER PREPAREDNESS FOR EFFECTIVE RESPONSE PROJECT (PREPARE)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its actions related to disaster risk reduction, climate change adaptation, and resilience building (Actions 2a, 2b, 2i, 2j, 2k). The PREPARE project is explicitly designed to strengthen disaster preparedness, response, and recovery in two Pacific island nations highly vulnerable to climate change and natural disasters — themes that map directly onto Actions 2i (adaptation and resilience strategies to natural disasters), 2j (integrating displacement considerations into disaster preparedness), and 2k (addressing vulnerabilities of persons affected by disasters). The executive summary and conclusions discuss at length the development of contingency plans, SOPs, emergency response frameworks, and community-level resilience — all core to GCM Objective 2's disaster-related actions. The post-typhoon Maysak reconstruction response (USD 41.5 million, 422 homes, 153 public infrastructure projects) provides concrete evidence on post-disaster recovery. The sustainability section discusses the ongoing vulnerability of these nations and the structural factors (climate change, poverty, remoteness) that drive displacement risk. Sections on effectiveness, impact, and sustainability appear particularly valuable for human reviewers. The excerpts also touch on gender dimensions of disaster response, relevant to Action 2b. It is possible the full report contains additional evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT:  NIGERIA: STRENGTHENING REINTEGRATION FOR RETURNEES (SRARP)  - PHASE II
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The entire evaluation concerns a project explicitly designed to support sustainable reintegration of returning Nigerian migrants — the core subject of GCM Objective 21. The project title, evaluation questions, and all major findings directly address return and reintegration themes. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly relevant to GCM Objective 21 actions: action 21b (gender-responsive return and reintegration programmes with social and financial support) is addressed through the project's needs-based assistance model, gender mainstreaming analysis, and psychosocial support; action 21h (sustainable reintegration through social protection, psychosocial assistance, vocational training, employment, and financial services) is the central analytical focus, with detailed RSI measurements across economic, social, and psychosocial dimensions; action 21i (addressing needs of communities to which migrants return) is addressed in the sustainability section on community-based reintegration. The report appears to provide rare quantitative impact assessment evidence on reintegration sustainability outcomes using a Reintegration Sustainability Index, with pre-post and difference-in-differences analysis. For a synthesis specialist, the Impact section (pre-post sustainability analysis and impact attribution), the Sustainability section (institutional and social sustainability), and the Effectiveness section on reintegration assistance outcomes would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The gender section's analysis of gender-differentiated reintegration outcomes and the recommendations on continuum of care, community involvement, and private sector engagement would also likely add value. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: MENTORSHIP PROGRAMME II: ASSESSMENT REPORT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 18, particularly regarding on-the-job training and skills development approaches. The entire report evaluates a structured mentorship programme designed to build the professional skills and technical competencies of immigration officials across three departments. This aligns substantively with Action 18j (mentoring and on-the-job training programmes to recognize and develop skills) and Action 18h (on-the-job training and access to advanced training programmes). The report documents specific skills development outcomes: statistical data analysis using SPSS (Planning and Research), budget forecasting and auditing (Administration and Finance), and customer service and Business English (Visa and Aliens). Pre- and post-assessment data quantify skill growth, and the satisfaction survey provides mentee perspectives on learning outcomes. While the skills development here concerns government officials rather than migrant workers per se, the report appears to offer substantive evidence on the design, delivery, and effectiveness of mentorship-based skills development programmes — methodological insights potentially transferable to migrant skills development contexts. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the Evaluation of Findings sections for each department (pages 5-12) and the satisfaction survey results (pages 13-16) as starting points, noting that the subject population is immigration officials rather than migrants themselves.

References.Title: RAPPORT D'EVALUATION RENFORCEMENT DES CAPACITES POUR UNE GESTION STRATEGIQUE DES RESSOURCES DE LA DIASPORA DE LA RD CONGO
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to contribute meaningful evidence relevant to GCM Objective 1, particularly around the development and operationalization of diaspora mapping/profiling systems. The project evaluated explicitly includes the creation of a standardized cartography (mapping) system to profile Congolais de l'Étranger (CDE), the development of a database (base de données), and a website for diaspora information — all of which relate to Actions 1c (building national capacities in data collection and analysis), 1d (collecting data on diaspora contributions to sustainable development), and 1j (developing country-specific migration profiles). The evaluation appears to assess the effectiveness of these data infrastructure efforts in some depth, noting that the database was created but not operationalized, that the website lacked content, and that only 3 of the intended officials were trained on maintenance. The sections on Outcome 2 (standardized cartography system) and the associated outputs (2.1 and 2.2) would likely be most valuable for a synthesis specialist. The evaluation also touches on the mapping of Congolese diaspora in Canada, UK, and Belgium. However, the report's primary focus is on capacity-building broadly, and data systems are one component among several. The depth of analysis on data quality, comparability, or statistical standards is limited based on the excerpts reviewed.

References.Title: RAPPORT D'EVALUATION RENFORCEMENT DES CAPACITES POUR UNE GESTION STRATEGIQUE DES RESSOURCES DE LA DIASPORA DE LA RD CONGO
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to be essentially core evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The entire project evaluated — 'Renforcement des capacités pour une gestion stratégique des ressources de la diaspora de la RD Congo' — is explicitly focused on enabling the Congolese government to better mobilize and manage diaspora resources for socioeconomic development. This directly addresses multiple associated actions: Action 19d (establishing dedicated diaspora offices/focal points and government structures), Action 19e (developing targeted support programmes and investment funds for diaspora), Action 19j (mapping diasporas and their skills to maintain links with country of origin), and Action 19c (investing in research on non-financial contributions of migrants and diasporas). The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of: an interministerial coordination mechanism for diaspora engagement; a diaspora mapping/cartography system covering Congolese in Canada, UK, and Belgium; a feasibility study for a diaspora investment fund; capacity-building of government officials on diaspora mobilization strategies; and outreach missions to diaspora communities. The conclusions and recommendations sections appear particularly rich for synthesis purposes, addressing sustainability challenges, appropriation failures, and lessons learned about institutional design for diaspora engagement. Sections on Outcomes 1–4 and the recommendations matrix (Section 7) would be priority reading for a synthesis specialist. The evaluation also provides evidence on what does not work — political instability, ministerial reshuffles, lack of communication channels — which is valuable for understanding implementation barriers in diaspora governance.

References.Title: RAPPORT D'EVALUATION RENFORCEMENT DES CAPACITES POUR UNE GESTION STRATEGIQUE DES RESSOURCES DE LA DIASPORA DE LA RD CONGO
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report likely contributes relevant evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 20, specifically around the feasibility of diaspora investment funds and remittance channeling mechanisms. The project includes a dedicated study on 'mieux canaliser le rapatriement des fonds de la diaspora à travers un fond d'investissement' (better channeling diaspora remittances through an investment fund), which relates to Action 20g (developing programmes to promote investments from remittance senders in local development) and Action 20e (innovative financial solutions). The evaluation notes that this feasibility study was completed and a restitution workshop held, but that its recommendations remain largely unknown to key stakeholders. The sections on Output 4.1 and the recommendations in Section 7 (R6) appear most relevant for a synthesis specialist. However, the analysis of remittance transfer costs, financial inclusion mechanisms, or regulatory frameworks — which are central to GCM Objective 20 — does not appear to be a primary focus based on the excerpts reviewed. The relevance is real but secondary to the diaspora governance theme.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation: Migration Profile - Rwanda
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project evaluated — 'Migration Profile-Rwanda' — is explicitly and centrally about developing a country-specific migration profile (directly corresponding to Action 1j) and building national capacities for migration data collection, analysis and dissemination (Action 1c). The report title, evaluation questions, and virtually all findings and conclusions directly address migration data consolidation, the development of standard operating procedures (SOPs) for data collection, and the challenge of scattered, unconsolidated migration data across government departments. The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of a 'whole of government' approach to migration data (Action 1i), the development of disaggregated data (with a noted gap in gender-disaggregated data), and the sustainability of data collection systems. Actions 1a, 1c, 1i, and 1j appear most directly addressed. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on effectiveness (partial achievement of the MP), sustainability (conditions for a living document), and the gender section (lack of gender-disaggregated data) would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The report also offers lessons on the institutional and political challenges of formalizing migration data systems, which would likely enrich any evidence synthesis on this objective. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF IOM RAVL - FINAL REPORT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The RAVL program is explicitly and entirely focused on assisted voluntary return and reintegration — the core subject of GCM Objective 21. The report title, evaluation questions, and all major sections directly address return and reintegration processes, making this a fundamental source. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly relevant to associated actions: action 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes with free, prior and informed consent) is addressed through the evaluation of RAVL's voluntary return process, pre-departure counseling, and reintegration grant mechanisms; action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through social protection, psychosocial assistance, vocational training, employment and financial services) is extensively covered through the analysis of the €800 reintegration grants, micro-enterprise support, IOM counseling, and barriers to reintegration; action 21a (bilateral and multilateral cooperation frameworks for safe and dignified return) is addressed through the analysis of IOM coordination between sending and receiving missions across 18 countries; action 21d (consular assistance to returning migrants) is covered through the discussion of consulate cooperation; and action 21f (national monitoring mechanisms on return) is addressed through the evaluation of RAVL's monitoring systems and their limitations. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on reintegration outcomes (returnees' economic situation, micro-enterprise development, barriers to reintegration), the analysis of inter-mission coordination, the discussion of monitoring challenges, and the recommendations on transit centers, NGO partnerships, and grant disbursement simplification would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional detailed findings on reintegration effectiveness beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: SYNTHESIS OF ROHINGYA RESPONSE EVALUATIONS OF IOM, UNICEF AND UNHCR
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The synthesis report devotes a dedicated section (4.6) to data collection and information sharing in the Rohingya response, and data-related issues appear prominently across all three underlying evaluations. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of multiple parallel data collection systems, data quality concerns, data-sharing failures between agencies, and the disconnect between data collected and operational decision-making. The use of qualitative coding software (MaxQDA) and the mapping of data-related issues across evaluations further signals methodological attention to evidence quality. Actions 1a (harmonizing data collection methodologies) and 1c (building national capacities in data collection) appear most related, as the report discusses how IOM and UNHCR developed parallel, non-interoperable systems with different indicators. The section on 'Needs and Population Monitoring' and 'Multiple registration datasets' in Table 1 also suggests coverage of data standardization issues relevant to Actions 1b and 1e. For a synthesis specialist, Section 4.6 and the recommendations section on 'Data collection and information-sharing' (Section 4.8, point 2) would be the most valuable starting points. The report's value is somewhat limited by its focus on a specific refugee crisis context rather than broader migration data governance, but the operational lessons on inter-agency data harmonization appear substantive.

References.Title: SYNTHESIS OF ROHINGYA RESPONSE EVALUATIONS OF IOM, UNICEF AND UNHCR
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. Protection of vulnerable populations — including women, children, and refugees in extreme destitution — is a central theme running throughout the synthesis. Section 4.3 provides substantive analysis of protection gaps, including failures to address gender-based violence, trafficking risks, and the absence of minimum protection standards (lights, locks, gender-segregated latrines) for extended periods. Section 4.4 addresses engagement with affected populations, including the most vulnerable. The report discusses specific protection concerns for women and girls (Action 7c), unaccompanied children and child protection (Actions 7e, 7f), access to legal assistance (Action 7g), and the situation of migrants caught in crisis (Action 7j). The PSEA discussion in Section 4.3 and the trafficking risk analysis are directly relevant to Action 7b. The synthesis also discusses how the absence of a protection framework as the central lens of the response led to systematic gaps in addressing vulnerabilities. For a synthesis specialist, Sections 4.3 (Protection), 4.4 (Engaging with affected populations), and the recommendations in Section 4.8 on protection would be the most valuable starting points. The report's value is somewhat moderated by its focus on a specific refugee crisis rather than broader migration vulnerability frameworks, but the depth of analysis on protection gaps is substantial.

References.Title: SYNTHESIS OF ROHINGYA RESPONSE EVALUATIONS OF IOM, UNICEF AND UNHCR
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. Service delivery to Rohingya refugees is a central theme of the synthesis, with Section 4.6 providing substantive analysis of camp management, service delivery quality, coverage versus quality trade-offs, and inter-agency fragmentation in service provision. The report discusses access to health, WASH, education, and protection services, and critically examines how government restrictions limited service quality. The analysis of how coverage was prioritized over quality for too long, and how inter-agency competition created inefficiencies in service delivery, is directly relevant to Actions 15c (accessible service points) and 15e (health needs of migrants). The UNICEF evaluation's focus on education and WASH, and the discussion of integrated programming, also touches on Action 15f (inclusive education). The report's discussion of how restrictions on materials and land affected service quality provides evidence on structural barriers to service access. For a synthesis specialist, Section 4.6 and the recommendations on integrated programming in Section 4.8 would be the most valuable starting points. The report's value is somewhat moderated by its refugee-specific context, but the depth of analysis on service delivery systems and gaps is substantial.

References.Title: FINAL EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “TECHNICAL COOPERATION ON MIGRATION MANAGEMENT AND CAPACITY BUILDING”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project appears to have a substantive focus on identifying and responding to vulnerable migrants, with the protection-sensitive approach explicitly embedded in project design. The excerpts indicate attention to unaccompanied children, women, persons with disabilities, trauma survivors, and trafficking victims — directly relating to actions 7b and 7c. The gender-balanced mobile team composition is described as enabling adequate response to female migrants and families, and the registration process is noted to have revealed more information on migrant profiles and needs. The AVRR component also addresses migrants in vulnerable situations (action 7j). Recommendations suggest further engagement in human rights monitoring and emergency services for stranded migrants. The conclusions and recommendations sections appear particularly relevant for human reviewers to examine, as they contain the most substantive treatment of vulnerability identification and response. However, vulnerability is addressed as one component of a broader migration management project rather than as the sole analytical focus, which limits the score somewhat.

References.Title: FINAL EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “TECHNICAL COOPERATION ON MIGRATION MANAGEMENT AND CAPACITY BUILDING”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The project explicitly addresses humanitarian border management (HBM), with capacity building of national authorities in HBM mentioned as a key complementary activity and recommendation. The establishment of a first instance registration site at the border area (Bozaj), the deployment of a mobile team for registration and referral, and the focus on protection-sensitive and gender-responsive border procedures all relate to actions 11a, 11b, and 11c. The report appears to assess the effectiveness of border management capacity building, including training outcomes and operational setup. The conclusions on effectiveness (Results 1 and 2) and the recommendations for further HBM capacity building appear particularly relevant for human reviewers. The report's treatment of border management is substantive enough to contribute to a synthesis, though it is embedded within a broader migration management framework rather than being exclusively focused on border management.

References.Title: FINAL EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “TECHNICAL COOPERATION ON MIGRATION MANAGEMENT AND CAPACITY BUILDING”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be a valuable source for a synthesis on GCM Objective 12. The project's core activities appear to center on establishing and operationalizing a first instance registration site, developing standard operating procedures (SOPs) for registration, and implementing gender-responsive and protection-sensitive screening and referral processes — directly relating to actions 12b, 12c, and 12e. The mobile team's role in identification and referral of vulnerable migrants, the DTM-based reporting mechanism, and the focus on mixed movements are all substantively addressed. The partial achievement of Result 2 (registration site operational but SOPs not yet formulated) provides evaluative evidence on the challenges of institutionalizing screening procedures. The effectiveness section and conclusions on Results 1 and 2 appear most relevant for human reviewers to examine. The report likely offers practical insights on what works and what remains challenging in establishing referral mechanisms in a transit country context.

References.Title: FINAL EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “TECHNICAL COOPERATION ON MIGRATION MANAGEMENT AND CAPACITY BUILDING”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute substantial evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The Assisted Voluntary Return and Reintegration (AVRR) component is one of the four main results of the project, and Result 4 (AVRR support) is described as fully achieved — making it the most successfully completed element of the project. The excerpts indicate that AVRR activities were conducted for pending and rejected asylum seekers and irregular migrants wishing to return voluntarily, with specific mention of returns to Morocco and consideration of family situations and children's best interests. This directly relates to actions 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes based on free, prior and informed consent) and 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration). The contextual discussion of why migrants choose voluntary return (stranded position, exhaustion, family considerations) also provides relevant evidence. Recommendations for continued AVRR capacity building appear in the report. The effectiveness section on Result 4 and the AVRR-related conclusions appear most relevant for human reviewers to examine as starting points for deeper review.

References.Title: Ex-Post Evaluation of the Migrant Registration Support for Tanzania (MiREG) project
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 4. The MiREG project's core activity was biometric registration of irregular migrants — directly relevant to actions 4a (improving civil registry systems, reaching unregistered persons) and 4g (issuance of registration cards to facilitate participation in community life). The project registered 5,689 irregular migrants in Tanga using biometric technology, and Output 2.1 explicitly addresses making 'migrant registration accessible to undocumented persons.' The evaluation discusses challenges in the status determination process following registration, touching on action 4f (ensuring migrants without proof of identity are not precluded from services). The conclusions and recommendations sections appear particularly relevant — especially the discussion of whether registered migrants had their status determined and the advocacy recommendations. Synthesis specialists would want to review the effectiveness and impact sections, as well as recommendations 1 and 7, which address the link between registration and status outcomes. The report's treatment of biometric registration as a tool for regularization is likely valuable for evidence on documentation-related GCM actions, though border management and capacity building are co-dominant themes.

References.Title: EVALUATION INTERNE DU PROJET
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 6, particularly regarding decent work for domestic workers and protection from exploitation. The project explicitly promotes ILO Convention 189 on decent work for domestic workers, which aligns directly with Actions 6a (ratification of relevant international instruments), 6b (promoting full respect for human and labour rights of migrant domestic workers), and 6k (specific measures for women migrant workers, especially in domestic work). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: the feasibility study for C189 ratification, sensitization of 45 parliamentarians on C189 standards, and capacity-building of actors on domestic workers' rights. The report appears to document concrete progress toward ratification (instruments prepared, budgeted for 2014) and the creation of a national advocacy working group. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on Effet direct 1 (legal framework strengthening), the results matrix analysis on Produit 1.1 and 1.2, and the recommendations on ratification and decent work would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The report's focus on domestic workers as a specific vulnerable category of workers facing exploitation and abuse is directly relevant to Action 6k. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION INTERNE DU PROJET
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute some evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7, particularly regarding vulnerability identification and referral for trafficking victims and domestic workers. The project explicitly addresses vulnerability of women and children (domestic workers and trafficking victims), aligning with Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations, including domestic workers and trafficking victims), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies addressing needs of migrant women and children), and 7k (involving local authorities in identification, referral and assistance). The development and validation of Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for victim referral, the creation of the CONATT coalition, and training of actors on the referral system appear to be substantive outputs with analytical discussion. The excerpts also note attention to gender and disability approaches in capacity-building. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on Effet direct 2 (referral system for trafficking victims), Produits 2.1 and 2.2, and the discussion of the SOPs adoption process would appear most relevant. The report's treatment of vulnerability is primarily within a domestic/trafficking context rather than broader migration vulnerability, which somewhat limits its scope relative to the full breadth of Objective 7.

References.Title: EVALUATION INTERNE DU PROJET
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Anti-trafficking is one of the two explicit primary subjects of the evaluated project, appearing prominently in the project title, evaluation questions, and throughout the results matrix. The excerpts suggest multiple substantive outputs and analytical discussions directly addressing this objective: the development and validation of national SOPs for trafficking prevention and victim assistance (Actions 10e, 10g, 10h); dissemination of the 2011 anti-trafficking law (Action 10a-related); training of state and non-state actors including border police from 5 Central African countries on identification and referral (Action 10i); creation of the CONATT coalition of 22 CSOs (Action 10e); awareness-raising campaigns reaching parliamentarians, traditional leaders, students, and communities (Action 10j); and the referral system for trafficking victims (Action 10h). The report also discusses the legal framework (2011 law), institutional capacity-building, and multi-stakeholder coordination. For a synthesis specialist, the results matrix sections on Effets directs 1, 2, and 3, the analysis of SOPs adoption, the discussion of the referral system, and the recommendations on victim support infrastructure would appear particularly valuable. The regional dimension (training of police from RCA, RDC, Congo, Tchad, Cameroun) adds cross-border relevance. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION INTERNE DU PROJET
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12, particularly regarding referral mechanisms and SOPs. The development, validation, and adoption of Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for trafficking victim identification and referral is a central project output, directly relevant to Action 12c (gender-responsive and child-sensitive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures) and Action 12b (specialized training for first responders and government officials on identification and referral of trafficking victims and vulnerable migrants). The excerpts describe training of state and non-state actors, validation workshops, and the adoption of the referral system by key ministries (MINTSS, MINAS). The creation of a national referral system with agreed procedures among multiple actors is substantively analyzed. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on Effet direct 2, Produits 2.1 and 2.2, and the results matrix analysis on SOPs adoption and actor training would appear most relevant as starting points for deeper review. The report's treatment of referral system development in a low-resource context may offer useful evidence on implementation challenges and multi-stakeholder coordination.

References.Title: RAPPORT D'ÉVALUATION EXTERNE DU PROJET « SOUTENIR LES MIGRATIONS SÛRES ET INFORMÉES AU NIGER »
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 3. The entire project evaluated — 'Soutenir les migrations sûres et informées au Niger' — is explicitly centered on providing accurate, timely information to migrants and potential migrants to support informed decision-making. This aligns directly with the objective's core theme and several associated actions. The project title itself references 'safe and informed migration decisions,' and the evaluation assesses a comprehensive information and communication campaign targeting migrants in transit and potential migrants in Niamey and Agadez. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: (a) multi-channel information dissemination (radio, video, theater, peer-to-peer) relating to action 3e on awareness-raising campaigns; (b) information points and community outreach relating to action 3c; (c) pre-departure and in-transit information provision relating to action 3d; and (d) the effectiveness of different communication formats (testimonials vs. sketches) in conveying migration risks. Multiple lessons learned and recommendations directly address information strategy design, content production, and dissemination effectiveness. The executive summary, lessons learned sections (particularly i, vi, xi), and the recommendations matrix would appear most valuable for human reviewers conducting a synthesis on this objective. The report likely offers rich evidence on what communication approaches work for informing migrants about irregular migration risks in West African transit contexts.

References.Title: RAPPORT D'ÉVALUATION EXTERNE DU PROJET « SOUTENIR LES MIGRATIONS SÛRES ET INFORMÉES AU NIGER »
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 17, particularly regarding action 17c on promoting independent, objective media reporting on migration and action 17f on awareness-raising campaigns. The project explicitly involves training journalists on migration issues (RJNM, RJPR/Agadez), producing evidence-based media content, and promoting accurate public discourse about migration. The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of journalist capacity-building, media content production (documentaries, radio magazines, sketches), and public awareness campaigns — all directly relevant to shaping evidence-based public perceptions of migration. Lesson learned i discusses the link between journalist training and behavior change; lesson xiii addresses culturally appropriate communication strategies; and multiple recommendations address strengthening journalist networks (RJNM, RJAM) and media partnerships. The sections on journalist training outcomes, media content production, and the RJNM network development would appear most valuable for human reviewers. However, the report's primary focus is on informing migrants themselves rather than on combating discrimination or xenophobia specifically, which limits its direct relevance to some of Objective 17's core themes.

References.Title: EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF IOM ZIMBABWE'S COMMUNITY BASED PLANNING PROGRAMME 2008 - 2013
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. The evaluation appears to explicitly examine a community-based planning process designed to integrate IDPs and migrants into host communities as active participants — directly addressing Actions 16a (promoting mutual respect and social cohesion), 16c (developing inclusion policy goals), and 16f (community centres/programmes facilitating migrant participation). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how the CBP process fostered social cohesion: the report documents that formerly displaced persons were no longer viewed as 'trouble makers' or 'aliens' but as equal contributing members, and that IDPs and host communities planning together met conditions for durable solutions including freedom from discrimination and harassment. The effectiveness section on 'reintegration of IDPs and migrants into broader communities' appears particularly relevant, as does the discussion of shared visioning processes that built trust between IDPs and host communities. The conclusions and lessons on community ownership and participation would likely add value to a synthesis on social inclusion. Specialists would particularly want to review the effectiveness findings on reintegration outcomes and the sections on CBP principles related to inclusion and shared vision, noting that the report focuses on internal displacement rather than international migration, which may limit direct applicability.

References.Title: EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF IOM ZIMBABWE'S COMMUNITY BASED PLANNING PROGRAMME 2008 - 2013
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly on sustainable reintegration. The evaluation explicitly examines IOM's programming aimed at achieving 'durable solutions' for IDPs and migrants through community reintegration — directly addressing Action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration into community life through social protection, employment, and inclusion) and Action 21i (identifying needs of communities to which migrants return). The report documents outcomes related to reintegration: IDPs being accepted as equal community members, joint planning between IDPs and host communities, and the role of livelihoods interventions in supporting economic reintegration. The CBP process is framed throughout as an entry point for durable solutions and reintegration. The effectiveness section on 'reintegration of IDPs and migrants into broader communities' and the conclusions on community ownership appear particularly relevant. Specialists would want to review these sections, noting that the report addresses internal displacement rather than international return and readmission, which may limit applicability to some associated actions (e.g., 21a-21g on readmission agreements and return procedures), but the reintegration dimensions appear substantively covered.

References.Title: IMPROVING ACCESS TO HEALTH, WATER AND SANITATION SERVICES, EDUCATION, AND FREEDOM FROM VIOLENCE IN COX'S BAZAR
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The evaluation appears to examine a multi-sectoral humanitarian response targeting a highly vulnerable displaced population (Rohingya/UMNs), with explicit attention to women at risk of SGBV, people with disabilities, children (including nutrition and vaccination), and psychosocial support needs. These align closely with Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerability), 7c (gender-responsive policies including health care and counselling), and 7e/7f (child protection). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: disability-inclusive services (Handicap International component), psychosocial/trauma counselling, maternal health, and protection from SGBV. Recommendations 6.2.3 (psychosocial support) and 6.2.2 (SAM children) appear particularly relevant. The KAP Survey results on SGBV awareness also touch on Action 7c. Specialists reviewing this report for a synthesis on Objective 7 would likely want to examine the findings sections on health, protection, and disability services, as well as the recommendations on psychosocial support and child nutrition. Note that the population is forcibly displaced rather than voluntary migrants, which may affect applicability depending on the synthesis scope.

References.Title: IMPROVING ACCESS TO HEALTH, WATER AND SANITATION SERVICES, EDUCATION, AND FREEDOM FROM VIOLENCE IN COX'S BAZAR
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The project title itself — 'Improving Access to Health, Water and Sanitation Services, Education, and Freedom from Violence in Cox's Bazar' — directly mirrors the language of this objective. The evaluation appears to comprehensively assess access to health care, WASH, nutrition, education, and psychosocial services for a displaced migrant population, aligning closely with Actions 15c (holistic accessible service points), 15e (health needs of migrants in national/local health policies), and 15f (inclusive education). The excerpts suggest extensive findings on: health facility satisfaction (87-99.7% satisfaction rates), vaccination coverage (92% of children), WASH outcomes (reduction in open defecation), disability-inclusive services, and nutrition support. Recommendations address service quality consistency, coverage gaps, and infrastructure improvements. The inclusion of host communities alongside displaced persons adds comparative value. For a synthesis specialist, the findings sections on health, WASH, and nutrition outcomes, the KAP Survey results, and the OECD-DAC analysis (Section 5) would appear to be the most valuable starting points. The report likely provides rich evidence on what works in delivering basic services to displaced populations in resource-constrained settings, which is directly relevant to Actions 15c and 15e.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “OPEN SOUTH AMERICA”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 3. The 'Open South America' platform is explicitly and centrally a tool for providing accurate, timely, accessible and transparent information to migrants, governments and the public — which is the core commitment of GCM Objective 3. The project title, evaluation questions, and virtually all findings and conclusions directly address information provision mechanisms. The excerpts suggest strong alignment with Action 3a (centralized publicly accessible national/regional website on migration options, including border restrictions, quarantine requirements, travel policies), Action 3b (systematic bilateral and regional cooperation to exchange migration information through joint databases), and Action 3e (multilingual information campaigns). The platform covers information in Spanish, Portuguese and English, addresses multiple countries simultaneously, and was designed to combat misinformation — all directly relevant to Objective 3 commitments. The evaluation's conclusions on effectiveness, sustainability, and governance of such information platforms would likely provide valuable evidence for synthesis specialists. Sections on the platform's development, stakeholder engagement, sustainability challenges, and the measurement framework weaknesses appear particularly worth prioritizing for deeper review. The full report likely contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT: SRI LANKA: UNDERSTANDING MIGRATION, ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION AND CLIMATE CHANGE
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The MECC Project appears to have been substantially grounded in research and data generation — the excerpts describe a joint rapid assessment (IOM/FAO/WFP) that identified evidence gaps on climate-migration linkages, and Output 1.1 explicitly calls for an 'in-depth research report on human mobility and climate change.' The project appears to have generated primary research data that is now being used by university researchers, suggesting data dissemination and use. The evaluation also employed disaggregated data approaches (gender-disaggregated community assessments). These elements relate to GCM Actions 1d (data on effects of migration), 1k (research on migration-sustainable development interrelationship), and potentially 1j (country-specific migration profiles). However, data collection and analysis appear to be instrumental to the project's policy objectives rather than the primary focus of the evaluation itself. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on Output 1.1 and the research methodology, as well as the discussion of how research findings fed into policy — framed as pointers for further review rather than definitive characterizations.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT: SRI LANKA: UNDERSTANDING MIGRATION, ENVIRONMENTAL DEGRADATION AND CLIMATE CHANGE
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its sub-themes on climate change, environmental degradation, and migration. The project title itself — 'Understanding Migration, Environmental Degradation and Climate Change' — directly maps onto the core concerns of Objective 2. The excerpts suggest the evaluation extensively examines: (a) the nexus between climate-induced displacement and internal migration in Sri Lanka (Actions 2h, 2i); (b) community resilience and adaptation strategies to slow-onset disasters (Action 2i); (c) integration of mobility considerations into national climate change policy (Action 2h); and (d) livelihood pilots to reduce forced migration (Action 2b on resilience and disaster risk reduction). The executive summary explicitly states the project aimed to 'mainstream human mobility considerations into climate change and environmental strategies' and to 'enhance resilience of communities.' The evaluation's conclusions on sustainability and impact further address long-term adaptation. For synthesis specialists, the sections on Relevance (project alignment with national climate priorities), Impact (policy influence on National Climate Change Policy 2023), and the community livelihood pilot findings would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The full report likely contains additional evidence on climate-migration dynamics beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Evaluation of IOM Accountability to Affected Populations
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 23, specifically regarding IOM's institutional role in inter-agency coordination and collective accountability mechanisms. The report appears to substantively examine IOM's engagement with IASC coordination structures, inter-agency AAP working groups, and collective accountability frameworks — themes that connect to the governance and partnership dimensions of GCM Objective 23. The concept of 'collective accountability' is described as a 'frequent and important concept' throughout the evaluation, with analysis of how IOM coordinates with other UN agencies and partners on joint CFMs and needs data. Recommendation 9 explicitly addresses continued engagement in inter-agency coordination spaces (IASC-promoted initiatives, global and country-level coordination structures), and the conclusions discuss IOM's participation in IASC as bringing benefits in resource mobilization and awareness. These elements appear to connect to actions 23a (supporting collective GCM implementation) and 23e (bilateral/multilateral partnerships on migration policy). However, a synthesis specialist should note that this report examines inter-agency coordination primarily as an operational feature of AAP delivery rather than as a governance mechanism for GCM implementation per se — the international cooperation discussed is largely programmatic coordination rather than global migration governance architecture. The sections on collective accountability in the conclusions, and Recommendation 9 with its associated activities, would appear most relevant for human reviewers to prioritize. The full report may contain additional evidence on inter-agency coordination beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Report on IOM Development Fund 2019 Ex-post Evaluations
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 23, particularly regarding capacity-building for migration governance in developing Member States. The IOM Development Fund's explicit mandate — 'supporting developing Member State efforts to strengthen their migration management capacity' — aligns closely with Actions 23a (technical and financial assistance to support GCM implementation) and 23d (capacity-building mechanisms). The report synthesizes lessons from 28 projects across multiple developing countries, providing what appears to be substantive evidence on what works in building national migration governance capacity through international cooperation. Key themes in the excerpts — interministerial coordination, sustainability of capacity-building interventions, ownership by national partners, and the role of IOM as a technical assistance provider — are directly relevant to how international cooperation for migration governance is structured and delivered. The overall recommendations section, particularly those directed at 'regional offices' and 'project developers,' and the findings on sustainability and effectiveness of capacity-building, would likely be most valuable for a synthesis specialist to review. However, it is important to note that the report's primary object of inquiry is project management quality rather than international cooperation architecture per se — the international cooperation dimension is the operational context. Specialists should weigh this when assessing depth of evidence on Objective 23 specifically.

References.Title: EX-POST EVALUATION REPORT OF THE PROJECT: REGIONAL CAPACITY-BUILDING FOR THE PRODUCTION AND ANALYSIS OF REGIONAL MIGRATION INFORMATION IN MESOAMERICA AND THE CARIBBEAN
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The evaluated project is explicitly and centrally focused on building regional capacity for the production, systematization, and analysis of migration data in Mesoamerica and the Caribbean — which maps almost directly onto the core commitments of Objective 1. The project title, stated objectives, evaluation questions, and all five outputs are oriented around migration data collection, processing, dissemination, and use for evidence-based policymaking. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple associated actions: Action 1a (harmonizing methodologies and improving data collection strategies at regional level), Action 1b (improving international comparability of migration statistics — a recurring challenge discussed throughout), Action 1c (building national capacities in data collection, analysis and dissemination — the project's primary outcome), Action 1e (collaboration between global and regional databases, including explicit discussion of PRIMI's relationship with IOM's Migration Data Portal), Action 1f (establishing regional mechanisms/observatories for migration data), and Action 1i (using administrative records for statistical purposes, highlighted as an innovative feature of PRIMI). The evaluation's conclusions and recommendations sections appear particularly rich for synthesis purposes, addressing data harmonization challenges, capacity gaps, platform sustainability, and the relationship between data quality and evidence-based policymaking. The gender mainstreaming section also touches on data disaggregation by sex, age, and gender (Action 1a). Human reviewers conducting a synthesis on Objective 1 should prioritize the effectiveness, sustainability, and coherence sections, as well as the recommendations throughout — these appear to contain the most operationally relevant evidence on what works and what doesn't in regional migration data capacity-building.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE EU-IOM KNOWLEDGE MANAGEMENT HUB (KMH)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The KMH evaluation substantially addresses the development, harmonization, and institutionalization of M&E frameworks and data collection systems for return and reintegration programmes. The excerpts suggest that a core function of the KMH is improving monitoring, data collection, and knowledge management across IOM operations — directly touching on actions 1a (harmonizing methodologies for data collection) and 1c (building national capacities in data collection and analysis). The evaluation's conclusions and recommendations explicitly address the quality of the M&E Package, the need for harmonized data collection, and the institutionalization of KM functions. Conclusion 2 notes that the logframe and data collection system do not adequately support results-orientation, and Recommendation 3 calls for a Roadmap to revise the institutional M&E Package with provisions for harmonizing data collection and supporting comparable performance indicators.
