The lessons learned also emphasize the importance of maintaining outcome orientation in monitoring. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on M&E Package development (Conclusions 2, 6, 11), the recommendations on data harmonization, and the discussion of the KMH Platform as a knowledge repository would appear most relevant. The report's focus is not exclusively on data systems — it covers a broader range of KMH functions — but data and evidence management appear to be a substantive secondary theme throughout the excerpts reviewed.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE EU-IOM KNOWLEDGE MANAGEMENT HUB (KMH)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Return and reintegration is the explicit and central subject of the entire evaluation — the KMH is described as 'a global support and resource centre' specifically focused on 'harmonisation and strengthening of models of intervention, capacities, and evidence-based knowledge on return and reintegration.' The report title, evaluation questions, conclusions, recommendations, and lessons learned all directly and substantively address the themes of GCM Objective 21. The evaluation appears to provide rich evidence on: the design and harmonization of return and reintegration programmes (Actions 21a, 21b); sustainable reintegration approaches including social, economic, and psychosocial dimensions (Action 21h); the role of partner countries and local systems in supporting returnees (Action 21i); monitoring and evaluation of reintegration outcomes; and the development perspective in reintegration programming. Lessons Learned 1-4 directly address the conditions for sustainable reintegration, the importance of development perspectives, and the need for coordinated action between migration management and development actors — all highly relevant to Actions 21b, 21h, and 21i. Conclusions 1, 6, 7, 10, and 11 provide substantive assessments of reintegration programme quality, capacity building, and sustainability. Recommendations address M&E Package revision, partner country engagement, and institutionalization of reintegration support functions. For a synthesis specialist, the executive summary, lessons learned section, and conclusions 1, 6, 7, 10, and 11 would appear to be the highest-priority sections for review. The full report likely contains additional detailed evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Apoyo a la integración socioeconómica y fortalecimiento de los sistemas de salud y de las organizaciones de la sociedad civil en el contexto de COVID -19 y su impacto en la población venezolana y receptora.
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The entire project — and thus the evaluation — is explicitly centered on improving access to basic services (health and education) for Venezuelan migrants and host communities in Colombia. This theme appears prominently in the project title, evaluation objectives, and both result areas. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly relevant to Actions 15e (health needs of migrants, strengthening service capacities, non-discriminatory access, culturally-sensitive delivery) and 15f (inclusive and equitable quality education for migrant children, non-discriminatory access, school integration). Key areas of likely value for a synthesis include: (1) the evaluation's assessment of how vaccination campaigns and sexual and reproductive health services were adapted to reach mobile, vulnerable migrant populations — including through extramural service delivery and community health networks; (2) analysis of barriers to educational access for NNA migrants (uniforms, transport, food, infrastructure, xenophobia) and how the project addressed them; (3) conclusions on the 'humanization' of service delivery as a key factor in reaching migrants; and (4) recommendations on strengthening institutional alliances to sustain migrant-inclusive service delivery. The conclusions and recommendations sections, as well as the sections on Resultado 1 and Resultado 2, would appear particularly valuable as starting points for human reviewers. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Apoyo a la integración socioeconómica y fortalecimiento de los sistemas de salud y de las organizaciones de la sociedad civil en el contexto de COVID -19 y su impacto en la población venezolana y receptora.
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. Social cohesion and migrant integration appear to be explicit secondary themes of the project, particularly in the education component. The excerpts suggest substantive content on: intercultural integration activities in schools (Action 16h — multicultural activities; Action 16i — welcoming school environments); anti-xenophobia and anti-discrimination programming; cultural sensitivity workshops using arts-based approaches; and the promotion of inclusive school environments for migrant children. The conclusions section explicitly discusses 'cambio de conciencia cultural,' increased participation of migrant students in school governance, and reduced xenophobia as project outcomes. These connect to Actions 16a (mutual respect, integration policies), 16f (community-level intercultural dialogue), 16h (multicultural activities), and 16i (welcoming school environments). However, social cohesion appears as one of two major themes alongside health service access, and the analytical depth on integration outcomes seems to rely heavily on qualitative perceptions rather than systematic measurement. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the conclusions section (pages 67-69) and the sections on Resultado 1 activities, particularly the cultural integration activities (A.1.5), as well as the recommendations on sustaining cultural transformation. The report's focus on the Colombia-Venezuela migration context adds geographic specificity that may be valuable for regional evidence syntheses.

References.Title: Apoyo a la integración socioeconómica y fortalecimiento de los sistemas de salud y de las organizaciones de la sociedad civil en el contexto de COVID -19 y su impacto en la población venezolana y receptora.
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to contain relevant content for a synthesis on GCM Objective 17, though it is not the primary focus. The evaluation explicitly addresses xenophobia and racial discrimination reduction as project outcomes, with the conclusions section discussing how the project contributed to 'cambio de conciencia sobre el otro' and improvements in empathic and communicative skills among students and teachers. Anti-xenophobia campaigns are listed as project activities (A.1.6), and the conclusions note that while discriminatory practices persist, cultural transformation was achieved in educational institutions. This connects to Actions 17f (awareness-raising campaigns on positive contributions of migration) and 17g (engaging educators and community leaders to prevent intolerance). The report also discusses gender-based discrimination reduction, including increased female participation in school governance and greater freedom of expression around sexual and gender identities. However, discrimination reduction appears as a secondary outcome of the education component rather than a primary analytical focus, and the evidence base appears largely qualitative. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the conclusions section (pages 67-69) on discrimination and xenophobia outcomes, and the recommendations on sustaining cultural transformation programs, while noting the limited systematic measurement of discrimination reduction.

References.Title: NIGERIA: STRENGTHENING ASSISTANCE FOR RETURNEES AND POTENTIAL MIGRANTS AND PROMOTING SAFE MIGRATION PRACTICES IN COMMUNITES OF ORIGIN
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The evaluation is explicitly and comprehensively focused on Assisted Voluntary Return and Reintegration (AVRR) in Nigeria — the core subject of GCM Objective 21. The project title, evaluation objectives, and all major evaluation criteria directly address return and sustainable reintegration. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across multiple dimensions directly corresponding to GCM Objective 21 actions: Action 21b (gender-responsive voluntary return and reintegration programmes with free, prior and informed consent) is central to the entire evaluation; Action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through social protection, psychosocial assistance, vocational training, employment, and financial services) appears to be the primary analytical focus; Action 21i (identifying and addressing needs of communities to which migrants return, including CBR projects) is explicitly evaluated. The evaluation covers economic, social, and psychosocial reintegration sustainability; private sector partnerships; government capacity building; and community-based reintegration — all directly relevant to Objective 21. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on reintegration sustainability (economic, social, psychosocial), CBR project impacts, private sector partnerships, M&E of reintegration outcomes, and all nine recommendations would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is likely that the full report contains substantially more evidence on reintegration mechanisms, outcomes, and lessons learned than what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Final Internal Evaluation of the project: “Comprehensive response to the humanitarian needs of the displaced population in Burundi”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The DTM (Displacement Tracking Matrix) component appears to be a substantive focus of the evaluation, with dedicated sections describing how IOM Burundi conducted 36 assessments across 18 provinces, trained 626 DTM focal points, and built data collection capacity with the Burundi Red Cross (BRC). The excerpts suggest analysis of data collection methodologies, disaggregation by gender and vulnerability characteristics, capacity building for national data systems, and the use of data to inform humanitarian response. These themes relate closely to Actions 1c (building national capacities in data collection), 1d (collecting data on effects of migration), and 1j (developing country-specific migration profiles). The evaluation also discusses data quality, report production, and the National Platform's capacity gaps in data management — directly relevant to evidence synthesis on this objective. Sections most worth reviewing for a synthesis specialist would be 6.1.1 (DTM Component under Relevance), 6.2.1 (DTM under Efficiency), and 6.3.1 (DTM under Sustainability), as well as the recommendations on strengthening NP data management. The report appears to offer operational-level evidence on how DTM systems function in a humanitarian displacement context, which would be valuable though not exhaustive for a synthesis on Objective 1.

References.Title: Final Internal Evaluation of the project: “Comprehensive response to the humanitarian needs of the displaced population in Burundi”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. The IBM (Immigration and Border Management) component is one of four substantive project components, with dedicated evaluation sections. The excerpts describe capacity building for border management agencies (750 police officers trained on HBM, border security, and SOP development), development of Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) for Humanitarian Border Management (HBM), inter-agency coordination meetings (10 meetings held), and infrastructure improvements at border points. These relate closely to Actions 11a (international border management cooperation and capacity-building), 11b (effective integrated border management structures), and 11g (cross-border collaboration on treatment of persons crossing borders). The evaluation also discusses sustainability of border management capacity through training of trainers and institutional ownership by the CGM. Sections most worth reviewing for a synthesis specialist include 6.1.4 (IBM under Relevance), 6.2.4 (IBM under Efficiency), 6.3.4 (IBM under Sustainability), and the lessons learned section on police training. The report appears to offer operational evidence on humanitarian border management capacity building in a displacement context, which would be valuable for a synthesis on Objective 11, though border management is one of several components rather than the sole focus.

References.Title: Final Internal Evaluation of the project: “Comprehensive response to the humanitarian needs of the displaced population in Burundi”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. Social cohesion and reintegration of returnees and IDPs into host communities appear to be central themes of the project and evaluation. The TRD (Transition and Recovery) component explicitly focuses on improving social cohesion, with community dialogues, Quick Impact Projects (QIPs) implemented through Cash-for-Work, and infrastructure rehabilitation designed to foster inclusion of returnees and IDPs into host communities. The excerpts contain direct beneficiary testimony on social integration outcomes, quantitative data (97.5% felt part of the community, 100% engaged more with community members), and analysis of mechanisms for social cohesion (community dialogues, QIP monitoring committees, local authority involvement). These relate closely to Actions 16a (promoting mutual respect and social cohesion), 16c (national policy goals for inclusion), and 16f (community centres/programmes for migrant participation). The evaluation also examines sustainability of social cohesion gains through institutional handover and community ownership. Sections most worth reviewing for a synthesis specialist include 6.1.3 (TRD under Relevance), 6.2.3 (TRD under Efficiency), 6.3.3 (TRD under Sustainability), the beneficiary testimonies, and the lessons learned section. The report appears to offer substantive operational evidence on community-driven approaches to social cohesion in a return and displacement context, which would be highly valuable for a synthesis on Objective 16.

References.Title: Final Internal Evaluation of the project: “Comprehensive response to the humanitarian needs of the displaced population in Burundi”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Safe and dignified return and sustainable reintegration of Burundian refugees from Tanzania appear to be core themes of the project and evaluation. The project explicitly addresses voluntary repatriation (through the Voluntary Repatriation Program), spontaneous returns, and the reintegration of returnee households (3,104 returnee households reintegrated socioeconomically, 2,382 provided housing assistance). The evaluation examines reintegration mechanisms including shelter assistance, social cohesion activities, QIPs, and host community absorption capacity — all directly relevant to Actions 21b (gender-responsive return and reintegration programmes), 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through social protection, employment, and financial services), and 21i (identifying needs of communities to which migrants return). The report also discusses coordination with the Burundian government, the Voluntary Repatriation Program, and cross-border dynamics with Tanzania. Sections most worth reviewing for a synthesis specialist include the executive summary on reintegration outcomes, 6.1.2 and 6.1.3 (Relevance findings on S/NFI and TRD), 6.3.2 and 6.3.3 (Sustainability of reintegration activities), beneficiary testimonies, and recommendations on reintegration harmonization. The report appears to offer substantive operational evidence on multi-sectoral reintegration programming in a voluntary return context, which would be highly valuable for a synthesis on Objective 21.

References.Title: REPORT ON IOM DEVELOPMENT FUND FUNDING ANALYSIS (2005-2015)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7, particularly regarding health-related vulnerabilities of migrants. The report appears to substantively address the health needs of vulnerable migrant populations — a theme that runs throughout the IDF-funded project portfolio reviewed. The second most common H2 thematic area addressed across projects was 'addressing the health needs of migrants and vulnerable populations,' covering displaced persons, victims of trafficking, sex workers, returnees, irregular migrants, migrant detainees, and migrants in border areas (9 out of 31 projects). This directly relates to Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations) and 7c (gender-responsive migration policies addressing health care and psychological services). The report also discusses projects addressing mental health and psychosocial support, access to health services for irregular migrants, and detention of irregular migrants — touching on Actions 7c and 7f. The sections on 'Key Issues Addressed by the Funded Projects' and 'Migration Health Themes addressed by IDF projects' would appear most relevant for human reviewers to prioritize. The cross-cutting issues table (Table 4) and Figure 9 on cross-cutting issues also appear worth examining. It should be noted that vulnerability is addressed primarily through a health lens, which may limit direct applicability to broader vulnerability frameworks under Objective 7.

References.Title: REPORT ON IOM DEVELOPMENT FUND FUNDING ANALYSIS (2005-2015)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly Action 15e on incorporating health needs of migrants in national and local health care policies and plans. The report's entire focus is on IOM's Migration Health programming funded through IDF — examining how projects have strengthened government capacity to provide migrant-inclusive health services across multiple countries and regions over a decade. The excerpts suggest that multiple conclusions and recommendations directly address migrant access to health services, health system strengthening, and policy development for migrant-inclusive health systems. The H2 thematic area 'Promoting Migrant-Inclusive Health Systems & Services' (4 projects) and 'Addressing Health Needs of Irregular and Vulnerable Migrants' (9 projects) are directly aligned with Action 15e. The report documents concrete outcomes: development of migration health policies in Sri Lanka, establishment of migration health units within Ministries of Health, integration of migrant health into national frameworks in multiple countries, and capacity strengthening of governments to provide migrant-friendly services. The sections on 'Key Issues Addressed by the Funded Projects,' 'Migration Health Themes addressed by IDF projects' (Figure 8), and the Conclusions section appear particularly valuable for human reviewers. The recommendations on NCD programming, SDG alignment, and evaluation practices also appear relevant. The report likely provides comparative evidence across 31 projects in multiple regions, which would be valuable for a synthesis on health service access for migrants.

References.Title: STRENGTHENING THE CAPACITY OF THE GOVERNMENT OF SEYCHELLES TO COMBAT TRAFFICKING IN PERSONS - ENDLINE EVALUATION REPORT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would almost certainly be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. Counter-trafficking is the explicit and sole primary subject of the evaluation — the project title itself is 'Strengthening the Capacity of the Government of Seychelles to Combat Trafficking in Persons.' The report appears to directly address multiple associated actions under Objective 10: Action 10a (Palermo Protocol obligations are explicitly discussed throughout); Action 10g (strengthening legislation — the passing of the 2014 counter-trafficking law is a key outcome); Action 10h (protection and assistance to VoTs, including SOP development and shelter establishment); Action 10i (training programmes for law enforcement, civil society, and journalists to identify TiP signs); and Action 10j (mass media awareness campaigns on TiP risks). The evaluation assesses the effectiveness of capacity-building for law enforcement and civil society to identify VoTs, the development of a National Strategic Framework and Action Plan, the functioning of the National Committee on Counter Trafficking (NCCTiP), and the first-ever TiP prosecution case in Seychelles. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on effectiveness (training outcomes, VoT identification capacity), sustainability (institutional framework, NCCTiP continuity, law enactment), and impact (ranking improvements, first prosecution) would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The recommendations on Training of Trainers, police training gaps, and continued engagement also offer practical insights. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EX-POST INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “STRENGTHENING DISASTER PREPAREDNESS AND RESPONSE IN SIERRA LEONE” (DP.1885)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its actions related to disaster risk reduction, resilience-building, and displacement preparedness. The project's core focus — strengthening disaster preparedness and response in Sierra Leone — directly aligns with Actions 2i (developing adaptation and resilience strategies to natural disasters), 2j (integrating displacement considerations into disaster preparedness strategies), and 2b (investing in resilience and disaster risk reduction). The excerpts indicate that the project implemented community-based DRR trainings, disaster risk assessments, and coordination mechanisms for disaster response — all substantively relevant to these actions. The context section explicitly references the Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction (Action 2a). The project also developed an IDP registration tool, connecting to displacement management under Action 2j. The conclusions and recommendations sections appear to contain substantive analysis of what worked in building community resilience and what gaps remain. For a synthesis specialist, the effectiveness section (on community resilience outcomes), the sustainability section (on lasting DRR capacity), and the recommendations on continuing DRR activities would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The report appears to be one of relatively few evaluations focused specifically on disaster preparedness and community resilience in a West African context, which adds to its potential value for synthesis.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “MIGRATION AS A LIVELIHOOD  DIVERSIFICATION STRATEGY IN THE DELTA (MILDAS)”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 3. The MILDAS project appears centrally focused on providing accurate migration information to prospective migrants and migrant-sending households — a core theme of Objective 3. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: (1) safe migration training curricula and their effectiveness (pre/post tests), relating to Action 3e (awareness-raising and pre-departure orientation); (2) Safe Migration Promoters (SMPs) as community-level information providers, relating to Action 3c (information points along migration routes); (3) the 'Miss Migration' social media tool for information dissemination; and (4) training of CSOs to deliver migration information. The evaluation's effectiveness, sustainability, and lessons learned sections appear to directly assess the quality and reach of information provision mechanisms. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the effectiveness section on migration training outcomes, the sustainability discussion on SMPs and knowledge retention, and the lessons learned section on cost-effective information provision. The report appears to offer practical evidence on what works in community-level migration information delivery in a low-income rural context.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “MIGRATION AS A LIVELIHOOD  DIVERSIFICATION STRATEGY IN THE DELTA (MILDAS)”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The MILDAS project is explicitly framed around migration as a livelihood diversification strategy and the contributions of migration to household and community development — core themes of Objective 19. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: (1) how remittances and migration income contribute to household livelihoods (relating to Action 19a and 19b on integrating migration into development planning); (2) financial literacy and business development coaching to help migrant-sending households leverage remittances productively (relating to Action 19e on targeted support programmes); (3) the CHIME research on migration-development linkages (relating to Action 19c on research into non-financial contributions of migrants). The evaluation's overall framing — assessing whether migration can be managed as a development strategy — directly engages with the migration-development nexus central to Objective 19. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the effectiveness section on financial management outcomes, the sustainability discussion on revolving funds, and the lessons learned section on what approaches worked for connecting migration to development outcomes. The report appears to offer practical field-level evidence from a rural Myanmar context on the conditions under which migration contributes to sustainable development.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “MIGRATION AS A LIVELIHOOD  DIVERSIFICATION STRATEGY IN THE DELTA (MILDAS)”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute some evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 20. The MILDAS project includes components directly related to financial inclusion of migrants and their families: revolving funds providing low-interest loans to cover migration costs, financial literacy training, and business development coaching for remittance-receiving households. These relate to Actions 20f (financial literacy and inclusion through education and training), 20g (promoting investments from remittance senders in local development), 20h (enabling migrant women to access financial literacy training), and 20i (providing banking solutions and financial instruments for migrants). The evaluation provides substantive assessment of the revolving fund model's effectiveness and sustainability, noting good loan repayment rates and management committee capacity, while also critiquing the financial literacy coaching as not cost-effective. The recommendations section also calls for further research on remittance transfer methods and costs. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the revolving fund sustainability discussion, the gender responsiveness section on women's access to loans, and the lessons learned on financial inclusion approaches. The report appears to offer practical evidence on community-based financial inclusion mechanisms for internal migrants in a low-income rural context.

References.Title: STRENGTHENING LABOUR MIGRATION MANAGEMENT IN LESOTHO
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 5, particularly regarding bilateral labour mobility agreements (Action 5a). The project explicitly worked toward a Bilateral Labour Agreement (BLA) between Lesotho and Mauritius, developed BLA guidelines aligned to regional frameworks, and facilitated a learning visit to Mauritius that resulted in a Declaration of Intent to sign an MOU. The evaluation discusses the negotiation process, the empowerment of government officials to negotiate more beneficial deals, and the challenges in finalizing the MOU. The project also engaged trade unions and employers' federations (relevant to Action 5e on skills matching with private sector and trade unions). The SADC regional framework alignment (Action 5b on regional labour mobility cooperation) is also explicitly discussed. Sections most worth reviewing for a synthesis specialist include: the discussion of the Mauritius MOU/BLA process in Section 3.1a and the Best Practices section (Declaration of Intent as good practice), the results framework analysis for Outcome 2, and the OECD-DAC criteria section on relevance. It is possible the full report contains additional detail on the BLA development process beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: “ STRENGTHENING LABOUR MIGRATION GOVERNANCE THROUGH REGIONAL COOPERATION IN COLOMBO PROCESS COUNTRIES"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 6. 'Fostering Ethical Recruitment' is explicitly one of the five thematic priorities of the project, and the TAWG on recruitment appears to have been among the most active (noted as holding more meetings than others). The evaluation discusses efforts to improve recruitment monitoring practices, complaints mechanisms, and the engagement of civil society (including MFA) in recruitment-related discussions. The cancelled knowledge product on 'mapping of existing recruitment monitoring practices and complaints mechanisms' and the ILO partnership on labour standards ratification are directly relevant to Actions 6c, 6f, and 6a respectively. The evaluation also addresses gender dimensions of recruitment, particularly for domestic workers (Actions 6k, 6i). The sections on TAWG effectiveness, the cancelled recruitment monitoring mapping, and the discussion of ILO collaboration on labour standards would appear most relevant for human reviewers to prioritize. The report's analysis of why regional-level recruitment governance discussions failed to translate into national-level policy change may also offer valuable insights for synthesis on the governance dimensions of fair recruitment. It is possible the full report contains more detailed findings on recruitment-specific outcomes than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: “ STRENGTHENING LABOUR MIGRATION GOVERNANCE THROUGH REGIONAL COOPERATION IN COLOMBO PROCESS COUNTRIES"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute some evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 18. 'Skills and Qualification Recognition' is explicitly one of the five thematic priorities of the project, and the Skills Pilot (sub-contracted to ILO) was a major project component — albeit one that was ultimately cancelled due to failure to identify employers in the UAE willing to meet Sri Lankan minimum wage requirements. The evaluation includes a dedicated case study on the Skills Pilot's effectiveness (noted in the annex). The TAWG on skills appears to have been active, and the evaluation discusses why regional-level skills discussions failed to translate into national-level action. These elements relate to GCM Objective 18 actions on bilateral skills partnerships (Action 18e), mutual recognition frameworks (Actions 18a, 18c), and skills matching (Action 18b). The sections on the Skills Pilot case study, the skills TAWG discussions, and the analysis of barriers to national-level implementation would appear most relevant for human reviewers. The evaluation's candid assessment of why the skills pilot failed may offer particularly valuable lessons for synthesis on implementation challenges in this area. It is possible the full report contains more detailed findings on skills recognition outcomes than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN EX-POST DEL PROYECTO “FORTALECIMIENTO DE LA CAPACIDAD DE LOS GOBIERNOS Y DE LOS MEDIOS DE COMUNICACIÓN DEL MERCOSUR PARA PROMOVER LOS APORTES POSITIVOS DE LA MIGRACIÓN”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 17, particularly Action 17c (promoting independent, objective and quality reporting by media outlets, sensitizing media professionals on migration-related issues and terminology, investing in ethical reporting standards) and Action 17f (promoting awareness-raising campaigns to inform public perceptions regarding positive contributions of migration). The project's core purpose — strengthening capacity of media and governments in MERCOSUR to promote positive contributions of migration — directly aligns with this objective's central theme of promoting evidence-based public discourse and countering xenophobia and stigmatization. The excerpts suggest that multiple conclusions and recommendations directly address: (1) training journalists to use rights-based, gender-sensitive framing and avoid stigmatizing language; (2) the Premio Suramericano de Periodismo as a mechanism to recognize and amplify quality migration journalism; (3) the use of evidence and data sources to counter stereotypes about migrants; and (4) the challenge of sustaining these media capacity-building efforts. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on project conclusions (particularly conclusions 7 and 8 on impact, and 9-10 on sustainability), the theory of change analysis, and the recommendations on institutionalizing the journalism prize and scaling training would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. The report likely offers concrete evidence on what works in media sensitization programs on migration, a relatively underexplored area in GCM implementation evidence. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE “TAJIK-AFGHAN INTEGRATION, RESILIENCE AND REFORM BUILDING PROGRAMME” (TAIRR PROGRAMME)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The programme explicitly targets vulnerable populations — returned migrants with re-entry bans, persons with disabilities, women, and those with psychological needs — and the evaluation devotes substantive attention to how vulnerabilities were identified and addressed. The conclusions section includes a dedicated discussion of vulnerability (financial, disability, psychological), noting that 17% of grantees were persons with disabilities, that psychological support was provided through reintegration planning sessions, and that the programme intentionally brought attention to psychological needs post-reformulation. This relates to Actions 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations), 7c (gender-responsive policies addressing health, psychological counselling), and 7k (involving local authorities in identification and assistance). The evaluation also notes limitations — one psychologist session was insufficient for serious psychosocial issues including suicidal thoughts — which provides nuanced evidence on gaps in vulnerability response. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the cross-cutting issues section on vulnerability and the conclusions on gender and vulnerability, as well as the impact section discussing individual-level changes from reintegration planning sessions.

References.Title: FINAL INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE “TAJIK-AFGHAN INTEGRATION, RESILIENCE AND REFORM BUILDING PROGRAMME” (TAIRR PROGRAMME)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly its associated actions on sustainable reintegration. The programme's component three is explicitly focused on reintegration support for returned migrant workers with re-entry bans, and the evaluation devotes substantial analytical attention to reintegration outcomes, approaches, and sustainability. The excerpts suggest multiple conclusions directly addressing reintegration themes: case management by Migration Services officials, reintegration planning sessions (RPS), psychosocial support, referral to services, and community-level reintegration through migration sub-committees. These relate closely to Action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through social protection, psycho-social assistance, vocational training, employment opportunities) and Action 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes). The evaluation also addresses sustainability of reintegration approaches, government ownership, and the piloting of good practices — all highly relevant to a synthesis on sustainable reintegration. The conclusions section on sustainability and the cross-cutting issues section on vulnerability and gender would appear particularly valuable for human reviewers. The evaluation's attention to the specific needs of re-entry banned migrants — a relatively underexplored population — adds distinctive value. It is possible the full report contains additional reintegration-specific evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Evaluation of the project “Regional Migration Policy and Knowledge Management Hub in South America”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 23, particularly regarding institutional capacity-building for migration governance and knowledge management as a foundation for coordinated international action. The project explicitly positions itself within IOM's governance pillar and is aligned with the UN Network on Migration, the Global Compact on Migration, the IOM Policy Hub, and IOM's Global Migration Data Analysis Centre — all mechanisms central to GCM Objective 23's vision of strengthened international cooperation architecture. The KM Hub is designed to support coordination across migration actors and to position IOM South America as a regional knowledge referent, which relates to actions 23a (technical assistance for GCM implementation) and 23d (capacity-building mechanisms). The Regional KM Strategy 2022-2027 appears to be an institutional mechanism for building the organizational infrastructure needed to support coherent, evidence-based migration governance across a multi-country region — directly relevant to the spirit of Objective 23. Conclusions 1, 2, and 8-10 in the excerpts address the strategy's alignment with global frameworks, its sustainability, and its role in strengthening IOM's regional coordination capacity. Recommendations 1-4 on governance, capacity development, and indicator frameworks would likely be of interest to synthesis specialists examining how international organizations build institutional capacity to support GCM implementation. Specialists should particularly review the sections on coherence (Conclusions 1-2), the Theory of Change discussion, and the sustainability conclusions (Conclusion 10), as these appear most directly relevant to Objective 23's focus on international cooperation architecture. It should be noted that this is a borderline case: the report primarily evaluates an internal IOM organizational initiative rather than an inter-state cooperation mechanism per se, and the international cooperation dimension is more contextual than analytical in places. Human reviewers should assess whether the institutional capacity-building focus provides sufficient evidence for a synthesis on Objective 23.

References.Title: Mid-Term Evaluation of Midnimo 2 Project: Support for the Attainment of Durable Solutions in Areas Impacted by Displacement and Returns, in Galmudug and Hirshabelle States
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. Social cohesion and community integration appear to be explicit themes of the Midnimo 2 project, with IOM's component specifically titled 'Community Empowerment and Social Cohesion.' The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: community participatory planning processes (CAPs) as tools for inclusion (Action 16a, 16c); the formation of Community Action Groups, Core Facilitation Teams, and Community Based Monitoring and Evaluation groups as mechanisms for community participation (Action 16f); mentorship programmes and innovation challenges for youth and IDPs (Action 16g); and the integration of IDPs, returnees, and host communities through consultative platforms. The report also discusses gender parity in beneficiary selection and committee formation (Action 16e). The lessons learnt section explicitly addresses how the project 'created consultative platforms between partners, IMC, local authorities' and brought communities closer to government. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on CAP development, community committee formation, and the comparison between Midnimo 1 and Midnimo 2 on gender and inclusion would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The report's focus on internal displacement rather than international migration is a limitation to note, but the social cohesion and inclusion mechanisms analyzed are substantively relevant.

References.Title: Mid-Term Evaluation of Midnimo 2 Project: Support for the Attainment of Durable Solutions in Areas Impacted by Displacement and Returns, in Galmudug and Hirshabelle States
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly on sustainable reintegration. The Midnimo 2 project is explicitly framed around 'durable solutions' for displacement-affected populations including returnees, which aligns closely with Action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration) and Action 21i (identifying needs of communities to which migrants return). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: reintegration of IDPs and returnees into local communities; local integration capacity as the preferred durable solution option; community-based planning processes that identify returnee needs; livelihood support and economic empowerment for returning populations; and the role of government institutions in supporting reintegration. The recommendation to 'Establish the Foundations for Durable Solutions by enhancing local integration capacity' directly addresses reintegration sustainability. The comparison between Midnimo 1 and Midnimo 2 on livelihoods integration and gender-responsiveness in reintegration programming would appear particularly valuable for synthesis specialists. The sections on CAP development, IMC coordination, and the conclusions on durable solutions would be priority areas for review. Note that the report addresses internal displacement/return rather than international return and readmission, which is a contextual limitation for GCM synthesis purposes.

References.Title: EVALUATION DE MI-PARCOURS DU PROJET "MIGRANT RESPONSE AND RESOURCE MECHANISM (MRRM) PHASE II"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The MRRM project is explicitly designed to provide humanitarian assistance to vulnerable migrants in transit — a core theme of Objective 7. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: direct assistance to migrants in transit centers (Actions 7b, 7k), protection of migrants on migration routes (Action 7a), gender-responsive approaches (Action 7c), and psychosocial support for returnees (Action 7b, 7c). The conclusions note 'very high performance' on the assistance component and 'satisfactory' reintegration results. Recommendations specifically address psychosocial support, child-friendly spaces in transit centers (Action 7e, 7f), and vulnerability identification. The project context describes migrants facing exploitation, detention, torture, rape, and slavery — and the MRRM's response to these vulnerabilities. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on assistance performance, the recommendations on child-friendly spaces and psychosocial support, and the findings on transit center operations would appear particularly valuable as starting points. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence on vulnerability identification and referral mechanisms beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUATION DE MI-PARCOURS DU PROJET "MIGRANT RESPONSE AND RESOURCE MECHANISM (MRRM) PHASE II"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The MRRM project explicitly provides direct humanitarian assistance and basic services to migrants in transit centers — a core theme of Objective 15. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of service delivery in transit centers (Actions 15c, 15e), with the evaluation noting 'very high performance' on the assistance component. The project context describes migrants needing protection and basic services, and the evaluation assesses the quality and reach of these services. The recommendation to include child-friendly spaces in transit centers (Action 15f) and the discussion of psychosocial support touch on specific service dimensions. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on transit center service delivery performance and the findings on beneficiary satisfaction would appear particularly valuable. The report likely contains more detailed evidence on service provision quality and access than the excerpts reveal, making it worth reviewing for a synthesis on this objective.

References.Title: EVALUATION DE MI-PARCOURS DU PROJET "MIGRANT RESPONSE AND RESOURCE MECHANISM (MRRM) PHASE II"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Return and reintegration is explicitly one of the three core components of the MRRM project and appears prominently in the evaluation's findings, conclusions, and recommendations. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: voluntary return assistance (Action 21b), sustainable reintegration support (Action 21h), regional cooperation for reintegration across West African countries of origin (Action 21a), and lessons learned on reintegration programming quality. The conclusions specifically note 'satisfactory performance on reintegration activities' and identify lessons learned. The recommendations provide detailed guidance on improving reintegration programming — including pre-departure sensitization, associative actor involvement in return zones, budget-beneficiary balance, beneficiary participation, and psychosocial support (all relating to Action 21b and 21h). The regional dimension (IOM offices in countries of origin implementing reintegration services) is also relevant to Action 21a. For a synthesis specialist, the reintegration findings and recommendations sections would appear particularly valuable, as would the discussion of regional cooperation frameworks. The full report likely contains detailed case evidence on reintegration outcomes that would be highly valuable for an evidence synthesis on Objective 21.

References.Title: FACILITATE SAFE AND REGULAR MIGRATION THROUGH ETHIAL RECRUITMENT OF MIGRANT WORKERS FROM ETHIOPIA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 6. Ethical recruitment is the explicit and central subject of the evaluated project — appearing in the project title ('Ethical Recruitment of Migrant Workers from Ethiopia'), all evaluation questions, and throughout findings, conclusions, and recommendations. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis directly addressing multiple associated actions under Objective 6: Action 6c (improving regulations on recruitment agencies, prohibiting fee-charging, monitoring the recruitment industry) is addressed through the IRIS standards training and the Recruitment Monitoring and Registration Manual; Action 6d (written contracts, worker awareness of rights) is touched upon through capacity building for PEAs and government; Action 6f (strengthening enforcement through labour inspectors and authorities) is addressed via training of MoLS officials and labour attachés; Action 6l (IOM IRIS standards) is explicitly central — the project is built around IRIS certification and standards adoption. The executive summary, conclusions (C1–C12), and recommendations (R1–R4) all directly address fair and ethical recruitment systems, PEA regulation, government capacity, and worker protection. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on IRIS training outcomes, PEA engagement, government capacity building, and the assessment of sustainability of ethical recruitment standards would appear particularly valuable as starting points for deeper review. It is likely that the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FACILITATE SAFE AND REGULAR MIGRATION THROUGH ETHIAL RECRUITMENT OF MIGRANT WORKERS FROM ETHIOPIA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 14. A significant component of the project (Output 1.2) focuses explicitly on building the capacity of labour attachés and embassy/consular officials in destination countries to promote ethical recruitment and protect migrant workers' rights. Activity 1.2.1 involves sensitization workshops with embassies in destination countries; Activity 1.2.4 involves training for labour attachés/embassy or consular officials. This directly relates to Action 14a (building consular capacities and training consular officers) and Action 14d (strengthening consular capacities to identify, protect and assist nationals abroad who are victims of labour rights violations). The assessment report and training manual developed for consular officials appear to be substantive deliverables. However, consular protection is one of two main outcome areas (alongside IRIS/PEA regulation), and the excerpts suggest the IRIS/PEA strand received more analytical attention. A synthesis specialist would particularly want to review the sections on Output 1.2 activities, the training content for labour attachés, and any findings on the effectiveness of consular capacity building — framing these as pointers for further review rather than definitive characterizations.

References.Title: Strengthening of territorial capacities for monitoring the Implementation Framework Plan and managing compliance with the commitments established in the Final Peace Agreement - SIIPO
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project evaluated appears to be fundamentally concerned with improving the capacity of territorial entities to generate, process, store, and deliver data related to peace accord implementation — which maps closely onto actions 1c (building national capacities in data collection, analysis and dissemination), 1i (enhancing collaboration to produce migration/policy-related statistics using administrative records), and 1j (developing country-specific profiles using disaggregated data). The evaluation explicitly assesses whether 170 municipalities and 19 gobernaciones have adequate data management capacities, and the diagnostic component of the project is essentially a data capacity assessment exercise. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of data quality attributes (oportunidad, completitud, integridad — timeliness, completeness, integrity), data gaps, and information system interoperability (SIIPO, SIEE). The gender-disaggregated data dimension (51 gender indicators in the PMI) also aligns with Objective 1's emphasis on sex-disaggregated data. While the context is peace accord monitoring rather than migration per se, the methodological and institutional lessons on subnational data capacity building appear transferable. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the conclusions on data capacity diagnostics, the recommendations on metadata, ranking systems, and responsible officials for data reporting, as well as the lessons learned on using secondary data sources. Note that the full report may contain additional relevant evidence beyond these excerpts.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE IOM PROJECT "DISASTER RISK REDUCTION THROUGH BUILDING COMMUNITY RESILIENCE IN PAPUA NEW GUINEA (DRRBCR) - PHASE II
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears highly relevant to GCM Objective 2, particularly its sub-themes on disaster risk reduction, climate change adaptation, and community resilience. The DRRBCR project is explicitly designed to reduce vulnerability to natural disasters in PNG — one of the world's most disaster-prone countries — through community-based disaster risk management (CBDRM), government capacity building, and DRR strategies. This aligns closely with Actions 2b (resilience and disaster risk reduction), 2i (adaptation and resilience strategies to natural disasters), 2j (integrating displacement considerations into disaster preparedness), and 2a (Sendai Framework implementation). The report explicitly references the Sendai Framework as a guiding framework. The executive summary and key findings sections appear particularly valuable for a synthesis specialist, as they discuss community-level resilience building, government capacity for DRM, and sustainability of DRR approaches. The evaluation's analysis of what works in community-based DRR programming in a highly disaster-prone Pacific Island context would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on reducing adverse drivers of migration, particularly those related to environmental and disaster-related displacement pressures.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN INTERMEDIA E IDENTIFICACIÓN DE LECCIONES Y BUENAS PRÁCTICAS DEL PROYECTO “FORTALECIMIENTO DE LA RESPUESTA REGIONAL A LA MIGRACIÓN A GRAN ESCALA DE LOS NACIONALES VENEZOLANOS EN ECUADOR”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The evaluation appears to center substantially on identifying, targeting, and responding to vulnerabilities among Venezuelan migrants and refugees in Ecuador — which aligns closely with the core theme of this objective. The excerpts suggest that vulnerability identification tools and targeting mechanisms are primary subjects of evaluation (Adecuación criterion, hallazgos 1-3), with conclusions noting that 'herramientas de identificación de vulnerabilidades' improved assistance targeting. The evaluation also covers psychosocial support (C2, R6), gender-responsive approaches (C20), protection of women at risk and GBV reduction (C15), and access to legal status (C10, C20) — all relating to Actions 7b, 7c, 7g, and 7h. The coverage criterion analysis (C12-C14) directly examines whether the most vulnerable populations were reached. The gender-differentiated impact analysis (C20) is particularly relevant to Action 7c. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the Adecuación, Eficacia, Cobertura, and Impacto sections, as well as the conclusions and recommendations on vulnerability targeting and psychosocial support. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN INTERMEDIA E IDENTIFICACIÓN DE LECCIONES Y BUENAS PRÁCTICAS DEL PROYECTO “FORTALECIMIENTO DE LA RESPUESTA REGIONAL A LA MIGRACIÓN A GRAN ESCALA DE LOS NACIONALES VENEZOLANOS EN ECUADOR”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. The evaluation appears to substantively examine migrants' access to basic services — including health, education, housing, and psychosocial support — as core outcomes of the project. The Eficacia conclusions (C2) explicitly state that project assistance contributed to 'acceso a derechos de salud, educación, trabajo, vivienda y movilidad,' directly mapping to Actions 15e and 15f. The coverage analysis (C12-C14) examines whether the most vulnerable migrants accessed these services, and complementary activities like 'Espacios Amigables' and digital channels (C13) relate to Action 15c (accessible service points). The evaluation also discusses barriers to service access related to migration status (C10, C20), relevant to Action 15b. The gender-differentiated impact analysis (C20) adds nuance on differential service access outcomes. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the Eficacia, Cobertura, and Impacto sections, as well as the conclusions on service access outcomes. The report appears to offer practical evidence on what types of assistance effectively reach vulnerable migrant populations, which would be valuable for a synthesis on Objective 15.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN INTERMEDIA E IDENTIFICACIÓN DE LECCIONES Y BUENAS PRÁCTICAS DEL PROYECTO “FORTALECIMIENTO DE LA RESPUESTA REGIONAL A LA MIGRACIÓN A GRAN ESCALA DE LOS NACIONALES VENEZOLANOS EN ECUADOR”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute relevant evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. The evaluation appears to substantively examine social and economic integration of Venezuelan migrants in Ecuador. The Eficacia analysis discusses sensibilización campaigns and intercultural activities as mechanisms reducing barriers to integration (C2, R4), directly relating to Actions 16a and 16h. The Conectividad criterion examines whether assistance supports longer-term life projects and economic self-sufficiency (C10), relating to Actions 16c and 16d. Recommendations on entrepreneurship training over vocational training (R11) and labor market insertion plans (R11) relate to Action 16d. The gender-differentiated impact analysis (C20) touches on women's economic empowerment (Action 16e). The evaluation also covers psychosocial stabilization as a component of integration support. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the Eficacia, Conectividad, Cobertura, and Impacto sections for evidence on integration outcomes and the effectiveness of intercultural activities. The report appears to offer practical evidence on what works for migrant integration in a South American context.

References.Title: Evaluation ex-post interne du projet « Renforcement des capacités des autorités sanitaires pour promouvoir l’accès aux soins de santé des migrants et des communautés transfrontalières dans la région de Kédougou au Sénégal » [MA.0464]
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project explicitly targets vulnerable migrant populations — including women, children, sex workers, and cross-border communities — in precarious mining environments with limited health infrastructure. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of vulnerability factors (lack of WASH infrastructure, disease exposure, sexual and reproductive health risks, child trafficking) and responses to them. The evaluation addresses how the project identified and assisted migrants in situations of vulnerability (Actions 7b, 7c, 7k), with specific attention to gender-responsive approaches and the particular needs of women and children. The impact section notes significant outcomes including creation of a sex workers' association, reduction in child trafficking cases, and improved health-seeking behavior. The gender and human rights section (scoring 4/5) provides additional depth on vulnerability-sensitive programming. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the impact section and the gender/human rights assessment, as well as the lessons learned on community engagement (LA4, LA7), which appear most relevant for understanding vulnerability response in mobile mining contexts. It is possible the full report contains more detailed vulnerability analysis beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Evaluation ex-post interne du projet « Renforcement des capacités des autorités sanitaires pour promouvoir l’accès aux soins de santé des migrants et des communautés transfrontalières dans la région de Kédougou au Sénégal » [MA.0464]
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 15. Access to basic health services for migrants is the explicit and central focus of the entire project — as stated in the project title ('promouvoir l'accès aux soins de santé des migrants et des communautés transfrontalières'). The evaluation appears to directly assess the effectiveness of interventions designed to improve migrant access to healthcare (Action 15e), including capacity building of health authorities, community health surveillance, behavior change communication, and WASH interventions. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis across all six CAD criteria of how health service access was improved for a highly vulnerable migrant population (artisanal miners, women, children, sex workers) in a context of weak health infrastructure. Key themes directly relevant to Action 15e include: reducing communication barriers (multilingual/multicultural outreach), strengthening health system capacities, non-discriminatory access regardless of nationality or migration status, and culturally-sensitive service delivery. The impact section, efficacy section, and gender/human rights assessment would appear particularly valuable for synthesis specialists. The lessons learned (especially LA7 on proximity health services and financial barriers) and good practices (BP4-BP7) also offer actionable evidence. The full report likely contains additional depth on health access mechanisms beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN EX-POST DEL PROYECTO “ENFOQUE INTEGRAL PARA LOS FLUJOS MIGRATORIOS Y LA MEJORA DE LAS CONDICIONES DE LOS MIGRANTES EN MÉXICO”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 3. A central product of the evaluated project was the development of directories (Directorios) for migrants in transit through Mexico and for shelters (albergues), which directly relates to providing accessible information to migrants at stages of migration — aligning with Action 3c (information points along migration routes) and Action 3d (information for newly arrived migrants). The executive summary notes these directories 'continue to be used' and contain differentiated information for children, women, and LGBTI populations. Recommendations address dissemination strategies, digital accessibility, and updating mechanisms for these directories. The Guías de Grupo Beta are also mentioned as resources for migrants in transit. The sections on Output 1.1 recommendations and the sustainability section appear most relevant for human reviewers to prioritize. Border management information provision and the revista Ser Migrante as a knowledge-sharing platform also touch on information dissemination themes. Information provision appears to be one of the project's core activities, though the evaluation covers multiple themes simultaneously.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN EX-POST DEL PROYECTO “ENFOQUE INTEGRAL PARA LOS FLUJOS MIGRATORIOS Y LA MEJORA DE LAS CONDICIONES DE LOS MIGRANTES EN MÉXICO”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The project's stated general objective was to 'strengthen actions that favor the protection and social and community inclusion of irregular migrants in Mexico,' which directly aligns with addressing vulnerabilities. The context section explicitly identifies vulnerabilities including sexual and gender-based violence, labor exploitation of children, discrimination, and xenophobia. The directories include differentiated information for vulnerable subgroups (children, adolescents, women, LGBTI community), touching on Actions 7b and 7c. The training of public servants on migrant rights (Output 3.1) relates to Action 7k (involving local authorities in identification and assistance of vulnerable migrants). The gender section discusses limited but present attention to gender-responsive approaches. The report's conclusions and recommendations on capacity building for protection, shelter networks, and inter-agency coordination for vulnerable migrant populations appear particularly relevant. Human reviewers should prioritize the sections on Outputs 1.1, 3.1, the gender section, and the conclusions for evidence on vulnerability response mechanisms. Border enforcement context (Programa Frontera Sur) and its effects on migrant vulnerability is also discussed substantively.

References.Title: MID-TERM INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT: “APPUI AU RENFORCEMENT DE LA GESTION DE LA MIGRATION AU NIGER” (STRENGTHENING MIGRATION MANAGEMENT IN NIGER)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 3, particularly regarding awareness raising and information provision to migrants. The project's third objective — 'Migrants in transit in Niger are informed about the dangers of, and alternatives to, irregular migration' — directly aligns with GCM Action 3e (multilingual, gender-responsive information campaigns). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of awareness raising activities: Community Mobilisers conducted over 1,588 outings, sensitizing 45,727 migrants; theater activities reached 1,275 people; 270 posters were placed in key locations. The conclusions assess the quality and relevance of awareness raising materials, and multiple recommendations address coordination of information campaigns (Recommendations 17, 33). The report also touches on information provision at transit centers and pre-departure orientation. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the effectiveness section on Output 3 and the sustainability conclusions regarding awareness raising campaigns, as these appear to offer operational evidence on information dissemination to migrants in transit — relating to Actions 3c and 3e. The full report likely contains more detail on methodology and outcomes of these campaigns than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: MID-TERM INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT: “APPUI AU RENFORCEMENT DE LA GESTION DE LA MIGRATION AU NIGER” (STRENGTHENING MIGRATION MANAGEMENT IN NIGER)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to offer relevant evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 4, particularly regarding consular documentation for migrants in transit. The project's second objective explicitly addresses state authorities' capacity to manage returns, with a key component being the facilitation of travel documents. The excerpts indicate that 9,673 travel documents were issued during the project period, 8 field missions were organized to speed up return procedures, and consular services were supported for multiple countries. Recommendation 3 specifically addresses advocacy with Liberia and Guinea Conakry to speed up consular processes for travel documents. The sustainability section discusses the durability of consular service support. These elements relate directly to GCM Action 4c (adequate, timely, reliable consular documentation) and Action 4a (civil registry systems). Synthesis specialists would want to review the effectiveness section on Output 2 and the sustainability conclusions on consular services, as these appear to provide operational evidence on documentation challenges and solutions in a transit country context. The report's treatment of this theme appears substantive enough to warrant review for a synthesis on documentation and consular services.

References.Title: MID-TERM INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT: “APPUI AU RENFORCEMENT DE LA GESTION DE LA MIGRATION AU NIGER” (STRENGTHENING MIGRATION MANAGEMENT IN NIGER)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 14. The project's second objective explicitly focuses on enhancing state authorities' capacity to manage returns, with consular services as a central mechanism. The excerpts indicate: 9,673 travel documents were issued through DST and embassies/consulates; 8 field missions were organized to speed up return procedures involving multiple embassies (Ghana, Sudan, Liberia, Guinea); there was a 'reduced waiting time for receipt of travel documents'; and sustainability challenges around consular service support are analyzed. Recommendation 3 specifically addresses consular processes for Liberia and Guinea Conakry nationals. The sustainability section explicitly discusses the durability of 'Consular Services in the Countries of Origin of Migrants' component. These elements relate directly to GCM Actions 14a (building consular capacities), 14c (bilateral agreements on consular assistance), and 14d (identifying and assisting nationals in vulnerable situations). Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the effectiveness section on Output 2 and the sustainability conclusions on consular services, as these appear to provide operational evidence on consular cooperation challenges in a transit country context — including the diplomatic complexities of facilitating returns when countries lack diplomatic presence. The full report likely contains more detail on specific consular cooperation mechanisms than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: MID-TERM INTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT: “APPUI AU RENFORCEMENT DE LA GESTION DE LA MIGRATION AU NIGER” (STRENGTHENING MIGRATION MANAGEMENT IN NIGER)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The project's second objective explicitly focuses on enhancing state authorities' capacity to manage returns effectively, and return management appears as a central theme throughout the evaluation. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: 9,673 travel documents issued to facilitate returns; 8 field missions organized to speed up return procedures; consular cooperation with multiple countries of origin; challenges with countries lacking diplomatic presence (Liberia, Guinea Conakry); and the MRRM's voluntary return assistance component. The sustainability section explicitly analyzes the durability of return management support, noting the need for regional articulation with ECOWAS and the African Union. Multiple recommendations address return management (Recommendations 3, 7, 22). The report also appears to address the human rights-based approach to return, given the project's stated commitment to 'concerted and human rights-based management of migration flows.' These elements relate directly to GCM Actions 21a (bilateral cooperation frameworks for return), 21b (voluntary return programmes), 21c (identification of nationals and travel documents), and 21d (consular assistance for returning migrants). Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the effectiveness section on Output 2, the sustainability conclusions on consular services and return management, and the recommendations on diplomatic engagement — as these appear to provide operational evidence on the practical challenges of facilitating dignified return in a transit country context, including the specific challenge of countries without diplomatic presence. The full report likely contains considerably more detail on return management mechanisms and outcomes than the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: End of Project Evaluation of the Project ‘Enhancing Gambian Authorities’ Preparedness and Response to COVID-19'
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 11. Border management in an integrated and coordinated manner appears to be one of the two central themes of the evaluated project. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: inter-agency border coordination mechanisms (Outcome 1, Output 1.1), establishment of Inter-Agency Border Coordination Committees (IABCCs) at multiple land border posts, cross-border information sharing with Senegalese authorities, border surveillance and health screening at Points of Entry (Outcome 3, Output 3.1), and provision of border equipment for surveillance. These elements relate closely to Actions 11a (international border management cooperation, capacity-building), 11b (integrated border management structures and procedures), and 11g (cross-border collaboration on treatment of persons crossing borders). The evaluation's assessment of IABCC effectiveness, challenges with porous borders, equipment adequacy, and sustainability of coordination mechanisms would likely add value to a synthesis on integrated border management. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the sections on Outcome 1 (coordination and partnership at POEs), Output 1.1 (inter-agency coordination meetings), Outcome 3 (surveillance and preparedness), and the sustainability section (2.7.1) on institutionalization of coordination mechanisms. The health-security nexus dimension of border management in a pandemic context may also be of interest. It should be noted that border management here is framed primarily through a health/pandemic lens rather than a purely migration governance lens, which may limit direct applicability to some Objective 11 synthesis questions.

References.Title: End of Project Evaluation of the Project ‘Enhancing Gambian Authorities’ Preparedness and Response to COVID-19'
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute some evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly regarding health services and WASH access for migrants and border communities. The project explicitly addressed access to water and sanitation services (Outcome 4, Output 4.1) through construction of solar-powered boreholes and handwashing stations in border communities, and provided hygiene kits to households. The health dimension — including COVID-19 health screening, infection prevention and control measures, and health worker engagement — also relates to Action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in health care policies and plans). The project's explicit targeting of migrants, mobile populations, and border communities for service provision is directly relevant to the Objective 15 framework of ensuring migrants can access basic services regardless of status. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the sections on Outcome 4 and Output 4.1 (WASH provision), as well as the RCCE sections (Outcomes 2-3) for evidence on health service access in border migration contexts. The evaluation's assessment of adequacy and sustainability of WASH provision, and the challenges noted (inadequate water supply, erratic electricity), may offer useful evidence on implementation gaps. It should be noted that the service provision here is framed primarily as an infection prevention measure rather than a rights-based access framework, which may limit direct applicability to some Objective 15 synthesis questions.

References.Title: CONNECTING DIASPORA FOR DEVELOPMENT (CD4D) FINAL REPORT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The CD4D programme is explicitly designed to harness diaspora contributions to sustainable development in countries of origin through knowledge transfer and capacity building — directly addressing the core theme of this objective. The report appears to provide substantive analysis across multiple associated actions: Action 19c (research on non-financial contributions of migrants and diasporas, including knowledge and skills transfer) is directly addressed through the evaluation's focus on knowledge transfer outcomes; Action 19i (enabling diaspora to carry out professional activities and engage in knowledge transfer in home countries) is the operational core of the CD4D programme; Action 19j (partnerships for knowledge and skills transfer between countries of origin and destination) is addressed through the programme's institutional linkages. The report title, evaluation questions, and all major findings sections are oriented around diaspora-led knowledge transfer and capacity development. Sections 5.2 (knowledge transfer methods), 5.3 (contributions and changes through CD4D), 5.4 (enablers and barriers), and 5.5 (connections established) would all appear highly relevant for a synthesis specialist. The 15 recommendations also provide actionable evidence on programme design for diaspora engagement. The report also covers multiple country contexts (Afghanistan, Ethiopia, Iraq, Sierra Leone, Somalia), adding comparative value. It is possible the full report contains additional evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal, particularly on longer-term development impacts.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN EX POST DEL PROYECTO “FORTALECIMIENTO DE CAPACIDADES DEL SECTOR EDUCATIVO A TRAVÉS DEL ARTE PARA PROMOVER ESCUELAS LIBRES DE XENOFOBIA EN EL PERÚ”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16, particularly Action 16i on promoting welcoming and safe school environments for migrant children, incorporating migration content in education curricula, and preventing discrimination, racism, xenophobia and intolerance. The project's explicit purpose — strengthening the education sector's capacity to promote schools free of xenophobia and foster sociocultural integration of migrant students — maps directly onto this action. The evaluation appears to assess the effectiveness of a gamified pedagogical tool ('Guardianes de TX-369') and teacher training workshops designed to build inclusive school environments, which is substantively relevant to Action 16i. The excerpts also touch on Action 16a (promoting mutual respect and social cohesion) and Action 16h (multicultural activities).
