The conclusions and recommendations sections discuss sustainability of integration approaches, articulation with national curricula, and the role of local education authorities (UGELs/DREs) — all relevant to understanding what works in school-based social cohesion programming for migrants. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the findings on teacher capacity-building effectiveness, the sustainability analysis, and the theory of change discussion, as these appear to offer substantive evidence on school-based inclusion interventions. The evaluation's mixed-methods approach and its honest assessment of limited impact and sustainability challenges would add nuance to a synthesis on this objective.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN EX POST DEL PROYECTO “FORTALECIMIENTO DE CAPACIDADES DEL SECTOR EDUCATIVO A TRAVÉS DEL ARTE PARA PROMOVER ESCUELAS LIBRES DE XENOFOBIA EN EL PERÚ”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute relevant evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 17, particularly Actions 17f (awareness-raising campaigns on positive contributions of migration) and 17g (engaging educators to detect and prevent intolerance, racism and xenophobia). The project is explicitly designed to combat xenophobia and racial discrimination in school settings, and the evaluation assesses the effectiveness of this anti-discrimination intervention. The executive summary notes the project aimed to work on 'discrimination, xenophobia and racism' themes, and the theory of change targets attitude change among educational actors. The evaluation's conclusions on the limited short-term impact and sustainability challenges of the intervention, as well as the recommendations on integrating anti-xenophobia content into national curricula, would be directly relevant to a synthesis on evidence-based approaches to countering xenophobia. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the impact and sustainability sections of the findings, as well as the conclusions (C1-C8) and recommendations, which appear to offer substantive — if sobering — evidence on the effectiveness of school-based anti-xenophobia programming. The report's honest assessment of low recall and limited behavioral change among teachers adds important nuance. Note that Objectives 16 and 17 are both highly relevant; this reflects the dual focus of the project on inclusion/cohesion and anti-discrimination, which are genuinely distinct GCM themes even if related.

References.Title: TECHNICAL SUPPORT TO GLOBAL CONSULTATION ON MIGRANT HEALTH – SRI LANKA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly Action 15e on incorporating the health needs of migrants into national and local health care policies and plans. The project's entire focus is on mainstreaming migrant health into policy — a core concern of this objective. The excerpts suggest the project explicitly aimed to identify 'gaps, mainstreaming opportunities, new challenges and priority areas for key policy strategies related to migration and health,' which aligns directly with the spirit of Action 15e. The 2nd GCMH brought together 132 stakeholders from governments, academic institutions, NGOs, and international organizations to build consensus on migrant health policy strategies, and the resulting Colombo Statement represents a concrete policy output. The background papers produced by the project appear to have provided evidence on health system capacity, access barriers, and policy frameworks. The report also notes that migrants face barriers including lack of healthcare access, discrimination, stigmatization, and inadequate health system capacity — all themes under Objective 15. Recommendations on sustainability, follow-up mechanisms, and cascading findings to national and regional levels are also relevant to how health service inclusion for migrants can be institutionalized. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the findings sections on the Scientific Committee's outputs, the Colombo Statement, and the recommendations on sustainability and national-level dissemination. It should be noted that the excerpts accessed are limited and the full report may contain additional relevant evidence on health service access and policy mainstreaming.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE MIGRANT OUTREACH AND MESSAGING IN INDONESIA (MOMI) PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 3. The MOMI project's core purpose is explicitly to provide accurate, timely, and accessible information to irregular migrants at a key transit stage — directly aligning with this objective's theme. The project title itself ('Migrant Outreach and Messaging in Indonesia') signals this centrality. The excerpts indicate that the evaluation assesses: multilingual information dissemination to over 7,000 migrants (action 3c, 3d, 3e); awareness-raising campaigns on dangers of irregular pathways and Australian government policies (action 3e); the use of native-speaking Community Liaison Officers to deliver information in migrants' mother tongues (action 3c, 3d); and the effectiveness of information provision, with ~80% of respondents satisfied with frequency. The conclusions and recommendations directly address information delivery mechanisms, content, and reach. Actions 3c (information points along migration routes), 3d (targeted information for newly arrived migrants), and 3e (multilingual awareness-raising campaigns) appear most directly addressed. Human reviewers should prioritize the effectiveness section, the conclusions on multilingual officer performance, and the survey results on migrant awareness levels (Annexes 3 and 4).

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE MIGRANT OUTREACH AND MESSAGING IN INDONESIA (MOMI) PROJECT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 9. The MOMI project is explicitly framed as part of efforts to 'combat people smuggling, human trafficking and related transnational crime' under the Bali Process. The evaluation assesses: awareness-raising campaigns that produced 'a significant hardening in attitudes against people smuggling ventures' (action 9f); community engagement with key opinion formers that 'doubled the rate of interceptions of IMs since 2010' (action 9f); behavioral change among migrants and communities regarding people smuggling; and coordination between IOM, Indonesian National Police, and immigration authorities. The project's focus on dissuading migrants from using smuggling networks and engaging coastal communities aligns with action 9f (preventing smuggling through public information and community partnerships). The conclusions on community attitude change and interception rates appear particularly relevant. Human reviewers should prioritize the impact section (Section 3.1 conclusions on impact) and the discussion of community engagement outcomes. Note that the project's approach is primarily preventive/informational rather than law enforcement, so actions 9b-9d on intelligence sharing and prosecution are less directly addressed.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE EU-COMMUNITY STABILISATION PROGRAMME IN KOSOVO
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. The CSP programme appears explicitly focused on social inclusion and cohesion of minority communities in Kosovo, including returnees and IDPs, through economic empowerment and community-level interventions. The report discusses interethnic community projects, promotion of cultural preservation, women's economic empowerment, and the creation of shared economic spaces across ethnic groups — all closely aligned with Actions 16a (mutual respect and social cohesion), 16d (labour market integration), 16e (empowering migrant women), 16f (community centres and intercultural dialogue), and 16h (multicultural activities). The executive summary section on 'Promotion of human rights and Gender equality' and the impact section describing interethnic community projects would appear particularly relevant for human reviewers. The programme's model of inclusive employment for isolated minority communities, and its documented outcomes on social cohesion and women's empowerment, would likely add value to a synthesis on social inclusion. Specialists should note that the 'migrants' in this context are primarily internal minority community members and returnees rather than international migrants, which may affect applicability depending on the synthesis scope.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF THE EU-COMMUNITY STABILISATION PROGRAMME IN KOSOVO
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly its reintegration dimension. The CSP programme appears explicitly designed to support sustainable return and reintegration of IDPs and minority community members in Kosovo, and the evaluation directly assesses the programme's contribution to this goal. The report discusses: (1) the programme's role in facilitating voluntary return and sustainable reintegration (executive summary section on 'CSP contribution to return and reintegration'); (2) the effectiveness of livelihood support (individual business grants) for returnees achieving 100% success rates; (3) coordination with the EU-Return and Reintegration Kosovo (RRK) programme; (4) the sustainability of return outcomes; and (5) recommendations for promoting the CSP model at regional venues for return and reintegration. These directly address Actions 21b (gender-responsive return and reintegration programmes), 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through employment and decent work), and 21i (addressing needs of communities to which migrants return). The sections most relevant for human reviewers to prioritize include: the executive summary sections on 'CSP contribution to return and reintegration' and 'Sustainability,' Conclusions 11 and 4 on sustainable return and CSP modelling, and the impact section. The full report likely contains additional evidence on reintegration outcomes beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Evaluation of IOM role in the establishment and  operationalisation of UN regional and country  networks on migration within the framework of the  UN Network on Migration
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The evaluation dedicates a substantive section (Q7) to IOM's effectiveness in collecting and analysing migration data to inform the work of regional and country networks. The excerpts suggest coverage of IOM's data initiatives including the Displacement Tracking Matrix (DTM), Migration Data Portal, Missing Migrants Project, Migration Governance Indicators, and Migration Profiles — touching on Actions 1c, 1e, and 1j. The report also discusses challenges in data consolidation among UN agencies, data standards, and risks of politicization of data. There is mention of the UNNM Workplan 2019-2020 including a global programme to build national capacities in data collection (directly related to Action 1c), co-led by UNDESA, IOM and ILO. However, data is one of several themes covered and does not appear to be the central focus of the evaluation. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review Section 3.3 (Q7) specifically, as well as references to the Migration Network Hub as a data-sharing platform. The excerpts suggest this section contains substantive analysis rather than passing mentions, though the full depth of treatment cannot be confirmed from excerpts alone.

References.Title: Evaluation of IOM role in the establishment and  operationalisation of UN regional and country  networks on migration within the framework of the  UN Network on Migration
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 23. Unlike the common false positive risk where cooperation language merely describes operational context, this evaluation explicitly examines international cooperation and multilateral governance mechanisms as its primary object of inquiry. The evaluation's stated purpose is to assess IOM's role in establishing and operationalising the UN Network on Migration — a multilateral governance architecture created specifically to implement the GCM through international cooperation. The report title, evaluation questions, findings, conclusions, and all six recommendations directly address how international cooperation mechanisms function, their effectiveness, sustainability, and governance. This appears to be one of the few evaluation reports where Objective 23 is genuinely the central subject rather than ambient context. Actions 23a (supporting collective GCM implementation through whole-of-government and whole-of-society approaches), 23d (use of capacity-building mechanisms), and 23e (bilateral, regional and multilateral partnerships for migration policy) appear particularly well-covered. For synthesis specialists, the sections on coherence (Q5-Q6), effectiveness of coordination (Q9), sustainability (Q18), and all six recommendations would appear most valuable as starting points. The analysis of the MMPTF as a multilateral funding instrument, the Champion Countries Initiative, and the Migration Network Hub as international cooperation tools would also likely contribute meaningfully to a synthesis on Objective 23. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: STRENGTHENING THE ROLE OF CIVIL SOCIETY IN ELIMINATING HUMAN TRAFFICKING IN ZIMBABWE (2010 - 2012)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The entire evaluation is explicitly focused on a two-year IOM project to eliminate human trafficking in Zimbabwe — making trafficking prevention and response the central subject of analysis. The excerpts suggest substantive coverage of multiple associated actions: Action 10a (Palermo Protocol ratification is a key recommendation); Action 10c (monitoring trafficking routes and strengthening cooperation); Action 10e (measures addressing vulnerabilities of trafficking victims, access to justice); Action 10g (strengthening legislation and prosecution); Action 10h (protection and assistance to trafficking victims, including physical, psychological and social recovery); Action 10i (training programmes for law enforcement, border officials, magistrates, public prosecutors); and Action 10j (awareness-raising campaigns on trafficking risks). The report appears to provide evidence on policy development processes, referral network establishment, capacity building of government and CSOs, community awareness outcomes, and victim reintegration assistance. For a synthesis specialist, the most valuable sections would likely include: Section 4.2.1 (policy and legal framework development), Section 4.2.2 (national anti-trafficking network and referral system), Section 4.2.3 (capacity development), Section 4.2.4 (community awareness), Section 4.2.5 (care and support for VoT), and the conclusions and recommendations. The evaluation's multi-level approach (policy, intermediary, community) and its assessment of what worked and what didn't in a specific country context would likely provide valuable evidence for synthesis work on this objective.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN EX-POST DEL PROYECTO “ESTABLECIMIENTO DE CIUDADES HERMANAS EN MEXICO PARA LA INTEGRACION DE PERSONAS MIGRANTES”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 16. The evaluation's primary subject — the 'Establecimiento de Ciudades Hermanas para la integración de personas migrantes' — is explicitly and centrally focused on social inclusion, cohesion, and the integration of migrants into receiving communities, which are the core themes of GCM Objective 16. The project title itself signals direct alignment, and the evaluation appears to assess multiple dimensions of integration: labor, social, cultural, economic, and academic inclusion. Several associated actions appear well-covered: Action 16a (mutual respect, integration policies, social cohesion) is addressed through the sister-city mechanism and intercultural activities; Action 16f (community centres and intercultural dialogue) maps closely onto the Comité de Ciudades Hermanas and Mesa Intersectorial; Action 16h (multicultural activities through arts, culinary festivals) corresponds directly to activities like 'Sabores de Amistad' and 'Muros de esperanza'; and Action 16e (empowering migrant women) is discussed critically in the gender sections. The evaluation's conclusions on institutional narrative change, collective action for integration, and the gap between gender-sensitive design and gender-neutral implementation would likely add substantive evidence to a synthesis on this objective. For human reviewers, the sections most worth prioritizing appear to be: the executive summary findings on impact and transversal themes (paragraphs 13-15), conclusions 2, 9, 12-14, and recommendations 16 and 18, as well as the lessons learned and good practices sections. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN EX-POST DEL PROYECTO “ESTABLECIMIENTO DE CIUDADES HERMANAS EN MEXICO PARA LA INTEGRACION DE PERSONAS MIGRANTES”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 17. The evaluation explicitly discusses the change in institutional narrative toward migration in Tapachula as a significant impact — described as a shift in how public institutions frame migration and design policies. This aligns with Actions 17f (awareness-raising campaigns on positive contributions of migration) and 17g (engaging community leaders to promote mutual respect). The project's activities — including 'Muros de esperanza,' 'Cartas por la paz,' and 'Embajadoras y embajadores de la Hermandad' — appear designed partly to counter xenophobia and promote positive perceptions of migrants. The evaluation also references xenophobia as a barrier to integration in the contextual sections. However, discrimination and public discourse are not the sole focus; they appear as one dimension of a broader integration initiative. The sections most worth reviewing for a synthesis on GCM Objective 17 would appear to be the impact criterion findings (paragraph 13 in the executive summary), conclusion 14 on narrative change, and the descriptions of Hermanamiento activities. Specialists should note that the analytical depth on anti-discrimination mechanisms specifically is moderate rather than comprehensive based on the excerpts reviewed.

References.Title: EVALUATING IOM'S RETURN AND REINTEGRATION ACTIVITIES FOR RETURNEES AND OTHER DISPLACED POPULATIONS - AFGHANISTAN
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The evaluation appears to place significant emphasis on identifying and responding to vulnerable sub-groups within returnee and IDP populations — including unaccompanied minors (UAMs), female-headed households, drug-affected households, and IDPs — which aligns closely with Actions 7b, 7c, 7e, and 7f. The executive summary and conclusions contain substantive analysis of how vulnerability criteria are operationalized (or fail to be), how specific groups fall through gaps in assistance, and how tailored interventions could better address their needs. The recommendation to develop stand-alone programmes for UAMs, displaced women, and drug-affected households reflects direct engagement with vulnerability-responsive programming. Sections on beneficiary selection mechanisms, the treatment of unaccompanied minors, and the discussion of female-headed households would appear particularly valuable for human reviewers conducting a synthesis on this objective. The excerpts suggest this is one of the report's stronger thematic threads, though it is embedded within a broader programme evaluation rather than being the sole focus.

References.Title: EVALUATING IOM'S RETURN AND REINTEGRATION ACTIVITIES FOR RETURNEES AND OTHER DISPLACED POPULATIONS - AFGHANISTAN
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. Return and reintegration is the explicit and central subject of this evaluation — the report title, evaluation questions, and all major analytical sections directly address safe return and sustainable reintegration of Afghan returnees and IDPs. The evaluation appears to provide substantive evidence on multiple associated actions: Action 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes, voluntary return, reintegration support), Action 21h (sustainable reintegration through social protection, vocational training, employment, and financial services), and Action 21i (addressing needs of communities to which migrants return). The report's analysis of post-arrival assistance, livelihood programming, and shelter assistance — and their effectiveness in supporting reintegration — constitutes core evidence on what works and what does not in return and reintegration programming. Key analytical contributions likely include: the tension between short-term return assistance and long-term reintegration sustainability; the role of vulnerable sub-group targeting (UAMs, female-headed households, drug-affected households, IDPs); the importance of continuum between return and reintegration phases; and the challenges of coordination among actors. The executive summary, conclusions (Chapter 7), and the three-step action plan would appear particularly valuable starting points for human reviewers. The full report likely contains chapter-level findings on each programme component (Chapters 3-5) that would provide granular evidence for a synthesis. This report appears to be among the most directly relevant possible sources for GCM Objective 21.

References.Title: FINAL EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT : “MOVING TOWARDS SUSTAINABLE APPROACHES TO PREVENT VIOLENT EXTREMISM IN THE WESTERN BALKANS”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 16, particularly around social cohesion, community inclusion, and empowerment. The project's core objectives — improving social cohesion, building community resilience, empowering youth through critical thinking and civic engagement — align closely with Actions 16a (promoting mutual respect and social cohesion), 16f (community centres and intercultural dialogue), and 16g (peer-to-peer training and civic integration). The report appears to provide substantive analysis of what works in building inclusive communities, including the role of participatory initiatives, community dialogues, and youth-led initiatives. Key sections worth reviewing for a synthesis specialist include the effectiveness and impact sections (sections 5.x on outcomes), the good practices section (5.8), and the conclusions on civic engagement and community cohesion outcomes. The caveat is that the target population is not migrants per se but Western Balkan youth vulnerable to extremism — the social cohesion evidence is generated in a non-migration-specific context. However, the community empowerment and social cohesion methodologies and outcomes evidence may still be valuable for a synthesis on Objective 16, particularly regarding what approaches foster inclusion and reduce polarization.

References.Title: FINAL EXTERNAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT : “MOVING TOWARDS SUSTAINABLE APPROACHES TO PREVENT VIOLENT EXTREMISM IN THE WESTERN BALKANS”
References.Abstract: The excerpts suggest this report would likely contribute some evidence relevant to GCM Objective 17, particularly around countering divisive narratives, promoting evidence-based discourse, and reducing polarization. The project explicitly addresses far-right nationalist rhetoric, divisive narratives promoted by politicians and media, and ethnic/religious extremism — themes closely aligned with Actions 17c (promoting independent, objective reporting and sensitizing media professionals), 17f (awareness-raising campaigns on positive contributions), and 17g (engaging community leaders to detect and prevent intolerance). The Mediality (media literacy) workshops, social media campaigns, and the project's communication strategy around countering polarized narratives are particularly relevant. The good practices section (5.8.2) discusses media campaign approaches and the strategy of fighting extremism 'under the cape of polarized and divisive narratives.' The cross-cutting section on Human Rights also touches on inclusiveness and open-mindedness. A synthesis specialist should note that the focus is on violent extremism prevention rather than migration-specific discrimination, but the evidence on countering divisive narratives and promoting constructive public discourse is substantively relevant to Objective 17's themes.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “STRENGTHENING INSTITUTIONAL CAPACITY TO COORDINATE NATIONAL ANTI-TRAFFICKING RESPONSE, INVESTIGATION AND PROSECUTION OF TRAFFICKING OFFENDERS IN MALI ”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The entire project evaluated is explicitly dedicated to combating trafficking in persons in Mali — this is the primary and exclusive subject of the evaluation. The project title, objectives, activities, findings, conclusions, and recommendations all directly address core themes of GCM Objective 10. Specifically, the report appears to provide substantive evidence on: (a) strengthening legislation and prosecution capacity (Actions 10g, 10i) through training of judges, prosecutors, and law enforcement agents; (b) identification and protection of trafficking victims (Actions 10e, 10h) through shelter support, psychosocial assistance, and direct victim assistance; (c) national referral mechanisms and SOPs for victim protection (Actions 10e, 10h); (d) institutional coordination through the National Committee Against TIP/NCTIP (Actions 10b, 10d); (e) MoU development between law enforcement and NGOs/CSOs for victim transfer (Action 10e); and (f) capacity building for identification of trafficking signs (Action 10i). For a synthesis specialist, the sections on effectiveness (training outcomes, NRM/SOP development, shelter capacity), sustainability (institutionalization of anti-trafficking mechanisms), and recommendations (long-term vision for GoM engagement) would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The report also provides evidence on implementation challenges in building anti-trafficking institutional capacity in a fragile state context, which would add methodological value to a synthesis. It is likely that the full report contains additional depth beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: FINAL EVALUATION OF THE PROJECT “STRENGTHENING INSTITUTIONAL CAPACITY TO COORDINATE NATIONAL ANTI-TRAFFICKING RESPONSE, INVESTIGATION AND PROSECUTION OF TRAFFICKING OFFENDERS IN MALI ”
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12, particularly regarding Action 12b (specialized human rights training for first responders and government officials) and Action 12c (gender-responsive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures). The project explicitly developed SOPs for a National Referral Mechanism (NRM) for trafficking victims, conducted Training of Trainers for judges and prosecutors, and built capacity among law enforcement agents — all directly relevant to Actions 12b and 12c. The report's analysis of the training component (ToT methodology, content development, challenges in reaching LEAs) and the NRM/SOP development process would likely provide useful evidence for a synthesis on screening and referral systems. However, the referral mechanism focus is specifically on trafficking victims rather than broader migration screening, which somewhat limits its scope relative to GCM Objective 12's full range. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the effectiveness sections on training outcomes and the NRM/SOP development, as well as the sustainability recommendations on institutionalizing these mechanisms. The excerpts suggest this is a secondary rather than primary theme relative to GCM Objective 10.

References.Title: STRENGTHENING RESPONSES TO CHILD TRAFFICKING AND MODERN SLAVERY: A PILOT PROJECT TO SUPPORT FOSTER CARERS LOOKING AFTER ALBANIAN AND VIETNAMESE UNACCOMPANIED CHILDREN
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7, particularly around Actions 7e, 7f, and 7b. The report explicitly examines a pilot programme designed to reduce vulnerability to exploitation for unaccompanied asylum-seeking children and trafficked children — a core concern of Objective 7. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: child protection systems and foster care as a mechanism for protecting unaccompanied and separated children (Action 7f); the role of specialized support in addressing vulnerabilities of migrant children (Action 7b); best interests of the child considerations including age assessment and legal guardianship (Action 7e); and the importance of cultural awareness, empathy, and trust-building in reducing vulnerability and missing episodes. The report also touches on mental health support, access to services, and safeguarding practices. Key sections for human reviewers to prioritize would include: 'Outcome 1' and its qualitative analysis (pages 13-19), 'Case Study 2' on trafficking identification (page 23-24), the 'Foster Carer Forums - Summary' and 'Foster Carer Training - Summary' (pages 19, 25), and the 'Conclusions' (page 35). The report appears to be one of several themes covered (it also addresses trafficking prevention specifically), but vulnerability reduction for unaccompanied children appears to be a central organizing concern throughout. Specialists synthesizing evidence on child-sensitive protection mechanisms and unaccompanied child care arrangements would likely find this report valuable.

References.Title: STRENGTHENING RESPONSES TO CHILD TRAFFICKING AND MODERN SLAVERY: A PILOT PROJECT TO SUPPORT FOSTER CARERS LOOKING AFTER ALBANIAN AND VIETNAMESE UNACCOMPANIED CHILDREN
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 10, particularly around Actions 10e, 10i, and 10h. The report explicitly evaluates a pilot programme — the Child Trafficking Protection Fund project — designed to reduce vulnerability to trafficking and modern slavery for unaccompanied asylum-seeking children, with child trafficking prevention as its explicit primary subject. The excerpts suggest: substantive analysis of capacity-building for identifying trafficking indicators and protecting potential victims (Action 10i — training programmes to identify signs of trafficking); evidence on how increased knowledge and confidence among foster carers led to improved identification of trafficking victims and NRM referrals (Action 10e — measures addressing vulnerabilities of those at risk of trafficking); discussion of the National Referral Mechanism as a protection pathway (Action 10h — protection and assistance for trafficking victims); and cultural and country-specific knowledge development for Albanian and Vietnamese children identified as high-risk nationalities. Case Study 2 (page 23-24) appears particularly valuable, illustrating how training led to a trafficking identification and NRM referral. The 'Foster Carer Training - Summary' (page 25), 'Overall findings' (page 26), and 'Conclusions' (page 35) would be priority sections for human reviewers. The report likely offers evidence on what works in community-level trafficking identification and victim support, which is relatively rare in the evaluation literature. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: REAL TIME EVALUATION OF IOM'S RESPONSE TO THE ROHINGYA CRISIS
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The Needs and Population Monitoring (NPM) component appears to be a primary focus of the evaluation, with dedicated sections analyzing data collection methodology, coverage, disaggregation practices, and use of data for coordination. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of how NPM data is gathered, interpreted, and shared — touching on Actions 1a (comprehensive data strategy), 1c (building data collection capacities), and 1j (country-specific profiles). The report appears to critically examine the separation between data gathering and interpretation, the limitations of relying on male majhis as key informants, the need for sex- and age-disaggregated data, and information management problems in the collective response. Specific sections worth prioritizing for human review include the NPM conclusions (section b in the summary) and the recommendations R1-R4 on NPM, as well as the 'Social identity-sensitive programming' subsection in section VI. The report also notes that data disaggregated by sex and age does not automatically translate into gender-sensitive programming — a nuanced observation relevant to Action 1d. The excerpts suggest this is one of the report's dominant themes, though it is framed within a humanitarian emergency context rather than a national migration statistics framework.

References.Title: REAL TIME EVALUATION OF IOM'S RESPONSE TO THE ROHINGYA CRISIS
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. Protection of vulnerable populations — particularly Rohingya refugees experiencing GBV, trafficking, and other protection threats — appears to be a primary focus of the evaluation. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of unmet protection needs, GBV case management, counter-trafficking efforts, and the failure to integrate protection into basic service sectors — relating to Actions 7a (reviewing policies that may exacerbate vulnerabilities), 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies addressing GBV), and 7k (involving local authorities in identification and referral). The report also examines the particular vulnerabilities of women, children, and ethnic minorities, and critiques the inadequate attention to gender and social identity in programming. Sections worth prioritizing for human review include the Protection conclusions (section c in the summary), the 'Participation' and 'Social identity-sensitive programming' subsections in section VI, and the protection recommendations R1-R4. The humanitarian emergency context is specific, but the analysis of protection gaps and integration failures may offer transferable insights for synthesis on vulnerability reduction.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation: Setting Standards on protection and assistance to vulnerable migrants in support of the Global Action to Prevent and Address Trafficking in Persons and the Smuggling of Migrants
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 7. The entire project is explicitly centered on developing a comprehensive framework — the Determinants of Migrant Vulnerability (DoMV) model and accompanying Handbook — for identifying, assessing, and responding to migrant vulnerability. This directly aligns with the core theme of Objective 7 and several of its associated actions. The project title itself references 'protection and assistance to vulnerable migrants,' and the Handbook addresses vulnerability at individual, family, community, and structural levels — a multi-dimensional approach highly relevant to Actions 7a (reviewing policies to avoid creating vulnerabilities), 7b (comprehensive policies for migrants in vulnerable situations), 7c (gender-responsive migration policies), and 7l (developing national policies based on GMG Principles on human rights protection of migrants in vulnerable situations). The excerpts suggest the Handbook explicitly covers women at risk, children, victims of trafficking and smuggling, and other vulnerable groups — all named in Action 7b. The evaluation's conclusions on the Handbook's potential to 'change the way governments identify vulnerability in the migrant population and provide more effective operational and programmatic responses' are directly pertinent to a synthesis on this objective. For human reviewers, the executive summary's discussion of the DoMV model's scope, the conclusions on potential impact, and the recommendations on institutionalization and training would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The full report likely contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation: Setting Standards on protection and assistance to vulnerable migrants in support of the Global Action to Prevent and Address Trafficking in Persons and the Smuggling of Migrants
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 10. The project is explicitly part of GLO.ACT — the Global Action to Prevent and Address Trafficking in Human Beings and the Smuggling of Migrants — and the Handbook directly addresses protection and assistance for victims of trafficking. The project title references trafficking in persons, and the evaluation discusses the Handbook's potential to improve how governments identify vulnerability and provide protection responses, which relates to Actions 10e (addressing vulnerabilities of those at risk of trafficking), 10g (avoiding criminalization of victims), and 10h (providing protection and assistance to victims). The excerpts indicate the Handbook covers identification, assistance, and protection of migrants vulnerable to violence, exploitation, and abuse — core themes under Objective 10. The evaluation's discussion of the Handbook's potential to strengthen IOM's position as an international actor and its use in GLO.ACT Phase 2 is also relevant. For human reviewers, the executive summary's description of the Handbook's scope, the conclusions on potential impact on government practices, and the recommendations on training and institutionalization would appear most pertinent. The report appears to contribute evidence primarily on the protection and assistance side of anti-trafficking responses rather than on prosecution or law enforcement dimensions.

References.Title: Ex-post Evaluation: Setting Standards on protection and assistance to vulnerable migrants in support of the Global Action to Prevent and Address Trafficking in Persons and the Smuggling of Migrants
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute some evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 12. The DoMV model and Handbook appear to be directly relevant to screening, assessment, and referral of migrants in vulnerable situations — core themes under Objective 12. The Handbook is described as providing operational guidance for identifying vulnerability, which relates to Action 12b (specialized training for first responders and government officials on identification and referral of vulnerable migrants) and Action 12c (gender-responsive and child-sensitive referral mechanisms with standardized operating procedures). The evaluation notes the Handbook's potential to change how governments identify vulnerability and provide protection responses, suggesting relevance to standardized assessment approaches. However, the report focuses on the development of the Handbook as a tool rather than on migration procedure systems more broadly. For human reviewers, the sections on the Handbook's content and scope, and the recommendations on training and institutionalization, would appear most relevant for a synthesis on Objective 12, though border management and formal migration procedure contexts are not the primary frame of the evaluation.

References.Title: INTER-AGENCY HUMANITARIAN EVALUATION OF THE RESPONSE TO THE HUMANITARIAN CRISIS IN SOMALIA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly regarding the interplay between humanitarian response, displacement dynamics, and resilience. The report appears to engage substantively with several themes closely aligned with this objective. The excerpts discuss how the 2021-2023 drought drove displacement and acute food insecurity in Somalia, how humanitarian assistance both prevented and shaped displacement patterns (serving as a 'pull factor' to informal IDP camps), and how the response contributed to the erosion of resilience of affected communities. These themes connect directly to Actions 2b (investing in resilience and disaster risk reduction), 2f (strengthening collaboration between humanitarian and development actors), 2h (joint analysis of migration movements resulting from disasters), 2i (adaptation and resilience strategies), and 2j (integrating displacement considerations into disaster preparedness). The nexus section (paragraph 25) explicitly discusses the limitations of development interventions in addressing structural drivers of emergencies and the erosion of resilience. Recommendation 1 directly addresses how humanitarian aid can create negative incentives and undermine longer-term development — highly relevant to the spirit of Objective 2. The Somalia context — with over 30 years of conflict, recurrent climate shocks, and famine risk — makes this a rich case for understanding structural drivers of displacement. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the sections on unintended effects (paragraphs 9, 11), the nexus discussion (paragraph 25), and Recommendation 1, which appear most directly relevant to Actions 2f, 2i, and 2j. The report's focus is humanitarian rather than development, which limits its depth on some structural drivers, but the coverage of displacement dynamics and resilience erosion is substantive.

References.Title: TERMINAL EVALUATION REPORT ON THE 'SARDD' PROJECT IMPLEMENTED BY THE INTERNATIONAL ORGANIZATION FOR MIGRATION (IOM)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly around actions related to disaster preparedness, resilience, and addressing structural drivers of displacement. The SARDD project explicitly targets IDPs displaced by conflict and resource scarcity in Ethiopia, and the project rationale section describes the structural factors (violent conflicts over water and pasture, loss of livestock, food insecurity) that compelled displacement. The project's livelihood restoration components — restocking, IGA schemes, home gardening — directly relate to Action 2b (investing in programmes to address adverse drivers including food security, employment creation, and resilience) and Action 2i (developing resilience strategies). The conclusion's recommendation for 'lasting solutions to root causes' and continued livelihood support also aligns with Action 2f (strengthening humanitarian-development collaboration). However, the report focuses on post-displacement assistance rather than prevention of displacement per se, which limits its direct relevance to the 'minimize drivers' framing. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review the background/rationale section (1.1) on conflict drivers and the findings on livelihood restoration outcomes (section 2.3.3, referenced but not fully excerpted), as well as the conclusions and recommendations sections.

References.Title: A THEMATIC EVALUATION OF THE DISPLACEMENT TRACKING MATRIX (DTM)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The DTM (Displacement Tracking Matrix) is explicitly an information management system designed to collect, analyze, and disseminate data on displaced and mobile populations — which is the core subject of this evaluation. The report appears to directly address data collection methodologies, harmonization of tools, data quality, dissemination of information products, and the use of data for evidence-based programming. The executive summary discusses DTM's role as 'the largest source of primary data on internal displacement,' and multiple recommendations address harmonizing data collection tools, developing M&E mechanisms, and expanding data-sharing protocols. Actions 1a (comprehensive strategy for improving migration data), 1c (building national capacities in data collection), and 1e (collaboration between global databases) appear particularly well-covered. The sections on lessons learned and best practices (Sections 5 and 6) contain specific operational insights on data collection methodologies, tool design, and stakeholder engagement that would be directly relevant. The recommendations section also addresses data governance and IOM's Data Protection Principles. Synthesis specialists should prioritize the executive summary, lessons learned, best practices, and recommendations sections as starting points for deeper review.

References.Title: A THEMATIC EVALUATION OF THE DISPLACEMENT TRACKING MATRIX (DTM)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 3. The DTM is explicitly described as a system that 'regularly captures, processes and disseminates multi-layered information on the mobility, locations, vulnerabilities, and needs of displaced and mobile populations.' The evaluation assesses the timeliness, accuracy, and accessibility of DTM information products — directly relevant to Action 3b (systematic cooperation to exchange information on migration trends) and potentially Action 3c (information points along migration routes). The executive summary discusses how DTM outputs 'have helped provide timely and targeted assistance' and the recommendations address strengthening public outreach and dissemination. However, the report's focus is more on the institutional and operational aspects of the DTM system itself rather than on information provision to migrants per se, which limits its direct relevance to Objective 3's emphasis on informing migrants' decisions. Synthesis specialists should review the effectiveness section of the executive summary and the recommendations on stakeholder engagement and data dissemination.

References.Title: EVALUACIÓN DE EFECTOS DE LA ESTRATEGIA COMUNITARIA DE LA AGENCIA PARA LA REINCORPORACIÓN Y LA NORMALIZACIÓN ARN.
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears likely to offer relevant evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly around sustainable reintegration. The evaluation's primary subject — the ARN's Estrategia Comunitaria, including the Modelo de Reconciliación Comunitaria (MRC) and community initiatives — directly addresses the reintegration of former combatants (PPR) into communities, examining outcomes related to social cohesion, trust, community participation, and the sustainability of reintegration processes. While the population is domestic excombatants rather than international migrants, the evidence on community-based reintegration approaches, the role of receiving communities, and the conditions that enable or hinder sustainable reintegration (Actions 21h and 21i) appears substantive and potentially transferable. The excerpts suggest dedicated analysis of reintegration outcomes, including the challenges of PPR participation visibility, organizational sustainability, and institutional linkages. The sections on MRC effects, capital social fortalecimiento, and the Análisis de Trayectoria of community initiatives would likely be most valuable for a synthesis specialist to review. The recommendation sections also appear to address reintegration sustainability directly. Specialists should note the domestic (non-migration) context and assess transferability carefully.

References.Title: COOPERACIÓN INTERNACIONAL ENTRE EL MINISTERIO DE SALUD Y PROTECCIÓN SOCIAL DE COLOMBIA (MSPS) Y LA ORGANIZACIÓN INTERNACIONAL PARA LAS MIGRACIONES (OIM)
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 15, particularly regarding health service access for vulnerable and migrant-affected populations. The Convenio's core purpose — strengthening health system capacity in Colombian territorial entities to serve populations vulnerable due to conflict, displacement, poverty, and migration — aligns substantially with Action 15e (incorporating health needs of migrants in national and local health care policies). The report appears to provide substantive analysis of: health service delivery to displaced and conflict-affected populations across multiple dimensions (vaccination/PAI, TB, mental health, sexual and reproductive health, maternal health); capacity building of health secretariats to reach dispersed and underserved populations; barriers to health access including geographic dispersion, administrative weaknesses, and lack of stable personnel; and the role of international cooperation in bridging service gaps. The conclusions and recommendations sections discuss sustainability of health service access and the need for continued capacity support. Sections most likely worth reviewing for a synthesis specialist include the descriptions of Dimensiones 2, 3, 5, 6, and 9, as well as the conclusions (Section VII) and recommendations (Section VIII) regarding territorial health entities. The report's focus is on the Colombian health system rather than on migrant-specific service access per se, which limits its direct applicability, but the evidence on reaching vulnerable and displaced populations through strengthened health systems appears substantive.

References.Title: Ex-Post Evaluation Report "Developing capacities for forecasting and planning migration across the Mediterranean"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project's core purpose appears to be building Egypt's capacity to collect, analyze, and use migration data for evidence-based policymaking — directly aligning with this objective's theme and several associated actions. The establishment of the Migration Data Analysis Unit (MDAU) at CAPMAS, the development of labour migration forecasting methodology, and the production of the first labour market analysis report are all central outputs described extensively in the excerpts. These activities appear to directly relate to Actions 1c (building national capacities in data collection and analysis), 1d (collecting and analyzing data on effects and benefits of migration), 1h (conducting surveys to collect information on migrants), and 1j (developing country-specific migration profiles including labour market needs). The evaluation's findings on MDAU sustainability, CAPMAS technical capacity gaps, and recommendations for follow-up capacity building would likely provide valuable evidence for a synthesis on national migration data systems. Human reviewers should prioritize the project description section (3.1), the effectiveness and sustainability sections of the evaluation, and the recommendations — particularly those addressing CAPMAS capacity strengthening and the MDAU's ongoing function.

References.Title: Ex-Post Evaluation Report "Developing capacities for forecasting and planning migration across the Mediterranean"
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 5. The project's stated overall goal is to support the Government of Egypt to 'better plan and maximize the potential for demand-driven migration' and to 'meaningfully engage in labour negotiation with its selected EU counterparts to maximize the potential for demand-driven labour migration.' This directly relates to Actions 5a (bilateral labour mobility agreements), 5b (facilitating cross-regional labour mobility through cooperation arrangements), 5c (reviewing pathways for regular migration to optimize skills matching), and 5d (developing flexible labour mobility schemes). The multi-sectoral policy debates in Rome and Brussels aimed at facilitating mutually beneficial labour migration schemes between Egypt and EU countries are particularly relevant. The evaluation's discussion of the Valletta Action Plan context, the GoE's engagement with EU counterparts, and the THAMM follow-up initiative all relate to developing regular migration pathways. Human reviewers should prioritize the executive summary's discussion of the project's overall objective, the project description in section 3.1 (particularly the policy debates component), and the impact and sustainability sections discussing the GoE Action Plan and EU partnerships.

References.Title: INTERNAL INDEPENDENT MID-TERM EVALUATION OF IOM’S GLOBAL MIGRATION DATA PORTAL
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The evaluation's primary subject — the IOM Global Migration Data Portal managed by GMDAC — is explicitly named in GCM Action 1e as a key instrument for consolidating migration data in a transparent and user-friendly manner. The report appears to directly examine the Portal's effectiveness in facilitating access to migration data, its comprehensiveness, reliability, and timeliness, as well as its use by national statistical officers (NSOs), policymakers, researchers, and media — all core themes under Actions 1a, 1c, 1e, and 1j. Multiple conclusions address data availability, user satisfaction with data content, and capacity-building tools, which align closely with Actions 1c and 1e. Recommendations on linking to national data sources, improving country profiles, expanding SDG/GCM data sections, and enabling data extraction in different formats are directly relevant to Actions 1a, 1e, and 1j. The executive summary, conclusions (sections 1.1, 1.2, 2.1, 3.1, 4.1–4.3, 5.1–5.2), and recommendations sections would appear particularly valuable for human reviewers conducting a synthesis on this objective. The report likely represents one of the most directly relevant evaluations possible for Action 1e specifically, given the Portal is named therein. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: REINTEGRATION SUPPORT FOR CHADIAN RETURNEES FROM THE CENTRAL AFRICAN REPUBLIC
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21, particularly its associated actions on sustainable reintegration (Actions 21b, 21h, and 21i). The project is explicitly a reintegration support programme for Chadian returnees, and the evaluation comprehensively assesses the effectiveness, impact, sustainability, and relevance of reintegration interventions. The report appears to directly address Action 21b (gender-responsive and child-sensitive return and reintegration programmes with social and financial support), Action 21h (facilitating sustainable reintegration through vocational training, employment opportunities, decent work, and social services), and Action 21i (identifying and addressing needs of communities to which migrants return). The evaluation covers: income generating activities for female returnees; community infrastructure rehabilitation to strengthen absorptive capacity; community cohesion and host-returnee relations; sustainability of reintegration outcomes; and the particular vulnerabilities of female returnees. The project's dual focus on individual returnee economic empowerment and community-level absorptive capacity is directly relevant to the GCM's vision of sustainable reintegration. For a synthesis specialist, the sections on Component 2 (IGA activities), the sustainability assessments for each activity, the conclusions on community cohesion, and the recommendations on livelihoods interventions would appear particularly valuable as starting points. The evaluation's honest treatment of unintended impacts (beneficiaries abandoning livelihoods) and sustainability challenges also adds analytical value. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: RAVEL - EVALUATION OF THE REGIONAL ASSISTED VOLUNTARY RETURN AND REINTEGRATION PROGRAMME FOR STRANDED MIGRANTS IN EGYPT AND LIBYA
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would very likely be essential evidence for a synthesis on GCM Objective 21. The RAVEL program is explicitly and comprehensively focused on Assisted Voluntary Return and Reintegration (AVRR) — the core subject of GCM Objective 21. The report title, evaluation questions, and all five specific objectives directly address return and reintegration themes. The excerpts suggest extensive analysis across multiple dimensions of GCM Objective 21: (1) voluntary return processes and consent (Actions 21b, 21e) — the report evaluates the effectiveness of AVRR ex-Egypt and ex-Libya, including the role of referral networks and cooperation with national authorities; (2) sustainable reintegration (Action 21h) — the report dedicates substantial analysis to reintegration assistance, business start-up support, vocational training, psychosocial assistance, and the adequacy of reintegration grants; (3) cooperation frameworks (Action 21a) — the report examines inter-agency cooperation, government partnerships in transit and origin countries, and capacity-building of civil society; (4) emergency evacuation and return (Action 21b) — the Libyan civil war response and collective reintegration for evacuees is analyzed in depth; (5) monitoring and follow-up (Action 21f) — the report discusses monitoring challenges and recommendations for improvement. The conclusions and recommendations sections appear particularly rich for synthesis purposes, especially the detailed strengths/weaknesses/recommendations table covering pre-departure, reintegration, and capacity-building components. The discussion of reintegration sustainability challenges — including grant adequacy, unclaimed assistance in South Sudan, and business viability — would likely provide valuable nuanced evidence on what makes reintegration sustainable or not. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.

References.Title: TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE TO THE MINISTRY OF PEACE AND RECONSTRUCTION TO ENSURE EFFECTIVE ACCESS OF CONFLICT AFFECTED PERSONS TO VICTIMS ASSISTANCE PROCESSES IN NEPAL
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to have meaningful relevance for a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. A central output of the evaluated project is the establishment of a 'Centralized Database for Conflict Affected Persons Assistance (CD-CAP)' — a database consolidating past and ongoing victim assistance programs conducted by agencies and organizations in Nepal. The excerpts suggest this directly relates to Actions 1c (building national capacities in data collection and analysis), 1e (supporting collaboration between existing databases), and potentially 1j (developing country-specific profiles). The evaluation discusses challenges around data sharing (NGOs and UN agencies being reluctant to share information), the database's design and usability, and its potential to create institutional memory — all substantive content for a synthesis on migration-related data systems. The 'Reparation Quality Assessment Report' also appears to draw on data collection about conflict-affected persons. Specialists would want to review the 'Overview of the Project,' 'Results of the Evaluation,' and 'Impact' sections, which discuss the database's design, uptake challenges, and potential for evidence-based policymaking. Note that the data focus here is specific to conflict-affected persons and reparations, not international migration data broadly, which somewhat limits direct applicability.

References.Title: Final Evaluation: Capacity Building for Sustainable Development in Tonga
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The project's first output was explicitly dedicated to producing a migration and sustainable development baseline research report — a data collection exercise covering remittances, diaspora data, labour mobility, deportees, and the migration-environment-climate change nexus. The excerpts suggest substantive engagement with data gaps as a driver of the project, noting that 'data gaps in migration and development have limited the capacity of the GoT to develop evidence-based migration and sustainable development policy and programmes.' This directly relates to Actions 1d (collecting data on migration's effects and contributions to sustainable development), 1j (developing country-specific migration profiles), and 1k (research on the interrelationship between migration and sustainable development). The evaluation also discusses disaggregated data challenges (missing participant lists, lack of disaggregated data), touching on Action 1a and 1c. However, data collection is one of two outputs rather than the sole focus, and the depth of analysis on data systems per se appears limited compared to the policy development component. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the sections on Output 1 achievement (Table 5), the context section on data gaps, and the efficiency/monitoring sections, which appear to contain the most relevant content on data availability and evidence-based policymaking.

References.Title: Final Evaluation: Capacity Building for Sustainable Development in Tonga
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report appears to have meaningful relevance for a synthesis on GCM Objective 2, particularly its actions related to climate change, environmental degradation, and migration (Actions 2h, 2i, 2l). The project explicitly addresses the nexus between climate change, environmental degradation, and migration in Tonga — described as one of the most at-risk countries to natural hazards — and frames well-managed migration as a climate change adaptation strategy. The MSDP developed under the project directly engages with migration as a response to climate-driven pressures on livelihoods. The excerpts reference alignment with the SAMOA Pathway, the Paris Agreement, and the Cancun Adaptation Framework, and note that 'climate change has an impact on communities in terms of their economic sustainability, generating a need to better manage alternatives that lead to a sustainable development.' Action 2b (investing in programmes addressing adverse drivers including climate change adaptation) and Action 2i (developing adaptation and resilience strategies taking into account migration implications) appear particularly relevant. However, the project's focus is on policy development and capacity building rather than direct intervention to address drivers of migration, which limits the depth of evidence on this objective. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the relevance section (10.1.1) and the context/purpose sections for the most substantive content on climate-migration linkages.

References.Title: Final Evaluation: Capacity Building for Sustainable Development in Tonga
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The project's central purpose — developing a Migration and Sustainable Development Policy for Tonga — directly addresses the objective of enabling migrants and diasporas to contribute to sustainable development. The project title, evaluation questions, and stated objectives all explicitly engage with migration as a sustainable development strategy. The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: diaspora data collection (Action 19c on research into non-financial contributions), mainstreaming migration into national development planning (Action 19b — the MSDP is explicitly described as mainstreaming migration into MDA frameworks and the national budget), cross-government coordination mechanisms (TWG), and the role of remittances in Tonga's development context. The MSDP itself appears to operationalize Action 19b by integrating migration into sectoral policies across multiple ministries. The baseline research report covers remittances, diaspora data, and labour mobility — directly relevant to Actions 19c and 19d. The evaluation's coherence section discusses complementarity with other development initiatives, and the sustainability section examines whether development contributions from migration will be maintained. Synthesis specialists would particularly want to review sections 10.1 (relevance and alignment), 10.3 (effectiveness and achievement of results including the MSDP), 10.6 (sustainability), and the recommendations on MSDP implementation, as these appear to contain the most substantive evidence on migration-development policy integration in a small island developing state context.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF IOM’S INSTITUTIONAL APPROACH AND CONTRIBUTION TO THE 2030 AGENDA FOR SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely contribute meaningful evidence to a synthesis on GCM Objective 1. The evaluation appears to substantively address migration data challenges as a recurring theme throughout its findings and conclusions. The excerpts indicate that 'lack of data and analysis on migration issues and socioeconomic facets' is identified as a key challenge for IOM Country Offices, and that IOM's work on integrating migration into SDG statistics is explicitly discussed. The report references GMDAC's work to improve migration data and analysis, technical assistance to integrate migration in SDG statistics, and the development of internationally accepted indicators disaggregated by migration status — all of which relate to Actions 1a, 1c, 1d, and 1g. The evaluation also discusses IOM's contributions to CCA and UNSDCF processes, which depend on migration data availability. However, data collection and analysis appear to be a supporting theme rather than the central focus of the evaluation, which is primarily about IOM's institutional strategy on migration and sustainable development. Synthesis specialists would likely want to review the effectiveness and impact sections, particularly the discussions on SDG reporting challenges, data gaps at country level, and the role of GMDAC, as these appear most directly relevant to Objective 1 associated actions.

References.Title: EVALUATION OF IOM’S INSTITUTIONAL APPROACH AND CONTRIBUTION TO THE 2030 AGENDA FOR SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT
References.Abstract: Based on the excerpts reviewed, this report would likely be essential for a synthesis on GCM Objective 19. The evaluation appears to explicitly examine IOM's institutional strategy for migration and sustainable development, which is fundamentally aligned with Objective 19's core theme of enabling migrants and diasporas to contribute to sustainable development. The report's primary subject — IOM's M&SD Strategy and its contribution to the 2030 Agenda — directly addresses Actions 19a (full implementation of the 2030 Agenda for sustainable development) and 19b (integrating migration into development planning at local, national, regional and global levels). The excerpts suggest substantive analysis of: how migration is mainstreamed into national development planning (CCA, UNSDCF processes); IOM's programmatic work generating evidence on migration-development linkages; the role of the GCM and UNNM in advancing migration governance aligned with the 2030 Agenda; and IOM's contributions to SDG reporting and voluntary national reviews. Multiple findings and recommendations directly address these themes. For a synthesis specialist, the effectiveness section (on IOM's influence on legislation, policy and institutional arrangements), the impact section (on incorporating migration in VNRs and development planning), and the recommendations on capacity building and SDG mainstreaming would appear particularly valuable as starting points. Actions 19a, 19b, and 19c appear most directly covered. It is possible the full report contains additional relevant evidence beyond what the excerpts reveal.
